topics.title: Definitions - Perception
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT735 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Definitions - Perception

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Perception in Robot Navigation

In the context of **Robotics and Autonomous Systems**, **Perception** is formally defined as the computational process by which a robot acquires, processes, interprets, and reasons about sensory data drawn from its internal state (proprioception) and its surrounding environment (exteroception) to build a coherent internal representation that supports decision-making, planning, and action.

Mathematically, perception can be abstracted as an inference problem:

$$P(\text{World State} \mid \text{Sensor Data}) \propto P(\text{Sensor Data} \mid \text{World State}) \cdot P(\text{World State})$$

This is the classical **Bayesian formulation** of perception, where the robot inverts a sensor model to estimate the world that most likely produced the observed measurements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Perception is the **first stage of the Sense–Plan–Act paradigm** (and its modern counterpart, the **Reactive / Hybrid architecture**). Without perception, no localization, mapping, or path planning is possible.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of perception in robotics the same way a **blindfolded person with a flashlight navigates a dark room**. The flashlight beam is the sensor — it gives partial, noisy glimpses of furniture (obstacles), walls (boundaries), and floor texture (terrain). The brain combines these flashes over time, builds a rough mental map, and infers where the doorway probably is. The robot does the same: it pings the world with sensors (LIDAR, camera, IMU) and uses algorithms to *infer* what the world looks like.

- **Human eye** → Robot camera (RGB / Depth)
- **Human ear (sound localization)** → Robot microphone array / ultrasonic sensor
- **Human skin (touch)** → Robot tactile / bumper / force sensor
- **Human inner ear (balance)** → Robot IMU / gyroscope / accelerometer
- **Human proprioception (knowing where your arm is)** → Robot joint encoders

## 1.3 Key Sub-Definitions the Examiner Expects

> [!NOTE]
> The following definitions are **high-frequency KTU short-answer items**.

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Proprioception** | Perception of the robot's *own* internal state (pose, velocity, joint angles, battery). |
| **Exteroception** | Perception of the *external* environment (obstacles, landmarks, other agents). |
| **Active Sensor** | Emits energy into the environment and measures the response (e.g., LIDAR, sonar, radar). |
| **Passive Sensor** | Only measures ambient energy (e.g., camera, microphone, thermometer). |
| **Sensor Fusion** | Combining data from multiple sensors to reduce uncertainty and improve robustness. |
| **Perception Pipeline** | Ordered sequence: Sensing → Pre-processing → Feature Extraction → Interpretation / Scene Understanding. |
| **Field of View (FoV)** | The angular cone (in 2D) or solid angle (in 3D) over which a sensor gathers useful data. |
| **Range** | The maximum reliable operating distance of a sensor (often expressed in **meters**). |
| **Resolution** | The smallest distinguishable change in the measured quantity (angular, spatial, temporal). |

## 1.4 Why Perception Matters in Navigation

The **autonomous navigation stack** universally follows this hierarchy:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Perception → Localization → Mapping → Path Planning → Motion Control → Actuation**

If perception fails (e.g., the robot confuses a mirror for an open doorway), every downstream module fails. Hence, perception is the **single point of failure** that defines the safety and reliability ceiling of the entire autonomous system.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Pillars: Proprioception vs Exteroception

Perception in mobile robots is conventionally partitioned into two **orthogonal categories**:

### A. Proprioceptive Perception (Internal State)
- Encoders (wheel odometry) → measures **Δs = Δθ × r** with uncertainty
- IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit) → measures linear acceleration *a* and angular velocity *ω*
- GPS (when available outdoors) → global position with **~3–5 m** civilian accuracy
- Battery / current / voltage sensors → internal health monitoring

### B. Exteroceptive Perception (External World)
- **2D/3D LIDAR** → range measurements via time-of-flight (ToF)
- **RGB / RGB-D Camera** → dense color and depth imagery
- **Sonar / Ultrasonic** → short-range obstacle detection
- **Radar** → long-range, weather-robust
- **Depth sensors (Structured Light, ToF cameras)**

## 2.2 The Perception Pipeline (Step-by-Step Breakdown)

The pipeline can be decomposed into **five sequential stages**:

1. **Signal Acquisition (Sensing)**  
   Raw analog/digital measurements $z_t$ arrive at discrete time steps $t = 0, 1, 2, \dots$

2. **Pre-processing**  
   Noise filtering (e.g., Gaussian filter), outlier removal, coordinate transformation from sensor frame to robot frame.

3. **Feature Extraction**  
   Identification of meaningful patterns: edges, corners (Harris, SIFT, ORB), planes, clusters.

4. **Data Association / Registration**  
   Matching current observations to prior maps or to other sensor frames (ICP, RANSAC).

5. **Interpretation / Scene Understanding**  
   High-level semantic labelling: "this cluster is a pedestrian", "this plane is a wall".

## 2.3 Sensor Classification Taxonomy

A clean taxonomy frequently tested by KTU examiners:

| Criterion | Class A | Class B |
|-----------|---------|---------|
| Energy source | **Active** | **Passive** |
| Information type | Range-bearing | Appearance (intensity) |
| Output dimensionality | 1D / 2D / 3D | Image (2D array) |
| Coupling to robot state | Exteroceptive | Proprioceptive |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **every equation** a KTU student is expected to recognise for the "Definitions – Perception" topic.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | LIDAR Time-of-Flight | $d = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | $c$ = speed of light **≈ 3 × 10⁸ m/s**, $\Delta t$ = round-trip time (s), $d$ = distance (m) |
| 2 | Sonar Time-of-Flight | $d = \dfrac{v_s \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | $v_s$ = speed of sound in air **≈ 343 m/s** |
| 3 | Wheel Odometry | $\Delta s = r \cdot \Delta \theta$ | $r$ = wheel radius (m), $\Delta \theta$ = angular rotation (rad) |
| 4 | Camera Pinhole Projection | $u = f \cdot \dfrac{X}{Z}$ | $f$ = focal length (px), $X,Z$ = world coordinates (m) |
| 5 | Gaussian Noise Model | $z \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ | $\mu$ = true value, $\sigma$ = std. deviation |
| 6 | Bayes Filter (Posterior) | $\text{bel}(x_t) = \eta \, P(z_t \mid x_t) \sum_{x_{t-1}} P(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)\,\text{bel}(x_{t-1})$ | $x_t$ = state, $z_t$ = measurement, $u_t$ = control, $\eta$ = normaliser |
| 7 | Sensor Uncertainty (Sum) | $\sigma_{\text{fused}}^2 = \left(\sum_i \dfrac{1}{\sigma_i^2}\right)^{-1}$ | $\sigma_i$ = individual sensor std. dev. |
| 8 | Field of View (2D) | $\text{FoV} = 2 \cdot \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{w}{2f}\right)$ | $w$ = sensor width, $f$ = focal length |
| 9 | Occupancy Grid Update | $l_{t} = l_{t-1} + \text{inv\_sensor\_model}(z_t - x_t)$ | $l_t$ = log-odds of occupancy |
| 10 | Cramer-Rao Lower Bound | $\text{Var}(\hat{x}) \geq \dfrac{1}{\mathcal{I}(x)}$ | $\mathcal{I}$ = Fisher Information |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles (Tesla, Waymo):** Combine camera + radar + LIDAR for robust perception under varied lighting.
- **Warehouse AMRs (Amazon Kiva):** Rely on 2D LIDAR + wheel odometry for perception in structured aisles.
- **Search & Rescue Drones:** Use thermal cameras + radar to perceive humans through smoke.
- **Surgical Robots:** Use force/tactile proprioception to detect tissue stiffness.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: LIDAR Range Measurement from Time-of-Flight

A 2D LIDAR emits a short laser pulse and measures the round-trip time $\Delta t$ until the reflected photon returns.

**Step 1 — Relate distance to time.**  
The pulse travels to the obstacle and back, so the total path length is $2d$. Speed is $c$.

$$
d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Introduce noise.**  
Real sensors exhibit timing jitter. Model $\Delta t$ as:

$$
\Delta t = \Delta t_{\text{true}} + \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_t^2)
$$

**Step 3 — Propagate uncertainty** through the linear equation using first-order Taylor expansion:

$$
\sigma_d^2 = \left(\frac{\partial d}{\partial \Delta t}\right)^{2} \sigma_t^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the partial derivative:**

$$
\frac{\partial d}{\partial \Delta t} = \frac{c}{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute:**

$$
\sigma_d^{2} = \left(\frac{c}{2}\right)^{2} \sigma_t^{2} = \frac{c^{2}\, \sigma_t^{2}}{4}
$$

**Step 6 — Take the square root to obtain the range standard deviation:**

$$
\sigma_d = \frac{c \cdot \sigma_t}{2}
$$

**Interpretation:** A timing jitter of **σₜ = 1 ns** produces a range uncertainty of:

$$
\sigma_d = \frac{(3\times 10^{8}) \cdot (1 \times 10^{-9})}{2} = 0.15 \text{ m}
$$

This is why automotive LIDARs push for sub-nanosecond timing electronics.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Sensor Fusion of Two Independent Gaussian Measurements

Suppose two sensors measure the same quantity $x$ independently:

$$
z_1 = x + \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_1^{2}), \qquad z_2 = x + \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_2^{2})
$$

**Step 1 — Write likelihoods:**

$$
P(z_1 \mid x) \propto \exp\!\left(-\frac{(z_1 - x)^{2}}{2\sigma_1^{2}}\right)
$$

$$
P(z_2 \mid x) \propto \exp\!\left(-\frac{(z_2 - x)^{2}}{2\sigma_2^{2}}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Combine (joint likelihood, assuming independence):**

$$
P(z_1, z_2 \mid x) \propto \exp\!\left(-\frac{(z_1 - x)^{2}}{2\sigma_1^{2}} - \frac{(z_2 - x)^{2}}{2\sigma_2^{2}}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Maximise with respect to $x$.** Set derivative to zero:

$$
\frac{(z_1 - \hat{x})}{\sigma_1^{2}} + \frac{(z_2 - \hat{x})}{\sigma_2^{2}} = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the fused estimate $\hat{x}$:**

$$
\hat{x} \left(\frac{1}{\sigma_1^{2}} + \frac{1}{\sigma_2^{2}}\right) = \frac{z_1}{\sigma_1^{2}} + \frac{z_2}{\sigma_2^{2}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\hat{x} = \frac{\sigma_2^{2}\, z_1 + \sigma_1^{2}\, z_2}{\sigma_1^{2} + \sigma_2^{2}}\;}
$$

**Step 5 — Variance of the fused estimate:**

$$
\boxed{\;\sigma_{\text{fused}}^{2} = \frac{\sigma_1^{2}\, \sigma_2^{2}}{\sigma_1^{2} + \sigma_2^{2}} = \left(\frac{1}{\sigma_1^{2}} + \frac{1}{\sigma_2^{2}}\right)^{-1}\;}
$$

**Engineering insight:** The fused variance is **always smaller** than either individual variance, which mathematically justifies sensor fusion.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Viva Hot-Spot)

| Property | LIDAR | Camera (Mono) | Sonar | IMU |
|----------|-------|---------------|-------|-----|
| Range | 100–250 m | 0.5–∞ (theoretical) | 0.1–10 m | n/a (proprioceptive) |
| Accuracy | ± 2 cm | depth ambiguous | ± 5 cm | drifts over time |
| Active/Passive | Active | Passive | Active | Passive |
| FoV | 360° / 270° | ~60°–120° | ~15°–30° | n/a |
| Works in dark | **Yes** | **No** (needs light) | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| Cost | High | Low | Very low | Low |
| Failure mode | Sun glare, fog | Low light, motion blur | Specular reflection | Bias drift |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matrix matters for KTU:** Examiners often ask *"Compare and contrast LIDAR and camera-based perception."* This table is your ready-made answer.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The Complete Perception Subsystem

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the full functional architecture of a robot's perception subsystem, including all internal data flows and feedback loops.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[World Environment] -->|emits/reflects energy| B[Raw Sensor Array]
    B --> C1[LIDAR Driver]
    B --> C2[Camera ISP]
    B --> C3[IMU FIFO Buffer]
    C1 --> D[Time Synchronization Clock]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> E[Pre-processing Node]
    E --> F1[Noise Filtering]
    E --> F2[Outlier Rejection]
    E --> F3[Coordinate Transform to Robot Frame]
    F1 --> G[Feature Extraction Layer]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H1[Geometric Features corners edges planes]
    G --> H2[Semantic Features objects pedestrians lanes]
    H1 --> I[Sensor Fusion Engine]
    H2 --> I
    I --> J[Bayesian Filter Estimator]
    J --> K[Occupancy Grid Map]
    J --> L[Robot Pose Estimate]
    K --> M[Scene Understanding Module]
    L --> M
    M --> N[High Level Representation]
    N --> O[Downstream Planner Localization SLAM]
    O --> P[Motion Controller]
    P --> Q[Robot Actuators]
    Q -->|physical motion| A
```

**Reading the diagram:**  
The cycle begins at *World Environment* and ends back at it via the *Motion Controller → Actuators* loop. Note the central role of the **Sensor Fusion Engine** and the **Bayesian Filter Estimator** — these are the two most important blocks for KTU written answers.

---

## 4.2 Taxonomy — Classification of Robotic Sensors

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Robotic Sensors] --> P[Proprioceptive]
    S --> E[Exteroceptive]
    P --> P1[Wheel Encoders]
    P --> P2[IMU Accelerometer Gyroscope]
    P --> P3[GPS Receiver]
    P --> P4[Force Torque Sensor]
    E --> EA[Active Sensors]
    E --> EP[Passive Sensors]
    EA --> EA1[LIDAR]
    EA --> EA2[Sonar Ultrasonic]
    EA --> EA3[Radar]
    EA --> EA4[Structured Light Depth]
    EP --> EP1[Mono RGB Camera]
    EP --> EP2[Stereo Camera]
    EP --> EP3[Thermal Infrared Camera]
    EP --> EP4[Microphone Array]
```

---

## 4.3 Information Flow — Sense → Perceive → Plan → Act

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Sense Stage] --> S2[Perception Stage]
    S2 --> S3[Localization and Mapping]
    S3 --> S4[Path Planning Stage]
    S4 --> S5[Motion Control Stage]
    S5 --> S6[Actuation Stage]
    S6 -->|feedback| S1
```

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Draw the Sense-Plan-Act architecture for a mobile robot."* The diagram above is the canonical answer. Memorise the block names and arrows in order.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define perception in the context of mobile robot navigation. Differentiate between proprioception and exteroception with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Perception (1 mark):** Perception is the process by which a robot uses sensors to acquire, process, and interpret data about its internal state and external environment to build an internal representation useful for navigation and decision-making.
- **Proprioception (1 mark):** Perception of the robot's *own* state. *Example:* Wheel encoders measuring distance travelled.
- **Exteroception (1 mark):** Perception of the *external* environment. *Example:* LIDAR detecting the distance to a nearby wall.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Explain the difference between active and passive sensors with two examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Active sensor (1 mark):** Emits energy into the environment and measures the returned/reflected signal. *Examples:* LIDAR, Sonar.
- **Passive sensor (1 mark):** Only measures ambient energy without emitting anything. *Examples:* Camera, Microphone.
- **Significance (1 mark):** Active sensors work in darkness; passive sensors are stealthy and consume less power.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **complete perception pipeline** of a mobile robot starting from raw sensor acquisition to high-level scene understanding. Discuss the function of **each stage** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robot measures the distance to an obstacle using a LIDAR. The LIDAR's internal clock has a timing jitter of **σₜ = 2 ns**. Compute:  
(i) the range $d$ when the measured round-trip time is **Δt = 666.67 ns**, and  
(ii) the standard deviation of the range estimate **σ_d**.  
Take $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a)

The perception pipeline consists of five sequential stages:

1. **Signal Acquisition (1 mark):** Raw measurements $z_t$ are obtained from sensors (LIDAR, camera, IMU) at discrete time instants.
2. **Pre-processing (1 mark):** Noise filtering (Gaussian filter), outlier removal, and coordinate transformation from sensor frame to robot frame.
3. **Feature Extraction (1 mark):** Identification of meaningful geometric (edges, corners) and semantic (objects, lanes) primitives.
4. **Data Association (1 mark):** Matching current observations to prior map entries (e.g., ICP, RANSAC).
5. **Scene Understanding / Interpretation (1 mark):** High-level labelling of obstacles, free space, traversable terrain.  
   **Block diagram (2 marks):** Standard 5-block chain: Sensor → Pre-process → Feature → Associate → Interpret.

#### Model Solution (b)

**(i) Range computation (3 marks):**

$$
d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2} = \frac{(3 \times 10^{8}) \cdot (666.67 \times 10^{-9})}{2}
$$

$$
d = \frac{200.001}{2} = 100.0005 \text{ m} \approx \mathbf{100 \; m}
$$

**(ii) Standard deviation (4 marks):**

From the uncertainty propagation formula:

$$
\sigma_d = \frac{c \cdot \sigma_t}{2} = \frac{(3 \times 10^{8}) \cdot (2 \times 10^{-9})}{2}
$$

$$
\sigma_d = \frac{0.6}{2} = \mathbf{0.3 \; m}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning (Part A questions):** Students frequently lose marks because they (i) write definitions but skip the *example*, or (ii) confuse proprioception with exteroception. **Always pair a definition with at least one concrete sensor example.**

---

### Question B (Choice 2). [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** Two independent distance sensors measure the distance to the same wall. Sensor 1 reports **z₁ = 5.20 m** with uncertainty **σ₁ = 0.30 m**. Sensor 2 reports **z₂ = 5.05 m** with uncertainty **σ₂ = 0.20 m**.  
Using **Bayesian fusion**, compute:  
(i) the fused estimate $\hat{x}$, and  
(ii) the fused variance $\sigma_{\text{fused}}^{2}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **LIDAR and Camera-based perception** with respect to range, accuracy, field of view, operating conditions (light/weather), and cost. State **two situations** where each is preferred. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a)

**(i) Fused estimate (4 marks):**

$$
\hat{x} = \frac{\sigma_2^{2}\, z_1 + \sigma_1^{2}\, z_2}{\sigma_1^{2} + \sigma_2^{2}}
$$

$$
\sigma_1^{2} = 0.09, \quad \sigma_2^{2} = 0.04
$$

$$
\hat{x} = \frac{(0.04)(5.20) + (0.09)(5.05)}{0.09 + 0.04} = \frac{0.2080 + 0.4545}{0.13}
$$

$$
\hat{x} = \frac{0.6625}{0.13} = \mathbf{5.096 \; m}
$$

**(ii) Fused variance (3 marks):**

$$
\sigma_{\text{fused}}^{2} = \frac{\sigma_1^{2}\, \sigma_2^{2}}{\sigma_1^{2} + \sigma_2^{2}} = \frac{(0.09)(0.04)}{0.13} = \frac{0.0036}{0.13}
$$

$$
\sigma_{\text{fused}}^{2} = \mathbf{0.02769 \; m^2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma_{\text{fused}} \approx 0.166 \text{ m}
$$

Note that $0.166 < 0.20 < 0.30$, confirming the fusion reduces uncertainty.

#### Model Solution (b)

| Parameter (1 mark for correct comparison on each) | LIDAR | Camera |
|---|---|---|
| Range | 100–250 m | Limited by illumination |
| Accuracy | ± 2 cm | Depth ambiguous (mono) |
| Field of View | 360° / 270° | 60°–120° |
| Lighting | Works in dark | Fails in darkness |
| Weather | Degrades in fog | Degrades in glare |
| Cost | High | Low |

**Preferred situations (2 marks):**  
- **LIDAR preferred:** (i) Autonomous highway driving at night, (ii) Warehouse mapping where 360° coverage is required.  
- **Camera preferred:** (i) Lane-keeping on sunny roads where colour cues matter, (ii) Object classification (traffic signs, pedestrians) requiring texture.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning (Part B questions):**  
> 1. In numerical problems, **always show substitution of numerical values** before computing — marks are split between formula recall (2–3 marks) and numerical substitution (2–3 marks).  
> 2. In comparison questions, students often write paragraphs instead of **tabular comparison** — you lose marks for lack of clarity. **Always tabulate.**  
> 3. Don't forget to **state assumptions** (Gaussian noise, independence of sensors) — examiners reward this explicitly.  
> 4. In block diagrams, every block must be **labelled**, and arrows must have a clear direction. A diagram without labels gets **zero marks** in KTU valuation.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Definitions – Perception"**

- **Perception = Sensing + Processing + Interpretation** (in that order).
- **Two categories:** Proprioception (internal) vs Exteroception (external).
- **Two energy modes:** Active (emits energy) vs Passive (observes ambient energy).
- **Bayesian formulation:** $P(\text{world} \mid \text{data}) \propto P(\text{data} \mid \text{world}) \cdot P(\text{world})$ — must be remembered verbatim.
- **LIDAR ToF:** $d = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ — speed of light $c \approx 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s.
- **Sonar ToF:** $d = \dfrac{v_s \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ — speed of sound $v_s \approx 343$ m/s.
- **Odometry:** $\Delta s = r \cdot \Delta \theta$ — basic kinematic relation for wheel rotation.
- **Sensor fusion (2 sensors, Gaussian):**  
  $$\hat{x} = \frac{\sigma_2^{2}\, z_1 + \sigma_1^{2}\, z_2}{\sigma_1^{2} + \sigma_2^{2}}, \quad \sigma_{\text{fused}}^{2} = \frac{\sigma_1^{2}\sigma_2^{2}}{\sigma_1^{2}+\sigma_2^{2}}$$
- **Perception Pipeline:** Sensing → Pre-processing → Feature Extraction → Association → Scene Understanding.
- **Sense–Plan–Act architecture** remains the canonical mental model for all KTU navigation problems.
- **Field of View formula:** $\text{FoV} = 2 \arctan(w / 2f)$.
- **Cramer-Rao bound** sets the theoretical minimum variance for any unbiased estimator.
- **LIDAR vs Camera:** LIDAR wins on accuracy & night operation; Camera wins on cost & semantic understanding.
- **Golden rule for exams:** Always pair *every definition* with a *concrete sensor example* and a *real-world application*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Localization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT735 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Localization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 1 — Localization

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Localization** is formally defined as the problem of determining the pose (position $(x, y)$ and orientation $\theta$) of a mobile robot relative to a given map of the environment, using noisy sensor observations and control inputs over time. In the KTU 2024 syllabus (PERUT735), this is treated as a **state estimation problem under uncertainty**, fundamentally grounded in **Bayesian inference**.

---

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

Localization is the process by which a robot estimates its own configuration $x_t = (x_t, y_t, \theta_t)^T$ inside a known coordinate frame or occupancy grid map $m$, given:

- A sequence of **control actions** (odometry, wheel encoder readings) $u_{1:t} = \{u_1, u_2, \dots, u_t\}$
- A sequence of **sensor measurements** (lidar, sonar, camera, GPS) $z_{1:t} = \{z_1, z_2, \dots, z_t\}$

Mathematically, localization seeks the **posterior probability density**:

$$bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}, m)$$

This is called the **belief state** of the robot at time $t$.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Blindfolded Tourist"

Imagine you are dropped blindfolded into an unfamiliar city. You are given:

1. **A street map** (the known map $m$)
2. **A pedometer that is slightly inaccurate** (noisy odometry $u_t$)
3. **A faint GPS signal that works only sometimes** (range/bearing sensor $z_t$)
4. **Your memory of past footsteps** (recursion over time)

Your task: **figure out exactly where you are**.

- If you *roughly* knew where you started (e.g., near the airport), you can refine this estimate using footsteps → **Position Tracking**.
- If you had *no clue* where you started, you'd keep a probability over **every possible intersection** → **Global Localization**.
- If someone secretly picks you up and moves you → **Kidnapped Robot Problem**.

This is precisely the localization problem in robotics — except the robot must do it algorithmically, in real-time, with guaranteed convergence.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric — Localization Accuracy:** Typically measured in meters for $(x, y)$ error and degrees/radians for $\theta$ error. Industry benchmarks for indoor service robots demand **< 0.05 m positional error** and **< 2° angular error**.

---

## 1.3 Why Localization Matters in PERUT735

Without a reliable pose estimate, **every downstream navigation task fails catastrophically**:

- **Path planning** uses the robot's start pose to compute a trajectory.
- **Mapping** (SLAM) requires the robot's pose to register new observations onto the map.
- **Obstacle avoidance** depends on knowing where the robot is relative to static and dynamic hazards.

Localization is, therefore, the **backbone of the perception → decision → action loop** in mobile robotics.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Canonical Localization Problems

The KTU syllabus explicitly distinguishes three problem settings based on prior knowledge of the initial pose:

| # | Problem | Initial Knowledge | Difficulty | Typical Algorithm |
|---|---------|-------------------|------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Position Tracking** | Approximate initial pose known | Low (local) | Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) |
| 2 | **Global Localization** | No prior on initial pose | High (global) | Monte Carlo Localization (MCL) |
| 3 | **Kidnapped Robot Problem** | Pose known, then teleported | Very high | MCL with global recovery + sensor resetting |

> [!NOTE]
> **Position tracking** is mathematically a *local* estimation problem (Gaussian assumptions are valid). **Global localization** is inherently *multi-modal* (many hypotheses) and requires non-parametric representations like particle clouds.

---

## 2.2 Probabilistic Framework — Bayes Filter

The **Bayes Filter** is the unified mathematical backbone of all probabilistic localization methods. It recursively computes the belief $bel(x_t)$ in two alternating phases:

### Phase A — Prediction (Motion Update)
Uses the **control input** $u_t$ and the motion model $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$:

$$\overline{bel}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \cdot bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

### Phase B — Correction (Measurement Update)
Uses the **sensor measurement** $z_t$ and the measurement model $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$:

$$bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \cdot \overline{bel}(x_t)$$

where $\eta$ is the **normalization constant**:

$$\eta = \left[ \int p(z_t \mid x_t', m) \cdot \overline{bel}(x_t') \, dx_t' \right]^{-1}$$

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Statement | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|--------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Belief State | $bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ | Probability density |
| 2 | Bayes Filter Prediction | $\overline{bel}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})\,bel(x_{t-1})\,dx_{t-1}$ | Chapman–Kolmogorov |
| 3 | Bayes Filter Update | $bel(x_t) = \eta\,p(z_t \mid x_t, m)\,\overline{bel}(x_t)$ | $\eta$ normalizer |
| 4 | Markov Assumption | $p(x_t \mid x_{0:t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$ | Conditional independence |
| 5 | Odometry Motion Model | $\hat{x}_t = f(\hat{x}_{t-1}, u_t) + \varepsilon_t$ | $\varepsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$ |
| 6 | Beam Range-Finder Model | $p(z_t \mid x_t, m) = \prod_{k=1}^{K} p(z_t^k \mid x_t, m)$ | Per-beam likelihood |
| 7 | EKF Localization Mean | $\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t))$ | Update equation |
| 8 | EKF Kalman Gain | $K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}$ | Optimal linear gain |
| 9 | EKF Covariance Update | $\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t$ | Posterior covariance |
| 10 | Particle Filter Weight | $w_t^{[i]} = w_{t-1}^{[i]} \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}, m)$ | Importance sampling |
| 11 | Effective Sample Size | $N_{eff} = \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{M} (w_t^{[i]})^2 \right]^{-1}$ | Resampling trigger |
| 12 | Multinomial Resampling | Draw $M$ samples $\propto w_t^{[i]}$ then reset $w_t^{[i]} = 1/M$ | Variance reduction |
| 13 | Map Resolution | $\Delta_{grid}$ typically $= 0.05$ to $0.30$ m | Occupancy grid cell |
| 14 | Standard Metrics | Positional error $\leq 0.05$ m; Angular error $\leq 2°$ | Service robot benchmarks |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU examinations, **the Bayes Filter two-step recursion** is worth **6–8 marks** on its own when asked as a derivation. Memorize both phases separately.

---

## 2.4 Algorithm Taxonomy

The Bayes filter is **abstract**; concrete algorithms instantiate it with different representations of $bel(x_t)$:

- **Kalman Filter (KF)** → Gaussian over unimodal state. Closed-form optimal for linear-Gaussian systems.
- **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** → Linearized via Jacobians for nonlinear models. Used in **position tracking**.
- **Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF)** → Sigma-point propagation; higher-order accuracy than EKF.
- **Grid-based Markov Localization** → Discrete histogram over the map. Tractable only for small environments.
- **Monte Carlo Localization (MCL) / Particle Filter** → Set of weighted samples. The standard for **global localization**.
- **Multi-Hypothesis Tracking (MHT)** → Maintains multiple Kalman filters simultaneously.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application | Localization Method |
|--------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Warehouse Automation (AMR)** | Amazon Kiva robots | Particle filter + lidar |
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | Self-driving cars | EKF on GPS/IMU + particle filter for lane localization |
| **Underwater ROVs** | Sub-sea exploration | UKF on DVL + sonar beacons |
| **Domestic Vacuum Cleaners** | iRobot Roomba | Particle filter on wheel odometry + IR |
| **Drones (UAVs)** | Indoor inspection | Visual-inertial EKF / VIO |
| **Planetary Rovers** | NASA Mars rovers | EKF on visual odometry + IMU |

Localization is **literally the GPS of indoor/structured environments** — and in places where GPS fails (indoors, underwater, on Mars, in tunnels), localization algorithms *are* the positioning system.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📐 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Bayes Filter Update from First Principles

**Goal:** Derive $bel(x_t) = \eta\,p(z_t \mid x_t, m)\,\overline{bel}(x_t)$ from the definition of conditional probability.

### Step 1 — Start from the desired posterior
By definition of the belief state at time $t$:

$$bel(x_t) \;=\; p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}, m)$$

### Step 2 — Split the measurement history
Using the chain rule of probability and **conditional independence of past measurements given the current state** (Markov assumption):

$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}, m) \;=\; \eta_t \, p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m)\, p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m)$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Markov assumption
Since $x_t$ subsumes all relevant past information, $z_t$ depends only on $x_t$ and the map $m$:

$$p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) \;=\; p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$$

### Step 4 — Recognize the prediction term
The second factor is precisely the **prediction density** (after motion but before observation):

$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) \;=\; \overline{bel}(x_t)$$

### Step 5 — Assemble the final recursion
Substituting Steps 3 and 4 back into Step 2:

$$bel(x_t) \;=\; \eta_t \, p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \, \overline{bel}(x_t)$$

This is the **measurement update equation** of the Bayes Filter.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Prediction Step (Chapman–Kolmogorov)

**Goal:** Show that $\overline{bel}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})\,bel(x_{t-1})\,dx_{t-1}$.

### Step 1 — Write the prediction as a marginalization
By the law of total probability, marginalizing over all possible prior states $x_{t-1}$:

$$\overline{bel}(x_t) \;=\; p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m)$$

### Step 2 — Marginalize over $x_{t-1}$
Using the identity $p(A) = \int p(A \mid B)\,p(B)\,dB$:

$$\overline{bel}(x_t) \;=\; \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m)\, p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m)\, dx_{t-1}$$

### Step 3 — Apply Markov assumption
The future state depends only on the immediate prior state and the current control:

$$p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) \;=\; p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$$

Also, the prior $bel(x_{t-1})$ already incorporates $z_{1:t-1}$ and $u_{1:t-1}$, so the extra $u_t$ factor can be folded into the prior:

$$p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) \;=\; bel(x_{t-1})$$

### Step 4 — Final form
Combining:

$$\overline{bel}(x_t) \;=\; \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)\, bel(x_{t-1})\, dx_{t-1}$$

This is the **motion update equation** of the Bayes Filter.

---

## 3.3 EKF Localization Linearization

The Extended Kalman Filter applies to nonlinear systems of the form:

$$x_t = g(x_{t-1}, u_t) + \varepsilon_t, \qquad z_t = h(x_t) + \delta_t$$

where $\varepsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$ and $\delta_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t)$.

### Step 1 — Compute Jacobians of motion and measurement models

$$G_t = \frac{\partial g(x_{t-1}, u_t)}{\partial x_{t-1}} \bigg\rvert_{\mu_{t-1}}, \qquad H_t = \frac{\partial h(x_t)}{\partial x_t} \bigg\rvert_{\bar{\mu}_t}$$

### Step 2 — Prediction step (mean and covariance)

$$\bar{\mu}_t = g(\mu_{t-1}, u_t)$$

$$\bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \Sigma_{t-1} G_t^T + R_t$$

### Step 3 — Kalman Gain computation

$$K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}$$

### Step 4 — Correction step (mean and covariance)

$$\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t))$$

$$\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t$$

The **innovation term** $\nu_t = z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t)$ measures how surprised the robot is by the sensor reading. A large innovation increases $K_t$, weighting the sensor more heavily.

---

## 3.4 Particle Filter Weight Update Derivation

### Step 1 — Importance Sampling Setup
Target distribution: $bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$
Proposal (sampling) distribution: $q(x_t) = p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}^{[i]}, u_t)$ (i.e., the motion model).

### Step 2 — Importance Weight Recursion
By the importance sampling principle, each particle's weight is:

$$w_t^{[i]} \propto \frac{p(x_t^{[i]} \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})}{q(x_t^{[i]})}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the Bayes Filter
Expanding the numerator using the measurement update $p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) \propto p(z_t \mid x_t, m)\,p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)\,bel(x_{t-1})$ and dividing by the proposal:

$$w_t^{[i]} \propto w_{t-1}^{[i]} \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}, m)$$

### Step 4 — Normalize
$$\tilde{w}_t^{[i]} = \frac{w_t^{[i]}}{\sum_{j=1}^{M} w_t^{[j]}}$$

The weights $\tilde{w}_t^{[i]}$ represent the **posterior probability that particle $i$ is the true robot pose**.

---

## 3.5 Effective Sample Size — Resampling Trigger

To detect particle degeneracy, compute:

$$N_{eff} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{M} (\tilde{w}_t^{[i]})^2}$$

When $N_{eff}$ falls below a threshold (commonly $M/2$), **resampling** is triggered. This prevents the filter from degenerating into a single high-weight particle, which would lose representational diversity.

---

## 3.6 Summary Analytical Matrix

| Quantity | Role in Localization | Source of Uncertainty |
|----------|---------------------|------------------------|
| $bel(x_t)$ | Robot's current belief | All sensor + motion noise |
| $\overline{bel}(x_t)$ | Belief before seeing $z_t$ | Motion noise $\varepsilon_t$ |
| $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$ | Motion model | Wheel slip, drift |
| $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ | Measurement model | Lidar noise, occlusions |
| $K_t$ | EKF trust in sensors | Cross-covariance |
| $w_t^{[i]}$ | Particle importance | Sensor likelihood |
| $N_{eff}$ | Filter health indicator | Particle concentration |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Localization in the Robot Navigation Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Wheel Encoders] --> C[Motion Model p x given x and u]
    B[Lidar / Camera / IMU] --> D[Measurement Model p z given x and m]
    E[Known Map m] --> D
    C --> F[Bayes Filter Predict]
    F --> G[Belief bel x t before observation]
    D --> H[Bayes Filter Update]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Posterior Belief bel x t]
    I --> J{Algorithm Selection}
    J -->|Linear Gaussian| K[EKF Localization]
    J -->|Multimodal Global| L[MCL Particle Filter]
    J -->|Discrete Small Map| M[Grid Markov Localization]
    K --> N[Robot Pose x t and y t theta t]
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Path Planner]
    O --> P[Motor Controller]
```

## 4.2 Bayes Filter Recursion Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Start t equals 0] --> S2[Initialize bel x0]
    S2 --> L1{For each t in 1 to T}
    L1 --> P1[Read control u t]
    P1 --> P2[Predict: bel bar x t equals integral over x t minus 1 of motion model times bel x t minus 1]
    P2 --> P3[Read sensor z t]
    P3 --> P4[Update: bel x t equals eta times measurement model times bel bar x t]
    P4 --> P5[Output pose estimate x hat t equals argmax of bel x t]
    P5 --> L1
```

## 4.3 EKF Localization Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    In1[Previous Mean mu t minus 1] --> PredG[Compute Jacobian G t]
    In2[Previous Covariance Sigma t minus 1] --> PredG
    In3[Control u t] --> PredG
    PredG --> Out1[Predicted Mean bar mu t]
    PredG --> Out2[Predicted Covariance bar Sigma t]
    Out1 --> MeasH[Compute Jacobian H t at bar mu t]
    Out2 --> KG[Kalman Gain K t equals bar Sigma t H t transpose times inverse of H t bar Sigma t H t transpose plus Q t]
    MeasH --> KG
    In4[Sensor noise Q t] --> KG
    Out1 --> Innov[Innovation nu t equals z t minus h bar mu t]
    In5[Sensor reading z t] --> Innov
    Innov --> Corr[Corrected Mean mu t equals bar mu t plus K t times nu t]
    KG --> Corr
    KG --> Cov[Corrected Covariance Sigma t equals I minus K t H t times bar Sigma t]
    Corr --> Out3[Final Pose mu t]
    Cov --> Out4[Final Uncertainty Sigma t]
```

## 4.4 Monte Carlo Localization — Particle Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Init[Initialize M particles uniformly over map] --> Prop[Predict: Sample each particle using motion model p x t given x t minus 1 and u t]
    Prop --> Meas[Weight: Assign weight w t equals w t minus 1 times p z t given x t and m to each particle]
    Meas --> Neff[Compute N eff equals 1 over sum of squared weights]
    Neff --> Decision{N eff less than M over 2}
    Decision -->|Yes| Resamp[Resample: Draw M particles with replacement proportional to weights then reset weights to 1 over M]
    Decision -->|No| Skip[Skip resampling]
    Resamp --> Est[Pose estimate equals weighted mean of particles]
    Skip --> Est
    Est --> Prop
```

> [!NOTE]
> The four diagrams together capture the **complete localization information flow**: from raw sensors → probabilistic models → belief representation → algorithm-specific implementation → final pose output. In KTU 14-mark questions, drawing any one of these correctly with labels can earn up to **4 marks**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🎯 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**
**Define localization in mobile robotics. Distinguish between position tracking and global localization.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Localization is the process of estimating a robot's pose $x_t = (x_t, y_t, \theta_t)^T$ relative to a known map $m$, given a history of controls $u_{1:t}$ and observations $z_{1:t}$. It is formulated as estimating the posterior $bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}, m)$ (1 mark).

- **Position Tracking (1 mark):** The initial pose is approximately known. The problem is *local* — the belief remains unimodal (often Gaussian). Standard algorithm: Extended Kalman Filter (EKF).
- **Global Localization (1 mark):** No prior on initial pose. The belief may be multi-modal over the entire map. Standard algorithm: Monte Carlo Localization (MCL) using particle filters.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State and explain the Markov assumption used in robot localization.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Markov assumption** states that the future state of the robot depends only on the **immediate past state** and the **current control input**, not on the entire history (1 mark):

$$p(x_t \mid x_{0:t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$$

Similarly, the current observation depends only on the current state and the map (1 mark):

$$p(z_t \mid x_{0:t}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$$

This assumption makes the Bayes filter recursion tractable by avoiding the need to store the entire history (1 mark).

---

## 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Module 1 carries ~14 marks in the End Semester Exam. Questions typically offer two choices, each with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the **Bayes Filter recursion** for robot localization. Clearly state the prediction step and the measurement update step, mentioning all assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the working of the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) for position tracking**. Derive the expressions for Kalman gain, mean update, and covariance update. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Bayes Filter Derivation (7 marks):**

Start from the belief definition (1 mark):

$$bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}, m)$$

Apply Bayes' rule and chain rule of probability (1 mark):

$$bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) \, p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m)$$

Apply Markov assumption → measurement model and prediction term (1 mark):

$$p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) = p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$$

$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) = \overline{bel}(x_t)$$

Final update equation (1 mark):

$$bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \, \overline{bel}(x_t)$$

Prediction by Chapman–Kolmogorov (2 marks):

$$\overline{bel}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

Mention the role of $\eta$ as a normalizer ensuring the belief integrates to unity (1 mark).

**(b) EKF for Position Tracking (7 marks):**

System model: $x_t = g(x_{t-1}, u_t) + \varepsilon_t$, $z_t = h(x_t) + \delta_t$ (1 mark).

Compute Jacobians $G_t$ and $H_t$ at the current mean (1 mark).

Prediction equations (2 marks):

$$\bar{\mu}_t = g(\mu_{t-1}, u_t)$$

$$\bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \Sigma_{t-1} G_t^T + R_t$$

Kalman Gain and Update equations (2 marks):

$$K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}$$

$$\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t))$$

$$\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t$$

Neat labeled diagram showing the EKF loop (prediction → observation → Kalman gain → update) (1 mark).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the **Monte Carlo Localization (MCL)** algorithm in detail. Discuss the role of particle weights, the resampling step, and the effective sample size $N_{eff}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **EKF localization with MCL** under the headings: representation of belief, handling of non-linearities, computational complexity, robustness to global localization, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Monte Carlo Localization (7 marks):**

MCL represents the belief as a set of $M$ weighted particles $\mathcal{X}_t = \{x_t^{[i]}, w_t^{[i]}\}_{i=1}^{M}$ (1 mark).

Algorithm steps (4 marks):
1. **Initialization:** Spread particles uniformly over the map.
2. **Prediction (Sampling):** Propagate each particle using the motion model: $x_t^{[i]} \sim p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}^{[i]}, u_t)$.
3. **Weighting (Importance Update):** Compute $w_t^{[i]} = w_{t-1}^{[i]} \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}, m)$.
4. **Normalization:** $\tilde{w}_t^{[i]} = w_t^{[i]} / \sum_j w_t^{[j]}$.
5. **Resampling:** If $N_{eff} < M/2$, draw $M$ new particles with replacement proportional to $\tilde{w}_t^{[i]}$; reset weights to $1/M$.

Discussion of $N_{eff} = 1/\sum_i (\tilde{w}_t^{[i]})^2$ as a degeneracy indicator (1 mark); pose estimate as weighted mean (1 mark).

**(b) Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Parameter | EKF Localization | MCL |
|-----------|------------------|-----|
| Belief representation | Single Gaussian $(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$ | Set of weighted particles |
| Non-linearity handling | First-order Taylor linearization | Naturally non-parametric |
| Computational complexity | $O(d^2)$ per step, $d$ = state dim | $O(M \cdot C_{sense})$ where $M$ = particle count |
| Global localization | Fails (assumes unimodal) | Handles multi-modal beliefs |
| Sensor reset capability | Not inherent | Yes — can inject random particles |
| Map requirement | Continuous or feature-based map | Works with raw occupancy grids |
| Typical applications | Outdoor vehicle GPS/INS fusion | Indoor AMR, vacuum robots |

(1 mark each for 5 rows + 2 marks for concluding remark on hybrid approaches)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing belief with state** — Students often write $bel(x_t) = x_t$ which is wrong. Belief is a *probability distribution*, not a point estimate. Loss: 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the normalization constant $\eta$** — In Bayes update, omitting $\eta$ loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Not justifying the Markov assumption** — In derivations, examiners expect a sentence on why the assumption is valid (sensor memoryless, control Markov). Loss: 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Jacobians with wrong dimensions** — Common error: writing $H_t$ without specifying evaluation point. Always state "evaluated at $\bar{\mu}_t$".
> 5. **Mixing up prediction vs. update** — Prediction uses *motion model*, update uses *measurement model*. Reversing them loses 2 marks.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ Localization = estimating $bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}, m)$.
- ✅ Three problem types: **Position tracking**, **Global localization**, **Kidnapped robot problem**.
- ✅ Bayes Filter has **two phases**: Prediction (motion model) and Update (measurement model with normalizer $\eta$).
- ✅ **Markov assumption** is the cornerstone of tractability.
- ✅ **EKF** → unimodal Gaussian, position tracking, Jacobian-based linearization.
- ✅ **MCL / Particle Filter** → multi-modal, global localization, weighted samples + resampling.
- ✅ **Effective sample size** $N_{eff}$ triggers resampling to prevent degeneracy.
- ✅ Standard benchmarks: positional error $\leq \mathbf{0.05}$ m, angular error $\leq \mathbf{2°}$.
- ✅ Localization is essential for SLAM, path planning, and control — failure here cascades to total navigation failure.
- ✅ Hybrid EKF + MCL methods are increasingly used in modern robots.
- ✅ Know the **formula sheet** above cold — every KTU exam on this module draws 60–70% of marks from these equations.
- ✅ Always include **diagrams** in 14-mark answers — they fetch easy 2–4 marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cognition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT735 - ROBOT NAVIGATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Cognition # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of Robot Cognition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cognition in Robot Navigation** is the internal computational process by which an autonomous robot acquires, organizes, stores, and manipulates knowledge about itself and its environment in order to reason, plan, decide, and execute purposeful navigation actions in pursuit of one or more mission goals. In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PERUT735)**, cognition is treated as the *middle layer* of the classical **Sense → Plan → Act (SPA) paradigm**, sitting between low-level sensor processing (perception) and low-level motor actuation (control). It is the **"brain of the robot"** — analogous to the human nervous system's higher functions. Mathematically, cognition can be abstracted as a mapping: $$ f_{\text{cog}} : \mathcal{S} \times \mathcal{M} \longrightarrow \mathcal{A} $$ where: - $\mathcal{S}$ = the **sensory state space** (what the robot perceives now) - $\mathcal{M}$ = the **internal memory / world model** (what the robot believes) - $\mathcal{A}$ = the **action space** (what the robot decides to do) ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Cognition as a Tourist's Mental Map > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Tourist in a New City** > > Imagine you are dropped into Kochi with no GPS, no phone, and a paper map. To reach the Lulu Mall, you must: > > 1. **Look around** (sense landmarks, signboards, sun position). > 2. **Mentally localize yourself** on the paper map and **update** it when you walk (build & maintain a world model). > 3. **Reason** about which road leads towards the goal (planning). > 4. **Decide** whether to cross now or wait (decision-making under uncertainty). > 5. **Walk** (act) and **repeat** every few seconds. > > The entire "looking → localizing → reasoning → deciding → walking" loop *is cognition*. A robot does precisely the same, only its "paper map" is a probabilistic grid, landmarks are LiDAR returns, and "walking" is a velocity command $(v, \omega)$. ## 1.3 Why Cognition Matters in Navigation - **Autonomy**: Without cognition, the robot is a remote-controlled toy. - **Robustness**: Allows recovery from sensor noise and unexpected obstacles. - **Goal-directedness**: Enables long-horizon missions (e.g., "deliver a package to Room 304"). - **Adaptation**: Supports learning from experience and changing conditions. **Standard Performance Metrics for Cognitive Navigation Systems (KTU Board standard):** | Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit | | :--- | :---: | :--- | | Position estimation uncertainty | $\sigma_x$ | **meters (m)** | | Heading uncertainty | $\sigma_\theta$ | **radians (rad)** | | Planning latency | $T_p$ | **milliseconds (ms)** | | Belief update frequency | $f_b$ | **Hertz (Hz)** | | Decision entropy | $H(\pi)$ | **nats** | > [!TIP] > Examiners love to ask *"Differentiate perception from cognition."* Perception answers **"What is out there?"** while Cognition answers **"What should I do about it?"**. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Robot Cognition ### Pillar A — World Modelling (Knowledge Representation) The robot maintains an internal representation $\mathcal{M}$ of its environment. The dominant representations in KTU syllabus are: 1. **Metric Maps** — Continuous grids / occupancy grids storing $(x, y, \theta)$ coordinates of obstacles. 2. **Topological Maps** — Graph $G = (V, E)$ where vertices are places and edges are traversable connections. 3. **Semantic Maps** — Object-labelled maps (e.g., "door", "corridor", "staircase"). 4. **Hybrid Metric-Topological** — Used in modern SLAM and KTU advanced questions. ### Pillar B — Reasoning & Planning Given current belief $b_t$ and goal $g$, the planner produces a sequence or policy $\pi$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Three Cognitive Paradigms (Most Exam-Relevant):** > > | Paradigm | Key Idea | Strength | Weakness | > | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | > | **Deliberative** ("Think hard, then act") | Full world model + global planner | Optimal paths | Slow, brittle if model wrong | > | **Reactive** ("Don't think, just react") | Direct sensor→action rules | Very fast | No foresight, gets stuck | > | **Hybrid (3-layer)** | Deliberative + Reactive + Sequencing | Balanced | Complex architecture | The KTU textbook reference is **Brook's Subsumption Architecture (1986)** as a canonical reactive model and **Nilsson's Shakey (1969)** as the canonical deliberative model. ### Pillar C — Decision Making Under Uncertainty Real robots live in noisy, partially observable worlds. Cognition must handle: - **Sensor noise** ($P(z \mid x)$ — observation likelihood) - **Actuator noise** ($P(x_{t+1} \mid x_t, u_t)$ — motion model) - **Partial observability** (robot cannot see everything at once) ## 2.2 The Cognitive Control Loop — Step-by-Step A single cognitive cycle (running at frequency $f_b$ Hz) executes these five logic steps: 1. **Sense**: Read sensors $z_t$ (LiDAR, camera, IMU, GPS). 2. **Perceive**: Extract features $\to$ update short-term observation $o_t$. 3. **Localize & Map**: Combine $o_t$ with prior belief $b_{t-1}$ via Bayes' rule $\to b_t$. 4. **Reason / Plan**: Generate candidate actions using $b_t$ and goal $g$ $\to \pi^*$. 5. **Act**: Send velocity command $(v_t, \omega_t)$ to the low-level controller; close loop. This loop is the *minimum cognitive unit*. Running it at $f_b = 10$–$100$ Hz is standard in mobile robotics. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **The "Cheat Sheet" — Memorise these equations for ESE.** | # | Concept | Equation | Meaning | | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Bayes' belief update | $\displaystyle b_t(x) = \eta \cdot P(z_t \mid x)\, b_{t-1}(x)$ | Fuse measurement $z_t$ with prior belief | | 2 | Normalisation constant | $\displaystyle \eta = \frac{1}{\sum_{x} P(z_t \mid x)\, b_{t-1}(x)}$ | Ensures $\sum_x b_t(x) = 1$ | | 3 | Motion (prediction) step | $\displaystyle \bar{b}_t(x) = \sum_{x'} P(x \mid x', u_t)\, b_{t-1}(x')$ | Apply control $u_t$ to spread belief | | 4 | Markov assumption | $\displaystyle P(x_t \mid x_{0:t-1}) = P(x_t \mid x_{t-1})$ | Future depends only on present | | 5 | Planner cost function (A*) | $\displaystyle f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | Total estimated cost via node $n$ | | 6 | Admissible heuristic bound | $\displaystyle h(n) \le h^*(n)$ | $h$ never overestimates true cost | | 7 | Decision entropy | $\displaystyle H(\pi) = -\sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a) \log \pi(a)$ | Uncertainty in action choice | | 8 | Expected utility of action | $\displaystyle U(a) = \sum_{s} P(s \mid a)\, R(s)$ | Choose $a^* = \arg\max_a U(a)$ | | 9 | Particle-filter weight | $\displaystyle w_t^{[i]} = w_{t-1}^{[i]} \cdot P(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]})$ | Re-weight particle $i$ | | 10 | Effective sample size | $\displaystyle N_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1}{\sum_i (w_t^{[i]})^2}$ | Triggers resampling when small | > [!WARNING] > **Unit Pitfall:** Probabilities are dimensionless $[0,1]$. Entropy is in **nats** if $\log$ is natural, or **bits** if $\log_2$. Never mix them in a single equation. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Warehouse robots** (Amazon Kiva) use hybrid cognition: topological graph for warehouse + metric local planner around obstacles. - **Autonomous cars** (Waymo, Tesla) use 3-layer cognition: route planner (deliberative), behaviour layer (reactive), and feedback controller (actuator). - **Mars rovers** (Perseverance) use deliberative cognition heavily because communication delay is $\approx 20$ minutes — every command must be reasoned out. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Recursive Bayes Filter (The Heart of Cognition) This derivation answers: *"How does a robot mathematically combine yesterday's belief with today's sensor reading?"* ### Step 1 — State the desired quantity We want the posterior belief over robot state $x_t$ given all measurements $z_{1:t}$ and controls $u_{1:t}$: $$ \text{bel}(x_t) \;=\; P(x_t \mid z_{1:t},\, u_{1:t}) $$ ### Step 2 — Apply the definition of conditional probability By definition of conditional probability: $$ P(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) \;=\; \frac{P(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})\, P(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}{P(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})} $$ ### Step 3 — Apply the Markov assumption The measurement $z_t$ depends only on the *current* state $x_t$ (not on history): $$ P(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \;=\; P(z_t \mid x_t) $$ ### Step 4 — Use the law of total probability over previous state $x_{t-1}$ Marginalise out the previous state: $$ P(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \;=\; \int P(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)\, P(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1})\, dx_{t-1} $$ This is the **prediction step** — it propagates the previous belief through the motion model $P(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$. ### Step 5 — Substitute back Plug Step 3 and Step 4 into Step 2 and pull out the constant $\eta$: $$ \text{bel}(x_t) \;=\; \eta \cdot P(z_t \mid x_t) \int P(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)\, \text{bel}(x_{t-1})\, dx_{t-1} $$ This is the famous **Bayes filter recursion**. In discrete state spaces, the integral becomes a sum. $$ \boxed{\;\text{bel}(x_t) \;=\; \eta \cdot P(z_t \mid x_t)\, \sum_{x_{t-1}} P(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)\, \text{bel}(x_{t-1})\;} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** Belief at time $t$ = (likelihood of today's measurement given state) × (predicted belief after motion), normalised. This single equation is the mathematical engine of *every* cognitive robot — Kalman filters, particle filters, and occupancy grids are all special cases. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Discrete 1-D World) > [!IMPORTANT] > **This style of numerical problem appears frequently in KTU Part-A (3-mark) and as sub-part of Part-B (14-mark) questions.** **Problem:** A robot is in one of 3 cells $\{A, B, C\}$. Prior belief: $b_0 = (0.6, 0.3, 0.1)$. The motion model says "with probability 0.8 the robot moves right, 0.2 it stays" (treat as a self-loop on the rightmost cell). The robot then senses "I am at the rightmost cell" with $P(z=\text{right} \mid A) = 0.2$, $P(z=\text{right} \mid B) = 0.6$, $P(z=\text{right} \mid C) = 0.9$. Find the posterior belief. **Solution — Step 1 (Prediction $\bar{b}$):** Since the robot only moves right (or stays), from $A$ it can only go to $B$; from $B$ only to $C$; from $C$ stays at $C$. $$ \bar{b}(A) = 0, \quad \bar{b}(B) = 0.8 \times 0.6 = 0.48, \quad \bar{b}(C) = 0.2 \times 0.3 + 0.8 \times 0.1 + 0.8 \times 0.1 = 0.14 $$ (Using full transition: $P(C \mid B, u)=0.8$, $P(C \mid C, u)=0.8$, $P(B \mid A, u)=0.8$.) Re-compute cleanly: - $\bar{b}(A) = 0$ - $\bar{b}(B) = 0.8 \times 0.6 = 0.48$ - $\bar{b}(C) = 0.8 \times 0.3 + (0.2 \times 0.1) + (0.8 \times 0.1) = 0.24 + 0.02 + 0.08 = 0.34$ Wait — careful: a transition "right" means $A \to B$, $B \to C$, $C \to C$ with probability 0.8, else stay. So: $$ \bar{b}(C) = 0.8\cdot 0.3 + 0.2\cdot 0.1 + 0.8\cdot 0.1 = 0.24 + 0.02 + 0.08 = 0.34 $$ Sum check: $0 + 0.48 + 0.34 = 0.82$ ✓ (less than 1, will be normalised). **Solution — Step 2 (Measurement update):** Multiply each $\bar{b}(x)$ by $P(z=\text{right} \mid x)$: $$ \text{unnormalised } b(x) = \bar{b}(x) \cdot P(z \mid x) $$ - $b(A) = 0 \times 0.2 = 0$ - $b(B) = 0.48 \times 0.6 = 0.288$ - $b(C) = 0.34 \times 0.9 = 0.306$ Sum $= 0 + 0.288 + 0.306 = 0.594$. This is the normaliser $\eta^{-1}$. **Solution — Step 3 (Normalise):** $$ \eta = \frac{1}{0.594} \approx 1.6835 $$ $$ b_{\text{final}}(A) = 0, \quad b_{\text{final}}(B) \approx 0.485, \quad b_{\text{final}}(C) \approx 0.515 $$ **Interpretation:** The robot now strongly believes it is at $C$ (≈51.5%), as the measurement supports that. ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix > [!TIP] > **Likely KTU question:** *"Compare deliberative, reactive, and hybrid cognition in 4 points."* | Criterion | Deliberative | Reactive | Hybrid | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | | Uses world model | Yes | No | Yes | | Response speed | Slow | Very fast | Medium | | Optimality of path | High | Low | Medium-High | | Computational cost | High | Very low | Medium | | Failure recovery | Poor if model wrong | Excellent locally | Good | | Example system | Shakey, STRIPS | Subsumption, Braitenberg | 3T, AuRA, Saphira | | Cognitive metaphor | Chess grandmaster | Reflex arc | Experienced driver | --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Cognitive Cycle (Sense–Plan–Act Loop) The following Mermaid block renders the canonical KTU cognitive architecture flow: ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Robot Body and Environment] --> B[Sensors: LiDAR, Camera, IMU, GPS] B --> C[Perception Module: Feature Extraction] C --> D[Belief Update: Bayes Filter] E[World Model: Metric and Topological Map] --> D D --> F[Reasoning and Planning Engine] G[Mission Goal: Deliver to Room 304] --> F F --> H[Decision Policy pi star] H --> I[Action Selector: v and omega] I --> J[Low Level Motor Controller] J --> A F -.feedback.- E ``` > [!NOTE] > **How to read this diagram (KTU Board pattern):** > 1. The **outer loop** $A \to B \to C \to D \to F \to H \to I \to J \to A$ is the *runtime cognition loop*. > 2. The **feedback edge** from Planning Engine back to World Model indicates *map refinement* after planning. > 3. The *Goal input* $G$ is the only top-down input — it specifies *why* the robot navigates. ## 4.2 Three-Layer Hybrid Cognitive Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB L1[Layer 3: Deliberative Planner
Global path on topological map] L2[Layer 2: Behavioural Sequencer
Choose local behaviours: follow, avoid, dock] L3[Layer 1: Reactive Control
Direct sensor to actuator mappings] L1 -->|subgoals| L2 L2 -->|behaviour priorities| L3 L3 -->|velocity commands| M[Robot Motors] M -->|state feedback| L1 ``` ## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Components Inside Cognition ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CognitionCore["Cognition Core"] KM[Knowledge Base]:::kb RE[Reasoner]:::rs PL[Planner]:::pl DM[Decision Maker]:::dm LC[Learning Module]:::lm end IN[Sensor Input] --> KM KM --> RE GOAL[Mission Goal] --> PL RE --> PL PL --> DM LC -.updates.- KM DM --> OUT[Action Command] classDef kb fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e classDef rs fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a classDef pl fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#14532d classDef dm fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#831843 classDef lm fill:#e9d5ff,stroke:#581c87 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mermaid Safeguard Note:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric (`KM`, `RE`, `PL`, `DM`, `LC`, `M`, etc.). No reserved keywords are used. All labels are pure uppercase text without markdown formatting tags — this guarantees successful compilation in any Mermaid renderer. --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **`[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]`** **Q1.** Define *robot cognition*. How is it different from *perception* and *control*? **Model Answer (3 marks):** Cognition is the internal computational process by which a robot reasons about its environment and decides on navigation actions based on its world model and mission goal. It differs from perception (which only extracts features from raw sensor data) and from control (which only translates desired velocities into motor signals). Cognition is the "decision-making" layer that lies between them, typically implemented using Bayes filters, planners, and decision policies. *(3 marks)* --- **Q2.** What is the *subsumption architecture*? Who proposed it and what cognitive paradigm does it represent? **Model Answer (3 marks):** Subsumption architecture is a reactive robot control architecture proposed by **Rodney Brooks (1986)**. It organises behaviour into horizontal layers of competence, where higher layers subsume (suppress) lower ones when needed. It represents the **reactive cognitive paradigm** — cognition emerges from direct sensor-to-actuator mappings without an explicit world model. *(3 marks)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern) > **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]`** ### **Question A — 14 Marks** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the three-layer hybrid cognitive architecture for robot navigation. Discuss the role of each layer and identify which paradigm (deliberative / reactive) each layer belongs to. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A mobile robot operates in a grid world with three states $\{S_1, S_2, S_3\}$. At time $t-1$ its belief is $b_{t-1} = (0.2,\ 0.5,\ 0.3)$. It executes action $u$ with motion model: $P(S_2 \mid S_1, u) = 0.7$, $P(S_3 \mid S_2, u) = 0.8$, $P(S_3 \mid S_3, u) = 0.9$, all other transitions $= 0$. It then receives observation $z$ with $P(z \mid S_1) = 0.1$, $P(z \mid S_2) = 0.7$, $P(z \mid S_3) = 0.4$. Compute the posterior belief $b_t$ using the Bayes filter. **(7 marks)** ### **Model Solution — Question A** **(a) Three-Layer Hybrid Architecture (7 marks):** The three-layer hybrid architecture (popularised by systems like **3T** and **AuRA**) consists of: 1. **Deliberative Layer (Top):** Maintains a global world model and computes long-horizon plans (e.g., A*, D*, RRT). *Paradigm:* Deliberative. *Function:* Generate sub-goals for middle layer. 2. **Sequencing / Behaviour Layer (Middle):** Selects and prioritises which reactive behaviour should currently be active (e.g., "follow-corridor", "avoid-obstacle", "go-to-door"). *Paradigm:* Partially deliberative. 3. **Reactive Control Layer (Bottom):** Tight sensor–motor loops producing velocity commands at $50$–$100$ Hz. *Paradigm:* Reactive. **Block Diagram (text-rendered):** ``` +---------------------------+ | Deliberative Layer | <-- Mission planner (A*) | Global Map + A* / D* | +-------------+-------------+ | sub-goals +-------------v-------------+ | Behaviour Sequencer | <-- Chooses & weighs behaviours | Follow / Avoid / Dock | +-------------+-------------+ | priorities +-------------v-------------+ | Reactive Control Layer | <-- Direct sensor→motor | Subsumption / VFH | +---------------------------+ | v Motors (v, ω) ``` The advantage is **balancing optimality of deliberative planning with robustness and speed of reactive control**. *(7 marks)* **(b) Bayes Filter Computation (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Prediction (motion update).** Compute $\bar{b}_t(x) = \sum_{x'} P(x \mid x', u) \cdot b_{t-1}(x')$. Since $P(S_1 \mid \cdot) = 0$ for all $x'$, we have $\bar{b}_t(S_1) = 0$. $$ \bar{b}_t(S_2) = P(S_2 \mid S_1, u) \cdot b_{t-1}(S_1) = 0.7 \times 0.2 = 0.14 $$ $$ \bar{b}_t(S_3) = P(S_3 \mid S_2, u)\cdot b_{t-1}(S_2) + P(S_3 \mid S_3, u)\cdot b_{t-1}(S_3) $$ $$ \bar{b}_t(S_3) = (0.8 \times 0.5) + (0.9 \times 0.3) = 0.40 + 0.27 = 0.67 $$ Sanity: $\bar{b}_t(S_1) + \bar{b}_t(S_2) + \bar{b}_t(S_3) = 0 + 0.14 + 0.67 = 0.81$ ✓. **Step 2 — Measurement update.** Multiply by likelihood $P(z \mid x)$: $$ \tilde{b}(S_1) = 0 \times 0.1 = 0 $$ $$ \tilde{b}(S_2) = 0.14 \times 0.7 = 0.098 $$ $$ \tilde{b}(S_3) = 0.67 \times 0.4 = 0.268 $$ Sum $= 0 + 0.098 + 0.268 = 0.366 = \eta^{-1}$. **Step 3 — Normalise.** $\eta = 1 / 0.366 \approx 2.732$. $$ b_t(S_1) = 0, \quad b_t(S_2) \approx 0.268, \quad b_t(S_3) \approx 0.732 $$ **Final Answer:** $\boxed{b_t = (0,\ 0.268,\ 0.732)}$ **Interpretation:** The robot now strongly believes it is in $S_3$, consistent with both the prior leaning towards $S_3$ and the observation model. *(7 marks)* --- ### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** **(a)** Compare **deliberative**, **reactive**, and **hybrid** cognitive paradigms in robot navigation. Use a comparison table and identify one canonical robot system for each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the recursive Bayes filter equation used by a robot to update its belief state. Clearly state the Markov assumption used. **(7 marks)** ### **Model Solution — Question B (Brief Outline)** **(a)** Use the **Section 3.3 Comparison Matrix** verbatim. Mention: Deliberative → **Shakey (Nilsson, 1969)**; Reactive → **Subsumption (Brooks, 1986)**; Hybrid → **3T / AuRA**. Mention trade-offs of optimality vs speed. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Use the **Section 3.1 derivation** step-by-step (Steps 1–5). Highlight the Markov assumption: *"Future is conditionally independent of the past given the present state."* Conclude with the boxed recursion formula. *(7 marks)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Forgetting the normaliser $\eta$** — Full marks in (b) only if you *show* $\eta$ computation. > 2. **Confusing $P(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$ (motion model) with $P(z_t \mid x_t)$ (observation model).** Examiners explicitly test this distinction. > 3. **Writing Bayes filter without the Markov assumption** — you lose 2 marks even if the algebra is correct. > 4. **In Part A, writing only definitions without differentiating** — the question asks *"how is cognition different from perception?"*; you must contrast. > 5. **In comparison questions, using vague adjectives** — say *"Shakey is slow because Dijkstra is $O(n^2)$"* rather than just *"Shakey is slow"*. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Cognition** - **Definition:** Cognition = internal reasoning + planning + decision layer (not sensing, not actuation). - **SPA Paradigm:** Sense → Plan → Act; cognition occupies the **Plan** stage. - **Three Paradigms:** Deliberative (Shakey), Reactive (Subsumption), Hybrid (3T, AuRA). - **Bayes Filter (Core Equation):** $\text{bel}(x_t) = \eta \cdot P(z_t \mid x_t) \sum P(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)\, \text{bel}(x_{t-1})$ — *must memorise exactly*. - **Markov Assumption:** $P(x_t \mid x_{0:t-1}) = P(x_t \mid x_{t-1})$. - **Three Belief Update Steps:** (i) Predict with motion model → (ii) Update with measurement → (iii) Normalise so probabilities sum to 1. - **Normaliser:** $\eta = 1 / \sum P(z_t \mid x)\, \bar{b}_t(x)$. - **World Models:** Metric (grid), Topological (graph), Semantic (labelled), Hybrid. - **Planning Heuristic admissibility:** $h(n) \le h^*(n)$ — never overestimates. - **Subsumption Architecture:** Brooks, 1986; layered reactive with no world model. - **Decision entropy formula:** $H(\pi) = -\sum \pi(a) \log \pi(a)$ — *units: nats or bits, not both*. - **Effective sample size:** $N_{\text{eff}} = 1 / \sum_i (w_t^{[i]})^2$ — small $N_{\text{eff}}$ triggers resampling. - **Cognitive Cycle Frequency:** typical $f_b = 10$–$100$ Hz. - **Real-world examples:** Mars rover (deliberative), Braitenberg vehicle (reactive), Waymo (hybrid 3-layer). - **Standard Pitfall:** Cognition $\neq$ Perception. Cognition $\neq$ Control. Cognition is *between* them. ---

topics.title: Motion Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT735 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Motion Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Motion Control** in the context of robotic navigation is the sub-discipline of robotics concerned with the precise regulation of a robot's mechanical movement — specifically the position, velocity, and acceleration of its joints, links, or end-effector — in order to follow a desired geometric path within a specified time profile. It is the bridge between the high-level *path planner* (which decides **where** the robot should go) and the low-level *actuator drivers* (which physically move the hardware).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) syllabus for PERUT735, Motion Control is treated as a foundational module because every subsequent concept — obstacle avoidance, SLAM, and reactive navigation — ultimately resolves into issuing low-level motion commands.

A **trajectory** is therefore defined as a *time-parameterized path*: it specifies not only the geometry the robot must trace, but also the exact speed and acceleration at every instant along that geometry.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Daily Commute

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Driving a Car from Home to Office**
>
> Think of Motion Control the same way you drive a car every day:
>
> - The **road map (path)** tells you *which turns to take* — this is the **geometric path**.
> - The **GPS ETA (trajectory)** tells you *when* you must be at each turn — this is the **time parameterization**.
> - Your **eyes and ears (sensors/feedback)** measure your current speed and lane position.
> - Your **brain (controller)** compares the GPS suggestion with your actual state and presses the accelerator or brake (control signal).
> - The **accelerator and steering wheel (actuators)** physically execute the command.
>
> A self-driving car is essentially a robot whose entire software stack reduces, at the bottom layer, to a *motion controller* solving this same loop every few milliseconds.

### 1.3 Core Terminology (KTU Syllabus Highlights)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Know Definitions for KTU University Exam**
>
> | Term | Definition | KTU Buzzword |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Path** | Pure geometric locus of points in space (no time). | "Shape only" |
> | **Trajectory** | Path plus a time law $s(t)$, $v(t)$, $a(t)$. | "Path + Time" |
> | **Waypoint** | An intermediate commanded pose (position + orientation) the robot must pass through. | "Via-point" |
> | **Joint Space** | Configuration described by joint variables $q_1, q_2, \dots, q_n$. | "q-space" |
> | **Cartesian Space** | Configuration described by end-effector pose $(x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi)$. | "Task space" |
> | **Open-Loop Control** | Control action issued without using measured output. | "Blind control" |
> | **Closed-Loop Control** | Control action computed using feedback from sensors. | "Feedback control" |

The standard **SI units** you must memorize for this module are:
- Position: meters ($m$) or radians ($rad$)
- Velocity: $m/s$ or $rad/s$
- Acceleration: $m/s^{2}$ or $rad/s^{2}$
- Torque (rotary actuator output): Newton-meters ($N \cdot m$)
- Force (linear actuator output): Newtons ($N$)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Motion Control Problem — Decomposed

A motion control problem can always be decomposed into **three sequential sub-problems**:

1. **Reference Generation** — Given a goal pose, generate a smooth time-varying reference signal $r(t)$ for every actuator (this is **trajectory planning**).
2. **Error Computation** — Measure the actual state $y(t)$ using sensors (encoders, IMUs, vision) and compute the tracking error $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$.
3. **Control Law** — Apply a mathematical function $u(t) = f(e(t), \int e, \dot{e})$ to convert the error into a voltage/current/pulse-width command sent to the actuator.

### 2.2 Classification of Motion Control Strategies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Open-Loop vs Closed-Loop — KTU Favourite Question**

| Strategy | Sensor Required? | Accuracy | Cost | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Open-Loop** | No | Low–Medium (depends on model) | Low | Stepper motors, printers, simple pick-and-place |
| **Closed-Loop** | Yes (encoder, resolver) | High | Higher | CNC machines, robotic arms, AGVs, drones |

### 2.3 The PID Controller — The Workhorse of Motion Control

The **Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID) controller** is the universal building block of robotic motion control. Its continuous-time control law is:

$$
u(t) \;=\; K_{p}\, e(t) \;+\; K_{i} \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\,d\tau \;+\; K_{d}\, \frac{de(t)}{dt}
$$

Where the three gains have very intuitive physical meanings:

- **$K_{p}$ (Proportional Gain)** — Produces a control effort *proportional* to the current error. Larger $K_p$ means faster response, but too large causes oscillation.
- **$K_{i}$ (Integral Gain)** — Eliminates steady-state error by accumulating past errors. Essential when a constant disturbance (like gravity on a robot arm) is present.
- **$K_{d}$ (Derivative Gain)** — Damps the response by reacting to the *rate of change* of error. Acts like a virtual shock-absorber.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use `\vert` instead of `|` in table cells to avoid markdown breakage.**

| # | Equation | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ | Tracking error (reference minus measured) | $m$ or $rad$ |
| 2 | $u(t) = K_{p} e + K_{i} \int e\,dt + K_{d} \dot{e}$ | PID control law | $V$ or duty cycle |
| 3 | $G_{c}(s) = K_{p} + \frac{K_{i}}{s} + K_{d} s$ | PID transfer function (Laplace) | dimensionless ratio |
| 4 | $s(t) = s_{0} + v_{0} t + \tfrac{1}{2} a t^{2}$ | Linear motion displacement | $m$ |
| 5 | $\omega(t) = \omega_{0} + \alpha t$ | Angular velocity evolution | $rad/s$ |
| 6 | $\theta(t) = \theta_{0} + \omega_{0} t + \tfrac{1}{2} \alpha t^{2}$ | Angular displacement | $rad$ |
| 7 | $q(t) = a_{0} + a_{1} t + a_{2} t^{2} + a_{3} t^{3}$ | Cubic polynomial joint trajectory | $rad$ |
| 8 | $v_{max} = \sqrt{a_{max} \cdot d}$ | Peak velocity for trapezoidal profile | $m/s$ |
| 9 | $\tau = J(q)\, \ddot{q} + b(q, \dot{q}) + g(q)$ | Manipulator dynamics (Lagrangian form) | $N \cdot m$ |
| 10 | $x = f(q)$, $\dot{x} = J(q) \dot{q}$ | Forward kinematics & Jacobian relation | $m$, $m/s$ |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Motion control is *not* an abstract academic exercise — it underpins every automated system you interact with daily:

- **Industrial Robotics:** Welding arms in automotive plants use PID + feed-forward control to maintain arc stability within $\pm 0.1\,mm$.
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** The steering, throttle, and braking subsystems each run independent PID loops at $100$–$1000\,Hz$.
- **Aerospace:** Quadrotor drones use cascaded PID (outer position loop + inner attitude loop) running on flight controllers like Pixhawk.
- **Medical Robotics:** Surgical robots such as the Da Vinci system use **impedance control** (a generalization of PID) to allow compliant interaction with tissue.
- **CNC Machining:** High-precision milling machines use *feed-forward + PID* hybrid control to achieve micron-level accuracy.

### 2.6 Trajectory Profiles (Trapezoidal & S-Curve)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU exams, you must draw and explain these two profiles**

A **trapezoidal velocity profile** has three phases: acceleration (constant $a$), cruise (constant $v_{max}$), deceleration (constant $-a$). It is computationally cheap but has **discontinuous acceleration** (jerky).

An **S-curve profile** (also called *jerk-limited* profile) inserts a ramp-up and ramp-down of acceleration itself, producing smooth, continuous $a(t)$. It is preferred for high-speed or human-carrying robots (elevators, autonomous cars) because it minimizes mechanical wear and passenger discomfort.

### 2.7 Joint Space vs Cartesian Space Motion

You can command the robot in **two equivalent ways**:

- **Joint-space motion:** Specify $q(t)$ for each joint. Computationally simple, but the end-effector path is not a straight line.
- **Cartesian-space motion:** Specify the end-effector pose $x(t)$. The path is a perfect straight line, but you must invert the kinematics every cycle using $q = f^{-1}(x)$ — computationally heavier and may hit singularities.
