## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Cubic Polynomial Trajectory

**Problem Setup:** A single robot joint must move from initial angle $q_{0}$ at time $t_{0}=0$ to final angle $q_{f}$ at time $t_{f}=T$. We want a *smooth* trajectory. We choose a cubic polynomial because it has exactly four free coefficients, perfectly matching the four boundary conditions (initial position, initial velocity, final position, final velocity).

$$
q(t) \;=\; a_{0} \;+\; a_{1}\, t \;+\; a_{2}\, t^{2} \;+\; a_{3}\, t^{3}
$$

**Step 1 — Apply the initial position condition** $q(0) = q_{0}$:

$$
q(0) = a_{0} + a_{1}(0) + a_{2}(0)^{2} + a_{3}(0)^{3} = a_{0}
$$

$$
\therefore \; a_{0} = q_{0}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute velocity by differentiation:**

$$
\dot{q}(t) = a_{1} + 2 a_{2} t + 3 a_{3} t^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the initial velocity condition** $\dot{q}(0) = v_{0}$ (usually zero for a "start from rest" motion):

$$
\dot{q}(0) = a_{1} + 2 a_{2}(0) + 3 a_{3}(0)^{2} = a_{1}
$$

$$
\therefore \; a_{1} = v_{0}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the final position condition** $q(T) = q_{f}$:

$$
q(T) = a_{0} + a_{1} T + a_{2} T^{2} + a_{3} T^{3}
$$

$$
q_{f} = q_{0} + v_{0} T + a_{2} T^{2} + a_{3} T^{3}
$$

$$
\therefore \; a_{2} T^{2} + a_{3} T^{3} = q_{f} - q_{0} - v_{0} T \quad \text{...(Equation A)}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the final velocity condition** $\dot{q}(T) = v_{f}$ (usually zero for "come to rest"):

$$
\dot{q}(T) = a_{1} + 2 a_{2} T + 3 a_{3} T^{2}
$$

$$
v_{f} = v_{0} + 2 a_{2} T + 3 a_{3} T^{2}
$$

$$
\therefore \; 2 a_{2} T + 3 a_{3} T^{2} = v_{f} - v_{0} \quad \text{...(Equation B)}
$$

**Step 6 — Solve Equation A and Equation B simultaneously** (assuming $v_{0}=v_{f}=0$ for simplicity, the most common KTU case):

$$
a_{2} T^{2} + a_{3} T^{3} = q_{f} - q_{0}
$$

$$
2 a_{2} T + 3 a_{3} T^{2} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 2 a_{2} = -3 a_{3} T \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; a_{2} = -\tfrac{3}{2} a_{3} T
$$

**Step 7 — Substitute back into Equation A:**

$$
\left(-\tfrac{3}{2} a_{3} T\right) T^{2} + a_{3} T^{3} = q_{f} - q_{0}
$$

$$
-\tfrac{3}{2} a_{3} T^{3} + a_{3} T^{3} = q_{f} - q_{0}
$$

$$
-\tfrac{1}{2} a_{3} T^{3} = q_{f} - q_{0}
$$

$$
\boxed{\; a_{3} = \dfrac{2(q_{0} - q_{f})}{T^{3}} \;}
$$

$$
a_{2} = -\tfrac{3}{2} \cdot \dfrac{2(q_{0} - q_{f})}{T^{3}} \cdot T = \dfrac{3(q_{f} - q_{0})}{T^{2}}
$$

**Step 8 — Final compact form of the cubic trajectory:**

$$
\boxed{\; q(t) \;=\; q_{0} \;+\; \dfrac{3(q_{f} - q_{0})}{T^{2}}\, t^{2} \;-\; \dfrac{2(q_{f} - q_{0})}{T^{3}}\, t^{3} \;}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Given:** A robot joint starts at $q_{0} = 10^{\circ}$ and must reach $q_{f} = 70^{\circ}$ in $T = 4$ seconds, starting and ending at rest.

**Compute coefficients:**

$$
a_{3} = \dfrac{2(10 - 70)}{4^{3}} = \dfrac{2(-60)}{64} = -\dfrac{120}{64} = -1.875 \; \text{deg/s}^{3}
$$

$$
a_{2} = \dfrac{3(70 - 10)}{4^{2}} = \dfrac{3 \cdot 60}{16} = \dfrac{180}{16} = 11.25 \; \text{deg/s}^{2}
$$

**Trajectory equation:**

$$
q(t) = 10 + 11.25\, t^{2} - 1.875\, t^{3}
$$

**Verification at endpoints:**

$$
q(0) = 10 + 0 - 0 = 10^{\circ} \;\;\checkmark
$$

$$
q(4) = 10 + 11.25(16) - 1.875(64) = 10 + 180 - 120 = 70^{\circ} \;\;\checkmark
$$

### 3.3 Derivation — Trapezoidal Velocity Profile Peak Velocity

**Given:** A linear segment of distance $d$ must be covered in time $T$ with acceleration $a_{max}$ and deceleration $-a_{max}$. Find the peak velocity $v_{max}$.

**Case 1 — Trapezoidal profile exists** (long distance, short time):
The acceleration phase duration is $t_{a} = \dfrac{v_{max}}{a_{max}}$. By symmetry, deceleration also lasts $t_{a}$. The cruise phase lasts $t_{c} = T - 2 t_{a}$.

Total distance equals area under velocity-time graph (a trapezoid):

$$
d = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot T \cdot v_{max} \;-\; \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot t_{a} \cdot v_{max}
$$

$$
d = \tfrac{1}{2} v_{max} (T + t_{c}) = \tfrac{1}{2} v_{max} (T + T - 2 t_{a}) = v_{max} (T - t_{a})
$$

Substituting $t_{a} = v_{max}/a_{max}$:

$$
d = v_{max}\left(T - \dfrac{v_{max}}{a_{max}}\right)
$$

Solving the quadratic $v_{max}^{2} - a_{max} T\, v_{max} + a_{max}\, d = 0$ yields:

$$
\boxed{\; v_{max} = \dfrac{a_{max} T}{2} \left(1 - \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{4 d}{a_{max} T^{2}}}\right) \;}
$$

**Case 2 — Triangular profile** (very short distance / long time):
The robot never reaches the commanded $v_{max}$. Then:

$$
\boxed{\; v_{max} = \sqrt{a_{max} \cdot d} \;}
$$

This is the formula you should memorize for short, quick motions.

### 3.4 PID Tuning — Ziegler-Nichols Method (Summary)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU examiners love asking "How would you tune a PID in practice?"**

The classical **Ziegler-Nichols** open-loop tuning gives the following table:

| Controller Type | $K_{p}$ | $K_{i}$ | $K_{d}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P-only** | $0.5\, K_{u}$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| **PI** | $0.45\, K_{u}$ | $0.54\, K_{u} / T_{u}$ | $0$ |
| **PID (full)** | $0.6\, K_{u}$ | $1.2\, K_{u} / T_{u}$ | $0.075\, K_{u} T_{u}$ |

Where $K_{u}$ is the **ultimate gain** (the proportional gain at which the system oscillates continuously) and $T_{u}$ is the **oscillation period** under that gain.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Motion Control Loop

The diagram below shows the canonical feedback motion control loop. Note that every node uses an alphanumeric identifier prefix to comply with Mermaid rendering safeguards.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ref[Reference r of t] --> sumJunction{Summing Junction}
    sensor[Sensor Measurement y of t] --> sumJunction
    sumJunction --> errGen[Tracking Error e of t]
    errGen --> pidBlock[PID Controller Kp Ki Kd]
    pidBlock --> ctrlSig[Control Signal u of t]
    ctrlSig --> actuator[Actuator DC Motor Servo]
    actuator --> plant[Robot Plant Joint Link]
    plant --> outPhys[Physical Motion x of t]
    outPhys --> sensor
    outPhys --> perfOut[Navigation Output]

    classDef logic fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000
    classDef hardware fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    class ref,sumJunction,errGen,pidBlock,ctrlSig class:logic
    class sensor,actuator,plant,outPhys,perfOut class:hardware
```

### 4.2 Hierarchical Motion Control Architecture

Modern robotic systems use a **layered (hierarchical) architecture** where higher layers issue references to lower layers at progressively higher rates.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L5[Level 5 Mission Planner] --> L4[Level 4 Path Planner Geometric]
    L4 --> L3[Level 3 Trajectory Planner Time Parameterized]
    L3 --> L2[Level 2 Motion Controller PID or MPC]
    L2 --> L1[Level 1 Hardware Drivers PWM CAN EtherCAT]
    L1 --> L0[Level 0 Actuators Motors Gearboxes]

    L5 --- note1[Rate: 1 Hz]
    L4 --- note2[Rate: 10 Hz]
    L3 --- note3[Rate: 100 Hz]
    L2 --- note4[Rate: 1 kHz]
    L1 --- note5[Rate: 10 kHz]
```

### 4.3 PID Internal Structure (Compensator Block Detail)

This diagram zooms into the PID block to show how the three terms — Proportional, Integral, Derivative — are computed in parallel and summed.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    eInput[Error e of t] --> P[Proportional Path Kp]
    eInput --> I[Int Path Ki over s]
    eInput --> D[Derivative Path Kd times s]
    P --> sumI[Summer]
    I --> sumI
    D --> sumI
    sumI --> uOut[Control Output u of t]
```

### 4.4 Trajectory Profile Schematic (Trapezoidal Velocity)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Phase1[Phase 1 Acceleration]
        A0[Velocity 0] -->|constant slope amax| A1[Velocity vmax]
    end
    subgraph Phase2[Phase 2 Cruise]
        A1 -->|constant vmax| A2[Velocity vmax]
    end
    subgraph Phase3[Phase 3 Deceleration]
        A2 -->|constant slope minus amax| A3[Velocity 0]
    end
    A3 --> finish[Reached Goal Pose]

    Phase1 --- tAxis1[Time ta]
    Phase2 --- tAxis2[Time tc]
    Phase3 --- tAxis3[Time ta]
```

### 4.5 Cascade Control Architecture (Used in Drones and CNC)

Cascade control uses two nested loops: an outer "slow" loop and an inner "fast" loop. This is essential for high-performance robotic motion control because the inner loop rejects disturbances before they propagate.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    posRef[Position Reference] --> posErr{Sum}
    posSensor[Position Sensor] --> posErr
    posErr --> posCtrl[Outer Position PID]
    posCtrl --> velRef[Velocity Reference]
    velRef --> velErr{Sum}
    velSensor[Velocity Sensor] --> velErr
    velErr --> velCtrl[Inner Velocity PI]
    velCtrl --> motorCmd[Motor Command]
    motorCmd --> motorPlant[DC Motor]
    motorPlant --> motorOut[Motion]
    motorOut --> posSensor
    motorOut --> velSensor
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Differentiate between **open-loop** and **closed-loop** motion control systems. Give one robotic example of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Open-Loop | Closed-Loop |
|---|---|---|
| **Feedback** | No sensor measures the actual output | Sensor (encoder, IMU) feeds actual output back |
| **Accuracy** | Depends on accuracy of the model/calibration | Self-correcting; high accuracy |
| **Cost & Complexity** | Low cost, simple hardware | Higher cost, more wiring, more code |
| **Disturbance Rejection** | Poor — disturbances accumulate | Excellent — controller compensates continuously |

**Examples:**
- *Open-loop:* A stepper-motor-driven 3D printer extruder — the controller assumes the stepper rotated exactly the commanded number of steps.
- *Closed-loop:* A DC servo motor on a robotic arm joint with an optical encoder providing position feedback to a PID controller.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
What is the difference between a **path** and a **trajectory** in robot motion control? Why is the distinction important?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **path** is a purely geometric curve in space — a sequence of points the end-effector must pass through, with no timing information. A **trajectory** is a path together with a time law — it specifies position, velocity, and acceleration *as functions of time*.

**Why the distinction matters:**
1. **Smoothness & Dynamics:** Two trajectories following the same path can demand completely different actuator torques. A slow trajectory may be feasible for a weak motor; a fast one may not.
2. **Collision Avoidance:** In dynamic environments, the robot must arrive at each waypoint at a specific time to avoid moving obstacles — only a *trajectory* captures this.
3. **Jerk Minimization:** Smooth trajectories (jerk-limited) protect mechanical components and are mandatory in human-collaborative robots (cobots).

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **closed-loop motion control system** for a single robotic joint. Identify each block and the nature of the signals flowing between them. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robot joint must move from $q_{0} = 20^{\circ}$ to $q_{f} = 80^{\circ}$ in $T = 5$ seconds, starting and ending at rest. Derive the **cubic polynomial trajectory** $q(t)$ for this joint, and compute $q(2.5\,s)$, $\dot{q}(2.5\,s)$, and $\ddot{q}(2.5\,s)$. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Block Diagram & Architecture (7 marks):**

The closed-loop motion control system consists of six functional blocks connected in a feedback loop:

1. **Reference Generator** — Produces the desired trajectory $r(t)$ (the cubic polynomial computed offline or online).
2. **Comparator (Summing Junction)** — Computes $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$, the instantaneous tracking error.
3. **Controller (PID block)** — Applies the control law $u(t) = K_{p} e + K_{i}\int e + K_{d}\dot{e}$ to convert the error into a control signal.
4. **Amplifier / Driver** — Boosts the low-power control signal to a level sufficient to drive the actuator (e.g., H-bridge for DC motors).
5. **Actuator + Plant (DC motor + gearbox + link)** — Converts electrical energy into mechanical motion of the joint.
6. **Sensor (Optical Encoder / Resolver)** — Measures the actual joint angle and feeds it back to the comparator.

**Signal nature:**
- $r(t)$, $y(t)$, $e(t)$ — typically analog voltages or digital counts.
- $u(t)$ — PWM duty cycle or analog voltage.
- Motor input — current ($A$) or voltage ($V$).
- Output — angular position $q$ in $rad$ or encoder counts.

**Key marking points:** Mention that *negative feedback* is essential; explain why disturbance rejection requires closed loop; show that without feedback the system is "blind."

**(b) Cubic Trajectory Derivation (7 marks):**

Using the formula derived in Section 3.1 with $v_{0} = v_{f} = 0$, $q_{0} = 20^{\circ}$, $q_{f} = 80^{\circ}$, $T = 5\,s$:

$$
a_{3} = \dfrac{2(q_{0} - q_{f})}{T^{3}} = \dfrac{2(20 - 80)}{125} = \dfrac{-120}{125} = -0.96 \; \text{deg/s}^{3}
$$

$$
a_{2} = \dfrac{3(q_{f} - q_{0})}{T^{2}} = \dfrac{3 \cdot 60}{25} = 7.2 \; \text{deg/s}^{2}
$$

So the trajectory equation is:

$$
\boxed{\; q(t) = 20 + 7.2\, t^{2} - 0.96\, t^{3} \quad \text{(in degrees)} \;}
$$

**Compute at $t = 2.5\,s$:**

$$
q(2.5) = 20 + 7.2(6.25) - 0.96(15.625)
$$

$$
q(2.5) = 20 + 45 - 15 = 50^{\circ}
$$

**Velocity (first derivative):**

$$
\dot{q}(t) = 14.4\, t - 2.88\, t^{2}
$$

$$
\dot{q}(2.5) = 14.4(2.5) - 2.88(6.25) = 36 - 18 = 18 \; \text{deg/s}
$$

**Acceleration (second derivative):**

$$
\ddot{q}(t) = 14.4 - 5.76\, t
$$

$$
\ddot{q}(2.5) = 14.4 - 5.76(2.5) = 14.4 - 14.4 = 0 \; \text{deg/s}^{2}
$$

**Physical interpretation:** At the exact midpoint of a cubic trajectory (with zero start/end velocity), the acceleration is **zero** and the velocity is at its **peak** ($18\,deg/s$). This is a hallmark of symmetric cubic motion.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **PID control algorithm** for motion control. With the help of a diagram, describe the contribution of each of the three terms (P, I, D) to the closed-loop response. Also write the transfer function of a PID controller. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a trapezoidal velocity profile with $d = 2\,m$, $a_{max} = 1\,m/s^{2}$, and total time $T = 3\,s$, determine whether the profile is **trapezoidal or triangular**, and compute the peak velocity $v_{max}$. Also calculate the time spent in each phase. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) PID Algorithm (7 marks):**

The PID control law in the time domain is:

$$
u(t) \;=\; K_{p}\, e(t) \;+\; K_{i} \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\,d\tau \;+\; K_{d}\, \frac{de(t)}{dt}
$$

**Laplace-domain transfer function** (with derivative filter ignored for simplicity):

$$
G_{c}(s) \;=\; \frac{U(s)}{E(s)} \;=\; K_{p} + \frac{K_{i}}{s} + K_{d}\, s
$$

**Contribution of each term:**

- **P-Term (Proportional, $K_{p} e$):** Provides control effort proportional to instantaneous error. Larger $K_{p}$ → faster rise time but more overshoot and oscillation. Cannot eliminate steady-state error alone.
- **I-Term (Integral, $K_{i} \int e\, dt$):** Accumulates past error. Eliminates steady-state error (essential when a constant load like gravity is present). Too large → sluggish and oscillatory.
- **D-Term (Derivative, $K_{d} \dot{e}$):** Predicts future error by looking at its rate of change. Acts as a damper, reduces overshoot, improves stability. Cannot act on a static error alone.

**Practical filter:** A real derivative term includes a low-pass filter $G_{d}(s) = \dfrac{K_{d}\, s}{1 + \tau_{f}\, s}$ to avoid amplifying high-frequency sensor noise.

**(b) Trapezoidal vs Triangular Profile (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Test whether a trapezoidal profile is feasible.**

If trapezoidal, the maximum achievable distance in time $T$ with acceleration $a_{max}$ is reached when the cruise phase has zero duration (triangular limit). The distance in a triangular profile is:

$$
d_{triangular} = a_{max} \cdot \left(\frac{T}{2}\right)^{2} = 1 \cdot (1.5)^{2} = 2.25 \; m
$$

Since the required distance $d = 2\,m < 2.25\,m$, **a trapezoidal profile is feasible** (the robot will reach $v_{max}$ before decelerating).

**Step 2 — Solve for $v_{max}$.**

The distance-time relation for a symmetric trapezoidal profile (accel, cruise, decel) is:

$$
d = v_{max}\, T - \dfrac{v_{max}^{2}}{a_{max}}
$$

Substituting:

$$
2 = 3 v_{max} - \dfrac{v_{max}^{2}}{1} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; v_{max}^{2} - 3 v_{max} + 2 = 0
$$

$$
(v_{max} - 1)(v_{max} - 2) = 0
$$

$$
v_{max} = 2 \; m/s \quad \text{or} \quad v_{max} = 1 \; m/s
$$

The valid solution is $v_{max} = 2\; m/s$ (the larger peak — corresponds to a proper trapezoidal with a non-zero cruise phase; $v_{max}=1$ would be a fully triangular profile using the same $T$, which is a degenerate case).

**Verification:** With $v_{max}=2$, acceleration time $t_a = v_{max}/a_{max} = 2\,s$, deceleration also $2\,s$, so cruise time $t_c = 3 - 4 = -1\,s$ — **infeasible**!

Re-evaluate: the cruise phase must be non-negative. Since the required distance is *just under* the triangular maximum, the profile is actually **near-triangular**. The correct physical interpretation:

The peak velocity is reached at time $t_a = v_{max}/a_{max}$ and the robot immediately begins decelerating. The total distance for a pure triangular profile is $d = v_{max}^{2}/a_{max}$, giving:

$$
\boxed{\; v_{max} = \sqrt{a_{max} \cdot d} = \sqrt{1 \cdot 2} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414 \; m/s \;}
$$

This is the **peak velocity of the triangular profile**. Acceleration phase $t_a = v_{max}/a_{max} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414\,s$. Total motion time $T_{triangular} = 2 t_a = 2\sqrt{2} \approx 2.83\,s$, which is **less than the allowed $3\,s$**, confirming the robot idles briefly at the goal — a triangular profile is correct.

| Phase | Duration | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Acceleration | $1.414\,s$ | Velocity ramps from $0$ to $\sqrt{2}\;m/s$ |
| Deceleration | $1.414\,s$ | Velocity ramps from $\sqrt{2}\;m/s$ to $0$ |
| Idle | $0.172\,s$ | Robot stationary at goal (excess time) |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing path with trajectory** (3-mark killer): Always mention that trajectory includes time; path does not.
> 2. **Sign errors in cubic polynomial derivation:** A very common mistake is writing $a_3 = 2(q_f - q_0)/T^3$ instead of $2(q_0 - q_f)/T^3$. Verify by checking that $q(T) = q_f$.
> 3. **Forgetting initial/final velocity boundary conditions:** The cubic polynomial assumes **four** boundary conditions; do not leave $v_0, v_f$ unspecified.
> 4. **Units inconsistency:** Mixing degrees and radians in the same equation. Pick one and stay consistent. If converting, state the conversion factor.
> 5. **Saying "PID gives perfect control":** It does not — there are stability limits (gain margin, phase margin). Mention that excessively high $K_p$ leads to instability.
> 6. **Skipping the diagram in PID question:** A neat labelled block diagram of the PID compensator is worth at least 2 marks by itself.
> 7. **Confusing trapezoidal and triangular profiles:** Always check whether the calculated $t_c \geq 0$. If not, the profile degenerates to triangular — do not blindly apply the trapezoidal formula.

---

### Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Important Things to Remember (Print-and-Revise)**

- ✅ Motion Control = regulation of **position, velocity, and acceleration** along a desired path.
- ✅ **Path** = geometry only; **Trajectory** = path + time law.
- ✅ **Open-loop** = no feedback (cheap, inaccurate); **Closed-loop** = with feedback (accurate, robust).
- ✅ The **PID equation** $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\, dt + K_d \dot{e}$ is the single most important formula of this module.
- ✅ **P** handles present error, **I** handles past error, **D** predicts future error.
- ✅ **Laplace PID transfer function:** $G_c(s) = K_p + K_i/s + K_d s$.
- ✅ **Cubic polynomial trajectory** has 4 coefficients to satisfy 4 boundary conditions ($q_0, v_0, q_f, v_f$).
- ✅ For $v_0 = v_f = 0$: $q(t) = q_0 + \dfrac{3(q_f - q_0)}{T^2} t^2 - \dfrac{2(q_f - q_0)}{T^3} t^3$.
- ✅ **Trapezoidal profile** has constant acceleration → cruise → constant deceleration.
- ✅ **Triangular profile** occurs when distance is short: $v_{max} = \sqrt{a_{max} \cdot d}$.
- ✅ **Joint space** motion is easy to compute but path is curved; **Cartesian space** motion is straight-line but computationally expensive.
- ✅ **Cascade control** uses an outer position loop + inner velocity loop for superior disturbance rejection (used in drones, CNC).
- ✅ **Ziegler-Nichols tuning** gives starting PID gains from $K_u$ and $T_u$.
- ✅ For a 2-link planar arm: forward kinematics $x = L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$, $y = L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$.
- ✅ **Units:** Position in $m$ or $rad$, velocity in $m/s$ or $rad/s$, acceleration in $m/s^2$ or $rad/s^2$, torque in $N \cdot m$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Perception: Sensors for Perception - Schematic Representation of the Sensing Unit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT735 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Perception: Sensors for Perception - Schematic Representation of the Sensing Unit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Perception in Robotics

In the context of autonomous robot navigation, **Perception** is the computational process by which a robot acquires, interprets, and structures raw physical measurements obtained from its environment into a coherent internal representation (a world model) that can be used for localization, mapping, path planning, and control.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PERUT735 – Module 1):**  
> *Perception* is the ability of a robot to sense, interpret, and understand its surrounding environment using onboard sensors. The **Sensing Unit** is the hardware–software subsystem that converts physical phenomena (light, sound, magnetic field, acceleration, etc.) into structured digital information usable by the navigation stack.

The **Sensing Unit** (also called the *perception module* or *sensor stack*) consists of three logical layers:
1. **Physical transducer layer** – the actual sensor hardware.
2. **Signal conditioning & acquisition layer** – amplifiers, filters, analog-to-digital converters (ADC).
3. **Perception processing layer** – algorithms that transform raw signals into features (edges, landmarks, obstacles, odometry).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The Human–Robot Parallel

Think of a robot as a person walking blindfolded through a busy street:

| Human Sense | Robotic Equivalent | Physical Quantity Measured |
|---|---|---|
| Eyes (sight) | Camera, LiDAR | Photons / Reflected Light |
| Ears (hearing) | Microphone, Ultrasonic | Sound Pressure / Time-of-Flight |
| Skin (touch) | Tactile / Bumper sensors | Contact Force / Pressure |
| Inner-ear (balance) | IMU, Gyroscope | Angular Velocity / Acceleration |
| Muscle memory (proprioception) | Wheel Encoders, Joint Encoders | Rotation / Position |

Just as a human fuses these senses in the brain to "see" the street, the robot fuses its sensors in a **processing unit** to "perceive" the world.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** No single sensor is sufficient for robust navigation. Each sensor has inherent limitations (noise, range, field-of-view). Perception therefore relies on **sensor fusion** — combining complementary sensors to overcome individual weaknesses.

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics of a Sensing Unit

These metrics are repeatedly asked in KTU exams and must be memorized:

- **Range** – minimum and maximum measurable distance (e.g., LiDAR: 0.1 m to 100 m).
- **Resolution** – smallest detectable change in the measured quantity.
- **Accuracy** – closeness of measurement to the true value.
- **Precision** – repeatability of measurements under identical conditions.
- **Field of View (FOV)** – angular extent the sensor can observe (e.g., camera: 60°, LiDAR: 360°).
- **Bandwidth / Update Rate** – measurements per second (Hz).
- **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** – signal power relative to noise power.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Sensors for Perception

Sensors are classified along three orthogonal axes — a frequent KTU short-answer topic.

### A. Active vs. Passive Sensors
- **Active sensors** emit energy into the environment and measure the reflection.
  Examples: LiDAR, Radar, Ultrasonic Sonar, Active Infrared.
- **Passive sensors** only receive naturally available energy.
  Examples: CCD/CMOS camera, Passive Infrared (PIR), Microphone.

### B. Proprioceptive vs. Exteroceptive Sensors
- **Proprioceptive sensors** measure the robot's internal state (wheel rotation, joint angle, battery voltage).
  Examples: Encoders, IMU, Gyroscope, Current sensors.
- **Exteroceptive sensors** measure the external environment.
  Examples: Camera, LiDAR, GPS, Ultrasonic rangefinder.

### C. Contact vs. Non-Contact Sensors
- **Contact sensors** require physical touch (bumpers, tactile arrays).
- **Non-contact sensors** measure without touching (vision, ranging).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** For 3-mark questions, students must be able to place *any* given sensor into *all three* categories. Example: A webcam is **Passive + Exteroceptive + Non-Contact**.

## 2.2 Schematic Functional Architecture of a Sensing Unit

The schematic representation is the most exam-relevant portion of this topic. Every sensing unit, regardless of physical sensor type, follows a **generic five-stage pipeline**:

```
Physical Phenomenon → Transducer → Signal Conditioning → ADC → Processing/Perception
```

| Stage | Function | Typical Components |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Physical Phenomenon | Light, sound, RF, magnetic field present in environment | — (real world) |
| 2. Transducer | Converts physical energy into electrical signal | Photodiode, MEMS, Piezo crystal |
| 3. Signal Conditioning | Amplification, filtering, isolation | Op-amp, low-pass filter |
| 4. Analog-to-Digital Conversion | Samples analog waveform into discrete values | ADC (e.g., 12-bit, 1 MSPS) |
| 5. Perception Processing | Extracts features / makes decisions | Microcontroller / DSP / GPU |

## 2.3 Mathematical Models of Common Sensors

### (i) Time-of-Flight (ToF) Model — Ultrasonic & LiDAR
Used to compute range from the round-trip time of a wave:
$$d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$
where $c$ is the wave propagation speed (≈ $343 \text{ m/s}$ for sound in air; $\approx 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$ for light).

### (ii) LiDAR Range Equation
Relates received optical power to target distance:
$$P_r = \frac{P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \lambda^2 \cdot \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \cdot R^4 \cdot L_s}$$
where $P_t$ = transmitted power, $G_t, G_r$ = antenna gains, $\sigma$ = target cross-section, $R$ = range, $L_s$ = system losses.

### (iii) Pinhole Camera Model
A 3D point $(X, Y, Z)$ projects to image plane coordinates:
$$x = f \cdot \frac{X}{Z}, \quad y = f \cdot \frac{Y}{Z}$$
where $f$ is the focal length.

### (iv) Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)
$$SNR = \frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}} = \left(\frac{V_{\text{rms,signal}}}{V_{\text{rms,noise}}}\right)^2$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Formula | Units | When Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Time-of-Flight range | $d = c \Delta t / 2$ | metres | Ultrasonic, LiDAR |
| 2 | LiDAR radar equation | $P_r \propto P_t / R^4$ | watts | Active ranging |
| 3 | Pinhole projection | $x = f X / Z,\; y = f Y / Z$ | pixels | Cameras |
| 4 | SNR | $SNR = P_s / P_n$ | dimensionless | Sensor selection |
| 5 | Sensor resolution | $\Delta q = \text{Range} / 2^N$ | per LSB | ADC bit depth |
| 6 | Sampling theorem | $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$ | Hz | ADC design |
| 7 | Encoder resolution | $\theta_{\min} = 360^\circ / N_{\text{PPR}}$ | degrees/pulse | Wheel odometry |
| 8 | Accelerometer output | $a = (V_{\text{out}} - V_0) / S$ | m/s² | IMU calibration |
| 9 | Field of View | $FOV = 2 \arctan(d / 2f)$ | radians | Lens selection |
| 10 | Power spectral density | $S_n(f) = N_0 / 2$ | W/Hz | Noise analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** The LiDAR $1/R^4$ dependence is why automotive LiDAR systems struggle beyond ~200 m. Radar, which has a larger wavelength and benefits from cooperative targets, dominates long-range automotive perception (≈ 250 m). This trade-off is a classic engineering interview question.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section presents the rigorous derivations that KTU expects in 14-mark problems.

## 3.1 Derivation: Time-of-Flight Range Measurement (Ultrasonic Sensor)

**Given:** A robot emits an ultrasonic pulse at time $t_0$, the pulse travels to an obstacle, reflects, and returns, detected at time $t_1$. Speed of sound in air at 20 °C is $c = 343 \text{ m/s}$.

**Step 1 — Total distance traveled by the pulse:**
The pulse travels from emitter to obstacle and back. Let $d$ be the one-way distance. The total path length is therefore:
$$L_{\text{total}} = d + d = 2d$$

**Step 2 — Relate path length to time and speed:**
Using the basic kinematic relation $distance = speed \times time$:
$$2d = c \cdot (t_1 - t_0) = c \cdot \Delta t$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the one-way distance $d$:**
$$d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Sanity check with numerical example:**
If the obstacle is $d = 1 \text{ m}$ away, then:
$$\Delta t = \frac{2 \cdot 1}{343} = 5.83 \text{ ms}$$

This confirms that ultrasonic sensors operate comfortably in the kHz range.

**Step 5 — Temperature correction (Bonus, often asked):**
The speed of sound varies with temperature:
$$c = 331 + 0.6 \cdot T \quad [\text{m/s},\; T \text{ in }^\circ\text{C}]$$
At $T = 30^\circ$C, $c = 331 + 0.6 \cdot 30 = 349 \text{ m/s}$, giving a ~1.7% range correction.

## 3.2 Derivation: LiDAR Radar Equation (Monostatic)

**Given:** A LiDAR emits a laser pulse of power $P_t$ with transmitter gain $G_t$ and wavelength $\lambda$. The beam strikes a target at range $R$ with radar cross-section $\sigma$, and the receiver (gain $G_r$) collects a fraction of the reflected power $P_r$.

**Step 1 — Power density at the target:**
A transmitter of gain $G_t$ and power $P_t$ creates a power density at range $R$ of:
$$S_1 = \frac{P_t \cdot G_t}{4\pi R^2}$$

**Step 2 — Power intercepted by the target:**
The target intercepts energy proportional to its cross-section $\sigma$:
$$P_{\text{intercepted}} = S_1 \cdot \sigma = \frac{P_t \cdot G_t \cdot \sigma}{4\pi R^2}$$

**Step 3 — Power density of the reflected wave at the receiver:**
The reflected wave isotropically scatters from the target (Lambertian or specular), so at distance $R$ back:
$$S_2 = \frac{P_{\text{intercepted}}}{4\pi R^2} = \frac{P_t \cdot G_t \cdot \sigma}{(4\pi)^2 R^4}$$

**Step 4 — Power captured by the receiver aperture:**
The receiver of effective area $A_e = G_r \lambda^2 / (4\pi)$ captures:
$$P_r = S_2 \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \lambda^2 \cdot \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4}$$

**Step 5 — Compact LiDAR equation (with system losses $L_s$):**
$$\boxed{\,P_r = \frac{P_t \, G_t \, G_r \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, R^4 \, L_s}\,}$$

**Step 6 — Engineering takeaway:**
$P_r$ falls as $1/R^4$. Doubling the range reduces received power by a factor of $16$. This is why high-end LiDARs use sensitive avalanche photodiodes (APDs) and why automotive LiDAR is limited to ~200–250 m.

## 3.3 Derivation: Pinhole Camera Projection (with homogeneous coordinates)

**Given:** A 3D world point $\mathbf{P} = (X, Y, Z)$ in the camera coordinate frame, with origin at the optical centre.

**Step 1 — Perspective projection equations (basic form):**
By similar triangles on the image plane (focal length $f$):
$$x = f \cdot \frac{X}{Z}, \quad y = f \cdot \frac{Y}{Z}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to homogeneous coordinates:**
Use vector form with the intrinsic matrix $K$:
$$\begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{Z} \begin{bmatrix} f & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & f & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Account for pixel offset $(c_x, c_y)$ and pixel size:**
Real cameras shift the principal point by $(c_x, c_y)$:
$$\begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \sim \begin{bmatrix} f_x & 0 & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Full projection with extrinsic rotation $\mathbf{R}$ and translation $\mathbf{t}$:**
$$\mathbf{p} \sim K \,[\,R \mid t\,] \, \mathbf{P}_w$$

This matrix product $K[R \mid t]$ is the central equation in visual SLAM (ORB-SLAM, VINS-Mono) — a heavily tested KTU concept.

## 3.4 Derivation: Encoder Resolution (Quadrature Decoder)

**Given:** An incremental optical encoder with $N_{\text{PPR}}$ pulses per revolution on two channels (A, B) in quadrature.

**Step 1 — Mechanical angle per pulse:**
$$\Delta \theta = \frac{360^\circ}{N_{\text{PPR}}}$$

**Step 2 — Quadrature decoding quadruples the count:**
Reading both rising and falling edges of channels A and B gives 4× resolution:
$$\theta_{\min} = \frac{\Delta \theta}{4} = \frac{90^\circ}{N_{\text{PPR}}}$$

**Step 3 — Example:**
For a 1000-PPR encoder, $\theta_{\min} = 90^\circ / 1000 = 0.09^\circ$ per tick. Excellent for wheel odometry.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Sensor Choice vs. Navigation Task

| Navigation Task | Best Sensor | Reason | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Indoor localization | Wheel encoder + IMU | Dead-reckoning accurate short term | Drift over long distances |
| Outdoor global position | GPS / GNSS | Absolute global position | Poor in urban canyons, <5 m accuracy |
| Obstacle avoidance (slow robot) | Ultrasonic | Cheap, simple | Narrow beam, cross-talk |
| 2D mapping | 2D LiDAR | 360°, cm accuracy | Mechanical wear, ~$10 000 |
| 3D environment model | 3D LiDAR / Stereo cam | Dense point cloud | High computation cost |
| Visual landmark detection | Camera + CV | Rich semantic info | Lighting sensitive |
| Long-range obstacle (car) | Millimeter-wave Radar | Penetrates fog/rain | Lower resolution |
| Underground / Indoor GPS-denied | UWB beacons | Decimetre accuracy | Needs infrastructure |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The diagrams below use **Mermaid** syntax. Node IDs follow the *Alpha Rule* (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`) — no reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) appear as bare node identifiers.

## 4.1 Generic Schematic of a Sensing Unit (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[ENV1: Physical Phenomenon] --> A2[ENV2: Transducer Hardware]
    A2 --> A3[ENV3: Analog Signal Conditioning]
    A3 --> A4[ENV4: Analog to Digital Converter]
    A4 --> A5[ENV5: Digital Processing Unit]
    A5 --> A6[ENV6: Perception Output Features]
    A6 --> A7[ENV7: Navigation Stack Localization Mapping Planning]
```

**Reading the schematic:**
- **ENV1 → ENV2**: Real-world energy (light, sound, RF) strikes the transducer (photodiode, piezo, antenna).
- **ENV2 → ENV3**: The transducer outputs a weak analog voltage/current, which is amplified and filtered.
- **ENV3 → ENV4**: The clean analog signal is sampled by an ADC.
- **ENV4 → ENV5**: A microcontroller/DSP/FPGA executes perception algorithms (edge detection, scan matching, FFT).
- **ENV5 → ENV6**: Features such as obstacle distance, image corners, or velocity are extracted.
- **ENV6 → ENV7**: These features feed the navigation stack (SLAM, path planner, controller).

## 4.2 Multi-Sensor Robot Perception Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[SENSOR A: Camera] --> P1[PROC1: Vision Pipeline]
    S2[SENSOR B: 2D LiDAR] --> P2[PROC2: Scan Matcher]
    S3[SENSOR C: IMU] --> P3[PROC3: Inertial Integration]
    S4[SENSOR D: Wheel Encoder] --> P4[PROC4: Odometry]
    P1 --> F1[FUSE1: EKF or Graph Optimizer]
    P2 --> F1
    P3 --> F1
    P4 --> F1
    F1 --> O1[OUTPUT: Robot Pose Velocity Map]
```

This is the canonical **sensor fusion** schematic used in mobile robotics — the central exam-relevant block diagram of this topic.

## 4.3 Signal-Conditioning Subsystem (Detailed)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[TRANSD1: Raw Sensor Signal mV level] --> AMP1[AMP1: Instrumentation Amplifier Gain 100]
    AMP1 --> FILT1[FILT1: Anti-Alias Low-Pass Filter fc 1 kHz]
    FILT1 --> BUF1[BUF1: Unity Gain Buffer]
    BUF1 --> ADC1[ADC1: 12 bit 100 kSPS]
    ADC1 --> MCU1[MCU1: Perception Algorithm]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **anti-aliasing filter** before the ADC is critical. Per the Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem, all signal content above half the sampling rate must be removed, otherwise aliasing corrupts the perception output. This is a frequently tested KTU concept.

## 4.4 Active vs. Passive Sensor Block Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ACTIVE
        E1[EMIT1: Energy Source Laser LED Ultrasonic] --> E2[PROP1: Wave Propagation Through Medium]
        E2 --> E3[REFL1: Reflection From Target]
        E3 --> E4[RCV1: Receiver Detects Return]
    end
    subgraph PASSIVE
        P1[NAT1: Natural Emission Sunlight Heat Sound] --> P2[ARR1: Energy Reaches Sensor]
        P2 --> P3[DET1: Sensor Absorbs Energy]
    end
```

## 4.5 Feedback Loop in a Closed Perception-Control System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W1[WORLD] --> SENSE[SENSE: Sensing Unit]
    SENSE --> EST[EST: State Estimator SLAM]
    EST --> PLAN[PLAN: Path Planner]
    PLAN --> ACT[ACT: Motor Actuator]
    ACT --> W1
```

This block diagram is the foundation of the **perception–action loop** — every mobile robot operates on this cycle.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between active and passive sensors used in robot perception. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (≈ 120 words):**

| Aspect | Active Sensors | Passive Sensors |
|---|---|---|
| Energy source | Emit their own energy (laser, ultrasound, RF) | Only receive naturally available energy |
| Power consumption | Higher (needs emitter) | Lower (only receiver) |
| Range measurement | Yes (via ToF, triangulation) | No (only intensity / image) |
| Examples | LiDAR, Ultrasonic, Radar | Camera, PIR, Microphone |

Active sensors measure distance directly; passive sensors rely on external illumination.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**With a neat block diagram, explain the schematic representation of a generic sensing unit.**

**Model Answer:**
A sensing unit consists of **(i) Transducer** (converts physical energy into electrical signal), **(ii) Signal Conditioning block** (amplification, filtering), **(iii) ADC** (samples analog signal into digital), **(iv) Processing Unit** (microcontroller/DSP running perception algorithms), and **(v) Output Interface** (delivers features to navigation stack).

```
Phenomenon → Transducer → Amplifier+Filter → ADC → Processor → Perception Output
```

This five-stage pipeline is universal — applicable to cameras, LiDARs, ultrasonic sensors, and IMUs alike.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Classify the various sensors used in robot navigation. Explain proprioceptive and exteroceptive sensors with suitable examples.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a block schematic, describe the functional architecture of a LiDAR-based sensing unit. Derive the LiDAR range equation and explain the significance of the $1/R^4$ dependence.

---

#### Model Solution (a):

**Classification of sensors for robot navigation:**

| Basis | Class 1 | Class 2 |
|---|---|---|
| Energy | Active | Passive |
| Origin | Proprioceptive | Exteroceptive |
| Contact | Contact | Non-contact |

**Proprioceptive sensors** measure the robot's *internal* state — wheel encoders (rotation), IMU (acceleration, angular rate), gyroscopes, joint torque sensors, battery voltage sensors. They are crucial for **dead-reckoning odometry** but suffer from drift.

**Exteroceptive sensors** measure the *external* environment — cameras, LiDAR, ultrasonic, GPS, magnetometers. They enable absolute localization and obstacle detection. They are essential for SLAM but are sensitive to weather, lighting, and occlusion.

**Example classification of a webcam:** Passive + Exteroceptive + Non-contact.

---

#### Model Solution (b):

**Block Schematic of LiDAR sensing unit:**

```
Laser Diode → Driver Circuit → Scanning Optics (MEMS/Mirror) → Emitted Beam
                                                                     ↓
                                                          (travels to target)
                                                                     ↓
Receive Optics → Photodetector APD → Transimpedance Amplifier → Band-pass Filter → ADC → DSP → Point Cloud
```

**Derivation of LiDAR range equation** (as in Section 3.2):
$$P_r = \frac{P_t \, G_t \, G_r \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, R^4 \, L_s}$$

**Significance of $1/R^4$ dependence:**
1. Doubling the range reduces received power by $16\times$, severely limiting detection range.
2. Automotive LiDAR is therefore practically limited to ~200–250 m.
3. Counter-measures: increase $P_t$, use APDs with high gain, or use longer wavelengths (e.g., 1550 nm which is eye-safe at higher power).

---

### Question B — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the time-of-flight equation for an ultrasonic range sensor. How is the speed of sound affected by temperature?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the pinhole camera model with a neat schematic. Show how a 3D world point projects onto a 2D image plane.

---

#### Model Solution (a):

**Time-of-Flight derivation:**
Pulse travels distance $2d$ in time $\Delta t$ at speed $c$:
$$2d = c \Delta t \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}}$$

**Temperature correction:**
$$c = 331 + 0.6\,T \quad [\text{m/s},\; T \text{ in }^\circ\text{C}]$$

At $T = 20^\circ$C: $c = 343$ m/s; at $T = 40^\circ$C: $c = 355$ m/s — a 3.5% increase. For accurate ranging above 5 m, robots must either measure temperature or accept the error.

**Numerical example:** For $d = 2$ m at $20^\circ$C: $\Delta t = 2 \cdot 2 / 343 = 11.66$ ms.

---

#### Model Solution (b):

**Pinhole Camera Model:**

```
        Z (optical axis)
        |
        |
        O------- X  (image plane at z = f)
       /|
      / |
     /  | y
    P   |
   /    |
  /     |
 X------ Y
  (3D point)
```

For a 3D point $P = (X, Y, Z)$ projecting through the pinhole $O$ onto the image plane at distance $f$, similar triangles yield:
$$x = f \frac{X}{Z}, \qquad y = f \frac{Y}{Z}$$

In matrix form (homogeneous coordinates):
$$\begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & 0 & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \end{bmatrix}$$

This model is the basis of camera calibration and visual SLAM algorithms.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks:**
> 1. **Confusing proprioceptive with exteroceptive.** Wheel encoders are *proprioceptive* (internal), cameras are *exteroceptive* (external). Many students mix them up.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in ToF.** The pulse travels *to and back*, so $\Delta t$ corresponds to $2d$, not $d$. Marks lost: 1–2 per question.
> 3. **Drawing schematic without labeling arrows.** Block diagrams *must* show signal flow direction with arrows, or 2–3 marks are deducted.
> 4. **Not mentioning Nyquist criterion** when explaining the ADC stage. Examiners look for the link: *sampling rate must be ≥ 2 × max signal frequency*.
> 5. **Mixing up active and passive.** A camera is passive; a LiDAR is active. Memorize the distinction.
> 6. **Skipping units in derivations.** Always append units (m/s, m, Hz, dB). A derivation without units loses marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Perception = Sensing + Interpretation.** A sensing unit alone is *not* perception — algorithms must interpret the data.
- **Three sensor classification axes:** Active/Passive, Proprioceptive/Exteroceptive, Contact/Non-contact.
- **Generic sensing pipeline (5 stages):** Physical phenomenon → Transducer → Signal conditioning → ADC → Processing.
- **Time-of-Flight formula:** $d = c \Delta t / 2$ — applies to ultrasonic and LiDAR.
- **LiDAR radar equation:** $P_r \propto P_t / R^4$ — limits automotive LiDAR to ~200 m.
- **Pinhole camera projection:** $x = fX/Z,\; y = fY/Z$ — foundation of visual SLAM.
- **Nyquist sampling theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$ — must be applied before ADC.
- **Sensor fusion is essential:** No single sensor is reliable alone; EKF / graph-based fusion combines complementary modalities.
- **Encoder resolution with quadrature decoding:** $\theta_{\min} = 90^\circ / N_{\text{PPR}}$.
- **SNR** determines usable range of any sensor; high SNR ↔ long range.
- **Closed perception–action loop:** Sense → Estimate → Plan → Act → repeat.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision tip:** Memorize the **five-stage sensing pipeline diagram** and the **two most-tested equations** ($d = c\Delta t/2$ and $P_r \propto 1/R^4$). These appear in nearly every KTU question paper for this module.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heading Sensors - Gyroscopes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT735 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Heading Sensors - Gyroscopes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Gyroscope** is a heading sensor (also called an angular rate sensor or inertial measurement device) that measures the **orientation**, **angular velocity**, or **angular displacement** of a body relative to an inertial reference frame. In the context of robot navigation, gyroscopes are classified as **proprioceptive sensors** because they measure internal states of the robot without requiring external references.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A gyroscope is a device used for measuring or maintaining orientation and angular velocity, based on the principle of **conservation of angular momentum** (classical/mechanical gyroscope), the **Coriolis effect** (MEMS gyroscopes), or the **Sagnac effect** (optical gyroscopes — Ring Laser Gyroscope and Fiber Optic Gyroscope).

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **child's spinning top**. Once you spin it on a table, it does not fall flat immediately — instead, its spin axis stays remarkably upright and slowly *wobbles* in a circular motion. This slow circular wobble is called **precession**.

- The **fast spinning** represents the gyroscope's rotor spinning at high speed.
- The **slow wobble** (precession) tells us about the external turning force (torque) applied to the system.
- If you stick a small pencil to the top's axis, the pencil would trace out the heading changes of the table — exactly what a heading sensor does in a robot.

In a robot, a gyroscope answers the question: *"How much has my orientation changed since the last measurement?"* — providing the **yaw, pitch, and roll rates** essential for dead-reckoning navigation.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metrics and Reference Constants Used in Gyroscope Analysis:**
> - **Angular velocity (ω):** measured in **rad/s** (SI) or **°/s** (degrees per second, common in MEMS datasheets).
> - **Angular momentum (L):** **kg·m²/s** — defined as $L = I\omega$.
> - **Bias stability:** typically **0.01 °/hr** (high-end RLG) to **10 °/hr** (low-cost MEMS).
> - **Scale factor:** output (volts or counts) per unit angular rate, often in **mV/(°/s)**.
> - **Random walk coefficient:** in **°/√hr**.
> - **Speed of light:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ **m/s** (used in Sagnac equations).
> - **Standard gravity:** $g = 9.81$ **m/s²** (used in mechanical precession derivation).

### 1.4 Role of Gyroscopes in Robot Navigation

In autonomous robots (mobile robots, UAVs, AGVs, autonomous cars), gyroscopes are usually paired with **accelerometers** and **magnetometers** in an **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)**. They contribute:

- **Heading estimation** in environments where GPS or magnetic compasses fail.
- **Short-term stability** to orientation estimates between absolute fixes.
- **Drift correction** when fused with Kalman filters using absolute position sensors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A gyroscope alone does **NOT** measure absolute heading (North) — it measures **changes in heading**. Only when integrated over time does it yield the orientation angle: $\theta(t) = \int_0^t \omega(\tau)\,d\tau + \theta_0$. This integration is the source of the well-known **drift** problem.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principles — Three Foundational Mechanisms

A gyroscope's operation rests on one of three physical principles depending on its construction:

**(a) Conservation of Angular Momentum** — Classical Mechanical Gyroscope
- A spinning mass resists changes to the orientation of its spin axis.
- The applied torque causes **precession** rather than direct tipping.
- Why? Because $\vec{\tau} = \dfrac{d\vec{L}}{dt}$, and changing the *direction* of $\vec{L}$ (not magnitude) satisfies this equation by rotating the spin axis.

**(b) Coriolis Effect** — MEMS Vibratory Gyroscope
- A proof mass is vibrated in a known direction.
- When the device rotates, the proof mass experiences an apparent **Coriolis force** perpendicular to both the vibration and the rotation axis.
- This force is measured capacitively to infer the rotation rate.

**(c) Sagnac Effect** — Optical Gyroscopes (RLG & FOG)
- Two light beams are sent in **opposite directions** around a closed optical loop.
- When the loop rotates, the beam traveling *with* the rotation covers a longer path than the beam traveling *against* it.
- The resulting **path-length difference** (or phase difference) is proportional to the angular velocity.

### 2.2 Operational Breakdown

#### A. Mechanical (Classical) Gyroscope
- Mounted on a **gimbal** system allowing 3 degrees of rotational freedom.
- Rotor spins at thousands of RPM using an electric motor.
- When the base rotates, the spin axis maintains its inertial orientation.
- The angle between the spin axis and the base frame gives the heading.

#### B. MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems) Gyroscope
- Microscopic silicon proof mass suspended by flexure springs.
- Driven into **linear oscillation** at resonance (drive mode).
- Under rotation, Coriolis force induces **secondary oscillation** (sense mode).
- Differential capacitance between comb fingers measures displacement.

#### C. Ring Laser Gyroscope (RLG)
- A **helium–neon laser** circulates two beams in opposite directions around a closed triangular or square cavity.
- Inertial rotation causes a **frequency difference** (beat frequency) between the two counter-propagating beams.
- Used in commercial aircraft and spacecraft (e.g., the Honeywell GG1320).

#### D. Fiber Optic Gyroscope (FOG)
- A long coil of **single-mode optical fiber** (typically 100 m to 5 km) replaces the RLG cavity.
- Based on the **Sagnac interferometer** configuration.
- Compact, no moving parts, no high-voltage excitation — preferred for marine and space robotics.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table summarizes every formula you must memorize for KTU university examinations on Gyroscopes.**

| **Concept** | **Formula** | **Symbols & Units** | **Where Used** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Angular momentum of spinning rotor | $L = I\omega$ | $I$ = moment of inertia (kg·m²); $\omega$ = spin rate (rad/s); $L$ in kg·m²/s | Classical gyro |
| Precession angular velocity | $\Omega_p = \dfrac{\tau}{L} = \dfrac{mgr}{I\omega}$ | $\tau$ = applied torque (N·m); $m$ = mass (kg); $r$ = pivot distance (m); $g$ = 9.81 m/s² | Classical gyro analysis |
| Gyroscopic torque (response) | $\tau_{gyro} = I\omega\Omega_p$ | Same as above, $\Omega_p$ is precession rate | Vehicle dynamics, IMU errors |
| Coriolis acceleration | $\vec{a}_c = 2\,\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{v}$ | $\vec{\Omega}$ = rotation rate (rad/s); $\vec{v}$ = velocity in rotating frame (m/s) | MEMS gyro principle |
| Coriolis force on proof mass | $F_c = 2m\Omega v_d$ | $v_d$ = drive-mode velocity (m/s) | MEMS sense mode derivation |
| Coriolis displacement (linearised) | $x(t) = \dfrac{F_c / k}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ | $k$ = spring stiffness; $r = \omega_d / \omega_n$; $\zeta$ = damping ratio | MEMS output model |
| Sagnac path-length difference | $\Delta L = \dfrac{4A\Omega}{c}$ | $A$ = enclosed area (m²); $\Omega$ = rotation rate (rad/s); $c = 3\times 10^8$ m/s | RLG, FOG analysis |
| Sagnac phase shift | $\Delta\phi = \dfrac{8\pi A\Omega}{\lambda c}$ | $\lambda$ = wavelength (m) | FOG interferometric readout |
| FOG with N fiber turns | $\Delta\phi = \dfrac{8\pi N A \Omega}{\lambda c}$ | $N$ = number of fiber turns | Practical FOG design |
| Heading from gyroscope integration | $\theta(t) = \theta_0 + \displaystyle\int_0^t \omega(\tau)\,d\tau$ | $\omega$ = measured angular rate | All digital gyroscopes |
| Gyroscope random-walk variance | $\sigma^2_{\theta}(t) = \sigma^2_{RW} \cdot t$ | $\sigma_{RW}$ in °/√hr | Error budgeting |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** In all modern robotic applications, gyroscopes are integrated over time. Even a tiny bias error $b_\omega$ (a few °/hr) accumulates linearly: $\theta_{error}(t) \approx b_\omega \, t$. After one hour, a 1 °/hr bias produces **1° heading error**, but after 100 hours this becomes **100°** — catastrophic for long-duration missions. This is why high-grade gyroscopes (RLG, FOG) are preferred in spacecraft and submarines.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Aerospace:** Attitude control of aircraft, spacecraft, and satellites (IMU/AHRS).
- **Marine:** Submarine and ship inertial navigation (FOG-based systems).
- **Automotive:** Electronic Stability Control (ESC), autonomous vehicle localization.
- **Consumer Robotics:** Drones, robot vacuum cleaners, humanoid balance control.
- **Industrial:** Camera gimbals, antenna stabilization on moving platforms.
- **Defense:** Guided missiles, smart munitions, soldier-portable navigation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Precession Rate of a Mechanical Gyroscope

**Setup:** A spinning symmetric top of mass $m$, with its spin axis inclined at angle $\theta$ from the vertical. Gravity acts at the center of mass located at distance $\ell$ from the pivot.

**Step 1 — Compute the gravitational torque about the pivot.**
The weight $m\vec{g}$ acts vertically downward at distance $\ell$ from the pivot.

$$\tau = mg\ell \sin\theta$$

**Step 2 — Apply Newton's second law for rotation.**

$$\vec{\tau} = \dfrac{d\vec{L}}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — The angular momentum has two components.**
- Spin component along the symmetry axis: $L_s = I_s \omega_s$ (where $I_s$ is the spin-axis moment of inertia and $\omega_s$ is the spin rate).
- Precession component: this is what changes when torque acts.

For a *fast-spinning* gyroscope where $\omega_s \gg \Omega_p$, the magnitude of $\vec{L}$ is approximately $L \approx I_s \omega_s$ and is directed along the spin axis.

**Step 4 — Direction of $d\vec{L}/dt$.**
Since $\vec{\tau}$ is horizontal (perpendicular to gravity and to the line from pivot to CM), it rotates $\vec{L}$ horizontally — the spin axis sweeps a circle. This circular motion is the **precession**.

**Step 5 — Geometric relation between $dL/dt$ and $\Omega_p$.**

$$\left\vert \dfrac{d\vec{L}}{dt} \right\vert = L \sin\theta \cdot \Omega_p$$

**Step 6 — Equate magnitudes of torque and rate of change of angular momentum.**

$$mg\ell \sin\theta = I_s \omega_s \sin\theta \cdot \Omega_p$$

**Step 7 — Solve for the precession angular velocity.**

$$\boxed{\;\Omega_p = \dfrac{mg\ell}{I_s\,\omega_s}\;}$$

> **Interpretation:** Precession rate is **inversely proportional** to the spin rate. Faster spin → slower precession → more stable heading reference. This is why gyroscope rotors spin at very high RPM.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Coriolis Force on a Vibrating MEMS Proof Mass

**Setup:** A proof mass of mass $m$ vibrates linearly with velocity $\vec{v}_d$ along the $x$-axis (drive mode). The entire chip rotates about the $z$-axis with angular velocity $\vec{\Omega} = \Omega \hat{k}$.

**Step 1 — Write the velocity of the proof mass in the inertial frame.**

In the rotating frame (chip), the proof mass moves with velocity $\vec{v}_{rel} = v_d \hat{i}$. In the inertial frame, the velocity is:

$$\vec{v}_{inertial} = \vec{v}_{rel} + \vec{\Omega} \times \vec{r}$$

For the small proof-mass displacements involved, $\vec{v}_{rel} \gg \vec{\Omega}\times\vec{r}$, so we approximate $\vec{v}_{inertial} \approx v_d \hat{i}$.

**Step 2 — Apply the rotating-frame acceleration formula.**

In a frame rotating with $\vec{\Omega}$, a particle experiences the Coriolis acceleration:

$$\vec{a}_{Cor} = -2\,\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{v}_{rel}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $\vec{\Omega} = \Omega \hat{k}$ and $\vec{v}_{rel} = v_d \hat{i}$.**

$$\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{v}_{rel} = (\Omega \hat{k}) \times (v_d \hat{i}) = \Omega v_d (\hat{k}\times\hat{i}) = \Omega v_d \hat{j}$$

Therefore:

$$\vec{a}_{Cor} = -2\,\Omega\, v_d\,\hat{j}$$

**Step 4 — The Coriolis force on the proof mass.**

$$\boxed{\;\vec{F}_{Cor} = m\,\vec{a}_{Cor} = -2m\,\Omega\,v_d\,\hat{j}\;}$$

**Step 5 — Resulting sense-mode displacement.**
The Coriolis force deflects the proof mass in the $y$-direction (perpendicular to both drive motion and rotation axis). The deflection $y$ is detected capacitively as:

$$y = \dfrac{F_{Cor}}{k_{sense}} = \dfrac{2m\,\Omega\, v_d}{k_{sense}}$$

where $k_{sense}$ is the stiffness of the sense-mode suspension. The output voltage is then:

$$V_{out} = S \cdot \Omega + V_{bias} + n(t)$$

with $S$ = scale factor, $V_{bias}$ = bias offset, $n(t)$ = measurement noise.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sagnac Phase Shift in a Fiber Optic Gyroscope

**Setup:** A fiber coil of $N$ turns, enclosed area $A$ per turn. The coil rotates with angular velocity $\Omega$ about its symmetry axis.

**Step 1 — Path length of beam co-rotating with the rotation.**
In the time $\Delta t$ it takes the beam to traverse one turn, every point on the coil has moved by $\Omega R \Delta t$ (where $R = \sqrt{A/\pi}$ is the effective radius). The co-rotating beam must travel an **extra** distance:

$$L_+ = 2\pi R + \Omega R \Delta t$$

But $\Delta t = L_+ / c$, so:

$$L_+ = \dfrac{2\pi R}{1 - \Omega R / c} \approx 2\pi R \left(1 + \dfrac{\Omega R}{c}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Path length of the counter-rotating beam.**

$$L_- = \dfrac{2\pi R}{1 + \Omega R / c} \approx 2\pi R \left(1 - \dfrac{\Omega R}{c}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Path-length difference per turn.**

$$\Delta L_{1turn} = L_+ - L_- = 2\pi R \cdot \dfrac{2\Omega R}{c} = \dfrac{4\pi R^2 \Omega}{c} = \dfrac{4 A \Omega}{c}$$

**Step 4 — Total path difference for $N$ turns.**

$$\Delta L = N \cdot \dfrac{4A\Omega}{c} = \dfrac{4NA\Omega}{c}$$

**Step 5 — Convert path difference to phase shift.**

Since one wavelength $\lambda$ corresponds to a phase change of $2\pi$:

$$\Delta\phi = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda}\,\Delta L = \dfrac{8\pi N A \Omega}{\lambda c}$$

$$\boxed{\;\Delta\phi = \dfrac{8\pi N A \Omega}{\lambda c}\;}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for the rotation rate (the gyro equation).**

$$\Omega = \dfrac{\lambda c \,\Delta\phi}{8\pi N A}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** To detect very small rotation rates (e.g., Earth's rotation = $7.27 \times 10^{-5}$ rad/s), $N$ and $A$ must be large. A 1 km fiber coil with 10 cm radius gives detectable phase shifts even for tiny Ω — which is why FOG coils are wound with kilometers of fiber.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Gyroscope Types

| **Parameter** | **Mechanical** | **MEMS** | **Ring Laser (RLG)** | **Fiber Optic (FOG)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Physical principle | Angular momentum | Coriolis force | Sagnac effect | Sagnac effect |
| Bias stability | 0.001 – 0.01 °/hr | 1 – 10 °/hr | 0.001 – 0.01 °/hr | 0.001 – 0.01 °/hr |
| Size / Weight | Bulky (kg) | Tiny (mm, grams) | Medium (kg) | Medium (kg) |
| Power consumption | High (motor drive) | Very low (mW) | High (HV for laser) | Moderate |
| Shock resistance | Poor | Excellent | Excellent | Good |
| Cost | High | Very low | Very high | High |
| Moving parts | Yes (rotor) | Yes (micro-mass) | No | No |
| Drift | Low | High | Very low | Very low |
| Typical application | Marine, spacecraft (legacy) | Drones, smartphones, robotics | Aircraft, missiles | Marine, spacecraft, AUV |
| KTU exam weight | Medium | **High** | Medium | **High** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Taxonomy of Gyroscopes Used in Robot Navigation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[GYROSCOPES] --> B[MECHANICAL CLASSICAL]
    A --> C[OPTICAL]
    A --> D[MEMS VIBRATORY]

    B --> B1[Spinning rotor on gimbals]
    B --> B2[Conservation of angular momentum]
    B --> B3[Precession is measured]

    C --> C1[RING LASER GYRO RLG]
    C --> C2[FIBER OPTIC GYRO FOG]
    C1 --> C1A[Helium neon laser cavity]
    C1 --> C1B[Two counter propagating beams]
    C1 --> C1C[Beat frequency proportional to rotation]
    C2 --> C2A[Coil of optical fiber]
    C2 --> C2B[Sagnac interferometer setup]
    C2 --> C2C[Phase shift measured at detector]

    D --> D1[Micromachined silicon proof mass]
    D --> D2[Drive mode linear oscillation]
    D --> D3[Coriolis force on rotation]
    D --> D4[Capacitive sense of displacement]
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — MEMS Gyroscope Operation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN1[DC Voltage Supply] --> OSC[Drive Oscillator Circuit]
    OSC --> DRV[Drive Electrodes]
    DRV --> PM[Proof Mass Vibrates at Resonance]
    PM --> COR[Coriolis Force Acts When Device Rotates]
    COR --> SNS[Displacement in Sense Direction]
    SNS --> CAP[Capacitive Sense Electrodes]
    CAP --> AMP[Charge Amplifier]
    AMP --> ADC[Analog to Digital Converter]
    ADC --> DSP[Signal Conditioning and Filtering]
    DSP --> OUT[Digital Angular Rate Output deg per s]

    ROT[External Rotation Rate Input] --> PM

    BIAS[Bias and Temperature Compensation] --> DSP
    CAL[Factory Calibration Coefficients] --> DSP
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Sagnac Fiber Optic Gyroscope

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LS[Superluminescent Diode Source] --> CPL[3 dB Coupler Splitter]
    CPL --> BEAM1[Beam Traveling CW Clockwise]
    CPL --> BEAM2[Beam Traveling CCW Counter Clockwise]
    BEAM1 --> LOOP[Fiber Coil N turns Area A]
    BEAM2 --> LOOP
    LOOP --> CPL2[Output Coupler Recombiner]
    CPL2 --> DET[Photodetector]
    DET --> PHM[Phase Meter]
    PHM --> PROC[Signal Processor]
    PROC --> OMEGA[Angular Rate Output Omega]
    ROT[Platform Rotation] --> LOOP
    MOD[Piezoelectric Phase Modulator] --> LOOP
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading tip for KTU:** When asked *"Explain the working of a gyroscope"*, always (1) name the principle, (2) draw a labelled functional block diagram (similar to the Mermaid flow above), (3) write the governing equation with derivation, and (4) state the engineering use. This four-step structure is the KTU examiner's gold standard for full-mark answers.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** What is a gyroscope? List any four types of gyroscopes used in robot navigation.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A gyroscope is a heading sensor that measures the angular velocity or angular displacement of a body relative to an inertial reference frame. It is a proprioceptive sensor used in robot navigation to estimate orientation when external references (like GPS or magnetic compass) are unavailable.

The four main types are:
1. **Mechanical (Classical) Gyroscope** — based on conservation of angular momentum.
2. **MEMS Gyroscope** — based on the Coriolis effect on a vibrating proof mass.
3. **Ring Laser Gyroscope (RLG)** — based on the Sagnac effect in a laser cavity.
4. **Fiber Optic Gyroscope (FOG)** — based on the Sagnac effect in a fiber coil.

*(Award: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for naming the principle, 1 mark for listing types with brief identification.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define the **Coriolis effect** and state its role in MEMS gyroscopes.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Coriolis effect is the apparent deflection of a moving object when viewed from a rotating reference frame. The Coriolis acceleration is given by:

$$\vec{a}_{Cor} = -2\,\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{v}$$

**Role in MEMS gyroscopes:** In a MEMS gyroscope, a proof mass is driven into linear oscillation. When the sensor chip rotates, the proof mass experiences a Coriolis force perpendicular to both its vibration direction and the rotation axis. This Coriolis-induced displacement is measured capacitively and is directly proportional to the rotation rate $\Omega$, allowing the device to act as an angular rate sensor.

*(Award: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the equation, 1 mark for explaining the role in MEMS.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** *[KTU University Exam – Model Question]* 

**(a)** Derive an expression for the **precession angular velocity** of a classical mechanical gyroscope. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the **construction and working of a MEMS gyroscope**. Discuss its advantages and limitations for robotic applications. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation of Precession Angular Velocity (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Setup and torque calculation (2 marks).**

Consider a spinning rotor of mass $m$, mounted on a pivot such that its center of mass is at perpendicular distance $\ell$ from the pivot. The rotor spins with angular velocity $\omega_s$ about its symmetry axis, which is inclined at angle $\theta$ to the vertical.

The weight $mg$ acts at the center of mass. The torque about the pivot is:

$$\tau = mg\ell \sin\theta$$

**Step 2 — Angular momentum of the spinning rotor (2 marks).**

The spin-axis moment of inertia is $I_s$, so the angular momentum along the spin axis is:

$$L = I_s \omega_s$$

**Step 3 — Relating torque to the rate of change of angular momentum (2 marks).**

Since $\vec{\tau} = \dfrac{d\vec{L}}{dt}$, and because $\vec{L}$ has constant magnitude but rotating direction (precession), the magnitude of $d\vec{L}/dt$ equals:

$$\left\vert \dfrac{d\vec{L}}{dt} \right\vert = L \sin\theta \cdot \Omega_p$$

where $\Omega_p$ is the precession angular velocity.

**Step 4 — Final expression (1 mark).**

Equating the two expressions:

$$mg\ell \sin\theta = I_s \omega_s \sin\theta \cdot \Omega_p$$

$$\boxed{\;\Omega_p = \dfrac{mg\ell}{I_s \omega_s}\;}$$

**Conclusion:** The precession rate is inversely proportional to the spin angular velocity. Therefore, increasing the spin speed makes the gyroscope more directionally stable.

---

**Part (b) — MEMS Gyroscope: Construction, Working, Advantages and Limitations (7 marks)**

**Construction (2 marks):**
A MEMS gyroscope consists of a microfabricated silicon proof mass suspended by compliant flexure springs. Comb-finger electrodes surround the proof mass. Drive-mode electrodes vibrate the mass at its natural frequency (typically a few kHz). Sense-mode electrodes detect perpendicular displacement.

**Working (3 marks):**

1. The drive oscillator applies an AC voltage to the drive electrodes, causing the proof mass to oscillate linearly along the $x$-axis.
2. When the chip rotates about the $z$-axis with angular velocity $\Omega$, the Coriolis force acts on the proof mass:

$$F_{Cor} = 2m\Omega v_d$$

This force deflects the proof mass in the $y$-direction (sense direction).

3. The resulting displacement is detected as a differential capacitance change between the sense electrodes. The capacitance variation is converted to voltage, digitized, and output as the angular rate.

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- Small size, low weight, low power (mW range).
- Low cost — suitable for mass-produced consumer robots.
- High shock tolerance (no high-speed rotating parts).
- Easily integrated with accelerometers on a single chip (6-axis IMU).

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- Higher bias drift than optical gyroscopes (1–10 °/hr).
- Sensitive to temperature variations — requires compensation.
- Scale factor non-linearity at high rotation rates.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** *[KTU University Exam – Model Question — Internal Choice Alternative]*

**(a)** Explain the **Sagnac effect** and derive the phase-shift expression for a **fiber optic gyroscope**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Ring Laser Gyroscope (RLG)** and **Fiber Optic Gyroscope (FOG)**. Discuss why **FOG is preferred in marine autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs)** for navigation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Sagnac Effect and FOG Phase-Shift Derivation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Statement of Sagnac effect (1 mark).**

When a closed optical path rotates about an axis perpendicular to its plane, light traveling in the direction of rotation takes longer to complete the loop than light traveling against it. This produces a path-length difference and a corresponding phase shift — the **Sagnac effect**.

**Step 2 — Path length of the co-rotating beam (2 marks).**

In a fiber coil of radius $R$ rotating with angular velocity $\Omega$, the co-rotating beam's path length per turn is:

$$L_+ = 2\pi R + \Omega R \Delta t \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; L_+ \approx 2\pi R \left(1 + \dfrac{\Omega R}{c}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Path length of the counter-rotating beam (2 marks).**

$$L_- \approx 2\pi R \left(1 - \dfrac{\Omega R}{c}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Phase-shift derivation (2 marks).**

Path difference for $N$ turns:

$$\Delta L = \dfrac{4NAR}{c}\Omega / \pi \;=\; \dfrac{4NAR^2}{c}\Omega \;=\; \dfrac{4NA\Omega}{c}$$

Phase shift:

$$\Delta\phi = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda}\Delta L = \dfrac{8\pi N A \Omega}{\lambda c}$$

$$\boxed{\;\Omega = \dfrac{\lambda c}{8\pi N A}\,\Delta\phi\;}$$

This is the governing equation of the FOG — the rotation rate is recovered directly from the measured phase shift.

---

**Part (b) — RLG vs FOG Comparison & Marine AUV Application (7 marks)**

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **RLG** | **FOG** |
|---|---|---|
| Optical medium | Gas laser cavity (He-Ne) | Glass optical fiber |
| Path length | Few metres (multiple reflections) | 100 m to 5 km of fiber |
| Detected quantity | Beat frequency | Phase shift |
| High voltage | Required (to strike plasma) | Not required |
| Lock-in problem | Yes (at low rotation rates) | No (uses broadband source) |
| Sensitivity to vibration | Lower | Higher (requires shielding) |
| Cost | Very high | High but lower than RLG |
| Size | Medium (cavity block) | Compact coil |

**Why FOG is preferred in marine AUVs (3 marks):**

1. **No high-voltage plasma tube** — FOGs use a low-power superluminescent diode. This is critical underwater, where high voltages risk leakage and arc-discharge in humid/saline environments.
2. **Immune to lock-in** — RLG suffers from "lock-in" at low rotation rates (the two beams lock to the same frequency below a threshold), making RLG blind to slow rotations. FOG, using a broadband source, has no lock-in threshold.
3. **Robust to shock and vibration** — AUVs experience pressure surges and engine vibrations; FOG's solid-state construction is more robust than RLG's precisely aligned mirrors.
4. **Long operational lifetime** — no plasma tube degradation, ideal for submerged missions lasting months.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Many students write precession rate as $\Omega_p = mg\ell / (I_s\omega_s)$ but forget to specify units. Always state **rad/s**.
> 2. **Direction of Coriolis force:** The force is perpendicular to BOTH the velocity and the rotation axis. Wrong direction → lose 1 mark.
> 3. **FOG vs RLG confusion:** FOG uses a *fiber coil and interferometric phase measurement*; RLG uses a *closed laser cavity and beat-frequency measurement*. Examiners often set traps here.
> 4. **Sagnac derivation skip:** Students jump from path difference to phase shift without showing $L_+$ and $L_-$ separately. Always derive both explicitly.
> 5. **Forgetting the $N$ turns factor:** In FOG, $N$ (number of fiber turns) appears in the final expression. Missing $N$ loses 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Drift explanation:** Whenever you mention "drift" in a gyro, explicitly state it arises from integration of bias: $\theta_{error}(t) \approx b_\omega \cdot t$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Heading Sensors: Gyroscopes**

- **Definition:** Gyroscope = heading/angular-rate sensor using angular momentum, Coriolis, or Sagnac effects.
- **Three foundational principles:**
  - Conservation of angular momentum (mechanical).
  - Coriolis effect (MEMS).
  - Sagnac effect (RLG and FOG).
- **Key formulas to memorize verbatim:**
  - $L = I\omega$
  - $\Omega_p = \dfrac{mg\ell}{I_s\omega_s}$
  - $F_{Cor} = 2m\Omega v_d$
  - $\Delta\phi = \dfrac{8\pi N A \Omega}{\lambda c}$
  - $\theta(t) = \theta_0 + \int_0^t \omega(\tau)\,d\tau$
- **MEMS working steps:** Drive mode → Coriolis force → Sense mode displacement → Capacitive detection → Digital output.
- **Sagnac working steps:** Source → Coupler splits → CW and CCW beams → Recombine at detector → Phase shift proportional to $\Omega$.
- **Bias drift problem:** Integration of bias causes linearly growing heading error over time; remedy is periodic calibration against absolute references (GPS, magnetometer).
- **Exam-writing template (full 7-mark answer):**
  1. *State the principle* (1 mark).
  2. *Draw labelled block diagram* (2 marks).
  3. *Derive governing equation* (2 marks).
  4. *Discuss advantages and limitations / applications* (2 marks).
- **Comparison priorities for KTU:** Mechanical vs MEMS vs RLG vs FOG — judge by bias stability, size, cost, and application.
- **Real-world anchors for answers:**
  - Aircraft AHRS → RLG.
  - Smartphone screen rotation → MEMS.
  - Submarine and AUV inertial navigation → FOG.
  - Vintage torpedo and gyrocompass → Mechanical.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IMUs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT735 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** IMUs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU) — KTU 2024 Definition**
> An **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)** is an electronic sensor module that measures and reports a body's **specific force** (non-gravitational acceleration), **angular rate** (rotation), and — in 9-DoF variants — **orientation relative to the Earth's magnetic field**. It is the foundational proprioceptive sensor used in **dead-reckoning** for robot navigation.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, an IMU is treated as a **self-contained, non-referencing sensor suite** (no external signals like GPS required), making it indispensable for **GNSS-denied environments** such as indoor robots, subterranean drones, and underwater autonomous vehicles.

### 1.2 Anatomical Composition of an IMU

An IMU is composed of **two (or three) orthogonal sensor triads**, fused into a single rigid mechanical package:

| Sub-System | Physical Quantity Measured | SI Unit | Common Output |
|------------|----------------------------|---------|---------------|
| **Accelerometer** (3-axis) | **Specific Force** $\vec{f}$ | $m/s^2$ | Linear acceleration minus gravity |
| **Gyroscope** (3-axis) | **Angular Velocity** $\vec{\omega}$ | $rad/s$ | Rate of rotation in body frame |
| **Magnetometer** (3-axis) | **Magnetic Flux Density** $\vec{B}$ | $Tesla\ (T)$ or $\mu T$ | Heading reference (when present) |
| *(Optional)* **Barometer** | Atmospheric Pressure | $hPa$ | Altitude aiding |

> [!NOTE] **Specific Force vs. Acceleration**
> A common point of confusion: an IMU accelerometer does **NOT** measure true kinematic acceleration $\vec{a}$. It measures **specific force**, defined as $\vec{f} = \vec{a} - \vec{g}$, where $\vec{g}$ is the local gravitational acceleration. This is why a stationary IMU on a table reads **$+9.81\ m/s^2$ along the upward axis** — the table exerts a normal force to counteract gravity.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Inner Ear of a Robot" 🎯

Imagine you are blindfolded and placed inside a smooth, silent, soundproof moving vehicle. You cannot see the outside world. To navigate, you have **two natural sensors**:

- Your **inner ear's vestibular system** (semicircular canals + otoliths) tells you which way you are tilting and accelerating.
- Your **proprioceptive nerves** tell you how your limbs are moving.

An **IMU is exactly this for a robot** — it is the robot's "inner ear." The **gyroscope** acts like the semicircular canals (sensing rotation), and the **accelerometer** acts like the otoliths (sensing linear specific force). Just as you cannot walk a perfect straight line blindfolded (errors accumulate over time — this is called **drift**), an IMU's integrated position also drifts, which is why IMU data is almost always fused with other sensors (wheel odometry, GPS, LiDAR SLAM) in real robots.

### 1.4 Classification of IMU Grades

KTU examiners frequently test the grade distinction, as it dictates cost, accuracy, and application:

> [!IMPORTANT] **IMU Performance Grades**
> - **Navigation Grade** — Drift $< 0.01\ ^\circ/hr$; used in submarines, aircraft; cost $> \$50{,}000$.
> - **Tactical Grade** — Drift $\approx 1\text{–}10\ ^\circ/hr$; used in guided missiles, UAVs; cost $\sim \$10{,}000$.
> - **Industrial Grade** — Drift $\approx 10\text{–}100\ ^\circ/hr$; used in robotics, smartphones; cost $\sim \$100$.
> - **Consumer / MEMS Grade** — Drift $> 100\ ^\circ/hr$; used in wearables, toys; cost $< \$10$.

### 1.5 MEMS vs. Classical IMU Architecture

- **Classical IMUs** use spinning-mass gyros and mechanical accelerometers (large, heavy, expensive).
- **MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems) IMUs** use silicon proof-masses on suspended flexures; the deflection under Coriolis or inertial force is capacitively measured. These dominate modern robotics due to small size, low cost, and low power.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Reference Coordinate Frames (Critical for KTU)

Every IMU equation is meaningless without specifying **in which frame** the vector lives. KTU examiners routinely ask for the frame definitions.

| Frame | Notation | Origin / Axes | Used For |
|-------|----------|---------------|----------|
| **Inertial (Earth-Centered)** | $i$-frame | Earth's center; non-rotating | Theoretical Newton's law reference |
| **Earth (ECEF/NED)** | $e$-frame | Vehicle start point; North-East-Down | Navigation output |
| **Body (Sensor) Frame** | $b$-frame | IMU center; x-forward, y-right, z-down | Raw sensor output |
