| **Navigation Frame** | $n$-frame | Local North-East-Down | Stable reference for control |

> [!NOTE] **Frame Transformation Rule**
> A vector expressed in the body frame can be transformed to the navigation frame via the **Direction Cosine Matrix (DCM)** $\mathbf{C}_b^n$ (a $3\times 3$ rotation matrix):
> $$\vec{v}^n = \mathbf{C}_b^n \, \vec{v}^b$$
> where $\mathbf{C}_b^n$ is constructed from the roll ($\phi$), pitch ($\theta$), and yaw ($\psi$) Euler angles.

## 2.2 Gyroscope Measurement Model

A real gyroscope does not output the *true* angular velocity. The output $\tilde{\vec{\omega}}_{ib}^b$ is corrupted by bias, scale-factor error, and noise:

$$\tilde{\vec{\omega}}_{ib}^b = \vec{\omega}_{ib}^b + \vec{b}_g + \mathbf{S}_g \, \vec{\omega}_{ib}^b + \vec{n}_g$$

Where each term carries physical meaning:

- $\vec{\omega}_{ib}^b$ — **True angular velocity** of the body relative to the inertial frame, expressed in body axes.
- $\vec{b}_g$ — **Gyroscope bias** (turn-on bias + in-run bias drift); slowly time-varying.
- $\mathbf{S}_g$ — **Scale-factor and cross-coupling matrix** (manufacturing imperfection).
- $\vec{n}_g$ — **Angle Random Walk (ARW)** — high-frequency white noise.

## 2.3 Accelerometer Measurement Model

The accelerometer measures **specific force**, not acceleration. The model is:

$$\tilde{\vec{f}}^b = \vec{f}^b + \vec{b}_a + \mathbf{S}_a \, \vec{f}^b + \vec{n}_a$$

with $\vec{f}^b = \mathbf{C}_n^b \left(\vec{a}^n - \vec{g}^n\right)$. The terms are analogous: bias $\vec{b}_a$, scale-factor $\mathbf{S}_a$, and **Velocity Random Walk (VRW)** noise $\vec{n}_a$.

## 2.4 The Strapdown Navigation Equations (KTU Heart of the Topic)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Strapdown vs. Gimbaled IMUs**
> A **gimbaled IMU** mechanically isolates sensors on a stabilized platform (no rotation math needed). A **strapdown IMU** mounts sensors rigidly to the vehicle body, so the **math is done in software**. Modern robotics uses strapdown exclusively.

The strapdown equations form a coupled set of three ODEs that the navigation computer integrates at the IMU sample rate (typically 100–1000 Hz):

### Step 1 — Attitude Update
The orientation evolves according to:
$$\dot{\mathbf{C}}_b^n = \mathbf{C}_b^n \, [\vec{\omega}_{nb}^b]_\times$$

where $[\vec{\omega}_{nb}^b]_\times$ is the **skew-symmetric cross-product matrix** of the corrected angular velocity.

The **Earth-rate and transport-rate** must be removed from the raw gyro reading to obtain $\vec{\omega}_{nb}^b$:
$$\vec{\omega}_{nb}^b = \vec{\omega}_{ib}^b - \mathbf{C}_n^b (\vec{\omega}_{ie}^n + \vec{\omega}_{en}^n)$$

- $\vec{\omega}_{ie}^n$ = Earth's rotation rate = $7.2921 \times 10^{-5}\ rad/s$ (often negligible for short-duration robot runs).
- $\vec{\omega}_{en}^n$ = transport rate due to vehicle motion over curved Earth (negligible indoors).

### Step 2 — Velocity Update
$$\dot{\vec{v}}^n = \mathbf{C}_b^n \, \vec{f}^b + \vec{g}^n$$

### Step 3 — Position Update
$$\dot{\vec{p}}^n = \vec{v}^n$$

These three equations constitute the **strapdown mechanization**. KTU exams often ask students to draw or describe this block.

## 2.5 Quaternion-Based Attitude Update (Preferred Form)

To avoid **gimbal-lock** singularities in Euler angles, modern IMUs use **quaternions** $\mathbf{q} = [q_0, q_1, q_2, q_3]^T$:

$$\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \frac{1}{2} \, \mathbf{q} \otimes \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ \vec{\omega}_{nb}^b \end{bmatrix}$$

The discrete-time update is:
$$\mathbf{q}_{k+1} = \frac{\mathbf{q}_k + \frac{\Delta t}{2} \, \Omega(\vec{\omega}_k) \, \mathbf{q}_k}{\left\lVert \mathbf{q}_k + \frac{\Delta t}{2} \, \Omega(\vec{\omega}_k) \, \mathbf{q}_k \right\rVert}$$

where $\Omega(\vec{\omega})$ is the $4 \times 4$ skew-symmetric matrix and the denominator enforces unit-norm constraint.

## 2.6 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE] The following table consolidates every high-yield equation. **All absolute-value bars and norms are rendered as `\vert` or `\Vert` to keep the table syntax intact.**

| # | Equation | Physical Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|----------|------------------|--------------|
| 1 | $\vec{f} = \vec{a} - \vec{g}$ | Specific force definition | $m/s^2$ |
| 2 | $\tilde{\vec{\omega}}_{ib}^b = \vec{\omega}_{ib}^b + \vec{b}_g + \vec{n}_g$ | Gyro measurement model | $rad/s$ |
| 3 | $\tilde{\vec{f}}^b = \vec{f}^b + \vec{b}_a + \vec{n}_a$ | Accelerometer measurement model | $m/s^2$ |
| 4 | $\dot{\mathbf{C}}_b^n = \mathbf{C}_b^n [\vec{\omega}_{nb}^b]_\times$ | Attitude DCM propagation | $rad/s$ |
| 5 | $\vec{\omega}_{nb}^b = \vec{\omega}_{ib}^b - \mathbf{C}_n^b(\vec{\omega}_{ie}^n + \vec{\omega}_{en}^n)$ | Body-to-nav angular rate | $rad/s$ |
| 6 | $\dot{\vec{v}}^n = \mathbf{C}_b^n \vec{f}^b + \vec{g}^n$ | Velocity update | $m/s^2$ |
| 7 | $\dot{\vec{p}}^n = \vec{v}^n$ | Position update | $m/s$ |
| 8 | $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \frac{1}{2} \mathbf{q} \otimes \tilde{\vec{\omega}}_q$ | Quaternion kinematics | unitless |
| 9 | $\sigma_{ARW} = \sqrt{q^2 \cdot \Delta t}$ | Angle Random Walk integration | $^\circ / \sqrt{hr}$ |
| 10 | $\sigma_{BI} = \sigma_{FBS} + \dot{b} \cdot t$ | Bias Instability model | $^\circ / hr$ |

## 2.7 Allan Variance — Characterizing IMU Noise

> [!IMPORTANT] Allan Variance is the **KTU-favoured method** to characterize IMU noise. It plots $\sigma(\tau)$ versus averaging time $\tau$ on a log-log plot, revealing five distinct noise regimes:
> - **Quantization Noise** (short $\tau$) — limits resolution.
> - **Angle/Velocity Random Walk** — white noise floor.
> - **Bias Instability** (the "hump") — flicker noise floor; best performance point.
> - **Rate Random Walk** — random walk of bias.
> - **Rate Ramp** — deterministic drift.

The minimum of the Allan deviation curve defines the **Bias Instability (BI)**, a key datasheet parameter.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

IMUs are the workhorse sensor in:

1. **Aerial Robotics** — Quadrotor drones rely on IMU + Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) at 500 Hz to maintain attitude stability.
2. **Autonomous Vehicles** — IMU provides high-frequency motion prediction between sparse GPS updates.
3. **Humanoid Robots** — IMU in the torso combined with joint encoders enables zero-moment-point (ZMP) balance control.
4. **AR/VR Headsets** — IMU enables 6-DoF head tracking at low latency.
5. **Underwater ROVs** — IMU is the primary dead-reckoning sensor when GPS is unavailable.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT] **Derivation 1 — From Specific Force to Velocity and Position (the Nav-Equation Chain)**

### Setup

We wish to derive the kinematic relation that transforms the accelerometer's specific-force reading into navigation-frame velocity and position. This derivation is exhaustively required in KTU Part-B (14-mark) answers.

### Step A — Start from Newton's Second Law in the Inertial Frame

In the inertial frame, the true kinematic acceleration of the vehicle's center of mass obeys:

$$\vec{a}^i = \frac{d^2 \vec{r}^i}{dt^2}$$

where $\vec{r}^i$ is the position vector expressed in the inertial frame.

### Step B — Introduce Gravity and Recognize the Accelerometer Output

The accelerometer is a force sensor; it measures the non-gravitational force per unit mass acting on its proof-mass. Therefore the accelerometer reading $\vec{f}$ is related to the inertial acceleration by:

$$\vec{f} = \vec{a}^i - \vec{g}^i$$

Rearranging for the true acceleration:

$$\vec{a}^i = \vec{f} + \vec{g}^i$$

> This is the **first key result**: an accelerometer reading alone gives $\vec{a}^i$ only after adding back the gravitational vector.

### Step C — Project into the Navigation Frame

Because the navigation frame $n$ rotates relative to the inertial frame $i$ (with rate $\vec{\omega}_{in}^n$), we must use the **transport theorem**. For any vector $\vec{x}^n$:

$$\left.\frac{d\vec{x}}{dt}\right|_i = \left.\frac{d\vec{x}}{dt}\right|_n + \vec{\omega}_{in}^n \times \vec{x}^n$$

Applying this to the velocity $\vec{v}^n$ (velocity of the body w.r.t. Earth, expressed in the nav frame):

$$\vec{a}^i = \dot{\vec{v}}^n + \vec{\omega}_{in}^n \times \vec{v}^n - \vec{g}^n$$

(The minus sign on gravity appears because we conventionally express $\vec{g}^n$ as the gravity vector acting downward, while the term $\vec{\omega}_{ie}^n \times \vec{v}^n$ and $\vec{\omega}_{en}^n \times \vec{v}^n$ are folded into $\vec{\omega}_{in}^n$.)

### Step D — Solve for $\dot{\vec{v}}^n$

Substituting $\vec{a}^i = \vec{f} + \vec{g}^i$ from Step B:

$$\dot{\vec{v}}^n = \mathbf{C}_b^n \vec{f}^b - (2\vec{\omega}_{ie}^n + \vec{\omega}_{en}^n) \times \vec{v}^n + \vec{g}^n$$

> This is the **strapdown velocity differential equation**. The cross-product terms are the **Coriolis correction** ($2\vec{\omega}_{ie}^n \times \vec{v}^n$) and the **centripetal/transport correction** ($\vec{\omega}_{en}^n \times \vec{v}^n$). For indoor mobile robots with $v \le 1\ m/s$ and $\tau < 1$ min, both correction terms are typically below $10^{-4}\ m/s^2$ and may be safely neglected.

### Step E — Integrate to Obtain Velocity and Position

The final strapdown mechanization, **suitable for KTU board-answer writing**, is the triplet:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{\mathbf{C}}_b^n &= \mathbf{C}_b^n [\vec{\omega}_{nb}^b]_\times \\
\dot{\vec{v}}^n &= \mathbf{C}_b^n \vec{f}^b + \vec{g}^n - (2\vec{\omega}_{ie}^n + \vec{\omega}_{en}^n) \times \vec{v}^n \\
\dot{\vec{p}}^n &= \vec{v}^n
\end{aligned}$$

### Step F — Discrete-Time Implementation (What the Processor Actually Computes)

With IMU sample period $\Delta t$, a simple first-order Euler integrator gives:

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{C}_{b,k+1}^n &= \mathbf{C}_{b,k}^n \left( \mathbf{I}_{3 \times 3} + \Delta t \, [\vec{\omega}_{nb,k}^b]_\times \right) \\
\vec{v}_{k+1}^n &= \vec{v}_k^n + \Delta t \left( \mathbf{C}_{b,k}^n \vec{f}_k^b + \vec{g}^n \right) \\
\vec{p}_{k+1}^n &= \vec{p}_k^n + \Delta t \, \vec{v}_k^n
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE] For high-dynamic applications, KTU accepts mention of **higher-order integrators** such as **Runge-Kutta 4th order (RK4)** or **trapezoidal integration** to reduce discretization error.

---

> [!IMPORTANT] **Derivation 2 — Quaternion Update from Angular Velocity**

### Setup

We derive how a small body rotation $\Delta \vec{\theta}$ over interval $\Delta t$ updates the quaternion.

### Step A — Express a Small Rotation as a Quaternion

A rotation of angle $\Delta\theta = \Vert \vec{\theta} \Vert$ about unit axis $\hat{u}$ corresponds to the quaternion:

$$\Delta \mathbf{q} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos(\Delta\theta/2) \\ \hat{u} \, \sin(\Delta\theta/2) \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step B — Apply the First-Order Small-Angle Approximation

For $\Delta t$ small (e.g., $\le 0.01\ s$), $\Delta\theta \approx \Vert \vec{\omega} \Vert \Delta t$ is small, so:

$$\cos(\Delta\theta/2) \approx 1, \quad \sin(\Delta\theta/2) \approx \Delta\theta/2$$

Therefore:

$$\Delta \mathbf{q} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ \vec{\omega} \, \Delta t / 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step C — Compose with the Current Quaternion

Quaternion composition $\mathbf{q}_{k+1} = \Delta \mathbf{q} \otimes \mathbf{q}_k$ yields:

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{q}_{k+1} &\approx \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ \tfrac{1}{2} \vec{\omega}_k \Delta t \end{bmatrix} \otimes \mathbf{q}_k \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} q_{0,k} - \tfrac{\Delta t}{2} (q_{1,k}\omega_x + q_{2,k}\omega_y + q_{3,k}\omega_z) \\
\quad q_{1,k} + \tfrac{\Delta t}{2} (q_{0,k}\omega_x + q_{2,k}\omega_z - q_{3,k}\omega_y) \\
\quad q_{2,k} + \tfrac{\Delta t}{2} (q_{0,k}\omega_y + q_{3,k}\omega_x - q_{1,k}\omega_z) \\
\quad q_{3,k} + \tfrac{\Delta t}{2} (q_{0,k}\omega_z + q_{1,k}\omega_y - q_{2,k}\omega_x) \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step D — Normalize to Preserve Unit Length

Numerical drift slowly corrupts $\Vert \mathbf{q} \Vert$. Renormalization is mandatory:

$$\mathbf{q}_{k+1} \leftarrow \frac{\mathbf{q}_{k+1}}{\Vert \mathbf{q}_{k+1} \Vert}$$

> This completes the derivation of the standard strapdown quaternion update — a **high-frequency KTU favorite**.

---

> [!IMPORTANT] **Derivation 3 — Euler-Angle Rate Equations (for 3-2-1 ZYX sequence)**

The Euler-angle rate vector $\dot{\vec{\Theta}} = [\dot{\phi}, \dot{\theta}, \dot{\psi}]^T$ relates to the body angular velocity $\vec{\omega}_{nb}^b = [p, q, r]^T$ via:

$$\begin{bmatrix} p \\ q \\ r \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -\sin\theta \\ 0 & \cos\phi & \sin\phi \cos\theta \\ 0 & -\sin\phi & \cos\phi \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{\phi} \\ \dot{\theta} \\ \dot{\psi} \end{bmatrix}$$

Inverting the matrix (provided $\cos\theta \neq 0$):

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{\phi} &= p + (q \sin\phi + r \cos\phi) \tan\theta \\
\dot{\theta} &= q \cos\phi - r \sin\phi \\
\dot{\psi} &= (q \sin\phi + r \cos\phi) \sec\theta
\end{aligned}$$

> The singularities at $\theta = \pm 90^\circ$ are the famous **gimbal-lock** problem — the chief reason quaternions are preferred in production IMU code.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Error Budget for a MEMS IMU (Worked Numerical Example)

> A KTU 14-mark question frequently gives a numerical budget and asks the student to compute drift. Let us quantify the position drift of a stationary consumer-grade IMU left running for **$t = 60\ s$** with the following (typical) specs:

| Parameter | Symbol | Value |
|-----------|--------|-------|
| Accelerometer bias | $b_a$ | $0.5\ mg = 4.905 \times 10^{-3}\ m/s^2$ |
| Gyroscope bias | $b_g$ | $5\ ^\circ/hr = 2.42 \times 10^{-5}\ rad/s$ |
| Sample period | $\Delta t$ | $0.01\ s$ |

### Position Drift due to Accelerometer Bias

Because gravity is removed via the DCM, a constant bias in $\vec{f}^b$ projects as a constant acceleration error in the nav frame:

$$\Delta a = b_a = 4.905 \times 10^{-3}\ m/s^2$$

After $t = 60\ s$:

$$\Delta p_a = \frac{1}{2} b_a t^2 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 4.905 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 3600 \approx 8.83\ m$$

### Heading Error Growth due to Gyro Bias

Integrating bias gives a heading error $\Delta \psi = b_g t = 2.42 \times 10^{-5} \cdot 60 = 1.45 \times 10^{-3}\ rad \approx 0.083^\circ$.

A $0.083^\circ$ heading error, projected over a $60\ s$ travel distance, yields a cross-track error of:

$$\Delta p_\psi = v \cdot \sin(\Delta\psi) \cdot t \approx v \cdot \Delta \psi \cdot t$$

For a robot moving at $v = 1\ m/s$:

$$\Delta p_\psi \approx 1 \cdot 1.45 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 60 \approx 0.087\ m$$

> **Conclusion:** Accelerometer bias dominates *position* drift ($\sim 8.8\ m$), while gyro bias dominates *orientation* drift. This is why **sensor fusion with absolute aiding (GPS, UWB, LiDAR)** is non-negotiable in real robots.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🖼️ SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IMU Internal Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[3-Axis MEMS Proof Mass] --> B[Capacitive Sense Frontend]
    B --> C[Analog Front End Amplifier]
    C --> D[Sigma-Delta ADC]
    D --> E[Digital Signal Processor DSP]
    E --> F[Temperature Compensation LUT]
    F --> G[SPI / I2C Output Register]
    H[3-Axis Gyro Coriolis Structure] --> I[Drive and Sense ASIC]
    I --> D
    G --> J[Host Microcontroller]
    J --> K[Navigation Filter EKF]
    K --> L((Pose Estimate x y z roll pitch yaw))
```

**Reading the diagram:** The proof-mass and Coriolis structures share a common ADC and DSP pipeline, which is why modern IMU ICs (e.g., BMI088, MPU-9250) report synchronized accelerometer + gyro samples.

## 4.2 Strapdown Navigation Computation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw IMU Sample f_b and omega_ib_b at t_k] --> B[Subtract Bias and Noise]
    B --> C[Compute omega_nb_b]
    C --> D[Update Quaternion q k to q k plus 1]
    D --> E[Renormalize q k plus 1]
    E --> F[Extract DCM C b to n from q]
    F --> G[Transform Specific Force to Nav Frame]
    G --> H[Add Gravity and Remove Coriolis]
    H --> I[Integrate to Update Velocity v]
    I --> J[Integrate to Update Position p]
    J --> K((Output Pose p v q at t k plus 1))
    K --> A
```

> [!NOTE] This loop executes at the **IMU sample rate** (typically 100–1000 Hz). At each iteration, the previous pose estimate is used to rotate the new measurement into the navigation frame.

## 4.3 Coordinate Frame Transformation Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A((Inertial Frame i)) -- omega_ie --> B((Earth Frame e))
    B -- omega_en --> C((Navigation Frame n local NED))
    C -- omega_nb --> D((Body Frame b sensor mounted))
    D -- omega_bp --> E((Platform or Wheel Frame))
    style A fill:#e0f2ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style C fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900
    style D fill:#ffe0e0,stroke:#cc0000
```

**KTU Tip:** When asked "list the angular rates involved in strapdown mechanization," mention **$\omega_{ie}$ (Earth rotation), $\omega_{en}$ (transport rate), and $\omega_{nb}$ (body rate w.r.t. nav frame)** with explicit frame subscripts.

## 4.4 Allan Deviation Curve (Conceptual Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Short tau: Quantization Noise slope -1"] --> B["ARW region slope -1/2"]
    B --> C["Bias Instability hump flat minimum"]
    C --> D["Rate Random Walk slope +1/2"]
    D --> E["Rate Ramp slope +1"]
    style A fill:#f0f0f0
    style C fill:#ffd966,stroke:#bf9000
    style C fill:#ffd966,stroke:#bf9000
```

**Reading the diagram:** The flat minimum (highlighted in yellow) of the $\sigma(\tau)$ curve identifies the **Bias Instability (BI)** — a single number on the IMU datasheet that quantifies its long-term drift performance.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 🅰️ PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`
**Define an Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU). List its main components and state the physical quantities each measures.**

**Model Answer:**

An **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)** is a self-contained electronic sensor module that measures a body's **specific force** and **angular velocity**, and is the foundational proprioceptive sensor for robot navigation.

**Main components and measured quantities:**
1. **3-axis accelerometer** — measures **specific force** $\vec{f} = \vec{a} - \vec{g}$ in $m/s^2$.
2. **3-axis gyroscope** — measures **angular velocity** $\vec{\omega}$ in $rad/s$.
3. *(Optional 9-DoF)* **3-axis magnetometer** — measures Earth's magnetic flux density in $\mu T$ to provide absolute heading.
4. *(Optional aiding)* **Barometer / temperature sensor** — provides pressure altitude and thermal compensation.

*Standard 3-mark mapping: (1) Definition 1 m, (2) Components list 1 m, (3) Units and quantities 1 m.*

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`
**Distinguish between a strapdown IMU and a gimbaled IMU. Which is preferred in modern robotics and why?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | **Strapdown IMU** | **Gimbaled IMU** |
|--------|-------------------|------------------|
| Mounting | Sensors rigidly fixed to vehicle body | Sensors mounted on a stabilized, motor-driven platform |
| Isolation | No mechanical isolation from vehicle motion | Platform isolates sensors from rotations |
| Computation | All attitude math done in software via DCM/quaternion updates | Math is trivial (sensors stay aligned with nav frame) |
| Size, weight, cost | Small, light, cheap | Large, heavy, expensive |
| Reliability | High (no moving parts) | Lower (gimbals can jam) |
| Accuracy (raw) | Lower (sensors see full dynamics) | Higher |

**Modern robotics prefers strapdown IMUs** because of their small size, low cost, high reliability, and the availability of powerful embedded processors capable of running the strapdown navigation equations in real time at $\ge 400\ Hz$.

---

## 🅱️ PART B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice: A or B)

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> Students frequently lose 3–5 marks on this topic because they: (1) confuse *acceleration* with *specific force*, (2) omit the *Earth rotation* and *Coriolis* terms, (3) fail to write the matrix form of the strapdown equation, or (4) skip units and frame subscripts. **Always write the frame subscripts $^b$, $^n$, $^i$ explicitly.**

---

### ✍️ **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the strapdown navigation equations starting from Newton's second law. Clearly state the **attitude**, **velocity**, and **position** update laws and identify the role of gravity and Coriolis terms. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A consumer-grade MEMS IMU has an accelerometer bias of $b_a = 0.3\ mg$ and a gyroscope bias of $b_g = 10\ ^\circ/hr$. Compute the position drift after $t = 100\ s$ assuming the IMU remains stationary. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

#### 📚 Model Solution for Q.A(a)

**Step 1 — Inertial-frame Newton law:**
$$\vec{a}^i = \frac{d^2 \vec{r}^i}{dt^2}$$

**Step 2 — Specific force relation:**
$$\vec{a}^i = \vec{f} + \vec{g}^i$$

**Step 3 — Transport-theorem transformation to navigation frame:**
$$\dot{\vec{v}}^n = \mathbf{C}_b^n \vec{f}^b - (2\vec{\omega}_{ie}^n + \vec{\omega}_{en}^n) \times \vec{v}^n + \vec{g}^n$$

**Step 4 — Attitude and position kinematics:**
$$\dot{\mathbf{C}}_b^n = \mathbf{C}_b^n [\vec{\omega}_{nb}^b]_\times, \quad \dot{\vec{p}}^n = \vec{v}^n$$

**Step 5 — Body-rate correction:**
$$\vec{\omega}_{nb}^b = \vec{\omega}_{ib}^b - \mathbf{C}_n^b (\vec{\omega}_{ie}^n + \vec{\omega}_{en}^n)$$

> **Mark split:** (i) Newton's law & specific force 2 m, (ii) Transport theorem 2 m, (iii) Coupled ODE triplet 2 m, (iv) Earth-rate identification 1 m.

#### 📚 Model Solution for Q.A(b)

**Given:**
- $b_a = 0.3\ mg = 0.3 \times 9.81 \times 10^{-3} = 2.943 \times 10^{-3}\ m/s^2$
- $b_g = 10\ ^\circ/hr = \dfrac{10\pi}{180 \times 3600}\ rad/s = 4.848 \times 10^{-5}\ rad/s$
- $t = 100\ s$

**Acceleration drift from accelerometer bias (with gravity correctly removed, residual bias still integrates):**

After gravity compensation, a residual accelerometer bias $\Delta a = b_a$ remains, leading to position error:

$$\Delta p_a = \frac{1}{2} b_a t^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 2.943 \times 10^{-3} \times (100)^2$$

$$\Delta p_a = \frac{1}{2} \times 2.943 \times 10^{-3} \times 10000 = 14.715\ m$$

**Heading drift from gyroscope bias:**

$$\Delta \psi = b_g \cdot t = 4.848 \times 10^{-5} \times 100 = 4.848 \times 10^{-3}\ rad \approx 0.278^\circ$$

**Conclusion (Comment):**
A consumer-grade MEMS IMU drifts by **$\sim 14.7\ m$ in 100 s** even while stationary. This is unacceptably large for any robot that needs accurate localization, demonstrating that **standalone IMU navigation is impractical** for long-duration missions. Real robotic systems therefore **fuse IMU data with absolute sensors (GPS, LiDAR SLAM, wheel odometry, UWB)** using filters such as the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** or **Error-State Kalman Filter (ESKF)**.

> **Mark split:** (i) Unit conversion 2 m, (ii) Formula application 2 m, (iii) Numerical result 1 m, (iv) Engineering comment 2 m.

---

### ✍️ **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat diagram the working principle of a **MEMS accelerometer**. Compare it with a MEMS gyroscope in terms of the underlying physical principle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the aid of the **Allan Deviation plot**, describe the different noise regimes that characterize an IMU. Mention the formula for **Angle Random Walk (ARW)**. **(7 marks)**

#### 📚 Model Solution for Q.B(a)

**MEMS Accelerometer — Working Principle:**

A MEMS accelerometer consists of a silicon **proof-mass** suspended by compliant flexures between fixed outer plates. Under acceleration, the proof-mass displaces by $\Delta x$ relative to the frame; the displacement is detected as a change in differential capacitance $C_1 - C_2$ between interdigitated finger electrodes.

By Newton's law: $m \cdot a = k \cdot \Delta x + c \cdot \dot{\Delta x}$, where $k$ is the flexure stiffness and $c$ is damping. The steady-state displacement is:

$$\Delta x = \frac{m \cdot a}{k}$$

This capacitance change is amplified, digitized via a sigma-delta ADC, and reported as acceleration.

> *(Include ASCII diagram if needed:)*
> ```
>   |---k---[M]---k---|
>   |  C1    /\    C2 |
>   |_______/  \______|
>         Proof-mass
> ```

**MEMS Gyroscope — Working Principle (Coriolis Effect):**

A MEMS gyroscope drives a proof-mass into **linear oscillation** along the drive axis at frequency $\omega_d$. When the entire structure rotates with angular rate $\Omega$, the proof-mass experiences a **Coriolis force**:

$$\vec{F}_c = 2 m \, \vec{v}_{drive} \times \vec{\Omega}$$

This force is perpendicular to both the drive velocity and the rotation axis; it deflects the mass along the sense axis, which is again detected capacitively. The sense displacement is proportional to the input angular rate.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | MEMS Accelerometer | MEMS Gyroscope |
|--------|--------------------|----------------|
| Physical principle | Newton's 2nd law (inertial force) | Coriolis effect |
| Measured quantity | Specific force $\vec{f}$ | Angular velocity $\vec{\Omega}$ |
| Drive requirement | None (passive) | Active drive oscillator required |
| Bias stability | Typically better (mg level) | Worse (deg/hr level) |
| Noise term | Velocity Random Walk | Angle Random Walk |

> **Mark split:** (i) Accelerometer principle with diagram 3 m, (ii) Coriolis principle 2 m, (iii) Comparison 2 m.

#### 📚 Model Solution for Q.B(b)

**Allan Deviation — Five Noise Regimes:**

The Allan deviation $\sigma(\tau)$ is computed by averaging the IMU output over windows of length $\tau$ and plotting $\sigma$ versus $\tau$ on a log-log scale. Each segment has a characteristic slope and identifies a specific noise source:

| # | Regime | Log-Log Slope | Physical Cause | Mitigation |
|---|--------|---------------|----------------|------------|
| 1 | Quantization Noise | $-1$ | ADC finite resolution | Use higher-resolution ADC |
| 2 | Angle / Velocity Random Walk | $-1/2$ | White noise in sensor electronics | Improve SNR; averaging |
| 3 | Bias Instability (Flicker floor) | $0$ (flat minimum) | 1/f noise; electronic drift | Better components, calibration |
| 4 | Rate Random Walk | $+1/2$ | Random walk of bias | Difficult; environment control |
| 5 | Rate Ramp | $+1$ | Deterministic sensor drift | Factory calibration |

**Angle Random Walk (ARW) Formula:**

The ARW parameter $N$ (in $^\circ/\sqrt{hr}$ for gyros, $m/s/\sqrt{hr}$ for accelerometers) is the value of $\sigma(\tau)$ at $\tau = 1\ hr$ in the ARW region:

$$\sigma_{ARW}(\tau) = \frac{N}{\sqrt{\tau}}$$

Equivalently, the integrated angle noise after integration time $T$ is:

$$\sigma_\theta(T) = N \cdot \sqrt{T}$$

A typical consumer-grade gyroscope has $N \approx 0.1\ ^\circ/\sqrt{hr}$, whereas a tactical-grade gyro has $N \approx 0.005\ ^\circ/\sqrt{hr}$.

> **Mark split:** (i) Allan deviation concept 2 m, (ii) Five regimes table 3 m, (iii) ARW formula 2 m.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid-Revision Checklist — IMUs**
>
> - **IMU** = 3-axis accelerometer + 3-axis gyroscope (± optional magnetometer).
> - **Accelerometer** measures **specific force** $\vec{f} = \vec{a} - \vec{g}$, NOT true acceleration.
> - **Gyroscope** measures **angular velocity** $\vec{\omega}$ in the **body frame** $b$.
> - **Strapdown** is preferred over **gimbaled** in modern robotics (small, cheap, software-does-math).
> - **Three strapdown equations** are: attitude update, velocity update, position update — coupled, must be integrated simultaneously.
> - **Quaternion** is preferred over **Euler angles** to avoid gimbal lock at pitch $= \pm 90^\circ$.
> - **Earth rotation rate** $\omega_{ie} = 7.2921 \times 10^{-5}\ rad/s$; often negligible indoors.
> - **Coriolis term** $2\vec{\omega}_{ie}^n \times \vec{v}^n$ must appear in the velocity equation for full-credit answers.
> - **Bias Instability** is read from the **minimum** of the Allan deviation curve.
> - **ARW** has units $^\circ/\sqrt{hr}$ (gyro) or $m/s/\sqrt{hr}$ (accel); it equals $\sigma(\tau=1\,hr)$.
> - **MEMS accelerometer** uses **Newton's 2nd law + capacitive sense**; **MEMS gyroscope** uses **Coriolis effect**.
> - **Standalone IMU drifts unboundedly** — $\Delta p = \frac{1}{2} b_a t^2$; must fuse with absolute aiding (GPS, LiDAR, UWB, wheel odometry).
> - **Magnetometer** provides absolute heading; cannot be used indoors due to steel rebar/electronics interference.
> - Always specify **frame subscripts** $^b$, $^n$, $^i$, $^e$ when writing vectors — KTU markers deduct marks for ambiguity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Distance Sensors - ToF
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT735 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Distance Sensors - ToF

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Time-of-Flight (ToF) Distance Sensor** is a non-contact ranging device that determines the distance between the sensor and a target object by precisely measuring the time interval (Δt) required for a known electromagnetic or acoustic wavefront to propagate from the emitter, reflect off the target, and return to the receiver. The distance is then computed as:

$$d = \frac{v \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

where $v$ is the propagation velocity of the emitted wave (typically the speed of light $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$ for optical ToF / LiDAR, or the speed of sound $\approx 343 \text{ m/s}$ for ultrasonic ToF).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In *Robot Navigation*, ToF sensors are classified under the family of **Active Range Sensors**, as they energize the environment with their own signal — distinguishing them from passive sensors (like cameras) which only receive ambient energy.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine shouting *"Echo!"* into a deep canyon. You instinctively measure the time between your shout and the returning echo, and your brain instantly guesses how far the canyon wall is. That is exactly how a ToF sensor works — except instead of a human voice, it uses an ultra-fast laser pulse or ultrasound burst, and instead of a brain, it uses a high-precision electronic stopwatch accurate to **picoseconds** (10⁻¹² s).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the factor of 2?** The signal must travel *to* the object AND *back* to the sensor — covering the distance **twice**. Forgetting the `/2` is the most common KTU exam mistake!

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Speed of light in vacuum | $c$ | $2.998 \times 10^8$ **m/s** |
| Speed of sound in air (20°C) | $v_s$ | **343 m/s** |
| 1 nanosecond (light travel) | — | 30 cm |
| 1 picosecond (light travel) | — | 0.3 mm |
| Typical ToF range | $d$ | **0.1 m – 250 m** |
| Typical accuracy | $\sigma_d$ | ±**1 cm to ±5 cm** |
| Refresh / scan rate | $f$ | **10 Hz – 100 kHz** |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic:** "Light is lazy" — even at 3×10⁸ m/s, light takes ~3.3 ns to travel 1 meter. This is why ToF electronics must be astonishingly fast.

## 1.4 Classification of ToF Sensors in Robotics

1. **Optical ToF (LiDAR)** — uses laser pulses; long range, high accuracy.
2. **Acoustic ToF (Ultrasonic)** — uses sound bursts; short range, low cost.
3. **RF ToF (Radar)** — uses radio waves; weather-immune, very long range.
4. **Direct ToF (dToF)** — measures the pulse time directly (true stopwatch method).
5. **Indirect ToF (iToF)** — measures the **phase shift** of an amplitude-modulated continuous wave (AMCW).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Emission Stage:** The transmitter (laser diode, LED, piezo, or antenna) launches a wavefront of known velocity $v$ and known waveform (pulse / modulated CW).
2. **Propagation Stage:** The wave travels through the medium (air, vacuum) toward the target. If the medium index of refraction is $n$, the effective velocity becomes $v = c/n$.
3. **Reflection Stage:** The wave strikes the target. A portion of the energy is **back-scattered** toward the receiver (Lambertian / specular / retroreflective behaviour).
4. **Detection Stage:** The receiver (photodiode, microphone, antenna) converts the returning wavefront into an electrical signal.
5. **Timing / Demodulation Stage:** A high-speed counter or correlator extracts $\Delta t$ (for dToF) or phase $\phi$ (for iToF).
6. **Computation Stage:** An onboard MCU / FPGA computes $d$ using the range equation and publishes it via UART, CAN, I²C, or ROS topic.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 'How' matters:** Each stage introduces error. In KTU problems, you are often asked to *identify the dominant error source* — emitter jitter, multipath reflection, or target reflectivity.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $d = \dfrac{v \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | Basic ToF range equation | Pulse-based dToF (LiDAR, Ultrasonic) |
| 2 | $d = \dfrac{c \cdot \phi}{4\pi f_{mod}}$ | Phase-shift range equation | AMCW iToF (PMD, Kinect v2-style) |
| 3 | $d_{max} = \dfrac{c}{2 f_{mod}}$ | Maximum unambiguous range (iToF) | Anti-aliasing for phase method |
| 4 | $\Delta d = \dfrac{c}{2} \Delta t$ | Range resolution (dToF) | Sensor datasheet parameter |
| 5 | $d_{max,pulse} = \dfrac{c \cdot T_{rep}}{2}$ | Maximum unambiguous range (dToF) | Pulse-repetition-limited LiDAR |
| 6 | $R_{SNR} \propto \dfrac{P_t \cdot A_r \cdot \rho}{d^4}$ | Radar / LiDAR range equation | Energy budget for link |
| 7 | $\sigma_d = \dfrac{c}{2} \sigma_t$ | Range error vs timing jitter | Error propagation |
| 8 | $v_{sound} = 331.3 + 0.606 \cdot T$ | Speed of sound vs temp (°C) | Ultrasonic temperature compensation |
| 9 | $f_{doppler} = \dfrac{2 v_r f_0}{c}$ | Doppler shift for moving target | FMCW Radar |
| 10 | $E_{pulse} = P_{peak} \cdot \tau_{pulse}$ | Pulse energy for eye-safety | Class-1 LiDAR design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Guard:** Always use the **two-way** formula. A one-way formula (without the `/2`) is used only in *synchronization* problems where two separate clocks communicate, not in ranging.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility & Real-World Applications

- **Autonomous Mobile Robots (AMRs):** 360° LiDAR sweeps for obstacle avoidance (e.g., ROS `scan` topic).
- **AGVs in warehouses:** Safety-rated ToF bumpers and proximity zones.
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** 3D LiDAR point clouds for object detection at 100+ m.
- **Drone Altimetry:** Optical ToF altimeters (VL53L series) for terrain following.
- **SLAM (Simultaneous Localization & Mapping):** 2D LiDAR + wheel odometry for gmapping / Cartographer.
- **Smartphones:** iPhone Face-ID, autofocus assist, AR depth.
- **Industrial Automation:** Bin picking, conveyor tracking, volume measurement.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Basic Time-of-Flight Range Equation (dToF)

**Given:** A LiDAR emits a laser pulse at $t = 0$ and receives the back-reflection at $t = \Delta t$. Light propagates with velocity $c$.

**Find:** The one-way range $d$.

**Step 1 — Express the total optical path length:**
The pulse travels from the sensor to the target, covers a distance $d$, then returns, covering another distance $d$. The total optical path length $L_{total}$ is therefore:

$$L_{total} = d + d = 2d$$

**Step 2 — Relate path length to velocity and time:**

$$L_{total} = c \cdot \Delta t$$

**Step 3 — Equate the two expressions:**

$$2d = c \cdot \Delta t$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $d$:**

$$d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute numerical constants for intuition:**
For $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s:

$$d = \frac{(3 \times 10^8) \cdot \Delta t}{2} = 1.5 \times 10^8 \cdot \Delta t \quad [\text{meters, with } \Delta t \text{ in seconds}]$$

> **Engineering Insight:** A 1-meter range requires $\Delta t \approx 6.67$ ns. To resolve 1 cm, the timing jitter must be $\sigma_t \le 67$ ps — pushing silicon toward **SPAD (Single-Photon Avalanche Diode)** technology.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Indirect ToF (Phase-Shift) Range Equation

**Given:** A continuous wave of frequency $f_{mod}$ is amplitude-modulated. The returning wave exhibits a phase shift $\phi$ relative to the emitted wave.

**Step 1 — Write the emitted wave (reference signal):**

$$s_{tx}(t) = A \cos(2\pi f_{mod} t)$$

**Step 2 — Write the received wave (delayed by $\Delta t$):**

$$s_{rx}(t) = A' \cos(2\pi f_{mod} (t - \Delta t) + \phi_0)$$

**Step 3 — Extract the phase delay due to round-trip travel:**

$$\phi = 2\pi f_{mod} \cdot \Delta t$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\Delta t$:**

$$\Delta t = \frac{\phi}{2\pi f_{mod}}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into the basic range equation:**

$$d = \frac{c}{2} \cdot \frac{\phi}{2\pi f_{mod}}$$

**Step 6 — Final compact form:**

$$d = \frac{c \cdot \phi}{4\pi f_{mod}}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** When $\phi$ wraps past $2\pi$, the measurement becomes ambiguous. This sets the *unambiguous range*:

$$d_{max} = \frac{c}{2 f_{mod}}$$

For $f_{mod} = 20$ MHz, $d_{max} \approx 7.5$ m — perfect for indoor robots but useless for outdoor autonomous cars (which need MHz-to-GHz modulated LiDAR or dToF).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Range Error Propagation

**Given:** The timing measurement has Gaussian jitter of standard deviation $\sigma_t$. Derive the corresponding range error $\sigma_d$.

**Step 1 — Range equation:**

$$d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate w.r.t. $\Delta t$ (error propagation):**

$$\delta d = \frac{c}{2} \cdot \delta(\Delta t)$$

**Step 3 — Apply standard deviation operator:**

$$\sigma_d = \frac{c}{2} \sigma_t$$

**Step 4 — Substitute typical values** ($\sigma_t = 50$ ps for a quality SPAD-based LiDAR):

$$\sigma_d = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-12}}{2} = 7.5 \text{ mm}$$

> **Conclusion:** Even a tiny 50 picosecond timing uncertainty yields ~7.5 mm ranging error — this is why **femtosecond-stable clocks** and **TCSPC (Time-Correlated Single-Photon Counting)** are central to high-end ToF.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A wheeled mobile robot uses an ultrasonic ToF sensor. The speed of sound at the operating temperature is $v_s = 343$ m/s. The measured round-trip time is $\Delta t = 12$ ms. Find:
(a) the distance to the obstacle,
(b) the corresponding range error if the timing jitter is $\sigma_t = 50 \ \mu$s.

**Solution:**

**(a) Range computation:**

$$d = \frac{v_s \cdot \Delta t}{2} = \frac{343 \cdot 12 \times 10^{-3}}{2}$$

$$d = \frac{4.116}{2} = 2.058 \text{ m}$$

**(b) Error computation:**

$$\sigma_d = \frac{v_s \cdot \sigma_t}{2} = \frac{343 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-6}}{2}$$

$$\sigma_d = \frac{0.01715}{2} = 8.575 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} \approx 8.58 \text{ mm}$$

> **Final Answer:** $d = 2.058 \pm 0.0086$ m.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ToF Sensing — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ctrlA[Controller MCU FPGA] --> driveA[Driver Stage]
    driveA --> emitA[Emitter Laser LED Piezo Antenna]
    emitA --> propA[Propagation Medium Air Vacuum]
    propA --> targA[Target Object Obstacle]
    targA --> backA[Back Scattered Wavefront]
    backA --> recvA[Receiver Photodiode Mic Antenna]
    recvA --> tdcA[Timing TDC or Phase Detector]
    tdcA --> procA[Signal Processor DSP]
    procA --> outA[Range Output UART CAN ROS Topic]
    procA --> ctrlA
```

## 4.2 ToF Sensor Functional Block Architecture (Detailed)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TransmitPath["Transmit Path"]
        oscA[Oscillator fmod] --> modA[Modulator AMCW]
        modA --> ampA[Power Amplifier]
        ampA --> emitA[Emitter Diode]
    end
    subgraph ReceivePath["Receive Path"]
        recvA[Receiver APD SPAD] --> lnaA[Transimpedance Amplifier]
        lnaA --> filtA[Bandpass Filter]
        filtA --> mixA[Mixer / Correlator]
    end
    subgraph ProcessingPath["Digital Processing"]
        tdcA[TDC Stop Watch] --> dspA[DSP Algorithm]
        dspA --> dspA
        dspA --> calA[Calibration Unit]
        calA --> ifcA[Interface I2C UART CAN]
    end
    modA -.Reference Signal.-> mixA
    emitA -->|Optical or Acoustic Pulse| mediumA[Free Space]
    mediumA -->|Reflected Echo| recvA
    mixA --> tdcA
    dspA --> ifcA
```

## 4.3 dToF vs iToF Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA{Need Long Range > 10m?} -->|Yes| dToFA[Choose dToF Pulse LiDAR]
    startA -->|No| needA{Need High Frame Rate > 100 fps?}
    needA -->|Yes| dToFB[Choose dToF SPAD Array]
    needA -->|No| costA{Budget < 10 USD per Unit?}
    costA -->|Yes| iToFA[Choose iToF VL53L Series]
    costA -->|No| precA{Need Sub-mm Precision?}
    precA -->|Yes| iToFB[Choose iToF PMD Modulated]
    precA -->|No| iToFC[Choose iToF Standard Modulation]
```

## 4.4 Error Source Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    errA[ToF Range Error Sources] --> sysA[Systematic Errors]
    errA --> randA[Random Errors]
    sysA --> calA1[Clock Drift]
    sysA --> calA2[Temperature Variation]
    sysA --> calA3[Multipath Reflection]
    randA --> jitA[Timing Jitter]
    randA --> shoA[Shot Noise Photon Statistics]
    randA --> backA[Background Light Interference]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Define Time-of-Flight (ToF) sensor. List two advantages and two disadvantages of using optical ToF sensors in mobile robot navigation.**

**Model Answer:**
A Time-of-Flight (ToF) sensor is an active ranging device that measures the time interval $\Delta t$ taken by an emitted wave (light or sound) to travel to a target and return, computing distance as $d = v \Delta t / 2$.

**Advantages (any two):**
- Long measurement range (up to 250 m for LiDAR).
- No moving mechanical parts in solid-state variants (high reliability).
- Direct geometric measurement (no parallax, unlike stereo vision).
- Operates in complete darkness.

**Disadvantages (any two):**
- Multipath interference degrades accuracy in cluttered environments.
- Susceptible to interference from sunlight or other LiDARs.
- Expensive compared to ultrasonic/IR.
- Eye-safety regulations limit peak laser power.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Compare Direct Time-of-Flight (dToF) and Indirect Time-of-Flight (iToF) techniques.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | dToF | iToF |
|---|---|---|
| Principle | Measures pulse time directly | Measures phase shift of modulated CW |
| Range | Long (up to hundreds of metres) | Short (typically < 10 m) |
| Precision at short range | Moderate (limited by pulse width) | Very high (sub-mm possible) |
| Electronics | Needs TDC, SPADs | Needs correlator, cheaper |
| Ambient light | Robust | Susceptible |
| Example | Automotive LiDAR | Kinect v2, VL53L series |

---

## PART B — Full 14-Mark Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — June 2024 style]*

**(a)** Derive the basic ToF range equation starting from the principle of constant wave propagation velocity. A 2D LiDAR mounted on a differential-drive robot measures $\Delta t = 660$ ns for the nearest obstacle. Compute the distance, assuming vacuum propagation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the four dominant error sources in an optical ToF system and derive the relationship between range error $\sigma_d$ and timing jitter $\sigma_t$. A ToF camera has $\sigma_t = 30$ ps. Calculate the resulting range uncertainty. **(7 marks)**

---

### **SOLUTION — Question A**

#### (a) [7 marks]

**Derivation (3 marks):**
Let $v$ be the wave velocity, $\Delta t$ the round-trip time. The wave travels a path length $2d$ in time $\Delta t$:

$$2d = v \cdot \Delta t \implies d = \frac{v \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

With $v = c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s for vacuum.

**Numerical (4 marks):**

$$d = \frac{(3 \times 10^8) \cdot (660 \times 10^{-9})}{2}$$

$$d = \frac{198}{2} = 99 \text{ m}$$

**Verification:** $t_{one-way} = d/c = 99 / (3\times10^8) = 330$ ns ✓ (half of 660 ns).

#### (b) [7 marks]

**Four error sources (4 marks — 1 each):**
1. **Timing jitter** — random variation in the start/stop trigger of the TDC.
2. **Multipath reflection** — receiver picks up echoes from multiple surfaces.
3. **Background light / solar interference** — raises noise floor.
4. **Target reflectivity variation** — affects SNR and walk-error.

**Derivation (2 marks):**
From $d = (c \Delta t)/2$:

$$\sigma_d = \frac{\partial d}{\partial \Delta t} \cdot \sigma_t = \frac{c}{2} \sigma_t$$

**Numerical (1 mark):**

$$\sigma_d = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \cdot 30 \times 10^{-12}}{2} = 4.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 4.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Final Answer:** $\sigma_d = 4.5$ mm.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 style]*

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of an optical ToF ranging system. Clearly state the role of the TDC (Time-to-Digital Converter). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robot's ultrasonic ToF sensor operates at 40 kHz with $v_s = 343$ m/s. If the **maximum unambiguous range** of the system is 4.0 m, determine the maximum allowable round-trip time. The robot detects an echo at $\Delta t = 22$ ms. What is the actual distance, and what is the percentage error if the engineer mistakenly uses the one-way formula? **(7 marks)**

---

### **SOLUTION — Question B**

#### (a) [7 marks]

**Block diagram (3 marks):**

```
Oscillator → Modulator → Driver → Laser Diode → (Target) →
Photodiode → TIA → Comparator → TDC → MCU → Range Output
```

**Role of TDC (2 marks):**
The TDC (Time-to-Digital Converter) is the high-precision stopwatch that converts the very small time interval $\Delta t$ between the START pulse (laser fired) and the STOP pulse (photon detected) into a digital word with **picosecond resolution**. Without a TDC, the timing cannot be resolved finely enough to compute centimetre-level ranges.

**Other block roles (2 marks):**
- Oscillator: generates reference clock.
- Driver: boosts current to fire laser diode.
- Photodiode (SPAD/APD): detects returning photons.
- TIA: amplifies the weak photocurrent.
- MCU: runs the range equation and outputs via UART/CAN.

#### (b) [7 marks]

**Maximum allowable round-trip time (3 marks):**

$$\Delta t_{max} = \frac{2 \cdot d_{max}}{v_s} = \frac{2 \cdot 4.0}{343}$$

$$\Delta t_{max} = 0.02332 \text{ s} = 23.32 \text{ ms}$$

**Actual distance for $\Delta t = 22$ ms (2 marks):**

$$d = \frac{343 \cdot 22 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = \frac{7.546}{2} = 3.773 \text{ m}$$

**Percentage error if one-way formula is used (2 marks):**
The wrong (one-way) calculation gives:

$$d_{wrong} = v_s \cdot \Delta t = 343 \cdot 0.022 = 7.546 \text{ m}$$

$$\text{Percentage Error} = \frac{\vert d_{wrong} - d_{correct} \vert}{d_{correct}} \times 100$$

$$= \frac{\vert 7.546 - 3.773 \vert}{3.773} \times 100 = 100\%$$

**Final Answer:** Correct $d = 3.773$ m; wrong one-way formula gives **100% over-estimation** (exactly doubles the reading).

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully!**
> 1. **Forgetting the `/2` factor:** This single mistake doubles your answer. Examiners explicitly test this.
> 2. **Mixing c and v_s:** Optical ToF uses $c = 3\times10^8$ m/s; ultrasonic uses $v_s \approx 343$ m/s. Using c for an ultrasonic problem gives absurd answers (~ 6.6 km for 22 ms).
> 3. **Unit mismatch:** Converting nanoseconds to seconds ($\times 10^{-9}$) is the most common silly mistake. Always write units in every step.
> 4. **Phase-ambiguity confusion:** In iToF questions, students forget to specify the *modulation frequency* $f_{mod}$ — without it, $d_{max}$ cannot be computed.
> 5. **Confusing range *resolution* with *accuracy*:** Resolution = smallest detectable change; accuracy = closeness to true value. These are NOT the same.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ ToF = active ranging using emitted wave + echo; non-contact, geometric, fast.
- ✅ Master equation: $d = (v \cdot \Delta t)/2$ — *never forget the 2*.
- ✅ Optical ToF: $v = c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s; 1 m round-trip ≈ 6.67 ns.
- ✅ Ultrasonic ToF: $v_s \approx 343$ m/s; 1 m round-trip ≈ 5.83 ms.
- ✅ **dToF** → direct pulse timing → long range, needs TDC + SPAD.
- ✅ **iToF** → phase shift of AMCW → $d = c\phi / (4\pi f_{mod})$ → short range, high precision.
- ✅ Unambiguous range: $d_{max} = c / (2 f_{mod})$ for iToF; $d_{max} = c T_{rep}/2$ for dToF.
- ✅ Range error: $\sigma_d = (c/2) \cdot \sigma_t$ — timing jitter is king.
- ✅ Major error sources: jitter, multipath, ambient light, target reflectivity.
- ✅ Key robotics applications: obstacle avoidance, SLAM, altimetry, mapping.
- ✅ Block diagram components: Oscillator → Driver → Emitter → (Target) → Receiver → TDC/Phase-detector → DSP → Output.
- ✅ Always state: emitter type, medium, modulation scheme, and operating frequency in KTU answers.
- ✅ Doppler variant (FMCW Radar) gives *velocity* too: $f_D = 2 v_r f_0 / c$.
- ✅ Speed of sound varies with temperature: $v_s = 331.3 + 0.606 T$ (T in °C) — ultrasonic robots need temperature compensation.
- ✅ LiDAR = Light Detection And Ranging = optical ToF; the workhorse of autonomous navigation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Phase Shift
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT735 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Phase Shift

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Phase Shift** in the context of Robot Navigation refers to the temporal or angular displacement between two periodic signals (typically sinusoidal waveforms) that share the same frequency but are offset in time or space. It is measured as the fraction of a complete cycle (in degrees or radians) by which one signal leads or lags another reference signal.

In sensor-based robot navigation, phase shift is the fundamental physical observable in:
- **Phase-based ranging systems** (LIDAR, RADAR, ultrasonic sonar)
- **Inertial Measurement Units (IMUs)** with ring laser / fiber optic gyroscopes
- **Phase-Locked Loop (PLL)** circuits used in odometry and SLAM front-ends
- **Multi-signal sensor fusion** where phase coherence is critical

Mathematically, if a transmitted signal is $x_t(t) = A \sin(\omega t)$ and the received signal after interaction with the environment is $x_r(t) = A' \sin(\omega t + \phi)$, then $\phi$ is the **phase shift**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Phase Shift forms the cornerstone of *active ranging* techniques in mobile robotics. The **Sagnac effect** (used in ring laser gyroscopes) and **continuous-wave (CW) phase ranging** both exploit phase shift to deduce distance and angular velocity without any direct time-of-flight measurement.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine two runners sprinting on a circular track at the **exact same speed**. Although they are moving at identical velocity (same frequency), if one runner started a few meters ahead on the track, you would observe that the trailing runner is always a fixed number of strides *behind* the leader. That permanent "stride gap" is the **phase shift** — it is not about speed, but about *where in the cycle* each signal currently sits.

In a robot navigation context:
- **Signal A** → the wave *transmitted* by the robot's emitter
- **Signal B** → the wave *echoed* back from an obstacle or surface
- The **phase lag** between them is converted (via known wave speed) into **distance** or **displacement**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Phase shift measures a *fraction* of a wavelength. To resolve distances greater than one wavelength, techniques like **multi-frequency phase ranging** or **integer ambiguity resolution** are required — a standard exam trap in KTU questions.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following values are essential for phase-shift related numericals in KTU examinations:

| Quantity | Standard Value | Notation |
|----------|----------------|----------|
| Speed of light in vacuum | $c = 3 \times 10^8$ **m/s** | $c$ |
| Speed of sound in air (20°C) | $v \approx 343$ **m/s** | $v$ |
| Full cycle phase | $2\pi$ **rad** = **360°** | — |
| Sagnac scale factor (He-Ne) | $\approx 10^7$ (dimensionless) | $K$ |
| Standard RLG area | $\sim 10^{-2}$ **m²** | $A$ |

> [!TIP]
> Always convert phase to **radians** before multiplying by frequency or wavelength in derivations. A common KTU error is using degrees and obtaining a distance answer off by a factor of **57.296**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Why Phase Shift Matters in Robot Navigation

Robot navigation demands **localization** (where am I?) and **mapping** (what surrounds me?). Both can be addressed by measuring the **time delay** or **phase delay** of a propagating wave. Phase shift is preferred over direct time-of-flight in two scenarios:

1. **Sub-microsecond timing is impractical** — for short distances, the round-trip time of light is in nanoseconds, which is hard to measure with cheap clocks. Phase shift can resolve these distances with low-cost electronics.
2. **Interferometric sensitivity is needed** — gyroscopes exploiting the Sagnac phase shift can detect angular velocities of order **0.001 °/hr**, far below the reach of mechanical sensors.

### 2.1.2 The 'How' — Working Logic

1. **Emission**: The robot's transmitter emits a continuous sinusoidal wave of known frequency $f$ and wavelength $\lambda$.
2. **Propagation**: The wave travels through the medium (air, vacuum, optical fiber) at the medium's characteristic speed.
3. **Interaction**: The wave reflects off a target / interacts with a rotating frame / interferes with a reference path.
4. **Reception**: The receiver captures the modified wave.
5. **Phase Comparison**: A phase detector (e.g., mixer, lock-in amplifier) measures $\phi$.
6. **Inference**: $\phi$ is converted to distance $d$, velocity $v$, or angular rate $\Omega$ via the appropriate governing equation.

### 2.1.3 The 'Why' — Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles**: Phase-comparison monopulse radar for lateral positioning relative to lane markers.
- **Aerial Drones (UAV)**: Fiber Optic Gyroscope (FOG) yaw measurement based on the Sagnac phase shift.
- **Indoor Mobile Robots**: Ultrasonic phase-shift triangulation for sub-centimeter obstacle ranging.
- **Space Robotics**: Star-tracker phase interferometry for absolute attitude determination.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\phi = \omega \cdot \Delta t = 2\pi f \cdot \Delta t$ | Phase from time delay | **rad** |
| 2 | $\phi = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot d$ | Phase from optical path length $d$ | **rad** |
| 3 | $d = \dfrac{\lambda \cdot \phi}{2\pi}$ | Distance from phase shift (one-way) | **m** |
| 4 | $d = \dfrac{\lambda \cdot \phi}{4\pi}$ | Distance from phase shift (round-trip) | **m** |
| 5 | $\Delta \phi_{Sagnac} = \dfrac{8\pi A \Omega}{\lambda c}$ | Sagnac phase shift in a Ring Laser Gyro | **rad** |
| 6 | $\Omega = \dfrac{\lambda c \cdot \Delta \phi_{Sagnac}}{8\pi A}$ | Angular velocity from Sagnac shift | **rad/s** |
| 7 | $\phi_{beat} = 2\pi (f_1 - f_2) t$ | Beat phase in FMCW ranging | **rad** |
| 8 | $\Delta d = \dfrac{\lambda \cdot \Delta \phi}{2\pi}$ | Displacement resolution per radian | **m/rad** |
| 9 | $v_{max} = \dfrac{c}{2 f_{mod}}$ | Maximum unambiguous velocity (FMCW) | **m/s** |
| 10 | $\lambda = \dfrac{v}{f}$ | Wavelength in a medium of speed $v$ | **m** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion safety:** Use the vertical bar macros `\vert` (or `\mid`) — *not the literal pipe character* — in your own LaTeX notes when expressing absolute value. Example: $\lambda \mid_{medium} = v/f$.

## 2.3 Boundary Conditions & Validity Domains

- **Single-frequency CW ranging** is unambiguous only over $d \in [0,\ \lambda/2)$ for round-trip, and $[0,\ \lambda)$ for one-way.
- **Sagnac equation** assumes non-relativistic rotation ($\Omega \ll c/\lambda$).
- **Phase detector** linearity holds only when $\phi \in [-\pi,\ \pi]$ modulo $2\pi$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **LIDAR phase ranging** in self-driving cars (Velodyne, Luminar) operates at $\lambda \approx 905$ **nm** and $\lambda \approx 1550$ **nm** for eye-safety.
- **Mars Rover IMUs** use Hemispherical Resonator Gyroscopes whose mode patterns shift phase in proportion to angular rate.
- **Medical Surgical Robots** (e.g., Da Vinci) use phase-shift optical encoders for sub-arcsecond joint feedback.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Phase from Two-Way Time of Flight

Let the robot's ranging system emit a wave that travels to a target at distance $d$, reflects, and returns.

**Step 1**: Total propagation path length is the **two-way distance**:

$$L = 2d$$

**Step 2**: The corresponding travel time at the medium's speed $v$ is:

$$\Delta t = \dfrac{L}{v} = \dfrac{2d}{v}$$

**Step 3**: Phase accumulated by a sinusoid of angular frequency $\omega = 2\pi f$ is:

$$\phi = \omega \cdot \Delta t = 2\pi f \cdot \Delta t$$

**Step 4**: Substitute $\Delta t$ from Step 2:

$$\phi = 2\pi f \cdot \dfrac{2d}{v}$$

**Step 5**: Recognize that the wavelength in the medium is $\lambda = v/f$, giving $v = f \lambda$. Substituting:

$$\phi = \dfrac{2\pi f \cdot 2d}{f \lambda} = \dfrac{4\pi d}{\lambda}$$

**Step 6**: Invert to express distance in terms of measurable phase:

$$d = \dfrac{\lambda \phi}{4\pi}$$

> **Logic note:** The factor $4\pi$ (not $2\pi$) appears because of the **round-trip** nature. Examiners reward the student who correctly writes $4\pi$ for radar/sonar and $2\pi$ for one-way interferometry.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Sagnac Phase Shift in a Ring Laser Gyroscope

Consider a square ring of perimeter $L = 4a$ (side $a$) rotating at angular velocity $\Omega$ about an axis perpendicular to its plane.

**Step 1**: Light traveling **clockwise (CW)** traverses a path of length:

$$L_{CW} = L + \Delta L_{CW}$$

**Step 2**: Light traveling **counter-clockwise (CCW)** traverses a path of length:

$$L_{CCW} = L - \Delta L_{CW}$$

**Step 3**: The path-length difference is:

$$\Delta L = L_{CW} - L_{CCW} = \dfrac{4 A \Omega}{c}$$

where $A$ is the area enclosed by the loop. *(This follows from classical relativity — the moving medium "drags" the light.)*

**Step 4**: This path difference translates to a phase difference:

$$\Delta \phi = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot \Delta L$$

**Step 5**: Substitute $\Delta L$:

$$\Delta \phi = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot \dfrac{4 A \Omega}{c} = \dfrac{8 \pi A \Omega}{\lambda c}$$

**Step 6**: Invert to find angular velocity from measured phase:

$$\Omega = \dfrac{\lambda c \cdot \Delta \phi}{8 \pi A}$$

> **Logic note:** The Sagnac effect does **not** require relativity to first order; it emerges from classical addition of velocities. A common KTU pitfall is to assume an additional factor of 2 from time dilation — this is *incorrect* at low $\Omega$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Phase from Beat Frequency in FMCW LIDAR

FMCW (Frequency-Modulated Continuous Wave) LIDAR chirps a signal linearly from $f_0$ to $f_0 + \Delta f$ over sweep time $T$.

**Step 1**: The transmitted frequency is:

$$f_{tx}(t) = f_0 + \dfrac{\Delta f}{T} t$$

**Step 2**: The received signal at time $t$ was transmitted at time $t - \Delta t$, so its frequency is:

$$f_{rx}(t) = f_0 + \dfrac{\Delta f}{T} (t - \Delta t)$$

**Step 3**: The instantaneous beat frequency is:

$$f_b(t) = f_{tx}(t) - f_{rx}(t) = \dfrac{\Delta f \cdot \Delta t}{T}$$

**Step 4**: Beat frequency is constant in time for a stationary target. The beat phase over an observation window $\tau$ is:

$$\phi_{beat} = 2\pi f_b \tau = \dfrac{2\pi \Delta f \cdot \Delta t \cdot \tau}{T}$$

**Step 5**: Since $\Delta t = 2d/c$, the phase is:

$$\phi_{beat} = \dfrac{4\pi \Delta f \cdot d \cdot \tau}{c T}$$

**Step 6**: Solving for distance:

$$d = \dfrac{\phi_{beat} \cdot c T}{4\pi \Delta f \cdot \tau}$$

> **Logic note:** FMCW elegantly extends the unambiguous range beyond $\lambda/2$ by modulating the carrier, at the cost of computational complexity in FFT-based beat extraction.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Method | Measurable Quantity | Sensitivity | Max Unambiguous Range | KTU Frequency |
|--------|--------------------|-------------|------------------------|---------------|
| Direct Time-of-Flight | $\Delta t$ | Low (ns) | Unlimited | Moderate |
| Phase-shift CW (RF) | $\phi$ | High | $\lambda / 2$ | High |
| Phase-shift CW (Optical) | $\phi$ | Very High | $\lambda$ | High |
| FMCW Phase | $\phi_{beat}$ | Very High | $cT / (2\Delta f)$ | High |
| Sagnac (RLG/FOG) | $\phi_{Sagnac}$ | Extreme ($\sim 10^{-7}$ rad) | n/a (rotation only) | Very High |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Phase-Shift Ranging System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Signal Generator] --> A2[Power Amplifier]
    A2 --> A3[Transmit Antenna]
    A3 --> A4[Channel / Target]
    A4 --> A5[Receive Antenna]
    A5 --> A6[Low Noise Amplifier]
    A6 --> A7[Phase Detector]
    B1[Reference Oscillator] --> A7
    A7 --> A8[Phase Output phi rad]
    A8 --> A9[Distance / Velocity Processor]
    A9 --> A10[Robot Navigation Filter EKF SLAM]
```

**Reading guide for students:**
- `A1 → A2`: stable sine source drives the emitter.
- `A4`: the wave interacts with the robot's surroundings.
- `A7`: the **mixer / phase detector** is the heart of the system — its linearity dictates the ranging accuracy.
- `A10`: phase measurements feed into the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** or **SLAM** front-end.

## 4.2 Phase-Comparison Working Principle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Tx Signal sin omega t] --> P2[Free Space Propagation]
    P2 --> P3[Target Reflection]
    P3 --> P4[Rx Signal sin omega t plus phi]
    P4 --> P5[Reference sin omega t]
    P5 --> P6[Phase Comparator]
    P4 --> P6
    P6 --> P7[Phase Shift phi]
    P7 --> P8[Compute distance d = lambda phi over 4 pi]
```

**Reading guide:** The node `P6` performs the **multiplication + low-pass filtering** of the two sinusoids, outputting a DC level proportional to $\cos(\phi)$. An arctangent operation then recovers $\phi$.

## 4.3 Sagnac Ring Gyroscope Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Beam Splitter] --> S2[Mirror 1]
    S2 --> S3[Mirror 2]
    S3 --> S4[Mirror 3]
    S4 --> S5[Mirror 4]
    S5 --> S1
    S1 --> S6[Photodetector CW]
    S1 --> S7[Photodetector CCW]
    S6 --> S8[Phase Comparator]
    S7 --> S8
    S8 --> S9[Angular Rate Output Omega rad per s]
```

**Reading guide:** The two counter-propagating beams `S6` and `S7` traverse the **same physical path** but experience **opposite path-length changes** when the platform rotates. Their phase difference at `S8` is the **Sagnac phase shift**.

## 4.4 FMCW Phase Extraction Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F1[Chirp Generator] --> F2[Tx Antenna]
    F2 --> F3[Target at distance d]
    F3 --> F4[Rx Antenna]
    F4 --> F5[Mixer with Tx Sample]
    F5 --> F6[Low Pass Filter]
    F6 --> F7[ADC Sampling]
    F7 --> F8[FFT Beat Spectrum]
    F8 --> F9[Peak Frequency f_b]
    F9 --> F10[Phase Extraction arctan Qi over Ii]
    F10 --> F11[Combined Range Doppler Estimate]
```

**Reading guide:** The **FFT peak** in `F8` gives coarse range, while the **peak's complex phase** in `F10` gives sub-bin refinement — a hybrid that delivers millimeter precision.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define phase shift in the context of robot navigation. Mention its significance in active ranging systems.**

**Model Answer:**
Phase shift is the angular (or temporal) offset $\phi$ between two periodic signals of identical frequency $f$, expressed in radians or degrees. In robot navigation, it represents the **fractional wavelength delay** acquired by a wave after propagating to a target and back. Its significance lies in enabling **sub-wavelength distance resolution** without the need for ultra-fast time digitizers, and in providing the operating principle for **ring laser gyroscopes**, **ultrasonic sonars**, and **FMCW LIDARs** used in SLAM and dead-reckoning.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**State the Sagnac equation. Why is the effect considered non-relativistic to first order?**

**Model Answer:**
The Sagnac phase shift for a rotating ring of area $A$ at angular velocity $\Omega$ is:

$$\Delta \phi = \dfrac{8 \pi A \Omega}{\lambda c}$$

The effect arises from the **classical addition of light's speed to the tangential speed of the moving medium**, and yields a path difference of $\Delta L = 4A\Omega / c$. Time dilation contributes only at second order in $v/c$, which is negligible for terrestrial and aerospace rotations, justifying the non-relativistic treatment at engineering accuracy.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]

**(a)** With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **continuous-wave phase-comparison ranging system** used in mobile robot obstacle detection. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A robot's ultrasonic sensor operates at $f = 40$ **kHz** in air ($v = 343$ **m/s**). It emits a CW burst and receives an echo with a measured phase lag of $\phi = 1.2$ **rad**. Compute the **distance** to the obstacle. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Working Principle:**

1. **Signal generation** — A 40 kHz oscillator drives a piezoelectric transducer, producing a sinusoidal pressure wave in air.
2. **Propagation** — The wave radiates, strikes the obstacle, and scatters.
3. **Reception** — A second transducer (or the same in monostatic mode) captures the echo.
4. **Phase comparison** — A reference tap from the oscillator and the received signal are fed to a phase detector (multiplier + LPF).
5. **Distance inference** — The phase output $\phi$ is digitized and converted to distance.

**Block diagram (recap of §4.1):** Signal generator → Tx transducer → air channel → Rx transducer → LNA → phase detector → range output.

**Key equations:**

$$\phi = \dfrac{4\pi d}{\lambda}, \quad d = \dfrac{\lambda \phi}{4\pi}$$

**Advantages for robots:** low cost, low power, robust to dust and smoke (versus optical systems). **Limitations:** $\lambda \approx 8.6$ **mm** restricts unambiguous range; specular surfaces produce weak echoes.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1**: Compute wavelength.

$$\lambda = \dfrac{v}{f} = \dfrac{343}{40 \times 10^3} = 8.575 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}$$

**Step 2**: Apply the round-trip phase-to-distance formula.

$$d = \dfrac{\lambda \phi}{4\pi} = \dfrac{8.575 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.2}{4 \pi}$$

**Step 3**: Numerator:

$$8.575 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.2 = 1.029 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{m}$$

**Step 4**: Denominator:

$$4 \pi \approx 12.566$$

**Step 5**: Final division.

$$d = \dfrac{1.029 \times 10^{-2}}{12.566} \approx 8.19 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{d \approx 0.819\ \text{mm}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Trap (Part b):** Many students write $d = \lambda \phi / 2\pi$ (forgetting the **round-trip** factor 2) and obtain $1.64$ **mm** — exactly **double** the correct answer. Always confirm whether the geometry is one-way (interferometer) or two-way (radar/sonar) before writing the formula.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]

**(a)** Derive the **Sagnac phase shift** expression for a square ring laser gyroscope of side $a$ rotating with angular velocity $\Omega$. Show that the angular velocity is recoverable from the phase difference. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A ring laser gyroscope has a square cavity of side $a = 0.2$ **m**, emitting at $\lambda = 632.8$ **nm** (He-Ne). The detector measures $\Delta \phi = 10^{-5}$ **rad**. Compute the **angular velocity** of the host platform. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The full derivation is presented in §3.2. Key transitional points expected in your KTU answer script:

1. State the path-difference formula: $\Delta L = 4A\Omega/c$.
2. Convert to phase: $\Delta \phi = (2\pi/\lambda) \cdot \Delta L = 8\pi A \Omega / (\lambda c)$.
3. For a square of side $a$: $A = a^2$, so $\Delta \phi = 8\pi a^2 \Omega / (\lambda c)$.
4. Invert for angular velocity:

$$\Omega = \dfrac{\lambda c \cdot \Delta \phi}{8 \pi a^2}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1**: Compute area.

$$A = a^2 = 0.04\ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2**: Substitute into the inverse Sagnac equation.

$$\Omega = \dfrac{\lambda c \cdot \Delta \phi}{8 \pi A} = \dfrac{(632.8 \times 10^{-9}) (3 \times 10^8) (10^{-5})}{8 \pi (0.04)}$$

**Step 3**: Numerator:

$$632.8 \times 10^{-9} \times 3 \times 10^8 = 0.18984$$

$$0.18984 \times 10^{-5} = 1.8984 \times 10^{-6}$$

**Step 4**: Denominator:

$$8 \pi \times 0.04 = 8 \times 3.1416 \times 0.04 = 1.0053$$

**Step 5**: Final division.

$$\Omega = \dfrac{1.8984 \times 10^{-6}}{1.0053} \approx 1.888 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 6**: Convert to degrees per hour for intuition.

$$\Omega \approx 1.888 \times 10^{-6} \times \dfrac{180}{\pi} \times 3600 \approx 0.389\ ^\circ/\text{hr}$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{\Omega \approx 1.89 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{rad/s} \approx 0.39\ ^\circ/\text{hr}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Trap (Part b):** A frequent mistake is omitting the area $A = a^2$ substitution and plugging the **perimeter** $L = 4a = 0.8$ **m** in its place. This yields an angular velocity **off by a factor of 16**. Always check whether the formula requires area or perimeter. The Sagnac equation is an **area-based** relationship.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Phase shift** is the angular offset between two identical-frequency sinusoids; it is **dimensionless** but measured in **radians** or **degrees**.
- For **one-way** ranging (interferometer): $d = \lambda \phi / 2\pi$. For **round-trip** ranging (radar/sonar): $d = \lambda \phi / 4\pi$.
- The **Sagnac equation** $\Delta \phi = 8\pi A \Omega / (\lambda c)$ is the operating principle of **ring laser and fiber-optic gyroscopes** used in IMUs for robot attitude estimation.
- **FMCW** combines coarse range (from beat frequency) and fine range (from beat phase) to overcome the $\lambda/2$ ambiguity of pure CW ranging.
- Wavelength must be computed in the **propagation medium** ($v$ for air, $c$ for vacuum), not in vacuum.
- Phase detectors operate linearly only within $[-\pi, \pi]$; wraparound demands **integer-ambiguity resolution** algorithms.
- The **degree–radian conversion** ($1\ \text{rad} = 57.296^\circ$) is a recurring KTU scoring opportunity — always show the conversion step.
- Distance resolution per radian is $\lambda / 4\pi$ (round-trip) — smaller wavelengths yield finer resolution at the cost of greater ambiguity.
- Phase measurements feed into **EKF / Particle Filter** SLAM stacks as high-rate update channels complementary to wheel odometry.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Triangulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT735 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Triangulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Triangulation** is a geometric localization technique used in robot navigation wherein the position of an unknown point (the robot) is determined by measuring angles (bearings) or distances (ranges) from two or more known reference points (landmarks, beacons, or anchors) whose coordinates are already established in a global reference frame.

In formal KTU 2024 syllabus terminology:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Triangulation** is the process of computing the 2D or 3D pose of a mobile robot by forming one or more triangles whose vertices consist of the robot and two (or more) landmarks of known position, using angular or range measurements taken at the robot.

There are two principal sub-categories:

| Variant | Measured Quantity | Sensor Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Bearing Triangulation** (Angle-of-Arrival) | Angular bearings $\theta_1, \theta_2$ | Compass, vision camera, laser scanner |
| **Range Triangulation** (Distance-based) | Distances $d_1, d_2$ | Ultrasonic, LiDAR, UWB, stereo vision |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — The Forest Rescue Scenario:**
> Imagine you are lost in a forest at night with a map showing two radio towers whose positions you know. By pointing a directional antenna at Tower A and noting the compass bearing (say, $40^\circ$ East of North), you can draw a ray from Tower A. Repeating this for Tower B gives you a second ray. The point where the two rays intersect on your map is **your exact location**. This is triangulation in its purest form.
> If instead you used a rangefinder and measured the *distance* to each tower, you would draw a **circle** of that radius around each tower. The intersection of the two circles reveals your position. This is range-based triangulation.

The robot, acting as an observer at an unknown coordinate $(x, y)$, "looks" toward landmarks and converts those observations into geometric constraints. Two independent constraints (from two landmarks) are mathematically sufficient to uniquely solve for the two unknown coordinates in 2D.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- The standard metric for triangulation **accuracy** is the **Geometric Dilution of Precision (GDOP)**.
- Standard angular measurement units in KTU syllabus: **radians (rad)** or **degrees ($^\circ$)**.
- The conversion identity $1 \text{ rad} = \dfrac{180}{\pi} \approx 57.2958^\circ$ must be applied consistently.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

The triangulation pipeline proceeds through these structured steps:

1. **Map Preparation** — The robot is loaded with coordinates of $N \geq 2$ landmarks $L_i = (x_i, y_i)$ for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, N$.
2. **Observation Phase** — Onboard sensors measure either:
   - **Bearings** $\theta_i$ (angle from a fixed reference direction, usually North or the robot's heading) to each landmark, **OR**
   - **Ranges** $d_i = \sqrt{(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2}$ to each landmark.
3. **Geometric Constraint Formation** — Each measurement produces a constraint:
   - Bearing: $\tan(\theta_i) = \dfrac{y - y_i}{x - x_i}$
   - Range: $(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2 = d_i^2$
4. **Algebraic Solution** — For $N = 2$ landmarks, two equations are solved simultaneously (closed-form). For $N > 2$, a **least-squares** solution is computed.
5. **Error Estimation** — GDOP is evaluated to quantify position confidence.

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

- **Why $N \geq 2$?** A single constraint in 2D defines a line (bearing) or circle (range) — infinitely many candidate positions lie on it. Two non-parallel constraints intersect at exactly one point.
- **Why closed-form vs. least-squares?** Closed-form is faster but sensitive to noise. Least-squares (over $N > 2$ landmarks) reduces noise impact at the cost of computational complexity.
- **How does GDOP help?** It quantifies how landmark geometry amplifies measurement noise into position errors — a "bad" triangle (nearly collinear landmarks) yields poor GDOP.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in every KTU exam on this topic.**

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Range equation (Euclidean) | $d_i = \sqrt{(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2}$ | $d_i$ in metres |
| 2 | Bearing equation | $\theta_i = \operatorname{atan2}(y - y_i, x - x_i)$ | $\theta_i$ in rad |
| 3 | Bearing linear form | $y - y_i = (x - x_i)\tan(\theta_i)$ | Standard 2D line |
| 4 | Range-squared (circle) | $(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2 = d_i^2$ | Implicit circle |
| 5 | Law of cosines (range-triang.) | $b^2 = a^2 + c^2 - 2ac\cos(B)$ | Triangle sides $a, b, c$, angle $B$ |
| 6 | Law of sines | $\dfrac{a}{\sin A} = \dfrac{b}{\sin B} = \dfrac{c}{\sin C}$ | Angles $A, B, C$ |
| 7 | Closed-form (2 landmarks) | $x = \dfrac{(x_2 \tan\theta_2 - x_1 \tan\theta_1) + (y_1 - y_2)}{\tan\theta_2 - \tan\theta_1}$ | Solving 2 bearing equations |
| 8 | GDOP (2D, 2 landmarks) | $\text{GDOP} = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2}}{\sigma_\theta}$ | $\sigma$ = std. deviation |
| 9 | Gradient of constraint | $\nabla h_i = \begin{bmatrix} \sin\theta_i & -\cos\theta_i \end{bmatrix}^T$ | Jacobian row vector |
| 10 | Weighted LS solution | $\hat{\mathbf{p}} = (H^T W H)^{-1} H^T W \mathbf{z}$ | $\mathbf{p} = [x, y]^T$ |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Triangulation is foundational to:
- **GPS / GNSS** satellites use trilateration (range variant) from 4+ satellites.
- **Indoor robotics** uses UWB beacons for warehouse robot localization.
- **Aerial drone swarms** triangulate positions using onboard cameras and ground fiducials.
- **Autonomous vehicles** triangulate against lane markers and HD-map landmarks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Closed-Form Solution from Two Bearings

**Setup:** Robot at unknown $(x, y)$. Two landmarks at known positions $L_1 = (x_1, y_1)$ and $L_2 = (x_2, y_2)$. The robot measures the absolute bearings $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ from the global North reference.

**Step 1 — Write the line equation for bearing $\theta_1$:**

The line passing through $L_1$ with slope $\tan(\theta_1)$ is written as the implicit form:

$$
y - y_1 = (x - x_1) \tan(\theta_1)
$$

This represents every point on Earth that has bearing $\theta_1$ from $L_1$.

**Step 2 — Write the line equation for bearing $\theta_2$:**

$$
y - y_2 = (x - x_2) \tan(\theta_2)
$$

**Step 3 — Subtract the two equations to eliminate $y$:**

Begin by expanding both equations:

$$
y - y_1 = x \tan(\theta_1) - x_1 \tan(\theta_1)
$$
$$
y - y_2 = x \tan(\theta_2) - x_2 \tan(\theta_2)
$$

Subtract the first from the second:

$$
(y - y_2) - (y - y_1) = x \tan(\theta_2) - x_1 \tan(\theta_1) - x \tan(\theta_1) + x_2 \tan(\theta_2)
$$

The left-hand side simplifies to $y_1 - y_2$. Collecting $x$ terms on the right:

$$
y_1 - y_2 = x \left[ \tan(\theta_2) - \tan(\theta_1) \right] + x_2 \tan(\theta_2) - x_1 \tan(\theta_1)
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $x$:**

$$
x \left[ \tan(\theta_2) - \tan(\theta_1) \right] = y_1 - y_2 - x_2 \tan(\theta_2) + x_1 \tan(\theta_1)
$$

Therefore:

$$
x = \dfrac{x_1 \tan(\theta_1) - x_2 \tan(\theta_2) + y_1 - y_2}{\tan(\theta_1) - \tan(\theta_2)}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $x$ back into the first line equation to obtain $y$:**

$$
y = y_1 + (x - x_1) \tan(\theta_1)
$$

This yields the closed-form 2D position estimate.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Closed-Form Solution from Two Ranges

**Setup:** Robot at unknown $(x, y)$. Two landmarks at known positions $L_1 = (x_1, y_1)$ and $L_2 = (x_2, y_2)$. The robot measures distances $d_1$ and $d_2$.

**Step 1 — Write the two circle equations:**

$$
(x - x_1)^2 + (y - y_1)^2 = d_1^2
$$
$$
(x - x_2)^2 + (y - y_2)^2 = d_2^2
$$
