# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
Define **effector noise** in the context of robot navigation. Differentiate between *systematic* and *non-systematic* sources of effector noise with one example of each.

**Model Answer:**

Effector noise is the stochastic uncertainty between the motion commanded to a robot's actuators and the motion actually executed. It is modelled by the conditional density $p(x_{t}\mid u_{t}, x_{t-1})$ whose spread (variance) characterises the noise.

- **Systematic (deterministic)** sources are repeatable, predictable biases — e.g., a fixed mismatch of 2 mm between the diameters of the left and right wheels of a differential-drive robot, producing a permanent turning bias.
- **Non-systematic (stochastic)** sources are random, time-varying disturbances — e.g., momentary wheel slip on a wet patch of floor, producing a randomly varying translation error.

**(3 marks)** — 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for systematic with example, 1 mark for non-systematic with example.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
State the **covariance propagation equation** used in an Extended Kalman Filter for a mobile robot and explain the physical meaning of each of the two terms on the right-hand side.

**Model Answer:**

The propagation equation is:

$$
\Sigma_{t} = G_{x}\,\Sigma_{t-1}\,G_{x}^{T} + G_{u}\,Q_{t}\,G_{u}^{T}
$$

- The **first term** $G_{x}\Sigma_{t-1}G_{x}^{T}$ propagates the *previous* pose uncertainty through the (linearised) motion model. Physically, it says: *"if I was uncertain about where I was an instant ago, that uncertainty is rotated and stretched by the motion I just executed."*
- The **second term** $G_{u}Q_{t}G_{u}^{T}$ injects *new* uncertainty generated by the noisy actuator execution during the present control interval. Physically, it represents the *fresh* effector noise added by wheels, motors, and floor interaction.

**(3 marks)** — 1 mark for equation, 1 mark per term explanation.

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2 Internal Choice Option 1]**

A differential-drive robot at pose $(0, 0, 0)$ commands a pure translation of $d = 5\ \text{m}$ along its current heading $\theta = 0$ rad. The translation noise is $\sigma_{d} = 0.1\ \text{m}$ and the heading noise is $\sigma_{\theta} = 0.05\ \text{rad}$.

**(a)** Derive the $2\times 2$ position error covariance matrix $\Sigma_{xy}$ for this motion step. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** From $\Sigma_{xy}$, compute the semi-axes $(a, b)$ and orientation $\psi$ of the 1-$\sigma$ error ellipse. Sketch the ellipse in the $xy$-plane and comment on the relative magnitude of the major and minor axes. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Step 1:** Use the canonical formulas for the four entries:

$$
\sigma_{x}^{2} = \cos^{2}\theta\,\sigma_{d}^{2} + d^{2}\sin^{2}\theta\,\sigma_{\theta}^{2}
$$

$$
\sigma_{y}^{2} = \sin^{2}\theta\,\sigma_{d}^{2} + d^{2}\cos^{2}\theta\,\sigma_{\theta}^{2}
$$

$$
\sigma_{xy} = \cos\theta\sin\theta\,\sigma_{d}^{2} - d^{2}\sin\theta\cos\theta\,\sigma_{\theta}^{2}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $\theta = 0$, $d = 5$, $\sigma_{d} = 0.1$, $\sigma_{\theta} = 0.05$:

$$
\cos 0 = 1, \quad \sin 0 = 0
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{x}^{2} &= (1)^{2}\cdot(0.1)^{2} + 25\cdot(0)^{2}\cdot(0.05)^{2} = 0.01\ \text{m}^{2} \\
\sigma_{y}^{2} &= (0)^{2}\cdot(0.1)^{2} + 25\cdot(1)^{2}\cdot(0.05)^{2} = 25\cdot 0.0025 = 0.0625\ \text{m}^{2} \\
\sigma_{xy} &= (1)(0)\cdot(0.1)^{2} - 25\cdot(0)(1)\cdot(0.05)^{2} = 0\ \text{m}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Assemble the matrix:

$$
\Sigma_{xy} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.0100 & 0.0000 \\ 0.0000 & 0.0625 \end{pmatrix}\ \text{m}^{2}
$$

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1:** Compute the eigenvalues:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda_{\pm} &= \tfrac{1}{2}(0.0100 + 0.0625) \pm \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{4}(0.0100-0.0625)^{2} + 0^{2}} \\
&= 0.03625 \pm 0.02625
\end{aligned}
$$

So $\lambda_{+} = 0.0625\ \text{m}^{2}$ and $\lambda_{-} = 0.0100\ \text{m}^{2}$.

**Step 2:** Semi-axes:

$$
a = \sqrt{0.0625} = 0.250\ \text{m}, \quad b = \sqrt{0.0100} = 0.100\ \text{m}
$$

**Step 3:** Orientation:

$$
\psi = \tfrac{1}{2}\arctan\!\left(\frac{2 \cdot 0}{0.0100 - 0.0625}\right) = 0^{\circ}
$$

**Step 4:** Sketch description: The 1-$\sigma$ ellipse is centred at the mean pose $(5, 0)$, with major semi-axis $a = 0.25\ \text{m}$ along the *y*-direction and minor semi-axis $b = 0.10\ \text{m}$ along the *x*-direction.

**Comment:** The major axis is **perpendicular** to the direction of travel, not parallel. This is the *opposite* of the general rule, and it is a direct consequence of $\theta = 0$: with zero heading, the $d^{2}\sigma_{\theta}^{2}$ term contributes purely to $\sigma_{y}^{2}$. For any non-zero $\theta$, the major axis rotates to align with the heading direction.

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Internal-Choice Option*
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2 Internal Choice Option 2]**

A two-wheeled differential-drive robot is commanded to move with linear velocity $v = 0.5\ \text{m/s}$ and angular velocity $\omega = 0.2\ \text{rad/s}$ for a duration of $\Delta t = 2\ \text{s}$. The initial pose is $(0, 0, 0)$ and the prior covariance is the zero matrix (perfect initial knowledge). The control-noise covariance is $Q = \mathrm{diag}(0.01,\,0.0025)$.

**(a)** Write the deterministic motion function for the new pose and compute the expected new pose. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the linearised EKF, compute the posterior covariance $\Sigma_{t}$ and comment on which direction of pose uncertainty dominates. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

The deterministic motion function is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{t} &= x_{t-1} - \frac{v}{\omega}\sin\theta_{t-1} + \frac{v}{\omega}\sin(\theta_{t-1}+\omega\Delta t) \\
y_{t} &= y_{t-1} + \frac{v}{\omega}\cos\theta_{t-1} - \frac{v}{\omega}\cos(\theta_{t-1}+\omega\Delta t) \\
\theta_{t} &= \theta_{t-1} + \omega\Delta t
\end{aligned}
$$

With $v/\omega = 0.5/0.2 = 2.5$, $\theta_{t-1} = 0$, and $\omega\Delta t = 0.4\ \text{rad}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{t} &= 0 - 2.5\sin 0 + 2.5\sin(0.4) = 2.5 \times 0.3894 = 0.9736\ \text{m} \\
y_{t} &= 0 + 2.5\cos 0 - 2.5\cos(0.4) = 2.5(1 - 0.9211) = 0.1973\ \text{m} \\
\theta_{t} &= 0 + 0.4 = 0.4\ \text{rad}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Model Solution to (b)

Since $\Sigma_{t-1} = 0$, the first term vanishes and:

$$
\Sigma_{t} = G_{u}\,Q\,G_{u}^{T}
$$

Computing $G_{u}$ (differentiation of the motion function with respect to $v$ and $\omega$) and substituting the numerical values yields a $3\times 3$ covariance whose diagonal entries are the variances in $x$, $y$, and $\theta$. The dominant term is the **heading variance** $\sigma_{\theta}^{2}$ scaled by the arc-length squared — i.e., the *cross-track* direction dominates, exactly as predicted by the $d^{2}\sigma_{\theta}^{2}$ term in the earlier derivation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** When the prior covariance is **zero**, students often incorrectly drop the second term $G_{u}Q_{t}G_{u}^{T}$. This is the most common 2-mark loss in EKF prediction questions. **Always include both terms** in the propagation equation; the first may be zero but the second is the new noise added by the actuators.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Effector noise is the *random discrepancy* between commanded and executed motion, modelled probabilistically.
- **Sources:** Systematic (calibratable) vs. non-systematic (random). Both contribute; KTU typically asks for the non-systematic one in Part A.
- **Mathematical model:** Gaussian noise on translation and rotation, $\hat{\delta}_{\text{trans}} \sim \mathcal{N}(\delta_{\text{trans}},\sigma^{2})$ and similarly for rotation.
- **Master equation:** $\Sigma_{t} = G_{x}\Sigma_{t-1}G_{x}^{T} + G_{u}Q_{t}G_{u}^{T}$ — remember both terms.
- **Position variance formula:** $\sigma_{x}^{2} = \cos^{2}\theta\,\sigma_{d}^{2} + d^{2}\sin^{2}\theta\,\sigma_{\theta}^{2}$, and symmetrically for $\sigma_{y}^{2}$.
- **The $d^{2}\sigma_{\theta}^{2}$ term** is the dominant source of cross-track uncertainty for straight-line travel.
- **Error ellipse:** $a = \sqrt{\lambda_{+}}$, $b = \sqrt{\lambda_{-}}$, oriented at $\psi = \tfrac{1}{2}\arctan(2\sigma_{xy}/(\sigma_{x}^{2}-\sigma_{y}^{2}))$.
- **EKF pitfall:** Do not drop the second term when prior covariance is zero.
- **Physical intuition:** Effector noise is why dead-reckoning alone is **never** sufficient for long-range navigation; sensor-based corrections are mandatory.
- **Engineering relevance:** Covariance-bounded odometry is the foundation of every self-driving car, warehouse AMR, and Mars rover.

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Odometric Position Estimation** is the process of estimating a mobile robot's change in position and orientation (pose) by integrating the rotational motion of its driven wheels, as measured by incremental wheel encoders. The associated **Error Model** is a probabilistic or deterministic framework that characterizes the deviation between the estimated pose and the true pose, accounting for both deterministic (systematic) and stochastic (non-systematic) sources of uncertainty.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> The error model for odometry is foundational to Mobile Robot Localization under NEP 2020 outcome-based learning. It is directly linked to **CO2 (Apply error propagation techniques for robot pose estimation)** of the PERUT735 course.

Formally, given the previous pose $\mathbf{p}_{k-1} = [x, y, \theta]^T$ and wheel encoder readings $\Delta s_L, \Delta s_R$, the incremental update is:

$$\mathbf{p}_k = f(\mathbf{p}_{k-1}, \Delta s_L, \Delta s_R)$$

The error model quantifies the covariance:

$$\Sigma_k = J_f \Sigma_{k-1} J_f^T + Q_k$$

where $J_f$ is the Jacobian of the function $f$ and $Q_k$ is the process noise covariance matrix.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are blindfolded and asked to walk from your home to a nearby shop, counting only your footsteps. Even if you try to keep each step uniform, you will drift off course because your steps are not perfectly equal, and the ground is uneven. After 100 steps you may be **5 meters off**, after 1000 steps you may be **50 meters off**. This drift is exactly what happens to a robot that estimates its position by counting wheel rotations — the further it travels, the more uncertain its position becomes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Odometry errors grow **unbounded** with distance traveled. This is why odometry alone is insufficient for long-range navigation and must be fused with absolute sensors (GPS, LiDAR, beacons) using methods like Kalman Filtering or Particle Filters.

### 1.3 Important Standard Metrics

- **Encoder Resolution (E):** Number of encoder pulses per wheel revolution. Typical range: **500–20000 pulses/rev**.
- **Wheelbase (b):** Distance between driven wheels. Typical: **0.2 m to 0.6 m**.
- **Wheel Diameter (D):** Usually **0.1 m to 0.3 m**.
- **Error Growth Rate (K):** Empirical constant; **0.01–0.05 m error per meter traveled** for well-calibrated systems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Sources of Odometric Errors

Odometric errors are broadly classified into two categories:

#### 2.1.1 Systematic Errors (Deterministic, Predictable)
These are reproducible and can be corrected through calibration:

- **$E_d$ — Wheel Diameter Mismatch:** Left and right wheels may have slightly different effective diameters due to manufacturing tolerances, tire pressure differences, or wear.
- **$E_b$ — Wheelbase Uncertainty:** The actual wheelbase may differ from the nominal value used in the kinematic equations.
- **$E_{fs}$ — Finite Encoder Resolution:** Encoders output discrete pulses, introducing quantization in displacement.
- **$E_{\alpha}$ — Misalignment of Wheels:** Wheels may not be perfectly parallel, causing the robot to veer even on a straight command.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters:** Systematic errors accumulate **proportionally to distance traveled**. They are the dominant source of long-term drift in indoor environments.

#### 2.1.2 Non-Systematic Errors (Stochastic, Unpredictable)
These are environment-dependent and cannot be calibrated out:

- **Floor Unevenness:** Bumps, cracks, and gradients cause transient wheel slip.
- **Wheel Slippage:** Loss of traction on slippery surfaces.
- **External Forces:** Collisions with obstacles, human pushes.
- **Debris on Wheels:** Mud, dust, threads on the tire surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Non-systematic errors dominate in cluttered, real-world environments.** They are typically modeled as **zero-mean Gaussian white noise** in the error propagation framework.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|----------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\Delta s = \tfrac{1}{2}(\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L)$ | Linear displacement of robot center | m |
| 2 | $\Delta \theta = \tfrac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{b}$ | Change in heading angle | rad |
| 3 | $x_{k} = x_{k-1} + \Delta s \cos(\theta_{k-1} + \tfrac{\Delta\theta}{2})$ | Update of x-coordinate | m |
| 4 | $y_{k} = y_{k-1} + \Delta s \sin(\theta_{k-1} + \tfrac{\Delta\theta}{2})$ | Update of y-coordinate | m |
| 5 | $\theta_{k} = \theta_{k-1} + \Delta \theta$ | Update of heading | rad |
| 6 | $\Sigma_{k} = J_f \Sigma_{k-1} J_f^T + Q$ | Covariance propagation | m², rad² |
| 7 | $J_f = \tfrac{\partial f}{\partial \mathbf{p}}$ | Jacobian of motion model (3×3) | — |
| 8 | $\sigma_x^2 = k_x s^2$, $\sigma_y^2 = k_y s^2$, $\sigma_\theta^2 = k_\theta^2 s^2$ | Variance growth with distance | m², rad² |
| 9 | $E_d = \tfrac{D_R - D_L}{D}$ | Normalized wheel diameter error | — |
| 10 | $E_b = \tfrac{b_{actual} - b_{nominal}}{b_{nominal}}$ | Normalized wheelbase error | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on Symbol Usage:** In the variance formula, $\vert J \vert$ should be written using $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ in LaTeX. Within markdown tables, always use $\lvert x \rvert$ instead of $\vert x \vert$ to avoid breaking table syntax.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Odometric error models are used in dead-reckoning modules of self-driving cars to predict short-term motion between GPS updates.
- **Warehouse Robots (AMRs):** Amazon's Kiva robots use odometry combined with floor fiducial markers to maintain centimeter-level accuracy.
- **Planetary Rovers:** NASA's Mars rovers rely on wheel-odometry with explicit error modeling because absolute sensors are limited.
- **Medical Robotics:** Wheelchair navigation systems use odometric error models for collision avoidance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Odometric Update Equations

We start with a **differential-drive robot** with:
- Wheelbase (distance between wheels) = $b$
- Left and right wheel displacements over one sampling interval = $\Delta s_L, \Delta s_R$

**Step 1: Linear displacement of the robot's reference point (center of axle).**

Since the robot's center moves forward by the *average* of the two wheel displacements (assuming no-slip rolling):

$$\Delta s = \frac{\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L}{2}$$

**Step 2: Angular displacement of the robot.**

The right wheel moves farther than the left wheel, so the robot rotates. The arc traced by each wheel is $r \cdot \Delta \phi$, where $r$ is the wheel radius. For a small rotation $\Delta \theta$ about the ICC (Instantaneous Center of Curvature):

$$\Delta s_R = (R + b/2)\Delta\theta, \quad \Delta s_L = (R - b/2)\Delta\theta$$

**Step 3: Solving for $\Delta \theta$.**

Subtracting the two equations:

$$\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L = b \cdot \Delta\theta$$

Therefore:

$$\Delta \theta = \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{b}$$

**Step 4: Updating the global pose.**

The robot's center moves a distance $\Delta s$ along a direction that is the *midpoint* of the initial and final heading angles (this avoids the bias of using only the start or end angle):

$$\begin{aligned}
\theta_{mid} &= \theta_{k-1} + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2} \\
x_k &= x_{k-1} + \Delta s \cos(\theta_{mid}) \\
y_k &= y_{k-1} + \Delta s \sin(\theta_{mid}) \\
\theta_k &= \theta_{k-1} + \Delta\theta
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Error Covariance Propagation

Let the motion model be $\mathbf{p}_k = f(\mathbf{p}_{k-1}, \mathbf{u}_k) + \mathbf{w}_k$ where $\mathbf{w}_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_k)$ is process noise and $\mathbf{u}_k = [\Delta s_L, \Delta s_R]^T$.

**Step 1: Linearize the motion model using a first-order Taylor expansion around the current estimate $\hat{\mathbf{p}}_{k-1}$.**

$$\mathbf{p}_k \approx f(\hat{\mathbf{p}}_{k-1}, \mathbf{u}_k) + J_f \cdot (\mathbf{p}_{k-1} - \hat{\mathbf{p}}_{k-1}) + \mathbf{w}_k$$

where $J_f$ is the Jacobian of $f$ w.r.t. the pose $\mathbf{p}$.

**Step 2: Compute the Jacobian $J_f$.**

For the x-equation: $f_x = x_{k-1} + \Delta s \cos(\theta_{k-1} + \tfrac{\Delta\theta}{2})$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial f_x}{\partial x} &= 1 \\
\frac{\partial f_x}{\partial y} &= 0 \\
\frac{\partial f_x}{\partial \theta} &= -\Delta s \sin(\theta_{k-1} + \tfrac{\Delta\theta}{2})
\end{aligned}$$

For the y-equation: $f_y = y_{k-1} + \Delta s \sin(\theta_{k-1} + \tfrac{\Delta\theta}{2})$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial f_y}{\partial x} &= 0 \\
\frac{\partial f_y}{\partial y} &= 1 \\
\frac{\partial f_y}{\partial \theta} &= \Delta s \cos(\theta_{k-1} + \tfrac{\Delta\theta}{2})
\end{aligned}$$

For the heading: $f_\theta = \theta_{k-1} + \Delta\theta$:

$$J_f = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -\Delta s \sin(\theta + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2}) \\ 0 & 1 & \Delta s \cos(\theta + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2}) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Propagate the covariance.**

Since the linearized system has process noise with covariance $Q_k$, the propagated covariance is:

$$\Sigma_k = J_f \, \Sigma_{k-1} \, J_f^T + Q_k$$

**Step 4: Identify the process noise matrix $Q_k$.**

A common parameterization (Chatila & Laumond, 1985) is:

$$Q_k = \begin{bmatrix} k_x \Delta s^2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & k_y \Delta s^2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & k_\theta^2 \Delta s^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

where $k_x, k_y, k_\theta$ are empirically determined error growth constants.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A differential-drive robot with wheelbase $b = 0.5$ m has $\Delta s_L = 0.30$ m and $\Delta s_R = 0.42$ m. Previous pose: $(x, y, \theta) = (1.0, 2.0, \pi/4)$ rad. Compute the new pose.

**Step 1:** Compute $\Delta s$:

$$\Delta s = \frac{0.42 + 0.30}{2} = 0.36 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2:** Compute $\Delta \theta$:

$$\Delta\theta = \frac{0.42 - 0.30}{0.5} = 0.24 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the mid-heading:

$$\theta_{mid} = \frac{\pi}{4} + \frac{0.24}{2} = 0.7854 + 0.12 = 0.9054 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 4:** Update the pose:

$$\begin{aligned}
x_k &= 1.0 + 0.36 \cos(0.9054) = 1.0 + 0.36 \times 0.6173 = 1.2222 \text{ m} \\
y_k &= 2.0 + 0.36 \sin(0.9054) = 2.0 + 0.36 \times 0.7867 = 2.2832 \text{ m} \\
\theta_k &= 0.7854 + 0.24 = 1.0254 \text{ rad}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** Compute the propagated covariance (assume $k_x = 0.05$, $k_y = 0.05$, $k_\theta = 0.02$, prior $\Sigma_0 = \text{diag}(0.01, 0.01, 0.001)$):

The Jacobian evaluated at $\theta_{mid} = 0.9054$ rad:

$$J_f = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -0.36 \times 0.7867 \\ 0 & 1 & 0.36 \times 0.6173 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -0.2832 \\ 0 & 1 & 0.2222 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Computing $J_f \Sigma_0 J_f^T$ (carrying out the multiplication):

$$J_f \Sigma_0 J_f^T = \begin{bmatrix} 0.01 + 0.0008 & -0.0006 & 0 \\ -0.0006 & 0.01 + 0.0005 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.001 \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.0108 & -0.0006 & 0 \\ -0.0006 & 0.0105 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.001 \end{bmatrix}$$

Adding $Q_k = \text{diag}(0.05 \times 0.1296, 0.05 \times 0.1296, 0.0004 \times 0.1296)$:

$$Q_k = \begin{bmatrix} 0.00648 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.00648 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.0000518 \end{bmatrix}$$

Final covariance:

$$\Sigma_k = \begin{bmatrix} 0.0173 & -0.0006 & 0 \\ -0.0006 & 0.0170 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.00105 \end{bmatrix}$$

The position uncertainty grew from 0.01 m² to ~0.017 m² after just one step — illustrating the unbounded growth of odometric error.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Odometry Error Propagation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wheel Encoder Pulses] --> B[Compute Displacements]
    B --> C[Left and Right Wheel Displacements]
    C --> D[Kinematic Update f p,u]
    D --> E[Estimated Pose p_k]
    E --> F[Linearization Jacobian J_f]
    F --> G[Propagate Covariance Sigma_k]
    G --> H[Updated Uncertainty Ellipse]
    E --> I[Add Process Noise Q_k]
    I --> G
    H --> J[Output to Localization Filter]
    E --> J

    subgraph ERR1[Systematic Errors]
        E1[Wheel Diameter Mismatch]
        E2[Wheelbase Uncertainty]
        E3[Encoder Resolution]
    end

    subgraph ERR2[Non-Systematic Errors]
        N1[Floor Unevenness]
        N2[Wheel Slip]
        N3[External Forces]
    end

    ERR1 --> B
    ERR2 --> B
```

### 4.2 Error Growth and Uncertainty Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    P0[Pose at t0: Low Uncertainty] -->|Step 1| P1[Pose at t1: Sigma1]
    P1 -->|Step 2| P2[Pose at t2: Sigma2]
    P2 -->|Step 3| P3[Pose at t3: Sigma3]
    P3 -->|Step N| PN[Pose at tN: SigmaN - Unbounded]

    style P0 fill:#90EE90
    style PN fill:#FFB6C1
```

### 4.3 Functional Block Architecture of Odometric Error Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input: Encoder Pulses N_L, N_R] --> B[Tick to Distance Conversion]
    B --> C[Apply Systematic Error Model E_d, E_b]
    C --> D[Compute Linear and Angular Displacement]
    D --> E[Pose Update via Mid-Point Method]
    E --> F[Estimated Pose]
    D --> G[Compute Jacobian J_f]
    G --> H[Covariance Propagation]
    H --> I[Final Covariance Matrix Sigma_k]
    F --> J[Output to Estimator]
    I --> J
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** Notice how the encoder pulses pass through multiple stages, each introducing a different source of error. The covariance propagation happens *in parallel* to the pose update, providing a measure of *how much we trust* the estimate.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. Define odometric position estimation. List any four sources of systematic errors in odometry. (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**Model Answer:**
Odometric position estimation is the technique of computing a robot's pose (position and orientation) by integrating wheel rotations measured through encoders, assuming pure rolling without slip.

Four sources of systematic errors are:
1. Wheel diameter mismatch ($E_d$) between left and right wheels.
2. Uncertainty in the effective wheelbase ($E_b$).
3. Finite encoder resolution causing quantization.
4. Misalignment of wheels relative to the robot frame.

---

#### **Q2. Differentiate between systematic and non-systematic odometric errors with suitable examples. (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Systematic Errors | Non-Systematic Errors |
|---------|-------------------|----------------------|
| Nature | Deterministic, repeatable | Stochastic, random |
| Source | Robot hardware/calibration | Environment interactions |
| Correction | Calibration | Statistical modeling |
| Example | Wheel diameter mismatch | Wheel slip on wet floor |
| Growth | Proportional to distance | Highly variable |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> A common mistake students make in this topic is **forgetting to use the mid-point angle** in the kinematic update, instead using the *initial* or *final* heading. This introduces a first-order bias that costs **2-3 marks** in part (a) derivations. Additionally, in covariance propagation, students often forget to **transpose the Jacobian correctly** when computing $J_f \Sigma J_f^T$ — partial marks are awarded if the structure of the answer is correct but the arithmetic is wrong.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) Derive the odometric position update equations for a differential-drive robot with wheelbase $b$, given left and right wheel displacements $\Delta s_L$ and $\Delta s_R$. Show the complete mathematical derivation. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1:* Define the differential-drive geometry. The robot's reference frame is at the center of the axle. The left and right wheels are separated by wheelbase $b$.

*Step 2:* Compute the average linear displacement of the robot's center:
$$\Delta s = \frac{\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L}{2}$$

*Step 3:* Compute angular displacement using the arc length relation $r = R\theta$. For the right wheel arc $s_R = (R + b/2)\Delta\theta$ and left wheel arc $s_L = (R - b/2)\Delta\theta$:

$$\Delta\theta = \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{b}$$

*Step 4:* Apply the mid-point integration rule to update global coordinates. The direction of motion is the average of initial and final heading:

$$\theta_{mid} = \theta_{k-1} + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2}$$

*Step 5:* Final pose update:
$$x_k = x_{k-1} + \Delta s \cos(\theta_{mid})$$
$$y_k = y_{k-1} + \Delta s \sin(\theta_{mid})$$
$$\theta_k = \theta_{k-1} + \Delta\theta$$

**(b) With a neat diagram and mathematical formulation, explain the error model for systematic odometric errors proposed by Borenstein and Feng. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1:* Borenstein and Feng classified systematic odometry errors into two main categories:
- **Type A (Equal errors in both wheels):** Causes pure translation error in distance estimation.
- **Type B (Unequal errors in both wheels):** Causes orientation error accumulation.

*Step 2:* Define normalized error parameters:
$$\alpha_i = \frac{D_{i,actual} - D_{i,nominal}}{D_{i,nominal}}$$

*Step 3:* For a straight-line traversal of nominal distance $d_x$, the systematic translation error is:

$$e_d = \alpha_R \cdot d_x \quad \text{or} \quad e_d = \alpha_L \cdot d_x$$

*Step 4:* For a turn, the rotation error $\beta$ accumulates with each rotation:

$$e_\theta = \sum_i \beta_i \cdot \Delta\theta_i$$

*Step 5:* The total systematic position error after traveling distance $D$ is:

$$e_{total} = \alpha \cdot D + \frac{1}{2} \beta \cdot D^2 \cdot \sin(\theta)$$

*Step 6:* This is implemented in the **UMBmark test** (University of Michigan Benchmark) to calibrate systematic errors using a square path traversal.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a) A differential-drive robot with wheelbase $b = 0.4$ m and wheel diameter $D = 0.15$ m has left and right wheel displacements of $\Delta s_L = 0.20$ m and $\Delta s_R = 0.32$ m. The current pose is $(x, y, \theta) = (0, 0, 0)$. Compute the new pose and the propagated covariance, assuming $k_x = k_y = 0.05$, $k_\theta = 0.02$, and prior covariance $\Sigma_0 = \text{diag}(0.01, 0.01, 0.001)$. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1:* Compute the linear and angular displacements:
$$\Delta s = \frac{0.20 + 0.32}{2} = 0.26 \text{ m}$$
$$\Delta\theta = \frac{0.32 - 0.20}{0.4} = 0.30 \text{ rad}$$

*Step 2:* Compute the mid-point heading (initial $\theta = 0$):
$$\theta_{mid} = 0 + \frac{0.30}{2} = 0.15 \text{ rad}$$

*Step 3:* Pose update:
$$x_k = 0 + 0.26 \cos(0.15) = 0.26 \times 0.9888 = 0.2571 \text{ m}$$
$$y_k = 0 + 0.26 \sin(0.15) = 0.26 \times 0.1494 = 0.0388 \text{ m}$$
$$\theta_k = 0 + 0.30 = 0.30 \text{ rad}$$

*Step 4:* Compute the Jacobian at $\theta_{mid} = 0.15$:
$$J_f = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -0.26 \sin(0.15) \\ 0 & 1 & 0.26 \cos(0.15) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -0.0388 \\ 0 & 1 & 0.2571 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

*Step 5:* Compute $J_f \Sigma_0 J_f^T$:
- $(1,1)$: $0.01 + 0.0015 + 0.0000015 \approx 0.0102$
- $(1,2)$: $-0.0100 \times 0.0388 \times 0.2571 / \text{computation gives } -0.0000997$
- $(2,2)$: $0.01 + 0.00066 \approx 0.0101$

$$J_f \Sigma_0 J_f^T = \begin{bmatrix} 0.0102 & -0.0001 & 0 \\ -0.0001 & 0.0101 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.001 \end{bmatrix}$$

*Step 6:* Compute $Q_k$:
$$Q_k = \text{diag}(0.05 \times 0.0676, 0.05 \times 0.0676, 0.0004 \times 0.0676) = \text{diag}(0.00338, 0.00338, 0.000027)$$

*Step 7:* Final covariance:
$$\Sigma_k = \begin{bmatrix} 0.0136 & -0.0001 & 0 \\ -0.0001 & 0.0135 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.00103 \end{bmatrix}$$

The position uncertainty increased from 0.01 m² to ~0.0136 m² in a single step.

---

**(b) Discuss in detail the propagation of uncertainty in odometric position estimation. Explain the role of the process noise covariance matrix $Q_k$ and its physical interpretation. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1:* Define uncertainty propagation. The covariance matrix $\Sigma_k$ characterizes the 2D Gaussian uncertainty ellipse around the estimated pose.

*Step 2:* The recursive propagation equation is:
$$\Sigma_k = J_f \Sigma_{k-1} J_f^T + Q_k$$

*Step 3:* **Role of $J_f$:** It propagates the previous uncertainty into the new pose frame. It accounts for the geometric coupling between position and orientation uncertainty (e.g., a small angular error at a high speed causes large lateral position error).

*Step 4:* **Role of $Q_k$:** The process noise covariance matrix represents the *additional* uncertainty introduced at each step by unmodeled effects (slip, uneven floor, etc.).

*Step 5:* **Physical interpretation of $Q_k$:**
- $Q_k = \text{diag}(k_x \Delta s^2, k_y \Delta s^2, k_\theta^2 \Delta s^2)$
- The variance grows **quadratically with distance** $\Delta s$, reflecting the cumulative nature of odometric drift.
- $k_x, k_y$ represent the linear slip-induced errors; $k_\theta$ represents heading uncertainty growth.

*Step 6:* **Consequence:** As the robot travels, the uncertainty ellipse grows. This is why external sensors must periodically "correct" the pose estimate — the basis of the **Kalman Filter localization algorithm**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Read 5 minutes before the exam):**

- **Definition:** Odometry = pose estimation via wheel encoder integration. Error Model = framework quantifying drift.
- **Two error classes:** Systematic (calibratable, grows with distance) and Non-systematic (stochastic, environment-driven).
- **Key equations:**
  - $\Delta s = (\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L)/2$
  - $\Delta \theta = (\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L)/b$
  - Use **mid-point heading** $\theta + \Delta\theta/2$ in the update.
- **Covariance propagation:** $\Sigma_k = J_f \Sigma_{k-1} J_f^T + Q_k$ — memorize this exactly.
- **Jacobian $J_f$:** 3×3 matrix, with off-diagonal terms $-\Delta s \sin(\theta_{mid})$ and $\Delta s \cos(\theta_{mid})$.
- **Process noise $Q_k$:** Diagonal matrix, grows quadratically with $\Delta s$.
- **Borenstein-Feng model:** UMBmark test for systematic error calibration via square path.
- **Error growth:** Position error is **unbounded** with travel distance — never use odometry alone for long-range navigation.
- **Exam trap:** Always use **radians** (not degrees) in trigonometric functions.
- **Exam trap:** The Jacobian entry for the y-equation is $+\Delta s \cos(\theta_{mid})$, not $-\Delta s \sin(\theta_{mid})$.
- **Connection to next modules:** This error model feeds directly into the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** for localization in Module 3.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
