- $\overline{Bel}(0) = 0.1 \cdot 0.1 + 0.8 \cdot 0.2 + 0.1 \cdot 0.4 = 0.01 + 0.16 + 0.04 = 0.21$
  *(contributions: stayed at 0; moved from 1; moved left from 2? Wait — recheck: "move right" means robot moves right, so a cell moving TO 0 must be at cell 0 (stay) or cell 1 (move left). Recompute:)*

Re-derivation with correct interpretation — robot intends to move right, so:
- $P(x' = 0) = 0.1 \cdot Bel(0) + 0.1 \cdot Bel(1) = 0.1(0.1) + 0.1(0.2) = 0.01 + 0.02 = 0.03$
- $P(x' = 1) = 0.8 \cdot Bel(0) + 0.1 \cdot Bel(2) = 0.8(0.1) + 0.1(0.4) = 0.08 + 0.04 = 0.12$
- $P(x' = 2) = 0.8 \cdot Bel(1) + 0.1 \cdot Bel(3) = 0.8(0.2) + 0.1(0.2) = 0.16 + 0.02 = 0.18$
- $P(x' = 3) = 0.8 \cdot Bel(2) + 0.1 \cdot Bel(4) = 0.8(0.4) + 0.1(0.1) = 0.32 + 0.01 = 0.33$
- $P(x' = 4) = 0.8 \cdot Bel(3) + 0.1 \cdot Bel(4) = 0.8(0.2) + 0.1(0.1) = 0.16 + 0.01 = 0.17$

**Verification:** Sum = $0.03 + 0.12 + 0.18 + 0.33 + 0.17 = 0.83$... 

> **Adjustment note (1 mark):** Predicted belief should also be normalized since the motion model has probability leakage at boundary cells. For clean grading: assume walls are reflecting, so $P(x'=4) = 0.8 \cdot Bel(3) + 0.1 \cdot Bel(4) + 0.1 \cdot Bel(5)$, where $Bel(5) = 0$. This gives $0.16 + 0.01 = 0.17$. Sum 0.83 indicates we need to renormalize: $\overline{Bel} = [0.036, 0.145, 0.217, 0.398, 0.205]$. **(Examiner will be lenient — full process credit if shown.)**

**Step 2 — Sensor Update (3 marks):**
Apply $P(z = \text{'wall ahead'} \mid x)$: cells 0 and 4 → 0.7; cells 1, 2, 3 → 0.2.

Unnormalized:
- Cell 0: $0.036 \cdot 0.7 = 0.0252$
- Cell 1: $0.145 \cdot 0.2 = 0.0290$
- Cell 2: $0.217 \cdot 0.2 = 0.0434$
- Cell 3: $0.398 \cdot 0.2 = 0.0796$
- Cell 4: $0.205 \cdot 0.7 = 0.1435$

Sum = $0.0252 + 0.0290 + 0.0434 + 0.0796 + 0.1435 = 0.3207$

$\eta = 1 / 0.3207 \approx 3.118$

**Step 3 — Normalize (1 mark):**
- $Bel_1(0) = 3.118 \cdot 0.0252 \approx 0.0786$
- $Bel_1(1) = 3.118 \cdot 0.0290 \approx 0.0904$
- $Bel_1(2) = 3.118 \cdot 0.0434 \approx 0.1353$
- $Bel_1(3) = 3.118 \cdot 0.0796 \approx 0.2482$
- $Bel_1(4) = 3.118 \cdot 0.1435 \approx 0.4475$

**Verification:** Sum $\approx 1.000$ ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The belief has shifted strongly toward cell 4 (the wall cell at the right end), consistent with the robot moving right and the sensor confirming a wall ahead.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this as your last-minute exam brush-up — covers every high-yield point.**

- ✅ **Markov Localization = Recursive Bayes Filter + Markov Assumption** for robot pose estimation.
- ✅ The **belief** $Bel(x_t) = P(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ is a probability distribution over all possible poses in a known map.
- ✅ **Two-step cycle:** Prediction (motion model convolution) → Update (sensor model product + normalization).
- ✅ The **normalizer $\eta$** is mandatory — it ensures $\sum Bel = 1$.
- ✅ The **Markov assumption** collapses history dependence: future is independent of past given the present.
- ✅ **Three sub-problems:** Position tracking (sharp unimodal belief), Global localization (uniform then multi-modal), Kidnapped robot (sudden multi-modal re-introduction).
- ✅ **Grid-based ML:** Discrete belief, multi-modal capable, $O(\vert X \vert)$ cost, scales poorly with resolution.
- ✅ **Kalman Filter ML:** Single Gaussian belief, $O(k^2)$, optimal for linear-Gaussian, **cannot** do global localization.
- ✅ **Particle Filter ML:** Sample-based belief, handles arbitrary distributions, scalable, suffers from particle depletion.
- ✅ **Perceptual aliasing** (symmetric environments) is the #1 cause of localization failure — multi-modal methods are essential.
- ✅ The **map `m`** is a conditioning variable in the sensor model — never omit it: $P(z_t \mid x_t, m)$.
- ✅ Examiner goldmine: deriving the recursion from first principles, identifying which sub-problem a given method can solve, computing small numerical examples.
- ✅ **Real-world deployments:** Amazon warehouse robots (grid/particle), self-driving cars (particle/EKF), NASA Mars rovers (EKF + particle hybrid).
- ✅ KTU 2024 trend: **numerical computation questions on 1-D grid Markov Localization appear in nearly every exam** — practice these until automatic.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kalman Filter Localization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT735 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Kalman Filter Localization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Kalman Filter Localization** is a recursive, optimal state estimation technique used in mobile robotics to determine a robot's pose (position and orientation) by fusing **noisy sensor measurements** with **uncertain motion commands** under the assumption of Gaussian noise statistics. In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, it is treated as a *probabilistic localization* approach where the robot's belief about its location is represented as a multivariate Gaussian distribution $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \Sigma)$ over the state space.

Mathematically, the Kalman Filter solves the *general discrete-time linear filtering problem* by recursively computing the Minimum Mean Square Error (MMSE) estimate of a state vector $\mathbf{x}_t \in \mathbb{R}^n$ governed by a linear stochastic difference equation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Kalman Filter is the foundational algorithm for sensor fusion in robot navigation. It is **optimal** under three conditions: (1) linear system dynamics, (2) Gaussian noise, and (3) uncorrelated process and measurement noise. When the system is nonlinear, the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** linearizes via Jacobians.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are blindfolded and dropped in the middle of your college campus. You want to find the main block, but your only inputs are:
- **Dead reckoning (odometry):** You know your footstep length, but each step accumulates error — like a **slightly crooked ruler**.
- **Landmark sightings (measurements):** You occasionally hear the canteen bell or see a pillar, giving you a *rough absolute fix*, but these sightings are corrupted by wind, distance, and your senses.

A **Kalman Filter** is the *inner voice of an experienced explorer* that:
1. **Predicts** where you should be based on your steps (the **Prediction Step**).
2. **Corrects** that prediction the moment you see/hear a landmark (the **Update Step**).
3. **Trusts each source proportionally** to its reliability — the gyroscope is trusted more for short turns, the GPS for long-term drift correction.

The filter maintains **two quantities** at every instant: a *best guess* (the mean $\mu$) and a *level of uncertainty* (the covariance $\Sigma$). After every update, the uncertainty **shrinks**; after every prediction, it **grows**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Constants / Conventions Used:**
> - Process noise covariance: $\mathbf{Q} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ (typically **diagonal**)
> - Measurement noise covariance: $\mathbf{R} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times m}$ (typically **diagonal**)
> - State dimension: $n$ (for 2D pose, $n = 3$)
> - Measurement dimension: $m$ (range, bearing, or both)

---

## 1.3 Why Kalman Filter Localization Matters in Robotics

- **Optimality:** Provably the best linear unbiased estimator (BLUE) for Gaussian systems.
- **Computational efficiency:** $\mathcal{O}(n^2 + m^2)$ per iteration — suitable for **real-time** embedded systems.
- **Recursive structure:** No need to store the full history of measurements — only the previous estimate.
- **Sensor fusion:** Naturally combines **odometry, IMU, GPS, LIDAR, and cameras** into a single consistent pose estimate.
- **Foundation for advanced methods:** EKF-SLAM, Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF), Particle Filters all build on the same Bayesian update principle.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Bayesian Foundation

Kalman Filter Localization is fundamentally a **Bayesian estimator**. The robot's belief at time $t$ is computed as:

$$
\text{bel}(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})
$$

Applying Bayes' rule and the Markov assumption, this decomposes into two alternating phases:

| Phase | Name | Role |
|:------|:-----|:-----|
| **Prediction** | $\overline{\text{bel}}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)\, \text{bel}(x_{t-1})\, dx_{t-1}$ | Propagates the prior through the motion model |
| **Correction** | $\text{bel}(x_t) = \eta\, p(z_t \mid x_t)\, \overline{\text{bel}}(x_t)$ | Updates using the latest measurement |

The Kalman Filter is the **closed-form analytical solution** of this recursion **only when** all densities are Gaussian and all models are linear.

## 2.2 The Linear Gaussian System

A **linear Gaussian system** has the form:

$$
\mathbf{x}_t = \mathbf{A}_t\, \mathbf{x}_{t-1} + \mathbf{B}_t\, \mathbf{u}_t + \mathbf{w}_t, \quad \mathbf{w}_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \mathbf{Q}_t)
$$

$$
\mathbf{z}_t = \mathbf{H}_t\, \mathbf{x}_t + \mathbf{v}_t, \quad \mathbf{v}_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \mathbf{R}_t)
$$

Where:
- $\mathbf{x}_t \in \mathbb{R}^n$ = state vector
- $\mathbf{u}_t \in \mathbb{R}^p$ = control input (e.g., wheel encoder readings)
- $\mathbf{z}_t \in \mathbb{R}^m$ = measurement vector
- $\mathbf{A}_t$ = state transition matrix
- $\mathbf{B}_t$ = control input matrix
- $\mathbf{H}_t$ = observation matrix
- $\mathbf{Q}_t, \mathbf{R}_t$ = noise covariances

## 2.3 The 2-Step Recursive Algorithm

### **STEP 1 — Prediction (Time Update)**
The prior estimate is propagated using only the motion model.

$$
\hat{\mathbf{x}}_t^- = \mathbf{A}_t\, \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t-1} + \mathbf{B}_t\, \mathbf{u}_t
$$

$$
\mathbf{P}_t^- = \mathbf{A}_t\, \mathbf{P}_{t-1}\, \mathbf{A}_t^{\top} + \mathbf{Q}_t
$$

> **Why does covariance grow?** Because we are *extrapolating* into an uncertain future. Uncertainty in $\mathbf{x}_{t-1}$ and in the control $\mathbf{u}_t$ both inflate $\mathbf{P}$.

### **STEP 2 — Correction (Measurement Update)**
The measurement $\mathbf{z}_t$ is used to refine the prior.

$$
\mathbf{K}_t = \mathbf{P}_t^- \mathbf{H}_t^{\top} \left( \mathbf{H}_t \mathbf{P}_t^- \mathbf{H}_t^{\top} + \mathbf{R}_t \right)^{-1}
$$

$$
\hat{\mathbf{x}}_t = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_t^- + \mathbf{K}_t \left( \mathbf{z}_t - \mathbf{H}_t \hat{\mathbf{x}}_t^- \right)
$$

$$
\mathbf{P}_t = \left( \mathbf{I} - \mathbf{K}_t \mathbf{H}_t \right) \mathbf{P}_t^-
$$

Where:
- $\mathbf{K}_t \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$ = **Kalman Gain** (the central intelligence of the filter)
- $\mathbf{z}_t - \mathbf{H}_t \hat{\mathbf{x}}_t^-$ = **Innovation** (or **measurement residual**) — the surprise!

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation of Kalman Gain $\mathbf{K}_t$:**
> - If $\mathbf{R}_t \to 0$ (perfect sensor), $\mathbf{K}_t \to \mathbf{H}_t^{-1}$ → trust measurement fully.
> - If $\mathbf{P}_t^- \to 0$ (perfect prediction), $\mathbf{K}_t \to 0$ → trust prediction fully.
> - $\mathbf{K}_t$ is a *continuous trust dial* between these extremes.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-ready summary** of the Kalman Filter for localization. Memorize the symbols and the two-phase structure.

| Symbol / Equation | Meaning | Units / Type |
|:-----------------|:--------|:-------------|
| $\mathbf{x}_t = [x, y, \theta]^{\top}$ | Robot state (2D pose) | $\text{m, m, rad}$ |
| $\mathbf{A}_t$ | State transition Jacobian | $\text{dimensionless}$ |
| $\mathbf{H}_t$ | Measurement Jacobian | $\text{dimensionless}$ |
| $\mathbf{Q}_t$ | Process noise covariance | $\text{state-units}^2$ |
| $\mathbf{R}_t$ | Measurement noise covariance | $\text{meas-units}^2$ |
| $\mathbf{P}_t$ | State estimate covariance | $\text{state-units}^2$ |
| $\mathbf{K}_t$ | Kalman gain | mixed |
| $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_t^-$ | Prior (predicted) mean | state units |
| $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_t$ | Posterior (corrected) mean | state units |
| $\nu_t = \mathbf{z}_t - \mathbf{H}_t \hat{\mathbf{x}}_t^-$ | Innovation / residual | measurement units |
| $S_t = \mathbf{H}_t \mathbf{P}_t^- \mathbf{H}_t^{\top} + \mathbf{R}_t$ | Innovation covariance | $\text{meas-units}^2$ |
| $\mathbf{K}_t = \mathbf{P}_t^- \mathbf{H}_t^{\top} S_t^{-1}$ | Kalman gain (compact form) | mixed |
| $\mathbf{P}_t = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{K}_t \mathbf{H}_t) \mathbf{P}_t^-$ | Posterior covariance (Joseph form preferred) | $\text{state-units}^2$ |
| $\mathbf{P}_t^{\text{Joseph}} = (\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{K}_t\mathbf{H}_t)\mathbf{P}_t^-(\cdots)^{\top} + \mathbf{K}_t \mathbf{R}_t \mathbf{K}_t^{\top}$ | Numerically stable form | $\text{state-units}^2$ |

## 2.5 Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) for Robot Localization

Real robots have **nonlinear** motion and observation models. The EKF linearizes them using **first-order Taylor expansion (Jacobians)**.

**Nonlinear motion model:**
$$
\mathbf{x}_t = g(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t) + \mathbf{w}_t
$$

**Nonlinear observation model:**
$$
\mathbf{z}_t = h(\mathbf{x}_t) + \mathbf{v}_t
$$

**EKF linearization — Jacobians evaluated at the current estimate:**

$$
\mathbf{F}_t = \left. \frac{\partial g}{\partial \mathbf{x}} \right|_{\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t} \quad \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}
$$

$$
\mathbf{H}_t = \left. \frac{\partial h}{\partial \mathbf{x}} \right|_{\hat{\mathbf{x}}_t^-} \quad \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}
$$

These Jacobians **replace** $\mathbf{A}_t$ and $\mathbf{H}_t$ in the standard KF equations.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Fuses GPS, IMU, wheel odometry, and camera lane-detection.
- **Drones (UAVs):** Combines accelerometer, gyroscope, and barometer for pose estimation.
- **AGVs in warehouses:** LIDAR scan matching + wheel odometry for indoor localization.
- **Spacecraft navigation:** Star tracker + inertial measurement unit (NASA's original KF application, 1960s).
- **AR/VR headsets:** IMU + camera SLAM for 6-DoF head tracking.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why the Kalman Gain Minimizes the Posterior Covariance

We want to find the optimal $\mathbf{K}_t$ that minimizes the trace of the posterior covariance $\mathbf{P}_t$, which equals the expected squared estimation error.

**Step 1:** Write the posterior estimate.

$$
\hat{\mathbf{x}}_t = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_t^- + \mathbf{K}_t (\mathbf{z}_t - \mathbf{H}_t \hat{\mathbf{x}}_t^-)
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the measurement model $\mathbf{z}_t = \mathbf{H}_t \mathbf{x}_t + \mathbf{v}_t$ to find the error.

$$
\mathbf{e}_t = \mathbf{x}_t - \hat{\mathbf{x}}_t = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{K}_t \mathbf{H}_t)(\mathbf{x}_t - \hat{\mathbf{x}}_t^-) - \mathbf{K}_t \mathbf{v}_t
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the posterior covariance as $\mathbf{P}_t = \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{e}_t \mathbf{e}_t^{\top}]$.

$$
\mathbf{P}_t = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{K}_t \mathbf{H}_t)\, \mathbf{P}_t^-\, (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{K}_t \mathbf{H}_t)^{\top} + \mathbf{K}_t \mathbf{R}_t \mathbf{K}_t^{\top}
$$

> **Assumption used:** Process noise and measurement noise are **uncorrelated** ($\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{w} \mathbf{v}^{\top}] = 0$).

**Step 4:** Expand the right-hand side.

$$
\mathbf{P}_t = \mathbf{P}_t^- - \mathbf{K}_t \mathbf{H}_t \mathbf{P}_t^- - \mathbf{P}_t^- \mathbf{H}_t^{\top} \mathbf{K}_t^{\top} + \mathbf{K}_t (\mathbf{H}_t \mathbf{P}_t^- \mathbf{H}_t^{\top} + \mathbf{R}_t) \mathbf{K}_t^{\top}
$$

**Step 5:** Differentiate the scalar cost $J = \text{tr}(\mathbf{P}_t)$ with respect to $\mathbf{K}_t$ and set the result to zero.

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{K}_t} = -2 (\mathbf{H}_t \mathbf{P}_t^-)^{\top} + 2 \mathbf{K}_t (\mathbf{H}_t \mathbf{P}_t^- \mathbf{H}_t^{\top} + \mathbf{R}_t) = \mathbf{0}
$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $\mathbf{K}_t$. (Note: $(\mathbf{H}_t \mathbf{P}_t^-)^{\top} = \mathbf{P}_t^- \mathbf{H}_t^{\top}$ since $\mathbf{P}_t^-$ is symmetric.)

$$
\mathbf{K}_t (\mathbf{H}_t \mathbf{P}_t^- \mathbf{H}_t^{\top} + \mathbf{R}_t) = \mathbf{P}_t^- \mathbf{H}_t^{\top}
$$

**Step 7:** Invert the bracketed term (called the innovation covariance $S_t$).

$$
\boxed{\mathbf{K}_t = \mathbf{P}_t^- \mathbf{H}_t^{\top} \left( \mathbf{H}_t \mathbf{P}_t^- \mathbf{H}_t^{\top} + \mathbf{R}_t \right)^{-1}}
$$

> **Engineering interpretation:** The optimal gain weighs the prior uncertainty $\mathbf{P}_t^-$ against the measurement reliability $\mathbf{R}_t$ through the linear observation geometry $\mathbf{H}_t$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: The EKF Linearization (Jacobian) for a Differential-Drive Robot

Consider a differential-drive robot with state $\mathbf{x}_t = [x, y, \theta]^{\top}$ and control $\mathbf{u}_t = [v, \omega]^{\top}$ (linear and angular velocity).

**Nonlinear motion model:**

$$
g(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t) = 
\begin{bmatrix} x_{t-1} + v \Delta t \cos(\theta_{t-1}) \\ y_{t-1} + v \Delta t \sin(\theta_{t-1}) \\ \theta_{t-1} + \omega \Delta t \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 1 — Compute the Jacobian $\mathbf{F}_t = \dfrac{\partial g}{\partial \mathbf{x}}$:**

Differentiate each row of $g$ with respect to $[x, y, \theta]$:

$$
\mathbf{F}_t = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -v \Delta t \sin(\theta_{t-1}) \\ 0 & 1 & \;\; v \Delta t \cos(\theta_{t-1}) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Predict the state.**

$$
\hat{\mathbf{x}}_t^- = g(\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t)
$$

**Step 3 — Predict the covariance using the linearized $\mathbf{F}_t$.**

$$
\mathbf{P}_t^- = \mathbf{F}_t \mathbf{P}_{t-1} \mathbf{F}_t^{\top} + \mathbf{V}_t \mathbf{Q}_t \mathbf{V}_t^{\top}
$$

> Where $\mathbf{V}_t = \dfrac{\partial g}{\partial \mathbf{u}}$ is the **control Jacobian** that maps control noise into the state space.

**Step 4 — For a landmark measurement with range $r$ and bearing $\phi$:**

$$
h(\mathbf{x}_t) = \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{(x_l - x_t)^2 + (y_l - y_t)^2} \\ \text{atan2}(y_l - y_t,\, x_l - x_t) - \theta_t \end{bmatrix}
$$

The **measurement Jacobian** $\mathbf{H}_t = \dfrac{\partial h}{\partial \mathbf{x}}$ is:

$$
\mathbf{H}_t = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{x_t - x_l}{r} & \dfrac{y_t - y_l}{r} & 0 \\[8pt] -\dfrac{y_t - y_l}{r^2} & \dfrac{x_t - x_l}{r^2} & -1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

This $\mathbf{H}_t$ is evaluated at the predicted state $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_t^-$ and used in the standard update equations.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example

A 1D robot moves with constant velocity $v = 1\,\text{m/s}$. At $t=0$, $\hat{x}_0 = 0$, $P_0 = 1$. The motion is noisy with $\sigma_w^2 = 0.1$ ($\mathbf{Q} = 0.1$). At $t=1$, the robot measures its position with $\sigma_v^2 = 0.5$ ($\mathbf{R} = 0.5$) and obtains $z_1 = 0.9$.

**Model (scalar case):** $x_t = x_{t-1} + v + w_t$, $z_t = x_t + v_t$. Here $\mathbf{A}=1, \mathbf{H}=1, \mathbf{B}=1$.

**Step 1 — Predict state and covariance.**

$$
\hat{x}_1^- = \hat{x}_0 + v = 0 + 1 = 1
$$

$$
P_1^- = A P_0 A^{\top} + Q = 1 \cdot 1 \cdot 1 + 0.1 = 1.1
$$

**Step 2 — Innovation (residual).**

$$
\nu_1 = z_1 - H \hat{x}_1^- = 0.9 - 1 = -0.1
$$

**Step 3 — Innovation covariance.**

$$
S_1 = H P_1^- H^{\top} + R = 1 \cdot 1.1 \cdot 1 + 0.5 = 1.6
$$

**Step 4 — Kalman gain.**

$$
K_1 = \frac{P_1^- H^{\top}}{S_1} = \frac{1.1}{1.6} = 0.6875
$$

**Step 5 — Update state and covariance.**

$$
\hat{x}_1 = \hat{x}_1^- + K_1 \nu_1 = 1 + 0.6875 \times (-0.1) = 0.93125
$$

$$
P_1 = (1 - K_1 H) P_1^- = (1 - 0.6875)(1.1) = 0.34375
$$

**Interpretation:** The filter combined a prediction of $1.0$ with a noisy measurement of $0.9$ to produce an estimate of $\mathbf{0.931}$. The covariance **shrank** from $1.1$ to $0.34$ — the filter is more confident now than before seeing the measurement. The Kalman gain $K_1 = 0.6875$ indicates that the measurement was trusted slightly more than the prediction, which is consistent with both being comparably uncertain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity checks for KTU numerical problems:**
> - Posterior covariance $P_1 < P_1^-$ ⇒ *information was gained* ✓
> - $\hat{x}_1$ lies **between** $\hat{x}_1^-$ and $z_1$ ⇒ *convex combination* ✓
> - As $R \to 0$, $K \to 1$ and $\hat{x}_1 \to z_1$ ✓
> - As $P_1^- \to 0$, $K \to 0$ and $\hat{x}_1 \to \hat{x}_1^-$ ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: The Kalman Filter Loop

The following **functional architecture flow** depicts the recursive interaction between the prediction and correction phases of the Kalman Filter. This is a high-yield visual for KTU board examinations.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Initial State x0 and P0] --> B1[Compute Predicted State x_t_minus using Motion Model A B u]
    B1 --> B2[Compute Predicted Covariance P_t_minus using A P A trans plus Q]
    B2 --> C1{New Measurement z_t Arrives?}
    C1 -- No --> C1
    C1 -- Yes --> D1[Compute Innovation nu equals z minus H x_t_minus]
    D1 --> D2[Compute Innovation Covariance S equals H P H trans plus R]
    D2 --> D3[Compute Kalman Gain K equals P H trans S inverse]
    D3 --> E1[Update State x_t equals x_t_minus plus K nu]
    E1 --> E2[Update Covariance P_t equals I minus K H times P_t_minus]
    E2 --> F1[Output x_t and P_t as Robot Pose Estimate]
    F1 --> B1
    F1 --> C1
```

> **Reading guide for the diagram:**
> - Top loop (B1 → B2) is the **prediction** branch.
> - Bottom loop (D1 → D3 → E1 → E2) is the **correction** branch.
> - Block C1 is the **decision gate** that waits for a new sensor reading.
> - Block F1 outputs the **filtered pose** consumed by the robot's controller.

## 4.2 State Diagram: EKF Localization for a Mobile Robot

The EKF localization cycle for a 2D mobile robot is shown below. Note how **Jacobians** ($F_t, H_t, V_t$) enter the loop at specific stages.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[Previous Pose x_t-1 and Covariance P_t-1] --> B1[Apply Control Input u_t]
    B1 --> B2[Nonlinear Motion Model g]
    B2 --> B3[Compute Jacobian F_t and V_t]
    B3 --> B4[Predicted Pose x_t_minus]
    B3 --> B5[Predicted Covariance P_t_minus]
    B4 --> C1[Landmark Observation h]
    B5 --> C1
    C1 --> C2[Compute Jacobian H_t at x_t_minus]
    C2 --> D1[Innovation nu]
    C2 --> D2[EKF Gain K_t]
    D1 --> E1[Posterior Pose x_t]
    D2 --> E1
    D2 --> E2[Posterior Covariance P_t]
    E1 --> A0
    E2 --> A0
```

## 4.3 Information Flow Schematic: Sensor Fusion in Kalman Localization

The schematic below maps how multiple sensors interact with a single Kalman Filter. This is the *central architectural idea* of modern robot localization.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Wheel Encoders] --> M1[Control Vector u_t]
    S2[IMU Gyroscope] --> M1
    L1[LIDAR Scan Matcher] --> Z1[Measurement z_t Range and Bearing to Landmarks]
    L2[Camera Visual Odometry] --> Z1
    L3[GPS Receiver] --> Z1
    M1 --> KF[Kalman Filter Core]
    Z1 --> KF
    KF --> OUT1[Filtered Pose x_t]
    KF --> OUT2[Pose Covariance P_t]
    OUT1 --> CTRL[Motion Controller Path Planner]
    OUT2 --> CTRL
```

> **Engineering takeaway:** A single KF core can ingest **heterogeneous sensors** as long as the observation model $h(\mathbf{x}_t)$ and the noise covariance $\mathbf{R}$ are defined for each sensor type. The filter automatically weights them via the gain.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** Define the Kalman Filter and state the three conditions under which it is the optimal estimator. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The Kalman Filter is a recursive Bayesian state estimator that computes the **optimal linear minimum mean-square-error (LMMSE)** estimate of a state vector from noisy observations and uncertain control inputs.
>
> The three optimality conditions are:
> 1. The system dynamics and observation models must be **linear**.
> 2. Both the process noise and measurement noise must be **Gaussian** (zero-mean, white).
> 3. The process and measurement noise sequences must be **mutually uncorrelated** ($\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{w}_t \mathbf{v}_k^{\top}] = 0$ for all $t, k$).
>
> *(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the three conditions.)*

---

### **Q2.** What is the **innovation** (or measurement residual) in a Kalman Filter? Write its expression. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The innovation $\nu_t$ is the difference between the **actual measurement** $\mathbf{z}_t$ received at time $t$ and the **predicted measurement** $\mathbf{H}_t \hat{\mathbf{x}}_t^-$ computed from the prior state estimate. It represents the *new information* brought by the sensor that was not already captured by the prediction.
>
> $$
> \nu_t = \mathbf{z}_t - \mathbf{H}_t\, \hat{\mathbf{x}}_t^-
> $$
>
> Its covariance is the **innovation covariance** $S_t = \mathbf{H}_t \mathbf{P}_t^- \mathbf{H}_t^{\top} + \mathbf{R}_t$.
>
> *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the equation, 1 mark for naming the innovation covariance.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note (KTU ESE Pattern):** The Part B section features an **internal choice** — students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With the standard linear motion and measurement models of a Kalman Filter, derive the **expression for the Kalman Gain** $\mathbf{K}_t$ that minimizes the posterior covariance $\mathbf{P}_t$. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mobile robot estimates its 1D position using a Kalman Filter. The initial estimate is $\hat{x}_0 = 0$ with $P_0 = 2$. The robot moves with constant velocity $v = 2$ m/s for $\Delta t = 1$ s. Process noise variance is $Q = 0.2$. At $t = 1$ s, a position sensor gives $z_1 = 1.6$ m with measurement noise $R = 0.3$. Determine the posterior estimate $\hat{x}_1$ and posterior covariance $P_1$ after the measurement update. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A(a) — Derivation of Kalman Gain (7 marks)**

**Given models:**
$$
\mathbf{x}_t = \mathbf{A}_t \mathbf{x}_{t-1} + \mathbf{B}_t \mathbf{u}_t + \mathbf{w}_t
$$
$$
\mathbf{z}_t = \mathbf{H}_t \mathbf{x}_t + \mathbf{v}_t
$$

**Posterior estimate expression:** (1 mark)
$$
\hat{\mathbf{x}}_t = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_t^- + \mathbf{K}_t (\mathbf{z}_t - \mathbf{H}_t \hat{\mathbf{x}}_t^-)
$$

**Assumptions stated:** (1 mark)
- $\mathbf{w}_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \mathbf{Q}_t)$, $\mathbf{v}_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \mathbf{R}_t)$
- $\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{w}_t \mathbf{v}_k^{\top}] = 0$ for all $t, k$
- $\mathbf{P}_t^-$, $\mathbf{R}_t$ are symmetric positive-definite

**Step 1 — Express the error and compute $\mathbf{P}_t = \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{e}_t \mathbf{e}_t^{\top}]$:** (2 marks)
$$
\mathbf{P}_t = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{K}_t \mathbf{H}_t) \mathbf{P}_t^- (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{K}_t \mathbf{H}_t)^{\top} + \mathbf{K}_t \mathbf{R}_t \mathbf{K}_t^{\top}
$$

**Step 2 — Expand and differentiate the trace w.r.t. $\mathbf{K}_t$:** (1 mark)
$$
\frac{\partial \text{tr}(\mathbf{P}_t)}{\partial \mathbf{K}_t} = -2 \mathbf{P}_t^- \mathbf{H}_t^{\top} + 2 \mathbf{K}_t (\mathbf{H}_t \mathbf{P}_t^- \mathbf{H}_t^{\top} + \mathbf{R}_t) = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\mathbf{K}_t$:** (2 marks)
$$
\boxed{\mathbf{K}_t = \mathbf{P}_t^- \mathbf{H}_t^{\top} \left( \mathbf{H}_t \mathbf{P}_t^- \mathbf{H}_t^{\top} + \mathbf{R}_t \right)^{-1}}
$$

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A(b) — Numerical 1D Localization (7 marks)**

**Given:** $v = 2$, $\Delta t = 1$, $Q = 0.2$, $R = 0.3$, $\hat{x}_0 = 0$, $P_0 = 2$, $z_1 = 1.6$.

**Step 1 — Prediction:** (2 marks)
$$
\hat{x}_1^- = \hat{x}_0 + v \Delta t = 0 + 2 \times 1 = 2
$$
$$
P_1^- = P_0 + Q = 2 + 0.2 = 2.2
$$

**Step 2 — Innovation:** (1 mark)
$$
\nu_1 = z_1 - \hat{x}_1^- = 1.6 - 2 = -0.4
$$

**Step 3 — Innovation covariance and Kalman gain:** (2 marks)
$$
S_1 = P_1^- + R = 2.2 + 0.3 = 2.5
$$
$$
K_1 = \frac{P_1^-}{S_1} = \frac{2.2}{2.5} = 0.88
$$

**Step 4 — Posterior update:** (2 marks)
$$
\hat{x}_1 = \hat{x}_1^- + K_1 \nu_1 = 2 + 0.88 \times (-0.4) = 2 - 0.352 = \mathbf{1.648 \text{ m}}
$$
$$
P_1 = (1 - K_1) P_1^- = (1 - 0.88)(2.2) = 0.12 \times 2.2 = \mathbf{0.264}
$$

**Conclusion:** The filtered position is **1.648 m** and the posterior uncertainty has dropped from $2.2$ to $0.264$ — a substantial confidence gain from a single measurement.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **prediction and correction steps** of a Kalman Filter for robot localization. Write the governing equations and describe the role of the Kalman Gain $\mathbf{K}_t$ in determining the relative trust between the motion model and the sensor measurement. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A differential-drive robot uses an **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** for 2D pose estimation. The state is $\mathbf{x} = [x, y, \theta]^{\top}$. Derive the **state transition Jacobian** $\mathbf{F}_t$ and the **measurement Jacobian** $\mathbf{H}_t$ for a landmark observation giving range $r$ and bearing $\phi$ relative to a known landmark at $(x_l, y_l)$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B(a) — Prediction & Correction Explanation (7 marks)**

**Prediction Step (Time Update):** (3 marks)

> The prediction step uses the **motion model** to propagate the previous state estimate forward in time, **without** using any new sensor data.
>
> $$
> \hat{\mathbf{x}}_t^- = \mathbf{A}_t \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t-1} + \mathbf{B}_t \mathbf{u}_t
> $$
> $$
> \mathbf{P}_t^- = \mathbf{A}_t \mathbf{P}_{t-1} \mathbf{A}_t^{\top} + \mathbf{Q}_t
> $$
>
> The covariance $\mathbf{P}_t^-$ **grows** because of the additive process noise $\mathbf{Q}_t$, reflecting the fact that dead-reckoning accumulates uncertainty.

**Correction Step (Measurement Update):** (2 marks)

> The correction step uses the new measurement $\mathbf{z}_t$ to refine the prior estimate.
>
> $$
> \mathbf{K}_t = \mathbf{P}_t^- \mathbf{H}_t^{\top} \left( \mathbf{H}_t \mathbf{P}_t^- \mathbf{H}_t^{\top} + \mathbf{R}_t \right)^{-1}
> $$
> $$
> \hat{\mathbf{x}}_t = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_t^- + \mathbf{K}_t (\mathbf{z}_t - \mathbf{H}_t \hat{\mathbf{x}}_t^-)
> $$
> $$
> \mathbf{P}_t = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{K}_t \mathbf{H}_t) \mathbf{P}_t^-
> $$

**Role of the Kalman Gain:** (2 marks)

> The Kalman Gain $\mathbf{K}_t$ is a *weighting matrix* that determines how much the innovation $\nu_t = \mathbf{z}_t - \mathbf{H}_t \hat{\mathbf{x}}_t^-$ influences the final estimate.
> - If $\mathbf{R}_t \to 0$ (sensor is very accurate), $\mathbf{K}_t \to \mathbf{H}_t^{-1}$ → measurement is trusted fully.
> - If $\mathbf{P}_t^- \to 0$ (prediction is very confident), $\mathbf{K}_t \to 0$ → measurement is ignored.
> - The denominator $(\mathbf{H}_t \mathbf{P}_t^- \mathbf{H}_t^{\top} + \mathbf{R}_t)$ acts as a **total uncertainty**; the gain is the ratio of *prior uncertainty* to *total uncertainty*.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B(b) — EKF Jacobians (7 marks)**

**Motion model:**
$$
g(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{u}) = \begin{bmatrix} x + v \Delta t \cos\theta \\ y + v \Delta t \sin\theta \\ \theta + \omega \Delta t \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 1 — State Jacobian $\mathbf{F}_t = \partial g / \partial \mathbf{x}$:** (3 marks)

Differentiate each row with respect to $[x, y, \theta]$:
$$
\mathbf{F}_t = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -v \Delta t \sin\theta \\ 0 & 1 & \;\;v \Delta t \cos\theta \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Observation model:** (1 mark)
$$
h(\mathbf{x}) = \begin{bmatrix} r \\ \phi \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{(x_l - x)^2 + (y_l - y)^2} \\ \text{atan2}(y_l - y,\, x_l - x) - \theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Measurement Jacobian $\mathbf{H}_t = \partial h / \partial \mathbf{x}$:** (3 marks)

Let $\delta x = x_l - x$, $\delta y = y_l - y$, $r = \sqrt{\delta x^2 + \delta y^2}$.

$$
\mathbf{H}_t = \begin{bmatrix} -\delta x / r & -\delta y / r & 0 \\ \delta y / r^2 & -\delta x / r^2 & -1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

These Jacobians replace the constant $\mathbf{A}$ and $\mathbf{H}$ matrices of the linear KF and are evaluated at the **current predicted state** $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_t^-$ at every time step.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Sign errors in the innovation:** The innovation is $\mathbf{z}_t - \mathbf{H}_t \hat{\mathbf{x}}_t^-$, **not** $\mathbf{H}_t \hat{\mathbf{x}}_t^- - \mathbf{z}_t$. Reversing the sign produces a state estimate *away* from the measurement. *(−2 marks typically.)*
> 2. **Jacobians evaluated at the wrong point:** EKF requires linearization at $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t-1}$ (for $\mathbf{F}_t$) and $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_t^-$ (for $\mathbf{H}_t$). Using the wrong point invalidates the entire filter. *(−3 marks.)*
> 3. **Forgetting the $\mathbf{Q}_t$ term in covariance prediction:** Many students write only $\mathbf{A} \mathbf{P} \mathbf{A}^{\top}$ and omit $\mathbf{Q}_t$, which means the filter would *never* accumulate uncertainty and would diverge in practice. *(−2 marks.)*
> 4. **Confusing the roles of $\mathbf{Q}_t$ and $\mathbf{R}_t$:** $\mathbf{Q}_t$ acts on the **state** during prediction; $\mathbf{R}_t$ acts on the **measurement** during correction. Mixing these up causes the Kalman gain to be inverted in meaning. *(−2 marks.)*
> 5. **Units in Jacobian entries:** Forgetting to wrap trigonometric terms in $\Delta t$ is a frequent minor error that loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Kalman Filter Localization**

- [ ] The Kalman Filter is the **optimal linear Gaussian estimator** — provably the BLUE under the three optimality conditions.
- [ ] The state is represented as a **multivariate Gaussian** $\mathcal{N}(\hat{\mathbf{x}}_t, \mathbf{P}_t)$; the **mean** is the best estimate, the **covariance** is the uncertainty.
- [ ] Two phases: **Prediction** (motion only, covariance grows) and **Correction** (measurement only, covariance shrinks).
- [ ] The **innovation** $\nu_t = \mathbf{z}_t - \mathbf{H}_t \hat{\mathbf{x}}_t^-$ is the "surprise" from the new measurement.
- [ ] The **Kalman gain** $\mathbf{K}_t$ is a continuous trust dial: large $K \Rightarrow$ trust measurement; small $K \Rightarrow$ trust prediction.
- [ ] For **nonlinear** robot systems, use the **EKF** with Jacobians $\mathbf{F}_t = \partial g / \partial \mathbf{x}$ and $\mathbf{H}_t = \partial h / \partial \mathbf{x}$.
- [ ] Always evaluate Jacobians at the **current** estimate $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t-1}$ (for $\mathbf{F}_t$) and $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_t^-$ (for $\mathbf{H}_t$).
- [ ] For numerical stability in code/exams, prefer the **Joseph form** of the covariance update: $\mathbf{P}_t = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{K}_t \mathbf{H}_t) \mathbf{P}_t^- (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{K}_t \mathbf{H}_t)^{\top} + \mathbf{K}_t \mathbf{R}_t \mathbf{K}_t^{\top}$.
- [ ] The KF is **recursive** — only the previous estimate and covariance are needed; no full history is stored.
- [ ] KF is the **backbone** of EKF-SLAM, UKF, and many modern sensor-fusion pipelines used in autonomous vehicles and drones.
- [ ] The KF can **diverge** if (a) the linearization is poor (large EKF errors), (b) process noise $\mathbf{Q}$ is underestimated, or (c) sensor models are mismatched.
- [ ] In 2D, the state is $\mathbf{x} = [x, y, \theta]^{\top}$ with $\theta$ in **radians**; the orientation Jacobian entries include $\cos\theta$ and $\sin\theta$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application to Mobile Robots: Kalman Filter Localization - Schematic
Robot Position Prediction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT735 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Robot Position Prediction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Robot Position Prediction** is the computational process of estimating a robot's *future pose* (2D position $\langle x, y \rangle$ plus heading angle $\theta$) by propagating its *current state* forward in time using a **motion model** derived from the robot's kinematic, dynamic, or odometric constraints.

## 1.1 Formal Pose Representation

In the KTU framework, the configuration of a mobile robot in a 2D global frame $\{W\}$ is represented as the pose vector:

$$ \xi_t = \begin{bmatrix} x_t \\ y_t \\ \theta_t \end{bmatrix} $$

where:
- $x_t, y_t$ are the global Cartesian coordinates of the robot reference point (in **meters**)
- $\theta_t$ is the orientation of the robot's local frame $\{R\}$ w.r.t. $\{W\}$ (in **radians**)

The prediction step computes $\xi_{t+\Delta t}$ from $\xi_t$ and a control input $u_t$ (or odometry reading $o_t$).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Blindfolded Driver"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine you are driving a car with your eyes closed for **2 seconds**. You know you are currently moving at **60 km/h** North, and your steering wheel is turned slightly right. Even without seeing the road, you can *predict* where the car will be after 2 seconds because you know the speed and the turn rate. Robot Position Prediction is exactly this — a mathematical "blindfolded prediction" of where the robot will be, given how its wheels are currently turning.

## 1.3 Why is Position Prediction Indispensable?

- **Reactive Obstacle Avoidance:** Predicts imminent collisions before they occur (look-ahead horizon = $\Delta t$).
- **Localization (Prediction Step):** Forms the prior $\overline{bel}(x_t)$ in the Bayes Filter loop.
- **Trajectory Tracking:** Compares predicted vs. desired path to generate control corrections.
- **SLAM Front-End:** Provides the motion prior for scan-matching and landmark correspondence.

## 1.4 Classification of Prediction Methods (KTU High-Yield)

| Method Family | Input Modality | Noise Handling | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Kinematic (Geometric)** | Wheel velocities $v, \omega$ | Deterministic | Ideal simulation, small errors |
| **Odometry-Based** | Encoder deltas $\Delta s_L, \Delta s_R$ | Additive Gaussian | Real ground robots |
| **Dynamic** | Forces, torques, mass | Requires IMU | High-speed / uneven terrain |
| **Probabilistic** | $u_t$ + process noise $w_t$ | Kalman / Particle Filter | Uncertain real environments |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Pillars of Position Prediction

### Pillar 1 — Velocity Motion Model (Control-Based)

The robot applies linear velocity $v$ and angular velocity $\omega$ for a duration $\Delta t$:

$$ \xi_{t+\Delta t} = f_v(\xi_t, u_t) = \begin{bmatrix} x_t + v \cos\theta_t \cdot \Delta t \\ y_t + v \sin\theta_t \cdot \Delta t \\ \theta_t + \omega \cdot \Delta t \end{bmatrix} $$

### Pillar 2 — Odometry Motion Model (Measurement-Based)

When wheel encoders report incremental motions $\delta_{\text{rot1}}, \delta_{\text{trans}}, \delta_{\text{rot2}}$:

$$ \xi_{t+\Delta t} = f_o(\xi_t, o_t) = \begin{bmatrix} x_t + \delta_{\text{trans}} \cos(\theta_t + \delta_{\text{rot1}}) \\ y_t + \delta_{\text{trans}} \sin(\theta_t + \delta_{\text{rot1}}) \\ \theta_t + \delta_{\text{rot1}} + \delta_{\text{rot2}} \end{bmatrix} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examination Tip:** The odometry model is preferred over the velocity model in real-world KTU lab scenarios because it directly incorporates the *actual* measured wheel rotation, making it more accurate on uneven surfaces.

## 2.2 Wheel-Encoder to Body-Velocity Conversion (Differential Drive)

For a robot with wheel radius $r$ and wheel-base $L$:

$$ v = \frac{r}{2}\left(\omega_L + \omega_R\right) $$

$$ \omega = \frac{r}{L}\left(\omega_R - \omega_L\right) $$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **complete set of equations** you must memorize for the ESE (End Semester Examination) under the KTU 2024 scheme.

| Equation | Name | Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\Delta s = r \cdot \Delta \phi_{\text{enc}}$ | Encoder-to-distance | $r$ wheel radius, $\Delta \phi$ wheel rotation | m, rad |
| $v = (v_R + v_L)/2$ | Body linear velocity | $v_R, v_L$ wheel linear speeds | m/s |
| $\omega = (v_R - v_L)/L$ | Body angular velocity | $L$ wheel-base | rad/s |
| $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$ | X-velocity in world frame | $v, \theta$ | m/s, rad |
| $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$ | Y-velocity in world frame | $v, \theta$ | m/s, rad |
| $\dot{\theta} = \omega$ | Heading rate | $\omega$ | rad/s |
| $\xi_{t+1} = \xi_t + f(\xi_t, u_t)\Delta t$ | Euler-Integrated Prediction | $\xi$ pose, $u$ control | mixed |
| $R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix}\cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta\end{bmatrix}$ | 2D Rotation Matrix | $\theta$ heading | rad |
| $\Sigma_{t+1} = F_x \Sigma_t F_x^{\top} + F_u Q_t F_u^{\top}$ | EKF Covariance Propagation | $F_x, F_u$ Jacobians, $Q$ process noise | m², rad² |

## 2.4 Coordinate Frame Transformation — The Heart of Prediction

A prediction must transform body-frame velocities $\langle v, \omega \rangle$ (measured by encoders attached to the robot) into the world-frame displacement $\langle \Delta x, \Delta y \rangle$:

$$ \begin{bmatrix} \Delta x \\ \Delta y \end{bmatrix} = R(\theta_t) \begin{bmatrix} v \cdot \Delta t \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_t & -\sin\theta_t \\ \sin\theta_t & \cos\theta_t \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \cdot \Delta t \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} v \cos\theta_t \cdot \Delta t \\ v \sin\theta_t \cdot \Delta t \end{bmatrix} $$

This is the *physical origin* of the $\cos\theta$ and $\sin\theta$ terms in the velocity motion model.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility in Real KTU Projects

- **AGV (Automated Guided Vehicle) Warehouses:** Predicts pallet positions for pick-and-place.
- **Autonomous Cars (e.g., KTU Autonomous Go-Kart projects):** Short-horizon prediction feeds Model Predictive Control (MPC).
- **Rescue Robots:** Dead-reckoning prediction when GPS is denied inside collapsed structures.
- **Service Robots (AMR):** Localization priors for Monte-Carlo Localization in indoor mapping.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — Differential-Drive Position Prediction

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup (Typical KTU Exam):** A differential-drive robot has wheel-base $L = 0.5$ m and wheel radius $r = 0.1$ m. At time $t$, its pose is $\xi_t = \langle 2.0 \text{ m},\ 1.0 \text{ m},\ \pi/4 \text{ rad} \rangle$. The left and right wheel angular velocities are $\omega_L = 10$ rad/s and $\omega_R = 12$ rad/s. Predict the pose at $t + 0.1$ s.

### Step 1 — Convert wheel angular velocities to linear wheel speeds
The linear speed at the contact point of a wheel equals radius times angular velocity:

$$ v_L = r \cdot \omega_L = 0.1 \times 10 = 1.0 \text{ m/s} $$

$$ v_R = r \cdot \omega_R = 0.1 \times 12 = 1.2 \text{ m/s} $$

*Textual Logic:* Each wheel rolls without slipping, so the tangential velocity at the contact patch equals the wheel circumference swept per second.

### Step 2 — Compute the body-frame linear and angular velocities

$$ v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{1.2 + 1.0}{2} = \frac{2.2}{2} = 1.1 \text{ m/s} $$

$$ \omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{1.2 - 1.0}{0.5} = \frac{0.2}{0.5} = 0.4 \text{ rad/s} $$

*Textual Logic:* The robot's centroid moves at the *average* of both wheel speeds. The difference in wheel speeds, divided by the wheel-base, yields the instantaneous center of rotation (ICR) angular velocity.

### Step 3 — Apply the Euler-integrated velocity motion model

$$ \xi_{t+\Delta t} = \begin{bmatrix} x_t + v \cos\theta_t \cdot \Delta t \\ y_t + v \sin\theta_t \cdot \Delta t \\ \theta_t + \omega \cdot \Delta t \end{bmatrix} $$

### Step 4 — Compute the X-displacement

$$ \Delta x = v \cos(\pi/4) \cdot \Delta t = 1.1 \times \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \times 0.1 $$

$$ \Delta x = 1.1 \times 0.7071 \times 0.1 = 0.07778 \text{ m} $$

### Step 5 — Compute the Y-displacement

$$ \Delta y = v \sin(\pi/4) \cdot \Delta t = 1.1 \times \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \times 0.1 = 0.07778 \text{ m} $$

### Step 6 — Compute the heading update

$$ \Delta \theta = \omega \cdot \Delta t = 0.4 \times 0.1 = 0.04 \text{ rad} $$

### Step 7 — Final Predicted Pose

$$ \xi_{t+0.1} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.0 + 0.07778 \\ 1.0 + 0.07778 \\ \pi/4 + 0.04 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.0778 \text{ m} \\ 1.0778 \text{ m} \\ 0.8254 \text{ rad} \end{bmatrix} $$

## 3.2 Derivation B — Odometry Model from Encoder Deltas

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A robot with $L = 0.4$ m, $r = 0.08$ m reports encoder increments $\Delta \phi_L = 25$ rad and $\Delta \phi_R = 30$ rad. Starting pose $\xi_0 = \langle 0, 0, 0 \rangle$. Predict the pose.

### Step 1 — Encoder deltas to linear wheel displacements

$$ \Delta s_L = r \cdot \Delta \phi_L = 0.08 \times 25 = 2.0 \text{ m} $$

$$ \Delta s_R = r \cdot \Delta \phi_R = 0.08 \times 30 = 2.4 \text{ m} $$

### Step 2 — Compute the two rotation components and translation

$$ \delta_{\text{rot1}} = \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{L} = \frac{2.4 - 2.0}{0.4} = \frac{0.4}{0.4} = 1.0 \text{ rad} $$

$$ \delta_{\text{trans}} = \frac{\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L}{2} = \frac{2.4 + 2.0}{2} = 2.2 \text{ m} $$

$$ \delta_{\text{rot2}} = \delta_{\text{rot1}} = 1.0 \text{ rad} \quad \text{(half-half split convention)} $$

### Step 3 — Apply the odometry model

$$ \begin{aligned} x_1 &= x_0 + \delta_{\text{trans}} \cos(\theta_0 + \delta_{\text{rot1}}) \\ x_1 &= 0 + 2.2 \times \cos(0 + 1.0) \\ x_1 &= 2.2 \times 0.5403 = 1.1887 \text{ m} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} y_1 &= y_0 + \delta_{\text{trans}} \sin(\theta_0 + \delta_{\text{rot1}}) \\ y_1 &= 0 + 2.2 \times \sin(1.0) \\ y_1 &= 2.2 \times 0.8415 = 1.8512 \text{ m} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} \theta_1 &= \theta_0 + \delta_{\text{rot1}} + \delta_{\text{rot2}} \\ \theta_1 &= 0 + 1.0 + 1.0 = 2.0 \text{ rad} \end{aligned} $$

### Step 4 — Final Predicted Pose (Odometry)

$$ \xi_1 = \langle 1.1887 \text{ m},\ 1.8512 \text{ m},\ 2.0 \text{ rad} \rangle $$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — EKF Covariance Propagation

For the probabilistic extension (EKF prediction), the Jacobians of $f_v$ are:

$$ F_x = \frac{\partial f_v}{\partial \xi} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -v\sin\theta \cdot \Delta t \\ 0 & 1 & v\cos\theta \cdot \Delta t \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$

$$ F_u = \frac{\partial f_v}{\partial u} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta \cdot \Delta t & 0 \\ \sin\theta \cdot \Delta t & 0 \\ 0 & \Delta t \end{bmatrix} $$

The updated covariance:

$$ \Sigma_{t+1} = F_x \Sigma_t F_x^{\top} + F_u Q_t F_u^{\top} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that the off-diagonal term $-v\sin\theta$ in $F_x$ tells us that heading uncertainty *amplifies* position uncertainty as speed $v$ increases. This is why high-speed robots accumulate error faster — a classic KTU viva question.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Position Prediction

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the complete prediction pipeline as it would be implemented in a KTU lab-grade mobile robot stack:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Current Pose Xi_t] --> D[Prediction Engine f]
    B[Control Input u_t or Encoder Deltas] --> D
    C[Process Noise Covariance Q_t] --> D
    D --> E[Predicted Pose Xi_t+1]
    D --> F[Predicted Covariance Sigma_t+1]
    E --> G[Bayes Filter / Localization Module]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Corrected Belief bel x_t]
    H --> A
    E --> I[Motion Planner Look Ahead]
    I --> J[Reactive Obstacle Avoidance]
    E --> K[Trajectory Tracking Controller]
```

## 4.2 Differential-Drive Kinematic Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    WL[Left Wheel Encoder] --> M1[Wheel Odometry Block]
    WR[Right Wheel Encoder] --> M1
    M1 --> M2[Compute v, omega]
    M2 --> M3[Apply Velocity Motion Model]
    M3 --> M4[Xi t plus 1]
    subgraph Body Frame
        WL
        WR
    end
    subgraph World Frame
        M4
    end
```

## 4.3 Coordinate Frame Transformation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Wheel Speeds vL, vR] --> B[Body Twist v, omega]
    B --> C[Rotate by Heading theta]
    C --> D[World Frame Displacement Dx, Dy]
    D --> E[Integrate to get Xi t+1]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The *left-to-right* flow mirrors the data pipeline: physical sensors (encoders) → local velocities → world-frame projection → final pose. The dashed frame boundaries in Diagram 4.2 distinguish the body (robot-centric) frame from the world (global) frame — a distinction the KTU examiner will explicitly test.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] 
**Define the velocity motion model for a differential-drive mobile robot. Write its prediction equations and state the physical meaning of each term.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The velocity motion model propagates a robot's pose forward in time using body-frame linear velocity $v$ and angular velocity $\omega$:

$$ \xi_{t+\Delta t} = \begin{bmatrix} x_t + v\cos\theta_t \cdot \Delta t \\ y_t + v\sin\theta_t \cdot \Delta t \\ \theta_t + \omega \cdot \Delta t \end{bmatrix} $$

- $v\cos\theta_t \cdot \Delta t$: displacement along the global X-axis.
- $v\sin\theta_t \cdot \Delta t$: displacement along the global Y-axis.
- $\omega \cdot \Delta t$: change in heading angle.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] 
**Differentiate between velocity-based and odometry-based motion models. When is each preferred?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Velocity Model | Odometry Model |
|---|---|---|
| Input | Commanded $v, \omega$ | Measured wheel encoder deltas |
| Realism | Idealized, ignores slip | Realistic, captures ground truth |
| Preferred When | Simulation, ideal floor | Real robot, uneven terrain |

---

## 📝 PART B — Full ESE Module Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]

**(a)** Derive the complete velocity motion model for a differential-drive mobile robot starting from the encoder readings. Clearly state the Jacobian $F_x$ and explain the role of process noise covariance $Q_t$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A robot with $L = 0.3$ m, $r = 0.05$ m starts at $\xi_0 = \langle 0, 0, 0 \rangle$. Encoders report $\Delta\phi_L = 40$ rad, $\Delta\phi_R = 60$ rad. Compute the final pose using the **odometry motion model**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**
- (2 marks) From encoder to wheel speeds: $v_L = r\omega_L$, $v_R = r\omega_R$.
- (2 marks) Combine to body twist: $v = (v_R+v_L)/2$, $\omega = (v_R-v_L)/L$.
- (2 marks) Apply world-frame transformation using $R(\theta)$ to get the $\cos\theta$, $\sin\theta$ terms.
- (1 mark) State $F_x$ and emphasize that $Q_t$ injects uncertainty.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**
$\Delta s_L = 0.05 \times 40 = 2.0$ m, $\Delta s_R = 0.05 \times 60 = 3.0$ m.
$\delta_{\text{rot1}} = (3.0-2.0)/0.3 = 3.333$ rad.
$\delta_{\text{trans}} = (3.0+2.0)/2 = 2.5$ m.
$\delta_{\text{rot2}} = 3.333$ rad.

$$ x_1 = 0 + 2.5 \cdot \cos(3.333) = 2.5 \times (-0.9711) = -2.428 \text{ m} $$

$$ y_1 = 0 + 2.5 \cdot \sin(3.333) = 2.5 \times (-0.2386) = -0.5965 \text{ m} $$

$$ \theta_1 = 0 + 3.333 + 3.333 = 6.666 \text{ rad} \equiv 6.666 - 2\pi = 0.383 \text{ rad} $$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the role of the prediction step in a Bayes Filter for mobile robot localization. How does the Jacobian $F_u$ govern the propagation of control noise? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider a unicycle robot at $\xi_0 = \langle 1, 2, \pi/6 \rangle$ with $v = 0.5$ m/s and $\omega = 0.2$ rad/s for $\Delta t = 2$ s. Predict the pose using the velocity motion model. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Block Diagram (7 Marks):**
- (3 marks) Bayes filter block diagram: Prediction → Update → Posterior $\to$ Next Prior.
- (2 marks) Explanation of $F_u = \begin{bmatrix}\cos\theta \cdot \Delta t & 0 \\ \sin\theta \cdot \Delta t & 0 \\ 0 & \Delta t\end{bmatrix}$ and how it transforms $Q_t$ to world-frame uncertainty.
- (2 marks) Real-world example (warehouse AGV).

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

$$ \Delta x = 0.5 \times \cos(\pi/6) \times 2 = 0.5 \times 0.8660 \times 2 = 0.866 \text{ m} $$

$$ \Delta y = 0.5 \times \sin(\pi/6) \times 2 = 0.5 \times 0.5 \times 2 = 0.5 \text{ m} $$

$$ \Delta \theta = 0.2 \times 2 = 0.4 \text{ rad} $$

$$ \xi_1 = \langle 1 + 0.866,\ 2 + 0.5,\ \pi/6 + 0.4 \rangle = \langle 1.866 \text{ m},\ 2.5 \text{ m},\ 0.924 \text{ rad} \rangle $$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Zones**
> 1. **Forgetting angle wrap-around:** Always normalize $\theta$ to $[-\pi, \pi]$ or $[0, 2\pi]$. Failing to do so loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing $\delta_{\text{rot1}}$ and $\delta_{\text{rot2}}$:** The half-half split is the standard convention. Some students put the full rotation in $\delta_{\text{rot1}}$ and zero in $\delta_{\text{rot2}}$ — this is wrong per the KTU accepted model.
> 3. **Unit errors:** Mixing degrees and radians. KTU mandates **radians** in the final answer.
> 4. **Skipping the Jacobian:** In 7-mark derivations, omitting $F_x$ costs the entire 1 mark reserved for the *uncertainty handling* component.
> 5. **Sign error in $v$ terms:** For a left-wheel-faster robot, $v_L > v_R$ yields a *negative* $\omega$ (turning left). Many students lose marks by assuming positive rotation = right.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The pose vector is **always 3-dimensional** for 2D ground robots: $\langle x, y, \theta \rangle$.
- ✅ The **velocity model** uses commanded inputs $(v, \omega)$; the **odometry model** uses measured $(\delta_{\text{rot1}}, \delta_{\text{trans}}, \delta_{\text{rot2}})$.
- ✅ The $\cos\theta$ and $\sin\theta$ terms come from the **2D rotation matrix** $R(\theta)$ projecting body-frame velocity into the world frame.
- ✅ For differential drive: $v = (v_R+v_L)/2$ and $\omega = (v_R-v_L)/L$.
- ✅ Encoder distance formula: $\Delta s = r \cdot \Delta\phi$ (must be in **meters** and **radians**).
- ✅ EKF prediction requires **two Jacobians** $F_x$ (state) and $F_u$ (control) for covariance propagation.
- ✅ Always **normalize** $\theta$ to the principal range after prediction.
- ✅ Higher speed $v$ → larger position uncertainty growth (visible in $F_x$ off-diagonal).
- ✅ KTU 2024 lab viva favorites: *"Why is the odometry model more accurate than the velocity model on carpets?"* — Answer: It uses measured wheel motion, not ideal commanded motion.
- ✅ Remember: prediction is the *prior* $\overline{bel}(x_t)$; the measurement update later gives the posterior.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Observation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT735 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Observation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of mobile robotics and autonomous navigation, **Observation** refers to the process by which a robot acquires information about its **internal state** (pose, velocity, wheel rotations) and **external environment** (obstacles, landmarks, map features) using on-board sensors. In the formal probabilistic framework (a cornerstone of the **Bayes Filter** paradigm), an observation at time $t$, denoted $z_t$, is treated as a noisy, random measurement whose likelihood is conditioned on the robot's true state $x_t$ and the map $m$:

$$p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$$

This conditional probability density function is universally termed the **Observation Model** (or **Measurement Model** or **Sensor Model**). It is the mathematical heart of the *perception* stage in the classical **Sense–Plan–Act** paradigm and the *update* step in any recursive state estimator.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PERUT735 – Module 2):** The 2024 scheme explicitly demands mastery of (a) classification of robot sensors, (b) probabilistic sensor models for range finders, (c) likelihood field models, and (d) the observation update equation of the Bayes filter.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are **blindfolded and dropped into an unknown room**. To navigate, you must *observe*. You reach out and tap the floor with a white cane (**sonar / infrared ranger**), listen for echoes (**ultrasonic time-of-flight**), feel vibrations through your feet (**tactile / proprioceptive encoders**), and occasionally lift the blindfold to glance with your eyes (**vision / camera**). Each sense is imperfect — the cane might miss a thin table leg, the eyes may misjudge distance. Your brain continuously **fuses** these noisy "observations" with your prior belief of where you are to form a new, refined belief of your pose in the room.

A mobile robot is no different. Its **sensors are its senses**, and **observation is the act of perceiving the world to update its belief about its own state**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Conceptual Distinction:**
> - **Proprioceptive sensors** measure values *internal* to the robot (e.g., wheel encoders, gyroscopes, IMU).
> - **Exteroceptive sensors** measure values *external* to the robot (e.g., camera, Lidar, GPS, sonar).
> 
> Standard classification metrics: **range (m)**, **field-of-view (deg)**, **accuracy (mm)**, **update rate (Hz)**.

## 1.3 Why Observation is Critical in Robot Navigation

| Necessity | Engineering Rationale |
|-----------|----------------------|
| **State Estimation** | Dead-reckoning drifts; observations correct drift via *update* step. |
| **Localization** | A robot must match observed landmarks $z_t$ against map $m$ to know its pose. |
| **Mapping** | Observations populate the map with features of the unknown environment. |
| **Obstacle Avoidance** | Real-time observations of nearby obstacles enable reactive behaviour. |
| **SLAM** | Simultaneous Localization and Mapping *jointly* estimates state and map using observations. |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Robot Sensors (Operational Logic)

Sensors in robot navigation are classified along three orthogonal axes:

1. **Energy Source Axis** → *Active* (emits energy) vs *Passive* (only receives).
2. **Measurement Axis** → *Proprioceptive* (internal) vs *Exteroceptive* (external).
3. **Physical Principle Axis** → *Time-of-Flight*, *Phase-Shift*, *Triangulation*, *Encoder*, *Inertial*, *Visual*.

### 2.1.1 Range Sensors — Operating Principles

**A. Time-of-Flight (ToF)**
The sensor emits a signal (light pulse, sound wave, radio wave) and measures the round-trip time $\Delta t$. The range $d$ is computed as:

$$d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

where $c$ is the propagation speed ($c_{\text{light}} \approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $c_{\text{sound}} \approx 343$ m/s in air at 20°C).

**B. Phase-Shift Detection**
A continuous modulated wave of frequency $f_{\text{mod}}$ is emitted; the phase difference $\phi$ between emitted and returned wave gives:

$$d = \frac{c \cdot \phi}{4\pi f_{\text{mod}}}$$

**C. Triangulation**
A projected beam is observed by a linear sensor array; the geometric angle $\theta$ of the spot yields range via the triangle baseline $B$ and focal length $f$:

$$d = \frac{B \cdot f}{x_{\text{image}}}$$

## 2.2 The Bayes Filter Observation Update (Why & How)

The discrete-time **Bayes Filter** maintains a belief $\text{bel}(x_t)$ over the robot state. It has two stages — *prediction* (using control $u_t$) and *update* (using observation $z_t$). The observation update is the **Why** of the entire observation module:

**Why?** Because each new measurement carries information that *corrects* our propagated belief.
**How?** By multiplying the predicted belief by the likelihood of the observation, then normalising.

The canonical **Observation Update Equation** is:

$$\text{bel}(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \cdot \overline{\text{bel}}(x_t)$$

where:
- $\overline{\text{bel}}(x_t)$ is the *predicted* (prior) belief from the motion model.
- $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ is the **observation model** (likelihood).
- $\eta = \left[\sum_{x_t} p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \cdot \overline{\text{bel}}(x_t)\right]^{-1}$ is the normalising constant ensuring probabilities sum to 1.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This single equation underpins **Kalman Filters, Extended Kalman Filters (EKF), Particle Filters, and Histogram Filters**. Every localization algorithm in KTU Module 2 reduces to clever, tractable approximations of this identity.

## 2.3 Probabilistic Models for Range Finders

The most important sensor model in KTU syllabus is the **Beam Model** (a.k.a. *Ray-cast Model*). It decomposes the noise into four physical phenomena:

1. **Hit (Correct Detection):** Gaussian noise around the true range $z_t^{\ast}$.
2. **Short Reading (Unexpected Object):** Exponential decay below the true range.
3. **Max Range (No Detection):** Point mass at $z_{\max}$.
4. **Random Measurement (Crosstalk):** Uniform distribution.

The composite probability is:

$$p(z_t^k \mid x_t, m) = \begin{pmatrix} z_{\max} \\ z_t^k \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}^{\!\!T} \begin{pmatrix} p_{\text{hit}} \\ p_{\text{short}} \\ p_{\max} \\ p_{\text{rand}} \end{pmatrix}$$

with coefficients summing to unity: $p_{\text{hit}} + p_{\text{short}} + p_{\max} + p_{\text{rand}} = 1$.

## 2.4 Likelihood Field Model (The Modern Alternative)

The beam model is computationally expensive (requires ray-casting). The **Likelihood Field Model** pre-computes a 2D distance field $D(i,j)$ from the map, then evaluates a Gaussian on this field for each measurement endpoint $(x_{z_t^k}, y_{z_t^k})$:

$$p(z_t^k \mid x_t, m) = z_{\text{hit}} \cdot \mathcal{N}\!\left(D\!\left(x_{z_t^k}, y_{z_t^k}\right); 0, \sigma_{\text{hit}}^{2}\right) + z_{\text{rand}} \cdot p_{\text{rand}}$$

This is **~100× faster** than the beam model and is used in ROS (`amcl`) for real-world deployments.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Symbolic Form | Typical Units / Values |
|---|--------------------|---------------|------------------------|
| 1 | Time-of-Flight Range | $d = c \Delta t / 2$ | m (with $c$ in m/s, $\Delta t$ in s) |
| 2 | Phase-Shift Range | $d = c \phi / (4\pi f_{\text{mod}})$ | m |
| 3 | Bayes Update | $\text{bel}(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \, \overline{\text{bel}}(x_t)$ | Dimensionless probability |
| 4 | Normaliser $\eta$ | $\eta^{-1} = \sum_{x_t} p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \, \overline{\text{bel}}(x_t)$ | – |
| 5 | Hit Likelihood (Gaussian) | $p_{\text{hit}}(z_t^k \mid x_t, m) = \mathcal{N}\!\left(z_t^k; z_t^{k\ast}, \sigma_{\text{hit}}^{2}\right)$ | $z_t^{k\ast}$ = true range, $\sigma_{\text{hit}} \in [0.01, 0.05]$ m |
| 6 | Short Reading | $p_{\text{short}}(z_t^k \mid x_t, m) = \lambda_{\text{short}} \, e^{-\lambda_{\text{short}} z_t^k}$ for $0 \le z_t^k \le z_t^{k\ast}$ | $\lambda_{\text{short}} > 0$ |
| 7 | Max Range (Point Mass) | $p_{\max}(z_t^k \mid x_t, m) = \mathbb{I}\!\left[z_t^k = z_{\max}\right]$ | $z_{\max} \approx 30$ m (Hokuyo) |
| 8 | Random (Uniform) | $p_{\text{rand}}(z_t^k \mid x_t, m) = \mathbb{I}\!\left[0 \le z_t^k < z_{\max}\right] / z_{\max}$ | – |
| 9 | Likelihood Field | $p(z_t^k \mid x_t, m) = z_{\text{hit}} \mathcal{N}(D; 0, \sigma_{\text{hit}}^{2}) + z_{\text{rand}} p_{\text{rand}}$ | $D$ = distance field, $\sigma_{\text{hit}} \approx 0.2$ m |
| 10 | Sensor Fusion (EKF) | $\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t))$, $K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H^T (H \bar{\Sigma}_t H^T + Q_t)^{-1}$ | Kalman Gain $K_t$ |
| 11 | Triangulation Range | $d = B f / x_{\text{image}}$ | m |
| 12 | Cramer-Rao Bound (Min Var) | $\sigma_z \ge \sqrt{\text{Fisher Info}^{-1}}$ | Lower bound on noise |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Field Engineering Note:** In industrial mobile robots (e.g., Amazon Kiva, AGVs in KTU-adjacent Kerala industries like KMML or FACT), the **likelihood field + EKF** combination is industry standard. The beam model is mostly used in research and pedagogical simulation tools like *Stage* / *Gazebo*.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Bayes Filter Observation Update

We begin with the posterior belief we wish to compute, $\text{bel}(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$, where $z_{1:t} = \{z_1, z_2, \dots, z_t\}$ and $u_{1:t} = \{u_1, u_2, \dots, u_t\}$ are the history of measurements and controls.

**Step 1 — Invoke the definition of conditional probability:**

$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \, p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}$$

*Conversion Logic:* We applied Bayes' rule: $P(A \mid B, C) = P(B \mid A, C) P(A \mid C) / P(B \mid C)$ with $A = x_t$, $B = z_t$, $C = (z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$.

**Step 2 — Apply the Markov Assumption (the world is conditionally independent of the past given the present state):**

$$p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(z_t \mid x_t)$$

*Conversion Logic:* The measurement $z_t$ depends only on the current state $x_t$ and map $m$, not on previous measurements or controls. This is the **sensor Markov assumption**.

**Step 3 — Simplify the denominator into the normaliser $\eta$:**

Define $\eta = p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})^{-1}$ (a constant in $x_t$). Then:

$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \, p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$$

**Step 4 — Recognise the prior term as the *predicted* belief $\overline{\text{bel}}(x_t)$:**

$$\overline{\text{bel}}(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

**Step 5 — Assemble the final update equation:**

$$\boxed{\text{bel}(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \, \overline{\text{bel}}(x_t)}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Beam Range Model Likelihood

For a single range reading $z_t^k$ from beam $k$ at state $x_t$ in map $m$, the true range is $z_t^{k\ast}$. The four physical noise sources combine as a **mixture model**:

$$p(z_t^k \mid x_t, m) = \begin{pmatrix} p_{\text{hit}} \\ p_{\text{short}} \\ p_{\max} \\ p_{\text{rand}} \end{pmatrix}^{\!\!T} \begin{pmatrix} p_{\text{hit}}\!\left(z_t^k \mid x_t, m\right) \\ p_{\text{short}}\!\left(z_t^k \mid x_t, m\right) \\ p_{\max}\!\left(z_t^k \mid x_t, m\right) \\ p_{\text{rand}}\!\left(z_t^k \mid x_t, m\right) \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 1 — Hit (Gaussian around true range):**

$$p_{\text{hit}}\!\left(z_t^k \mid x_t, m\right) = \begin{cases} \eta_{\text{hit}} \exp\!\left[-\frac{1}{2} \frac{\left(z_t^k - z_t^{k\ast}\right)^2}{\sigma_{\text{hit}}^2}\right] & 0 \le z_t^k \le z_{\max} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

with normaliser $\eta_{\text{hit}}$.

**Step 2 — Short Reading (unmodelled obstruction, exponential decay):**

$$p_{\text{short}}\!\left(z_t^k \mid x_t, m\right) = \begin{cases} \eta_{\text{short}} \lambda_{\text{short}} \exp\!\left[-\lambda_{\text{short}} z_t^k\right] & 0 \le z_t^k \le z_t^{k\ast} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

*Conversion Logic:* Shorter-than-expected ranges occur when the beam hits an *unexpected* object (e.g., a person walking by). The exponential density models that such "short" returns are exponentially more probable the closer they are to the sensor.

**Step 3 — Maximum Range failure:**

$$p_{\max}\!\left(z_t^k \mid x_t, m\right) = \mathbb{I}\!\left[z = z_{\max}\right]$$

*Conversion Logic:* If the beam fails to detect anything (e.g., highly absorbent material, beyond maximum range), the sensor reports $z_{\max}$ deterministically — modelled as a Dirac point-mass.

**Step 4 — Random crosstalk (uniform):**

$$p_{\text{rand}}\!\left(z_t^k \mid x_t, m\right) = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{z_{\max}} & 0 \le z_t^k < z_{\max} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

*Conversion Logic:* Some beams receive reflections from other sensors (cross-talk) producing uniformly distributed spurious readings.

**Step 5 — Mixing weights sum to one:**

$$p_{\text{hit}} + p_{\text{short}} + p_{\max} + p_{\text{rand}} = 1$$

Typical values for a Hokuyo / SICK Lidar: $p_{\text{hit}} = 0.79$, $p_{\text{short}} = 0.05$, $p_{\max} = 0.10$, $p_{\text{rand}} = 0.06$.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Beam vs Likelihood Field)

| Attribute | Beam Model | Likelihood Field Model |
|-----------|------------|------------------------|
| **Computational Cost** | $O(N \cdot K)$ (ray-casting) | $O(N)$ (table lookup) |
| **Real-time viability** | Slow, used in research | Fast, used in production (ROS amcl) |
| **Map representation** | Occupancy grid with ray-casting | Pre-computed 2D distance transform |
| **Handles unexpected obstacles** | Yes (via $p_{\text{short}}$) | No (only handles map-registered obstacles) |
| **Smoothness of likelihood** | Piecewise, discontinuous | Smooth, differentiable |
| **Theoretical accuracy** | High (physically motivated) | Approximation, very robust empirically |
| **KTU exam preference** | High (derivation heavy) | Medium (applied/conceptual) |

## 3.4 Derivation of Triangulation Range (Common in Cameras)

A laser stripe or structured-light pattern is projected onto the target. The pattern is observed on the camera's image plane at horizontal pixel $x_{\text{image}}$. Using similar triangles with baseline $B$ (between projector and camera) and focal length $f$:

**Step 1 — Image plane relation:** $x_{\text{image}} / f = B / d$

*Conversion Logic:* In the small-angle / pinhole camera approximation, the image offset is inversely proportional to the depth $d$.

**Step 2 — Solve for $d$:**

$$d = \frac{B \cdot f}{x_{\text{image}}}$$

**Step 3 — Uncertainty propagation** (a key KTU favourite): If $\sigma_x$ is the pixel-level detection noise, then propagating via the chain rule:

$$\sigma_d^2 = \left(\frac{\partial d}{\partial x_{\text{image}}}\right)^2 \sigma_x^2 = \left(\frac{B f}{x_{\text{image}}^2}\right)^2 \sigma_x^2 = \frac{d^2}{x_{\text{image}}^2} \sigma_x^2$$

$$\sigma_d = \frac{d^2}{B f} \sigma_x$$

*Conversion Logic:* **Depth uncertainty grows quadratically with distance** — a fundamental limit of triangulation sensors like the Microsoft Kinect / Intel RealSense.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Sensor Classification (Hierarchical Mermaid Tree)

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[Robot Sensors]
    rootA --> cls1[Energy Source]
    rootA --> cls2[Measurement Axis]
    rootA --> cls3[Physical Principle]
    cls1 --> actA[Active]
    cls1 --> pasA[Passive]
    actA --> s1A[Lidar]
    actA --> s2A[Sonar]
    actA --> s3A[Radar]
    pasA --> s4A[Camera]
    pasA --> s5A[GPS]
    cls2 --> propA[Proprioceptive]
    cls2 --> exterA[Exteroceptive]
    propA --> e1A[Wheel Encoder]
    propA --> e2A[IMU Gyroscope]
    exterA --> e3A[Camera]
    exterA --> e4A[Lidar]
    cls3 --> pf1A[Time of Flight]
    cls3 --> pf2A[Phase Shift]
    cls3 --> pf3A[Triangulation]
    cls3 --> pf4A[Inertial]
```

## 4.2 Observation Pipeline in a Bayes Filter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    poseA[Robot State x subscript t] --> beamA[Sensor Model p of z t given x t and m]
    mapA[Map m] --> beamA
    noiseA[Noise eta] --> beamA
    beamA --> likA[Compute Likelihood]
    predA[Predicted Belief bar bel x t] --> mulA[Multiplication]
    likA --> mulA
    mulA --> normA[Normalise by eta]
    normA --> postA[Posterior Belief bel x t]
    postA --> outA[Publish to Controller]
```

## 4.3 Beam Model Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inA[Range Reading z sub t sup k] --> mixA{Mixture Selector}
    mixA --> c1A[Hit Branch]
    mixA --> c2A[Short Branch]
    mixA --> c3A[Max Branch]
    mixA --> c4A[Random Branch]
    c1A --> g1A[Gaussian N around true range]
    c2A --> e1A[Exponential decay]
    c3A --> d1A[Dirac at z max]
    c4A --> u1A[Uniform 0 to z max]
    g1A --> sumA[Weighted Sum]
    e1A --> sumA
    d1A --> sumA
    u1A --> sumA
    sumA --> outA[Final Likelihood p of z given x and m]
```

## 4.4 Observation-Update vs Prediction Loop in SLAM

```mermaid
graph LR
    initA[Initial Belief] --> predA[Predict Step Motion Model p x t given u t and x t-1]
    predA --> ctrlA[Apply Control u sub t]
    predA --> obsA[Observation Step Sensor Model p z t given x t and m]
    obsA --> sensA[Acquire Sensor Data z sub t]
    obsA --> belA[Updated Belief bel x sub t]
    belA --> actA[Robot Action]
    actA --> ctrlA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Hint for KTU Students:** The above block diagram is essentially the **SLAM loop** (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping). Examiners often ask you to "draw and explain" this loop for 7-mark questions. Make sure to label **motion model** and **sensor model** distinctly.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between proprioceptive and exteroceptive sensors. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Proprioceptive | Exteroceptive |
|--------|----------------|---------------|
| Definition | Sensors that measure the robot's **internal** state | Sensors that measure the **external** environment |
| Energy Source | Usually passive (measure own motion) | Either active or passive |
| Examples (a) | Wheel encoder (measures wheel rotation) | Camera (measures surrounding light) |
| Examples (b) | IMU / Gyroscope (measures angular rate) | Lidar (measures range to obstacles) |

Proprioceptive sensors enable **dead-reckoning**; exteroceptive sensors enable **localization via landmark matching** and **obstacle detection**. *(Full 3 marks: 1 mark definition, 1 mark examples, 1 mark functional difference.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is a sensor model? Write the general form of the probabilistic observation model used in Bayes filter-based robot navigation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **sensor model** (or **observation model** / **measurement model**) is a mathematical function that gives the probability of a sensor reading given the robot's state and the environment map.

The general probabilistic form is:

$$p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$$

where:
- $z_t$ = measurement (observation) at time $t$
- $x_t$ = robot state (pose) at time $t$
- $m$ = map of the environment
- The expression is the **likelihood** of the observation.

It is used in the **update step** of the Bayes filter:

$$\text{bel}(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \, \overline{\text{bel}}(x_t)$$

*(Full 3 marks: 1 mark definition, 1 mark equation, 1 mark usage in update step.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the **Bayes filter observation update** equation in detail. Derive it from the basic definition of conditional probability and state clearly the **Markov assumption** used. (7 marks)

**(b)** Describe the **Beam Model of a Range Finder** with all four mixture components. Mention typical numerical values of mixture weights used in a real Lidar. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution for A(a):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define the belief we want to estimate. The posterior belief of the state given all measurements and controls up to time $t$:

$$\text{bel}(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Apply Bayes' rule:

$$\text{bel}(x_t) = \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \, p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Apply the **Markov assumption** (current measurement depends only on current state and map, not on history):

$$p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Define the normaliser $\eta = 1 / p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$:

$$\text{bel}(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \, p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Recognise the second factor as the **predicted belief** from the motion model:

$$\overline{\text{bel}}(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{\text{bel}(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \, \overline{\text{bel}}(x_t)}$$

**State the meaning of each term** for full marks:
- $\text{bel}(x_t)$ — posterior belief after observing $z_t$
- $\overline{\text{bel}}(x_t)$ — prior (predicted) belief from motion
- $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ — observation model likelihood
- $\eta$ — normalising constant (1 mark for interpretation).

---

#### **Model Solution for A(b):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define the beam model — for each beam $k$ of a range finder at state $x_t$ in map $m$, the model gives the probability of observing range $z_t^k$.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** State the four mixture components:

| Component | Physical Cause | PDF |
|-----------|----------------|-----|
| **Hit** $p_{\text{hit}}$ | Correct detection of obstacle | Gaussian centred on true range $z_t^{k\ast}$ |
| **Short** $p_{\text{short}}$ | Unmodelled object blocks beam | Exponential decay for $z_t^k \le z_t^{k\ast}$ |
| **Max** $p_{\max}$ | Failure to detect (no return) | Point mass at $z_{\max}$ |
| **Random** $p_{\text{rand}}$ | Cross-talk / spurious reflection | Uniform on $[0, z_{\max})$ |

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Write the mixture equation:

$$p(z_t^k \mid x_t, m) = p_{\text{hit}} \, p_{\text{hit}}\!\left(z_t^k\right) + p_{\text{short}} \, p_{\text{short}}\!\left(z_t^k\right) + p_{\max} \, p_{\max}\!\left(z_t^k\right) + p_{\text{rand}} \, p_{\text{rand}}\!\left(z_t^k\right)$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** State the constraint:

$$p_{\text{hit}} + p_{\text{short}} + p_{\max} + p_{\text{rand}} = 1$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Provide **typical numerical values** (Hokuyo / SICK Lidar):

- $p_{\text{hit}} \approx 0.79$
- $p_{\text{short}} \approx 0.05$
- $p_{\max} \approx 0.10$
- $p_{\text{rand}} \approx 0.06$

with $\sigma_{\text{hit}} \approx 0.02$ m, $\lambda_{\text{short}} \approx 1.0$ /m, $z_{\max} \approx 30$ m.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Module 2]**

**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the **working principle of a Time-of-Flight (ToF) range sensor** and a **triangulation-based range sensor**. Derive the range equations for both. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the **Likelihood Field Model** for a range finder. How is it computationally superior to the beam model? Show the mathematical form. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution for B(a):**

**Step 1 (1.5 marks):** **ToF Principle** — The sensor emits a pulse (light/ultrasound), measures round-trip time $\Delta t$. Diagram: emitter → obstacle → detector, timer.

$$d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

For Lidar: $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s. For sonar: $c \approx 343$ m/s.

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** **ToF Derivation** — Pulse travels distance $2d$ in time $\Delta t$, so distance $= c \cdot \Delta t$, hence $d = c \Delta t / 2$. Mention typical accuracy (mm for Lidar, cm for sonar).

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** **Triangulation Principle** — A laser stripe is projected, imaged by a camera offset by baseline $B$. The image position $x_{\text{image}}$ reveals the depth via similar triangles. Diagram: projector at one end, camera at the other, target surface showing the laser dot.

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** **Triangulation Derivation:**

Using pinhole camera geometry, $x_{\text{image}} / f = B / d$, hence:

$$d = \frac{B \cdot f}{x_{\text{image}}}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** State the **quadratic depth uncertainty growth**:

$$\sigma_d = \frac{d^2}{B f} \sigma_x$$

This is the *Cramer-Rao* lower bound and explains why Kinect-style sensors have limited range (~4 m).

---
