#### **Model Solution for B(b):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Define the **likelihood field**. Pre-compute a 2D distance transform $D(i, j)$ of the occupancy grid, where $D$ gives the distance from cell $(i, j)$ to the nearest occupied cell. For each beam endpoint $(x_{z_t^k}, y_{z_t^k})$, evaluate a Gaussian on $D$.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Mathematical form:

$$p(z_t^k \mid x_t, m) = z_{\text{hit}} \cdot \mathcal{N}\!\left(D\!\left(x_{z_t^k}, y_{z_t^k}\right); 0, \sigma_{\text{hit}}^{2}\right) + z_{\text{rand}} \cdot p_{\text{rand}}$$

where $z_{\text{hit}} + z_{\text{rand}} = 1$ and $\mathcal{N}(d; 0, \sigma^2) = \frac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp(-d^2 / 2\sigma^2)$.

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Computational superiority:
- **Beam model** requires *ray-casting* at every particle evaluation → $O(N \cdot K)$ where $N$ = particles, $K$ = beams.
- **Likelihood field** uses *table lookup* into the pre-computed $D$ field → $O(N)$ per particle, independent of $K$.

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Practical impact: ~100× speedup. Used in **ROS amcl**, **Google Cartographer**, and most production AMCL systems. Mention that the trade-off is *loss of physical fidelity* (cannot model unexpected short readings).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing observation model with motion model** — students often write $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$ (motion) in place of $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ (observation). The sensor model takes the **measurement** as the **conditioning variable**, not the state evolution. **−2 marks** for this confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting the normaliser $\eta$** — Bayes update is only a valid PDF if normalised. Examiners deduct **1 mark** for omitting $\eta$.
> 3. **Mixing up beam model parameters** — $p_{\text{short}}$ uses **exponential**, not Gaussian. Writing a Gaussian for the short branch is a common error. **−1 mark**.
> 4. **In Triangulation derivation** — students forget that the projected spot appears at the *image plane* offset, not the *world* offset. Use the pinhole model: $x_{\text{image}} / f = B / d$.
> 5. **State Markov assumption explicitly** — KTU 2024 scheme emphasises Outcome-Based Education. A bare equation without stating the Markov assumption loses **1 mark**.
> 6. **Confusing active vs passive sensors** — a *camera* is **passive**; *Lidar* is **active**. **−0.5 mark** if reversed.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for "Observation" (Module 2):**

- ✅ **Observation = Sensing.** It is the *update* stage of the Bayes filter, using measurement likelihood $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$.
- ✅ **Two big sensor families:** Proprioceptive (encoders, IMU) vs Exteroceptive (camera, Lidar, GPS, sonar).
- ✅ **Two energy types:** Active (Lidar, sonar) vs Passive (camera).
- ✅ **Three ranging principles:** Time-of-Flight, Phase-Shift, Triangulation.
- ✅ **Bayes update equation** is the single most important formula on this topic:

$$\text{bel}(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \, \overline{\text{bel}}(x_t)$$

- ✅ **Beam model** has 4 components: Hit (Gaussian), Short (Exponential), Max (Point mass), Random (Uniform). Mixing weights sum to 1.
- ✅ **Likelihood field model** uses a pre-computed 2D distance transform; faster but less physically faithful.
- ✅ **Triangation sensors** have depth uncertainty $\sigma_d \propto d^2$ — a fundamental engineering limit.
- ✅ **ToF sensors** have range $d = c \Delta t / 2$.
- ✅ **Markov assumption** is the engine of the update equation: current measurement depends only on current state and map.
- ✅ **Normaliser $\eta$** is mandatory for a valid PDF.
- ✅ **Sensor noise parameters** are tunable: $\sigma_{\text{hit}} \approx 0.02$ m, $p_{\text{hit}} \approx 0.79$, $z_{\max} \approx 30$ m for industrial Lidars.
- ✅ **Observation** feeds **localization**, **mapping**, **SLAM**, and **obstacle avoidance** — the four pillars of robot navigation.
- ✅ **Engineering fact:** ROS `amcl` package uses **likelihood field + particle filter** for production-grade localization.
- ✅ **Sensors degrade with weather:** sonar fails in vacuum, Lidar fails in heavy fog, cameras fail in darkness — justifying **sensor fusion**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement Prediction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT735 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Measurement Prediction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Measurement Prediction** in robot navigation is the computational process of estimating the expected sensor reading a robot *should* observe, given its current belief about its own state (pose) and the known geometry of the environment. Formally, if the robot state at time $t$ is denoted as $x_t$ and the sensor model is $z_t = h(x_t) + \epsilon_t$, then the measurement prediction is given by:

$$\hat{z}_t = h(\mu_{t\mid t-1})$$

where $\mu_{t\mid t-1}$ is the *predicted mean state* (obtained from the state prediction step) and $h(\cdot)$ is the measurement function mapping state space to sensor observation space.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Measurement prediction is the *bridge* between the **kinematic/action layer** (where the robot predicts where it will be) and the **perception/sensor layer** (where the robot predicts what its sensors should report). It is the foundational operation of the **Kalman Filter Predict–Update cycle** and all its variants (EKF, UKF, Particle Filter).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **blindfolded in your own kitchen** and you know exactly where the refrigerator is (from memory). You take **3 steps forward**. Before reaching out to touch anything, your brain *predicts* that your hand should be roughly **1.5 metres from the fridge door**. That internally computed expected distance is the **measurement prediction**.

In robotics:
- The **robot's internal state** $\mu_{t\mid t-1}$ = "I am 3 steps ahead of where I started."
- The **measurement function** $h(\cdot)$ = "If I am here, the wall sensor *should* return 1.5 m."
- The **predicted measurement** $\hat{z}_t$ = 1.5 m (a number, not a real reading).
- The **actual measurement** $z_t$ is what the sensor really reports (say, 1.48 m).
- The **innovation** $\nu_t = z_t - \hat{z}_t$ is how surprised the robot is. This surprise is later used to correct the state estimate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is Measurement Prediction Necessary?**
> 1. It enables **sensor fusion** by comparing predicted vs actual readings.
> 2. It allows **anomaly detection** (e.g., a sudden large innovation means an unexpected obstacle).
> 3. It is the precondition for computing the **Kalman Gain**, which optimally weights the new measurement against the prior belief.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $\mu_{t\mid t-1}$ | Predicted mean state | m, rad |
| $\Sigma_{t\mid t-1}$ | Predicted state covariance | m², rad² |
| $\hat{z}_t$ | Predicted measurement | m, rad |
| $Q_t$ | Measurement noise covariance | m², rad² |
| $R_t$ | Process noise covariance | m², rad² |
| $\nu_t$ | Innovation (residual) | m, rad |
| $S_t$ | Innovation covariance | m², rad² |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Predict–Update Paradigm

Probabilistic robot navigation is built on a **recursive Bayesian filter** that operates in two alternating steps:

1. **State Prediction (Time Update):** Propagates the belief forward through the motion model.
2. **Measurement Prediction (Observation Update):** Propagates the belief through the sensor model.

Measurement prediction specifically happens in **both** halves of the cycle:
- **Before** the actual measurement arrives: the robot computes $\hat{z}_t = h(\mu_{t\mid t-1})$.
- **After** the actual measurement arrives: the robot computes the innovation $\nu_t = z_t - \hat{z}_t$ and uses it to update the state.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Flow

- **Step 1 — State Propagation:** The motion model $x_t = g(u_t, x_{t-1}) + \delta_t$ updates the belief to $\mu_{t\mid t-1}$.
- **Step 2 — Measurement Model Definition:** The function $h: \mathbb{R}^n \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^m$ maps the state space into the measurement space (e.g., distance to a known landmark).
- **Step 3 — Predicted Measurement Computation:** Apply $h$ to the *predicted* state, not the corrected one. This is the "prediction."
- **Step 4 — Innovation Computation:** Subtract the prediction from the actual sensor reading to obtain the residual $\nu_t$.
- **Step 5 — Covariance Propagation:** The uncertainty in the state $\Sigma_{t\mid t-1}$ must be mapped into measurement-space uncertainty via the Jacobian $H_t$.
- **Step 6 — Kalman Gain Calculation:** $K_t$ is computed from $S_t$ and tells the filter *how much* to trust the new measurement.
- **Step 7 — Belief Update:** The state mean and covariance are corrected using $\nu_t$ and $K_t$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Board-Exam Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Safeguard:** All absolute-value bars in the table below are written as `\vert` (not the pipe character) to preserve markdown table syntax.

| **Formula** | **Name** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| $\mu_{t\mid t-1} = A_t\,\mu_{t-1\mid t-1} + B_t\,u_t$ | State mean prediction | m, rad |
| $\Sigma_{t\mid t-1} = A_t\,\Sigma_{t-1\mid t-1}\,A_t^T + R_t$ | State covariance prediction | m², rad² |
| $\hat{z}_t = h(\mu_{t\mid t-1})$ | **Predicted measurement** | m, rad |
| $H_t = \dfrac{\partial h}{\partial x}\big\vert_{\mu_{t\mid t-1}}$ | Measurement Jacobian | dimensionless |
| $\nu_t = z_t - \hat{z}_t$ | Innovation / residual | m, rad |
| $S_t = H_t\,\Sigma_{t\mid t-1}\,H_t^T + Q_t$ | Innovation covariance | m², rad² |
| $K_t = \Sigma_{t\mid t-1}\,H_t^T\,S_t^{-1}$ | Kalman Gain | dimensionless |
| $\mu_{t\mid t} = \mu_{t\mid t-1} + K_t\,\nu_t$ | State update | m, rad |
| $\Sigma_{t\mid t} = (I - K_t\,H_t)\,\Sigma_{t\mid t-1}$ | Covariance update | m², rad² |
| $z_r = \sqrt{(x_l - x)^2 + (y_l - y)^2}$ | Range model | metres |
| $z_\theta = \mathrm{atan2}(y_l - y,\, x_l - x) - \theta$ | Bearing model | radians |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles (Self-Driving Cars):** Predicted lidar/ radar returns are compared with actual returns for **obstacle tracking and SLAM** (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping).
- **Aerospace Drones:** IMU + GPS measurement prediction enables robust **outdoor navigation** in GPS-denied zones.
- **Warehouse AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles):** Range-finder measurement prediction against a known map drives the **Monte-Carlo Localization** algorithm.
- **Underwater ROVs:** Sonar measurement prediction against bathymetric maps supports **AUV navigation** in deep-sea missions.
- **Space Rovers (e.g., NASA's Mars rovers):** Visual odometry + wheel odometry measurement prediction underpins **visual-inertial SLAM**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Range-Bearing Measurement Model and its Jacobian

Consider a 2-D robot with state $x = [x,\, y,\, \theta]^T$ that observes a stationary landmark at known position $m = [x_l,\, y_l]^T$ using a range-bearing sensor (e.g., 2-D lidar). The true measurement is:

$$z = \begin{bmatrix} z_r \\ z_b \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{(x_l - x)^2 + (y_l - y)^2} \\ \mathrm{atan2}(y_l - y,\, x_l - x) - \theta \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} \epsilon_r \\ \epsilon_b \end{bmatrix}$$

where $\epsilon_r \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_r^2)$ and $\epsilon_b \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_b^2)$ are zero-mean Gaussian noise terms. Define the predicted range $\hat{r} = \sqrt{(x_l - x)^2 + (x_l - x)^2}$ (an abuse: see below) and the predicted bearing $\hat{b} = \mathrm{atan2}(y_l - y,\, x_l - x) - \theta$.

### 3.1.1 Jacobian Computation

The measurement Jacobian $H = \dfrac{\partial h}{\partial x}$ is required for the EKF update. Compute each partial derivative component-wise.

**Range partials** (with $\Delta x = x_l - x$ and $\Delta y = y_l - y$):

$$\frac{\partial z_r}{\partial x} = \frac{-\Delta x}{\sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2}} = \frac{-\Delta x}{z_r}$$

$$\frac{\partial z_r}{\partial y} = \frac{-\Delta y}{\sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2}} = \frac{-\Delta y}{z_r}$$

$$\frac{\partial z_r}{\partial \theta} = 0$$

**Bearing partials** (using $\mathrm{atan2}$ derivative rules):

$$\frac{\partial z_b}{\partial x} = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2}$$

$$\frac{\partial z_b}{\partial y} = \frac{-\Delta x}{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2}$$

$$\frac{\partial z_b}{\partial \theta} = -1$$

> [!NOTE]
> The denominators $\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2 = z_r^2$ allow simplification in many KTU board problems.

### 3.1.2 Assembled Jacobian

Stacking the partials into a $2 \times 3$ matrix:

$$H = \begin{bmatrix} -\dfrac{\Delta x}{z_r} & -\dfrac{\Delta y}{z_r} & 0 \\[8pt] \dfrac{\Delta y}{z_r^2} & -\dfrac{\Delta x}{z_r^2} & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

This is the canonical EKF measurement Jacobian seen in *Probabilistic Robotics* (Thrun, Burgard, Fox) and forms the basis for all range-bearing EKF derivations.

## 3.2 Derivation: Innovation Covariance from First Principles

The innovation $\nu_t = z_t - \hat{z}_t$ is a random variable because both $z_t$ and $\hat{z}_t$ are random. The covariance of a sum/difference of random variables is the sum of their covariances. The covariance of $\hat{z}_t$ comes from propagating the state covariance $\Sigma_{t\mid t-1}$ through the (linearised) measurement function via $H_t$:

$$\mathrm{Cov}(\hat{z}_t) = H_t\,\Sigma_{t\mid t-1}\,H_t^T$$

The covariance of the actual measurement noise is the sensor noise covariance $Q_t$. Since the noise is independent of the state, the total innovation covariance is:

$$S_t = H_t\,\Sigma_{t\mid t-1}\,H_t^T + Q_t$$

### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step Justification

1. Define the innovation as $\nu_t = z_t - h(\mu_{t\mid t-1}) = H_t(x_t - \mu_{t\mid t-1}) + \epsilon_t$ (first-order Taylor expansion).
2. The first term $H_t(x_t - \mu_{t\mid t-1})$ has covariance $H_t\,\Sigma_{t\mid t-1}\,H_t^T$ (linear combination of a Gaussian).
3. The second term $\epsilon_t$ has covariance $Q_t$.
4. Independence $\Rightarrow$ covariance of the sum is the sum of the covariances.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A robot with state $\mu = [2.0,\, 3.0,\, \pi/4]^T$ and covariance $\Sigma = \mathrm{diag}(0.1,\, 0.1,\, 0.05)$ observes a landmark at $m = [7.0,\, 4.0]^T$. Sensor noise $Q = \mathrm{diag}(0.2^2,\, 0.05^2)$. Compute the predicted measurement $\hat{z}$, the measurement Jacobian $H$, and the innovation covariance $S$. Assume the actual sensor reading is $z = [5.10,\, -\pi/4 + 0.04]^T$ (i.e., range 5.10 m, bearing slightly off). Compute the innovation $\nu$.

### Step 1: Compute $\Delta x$ and $\Delta y$

$$\Delta x = x_l - x = 7.0 - 2.0 = 5.0$$

$$\Delta y = y_l - y = 4.0 - 3.0 = 1.0$$

### Step 2: Predicted Range

$$\hat{z}_r = \sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2} = \sqrt{25 + 1} = \sqrt{26} \approx 5.099\ \text{m}$$

### Step 3: Predicted Bearing

$$\hat{z}_b = \mathrm{atan2}(1.0,\, 5.0) - \pi/4 = 0.1974 - 0.7854 \approx -0.5880\ \text{rad}$$

### Step 4: Assembled Jacobian

$$H = \begin{bmatrix} -5.0/5.099 & -1.0/5.099 & 0 \\ 1.0/26.0 & -5.0/26.0 & -1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.9806 & -0.1961 & 0 \\ 0.0385 & -0.1923 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 5: Innovation Covariance

First compute $H\,\Sigma\,H^T$:

$$H\,\Sigma = \begin{bmatrix} -0.9806\cdot 0.1 & -0.1961\cdot 0.1 & 0 \\ 0.0385\cdot 0.1 & -0.1923\cdot 0.1 & -0.05 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.0981 & -0.0196 & 0 \\ 0.00385 & -0.0192 & -0.05 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$H\,\Sigma\,H^T = \begin{bmatrix} 0.0100 & 0.0030 \\ 0.0030 & 0.0528 \end{bmatrix}$$

(Values shown rounded for readability; full expansion yields the exact $2\times 2$ matrix.)

$$S = H\,\Sigma\,H^T + Q = \begin{bmatrix} 0.0100 + 0.04 & 0.0030 \\ 0.0030 & 0.0528 + 0.0025 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.0500 & 0.0030 \\ 0.0030 & 0.0553 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 6: Innovation

$$\nu = z - \hat{z} = \begin{bmatrix} 5.10 - 5.099 \\ (-\pi/4 + 0.04) - (-0.5880) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.001 \\ 0.040 + 0.5880 - 0.5880 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.001 \\ 0.040 \end{bmatrix}$$

(Recomputing the bearing difference: $(-\pi/4 + 0.04) = -0.7454$; subtract $\hat{z}_b = -0.5880$ gives $\nu_b = -0.1574$ rad. The example illustrates that even a small heading error produces a noticeable bearing residual.)

### Step 7: Kalman Gain

$$K = \Sigma\,H^T\,S^{-1}$$

This is computed in the next iteration. For the KTU exam, students are typically expected to state the formula and substitute numeric values.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Measurement Prediction inside the Kalman Filter Loop

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how measurement prediction slots into the broader Kalman filter cycle. All node identifiers are alphanumeric, and labels contain only raw uppercase text.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT: PREVIOUS STATE MEAN mu_tminus1]
    B[INPUT: PREVIOUS COVARIANCE Sigma_tminus1]
    C[INPUT: CONTROL COMMAND u_t]
    D[INPUT: SENSOR READING z_t]
    E[STATE PREDICTION BLOCK]
    F[STATE COVARIANCE PREDICTION BLOCK]
    G[MEASUREMENT PREDICTION BLOCK]
    H[JACOBIAN EVALUATION BLOCK]
    I[INNOVATION COMPUTATION BLOCK]
    J[KALMAN GAIN COMPUTATION BLOCK]
    K[STATE UPDATE BLOCK]
    L[COVARIANCE UPDATE BLOCK]
    M[OUTPUT: UPDATED BELIEF mu_t, Sigma_t]
    N[LOOP BACK TO NEXT TIME STEP]

    A --> E
    C --> E
    B --> F
    A --> F
    E --> G
    E --> H
    G --> I
    H --> I
    D --> I
    H --> J
    F --> J
    J --> K
    I --> K
    E --> K
    J --> L
    F --> L
    H --> L
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Functional Data-Flow of a Measurement-Prediction Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[ROBOT STATE BELIEF mu_tminus1]
    P2[MAP KNOWLEDGE]
    P3[SENSOR MODEL h OF X]
    P4[PREDICTED MEASUREMENT z_hat_t]
    P5[INNOVATION nu_t]
    P6[COVARIANCE S_t]
    P7[KALMAN GAIN K_t]
    P8[CORRECTED STATE mu_t]

    P1 --> P3
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P1 --> P4
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> P7
    P3 --> P6
    P1 --> P6
    P6 --> P7
    P7 --> P8
    P5 --> P8
    P1 --> P8
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart: Distinguishing State Prediction from Measurement Prediction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SP[START: TIME STEP T]
    SP1[MOTION MODEL G: X_t = G U_t X_tminus1]
    SP2[STATE PREDICTION mu_tminus1 TO mu_tbytminus1]
    SP3[COVARIANCE PREDICTION Sigma_tminus1 TO Sigma_tbytminus1]
    MP1[MEASUREMENT MODEL H: Z_t = H X_t]
    MP2[MEASUREMENT PREDICTION z_hat_t = H OF mu_tbytminus1]
    MP3[INNOVATION nu_t = z_t MINUS z_hat_t]
    MP4[INNOVATION COVARIANCE S_t]
    MP5[KALMAN GAIN K_t]
    SP4[STATE UPDATE mu_t]
    SP5[COVARIANCE UPDATE Sigma_t]
    SP6[END: READY FOR STEP T PLUS 1]

    SP --> SP1 --> SP2 --> SP3
    SP2 --> MP2
    SP3 --> MP3
    SP3 --> MP4
    MP1 --> MP2
    MP2 --> MP3
    MP3 --> MP5
    MP4 --> MP5
    MP5 --> SP4
    MP5 --> SP5
    MP3 --> SP4
    SP2 --> SP4
    SP2 --> SP5
    SP4 --> SP6
    SP5 --> SP6
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Before Attempting**
> - **Do not confuse state prediction with measurement prediction.** State prediction propagates the *state* through the motion model. Measurement prediction propagates the *state* through the *measurement model* to get an *expected sensor reading*. Examiners explicitly check this distinction for 1–2 marks.
> - **Always include the measurement noise covariance $Q_t$** when computing $S_t$. Omitting it is the most common 1-mark deduction.
> - **Jacobians must be evaluated at $\mu_{t\mid t-1}$, not at the true state $x_t$.** This is the definition of an EKF and is worth 2 marks in derivations.
> - **Units matter.** Mixing degrees and radians in the bearing model $\hat{z}_b$ is a frequent error.
> - **Sign of bearing residual:** Some students compute $\hat{z}_t - z_t$ instead of $z_t - \hat{z}_t$. Stick to the standard $\nu_t = z_t - \hat{z}_t$ as defined in the KTU reference materials.

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define measurement prediction in the context of robot navigation. Write the mathematical expression for the predicted measurement.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Measurement prediction is the process of computing the expected sensor reading the robot *should* observe, given its predicted state and a known measurement model. It bridges the motion-update phase and the sensor-update phase of the Kalman filter. Mathematically:

$$\hat{z}_t = h(\mu_{t \mid t-1})$$

where $\mu_{t \mid t-1}$ is the predicted mean state and $h(\cdot)$ is the (possibly non-linear) measurement function. (3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for the role, 1 for the formula.)

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between state prediction and measurement prediction. Mention the equations involved.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | State Prediction | Measurement Prediction |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Estimate future pose | Estimate future sensor reading |
| Model | Motion model $g(\cdot)$ | Measurement model $h(\cdot)$ |
| Input | $\mu_{t-1\mid t-1}$, $u_t$ | $\mu_{t\mid t-1}$ |
| Output | $\mu_{t\mid t-1}$, $\Sigma_{t\mid t-1}$ | $\hat{z}_t$ |
| Noise term | $R_t$ (process) | $Q_t$ (sensor) |

(3 marks: 1 for purpose distinction, 1 for the model distinction, 1 for the correct equations.)

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### Q.A(a) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the role of measurement prediction in the Kalman filter cycle with a clear block diagram representation. Derive the innovation covariance and the Kalman gain expressions. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark): Role of measurement prediction.** It converts the *predicted state* into a *predicted observation* so that it can be directly compared with the *actual sensor reading*. The difference (innovation) drives the state update.

**Step 2 (1 mark): Block diagram.** Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1. The flow is: state prediction → measurement prediction → innovation → Kalman gain → state update.

**Step 3 (2 marks): Innovation covariance derivation.** Starting from $\nu_t = z_t - h(\mu_{t\mid t-1})$, linearising $h$ about $\mu_{t\mid t-1}$:

$$\nu_t \approx H_t(x_t - \mu_{t\mid t-1}) + \epsilon_t$$

Since $x_t - \mu_{t\mid t-1} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \Sigma_{t\mid t-1})$ and $\epsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t)$, and the two are independent:

$$S_t = \mathrm{Cov}(\nu_t) = H_t\,\Sigma_{t\mid t-1}\,H_t^T + Q_t$$

**Step 4 (2 marks): Kalman gain derivation.** The Kalman gain minimises the posterior state covariance. Using the Schur complement or direct minimisation, the optimal gain is:

$$K_t = \Sigma_{t\mid t-1}\,H_t^T\,S_t^{-1}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark): State update.** $\mu_{t\mid t} = \mu_{t\mid t-1} + K_t \nu_t$ completes the cycle.

---

#### Q.A(b) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**A differential-drive robot at state $\mu = [1.0,\, 2.0,\, \pi/6]^T$ observes a landmark at $m = [4.0,\, 5.0]^T$ using a range-bearing sensor. Sensor noise covariance is $Q = \mathrm{diag}(0.15^2,\, 0.04^2)$. State covariance is $\Sigma = \mathrm{diag}(0.05,\, 0.05,\, 0.02)$. Compute (i) the predicted measurement $\hat{z}$, (ii) the measurement Jacobian $H$, and (iii) the innovation covariance $S$. Take the actual measurement as $z = [3.61,\, 0.46]^T$. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark): Compute $\Delta x$ and $\Delta y$.**
$$\Delta x = 4.0 - 1.0 = 3.0,\quad \Delta y = 5.0 - 2.0 = 3.0$$

**Step 2 (1 mark): Predicted range.**
$$\hat{z}_r = \sqrt{3.0^2 + 3.0^2} = \sqrt{18} \approx 4.243\ \text{m}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark): Predicted bearing.**
$$\hat{z}_b = \mathrm{atan2}(3.0,\, 3.0) - \pi/6 = \pi/4 - \pi/6 = \pi/12 \approx 0.2618\ \text{rad}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark): Jacobian.**
$$H = \begin{bmatrix} -3.0/4.243 & -3.0/4.243 & 0 \\ 3.0/18.0 & -3.0/18.0 & -1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.7071 & -0.7071 & 0 \\ 0.1667 & -0.1667 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 (2 marks): Innovation covariance.**
Compute $H \Sigma H^T$:
$$H \Sigma = \begin{bmatrix} -0.7071\cdot 0.05 & -0.7071\cdot 0.05 & 0 \\ 0.1667\cdot 0.05 & -0.1667\cdot 0.05 & -0.02 \end{bmatrix}$$
Then add $Q = \mathrm{diag}(0.0225, 0.0016)$:

$$S = H \Sigma H^T + Q = \begin{bmatrix} 0.05 & 0.0125 \\ 0.0125 & 0.0217 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.0225 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.0016 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.0725 & 0.0125 \\ 0.0125 & 0.0233 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark): Innovation.**
$$\nu = z - \hat{z} = \begin{bmatrix} 3.61 - 4.243 \\ 0.46 - 0.2618 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.633 \\ 0.198 \end{bmatrix}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

#### Q.B(a) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**With a suitable diagram, explain the extended Kalman filter (EKF) measurement update step. Why is linearisation needed, and where does the Jacobian $H_t$ enter the formulation? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark): EKF motivation.** Most real-world sensor models (e.g., range-bearing, camera) are non-linear. The standard Kalman filter requires linearity. The EKF handles non-linearity by linearising $h(\cdot)$ at every step.

**Step 2 (2 marks): Linearisation concept.** Given $z = h(x) + \epsilon$, a first-order Taylor expansion about $\mu_{t\mid t-1}$ gives:
$$h(x) \approx h(\mu_{t\mid t-1}) + H_t(x - \mu_{t\mid t-1})$$
where $H_t = \dfrac{\partial h}{\partial x}\big\vert_{\mu_{t\mid t-1}}$ is the Jacobian.

**Step 3 (2 marks): Diagram description.** (Refer to Section 4.3 flowchart.) The state goes through the non-linear measurement model to produce the predicted measurement; the Jacobian projects the state covariance into measurement space; the innovation is then used to compute the Kalman gain and update the state.

**Step 4 (2 marks): Role of $H_t$.** $H_t$ is the bridge between state-space uncertainty and measurement-space uncertainty. It appears in three places: the innovation covariance $S_t = H_t \Sigma H_t^T + Q_t$, the Kalman gain $K_t = \Sigma H_t^T S_t^{-1}$, and the covariance update $\Sigma_{t\mid t} = (I - K_t H_t)\Sigma_{t\mid t-1}$.

---

#### Q.B(b) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Discuss the importance of the innovation $\nu_t$ in measurement prediction. How is its covariance used to detect sensor faults or outliers? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark): Definition.** The innovation $\nu_t = z_t - \hat{z}_t$ is the discrepancy between what the robot expected to see and what it actually saw.

**Step 2 (1 mark): Statistical property.** $\nu_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, S_t)$ under the assumption that the measurement model and noise are correct.

**Step 3 (2 marks): Mahalanobis distance for outlier detection.** Define the normalised innovation squared (NIS):
$$\epsilon_t = \nu_t^T S_t^{-1} \nu_t \sim \chi^2_m$$
where $m$ is the measurement dimension. If $\epsilon_t$ exceeds a threshold (e.g., $5\%$ tail of the $\chi^2$ distribution), the measurement is flagged as an outlier.

**Step 4 (2 marks): Application to fault detection.** Large NIS values indicate:
- An unexpected obstacle (range sensor suddenly short).
- A landmark moved (in dynamic environments).
- A sensor malfunction (stuck or biased reading).

**Step 5 (1 mark): Engineering example.** In a self-driving car, a sudden NIS spike from the front radar can trigger emergency braking by the safety controller, even before the planner reacts.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Measurement prediction $\hat{z}_t = h(\mu_{t\mid t-1})$ is the expected sensor reading given the predicted state. [1 mark definition, 1 mark formula = frequently asked]
- **Innovation:** $\nu_t = z_t - \hat{z}_t$. Always subtract *actual minus predicted*. [1 mark]
- **Innovation covariance:** $S_t = H_t \Sigma_{t\mid t-1} H_t^T + Q_t$. Do not forget $Q_t$. [2 marks]
- **Kalman gain:** $K_t = \Sigma_{t\mid t-1} H_t^T S_t^{-1}$. Optimal weighting between prior and measurement. [2 marks]
- **Jacobian must be evaluated at $\mu_{t\mid t-1}$**, not at the true state. [2 marks in EKF derivations]
- **Range-bearing model:** $z_r = \sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2}$ and $z_b = \mathrm{atan2}(\Delta y, \Delta x) - \theta$. [Frequently asked numerical]
- **Bearing model is angle-wrapped** to $[-\pi, \pi]$ — a common source of errors.
- **NIS statistic:** $\nu^T S^{-1} \nu$ follows a $\chi^2$ distribution and is used for **outlier detection**. [3-mark favourite]
- **EKF vs UKF:** EKF linearises via Jacobian; UKF uses sigma points to avoid derivative computation. Mention briefly for 1 mark.
- **Units:** Keep ranges in metres, bearings in radians (unless explicitly told otherwise).
- **State prediction vs measurement prediction:** State uses motion model $g$ and $R_t$; measurement uses sensor model $h$ and $Q_t$. [Common 3-mark question]
- **Engineering applications:** Self-driving cars, AUVs, drones, AGVs, Mars rovers — always cite at least one for the "real-world utility" portion of a 7-mark answer.
- **Block-diagram fluency:** Be able to draw (or describe) the full predict–update cycle with measurement prediction clearly labelled. [3 marks in 7-mark answers]

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Matching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT735 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Matching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Robot Navigation**, **Matching** refers to the computational process of establishing **correspondences** (associations) between two sets of data representations — typically between observations acquired at different time instances, from different sensor modalities, or against a pre-existing map. It is a foundational operation in **Visual Odometry (VO)**, **Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM)**, and **Place Recognition**.

Mathematically, matching seeks to find a function $f$ that maps elements from a query set $Q = \{q_1, q_2, \dots, q_n\}$ to elements in a reference set $R = \{r_1, r_2, \dots, r_m\}$ such that a similarity metric $S(q_i, r_j)$ is maximized (or a distance metric is minimized).

$$
f: Q \rightarrow R, \quad f(q_i) = \arg\max_{r_j \in R} \, S(q_i, r_j)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PERUT735 - Module 2):**
> Matching is the core step that enables a robot to answer two critical questions: *"Where am I?"* (Localization via Loop Closure) and *"Is this the place I've seen before?"* (Place Recognition). Without robust matching, SLAM suffers from drift and catastrophic localization failure.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are walking through a large, unfamiliar city (the **environment**) and you have a guidebook containing photos of landmarks (the **map**). As you walk, you take photos with your camera. To know where you are, you compare your current photo with the photos in the guidebook.

- **Your current photo** = Query set $Q$
- **Guidebook photos** = Reference set $R$
- **The act of comparing** = **Matching**

If you find a photo of the "Eiffel Tower" in the guidebook that looks just like the tower in your current photo, you've made a **match** — and now you know your location!

In robotics, instead of guidebook photos, we use **feature descriptors** (mathematical fingerprints of image regions, 3D points, or LiDAR scans). The robot extracts these fingerprints from its current observation and searches for the most similar fingerprints in its memory to establish correspondences.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Matching is Hard (Real-World Challenges):**
> - **Viewpoint changes**: The robot sees the same object from different angles.
> - **Illumination changes**: Day vs. night, shadows, weather.
> - **Scale variations**: Objects appear bigger when closer.
> - **Dynamic objects**: Moving people, cars occluding static landmarks.
> - **Perceptual aliasing**: Two distinct places may look very similar.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

| Metric | Typical Value / Notation |
|---|---|
| SIFT descriptor dimension | **128** |
| SURF descriptor dimension | **64** |
| ORB descriptor dimension | **32** (binary) |
| FLANN search complexity | $O(\log n)$ (approx.) |
| Lowe’s ratio threshold | **0.7** to **0.8** |
| Inlier ratio (RANSAC) | $\geq \mathbf{0.3}$ (rule of thumb) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Matching Techniques in Robot Navigation

Matching techniques can be classified along **three primary axes**: the *sensor modality*, the *data representation*, and the *matching strategy*.

1. **Image-to-Image Matching (2D-2D)**
   - Used in monocular/stereo Visual Odometry and Loop Closure.
   - Features: Keypoints (corners, blobs) + Descriptors.
   - Algorithms: SIFT, SURF, ORB, BRIEF, AKAZE.

2. **Point Cloud Matching (3D-3D)**
   - Used in LiDAR-based SLAM (LOAM, LIO-SAM).
   - Methods: ICP (Iterative Closest Point), NDT (Normal Distributions Transform), G-ICP.

3. **Image-to-Point Cloud Matching (2D-3D)**
   - Used in sensor fusion and visual localization against 3D maps.
   - Solved via PnP (Perspective-n-Point) problem.

4. **Image-to-Map / Place Recognition**
   - Used for global relocalization.
   - Methods: Bag-of-Words (DBoW2, DBoW3), NetVLAD, VLAD, AP-GeM.

5. **Scan Matching (2D Laser)**
   - Used in 2D LiDAR SLAM (Hector, Karto, GMapping).
   - Methods: ICP, Correlative Scan Matching, CSM.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Pipeline of Feature Matching

A standard feature-based matching pipeline executes the following stages:

1. **Feature Detection**: Identify salient, repeatable keypoints in both the query and reference images.
   *Mathematical formulation*: Detect local extrema of a Difference-of-Gaussian (DoG) scale-space function.
   $$
   D(x, y, \sigma) = L(x, y, k\sigma) - L(x, y, \sigma)
   $$
   where $L(x, y, \sigma) = G(x, y, \sigma) * I(x, y)$ is the scale-space representation and $G$ is the Gaussian kernel.

2. **Feature Description**: Assign a compact, distinctive descriptor vector to each keypoint.
   *For SIFT*: A 128-D vector encoding local gradient histograms.
   *For ORB*: A 32-byte binary string from BRIEF, made rotation-invariant.

3. **Descriptor Matching**: For each descriptor in $Q$, find its nearest neighbor(s) in $R$.
   *Metric choice*: Euclidean distance for SIFT/SURF; Hamming distance for ORB/BRIEF.

4. **Outlier Rejection**: Remove incorrect matches using geometric verification.
   *Tools*: Lowe’s ratio test, RANSAC, MAGSAC.

5. **Geometric Estimation**: Estimate the transformation (pose change) consistent with the inlier correspondences.
   *Models*: Homography (planar), Essential/Fundamental matrix (general).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for Matching in Robot Navigation**

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Key Parameter | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Gaussian Scale-Space | $L(x,y,\sigma) = G(x,y,\sigma) * I(x,y)$ | $\sigma$: scale | Multi-scale feature detection |
| 2 | Difference-of-Gaussian | $D(x,y,\sigma) = L(x,y,k\sigma) - L(x,y,\sigma)$ | $k \approx \sqrt{2}$ | SIFT keypoint localization |
| 3 | SIFT Descriptor | 128-D vector of gradient histograms | orientation $\theta$ | Robust to scale + rotation |
| 4 | Euclidean Distance | $d(p,q) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(p_i - q_i)^2}$ | $n$ = descriptor size | Float descriptor matching |
| 5 | Hamming Distance | $d_H(b_1,b_2) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} b_1[i] \oplus b_2[i]$ | bitwise XOR | Binary descriptor matching |
| 6 | Lowe’s Ratio Test | $\frac{d_1}{d_2} < \tau, \; \tau \in [0.7, 0.8]$ | $d_1$: best, $d_2$: 2nd best | Discriminative match filtering |
| 7 | ICP Objective | $\min_{R,t} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \Vert R p_i + t - q_i \Vert^2$ | $R \in SO(3), t \in \mathbb{R}^3$ | 3D point cloud alignment |
| 8 | Homography (DLT) | $\lambda \begin{bmatrix} u' \\ v' \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = H \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $H$: 3×3, 8 DOF | Planar scene matching |
| 9 | Epipolar Constraint | $x'^T F x = 0$ | $F$: 3×3, 7 DOF | Stereo / motion matching |
| 10 | RANSAC Iterations | $k = \frac{\log(1-p)}{\log(1-w^n)}$ | $p$: success prob., $w$: inlier ratio | Robust estimation |
| 11 | FLANN Search | KD-tree, $O(\log n)$ approx. | randomized forests | Fast descriptor lookup |
| 12 | Bag-of-Words Score | $s(q,v) = \sum_{i} n_{q_i} \cdot \text{idf}(v_i)$ | tf-idf weighting | Loop closure / place recognition |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

| Field | Application of Matching |
|---|---|
| Autonomous Vehicles (Waymo, Tesla) | Lane/landmark matching, HD-map localization |
| Aerial Drones (DJI) | Visual-inertial odometry, GPS-denied navigation |
| AR/VR (Apple Vision Pro, Meta Quest) | Inside-out tracking, SLAM |
| Warehouse Robotics (Amazon Kiva) | Marker/fiducial matching for localization |
| Underwater ROVs | Sonar scan matching in murky waters |
| Planetary Rovers (NASA Perseverance) | Visual terrain relative navigation |
| Medical Robots | Image registration for surgical guidance |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Lowe’s Ratio Test Criterion

The **Lowe's ratio test** is the single most important outlier rejection step in descriptor-based matching. Below is its complete derivation and justification.

**Step 1 — Define the distance ratio.**
For a query descriptor $q$, let $r_1$ be its nearest neighbor (smallest distance $d_1$) and $r_2$ be its second-nearest neighbor (distance $d_2$). The ratio is:

$$
\rho = \frac{d(q, r_1)}{d(q, r_2)}
$$

**Step 2 — Analyze the distribution under the null hypothesis (incorrect match).**
If $q$ has no true match in $R$, the distances to all reference descriptors are roughly equal (high-dimensional random vectors are equidistant). Thus $d_1 \approx d_2$, and $\rho \to 1$.

**Step 3 — Analyze the distribution under the correct match hypothesis.**
If $r_1$ is the true match, $d_1$ is significantly smaller than $d_2$. Hence $\rho \to 0$.

**Step 4 — Threshold selection.**
Empirically, David Lowe found that thresholding at $\tau \in [0.7, 0.8]$ discards **90% of false matches** while retaining **90% of correct matches** on standard benchmarks.

$$
\text{Accept match} \iff \rho = \frac{d_1}{d_2} < \tau
$$

**Step 5 — Why this works (intuition via probability).**
The ratio $\rho$ behaves like a likelihood-ratio test between two hypotheses:

- $H_0$: $r_1$ is *not* a true match (uniform distances).
- $H_1$: $r_1$ *is* the true match (one distance much smaller).

A small $\rho$ provides strong evidence for $H_1$, providing a principled statistical filter.

### 3.2 Detailed Derivation: ICP (Iterative Closest Point)

ICP is the canonical algorithm for **3D point cloud matching**. Below is its full mathematical derivation.

**Given:** Two point sets — a **source** $P = \{p_i\}_{i=1}^{N}$ and a **target** $Q = \{q_j\}_{j=1}^{M}$, with $N, M$ typically equal (after resampling).

**Goal:** Find the rigid transformation $(R, t)$ that minimizes the sum of squared Euclidean distances between corresponding points:

$$
E(R, t) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \Vert R p_i + t - q_{c(i)} \Vert^2
$$

where $c(i)$ is the correspondence index of $p_i$ in $Q$.

**Step 1 — Compute centroids.**

$$
\bar{p} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i, \qquad \bar{q} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} q_{c(i)}
$$

**Step 2 — Center the points (subtract centroids).**

$$
p_i' = p_i - \bar{p}, \qquad q_i' = q_{c(i)} - \bar{q}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the $3 \times 3$ cross-covariance matrix $H$.**

$$
H = \sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i' \, q_i'^T
$$

**Step 4 — Compute SVD of $H$.**

$$
H = U \Sigma V^T
$$

**Step 5 — Enforce proper rotation (det = +1).**

$$
R = V \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \det(VU^T) \end{bmatrix} U^T
$$

This step is essential — without it, SVD may return a reflection ($det = -1$), which is not a valid rotation.

**Step 6 — Compute the translation.**

$$
t = \bar{q} - R \bar{p}
$$

**Step 7 — Iterate.**
Apply the transformation to $P$, recompute correspondences (nearest neighbor in $Q$), and re-estimate $(R, t)$ until convergence (change in $E$ below threshold, e.g., $10^{-6}$, or max iterations, e.g., **50**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Convergence Properties:**
> - ICP is **guaranteed to monotonically decrease** the error $E$ at each iteration.
> - ICP converges to a **local minimum**, not the global minimum.
> - A good **initial guess** (e.g., from odometry) is critical for global convergence.

### 3.3 Detailed Derivation: RANSAC Iteration Count

**Given:** A model requiring $n$ correspondences to be estimated (e.g., $n=8$ for a homography, $n=5$ for an essential matrix with known intrinsics). The inlier ratio is $w$ and the desired probability of success is $p$ (typically $p = 0.99$).

**Step 1 — Probability that a single random sample is all inliers.**

$$
P(\text{sample correct}) = w^n
$$

**Step 2 — Probability that a single random sample contains at least one outlier.**

$$
P(\text{sample bad}) = 1 - w^n
$$

**Step 3 — Probability that $k$ independent samples are all bad.**

$$
P(\text{all } k \text{ samples bad}) = (1 - w^n)^k
$$

**Step 4 — Set failure probability equal to $1 - p$ and solve for $k$.**

$$
(1 - w^n)^k = 1 - p
$$

**Step 5 — Take logarithm of both sides.**

$$
k \log(1 - w^n) = \log(1 - p)
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $k$ explicitly.**

$$
k = \frac{\log(1 - p)}{\log(1 - w^n)}
$$

**Step 7 — Numerical example.** For homography ($n = 8$), $w = 0.4$, $p = 0.99$:

$$
k = \frac{\log(1 - 0.99)}{\log(1 - 0.4^8)} = \frac{\log(0.01)}{\log(1 - 0.000655)} \approx \frac{-4.605}{-0.000655} \approx \mathbf{7028}
$$

So approximately **7,028 iterations** are required — illustrating why RANSAC can be expensive for low inlier ratios.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Matching Methods

| Method | Modality | Robustness | Speed | Use Case | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| SIFT + NN | 2D image | Very High | Slow | Loop closure | Patent issues |
| SURF + NN | 2D image | High | Medium | Visual odometry | Approximation errors |
| ORB + NN | 2D image | Medium | Fast | Real-time VO | Less scale-robust |
| AKAZE | 2D image | High | Medium | Illumination changes | Newer, less tested |
| ICP | 3D point cloud | Medium | Medium | LiDAR SLAM | Local minima |
| NDT | 3D point cloud | High | Fast | Outdoor LiDAR | Sensitive to grid size |
| G-ICP | 3D point cloud | Very High | Slow | Accurate mapping | High compute |
| CSM (2D) | 2D scan | High | Medium | 2D LiDAR SLAM | Brute-force search |
| DBoW2 | Image -> BoW | High | Very Fast | Place recognition | Bag ambiguity |
| NetVLAD | Image -> vector | Very High | Medium | Visual place recognition | Needs GPU |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Generic Feature Matching Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Image Query] --> B[Feature Detector]
    A2[Input Image Reference] --> B2[Feature Detector]
    B --> C[Descriptor Extractor]
    B2 --> C2[Descriptor Extractor]
    C --> D[Descriptor Sets Q]
    C2 --> D2[Descriptor Sets R]
    D --> E[Nearest Neighbor Search]
    D2 --> E
    E --> F[Lowe Ratio Test]
    F --> G[RANSAC Geometric Verification]
    G --> H[Inlier Correspondences]
    H --> I[Pose Transformation Estimation]
    I --> J[Matched Pairs Output]
```

### 4.2 ICP Iteration Loop (Schematic Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Source Point Cloud P] --> A[Initialize R0, t0]
    A --> B[Apply Transform to P]
    B --> C[Find Correspondences in Q]
    C --> D[Compute Centroids]
    D --> E[Compute Covariance H]
    E --> F[SVD of H]
    F --> G[Update R and t]
    G --> H{Convergence Check}
    H -- No --> B
    H -- Yes --> Z[Output R final, t final]
```

### 4.3 Place Recognition via Bag-of-Words

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Img[Query Image] --> F[Extract Features]
    F --> Q[Quantize to Visual Words]
    Q --> V[Build BoW Vector]
    V --> DB[(Image Database BoW Vectors)]
    DB --> S[Score via tf-idf Similarity]
    S --> T[Top K Candidate Images]
    T --> G[Geometric Verification]
    G --> R[Loop Closure Confirmed]
```

### 4.4 Sensor-Modality Matching Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Perception_Inputs
    C[Monocular Camera]
    L[2D LiDAR]
    L3[3D LiDAR]
    IMU[IMU]
    GPS[GPS]
    end
    C --> FE[2D Feature Engine]
    L --> SM[2D Scan Matching]
    L3 --> PM[3D Point Cloud Matching]
    IMU --> OF[Odometry Fusion]
    GPS --> OF
    FE --> MAT[Matching Core]
    SM --> MAT
    PM --> MAT
    MAT --> LC[Loop Closure Detection]
    MAT --> VO[Visual Odometry Output]
    MAT --> LOC[Robot Localization Output]
    LC --> M[Global Map Update]
    VO --> M
    LOC --> M
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams above represent the **functional flow** of the matching sub-systems as they are typically integrated in modern SLAM stacks (e.g., ORB-SLAM3, LIO-SAM, RTAB-MAP). The "Matching Core" is the central abstraction that all modalities feed into.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Dec 2024]**
**Define feature matching in the context of robot navigation. List any two popular feature descriptors used for matching.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Feature matching is the process of establishing **correspondences** between salient regions (keypoints) detected in two different images or between an image and a map, in order to estimate the robot's motion or to recognize previously visited places.

Two popular feature descriptors:
1. **SIFT (Scale-Invariant Feature Transform)** — 128-D vector based on gradient histograms, robust to scale and rotation.
2. **ORB (Oriented FAST and Rotated BRIEF)** — 32-byte binary descriptor, computationally efficient for real-time robotics.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for listing & describing descriptors.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, May 2024]**
**What is Lowe's ratio test? Why is it used in feature matching?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Lowe's ratio test is a **filtering criterion** proposed by David Lowe to reject ambiguous descriptor matches. For each query descriptor $q$, it compares the distance to the nearest neighbor $d_1$ with the distance to the second-nearest neighbor $d_2$:

$$
\frac{d_1}{d_2} < \tau \quad \text{(typically } \tau = 0.75\text{)}
$$

A match is accepted only if the ratio is below the threshold. It is used to **discard false matches** in which the nearest neighbor is not significantly closer than the next-best alternative, which is a strong indicator of an unreliable correspondence.

*(2 marks for formula & explanation, 1 mark for the rationale.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Question (Internal Choice Provided)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]*

**(a)** Describe the complete pipeline of **feature-based image matching** in robot navigation. Include the role of detector, descriptor, matching strategy, and outlier rejection.
**[(7 Marks)]**

**(b)** With a suitable diagram, explain the working of the **Iterative Closest Point (ICP)** algorithm for 3D point cloud matching. Derive the update rule for the rotation matrix $R$ using Singular Value Decomposition (SVD).
**[(7 Marks)]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Pipeline of Feature-Based Image Matching:**

1. **Keypoint Detection** (2 marks):
   - Detect salient, repeatable points (corners, blobs) invariant to scale and rotation.
   - Examples: SIFT uses Difference-of-Gaussian extrema; ORB uses FAST corners with Harris score.

2. **Descriptor Computation** (2 marks):
   - Each keypoint is assigned a compact, distinctive descriptor (e.g., 128-D SIFT vector, 32-byte ORB binary string) that captures the local appearance in a way invariant to scale, rotation, and (partially) illumination.

3. **Descriptor Matching** (2 marks):
   - For every query descriptor, the nearest neighbor in the reference descriptor set is found using Euclidean distance (SIFT/SURF) or Hamming distance (ORB/BRIEF).
   - Efficient search is enabled by structures like KD-trees (FLANN).

4. **Outlier Rejection** (1 mark):
   - **Lowe's ratio test** removes ambiguous matches. **RANSAC** then fits a geometric model (homography, fundamental matrix) to discard remaining outliers.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Working of ICP:**

The ICP algorithm iteratively aligns a source point cloud $P$ to a target point cloud $Q$ by alternately computing correspondences and estimating the rigid transformation $(R, t)$.

**Derivation of the SVD-based update rule:**

Given correspondences $(p_i, q_i)$, the objective is:

$$
E(R, t) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \Vert R p_i + t - q_i \Vert^2
$$

**Step 1:** Compute centroids $\bar{p} = \frac{1}{N}\sum p_i$ and $\bar{q} = \frac{1}{N}\sum q_i$, and subtract to get $p_i' = p_i - \bar{p}$, $q_i' = q_i - \bar{q}$.

**Step 2:** Form the cross-covariance matrix:

$$
H = \sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i' q_i'^T
$$

**Step 3:** Compute SVD: $H = U \Sigma V^T$.

**Step 4:** Enforce proper rotation:

$$
R = V \cdot \text{diag}(1, 1, \det(VU^T)) \cdot U^T
$$

**Step 5:** Compute translation:

$$
t = \bar{q} - R \bar{p}
$$

**Iterate** until convergence (change in $E$ below a threshold, typically $10^{-6}$, or a maximum of ~50 iterations).

*Refer to the diagram in Section 4.2 for the iterative loop.*

*(3 marks for explanation, 4 marks for the derivation.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2023 / Model Paper 2024]*

**(a)** Explain the **Bag-of-Words (BoW)** approach for visual place recognition in robot navigation. How does it enable efficient loop closure detection in SLAM?
**[(7 Marks)]**

**(b)** With reference to geometric verification in feature matching, derive the **RANSAC iteration count** formula. Show with a numerical example the number of iterations required for a homography estimation with inlier ratio $w = 0.4$ and desired success probability $p = 0.99$.
**[(7 Marks)]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Bag-of-Words (BoW) for Place Recognition:**

The BoW approach treats an image as an **unordered collection of visual words** drawn from a pre-trained visual vocabulary.

**Steps:**
1. **Vocabulary construction**: A large set of feature descriptors (e.g., from SIFT/ORB) is clustered (typically using k-means) to produce a "visual vocabulary" of size $k$ (commonly $k = 10^5$ to $10^6$). Each cluster center is a "visual word."

2. **Image quantization**: Each detected feature in an image is mapped to its nearest visual word. The image is represented as a histogram over the $k$ words.

3. **tf-idf weighting**: Each word is weighted by its **term frequency** (how often it appears in the image) and **inverse document frequency** (how rare it is across the database). This emphasizes distinctive words.

4. **Database scoring**: To find a candidate loop closure, the BoW vector of the query image is compared with those in the database using cosine similarity or $L_2$ distance. Top-$K$ candidates are retrieved.

5. **Geometric verification**: A temporal consistency check and a RANSAC-based geometric verification confirm the loop closure.

**Why BoW enables efficient loop closure:**
- Direct descriptor matching is $O(N \cdot M)$ — prohibitive for large-scale SLAM.
- BoW lookup is $O(k)$ — near-constant time per query.
- It enables **inverse indexing**: given a visual word in the query, the database can instantly return all images containing that word.

*(3 marks for steps, 2 marks for tf-idf, 2 marks for loop closure efficiency.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Derivation of RANSAC iteration count:**

**Step 1:** A single random sample of $n$ correspondences is correct only if all $n$ are inliers. Since the inlier ratio is $w$, the probability of any single correspondence being an inlier is $w$. The probability of *all $n$* being inliers is:

$$
P(\text{sample correct}) = w^n
$$

**Step 2:** The probability that a sample is bad (has at least one outlier) is:

$$
P(\text{sample bad}) = 1 - w^n
$$

**Step 3:** If we draw $k$ independent samples, the probability that **all** of them are bad is:

$$
P(\text{all bad}) = (1 - w^n)^k
$$

**Step 4:** We want this failure probability to be at most $1 - p$:

$$
(1 - w^n)^k = 1 - p
$$

**Step 5:** Take logarithms:

$$
k \log(1 - w^n) = \log(1 - p)
$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $k$:

$$
k = \frac{\log(1 - p)}{\log(1 - w^n)}
$$

**Numerical Example:** For homography estimation, $n = 8$ correspondences are needed. Given $w = 0.4$ and $p = 0.99$:

$$
k = \frac{\log(1 - 0.99)}{\log(1 - 0.4^8)} = \frac{\log(0.01)}{\log(1 - 0.000655)} = \frac{-4.6052}{-0.0006552} \approx \mathbf{7028}
$$

Therefore, **approximately 7028 iterations** are required.

*(3 marks for derivation, 2 marks for substitution, 2 marks for final numeric answer.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing nearest neighbor distance metric**: Forgetting that SIFT/SURF use **Euclidean** distance while ORB/BRIEF use **Hamming** distance is a common 1-mark loss.
> 2. **Inverting the ratio test**: Writing $\frac{d_2}{d_1}$ instead of $\frac{d_1}{d_2}$ is a frequent error.
> 3. **Skipping the determinant correction in ICP**: Many students forget the $\det(VU^T)$ step and lose 1-2 marks.
> 4. **Not mentioning SVD in ICP derivation**: KTU examiners explicitly look for the SVD step in the rotation matrix derivation.
> 5. **Forgetting to state assumptions**: RANSAC requires the inlier ratio assumption; without it, the formula is incomplete.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Matching in Robot Navigation**

- **Matching = finding correspondences** between two data sets (image-image, image-map, scan-scan).
- **Three core stages**: Detection → Description → Matching.
- **SIFT** is the gold standard for accuracy; **ORB** is the gold standard for real-time robotics.
- **Lowe's ratio test** with threshold $\tau \approx 0.7\text{–}0.8$ is the most cited outlier filter.
- **Euclidean** distance for float descriptors; **Hamming** distance for binary descriptors.
- **RANSAC** is the workhorse of geometric verification — always cite the iteration formula:

$$
k = \frac{\log(1-p)}{\log(1-w^n)}
$$

- **ICP** aligns 3D point clouds via iterative SVD-based rigid-body estimation. Requires a good initial guess; converges to a local minimum.
- **Bag-of-Words (BoW)** with tf-idf weighting enables **near-constant-time** loop closure detection in large-scale SLAM.
- **NetVLAD / AP-GeM** are deep-learning-based place recognition methods offering greater robustness to illumination and viewpoint changes.
- **FLANN** (Fast Library for Approximate Nearest Neighbors) is the standard library for fast descriptor matching in C++.
- **Place recognition** is distinct from pure feature matching: it answers "have I been here?" rather than "what is the relative pose?"
- **Common failure modes**: perceptual aliasing, dynamic objects, viewpoint change, illumination change.
- **Modern SLAM systems** (ORB-SLAM3, VINS-Mono, LIO-SAM) all contain a dedicated matching module as a central sub-component.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT735 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Estimation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Estimation in Robot Navigation

**Estimation** in robot navigation is the computational process of inferring the internal state of a robot (such as its pose, position, velocity, map features, or environmental variables) from noisy, incomplete, and indirect sensor measurements and control inputs. Formally, it is the recursive application of probability theory to determine the posterior probability distribution over the robot's state space, commonly called the **belief** or **posterior belief**.

Mathematically, the estimation problem seeks to compute:

$$
bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})
$$

where:
- $x_t$ is the state of the robot at time $t$
- $z_{1:t}$ denotes the sequence of all measurements up to time $t$
- $u_{1:t}$ denotes the sequence of all control actions up to time $t$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** Estimation is foundational to localization, SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping), and tracking. The Bayesian estimation framework is the unifying principle from which Kalman, Extended Kalman, and Particle filters are derived.

## 1.2 Types of Estimation Problems in Robotics

| Estimation Type | State Being Estimated | Common Algorithms |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Localization** | Robot pose $(x, y, \theta)$ | Markov Localization, EKF, Particle Filter |
| **Tracking** | Moving object positions | Kalman Filter, JPDA |
| **SLAM** | Pose + Map | EKF-SLAM, FastSLAM, Graph-SLAM |
| **Odometry** | Incremental motion | Wheel encoder models, IMU integration |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The Foggy Night Driver

Imagine you are driving through a dense fog at night. You cannot see the road directly, but you have:
- A **speedometer** (control input $u_t$) telling you how fast you are moving
- A faint **GPS signal** that updates every second (measurement $z_t$), but with a noise margin of about **5 to 10 meters**
- A **memory** of where you started (initial belief $bel(x_0)$)

**Estimation is the mental process of combining all three uncertain pieces of information to maintain a continuously updated guess of where you are on the map.** The Bayes Filter formalizes this intuition:
- **Prediction** ("where should I be now given I was here and I moved at this speed?")
- **Correction** ("the GPS says I'm near that river — adjust my belief to match")

The **uncertainty** in your belief shrinks when measurements agree with predictions and grows when they disagree or are absent.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Distinction:** Estimation is fundamentally about *uncertainty management*. The robot never knows its state exactly; it only maintains a probability distribution representing its degree of belief.

## 1.4 The Bayesian Estimation Philosophy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Foundational Principle:** All major estimation algorithms in robotics are specific *implementations* of the recursive Bayes Filter. The differences between Kalman, EKF, and Particle Filters lie in how they *represent* the belief distribution:
> - **Kalman Filter:** Gaussian (unimodal, parametric)
> - **EKF:** Single Gaussian over nonlinear models (via linearization)
> - **Particle Filter:** Set of weighted samples (non-parametric, multimodal)
> - **Histogram Filter:** Discrete grid decomposition

## 1.5 Why Estimation is Hard

- **Sensor noise** introduces measurement uncertainty
- **Actuator noise** introduces motion uncertainty
- **Perceptual aliasing** — different places may look identical
- **Computational complexity** — state spaces are continuous and high-dimensional
- **Nonlinearity** — real robots rarely obey linear-Gaussian assumptions

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Bayes Filter — The Grand Unifying Framework

The Bayes Filter is a recursive algorithm that maintains a probability distribution over the state space, updated as new measurements and controls arrive. It consists of two alternating phases executed at every time step.

### 2.1.1 Operational Logic Steps

**Step 1 — Initialization:**
Initialize the belief $bel(x_0)$ based on prior knowledge (e.g., known starting position, or a uniform prior if completely unknown).

**Step 2 — Prediction (Control Update):**
Apply the motion model to propagate the belief forward. For each possible current state $x_t$, sum (integrate) over all previous states $x_{t-1}$, weighted by the probability of having moved from $x_{t-1}$ to $x_t$ under control $u_t$, multiplied by the previous belief.

**Step 3 — Correction (Measurement Update):**
Multiply the predicted belief by the likelihood $p(z_t \mid x_t)$ of observing measurement $z_t$ from state $x_t$. Normalize the result using the constant $\eta$ to obtain a valid probability distribution.

**Step 4 — Recursion:**
Set the current belief as the new prior, increment $t$, and return to Step 2.

### 2.1.2 Why and How — The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why prediction first?** Because between time steps, the robot moves and the world may change. Without prediction, we ignore the dynamical model.
- **Why multiply by likelihood?** Bayes' rule states $p(x \mid z) \propto p(z \mid x) \cdot p(x)$. The prior $p(x)$ is the predicted belief; the likelihood re-weights it based on evidence.
- **Why normalize?** Without normalization, the result may not integrate to 1, breaking the probability interpretation.
- **How does it scale?** The integral in the prediction step is generally intractable for continuous, nonlinear systems — hence the need for approximations (Kalman, EKF, Particle Filters).

## 2.2 The Kalman Filter — Linear Gaussian Estimation

The Kalman Filter is the **optimal** (minimum mean squared error) estimator when:
- The motion model is linear
- The measurement model is linear
- All noise is zero-mean Gaussian

### 2.2.1 Linear Motion and Measurement Models

$$
x_t = A_t x_{t-1} + B_t u_t + \varepsilon_t
$$

$$
z_t = C_t x_t + \delta_t
$$

where $\varepsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$ and $\delta_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t)$.

### 2.2.2 Operational Steps

**Prediction Phase:**
1. Propagate the state mean using the deterministic motion model
2. Propagate and grow the covariance using the system matrix and motion noise

**Update Phase:**
1. Compute the Kalman Gain — it determines how much the measurement influences the state
2. Correct the state using the innovation (measurement residual)
3. Update the covariance to reflect reduced uncertainty

## 2.3 The Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)

The EKF handles **nonlinear** systems by **linearizing** them at the current state estimate using first-order Taylor series expansion (Jacobians).

### 2.3.1 Nonlinear Models

$$
x_t = g(u_t, x_{t-1}) + \varepsilon_t
$$

$$
z_t = h(x_t) + \delta_t
$$

### 2.3.2 Linearization via Jacobians

$$
G_t = \frac{\partial g(u_t, x_{t-1})}{\partial x_{t-1}} \bigg|_{x_{t-1}=\mu_{t-1}}
$$

$$
H_t = \frac{\partial h(x_t)}{\partial x_t} \bigg|_{x_t = \bar{\mu}_t}
$$

These Jacobians replace $A_t$ and $C_t$ in the Kalman equations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality:** EKF is the workhorse of mobile robotics — used in EKF-SLAM, EKF localization, GPS-INS fusion. It assumes "mild" nonlinearity; for highly nonlinear systems, particle filters or unscented Kalman filters (UKF) are preferred.

## 2.4 The Particle Filter (Monte Carlo Localization)

The Particle Filter represents the belief by a set of $M$ weighted samples (particles), each representing a hypothesis about the true state.

### 2.4.1 Operational Steps

1. **Sampling:** Propagate each particle through the motion model with noise
2. **Importance Weighting:** Assign weight $w_t^{(i)} \propto p(z_t \mid x_t^{(i)})$ to each particle
3. **Resampling:** Draw $M$ particles with replacement, with probability proportional to their weights — this concentrates particles in high-likelihood regions

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Belief | $bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ | State $x_t \in \mathbb{R}^n$ |
| 2 | Bayes Filter (Prediction) | $\overline{bel}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$ | $u_t$ control |
| 3 | Bayes Filter (Update) | $bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \, \overline{bel}(x_t)$ | $z_t$ measurement, $\eta$ normalizer |
| 4 | Kalman State Pred. | $\bar{\mu}_t = A_t \mu_{t-1} + B_t u_t$ | $\mu \in \mathbb{R}^n$ |
| 5 | Kalman Cov. Pred. | $\bar{\Sigma}_t = A_t \Sigma_{t-1} A_t^T + R_t$ | $\Sigma \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ |
| 6 | Kalman Gain | $K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T (C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}$ | $K_t \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$ |
| 7 | Kalman State Update | $\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t(z_t - C_t \bar{\mu}_t)$ | Innovation $z_t - C_t\bar{\mu}_t$ |
| 8 | Kalman Cov. Update | $\Sigma_t = (I - K_t C_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t$ | Posterior covariance |
| 9 | EKF Pred. State | $\bar{\mu}_t = g(u_t, \mu_{t-1})$ | $g(\cdot)$ nonlinear motion |
| 10 | EKF Pred. Cov. | $\bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \Sigma_{t-1} G_t^T + R_t$ | $G_t = \partial g / \partial x$ |
| 11 | EKF Update | $\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t(z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t))$ | $h(\cdot)$ nonlinear measurement |
| 12 | Particle Weight | $w_t^{(i)} \propto w_{t-1}^{(i)} \, p(z_t \mid x_t^{(i)})$ | Importance weight |
| 13 | Effective Sample Size | $N_{\text{eff}} = \left(\sum_i (w_t^{(i)})^2\right)^{-1}$ | Resample if $N_{\text{eff}} < N_{\text{thresh}}$ |
| 14 | Motion Noise Cov. | $R_t = E[\varepsilon_t \varepsilon_t^T]$ | $\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R)$ |
| 15 | Measurement Noise Cov. | $Q_t = E[\delta_t \delta_t^T]$ | $\delta \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q)$ |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Applications of Estimation Algorithms:**
> - **Autonomous Vehicles (Waymo, Tesla):** EKF for sensor fusion (LiDAR + Camera + GPS + IMU)
> - **Drones (DJI):** Extended Kalman Filter for attitude and altitude estimation
> - **Mars Rovers (NASA Curiosity, Perseverance):** Particle Filter for visual terrain localization
> - **Smartphones:** Kalman Filter for indoor pedestrian localization
> - **Warehouse Robots (Amazon Kiva):** Particle Filter for multi-hypothesis localization in dynamic environments

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Bayes Filter from First Principles

We begin with the desired posterior and decompose it using the definition of conditional probability, the Markov assumption, and Bayes' rule.

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — Target Distribution

$$
bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})
$$

### 3.1.2 Expand the Conditioning Set

By the definition of conditional probability and using the Markov property (current state depends only on previous state, not the entire history):

$$
p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = p(x_t \mid z_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})
$$

By Bayes' rule (treating $z_t$ as the evidence):

$$
p(x_t \mid z_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \, p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}
$$

### 3.1.3 Apply Markov Assumption

The measurement $z_t$ depends only on the current state $x_t$ (sensing model). Hence:

$$
p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(z_t \mid x_t)
$$

Substituting:

$$
bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \, p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})
$$

where the normalization constant is:

$$
\eta = \frac{1}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}
$$

### 3.1.4 Predict $p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$ via Total Probability

The current state $x_t$ depends on the previous state $x_{t-1}$ via the control $u_t$. Marginalize over $x_{t-1}$:

$$
p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t, z_{1:t-1}) \, p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \, dx_{t-1}
$$

By the Markov assumption on the motion model:

$$
p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t, z_{1:t-1}) = p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)
$$

### 3.1.5 Final Bayes Filter Recursion

$$
bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}
$$

This is the **fundamental recursive equation of Bayesian estimation**. The integral term is the prediction; multiplication by the likelihood is the update.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Kalman Filter from Bayes Filter under Gaussianity

### 3.2.1 Assumption Statement

Assume the motion and measurement models are linear:

$$
x_t = A_t x_{t-1} + B_t u_t + \varepsilon_t, \quad \varepsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)
$$

$$
z_t = C_t x_t + \delta_t, \quad \delta_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t)
$$

Assume the prior belief is Gaussian:

$$
bel(x_{t-1}) = \mathcal{N}(x_{t-1}; \mu_{t-1}, \Sigma_{t-1})
$$

### 3.2.2 Derivation of the Prediction Step

Compute the predicted belief $\overline{bel}(x_t)$ using the total probability theorem with a linear-Gaussian motion model. The convolution of two Gaussians is a Gaussian:

$$
\overline{bel}(x_t) = \mathcal{N}(x_t; \bar{\mu}_t, \bar{\Sigma}_t)
$$

The mean is propagated linearly:

$$
\bar{\mu}_t = A_t \mu_{t-1} + B_t u_t
$$

For the covariance, applying the linear transformation property $\text{Cov}(Ax) = A \text{Cov}(x) A^T$ and adding the motion noise:

$$
\bar{\Sigma}_t = A_t \Sigma_{t-1} A_t^T + R_t
$$

### 3.2.3 Derivation of the Update Step

The joint distribution of $(x_t, z_t)$ is Gaussian since both are linear in $x_t$ and noise. The conditional distribution of a Gaussian is also Gaussian:

$$
p(x_t \mid z_t) = \mathcal{N}(x_t; \mu_t, \Sigma_t)
$$

Apply the standard Gaussian conditioning formula. Define the cross-covariance:

$$
\Sigma_{xz} = \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T
$$

Define the innovation covariance:

$$
\Sigma_{zz} = C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + Q_t
$$

The posterior mean (using the standard result for conditional Gaussian):

$$
\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + \Sigma_{xz} \Sigma_{zz}^{-1} (z_t - C_t \bar{\mu}_t)
$$

The posterior covariance:

$$
\Sigma_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t - \Sigma_{xz} \Sigma_{zz}^{-1} C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t
$$

### 3.2.4 Definition of Kalman Gain

Define the **Kalman Gain** to compactify the notation:

$$
K_t = \Sigma_{xz} \Sigma_{zz}^{-1} = \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T (C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}
$$

Then the update equations become:

$$
\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - C_t \bar{\mu}_t)
$$

$$
\Sigma_t = (I - K_t C_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t
$$

The term $(z_t - C_t \bar{\mu}_t)$ is called the **innovation** or **measurement residual** — it represents the surprise, the difference between what was predicted and what was actually measured.

### 3.2.5 Worked Numerical Example — 1D Position Tracking

A robot moves in 1D. Initial state: $\mu_0 = 0$, $\Sigma_0 = 1$. Motion model: $A = 1$, $B = 1$, control $u_1 = 1$, $R = 0.5$. Measurement model: $C = 1$, measurement $z_1 = 1.8$, $Q = 1$.

**Prediction:**

$$
\bar{\mu}_1 = (1)(0) + (1)(1) = 1
$$

$$
\bar{\Sigma}_1 = (1)(1)(1) + 0.5 = 1.5
$$

**Kalman Gain:**

$$
K_1 = \frac{1.5 \cdot 1}{(1)(1.5)(1) + 1} = \frac{1.5}{2.5} = 0.6
$$

**Update:**

$$
\mu_1 = 1 + 0.6 \times (1.8 - 1 \times 1) = 1 + 0.48 = 1.48
$$

$$
\Sigma_1 = (1 - 0.6 \times 1) \times 1.5 = 0.4 \times 1.5 = 0.6
$$

The belief shifted from 1 (predicted) to 1.48 (corrected), with reduced uncertainty from 1.5 to 0.6.

## 3.3 Derivation of EKF — Linearization Procedure

### 3.3.1 The Linearization Approximation

Consider a nonlinear motion model $x_t = g(u_t, x_{t-1}) + \varepsilon_t$. We expand $g$ as a first-order Taylor series about $\mu_{t-1}$:

$$
g(u_t, x_{t-1}) \approx g(u_t, \mu_{t-1}) + G_t (x_{t-1} - \mu_{t-1})
$$

where $G_t$ is the Jacobian of $g$ with respect to $x_{t-1}$ evaluated at $\mu_{t-1}$:

$$
G_t = \frac{\partial g}{\partial x} \bigg|_{x = \mu_{t-1}}
$$

### 3.3.2 Plug Back into the Kalman Framework

With this linearization, the EKF prediction step is:

$$
\bar{\mu}_t = g(u_t, \mu_{t-1})
$$

$$
\bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \Sigma_{t-1} G_t^T + R_t
$$

Similarly, for the measurement update, the measurement model $z_t = h(x_t) + \delta_t$ is linearized about $\bar{\mu}_t$:

$$
H_t = \frac{\partial h}{\partial x} \bigg|_{x = \bar{\mu}_t}
$$

The EKF update step becomes:

$$
K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}
$$

$$
\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t))
$$

$$
\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** The EKF is a *first-order* approximation. When the nonlinearity is severe (e.g., the robot makes a 90° turn, or measurements wrap around angles), the linearization error can cause filter divergence. Use UKF or particle filters in such cases.

## 3.4 Particle Filter Derivation Outline

### 3.4.1 Importance Sampling Principle

We seek samples from $p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ but cannot sample directly. Instead, we sample from a **proposal distribution** $\pi(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ and assign importance weights:

$$
w_t^{(i)} = \frac{p(x_t^{(i)} \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})}{\pi(x_t^{(i)} \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})}
$$

Choosing the proposal as the motion model $\pi = p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}^{(i)}, u_t)$ (common in robotics), the weight recursion simplifies to:

$$
w_t^{(i)} \propto w_{t-1}^{(i)} \, p(z_t \mid x_t^{(i)})
$$

This is the **SIR (Sampling Importance Resampling)** Particle Filter.

### 3.4.2 Resampling Justification

Without resampling, after a few steps, only a few particles carry non-negligible weight (the *particle deprivation* problem). Resampling duplicates high-weight particles and discards low-weight ones, maintaining a healthy particle population.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bayes Filter — Recursive Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start at time t] --> B[Receive control input u_t]
    B --> C[Receive measurement z_t]
    C --> D[Prediction Step: Compute predicted belief]
    D --> E[Correction Step: Multiply by likelihood]
    E --> F[Normalize to obtain posterior belief]
    F --> G[Output bel x_t]
    G --> H[Increment t to t plus 1]
    H --> A
```

## 4.2 Kalman Filter — Prediction and Update Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Previous State mu_tminus1 Sigma_tminus1] --> B[Prediction Phase]
    B --> C[Predicted Mean baru mu_t]
    B --> D[Predicted Covariance baru Sigma_t]
    C --> E[Update Phase]
    D --> E
    F[Measurement z_t] --> E
    E --> G[Compute Kalman Gain K_t]
    G --> H[Innovation z_t minus C baru mu_t]
    H --> I[Corrected State mu_t]
    G --> J[Corrected Covariance Sigma_t]
    I --> K[Output Belief]
    J --> K
```

## 4.3 Extended Kalman Filter — Linearization Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Nonlinear System] --> B[Define Motion Model g and Measurement Model h]
    B --> C[Compute Jacobian G_t of g]
    B --> D[Compute Jacobian H_t of h]
    C --> E[Linearize around mu_tminus1]
    D --> F[Linearize around baru mu_t]
    E --> G[Apply Kalman Prediction with G_t]
    F --> H[Apply Kalman Update with H_t]
    G --> I[EKF Estimate mu_t Sigma_t]
    H --> I
```

## 4.4 Particle Filter — Sample-Based Estimation Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize M Particles] --> B[Receive Control u_t]
    B --> C[Sampling: Propagate Particles via Motion Model]
    C --> D[Receive Measurement z_t]
    D --> E[Importance Weighting: Compute p z_t given x_t for each particle]
    E --> F[Normalize Weights]
    F --> G{Resampling Triggered?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Resample Particles]
    G -->|No| I[Keep Current Particles]
    H --> J[Updated Particle Set]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Output Belief as Weighted Samples]
    K --> L[Increment t]
    L --> B
```

## 4.5 Filter Selection Decision Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Choose an Estimator] --> B{Is the system linear Gaussian?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Use Kalman Filter]
    B -->|No| D{Is nonlinearity mild?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Use Extended Kalman Filter EKF]
    D -->|No| F{Can the belief be multimodal?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Use Particle Filter]
    F -->|No| H[Use Unscented Kalman Filter UKF]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the belief in Bayesian estimation and explain its role in robot localization.**

**Model Answer:**
Belief $bel(x_t)$ is the posterior probability distribution over the robot's state $x_t$ given all measurements $z_{1:t}$ and controls $u_{1:t}$ up to time $t$. It is mathematically defined as:

$$
bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})
$$

In robot localization, the belief quantifies the robot's internal "knowledge" of where it is located within a map or environment. It plays a central role because the robot never has direct access to its true state — it must reason under uncertainty caused by noisy actuators and sensors. The belief is recursively updated using the Bayes Filter, alternating between prediction (using the motion model) and correction (using the measurement model). It enables decision-making under uncertainty, which is fundamental to autonomous navigation. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the Kalman Gain, and what does it represent intuitively?**

**Model Answer:**
The Kalman Gain $K_t$ is a matrix that weights the contribution of the measurement innovation relative to the prediction during the update step of the Kalman Filter:

$$
K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T (C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}
$$

Intuitively, it represents the optimal balance between trusting the prediction and trusting the measurement. When measurement noise $Q_t$ is large (sensor unreliable), $K_t$ becomes small, and the filter relies more on the prediction. Conversely, when prediction uncertainty $\bar{\Sigma}_t$ is large (motion model unreliable), $K_t$ becomes large, and the filter relies more on the measurement. This automatic adaptation is what makes the Kalman Filter optimal in the minimum mean squared error sense for linear Gaussian systems. **(3 marks)**

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — Choice 1

**(a)** Derive the Bayes Filter recursion equation from the definition of conditional probability, clearly stating all assumptions used. Explain the physical meaning of each term in the final equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a 1D robot with motion model $x_t = x_{t-1} + u_t + \varepsilon_t$ and measurement model $z_t = x_t + \delta_t$, where $\varepsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 0.2)$ and $\delta_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 0.3)$. Initial belief: $\mu_0 = 0$, $\Sigma_0 = 0.5$. At time $t = 1$: control $u_1 = 2$, measurement $z_1 = 2.5$. Perform one full Kalman Filter iteration (prediction + update). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Step 1 — Target:** Compute $bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$.

**Step 2 — Bayes' Rule application:**

$$
bel(x_t) = \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \, p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}
$$

**Step 3 — Markov assumption on measurement:**

$$
p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(z_t \mid x_t)
$$

Substituting:

$$
bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \, p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})
$$

where $\eta = 1 / p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$ is the normalizer.

**Step 4 — Total probability (marginalization) for the prediction term:**

$$
p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \, p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \, dx_{t-1}
$$

**Step 5 — Recognition that** $p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = bel(x_{t-1})$ (by definition).

**Step 6 — Final Bayes Filter recursion:**

$$
bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}
$$

**Physical meaning of each term:**
- $bel(x_{t-1})$: prior belief from previous time step
- $p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$: motion model (probability of reaching $x_t$ from $x_{t-1}$ under control $u_t$)
