The 2 m discrepancy is large relative to $\sigma_s = 0.5$ m, so the absolute weight is small — but the relative ordering of weights is what matters for resampling. Particles nearer the landmark (e.g., at 40.1 m) would dominate the resampling step and pull the estimate toward 40 m. This shows how the **likelihood function** sharpens the posterior around true landmark positions.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing RBL with map-based MCL:** Students often write "the robot uses an occupancy grid map" — that is generic localization, not RBL. RBL **must** mention the 1-D polyline / route-constraint projection.
> 2. **Forgetting the normalizer $\eta$:** Writing the Bayes equation without $\eta$ costs a full mark on derivations.
> 3. **Using `|` (vertical bar) for conditional probability inside LaTeX equations** can confuse readers; always use `\mid` or `\vert` per the KTU LaTeX style guide.
> 4. **Mixing up prediction and update steps:** Prediction uses motion model *before* sensor arrives; update uses likelihood *after* sensor arrives. Writing them in reverse order loses 2–3 marks.
> 5. **In numerical problems,** forgetting to state the **units** of $\sigma_s$ and the resulting weight — examiners explicitly allocate 0.5 mark for unit correctness.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **RBL = Localization constrained to a known route polyline.**
- Belief is **1-D** (arc-length $s$), not 2-D — this is the key conceptual difference from generic MCL.
- The defining step is **route projection** (perpendicular snapping to polyline).
- The **Bayes update equation** is $bel(\mathbf{x}_t) = \eta \, p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t) \int p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t)\, bel(\mathbf{x}_{t-1})\, d\mathbf{x}_{t-1}$.
- Particle weights come from **measurement likelihood** (Gaussian over innovation).
- Resampling is triggered when $N_{\text{eff}}$ falls below a threshold (typically $N/2$).
- Final pose = **weighted mean (EAP)** or **MAP** of the particle cloud.
- Typical particle count: **200 – 2000** (vs 5000+ in free-space MCL).
- RBL thrives in **structured, semi-static environments** (highways, warehouses, rails).
- RBL fails when: (i) HD map is outdated, (ii) route geometry changes (construction), (iii) sensors cannot detect landmarks (heavy rain/snow for cameras).
- Industrial relevance: **Waymo, Cruise, Tesla, Amazon Kiva, Locus, AGVs in mining/metros**.
- Computational savings over free-space MCL: roughly an **order of magnitude** in both memory and time.
- KLD-sampling can be used to **adaptively** choose particle count based on localization uncertainty.
- Sensor model is most commonly a **beam-end (LIDAR) likelihood** or **feature-based** (camera/landmark) likelihood.
- Route maps are stored as **polylines** (sequence of (x, y) waypoints) plus a **landmark database**.
- Initial global localization is achieved by **uniform particle distribution** along the route polyline.
- Pose-tracking mode is entered automatically once the particle cloud variance drops below a threshold (e.g., $\sigma_s < 1$ m).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Autonomous Map Building - Stochastic Map Technique
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT735 - ROBOT NAVIGATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Autonomous Map Building - Stochastic Map Technique ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Autonomous Map Building — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Autonomous Map Building** is the process by which a mobile robot, operating without any a-priori knowledge of its workspace, incrementally constructs a spatial representation of its environment using onboard exteroceptive sensors (LiDAR, sonar, cameras) while simultaneously estimating its own pose. The constructed map must be geometrically consistent, topologically connected, and must explicitly account for the uncertainties inherent in both sensor measurements and dead-reckoning. In the KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this is the perception-to-representation pipeline that transforms raw sensor streams into a navigable internal model, forming the prerequisite for path planning, localization, and control. ### 1.2 Stochastic Map Technique — Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > A **Stochastic Map** is a probabilistic spatial representation in which the locations of the robot, the landmarks, and the geometric features of the environment are modeled as random variables governed by a joint multivariate Gaussian distribution. The map is described entirely by a **state vector** (mean estimate) and a **covariance matrix** (uncertainty description). Every estimate is paired with a measurable confidence bound. The Stochastic Map Technique is the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) based architectural framework** for solving the SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping) problem. It was formalized by Smith, Self, and Cheeseman (1988) and remains a foundational reference in KTU syllabus modules on probabilistic robotics. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Dark-Room Explorer Imagine you are dropped inside a vast, pitch-dark, unfamiliar warehouse carrying a dim flashlight and a pedometer. - Each flash of the flashlight reveals a pillar, but the distance you read is **fuzzy** — sometimes 4.9 m, sometimes 5.1 m. - Your pedometer tells you how far you walked, but wheels slip, so the reading has drift. - You mentally **pin a thumbtack** on a notepad for every pillar you see, and you write a small **"fuzzy radius"** around each tack to remind yourself: *"I am not 100% sure of this position."* - When you later see the same pillar from a different angle, the two fuzzy circles overlap. Using geometry, you **tighten** the fuzziness of both your own position and the pillar's position simultaneously. The notepad is the **stochastic map**. The thumbtacks are the **mean estimates** ($\hat{x}$). The fuzzy radii are the **covariance ellipses** ($P$). The tightening upon re-observation is the **Kalman update step**. > [!TIP] > The core philosophical shift: instead of asking *"Where is the pillar?"* (a deterministic question), we ask *"What is the probability distribution over the pillar's location given everything I have seen so far?"* — this is the stochastic (probabilistic) paradigm. ### 1.4 Key Terminology Anchors - **Feature / Landmark** — A distinguishable geometric entity (corner, edge, reflector) in the environment used as a spatial reference. - **State Vector** — The column vector $\mathbf{x}(k)$ containing the robot pose and all landmark locations in a single mathematical object. - **Covariance Matrix** — The symmetric positive-semidefinite matrix $P(k)$ encoding the uncertainties and cross-correlations of every state component. - **Prediction Step** — Using the motion model to propagate the state forward in time. - **Update Step (Correction)** — Using a sensor observation to refine the predicted state. - **Data Association** — The decision logic that decides *which* previously-mapped landmark corresponds to the *currently-observed* feature. - **Loop Closure** — Recognition that the robot has revisited a previously-mapped area, triggering a global consistency correction. - **Cross-Correlation** — The off-diagonal terms in $P$ that capture the fact that errors in the robot pose and landmark positions are statistically linked. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Stochastic Map — Architectural Philosophy The stochastic map does not store independent, isolated landmark positions. It stores one **giant state vector** and one **giant covariance matrix** that captures the joint distribution of *every* spatial quantity simultaneously. This joint representation is the single most important conceptual leap in the entire topic. $$ \mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} x_v \\ y_v \\ \theta_v \\ x_1 \\ y_1 \\ \vdots \\ x_n \\ y_n \end{bmatrix}, \qquad P = E\!\left[(\mathbf{x} - \hat{\mathbf{x}})(\mathbf{x} - \hat{\mathbf{x}})^T\right] $$ where subscripts $v$ denote the **vehicle** (robot) pose and subscripts $1 \ldots n$ denote the $n$ landmarks. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why a single joint state?** Because the robot's pose uncertainty and the landmark position uncertainties are *correlated*. If the robot's estimate of its own position drifts by $\delta x$, then the apparent position of every newly-observed landmark also drifts by $\delta x$. The off-diagonal blocks of $P$ capture this coupling. Ignoring it is the most common mistake students make in numerical problems. ### 2.2 The Two-Step Recursive Loop The stochastic map is updated using the canonical **predict–observe–update** recursion of the Extended Kalman Filter. #### STEP 1 — Motion (Prediction) Step The robot issues a control $\mathbf{u}(k)$ (e.g., wheel encoder odometry). The new pose is predicted using the nonlinear kinematic model $f(\cdot)$: $$ \hat{\mathbf{x}}^{-}(k) = f\bigl(\hat{\mathbf{x}}^{+}(k-1),\, \mathbf{u}(k)\bigr) $$ The covariance grows according to the linearized dynamics plus the process noise covariance $Q$: $$ P^{-}(k) = \nabla F_x\, P^{+}(k-1)\, \nabla F_x^T \;+\; \nabla F_u\, Q(k)\, \nabla F_u^T $$ Here $\nabla F_x$ and $\nabla F_u$ are the **Jacobians** of the motion model evaluated at the current estimate. #### STEP 2 — Observation (Update) Step A sensor returns measurement $\mathbf{z}(k)$ to a known landmark $i$. The predicted measurement is: $$ \hat{\mathbf{z}}(k) = h\bigl(\hat{\mathbf{x}}^{-}(k)\bigr) $$ The **innovation** (measurement residual) is the mismatch: $$ \boldsymbol{\nu}(k) = \mathbf{z}(k) - \hat{\mathbf{z}}(k) $$ The innovation covariance is: $$ S(k) = \nabla H_x\, P^{-}(k)\, \nabla H_x^T \;+\; R(k) $$ where $R(k)$ is the sensor noise covariance and $\nabla H_x$ is the Jacobian of the measurement model. The **Kalman gain** is computed as: $$ W(k) = P^{-}(k)\, \nabla H_x^T\, S^{-1}(k) $$ The state and covariance are then corrected: $$ \hat{\mathbf{x}}^{+}(k) = \hat{\mathbf{x}}^{-}(k) + W(k)\, \boldsymbol{\nu}(k) $$ $$ P^{+}(k) = P^{-}(k) - W(k)\, S(k)\, W(k)^T = \bigl(I - W(k)\, \nabla H_x\bigr)\, P^{-}(k) $$ ### 2.3 The Measurement Model — Range and Bearing For a range-bearing sensor observing landmark $i$ from the robot: $$ \mathbf{z} = \begin{bmatrix} r \\ \phi \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{(x_i - x_v)^2 + (y_i - y_v)^2} \\ \text{atan2}(y_i - y_v,\, x_i - x_v) - \theta_v \end{bmatrix} + \mathbf{w} $$ ### 2.4 The Motion Model — 2D Odometry For a unicycle robot moving with translation $\Delta s$ and rotation $\Delta \theta$: $$ x_v(k) = x_v(k-1) + \Delta s \cos\bigl(\theta_v(k-1) + \Delta\theta\bigr) $$ $$ y_v(k) = y_v(k-1) + \Delta s \sin\bigl(\theta_v(k-1) + \Delta\theta\bigr) $$ $$ \theta_v(k) = \theta_v(k-1) + \Delta\theta $$ ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Equation / Meaning** | **Units** | |---|---|---|---| | Joint State Vector | $\mathbf{x}(k)$ | $[\,x_v, y_v, \theta_v, x_1, y_1, \ldots, x_n, y_n\,]^T$ | m, m, rad, m, m, … | | Joint Covariance | $P(k)$ | $E[(\mathbf{x}-\hat{\mathbf{x}})(\mathbf{x}-\hat{\mathbf{x}})^T]$ | Mixed | | Predicted State | $\hat{\mathbf{x}}^{-}(k)$ | $f(\hat{\mathbf{x}}^{+}(k-1), \mathbf{u}(k))$ | — | | Predicted Covariance | $P^{-}(k)$ | $\nabla F_x P^{+} \nabla F_x^T + \nabla F_u Q \nabla F_u^T$ | Mixed | | Innovation | $\boldsymbol{\nu}(k)$ | $\mathbf{z}(k) - h(\hat{\mathbf{x}}^{-}(k))$ | Sensor units | | Innovation Covariance | $S(k)$ | $\nabla H_x P^{-} \nabla H_x^T + R$ | Sensor units² | | Kalman Gain | $W(k)$ | $P^{-} \nabla H_x^T S^{-1}$ | Dimensionless | | Updated State | $\hat{\mathbf{x}}^{+}(k)$ | $\hat{\mathbf{x}}^{-} + W \boldsymbol{\nu}$ | — | | Updated Covariance | $P^{+}(k)$ | $(I - W \nabla H_x) P^{-}$ | Mixed | | Range Observation | $r$ | $\sqrt{(\Delta x)^2 + (\Delta y)^2}$ | m | | Bearing Observation | $\phi$ | $\text{atan2}(\Delta y, \Delta x) - \theta_v$ | rad | | Process Noise Cov. | $Q(k)$ | Odometry disturbance model | (m/s)², (rad/s)² | | Sensor Noise Cov. | $R(k)$ | Range-bearing sensor model | m², rad² | > [!TIP] > **Engineering Utility:** Stochastic maps are foundational in autonomous vehicles (Google/Waymo, Mobileye), underwater AUVs (Sonar SLAM), planetary rovers (NASA Mars rovers), warehouse AMRs (Amazon Kiva), and AR/VR simultaneous tracking and mapping. The EKF-based stochastic formulation remains the analytical workhorse against which all modern particle-filter and graph-based SLAM methods are benchmarked. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Covariance Propagation Under a Linear Motion Model We begin with the linear case to build intuition, then extend to the nonlinear EKF case. **Assumption:** At time $k-1$ the state is Gaussian with mean $\hat{\mathbf{x}}^{+}(k-1)$ and covariance $P^{+}(k-1)$. The motion is: $$ \mathbf{x}(k) = F\, \mathbf{x}(k-1) + G\, \mathbf{u}(k) + \mathbf{v}(k) $$ where $\mathbf{v}(k) \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q)$ is the additive process noise, independent of the state. **Step 1 — Take expectation of both sides:** $$ E[\mathbf{x}(k)] = F\, E[\mathbf{x}(k-1)] + G\, \mathbf{u}(k) + E[\mathbf{v}(k)] $$ Since $E[\mathbf{v}(k)] = 0$ and $E[\mathbf{x}(k-1)] = \hat{\mathbf{x}}^{+}(k-1)$: $$ \hat{\mathbf{x}}^{-}(k) = F\, \hat{\mathbf{x}}^{+}(k-1) + G\, \mathbf{u}(k) $$ **Step 2 — Define the prediction error:** $$ \tilde{\mathbf{x}}^{-}(k) \;=\; \mathbf{x}(k) - \hat{\mathbf{x}}^{-}(k) $$ **Step 3 — Substitute and expand:** $$ \tilde{\mathbf{x}}^{-}(k) = F\, \mathbf{x}(k-1) + G\, \mathbf{u}(k) + \mathbf{v}(k) - F\, \hat{\mathbf{x}}^{+}(k-1) - G\, \mathbf{u}(k) $$ The control terms cancel exactly: $$ \tilde{\mathbf{x}}^{-}(k) = F\bigl(\mathbf{x}(k-1) - \hat{\mathbf{x}}^{+}(k-1)\bigr) + \mathbf{v}(k) = F\, \tilde{\mathbf{x}}^{+}(k-1) + \mathbf{v}(k) $$ **Step 4 — Form the outer product $\tilde{\mathbf{x}}^{-}(k)\, \tilde{\mathbf{x}}^{-}(k)^T$:** $$ \tilde{\mathbf{x}}^{-}(k)\, \tilde{\mathbf{x}}^{-}(k)^T = \bigl(F \tilde{\mathbf{x}}^{+} + \mathbf{v}\bigr)\bigl(F \tilde{\mathbf{x}}^{+} + \mathbf{v}\bigr)^T $$ **Step 5 — Expand the product (three cross-terms):** $$ \tilde{\mathbf{x}}^{-}(k)\, \tilde{\mathbf{x}}^{-}(k)^T = F\, \tilde{\mathbf{x}}^{+}\, \tilde{\mathbf{x}}^{+T}\, F^T \;+\; F\, \tilde{\mathbf{x}}^{+}\, \mathbf{v}^T \;+\; \mathbf{v}\, \tilde{\mathbf{x}}^{+T}\, F^T \;+\; \mathbf{v}\, \mathbf{v}^T $$ **Step 6 — Take expectation; cross-terms vanish because $\tilde{\mathbf{x}}^{+}$ and $\mathbf{v}$ are independent zero-mean:** $$ P^{-}(k) = F\, P^{+}(k-1)\, F^T + Q $$ This is the **linear covariance propagation law**. Q.E.D. ### 3.2 Extension to Nonlinear Motion — The EKF Linearization For the nonlinear model $\mathbf{x}(k) = f(\mathbf{x}(k-1), \mathbf{u}(k)) + \mathbf{v}(k)$, we linearize via first-order Taylor expansion around the operating point $(\hat{\mathbf{x}}^{+}(k-1), \mathbf{u}(k))$: $$ f(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{u}) \approx f(\hat{\mathbf{x}}^{+}, \mathbf{u}) + \nabla F_x \bigl(\mathbf{x} - \hat{\mathbf{x}}^{+}\bigr) $$ where the Jacobian with respect to the state is: $$ \nabla F_x = \left.\frac{\partial f}{\partial \mathbf{x}}\right\vert_{\hat{\mathbf{x}}^{+},\, \mathbf{u}} $$ Substituting this linearization into the previous derivation yields: $$ P^{-}(k) = \nabla F_x\, P^{+}(k-1)\, \nabla F_x^T + \nabla F_u\, Q(k)\, \nabla F_u^T $$ ### 3.3 Derivation: Kalman Gain as the Optimal Linear Minimum-Variance Estimator We want to find the gain $W$ that minimizes the trace of the posterior covariance $P^{+}$ subject to the linear update $\hat{\mathbf{x}}^{+} = \hat{\mathbf{x}}^{-} + W \boldsymbol{\nu}$. **Step 1 — Write the update error:** $$ \tilde{\mathbf{x}}^{+} = \mathbf{x} - \hat{\mathbf{x}}^{+} = \mathbf{x} - \hat{\mathbf{x}}^{-} - W \boldsymbol{\nu} = \tilde{\mathbf{x}}^{-} - W \boldsymbol{\nu} $$ **Step 2 — Express the innovation in terms of the state error:** $$ \boldsymbol{\nu} = \mathbf{z} - H\, \hat{\mathbf{x}}^{-} = H(\mathbf{x} - \hat{\mathbf{x}}^{-}) + \mathbf{w} = H\, \tilde{\mathbf{x}}^{-} + \mathbf{w} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute:** $$ \tilde{\mathbf{x}}^{+} = \tilde{\mathbf{x}}^{-} - W(H \tilde{\mathbf{x}}^{-} + \mathbf{w}) = (I - WH)\, \tilde{\mathbf{x}}^{-} - W \mathbf{w} $$ **Step 4 — Form $P^{+} = E[\tilde{\mathbf{x}}^{+} \tilde{\mathbf{x}}^{+T}]$, expand, take expectation:** $$ P^{+} = (I - WH)\, P^{-}\, (I - WH)^T + W R W^T $$ **Step 5 — Take the derivative of $\text{tr}(P^{+})$ with respect to $W$ and set to zero:** $$ \frac{\partial\, \text{tr}(P^{+})}{\partial W} = -2 P^{-} H^T + 2 W H P^{-} H^T + 2 W R = 0 $$ **Step 6 — Solve for $W$:** $$ W\bigl(H P^{-} H^T + R\bigr) = P^{-} H^T $$ $$ \boxed{\,W = P^{-} H^T \bigl(H P^{-} H^T + R\bigr)^{-1} = P^{-} H^T S^{-1}\,} $$ This is the **optimal Kalman gain** in the linear MMSE sense. Q.E.D. ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example > **Problem:** A differential-drive robot with pose estimate $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = [0, 0, 0]^T$ (m, m, rad) and covariance $P^{-} = \text{diag}(0.25, 0.25, 0.04)$ observes landmark $i$ at true position $(3, 4)$. The sensor returns $z = [r, \phi]^T = [5.0,\, 0.927]^T$ with $R = \text{diag}(0.09, 0.0025)$. Compute the updated pose estimate. **Step 1 — Predicted range and bearing from $\hat{\mathbf{x}}$:** $$ \hat{r} = \sqrt{3^2 + 4^2} = 5.0 \text{ m}, \qquad \hat{\phi} = \text{atan2}(4, 3) - 0 = 0.9273 \text{ rad} $$ **Step 2 — Innovation:** $$ \boldsymbol{\nu} = \begin{bmatrix} 5.0 \\ 0.927 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 5.0 \\ 0.9273 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ -0.0003 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 3 — Measurement Jacobian (evaluated at the predicted pose):** $$ \nabla H_x = \begin{bmatrix} -\cos\hat{\phi} & -\sin\hat{\phi} & 0 \\ \sin\hat{\phi}/r & -\cos\hat{\phi}/r & -1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.6 & -0.8 & 0 \\ 0.16 & -0.12 & -1 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 4 — Innovation covariance:** $$ S = \nabla H_x P^{-} \nabla H_x^T + R $$ Compute $\nabla H_x P^{-} \nabla H_x^T$: $$ \begin{bmatrix} -0.6 & -0.8 & 0 \\ 0.16 & -0.12 & -1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.25 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.25 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.04 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -0.6 & 0.16 \\ -0.8 & -0.12 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix} $$ Multiplying left and right: $$ = \begin{bmatrix} 0.09 & 0.024 \\ 0.024 & 0.0064 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.09 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.0025 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.18 & 0.024 \\ 0.024 & 0.0089 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 5 — Inverse of $S$:** $$ S^{-1} = \frac{1}{(0.18)(0.0089) - (0.024)^2} \begin{bmatrix} 0.0089 & -0.024 \\ -0.024 & 0.18 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 12.16 & -32.79 \\ -32.79 & 245.96 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 6 — Kalman gain:** $$ W = P^{-} \nabla H_x^T S^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.25 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.25 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.04 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -0.6 & 0.16 \\ -0.8 & -0.12 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 12.16 & -32.79 \\ -32.79 & 245.96 \end{bmatrix} $$ After matrix multiplication: $$ W \approx \begin{bmatrix} -1.55 & 4.32 \\ -1.43 & -5.20 \\ 1.31 & -9.84 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 7 — Updated state:** $$ \hat{\mathbf{x}}^{+} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} -1.55 & 4.32 \\ -1.43 & -5.20 \\ 1.31 & -9.84 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ -0.0003 \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} -0.0013 \\ 0.0016 \\ 0.0030 \end{bmatrix} $$ The small correction reflects the fact that the measurement agreed with the prediction; the estimate is barely perturbed, exactly as expected for a low-innovation update. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 High-Level Stochastic Map Building Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[START Initialization] --> B[Initialize State Vector xv yv theta v] B --> C[Initialize Covariance Matrix P0 as Identity] C --> D[Acquire Odometry Control u k] D --> E[Compute Predicted State x minus k] E --> F[Linearize Motion Model to get Jacobian Fx] F --> G[Propagate Covariance P minus k] G --> H{Sensor Measurement z k Available?} H -- No --> D H -- Yes --> I[Perform Data Association] I --> J{Is Feature New?} J -- Yes --> K[Augment State Vector and Covariance] J -- No --> L[Compute Predicted Measurement h x] L --> M[Form Innovation nu k] M --> N[Linearize Measurement Model Hx] N --> O[Compute Innovation Covariance S k] O --> P[Compute Kalman Gain W k] P --> Q[Update State Estimate x plus k] Q --> R[Update Covariance P plus k] R --> S{Loop Closure Detected?} S -- Yes --> T[Trigger Global Consistency Correction] S -- No --> D K --> D T --> D ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the EKF Update ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PRED[Prediction Stage] P1[Previous State x plus] --> P2[Motion Model f] P3[Control Input u] --> P2 P2 --> P4[Predicted State x minus] P5[Previous Covariance P plus] --> P6[Jacobian Fx] P6 --> P7[Propagated Covariance P minus] P8[Process Noise Q] --> P7 end subgraph UPD[Update Stage] U1[Sensor Reading z] --> U2[Measurement Model h] U4[Predicted State x minus] --> U2 U2 --> U3[Innovation nu] U5[Propagated Covariance P minus] --> U6[Jacobian Hx] U6 --> U7[Innovation Covariance S] U9[Sensor Noise R] --> U7 U7 --> U8[Kalman Gain W] U3 --> U8 U8 --> U10[Corrected State x plus] U8 --> U11[Corrected Covariance P plus] end P4 --> U4 P7 --> U5 ``` ### 4.3 Map Representation: Geometric vs Topological vs Stochastic ```mermaid graph TB subgraph REP[Map Representation Paradigms] G[Geometric Metric Map
Grid Cells or Point Clouds] T[Topological Map
Nodes and Edges Graph] S[Stochastic Map
Joint Gaussian over Robot and Landmarks] end G --- T T --- S S --> OUT[Used in KTU PERUT735 Module 2] ``` ### 4.4 Data Association Logic ```mermaid flowchart TD DA[Incoming Observation z] --> CHK{Within Validation Gate of Existing Landmark?} CHK -- Yes --> MATCH[Assign Observation to Matched Landmark] CHK -- No --> NEW[Initialize as New Landmark and Augment State] MATCH --> UPD[Apply Standard EKF Update] NEW --> AUG[Add Row and Column to P and Entry to x] UPD --> END[Continue Recursion] AUG --> END ``` > [!NOTE] > The **validation gate** is typically a chi-squared threshold on the Mahalanobis distance $\boldsymbol{\nu}^T S^{-1} \boldsymbol{\nu}$. If the innovation is statistically consistent with the predicted landmark, it is accepted; otherwise the observation is treated as a new feature. This gate is critical for robustness in cluttered environments. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *Define a Stochastic Map. Explain why the joint covariance matrix is essential to the stochastic map representation rather than independent per-landmark variances.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A Stochastic Map is a probabilistic representation of the robot's environment in which the robot pose and all landmark locations are encoded as a single joint random variable described by a state vector $\mathbf{x}$ and covariance matrix $P$. The joint covariance is essential because the off-diagonal blocks capture **cross-correlations** between the robot pose and the landmark estimates. Whenever the robot's pose estimate drifts due to odometry error, every newly-observed landmark inherits a correlated uncertainty. Treating landmarks independently would lose this coupling, causing the map to become statistically inconsistent and the EKF update equations to be mathematically invalid. **(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for explanation of cross-correlation)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *State the Extended Kalman Filter prediction and update equations for the stochastic map and explain the role of the Kalman gain $W(k)$.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Prediction: $$ \hat{\mathbf{x}}^{-}(k) = f(\hat{\mathbf{x}}^{+}(k-1),\, \mathbf{u}(k)), \quad P^{-}(k) = \nabla F_x P^{+} \nabla F_x^T + \nabla F_u Q \nabla F_u^T $$ Update: $$ \boldsymbol{\nu} = \mathbf{z} - h(\hat{\mathbf{x}}^{-}), \quad W = P^{-} \nabla H_x^T ( \nabla H_x P^{-} \nabla H_x^T + R)^{-1} $$ $$ \hat{\mathbf{x}}^{+} = \hat{\mathbf{x}}^{-} + W \boldsymbol{\nu}, \quad P^{+} = (I - W \nabla H_x) P^{-} $$ The Kalman gain $W$ weights the innovation optimally. When the sensor is very accurate ($R$ small), $W$ is large and the estimate trusts the measurement. When the prior is very confident ($P^{-}$ small), $W$ is small and the estimate trusts the prediction. The gain is therefore an *automatic trust balancer*. **(3 marks: 1 for equations + 2 for role explanation)** --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2 Choice A]** **(a)** Derive the prediction step of the Extended Kalman Filter for a stochastic map, starting from the nonlinear motion model $\mathbf{x}(k) = f(\mathbf{x}(k-1), \mathbf{u}(k)) + \mathbf{v}(k)$. Clearly state the role of the Jacobian $\nabla F_x$ and the process noise covariance $Q(k)$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A mobile robot with state $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = [2.0,\ 1.0,\ \pi/4]^T$ moves with control $\mathbf{u} = [0.5,\ 0.1]^T$ (translation 0.5 m, rotation 0.1 rad). Using the unicycle model, compute the predicted pose. Assume the prior covariance $P^{+}$ is diagonal with entries $(0.16,\ 0.16,\ 0.01)$ and the process noise covariance $Q = \text{diag}(0.04,\ 0.04,\ 0.0025)$. Compute the predicted covariance $P^{-}$. Take Jacobians with respect to the previous pose only (ignore control Jacobian for simplicity). **(7 marks)** **Model Solution to (a):** > [!NOTE] > This question tests both the **L3 (Apply)** and **L4 (Analyze)** levels of Revised Bloom's Taxonomy. **Step 1 — State the motion model:** $$ \mathbf{x}(k) = f(\mathbf{x}(k-1), \mathbf{u}(k)) + \mathbf{v}(k), \quad \mathbf{v}(k) \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q) $$ **Step 2 — Compute the predicted mean by taking expectation:** $$ \hat{\mathbf{x}}^{-}(k) = f(\hat{\mathbf{x}}^{+}(k-1), \mathbf{u}(k)) $$ **Step 3 — Linearize $f$ via first-order Taylor expansion around $\hat{\mathbf{x}}^{+}(k-1)$:** $$ f(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{u}) \approx f(\hat{\mathbf{x}}^{+}, \mathbf{u}) + \nabla F_x \bigl(\mathbf{x} - \hat{\mathbf{x}}^{+}\bigr) $$ where the Jacobian matrix is: $$ \nabla F_x = \left.\frac{\partial f}{\partial \mathbf{x}}\right\vert_{\hat{\mathbf{x}}^{+}(k-1),\ \mathbf{u}(k)} $$ **Step 4 — Derive the error propagation as in Section 3.1, but with the linearized form:** $$ \tilde{\mathbf{x}}^{-}(k) = \nabla F_x \tilde{\mathbf{x}}^{+}(k-1) + \mathbf{v}(k) $$ **Step 5 — Form the outer product and take expectation. Cross-terms vanish due to independence and zero-mean noise:** $$ \boxed{P^{-}(k) = \nabla F_x\, P^{+}(k-1)\, \nabla F_x^T + Q(k)} $$ **Step 6 — Explain the role of $\nabla F_x$:** it is the local linear approximation of the nonlinear motion that maps prior uncertainty into the predicted uncertainty. **Role of $Q(k)$:** it captures the unmodeled disturbances and actuation noise that grow the covariance at every step. **Model Solution to (b):** **Step 1 — Apply the unicycle model with $\theta_v = \pi/4$:** $$ x_v^- = 2.0 + 0.5 \cos(\pi/4 + 0.1) = 2.0 + 0.5 \cos(0.8854) = 2.0 + 0.5 \times 0.6321 = 2.3161 \text{ m} $$ $$ y_v^- = 1.0 + 0.5 \sin(\pi/4 + 0.1) = 1.0 + 0.5 \times 0.7748 = 1.3874 \text{ m} $$ $$ \theta_v^- = \pi/4 + 0.1 = 0.8854 \text{ rad} $$ **Step 2 — Compute the state Jacobian for the unicycle model. From the partial derivatives:** $$ \nabla F_x = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -\Delta s \sin(\theta_v + \Delta\theta) \\ 0 & 1 & \Delta s \cos(\theta_v + \Delta\theta) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -0.3874 \\ 0 & 1 & 0.3161 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 3 — Compute $\nabla F_x P^{+} \nabla F_x^T$:** $$ P^{+} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.16 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.16 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.01 \end{bmatrix} $$ First multiply $\nabla F_x P^{+}$: $$ = \begin{bmatrix} 0.16 & 0 & -0.003874 \\ 0 & 0.16 & 0.003161 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.01 \end{bmatrix} $$ Then multiply by $\nabla F_x^T$: $$ \nabla F_x P^{+} \nabla F_x^T = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1603 & -0.00122 & -0.00387 \\ -0.00122 & 0.1610 & 0.00316 \\ -0.00387 & 0.00316 & 0.01 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 4 — Add $Q$:** $$ \boxed{P^{-} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2003 & -0.00122 & -0.00387 \\ -0.00122 & 0.2010 & 0.00316 \\ -0.00387 & 0.00316 & 0.0125 \end{bmatrix}} $$ The off-diagonal terms explicitly show the cross-correlations introduced by the rotation component of the motion. --- ### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2 Choice B]** **(a)** Explain the role of data association in stochastic map building. Discuss the validation gate using the Mahalanobis distance and describe what happens when a wrong association is made. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a suitable block diagram, describe the EKF-based stochastic map building algorithm. For a landmark at known position $(5, 5)$ observed by a robot at predicted pose $(1, 1, 0)$, derive the measurement Jacobian $\nabla H_x$. Assume range-bearing sensor model with $r = \sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2}$ and $\phi = \text{atan2}(\Delta y, \Delta x) - \theta_v$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution to (a):** > [!NOTE] > This question tests the **L2 (Understand)** and **L5 (Evaluate)** levels of Revised Bloom's Taxonomy. **Step 1 — Define data association:** Data association is the decision process that determines which previously-mapped landmark corresponds to the currently-observed sensor feature. It is the gatekeeper of the EKF update. **Step 2 — Validation gate using Mahalanobis distance:** The innovation $\boldsymbol{\nu} = \mathbf{z} - \hat{\mathbf{z}}$ is tested against the statistic: $$ d^2 = \boldsymbol{\nu}^T S^{-1} \boldsymbol{\nu} $$ If $d^2 < \chi^2_{d, \alpha}$ (chi-squared threshold for $d$ degrees of freedom at significance $\alpha$), the association is accepted; else rejected. **Step 3 — Consequence of a wrong association:** A wrong association treats an observation of a *different* physical landmark as if it came from the matched one. The innovation is large but happens to be statistically plausible, so the filter **trusts the wrong match** and corrupts both the landmark estimate *and*, through cross-correlation, the entire map. The covariance shrinks artificially, making the filter *overconfident* in a wrong map. **Step 4 — Mitigation:** Joint Compatibility Branch and Bound (JCBB), random sample consensus (RANSAC), or multi-hypothesis tracking (MHT) are used in advanced implementations. **Model Solution to (b):** **Step 1 — Block diagram (refer to Section 4.2 above).** The diagram shows the prediction stage feeding into the update stage, with the data association gate interposed before the innovation computation. **Step 2 — Compute predicted observation:** $$ \Delta x = 5 - 1 = 4, \qquad \Delta y = 5 - 1 = 4 $$ $$ r = \sqrt{16 + 16} = \sqrt{32} = 4\sqrt{2} \approx 5.657 \text{ m} $$ $$ \phi = \text{atan2}(4, 4) - 0 = \pi/4 \approx 0.7854 \text{ rad} $$ **Step 3 — Derive the Jacobian:** The measurement vector is $\mathbf{z} = [r, \phi]^T$. Taking partial derivatives with respect to the robot state $(x_v, y_v, \theta_v)$: $$ \frac{\partial r}{\partial x_v} = -\frac{\Delta x}{r} = -\frac{4}{5.657} = -0.7071 $$ $$ \frac{\partial r}{\partial y_v} = -\frac{\Delta y}{r} = -0.7071 $$ $$ \frac{\partial r}{\partial \theta_v} = 0 $$ $$ \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x_v} = \frac{\Delta y}{r^2} = \frac{4}{32} = 0.125 $$ $$ \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y_v} = -\frac{\Delta x}{r^2} = -0.125 $$ $$ \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial \theta_v} = -1 $$ Therefore: $$ \boxed{\nabla H_x = \begin{bmatrix} -0.7071 & -0.7071 & 0 \\ 0.125 & -0.125 & -1 \end{bmatrix}} $$ This Jacobian is then used in the Kalman gain and innovation covariance computations. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Stochastic Map Questions** > 1. **Forgetting the off-diagonal covariance terms (≈ 2 marks lost):** Many students compute $P$ as a diagonal matrix at every step. This is mathematically wrong because every observation introduces cross-correlations between the robot pose and all observed landmarks. Always write out the full matrix in the answer. > 2. **Linearizing at the wrong operating point (≈ 1 mark lost):** The Jacobian $\nabla H_x$ must be evaluated at the *predicted* state $\hat{\mathbf{x}}^{-}(k)$, not at the previous estimate $\hat{\mathbf{x}}^{+}(k-1)$. > 3. **Confusing prediction covariance with update covariance (≈ 1 mark lost):** $P^{-}$ has the noise term *added*; $P^{+}$ has it *removed* (or rather, the information content *increased*) via the Joseph form subtraction. > 4. **Skipping the innovation computation (≈ 1 mark lost):** Examiners award marks for explicitly writing $\boldsymbol{\nu} = \mathbf{z} - h(\hat{\mathbf{x}}^{-})$, not just for the gain. > 5. **Ignoring data association:** A stochastic map without correct data association is statistically unsound. Examiners frequently ask for a one-line justification of the validation gate. > 6. **Bearing angle wraparound:** When the predicted bearing is near $\pm\pi$ and the observed bearing is near $\mp\pi$, the innovation must be wrapped to the range $(-\pi, \pi]$. Failure to do this is a recurring 1-mark loss. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Stochastic Map = Joint Gaussian** representation of the entire robot + environment state, described by a single vector $\mathbf{x}$ and a single matrix $P$. - **The covariance matrix $P$ is symmetric and positive-semidefinite.** Off-diagonal entries are *not* zero and must be carried through every step. - **The two-step recursion is universal:** Predict (motion) → Update (measurement). This is the heartbeat of the EKF. - **Jacobians are mandatory** for nonlinear models. They are evaluated at the most recent estimate. - **Process noise $Q$** grows the covariance at every prediction; **sensor noise $R$** shrinks the effective information content of innovations. - **The Kalman gain is a trust-balancing device:** high $W$ means "trust the sensor," low $W$ means "trust the model." - **Data association is the gatekeeper** of the EKF update; use a chi-squared validation gate on the Mahalanobis distance. - **Loop closure** is the moment when the robot recognizes a previously-mapped area, producing a large information gain that reduces global uncertainty. - **Computational complexity** scales as $O(N^2)$ per update due to the full $P$ matrix, which is the chief weakness of the stochastic map approach for large environments. - **In KTU exams**, always derive the Jacobian explicitly, show the matrix dimensions, and write the final numerical answer in a box. - **The block diagram is mandatory** in any 14-mark question; examiners explicitly test process understanding via flow diagrams. - **Key synonyms to recognize in exam questions:** "probabilistic map," "EKF SLAM," "feature-based stochastic map," "Smith-Self-Cheeseman map" — all refer to the same architecture described above. ---

topics.title: Challenges - Cyclic Environments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT735 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Challenges - Cyclic Environments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition

A **Cyclic Environment** in robot navigation refers to an operational workspace in which the mobile robot can traverse along a closed-loop trajectory and return to a spatially or perceptually similar state. In probabilistic robotics (a core unit of the KTU PERUT735 syllabus), a cyclic environment is formally characterized as one where the **Markov localization belief** $bel(x_t)$ becomes **multi-modal** — meaning the probability density of the robot's pose $x_t$ exhibits **multiple distinct peaks** (local maxima) simultaneously, rather than a single unimodal Gaussian distribution.

Mathematically, a cyclic environment is one where the posterior belief cannot be represented as a single Gaussian $\mathcal{N}(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$ because the measurement likelihood function $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ produces multiple equally plausible pose hypotheses that are topologically connected through the environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Cyclic environments are the canonical benchmark that differentiates **Kalman Filter localization** (which fails) from **Particle Filter / Monte Carlo Localization (MCL)** (which succeeds). This distinction is a guaranteed high-weightage area in KTU End Semester Examinations.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **blindfolded person placed inside a large shopping mall** with dozens of identical, mirrored corridors stretching in a loop. As they walk, every step they take feels like a valid continuation, but they have **no way to distinguish where they currently are** — corridor A looks exactly like corridor B, which looks exactly like corridor C. They could be in any of them.

This is precisely the dilemma a robot faces in a **cyclic environment**:

- **Perceptual Aliasing:** Identical-looking locations produce identical sensor readings.
- **Loop Closure Ambiguity:** Revisiting a prior state cannot be confirmed deterministically.
- **Multi-Modal Belief:** The robot must maintain **several plausible "where I might be" hypotheses in parallel**, each with its own probability mass.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric for Cyclic Environment Difficulty:**
> The number of distinguishable modes (peaks) in the belief distribution is quantified by the **Effective Number of Particles** $N_{eff}$ in a particle filter, and the **entropy** $H(bel)$ of the belief. Lower $N_{eff}$ and higher entropy indicate severe cyclic ambiguity. Typical thresholds used in practice: $N_{threshold} \approx 0.5 \cdot N$.

### Real-World Engineering Examples

| Domain | Cyclic Environment Example | Why Cyclic? |
|---|---|---|
| Autonomous Vehicles | Underground parking garage with identical levels | Looping ramps, repeated pillars |
| Warehouse Robotics | Grid-based storage with repeated shelf bays | Symmetric layout |
| Indoor Service Robots | Office buildings with identical cubicles | Modular floor plans |
| Underwater AUVs | Pipe inspection networks | Branching loops |
| UAV Swarms | Agricultural fields with row crops | Parallel linear features |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Core Challenges in Cyclic Environments

The challenges are organized into a strict hierarchy per the KTU Module 2 learning outcomes:

**Challenge 1 — Global Localization Problem:**
The robot has no prior knowledge of its starting pose $x_0$. The initial belief is **uniformly distributed** over the entire state space $\mathcal{X}$:

$$bel(x_0) = \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{X} \vert}, \quad \forall x_0 \in \mathcal{X}$$

**Challenge 2 — Perceptual Aliasing (the heart of the cyclic problem):**
Two or more distinct poses $x^{(a)} \neq x^{(b)}$ produce **statistically indistinguishable** sensor measurements:

$$p(z \mid x^{(a)}, m) \approx p(z \mid x^{(b)}, m)$$

This breaks the unimodal Gaussian assumption central to the Extended Kalman Filter (EKF).

**Challenge 3 — The Kidnapped Robot Problem:**
The robot is **teleported abruptly** to an arbitrary new location without warning. Its belief must recover from a state of total ambiguity — the most demanding test of any localization algorithm.

### 2.2 Why the Kalman Filter Fails

The EKF maintains belief as a single Gaussian:

$$bel(x_t) = \mathcal{N}(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$$

In a cyclic environment, the true belief has **N modes**, so the EKF averages them into one incorrect peak at the **centroid** of all true modes. This produces catastrophic localization failure. This is a **frequently asked KTU question**.

### 2.3 Why the Particle Filter Succeeds

The Monte Carlo Localization (MCL) represents the belief as a **set of weighted samples** (particles) $\mathcal{X}_t = \{x_t^{(i)}, w_t^{(i)}\}_{i=1}^{N}$. Each particle is a hypothesis. Multi-modal beliefs are naturally represented because particles can cluster around **any number** of distinct peaks.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bayes Filter Recursion | $bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$ | Fundamental probabilistic update |
| 2 | Markov Assumption | $p(x_t \mid x_{0:t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) = p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_{t-1})$ | State is sufficient statistic |
| 3 | Particle Filter Prediction | $x_t^{(i)} \sim p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}^{(i)}, u_{t-1})$ | Propagate each hypothesis forward |
| 4 | Importance Weight Update | $w_t^{(i)} = w_{t-1}^{(i)} \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t^{(i)})$ | Score each hypothesis by sensor match |
| 5 | Normalization | $w_t^{(i)} = \dfrac{w_t^{(i)}}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} w_t^{(j)}}$ | Convert to valid probability |
| 6 | Effective Sample Size | $N_{eff} = \dfrac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (w_t^{(i)})^2}$ | Measure of particle degeneracy |
| 7 | Resampling Trigger | Resample if $N_{eff} < N_{threshold}$ | Restore particle diversity |
| 8 | Odometry Motion Decomposition | $\Delta \theta_1, \Delta t, \Delta \theta_2$ | Three-stage rotation-translation-rotation |
| 9 | Sensor Model | $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ | Likelihood given map $m$ |
| 10 | Belief Entropy (difficulty metric) | $H(bel) = -\sum_i bel(x_i) \log bel(x_i)$ | Uncertainty quantification |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Notation Note:** Absolute value $\vert \mathcal{X} \vert$ and conditional probability $\mid$ are rendered with `\vert` to preserve the integrity of the markdown table pipe characters.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Cyclic environment handling is the **backbone technology** behind:

- **Amazon Kiva warehouse robots** navigating feature-sparse shelving aisles
- **Tesla Full Self-Driving** parking structures with identical pillars
- **NASA Mars rovers** traversing rock fields with repetitive textures
- **Autonomous agricultural robots** in row-crop fields

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Bayes Filter Applied to a Cyclic Environment

We begin with the formal recursive Bayesian estimator. The goal is to derive the posterior belief $bel(x_t)$ that is mathematically capable of representing multi-modal distributions.

**Step 1 — Definition of Belief:**
The belief is defined as the posterior over the current state given all measurements and controls up to time $t$:

$$bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t-1})$$

**Step 2 — Apply Bayes' Rule to the Measurement Update:**
Using $p(A \mid B) = \dfrac{p(B \mid A) \, p(A)}{p(B)}$, we get:

$$bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) \, p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1})$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Markov Assumption:**
The current measurement depends only on the current state, and the current state depends only on the previous state and control:

$$p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) = p(z_t \mid x_t)$$
$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_{t-1}) \, p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-2}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back to obtain the recursive Bayes Filter:**

$$\boxed{bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}}$$

**Step 5 — Why this fails for Gaussian representations in cyclic environments:**
The integral is a **convolution** of the motion model with the prior belief. If $bel(x_{t-1})$ is multi-modal, the result is multi-modal. A single Gaussian cannot encode this — and this is the central proof of why the EKF fails in cyclic environments and why the particle filter is required.

### 3.2 Derivation: Monte Carlo Localization Update Rules

**Step 1 — Represent belief as a discrete set of weighted samples:**

$$bel(x_t) \approx \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_t^{(i)} \, \delta(x_t - x_t^{(i)})$$

where $\delta(\cdot)$ is the Dirac delta and $N$ is the number of particles.

**Step 2 — Prediction step (sample from the motion model):**
For each particle $i \in \{1, \dots, N\}$:

$$x_t^{(i)} \sim p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}^{(i)}, u_{t-1})$$

**Step 3 — Update step (importance sampling):**
The new importance weight is proportional to the measurement likelihood:

$$w_t^{(i)} = w_{t-1}^{(i)} \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t^{(i)})$$

**Step 4 — Normalization to obtain a valid probability distribution:**

$$w_t^{(i)} = \frac{w_t^{(i)}}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} w_t^{(j)}}$$

**Step 5 — Resampling (to combat particle degeneracy):**
Draw $N$ new particles with replacement, where the probability of drawing $x_t^{(i)}$ is $w_t^{(i)}$. The new weights are reset to $w_t^{(i)} = \frac{1}{N}$.

**Step 6 — Decision rule for resampling (effective particle count):**

$$N_{eff} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (w_t^{(i)})^2}$$

If $N_{eff} < N_{threshold}$ (typically $0.5N$), trigger resampling.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Algorithm Comparison in Cyclic Environments

| Criterion | Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) | Histogram Filter | Particle Filter (MCL) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Belief representation | Single Gaussian | Discrete grid | Weighted samples |
| Handles multi-modal belief | **No (fails)** | Yes | **Yes (optimal)** |
| Global localization | No | Yes | Yes |
| Kidnapped robot recovery | No | Limited | Yes |
| Computational cost | $O(d^2)$ | $O(\vert \mathcal{X} \vert)$ | $O(N)$ |
| Memory footprint | Low | High (full grid) | Moderate |
| Continuous spaces | Yes | No | Yes |
| KTU exam answer key recommendation | Mention failure mode | Mention resolution trade-off | **Recommended primary answer** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Particle Filter Localization in Cyclic Environments

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Robot at unknown pose in cyclic env] --> B[Initialize N particles uniformly across map]
    B --> C[Receive odometry control u_t-1]
    C --> D[Prediction Step: Sample x_t from motion model]
    D --> E[Receive sensor measurement z_t]
    E --> F[Update Step: Compute weight w_t = p of z_t given x_t]
    F --> G[Normalize weights to sum to 1]
    G --> H[Compute Effective Sample Size N_eff]
    H --> I{N_eff less than N_threshold?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Resampling: Draw N particles proportional to w_t]
    I -- No --> K[Retain current particles]
    J --> L[Output pose estimate as weighted mean of particles]
    K --> L
    L --> M{Convergence check: Single mode dominant?}
    M -- No --> C
    M -- Yes --> N[SUCCESS: Robot localized in cyclic env]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Multi-Modal Belief Evolution

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph S1["Time t=0: Global Ambiguity"]
        P1[Particle cloud: uniform]
    end
    subgraph S2["Time t=1: Multi-Modal Belief"]
        P2[Particles clustered in 3 identical corridors]
    end
    subgraph S3["Time t=5: Hypothesis Pruning"]
        P3[Particles concentrated in 1 correct corridor]
    end
    subgraph S4["Time t=10: Converged Localization"]
        P4[Single tight cluster at true pose]
    end
    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Challenge Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Cyclic Environment Challenges] --> B[Perceptual Aliasing]
    A --> C[Loop Closure Ambiguity]
    A --> D[Multi-Modal Belief]
    B --> E[Multiple identical sensor readings]
    C --> F[Cannot confirm revisits]
    D --> G[Requires non-Gaussian representation]
    E --> H[Solution: Particle Filter MCL]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Multi-hypothesis tracking with weighted samples]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define cyclic environment in the context of robot navigation. Why does it pose a challenge to traditional localization algorithms?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **cyclic environment** is one in which the robot can return to a perceptually similar or identical state by traversing a closed-loop path, causing the localization belief to become **multi-modal** (containing multiple distinct probability peaks). It challenges traditional algorithms like the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** because EKF represents belief as a single **unimodal Gaussian distribution**. In cyclic environments, multiple distinct poses produce statistically similar sensor measurements due to **perceptual aliasing**, and the EKF incorrectly averages these modes into one erroneous peak. Algorithms like the **Monte Carlo Localization (Particle Filter)** are required because they represent belief as a set of weighted samples, naturally supporting multi-modal distributions. **(3 marks: 1 definition + 1 EKF limitation + 1 particle filter advantage)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*What is perceptual aliasing? How does it manifest in cyclic environments?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Perceptual aliasing** is the phenomenon in which two or more **physically distinct locations** in the robot's environment produce **identical or near-identical sensor measurements**, making them indistinguishable to the robot's perception system. In cyclic environments, perceptual aliasing manifests when the robot traverses a **closed loop** and returns to a region with the same geometric structure, texture, or landmark configuration as a previously visited region. For example, in a warehouse with identical shelving bays, the robot's laser range finder produces the same scan signature at bay 1, bay 15, and bay 30. This causes the measurement likelihood $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ to assign **nearly equal probability** to multiple candidate poses, creating ambiguity that prevents deterministic localization. **(3 marks: 1 definition + 1 manifestation + 1 likelihood equation explanation)**

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 2]**

(a) **[7 marks]** Explain the **three core challenges** posed by cyclic environments to mobile robot localization. For each challenge, mathematically state how the belief $bel(x_t)$ is affected, and discuss why the **Extended Kalman Filter fails** to handle them.

(b) **[7 marks]** Derive the **Monte Carlo Localization (MCL) algorithm** step-by-step, including the prediction, update, normalization, and resampling steps. Show how the **effective sample size** $N_{eff}$ is computed and explain its role in the resampling decision.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Three Core Challenges [7 marks]**

**Challenge 1 — Global Localization [2 marks]:**
The robot is initialized with **no knowledge** of its starting pose $x_0$. The initial belief is uniform:

$$bel(x_0) = \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{X} \vert}, \quad \forall x_0 \in \mathcal{X}$$

This maximum-entropy prior is fundamentally **non-Gaussian** — it cannot be represented as a single $\mathcal{N}(\mu_0, \Sigma_0)$. The EKF requires an initial Gaussian and immediately collapses to a wrong estimate.

**Challenge 2 — Perceptual Aliasing [2.5 marks]:**
Multiple distinct poses produce indistinguishable sensor readings:

$$p(z_t \mid x_t^{(a)}, m) \approx p(z_t \mid x_t^{(b)}, m), \quad \text{for } x_t^{(a)} \neq x_t^{(b)}$$

The measurement update $\eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \int \dots$ then assigns comparable probability to multiple regions of the state space, generating a **multi-modal posterior**. The EKF, restricted to Gaussian form, averages these modes — producing a peak at a location where the robot **may not actually be**.

**Challenge 3 — Kidnapped Robot [2.5 marks]:**
The robot is teleported to a new pose $x_t^{new}$ without warning. Its belief must abruptly transition from confident to ambiguous. The EKF cannot recover from this discontinuity because the innovation $y_t = z_t - \hat{z}_t$ produces an inconsistent update that the linearized filter rejects as an outlier, leaving the robot permanently mislocalized.

**(b) Derivation of MCL Algorithm [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Sample-based belief representation [1 mark]:**

$$bel(x_t) \approx \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_t^{(i)} \, \delta(x_t - x_t^{(i)})$$

**Step 2 — Prediction [1.5 marks]:**
For each of the $N$ particles, sample a new pose from the motion model:

$$x_t^{(i)} \sim p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}^{(i)}, u_{t-1})$$

This propagates each hypothesis forward using odometry $u_{t-1}$, introducing motion noise to model uncertainty.

**Step 3 — Importance weight update [1.5 marks]:**

$$w_t^{(i)} = w_{t-1}^{(i)} \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t^{(i)})$$

Particles whose predicted pose explains the measurement $z_t$ well receive high weight; particles in inconsistent poses receive low weight.

**Step 4 — Normalization [1 mark]:**

$$w_t^{(i)} = \frac{w_t^{(i)}}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} w_t^{(j)}}$$

**Step 5 — Effective sample size and resampling [2 marks]:**

$$N_{eff} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (w_t^{(i)})^2}$$

If $N_{eff}$ falls below a threshold $N_{threshold} \approx 0.5N$, resampling is triggered: $N$ new particles are drawn with replacement, each with probability $w_t^{(i)}$. This eliminates low-weight particles and duplicates high-weight ones, restoring diversity. The new particle set is **multi-modal-ready** and ready for the next iteration.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 2]**

(a) **[7 marks]** With the help of suitable **block diagrams**, explain how **multi-modal belief** arises in cyclic environments. Compare the **Kalman Filter**, **Histogram Filter**, and **Particle Filter** in terms of their ability to handle such multi-modal beliefs. State clearly which is most suitable and why.

(b) **[7 marks]** A robot operates in a cyclic corridor environment with **three identical loops**. Initially, the robot's belief is uniform over the entire map. After the first sensor reading, the measurement likelihood assigns weights of 0.45, 0.35, and 0.20 to particles in loop 1, loop 2, and loop 3 respectively, across 1000 particles. Compute: (i) the number of particles assigned to each loop after normalization, (ii) the **effective sample size** $N_{eff}$, and (iii) state whether resampling should be triggered if $N_{threshold} = 600$.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Multi-Modal Belief and Algorithm Comparison [7 marks]**

**Multi-Modal Belief Origin [2 marks]:**
In cyclic environments, after a single sensor update, the posterior $bel(x_t)$ develops multiple peaks because the measurement likelihood $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ is high at multiple perceptually identical locations. The belief is no longer representable as a single Gaussian.

**Algorithm Comparison [5 marks]:**

| Algorithm | Belief Form | Multi-Modal Capable | KTU Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| Kalman Filter | $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \Sigma)$ | **No** | Fails — averages modes |
| Histogram Filter | Discrete grid over $\mathcal{X}$ | **Yes** | Works but $O(\vert \mathcal{X} \vert)$ memory |
| Particle Filter | Set of weighted samples $\{x^{(i)}, w^{(i)}\}$ | **Yes** | **Most suitable — flexible, scalable, optimal in limit $N \to \infty$** |

The particle filter is the gold standard for KTU exam answers on this topic.

**(b) Numerical Computation [7 marks]**

**(i) Normalized weights and particle allocation [3 marks]:**
Total weight sum:

$$\sum w = 0.45 + 0.35 + 0.20 = 1.00$$

(already normalized). Number of particles per loop:

$$N_1 = 0.45 \times 1000 = 450 \text{ particles in Loop 1}$$
$$N_2 = 0.35 \times 1000 = 350 \text{ particles in Loop 2}$$
$$N_3 = 0.20 \times 1000 = 200 \text{ particles in Loop 3}$$

**(ii) Effective sample size [2 marks]:**

$$N_{eff} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (w_t^{(i)})^2}$$

With three uniform groups of 1000 particles having weights 0.45, 0.35, 0.20:

$$\sum (w^{(i)})^2 = 450 \times (0.45)^2 + 350 \times (0.35)^2 + 200 \times (0.20)^2$$
$$= 450 \times 0.2025 + 350 \times 0.1225 + 200 \times 0.04$$
$$= 91.125 + 42.875 + 8.000 = 142.000$$

$$N_{eff} = \frac{1}{0.142} \approx 7.04 \text{ (effective particles out of 1000)}$$

**Correction using $N_{eff} = \frac{1}{\sum (w_i)^2}$ where $w_i$ are normalized weights per particle:**

Per-particle squared weight sum:

$$\sum (w_i)^2 = \frac{0.45^2 \times 450 + 0.35^2 \times 350 + 0.20^2 \times 200}{1000^2}$$

$$= \frac{91.125 + 42.875 + 8.000}{1{,}000{,}000} = \frac{142}{1{,}000{,}000} = 0.000142$$

$$N_{eff} = \frac{1}{0.000142} \approx 7042 \text{ particles}$$

Capped at $N_{eff} \leq N = 1000$, so $N_{eff} = 1000$.

**(iii) Resampling decision [2 marks]:**
Since $N_{eff} = 1000 > N_{threshold} = 600$, **resampling is NOT triggered**. The particle set remains well-diversified across the three loops, which is the desired behavior in a global localization phase — the robot should maintain multiple hypotheses until further measurements disambiguate them.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Most common mark-loss point:** Students forget to **normalize** the importance weights before computing $N_{eff}$ and lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Second most common mistake:** Stating that the Kalman Filter "cannot work in cyclic environments" without explaining that it assumes a **unimodal Gaussian** representation — the *reason* matters for full marks.
> 3. **Third pitfall:** Confusing the **kidnapped robot problem** with global localization. Global localization = unknown start; kidnapped robot = abrupt relocation during operation. Examiners frequently test this distinction.
> 4. **Equation presentation:** Always wrap multi-line derivations in `\begin{aligned} ... \end{aligned}` blocks; handwritten-style inline equations lose presentation marks under the 2024 scheme.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Cyclic environments** create **multi-modal belief distributions** that cannot be represented as a single Gaussian.
- [x] The **Extended Kalman Filter fails** in cyclic environments because it enforces a unimodal Gaussian assumption.
- [x] The **Particle Filter (Monte Carlo Localization)** is the standard solution — it represents belief as a set of weighted samples.
- [x] The **MCL algorithm** has four steps: **Prediction → Update → Normalization → Resampling**.
- [x] The **importance weight** update is $w_t^{(i)} = w_{t-1}^{(i)} \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t^{(i)})$.
- [x] The **effective sample size** is $N_{eff} = \dfrac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (w_t^{(i)})^2}$.
- [x] **Resampling** is triggered when $N_{eff} < N_{threshold}$ (typically $0.5N$).
- [x] **Three canonical challenges**: (1) Global localization, (2) Perceptual aliasing, (3) Kidnapped robot problem.
- [x] **Perceptual aliasing** = different poses producing statistically identical sensor measurements.
- [x] **Bayes filter recursion** is the mathematical foundation: $bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$.
- [x] **Markov assumption** justifies the recursive form: the current state is a sufficient statistic of all history.
- [x] For KTU 2024 exams, always include a **diagram** (block diagram or belief evolution sketch) when discussing localization algorithms — it earns 1–2 easy presentation marks.
- [x] **Histogram filter** is an alternative that handles multi-modality on grid-based state spaces but suffers from the **curse of dimensionality**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamic Environments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT735 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Dynamic Environments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — DYNAMIC ENVIRONMENTS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Dynamic Environment** in the context of mobile robotics is a structured or unstructured workspace in which at least one element of the world model (obstacles, agents, goals, or terrain features) undergoes a **state transition with respect to time**, denoted by the time-varying map function:

$$
\mathcal{M}(t) : \mathbb{R}^2 \times \mathbb{R}^+ \to \{0, 1\}
$$

where $\mathcal{M}(x, y, t) = 1$ represents a configuration $(x, y)$ occupied by an obstacle at time $t$. The complement $\mathcal{M}(x, y, t) = 0$ defines the **time-augmented free space** $\mathcal{F}_t$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A dynamic environment is a configuration space where obstacle occupancy is a **non-stationary random process**, requiring the planner to reason over the **spatio-temporal product space** $\mathcal{C} \times \mathbb{R}^+$ rather than the static $\mathcal{C}$-space alone.

### 1.2 Classification of Dynamic Environments

Dynamic environments are formally classified along two orthogonal axes:

| Axis | Static | Dynamic |
|------|--------|---------|
| **Known vs. Unknown** | A priori map available | Map built online via SLAM |
| **Predictable vs. Unpredictable** | Trajectory known | Trajectory estimated via probabilistic filter |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The four canonical sub-cases are (i) Known-Static, (ii) Known-Dynamic, (iii) Unknown-Static, and (iv) **Unknown-Dynamic** — the last being the most challenging and the focus of Module 2.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Analogy — Driving in Kerala City Traffic:**
> Imagine driving from **Thiruvananthapuram City Center to Technopark at 5:30 PM**. The map (the road network) is *known*, but the **other vehicles, pedestrians, and auto-rickshaws are dynamic obstacles** whose positions change every second. You cannot pre-compute a single collision-free path; instead, you constantly **predict** where others will be in 2–3 seconds and **re-plan** when reality deviates from prediction. This is exactly the dynamic environment problem in robotics.

- **Velocity** of an obstacle: $v_o$ measured in **m/s**.
- **Time Horizon** for prediction: $T_h$ typically **3 – 5 seconds**.
- **Re-planning frequency**: $f_r$ in **Hz** (often 10–20 Hz).
- **Spatial resolution** of the map: $\Delta x, \Delta y$ in **meters**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Model of a Dynamic Obstacle

A dynamic obstacle $\mathcal{O}_i$ at time $t$ is represented as a **time-parameterized geometric primitive** (typically a disc of radius $r_i$):

$$
\mathcal{O}_i(t) = \left\{ \, p \in \mathbb{R}^2 \; \big\vert \; \big\Vert p - c_i(t) \big\Vert_2 \leq r_i \right\}
$$

where $c_i(t)$ is the centroid trajectory. For a constant-velocity model:

$$
c_i(t) = c_i(t_0) + v_i \, (t - t_0)
$$

For a **constant-acceleration (kinematic)** model:

$$
c_i(t) = c_i(t_0) + v_i (t - t_0) + \tfrac{1}{2} a_i (t - t_0)^2
$$

## 2.2 Velocity Obstacle (VO) Concept — Fiorini & Shiller 1998

The **Velocity Obstacle** $\mathcal{VO}_{i \mid R}$ is the set of robot velocities $v_R$ that will cause a collision with obstacle $i$ at some future time, given current positions and velocities.

$$
\mathcal{VO}_{i \mid R} = \left\{ v_R \; \big\vert \; \exists \, t \in [0, \tau], \; (p_R + v_R t) \in \mathcal{O}_i(t) \right\}
$$

The **Reachable Velocity Obstacle (RVO)** extends this by accounting for the reactive response of both agents:

$$
v_R \notin \mathcal{VO}_{i \mid R} \quad \text{subject to} \quad v_R + v_i \in \mathcal{VO}_{i \mid R}
$$

## 2.3 Collision Cone Geometry

For a robot at $p_R$ and an obstacle at $p_O$ with relative position $\vec{\lambda} = p_O - p_R$ and relative velocity $v_{rel} = v_O - v_R$, the **collision cone** is:

$$
\mathcal{C}\mathcal{C} = \left\{ v_{rel} \; \big\vert \; \exists \, t > 0, \; \big\Vert \vec{\lambda} + v_{rel} \, t \big\Vert \leq r_R + r_O \right\}
$$

The condition for a collision-free choice of $v_R$ is:

$$
v_R \notin \mathcal{C}\mathcal{C} \oplus v_O
$$

where $\oplus$ denotes the **Minkowski sum**.

## 2.4 Time-to-Collision (TTC)

$$
\text{TTC} = \frac{-\left\langle \vec{\lambda}, v_{rel} \right\rangle - \sqrt{\langle \vec{\lambda}, v_{rel} \rangle^2 - \Vert v_{rel} \Vert^2 (\Vert \vec{\lambda} \Vert^2 - R^2)}}{\Vert v_{rel} \Vert^2}
$$

where $R = r_R + r_O$ is the **inflated radius**. A negative discriminant implies **no collision** in the future under the current velocity assumption.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table MUST be memorized for the KTU ESE.**

| Symbol | Formula / Definition | Units | Meaning |
|--------|---------------------|-------|---------|
| $\mathcal{VO}$ | set of $v_R$ causing collision in $[0, \tau]$ | m/s | Velocity Obstacle |
| $\text{TTC}$ | $\dfrac{-\langle \vec\lambda, v_{rel}\rangle - \sqrt{\Delta}}{\Vert v_{rel}\Vert^2}$ | s | Time to Collision |
| $\Delta$ | $\langle \vec\lambda, v_{rel}\rangle^2 - \Vert v_{rel}\Vert^2 (\Vert \vec\lambda\Vert^2 - R^2)$ | m²/s² | Collision Discriminant |
| $R$ | $r_R + r_O$ | m | Inflated Safety Radius |
| $\mathcal{F}_t$ | $\{p \mid \mathcal{M}(p, t) = 0\}$ | — | Time-varying Free Space |
| $c_i(t)$ | $c_i(t_0) + v_i \Delta t + \frac{1}{2} a_i \Delta t^2$ | m | Obstacle Trajectory |
| $D^*_{\text{Lite}}$ | $rhs(s) = \min(g(s), k_m(s) + h(s))$ | cost | Re-planning Key |
| $T_h$ | $3$ to $5$ | s | Prediction Horizon |
| $\omega_v$ | $v / \omega$ | m | ICR Radius (DWA) |
| $\sigma_{pos}$ | Kalman innovation std | m | Position Uncertainty |

## 2.6 Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles (Self-Driving Cars):** Collision avoidance on highways uses **VO/RVO** in real-time at 50 Hz on embedded GPUs.
- **Warehouse AMRs (Amazon Kiva):** Hundreds of robots share aisles — **multi-agent RVO** prevents deadlocks.
- **Aerial Swarms (DRONES):** Dynamic environments with aerodynamic disturbances require **RVO + MPC** hybrids.
- **Service Robots in Hospitals:** Predictable human motion (linear walk models) allows **3-second look-ahead planning**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Collision Cone Boundary

We derive the boundary of $\mathcal{C}\mathcal{C}$ — the set of relative velocities that graze (just touch) the inflated obstacle of radius $R$ at exactly one point in time.

**Step 1 — Setup.** Let $\vec{\lambda} = p_O - p_R$ and $v_{rel} = v_O - v_R$. The position of the obstacle relative to the robot at time $t$ is:

$$
\vec{\lambda}(t) = \vec{\lambda} + v_{rel} \, t
$$

**Step 2 — Grazing condition.** A grazing collision occurs when the relative distance equals $R$ at exactly one instant $t^* > 0$:

$$
\big\Vert \vec{\lambda} + v_{rel} \, t^* \big\Vert^2 = R^2
$$

**Step 3 — Expansion.** Squaring both sides:

$$
\big\langle \vec{\lambda} + v_{rel} \, t, \, \vec{\lambda} + v_{rel} \, t \big\rangle = R^2
$$

$$
\underbrace{\Vert \vec{\lambda} \Vert^2}_{a} + \underbrace{2 \langle \vec{\lambda}, v_{rel} \rangle}_{b} \, t + \underbrace{\Vert v_{rel} \Vert^2}_{c} \, t^2 = R^2
$$

This is a quadratic in $t$:

$$
c \, t^2 + b \, t + (a - R^2) = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Discriminant.** For real, positive solutions to exist (i.e., a collision is geometrically possible), the discriminant must be non-negative:

$$
\Delta = b^2 - 4 c (a - R^2) \geq 0
$$

$$
\Delta = \langle \vec{\lambda}, v_{rel} \rangle^2 - \Vert v_{rel} \Vert^2 \big( \Vert \vec{\lambda} \Vert^2 - R^2 \big) \geq 0
$$

**Step 5 — Time of first contact.** Solving the quadratic with the minus sign (earliest contact):

$$
t^* = \frac{-b - \sqrt{\Delta}}{2c} = \frac{-\langle \vec{\lambda}, v_{rel} \rangle - \sqrt{\Delta}}{\Vert v_{rel} \Vert^2}
$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion.** The **collision cone** consists of all $v_{rel}$ for which $\Delta \geq 0$ **and** $t^* \in (0, \tau]$. The robot must choose $v_R$ such that $v_{rel} = v_O - v_R$ lies **outside** this cone.

## 3.2 Derivation: Constant-Acceleration Obstacle Prediction

Given position $c_0$, velocity $v_0$, and acceleration $a$ at reference time $t_0$, derive the predicted position at time $t$.

**Step 1 — Kinematic integration.** Starting from Newton's second-order kinematic equation:

$$
\frac{d^2 c}{dt^2} = a \quad \text{(constant)}
$$

**Step 2 — First integration (velocity).**

$$
\frac{d c}{dt} = \int a \, dt = a \, t + C_1 = a \, t + v_0
$$

where $C_1 = v_0$ is the initial velocity at $t_0 = 0$ (without loss of generality).

**Step 3 — Second integration (position).**

$$
c(t) = \int (a \, t + v_0) \, dt = \tfrac{1}{2} a \, t^2 + v_0 \, t + C_2
$$

**Step 4 — Initial condition.** At $t = 0$, $c(0) = c_0$, so $C_2 = c_0$.

**Step 5 — Final closed form.**

$$
c(t) = c_0 + v_0 \, t + \tfrac{1}{2} \, a \, t^2
$$

**Step 6 — Uncertainty propagation.** If position has covariance $P_{c,0}$ and velocity $P_{v,0}$, the predicted covariance at time $t$ is:

$$
P(t) = P_{c,0} + 2 t \, P_{c,v} + t^2 P_{v,0} + \tfrac{t^4}{4} P_{a,0}
$$

This is **critical** for dynamic SLAM under measurement noise.

## 3.3 Derivation: D* Lite Re-planning Key Update

The **D* Lite** algorithm (Koenig & Likhachev, 2002) incrementally re-plans when the map changes due to a newly detected dynamic obstacle.

**Step 1 — State.** Maintain for every node $s$: $g(s)$ (current shortest cost from $s_{goal}$), $rhs(s)$ (one-step look-ahead), and $h(s)$ (heuristic).

**Step 2 — Update rule after an edge-cost change from $c(s, s')$ to $c'(s, s')$:**

$$
rhs(s) = \min_{s' \in \text{Succ}(s)} \big( g(s') + c(s, s') \big)
$$

**Step 3 — Key computation.** The priority key is:

$$
k(s) = \big[ \, k_1(s), \, k_2(s) \, \big]
$$

where

$$
k_1(s) = \min\big( g(s), \, rhs(s) \big) + h(s_{start}, s)
$$

$$
k_2(s) = \min\big( g(s), \, rhs(s) \big)
$$

The lexicographic ordering $\prec$ is:

$$
[k_1, k_2] \prec [k_1', k_2'] \iff k_1 < k_1' \;\text{or}\; (k_1 = k_1' \;\text{and}\; k_2 < k_2')
$$

**Step 4 — Termination.** Re-planning terminates when the start node $s_{start}$ is popped from the priority queue with $k(s_{start}) \geq k_{goal}$ — meaning the current path is provably optimal.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is a frequent KTU question topic.**

| Method | Re-planning | Optimality | Completeness | Computational Cost |
|--------|-------------|------------|--------------|--------------------|
| **A\*** | None (static) | Optimal | Complete | $O(b^d)$ |
| **D\*** | Incremental | Optimal | Complete | $O(\vert V \vert \log \vert V \vert)$ per change |
| **D\* Lite** | Incremental | Optimal | Complete | $O(\vert V \vert \log \vert V \vert)$ |
| **RRT\*** | Sample-based | Asymptotically optimal | Probabilistically complete | $O(n \log n)$ |
| **VO / RVO** | Reactive | Local | None in dense clutter | $O(n)$ |
| **DWA** | Reactive | Local | None | $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ |
| **MPC** | Receding horizon | Locally optimal | None | $O(T^3 n^3)$ |

where $b$ = branching factor, $d$ = depth, $\vert V \vert$ = number of vertices, $n$ = samples, $T$ = MPC horizon.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Dynamic-Environment Navigator

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sensors LiDAR Camera IMU] --> B[State Estimator EKF ESKF]
    B --> C[Dynamic Map Builder Octomap + Velocity Layer]
    C --> D[Predictor Constant Velocity Kalman]
    D --> E[Planner D Star Lite A Star]
    E --> F[Smoother Time Elastic Band]
    F --> G[Controller MPC DWA]
    G --> H[Robot Actuators]
    H --> I[World State Updated]
    I --> C
    J[Static Map Prior] --> C
    K[Human Intent Predictor LSTM] --> D
```

## 4.2 State Machine for Re-planning Trigger

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Track
    Track --> Predict: New scan acquired
    Predict --> Validate: Predict obstacle pose at t + T sub h
    Validate --> Execute: TTC exceeds safety margin
    Validate --> Replan: TTC below safety margin
    Replan --> Smooth: Compute new path
    Smooth --> Execute: Smoothed trajectory ready
    Execute --> Track: Command velocities
    Execute --> EmergencyStop: TTC below 0.5 s
    EmergencyStop --> Track: Obstacle cleared
```

## 4.3 Velocity Obstacle Geometric Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Robot pR] --> B[Relative Vector lambda]
    C[Obstacle pO] --> B
    B --> D[Collision Cone apex at pR]
    D --> E[Grazing ray 1]
    D --> F[Grazing ray 2]
    G[Selected vR Safe] --> H[Outside Cone]
    I[Forbidden vR] --> J[Inside Cone]
```

## 4.4 Spatio-Temporal State Grid

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Spatial Layer xy grid] --> C[Spatio Temporal Product]
    B[Temporal Layer t axis] --> C
    C --> D[Occupancy Probability P x y t]
    D --> E[Free Space F sub t]
    D --> F[Blocked Space B sub t]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Define a Velocity Obstacle. How does it differ from a Collision Cone in terms of representation and use in dynamic planning?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Velocity Obstacle (VO)** is the set of all robot velocities $v_R$ that would cause the robot to collide with a moving obstacle at some time $t \in [0, \tau]$, given the current relative geometry and velocities (Fiorini & Shiller, 1998).

- The **Collision Cone** is a geometric construct in the **relative-velocity space** representing directions that lead to collision.
- The **Velocity Obstacle** is a transformed region in the **robot's velocity space**, obtained by translating the collision cone by $-v_O$.

**Distinction:** Both represent the same physics, but the VO is directly used by the controller (it operates in $v_R$ space), whereas the collision cone is used in theoretical analysis. **(1 mark)** Definition, **(1 mark)** representation difference, **(1 mark)** use-case difference.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**What is Time-to-Collision (TTC)? Derive its expression for a robot approaching a static obstacle with relative velocity $v_{rel}$ at distance $\lambda$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**TTC** is the time remaining before a collision, assuming both the robot and obstacle maintain their current velocities. **(1 mark)**

For a static obstacle ($v_O = 0$), the relative velocity is $v_{rel} = -v_R$, and the relative distance vector is $\vec{\lambda} = p_O - p_R$. Setting the relative distance to the inflated radius $R$:

$$
\big\Vert \vec{\lambda} + v_{rel} \, t \big\Vert = R
$$

Solving the quadratic for $t$ and taking the positive root:

$$
\text{TTC} = \frac{-\langle \vec{\lambda}, v_{rel} \rangle - \sqrt{\langle \vec{\lambda}, v_{rel} \rangle^2 - \Vert v_{rel} \Vert^2 (\Vert \vec{\lambda} \Vert^2 - R^2)}}{\Vert v_{rel} \Vert^2}
$$

**(1 mark)** for setup, **(1 mark)** for solving the quadratic.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2 Internal Choice]`

**Q-A (a)** Discuss the architecture of a dynamic environment navigation system with reference to perception, prediction, planning, and control modules. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A dynamic navigation system consists of four tightly coupled modules operating in a **closed loop at 10–50 Hz**:

**(i) Perception Module (2 marks):** Fuses **LiDAR**, **camera**, and **radar** data using an Extended Kalman Filter (ESKF) to produce a pose estimate $\hat{x}_R$. A **dynamic map** (e.g., Octomap with velocity layers) maintains per-cell occupancy and velocity estimates.

**(ii) Prediction Module (2 marks):** Uses a **Constant Turn Rate and Acceleration (CTRA)** model or **Interacting Multiple Model (IMM)** filter to forecast obstacle positions over a horizon $T_h = 3$ s. The Kalman prediction step:

$$
\hat{x}_{k+1 \mid k} = A \, \hat{x}_{k \mid k} + B \, u_k
$$

$$
P_{k+1 \mid k} = A \, P_{k \mid k} A^\top + Q
$$

propagates both state and uncertainty.

**(iii) Planning Module (2 marks):** A **time-augmented A\*** or **D\* Lite** produces a kinematically feasible path. Reactive layers (**VO/RVO** or **DWA**) handle last-second deviations.

**(iv) Control Module (1 mark):** A **Model Predictive Controller (MPC)** tracks the trajectory while respecting velocity, acceleration, and safety constraints encoded as polyhedral sets.

**(b)** A robot at position $(0, 0)$ moves with velocity $v_R = (0.5, 0)$ m/s. A pedestrian at $(8, 0)$ moves with velocity $v_O = (-0.2, 0)$ m/s. Both are modeled as point agents. Compute the Time-to-Collision and state whether a collision will occur. Take $R = 0.5$ m. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute vectors.** **(1 mark)**

$$
\vec{\lambda} = p_O - p_R = (8, 0) - (0, 0) = (8, 0) \; \text{m}
$$

$$
v_{rel} = v_O - v_R = (-0.2 - 0.5, \; 0 - 0) = (-0.7, 0) \; \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute inner product and norms.** **(1 mark)**

$$
\langle \vec{\lambda}, v_{rel} \rangle = (8)(-0.7) + (0)(0) = -5.6 \; \text{m}^2/\text{s}
$$

$$
\Vert \vec{\lambda} \Vert^2 = 64 \; \text{m}^2, \quad \Vert v_{rel} \Vert^2 = 0.49 \; \text{m}^2/\text{s}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Compute discriminant.** **(2 marks)**

$$
\Delta = (-5.6)^2 - (0.49)(64 - 0.25) = 31.36 - (0.49)(63.75)
$$

$$
\Delta = 31.36 - 31.2375 = 0.1225 \; \text{m}^4/\text{s}^2
$$

$$
\sqrt{\Delta} = 0.35 \; \text{m}^2/\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute TTC.** **(1 mark)**

$$
\text{TTC} = \frac{-(-5.6) - 0.35}{0.49} = \frac{5.6 - 0.35}{0.49} = \frac{5.25}{0.49}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{TTC} \approx 10.71 \; \text{s}}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.** **(2 marks)**
Since $\Delta > 0$ and $\text{TTC} > 0$, a **collision will occur** in approximately **10.71 seconds** under the current velocity assumption. The robot must either change its heading or reduce speed. A safe response: choose $v_R'$ such that $v_{rel}' = v_O - v_R'$ lies **outside** the collision cone. For instance, steering in the $y$-direction with $v_R = (0.5, 0.3)$ m/s yields:

$$
v_{rel}' = (-0.7, -0.3), \quad \Vert v_{rel}' \Vert^2 = 0.58
$$

$$
\Delta' = \langle (8,0),(-0.7,-0.3)\rangle^2 - 0.58 \cdot 63.75 = (-5.6)^2 - 36.975 = 31.36 - 36.975 < 0
$$

A **negative discriminant** confirms the new velocity is **collision-free**. 

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2 Internal Choice]`

**Q-B (a)** With suitable diagrams, explain the **Velocity Obstacle (VO)** and **Reciprocal Velocity Obstacle (RVO)** methods for reactive collision avoidance in dynamic environments. Compare their oscillation behavior. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Velocity Obstacle (Fiorini & Shiller, 1998):** **(3 marks)** Given the current relative position $\vec{\lambda} = p_O - p_R$ and velocities $v_R, v_O$, the VO is the cone of $v_R$ values that cause collision in $[0, \tau]$. The robot picks any $v_R$ outside this cone. **Limitation:** When both agents use VO simultaneously, they oscillate — both swerve right, then both swerve left.

**Reciprocal Velocity Obstacle (van den Berg et al., 2008):** **(2 marks)** RVO assumes both agents take **equal responsibility** for avoidance. The forbidden region becomes:

$$
\mathcal{RVO} = \left\{ v_R \; \big\vert \; 2 v_R - v_R^{opt} \in \mathcal{VO} \right\}
$$

Choosing $v_R$ such that $2 v_R - v_R^{opt}$ lies **outside** $\mathcal{VO}$ ensures smooth, oscillation-free avoidance.

**Oscillation Comparison:** **(2 marks)**

| Method | Two-agent case | Dense crowd | Compute |
|--------|----------------|-------------|---------|
| **VO** | Oscillates | Deadlocks | $O(n)$ |
| **RVO** | Stable | Stable | $O(n)$ |
| **HRVO** | Stable, tighter | Stable | $O(n)$ |

**HRVO** (Hybrid RVO) further splits the responsibility adaptively, providing smoother trajectories at intersections.

---

**(b)** A differential-drive robot uses the **Dynamic Window Approach (DWA)**. The admissible velocities are $v \in [0, 0.5]$ m/s and $\omega \in [-1.0, 1.0]$ rad/s. The goal heading is $\theta_g = 30°$, the distance to the nearest dynamic obstacle is $d_{obs} = 1.2$ m, and the current velocity is $v_c = 0.2$ m/s, $\omega_c = 0$ rad/s.
