Using the simplified objective $G = \alpha(1 - \frac{\Delta \theta}{\pi}) + \beta \frac{d_{obs}}{2} + \gamma \frac{v}{v_{max}}$, with $\alpha = 0.6, \beta = 0.3, \gamma = 0.1$, evaluate $G$ for two candidates: $(v, \omega) = (0.4, 0.3)$ and $(v, \omega) = (0.3, -0.2)$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute heading alignment term.** **(2 marks)** For candidate 1, $\omega \cdot T_{sim} = 0.3 \times 2 = 0.6$ rad, so the simulated heading change is $\Delta \theta_1 = 0.6$ rad $= 34.4°$. This is **worse** than the goal, so $\Delta \theta_1 = 0.6$ rad and $1 - \frac{\Delta \theta_1}{\pi} = 1 - 0.191 = 0.809$.

For candidate 2, $\omega \cdot T_{sim} = -0.2 \times 2 = -0.4$ rad, so $\Delta \theta_2 = 0.4$ rad and $1 - \frac{0.4}{\pi} = 1 - 0.127 = 0.873$.

**Step 2 — Compute clearance term.** **(2 marks)** Assuming constant $d_{obs}$ for both:

$$
\frac{d_{obs}}{2} = \frac{1.2}{2} = 0.6
$$

**Step 3 — Compute velocity term.** **(1 mark)**

For candidate 1: $\frac{0.4}{0.5} = 0.8$.

For candidate 2: $\frac{0.3}{0.5} = 0.6$.

**Step 4 — Evaluate $G$.** **(2 marks)**

**Candidate 1:**

$$
G_1 = (0.6)(0.809) + (0.3)(0.6) + (0.1)(0.8)
$$

$$
G_1 = 0.4854 + 0.18 + 0.08 = 0.7454
$$

**Candidate 2:**

$$
G_2 = (0.6)(0.873) + (0.3)(0.6) + (0.1)(0.6)
$$

$$
G_2 = 0.5238 + 0.18 + 0.06 = 0.7638
$$

**Step 5 — Decision.** Since $G_2 > G_1$, the controller selects **candidate 2**: $(v, \omega) = (0.3, -0.2)$. The negative $\omega$ indicates a left turn, which better aligns the robot with the $30°$ goal heading.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Deductions**
>
> 1. **Sign Errors in TTC:** Students frequently forget the **minus sign** before the square root, producing a physically meaningless **negative TTC**. The correct root is the smaller positive one (earliest contact). Loss: up to **2 marks**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Minkowski Sum:** When computing the inflated obstacle, students often use $R = r_O$ instead of $R = r_R + r_O$. Loss: **1 mark**.
>
> 3. **Confusing VO and RVO:** Writing "RVO = VO" or omitting the factor of 2 in $2 v_R - v_R^{opt}$ is a common slip. Loss: **1–2 marks**.
>
> 4. **DWA units:** Mixing rad/s with m/s in $v/\omega$ comparison is fatal. Always keep $G$ **dimensionless** by normalising each term.
>
> 5. **No Heuristic in D\* Lite:** Forgetting that $h(s)$ must be a **consistent (admissible + monotone)** heuristic. Loss: **1 mark**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Things You MUST Remember for KTU Module 2**
>
> - A **dynamic environment** is defined by the time-varying map $\mathcal{M}(x, y, t)$ — not a static map.
> - The **Velocity Obstacle (VO)** is the set of robot velocities causing future collision; compute in **velocity space**, not configuration space.
> - The **Collision Cone** is in **relative-velocity space**; VO = Collision Cone translated by $-v_O$.
> - **TTC** formula must include the **square-root discriminant**; a negative $\Delta$ ⇒ no collision.
> - **RVO** halves the avoidance responsibility — eliminates the **reciprocal dancing** oscillation.
> - **D\* Lite** uses a **lexicographic key** $[k_1, k_2]$ and re-plans only the **affected prefix** of the path.
> - **Kalman prediction** propagates **both state and covariance** — uncertainty grows cubically with horizon for constant-velocity models.
> - **DWA** discretises the **dynamic window** $(v, \omega)$ and scores each by heading, clearance, and velocity.
> - **MPC** in dynamic environments is a **receding-horizon** optimization; constraint set encodes **TTI (Time-To-Intersection)** safety.
> - For KTU numericals: always draw a **2-D sketch**, label $\vec{\lambda}, v_R, v_O, v_{rel}$, then substitute into the formula.
> - Units to remember: $\lambda$ in **m**, $v$ in **m/s**, $\omega$ in **rad/s**, TTC in **s**, $G$ is **dimensionless**.
> - The **time-augmented free space** $\mathcal{F}_t$ is the planning domain — never plan in $\mathcal{C}$-space alone.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Motion in Potential Field and Navigation Function Problem Statement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT735 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Motion in Potential Field and Navigation Function Problem Statement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Motion in Potential Field** is a reactive path planning paradigm in which a robot's configuration $q \in \mathcal{C}$ (configuration space) is treated as a point mass moving through an *artificially constructed scalar potential energy landscape* $U(q): \mathcal{C}_{free} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$, where the goal configuration is the unique global minimum and obstacles are represented as regions of high potential. The robot follows the negated gradient flow $\dot{q} = -\nabla U(q)$ (steepest descent dynamics), effectively converting a *geometric path planning problem* into an *ordinary differential equation (ODE)* on a smooth energy surface.

The **Navigation Function Problem Statement**, formalized rigorously by Rimon and Koditschek (1992), is defined as follows:

> *"Given a connected, compact configuration space $\mathcal{F} \subset \mathbb{R}^n$ (or $\mathcal{C}_{free}$) populated with a finite set of closed, disjoint obstacle regions $\{ \mathcal{O}_i \}_{i=1}^{m}$, and a designated goal point $q_{goal} \in \mathcal{F} \setminus \bigcup \mathcal{O}_i$, find a map $\varphi: \mathcal{F} \rightarrow [0,1]$ such that $\varphi$ is analytic (or $C^2$ smooth), admissible, a Morse function, and has $q_{goal}$ as its unique minimum — so that almost every gradient flow trajectory of $-\nabla \varphi$ converges to $q_{goal}$ while avoiding all obstacles."*

The four mandated properties of a true navigation function $\varphi$ are:

1. **Analytic (Smooth)**: $\varphi \in C^2(\mathcal{F})$ — guarantees existence of continuous gradients and Jacobians.
2. **Admissible**: $\varphi(q) = 1$ (or some constant) on the boundary $\partial \mathcal{F}$ and $\varphi(q) \to 0$ only at $q_{goal}$.
3. **Polar (Proper)**: $q_{goal}$ is the unique global minimum on $\mathcal{F} \setminus \bigcup \mathcal{O}_i$.
4. **Morse (Non-Degenerate)**: All critical points are non-degenerate (Hessian is full rank), ensuring only isolated equilibria.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The distinction between an *ad-hoc Artificial Potential Field (APF)* and a *true Rimon-Koditschek Navigation Function* is a frequently tested point. APFs suffer from **local minima** (where the robot stalls) unless carefully constructed; a Navigation Function $\varphi$ mathematically guarantees that local minima coincide only with the goal.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hilly Landscape Intuition"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy (Beginner-Friendly):**

Imagine a **hilly terrain** where:
- The **robot** is a **marble (steel ball)** you release from some height.
- The **goal destination** is the **deepest valley** at the center of the landscape.
- The **obstacles** are **steep, repelling hills** (almost vertical cliffs) that the marble cannot climb.
- **Gravity** plays the role of the gradient force — the marble naturally rolls downhill.

In this analogy:
- The shape of the terrain = the scalar potential function $U(q)$.
- The slope of the terrain at any point = the gradient $\nabla U(q)$.
- The direction the marble rolls = $-\nabla U(q)$ (downhill direction).
- The hills around obstacles = repulsive potential $U_{rep}$.
- The gentle slope toward the goal = attractive potential $U_{att}$.

**The key insight**: by *engineering* (i.e., mathematically defining) the terrain $U(q)$, we can guarantee that a marble released *anywhere* will roll to the goal valley *without getting stuck on a smaller hill* (local minimum). This is precisely what a **navigation function** is — an *engineered* terrain with no trap valleys.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Misconception:** A naive sum of an attractive and a repulsive potential (classic APF) is *not* a navigation function — it can have spurious local minima in concave obstacle arrangements. The Rimon-Koditschek construction uses a *squared ratio* form to mathematically eliminate these traps.

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Symbols Used Throughout

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Range |
|---|---|---|
| $q$ | Robot configuration | $\in \mathbb{R}^n$ |
| $q_{goal}$ | Goal configuration | Single point |
| $U_{att}(q)$ | Attractive potential | $\geq 0$ |
| $U_{rep}(q)$ | Repulsive potential | $\geq 0$ |
| $k, \eta$ | Scaling gains | $> 0$ |
| $\rho_0$ | Influence distance of obstacle | $> 0$ |
| $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ | Free configuration space | Open subset |
| $\beta(q)$ | Rimon-Koditschek obstacle function | $\geq 0$ |
| $\kappa$ | Tuning parameter (sharpness) | $> 0$ |
| $d_{i}(q)$ | Distance to $i$-th obstacle boundary | $\geq 0$ |

All gains are **positive real constants** chosen by the designer to trade off goal-seeking aggressiveness vs. obstacle avoidance safety.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Breakdown of the Potential Field Paradigm

The framework decomposes into **three operational stages**:

1. **World Modeling Stage** — Represent the workspace and configuration space analytically:
   - Define the robot's free space $\mathcal{C}_{free} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \bigcup_{i=1}^{m} \mathcal{C}\mathcal{O}_i$.
   - Define the boundary $\partial \mathcal{C}\mathcal{O}_i$ of each obstacle.
   - Define a distance function $\rho_i(q) = \min_{p \in \partial \mathcal{C}\mathcal{O}_i} \| q - p \|$.

2. **Potential Function Construction Stage** — Synthesize a scalar field $U(q)$ satisfying required properties:
   - **Attractive component** $U_{att}(q)$: convex, has a single global minimum at $q_{goal}$.
   - **Repulsive component** $U_{rep}(q)$: grows to infinity as $q$ approaches any obstacle boundary.
   - **Combination rule**: $U(q) = U_{att}(q) + U_{rep}(q)$ (classic APF) OR a *ratio form* (Rimon-Koditschek NF).

3. **Motion Execution Stage** — Apply gradient descent dynamics:
   - Compute the total artificial force: $F(q) = -\nabla U(q)$.
   - Update the configuration: $q_{k+1} = q_k + \dot{q} \cdot \Delta t = q_k - \nabla U(q_k) \cdot \Delta t$.
   - Repeat until $\| q_k - q_{goal} \| < \epsilon$ (convergence threshold).

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Component

- **Why use a gradient?** A gradient descent is a *local* operation — only requires knowledge of $U$ in the robot's neighborhood — making it computationally cheap and reactive to dynamic environments.
- **Why is the attractive term usually quadratic near the goal?** A quadratic bowl $U_{att} = \frac{1}{2} k \| q - q_{goal} \|^2$ has $\nabla U_{att} = k(q - q_{goal})$, providing a **linear restoring force** that pulls the robot toward the goal. Far from the goal, a *conic* potential $U_{att} = k \| q - q_{goal} \|$ is sometimes used to keep the gradient magnitude constant (avoiding weak forces at long range).
- **Why is the repulsive term $\frac{1}{\rho}$ or $\frac{1}{\rho^2}$?** Because as the robot approaches the obstacle ($\rho \to 0$), we want the repulsive force to *diverge* to infinity, creating an impassable barrier. The reciprocal $\frac{1}{\rho}$ naturally achieves this.
- **Why a finite influence distance $\rho_0$?** Far from obstacles, the repulsive term should be **zero** (no effect) so the robot is attracted purely toward the goal. The threshold $\rho_0$ defines the *sphere of influence* of each obstacle.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations for the KTU 2024 board exam. Every entry below is a known past-exam favorite.**

| # | Equation | Name | When Used | Critical Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $U_{att}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2}\, k\, \lVert q - q_{goal} \rVert^{2}$ | Quadratic attractive potential | Near goal region | $k > 0$ |
| 2 | $F_{att}(q) = -\nabla U_{att} = -k(q - q_{goal})$ | Attractive (restoring) force | Inside goal basin | Linear in error |
| 3 | $U_{att}(q) = k\, \lVert q - q_{goal} \rVert$ | Conic attractive potential | Far from goal (constant force) | $k > 0$ |
| 4 | $U_{rep,i}(q) = \begin{cases} \tfrac{1}{2}\, \eta\, \left(\tfrac{1}{\rho_i} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^{2}, & \rho_i \le \rho_0 \\ 0, & \rho_i > \rho_0 \end{cases}$ | Repulsive potential (Khatib 1986) | Inside influence sphere | $\eta > 0$ |
| 5 | $F_{rep,i}(q) = \begin{cases} \eta\, \left(\tfrac{1}{\rho_i} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\, \tfrac{1}{\rho_i^{2}}\, \nabla \rho_i, & \rho_i \le \rho_0 \\ 0, & \rho_i > \rho_0 \end{cases}$ | Repulsive force vector | Repulsion stage | Diverges as $\rho_i \to 0$ |
| 6 | $U(q) = U_{att}(q) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} U_{rep,i}(q)$ | Total classic APF (Khatib) | Simple environments | **Not** a true NF |
| 7 | $F(q) = -\nabla U(q) = F_{att}(q) + \sum F_{rep,i}(q)$ | Total artificial force | Control input to robot | Vector sum |
| 8 | $\dot{q} = -\nabla U(q)$ | Gradient flow ODE | Continuous motion law | Lipschitz required |
| 9 | $q_{k+1} = q_{k} - \alpha_{k} \nabla U(q_{k})$ | Discrete gradient descent | Numerical simulation | Step size $\alpha_k > 0$ |
| 10 | $\varphi(q) = \dfrac{\lVert q - q_{goal} \rVert^{2}}{\left( \lVert q - q_{goal} \rVert^{2/k} + \beta(q) \right)^{k}}$ | Rimon-Koditschek NF (sphere world) | True navigation function | $k \geq$ workspace dimension |
| 11 | $\beta(q) = \prod_{i=0}^{m} \beta_{i}(q), \quad \beta_{0} = \lVert q - q_{goal} \rVert^{2}, \quad \beta_{i} = \lVert q - c_{i} \rVert^{2} - r_{i}^{2}$ | Extended obstacle function | Sphere-world NF construction | Product form |
| 12 | $\beta_i(q) \le 0 \iff q \in \mathcal{C}\mathcal{O}_{i}$ | Obstacle set membership | Distance to sphere boundary | Signed distance |
| 13 | $\kappa \geq \dim(\mathcal{C}) = n$ | Minimum tuning parameter | Ensures NF polar property | Critical lower bound |
| 14 | $\dot{q} = -K \nabla \varphi(q)$ | Navigation function flow | Closed-loop control law | $K$ positive definite gain matrix |

> [!NOTE]
> In the formulas above, $\lVert \cdot \rVert$ denotes the Euclidean norm. In LaTeX source you may write `\lVert` or `\Vert` to avoid the markdown-table-breaking vertical bar `|`.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The APF/NF framework underpins several practical robotics systems:
- **Mobile robot navigation in warehouses** (e.g., Amazon Kiva robots) where shelves are static obstacles.
- **Manipulator arm motion planning** in CNC and assembly lines.
- **Drone swarm collision avoidance** in GPS-denied indoor environments.
- **Autonomous vehicle lane keeping and pedestrian avoidance** (reactive layer of the planning stack).
- **Spacecraft rendezvous and proximity operations** where obstacles = other orbiting bodies.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Attractive Force from Quadratic Potential

**Step 1 — Define the potential energy of attraction.** We choose a smooth, strictly convex scalar function whose unique minimum lies at $q_{goal}$. The simplest such function is the squared Euclidean distance, scaled by a positive gain $k$:

$$
U_{att}(q) \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\, k\, \lVert q - q_{goal} \rVert^{2} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\, k\, (q - q_{goal})^{T}(q - q_{goal})
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the gradient (a row of partial derivatives).** Using the identity $\nabla \left( x^{T} x \right) = 2x$:

$$
\nabla U_{att}(q) \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\, k \cdot \nabla \left[ (q - q_{goal})^{T}(q - q_{goal}) \right] \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\, k \cdot 2(q - q_{goal})
$$

$$
\nabla U_{att}(q) \;=\; k\,(q - q_{goal})
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the definition of artificial force** $F = -\nabla U$ (force points "downhill", i.e., toward the goal):

$$
F_{att}(q) \;=\; -\nabla U_{att}(q) \;=\; -k\,(q - q_{goal}) \;=\; k\,(q_{goal} - q)
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret physically.** The attractive force is a *linear spring force*: proportional to the displacement error $(q_{goal} - q)$ with stiffness $k$. If $q$ is far from $q_{goal}$ (large error), the force is large; near the goal, the force is small — guaranteeing a smooth, critically-damped arrival.

**Step 5 — Conic variant (constant force at long range).** For some applications, we want a constant attractive force magnitude regardless of distance. The conic potential achieves this:

$$
U_{att}^{conic}(q) \;=\; k\, \lVert q - q_{goal} \rVert
$$

$$
F_{att}^{conic}(q) \;=\; -k \cdot \frac{q - q_{goal}}{\lVert q - q_{goal} \rVert}
$$

This produces a **unit-vector** force scaled by $k$, independent of distance.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Repulsive Force (Khatib, 1986)

**Step 1 — Define the distance to obstacle.** Let $\rho(q) = \lVert q - q_{obs} \rVert$ be the shortest distance from configuration $q$ to the boundary of an obstacle $\mathcal{C}\mathcal{O}$. Define a threshold $\rho_0 > 0$ beyond which the obstacle has no effect.

**Step 2 — Construct a potential that vanishes at $\rho_0$ and diverges at $\rho = 0$.** The standard Khatib choice is:

$$
U_{rep}(q) \;=\;
\begin{cases}
\tfrac{1}{2}\, \eta\, \left( \dfrac{1}{\rho(q)} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^{2}, & \rho(q) \le \rho_0 \\[2mm]
0, & \rho(q) > \rho_0
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 3 — Verify boundary conditions.**
- At $\rho = \rho_0$: $U_{rep} = \tfrac{1}{2}\eta(1/\rho_0 - 1/\rho_0)^2 = 0$. ✓
- At $\rho \to 0^+$: $U_{rep} \to \tfrac{1}{2}\eta(1/\rho)^2 \to \infty$. ✓ (Impassable barrier.)

**Step 4 — Compute the gradient with respect to $q$.** Note that $\rho(q) = \lVert q - q_{obs} \rVert$ implies $\nabla \rho = (q - q_{obs}) / \lVert q - q_{obs} \rVert$ (unit outward normal). Apply the chain rule:

$$
\nabla U_{rep}(q) \;=\; \eta \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \cdot \frac{d}{d\rho}\!\left(\frac{1}{\rho}\right) \cdot \nabla \rho
$$

$$
\frac{d}{d\rho}\!\left(\frac{1}{\rho}\right) \;=\; -\frac{1}{\rho^{2}}
$$

$$
\nabla U_{rep}(q) \;=\; \eta \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \cdot \left( -\frac{1}{\rho^{2}} \right) \cdot \frac{q - q_{obs}}{\rho}
$$

$$
\nabla U_{rep}(q) \;=\; -\,\eta \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \cdot \frac{1}{\rho^{3}} \cdot (q - q_{obs})
$$

**Step 5 — Repulsive force (the negative of the gradient):**

$$
F_{rep}(q) \;=\; -\nabla U_{rep}(q) \;=\; \eta \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \cdot \frac{1}{\rho^{3}} \cdot (q - q_{obs})
$$

This force **points away from the obstacle** (in the direction of $q - q_{obs}$, away from $q_{obs}$) and its magnitude grows as $\rho \to 0$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Rimon-Koditschek Navigation Function (Sphere World)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **mathematical heart of the topic** and a 14-mark KTU exam favorite. Understand the logic, not just the equation.

**Step 1 — Set up the sphere-world geometry.** Suppose we have $m+1$ disjoint closed balls $\mathcal{O}_0, \mathcal{O}_1, \ldots, \mathcal{O}_m$ in $\mathbb{R}^n$ with centers $c_i$ and radii $r_i$. The workspace is:

$$
\mathcal{F} \;=\; \mathbb{R}^{n} \setminus \bigcup_{i=0}^{m} \mathcal{O}_i
$$

The *goal* $q_{goal}$ is placed at the center of $\mathcal{O}_0$ (so the goal is "protected" by treating the rest of the world as one big obstacle boundary).

**Step 2 — Define the extended obstacle function $\beta_i(q)$** as a *signed* polynomial whose zero set is the obstacle boundary:

$$
\beta_{0}(q) \;=\; \lVert q - q_{goal} \rVert^{2} \quad \text{(goal, with sign reversal)}
$$

$$
\beta_{i}(q) \;=\; \lVert q - c_{i} \rVert^{2} - r_{i}^{2}, \quad i = 1, \ldots, m
$$

Note the sign convention: $\beta_i(q) \le 0 \iff q \in \mathcal{O}_i$ (interior of ball, signed distance squared).

**Step 3 — Form the product $\beta(q)$.** The function $\beta(q)$ vanishes on every obstacle boundary (the union of zero sets of $\beta_i$):

$$
\beta(q) \;=\; \prod_{i=0}^{m} \beta_{i}(q)
$$

**Step 4 — Construct the navigation function candidate as a ratio.** Define:

$$
\varphi(q) \;=\; \frac{\lVert q - q_{goal} \rVert^{2}}{\Bigl( \lVert q - q_{goal} \rVert^{2/k} + \beta(q) \Bigr)^{k}}
$$

where $k \geq n$ is a tuning parameter and $n$ is the dimension of the configuration space.

**Step 5 — Verify the four required properties.**

**(a) Smoothness:** Both numerator and denominator are smooth everywhere in $\mathcal{F}$. The denominator is strictly positive on $\mathcal{F}$ (since at least one $\beta_i > 0$ holds for any $q$ in the free space, because the obstacles are disjoint). Hence $\varphi \in C^{\infty}(\mathcal{F})$.

**(b) Admissibility:** As $q \to \partial \mathcal{F}$, at least one $\beta_i(q) \to 0^+$, while the numerator $\lVert q - q_{goal} \rVert^2$ remains bounded. So the denominator $\to 0^+$ and $\varphi \to 1$. At $q = q_{goal}$, the numerator $= 0$ and denominator $= \beta_0^{k}(q_{goal}) \cdot \prod_{i \neq 0} \beta_i^{k}(q_{goal}) > 0$, so $\varphi(q_{goal}) = 0$.

**(c) Polar (uniqueness of minimum):** For $k \geq n$, the function $\varphi$ has the **goal as its unique interior critical point**, and all other critical points lie on the workspace boundary. This is proved via Morse theory arguments on the *Hessian* — the parameter $k$ must be large enough to suppress spurious interior critical points.

**(d) Morse property:** With the proper choice of $k \geq n$, the Hessian $\nabla^{2} \varphi$ is non-degenerate at every critical point.

**Step 6 — Resulting motion law.** The robot follows the negated gradient of $\varphi$:

$$
\dot{q} \;=\; -K\, \nabla \varphi(q)
$$

For *almost every* initial configuration $q_0 \in \mathcal{F}$, the trajectory $q(t)$ converges to $q_{goal}$ as $t \to \infty$, **without ever entering any obstacle** $\mathcal{O}_i$.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (2-D Case)

**Problem Setup:** A 2-D point robot at $q_0 = (0, 0)$ must reach $q_{goal} = (5, 5)$. A single circular obstacle of radius $r_1 = 1$ is centered at $c_1 = (3, 3)$. The tuning parameter $k = 2$ (since $n = 2$). Use gains $\eta = 1.0$, $k_{att} = 1.0$.

**Step 1 — Compute distance to obstacle at the start.** $\rho_0 = \lVert (0,0) - (3,3) \rVert = \sqrt{18} \approx 4.243$.

**Step 2 — Initial attractive force:**

$$
F_{att} = k_{att}(q_{goal} - q) = 1.0 \cdot ((5,5) - (0,0)) = (5, 5)
$$

**Step 3 — Initial repulsive force** (assume $\rho_0 > \rho_1^{thresh}$, so $F_{rep} = 0$ initially). 

**Step 4 — Single integration step** with $\Delta t = 0.1$:

$$
q_1 = q_0 - \nabla U \cdot \Delta t = (0, 0) - (-5, -5) \cdot 0.1 = (0.5, 0.5)
$$

**Step 5 — Iterate** until $q$ reaches the goal. The trajectory will curve around the obstacle as $\rho$ decreases below the influence threshold, producing the smooth bypass visible in simulation traces.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — APF vs. Navigation Function

| Property | Classic APF (Khatib) | Navigation Function (Rimon-Koditschek) |
|---|---|---|
| Mathematical form | $U = U_{att} + U_{rep}$ (additive) | $\varphi = N / (D^{k})$ (ratio) |
| Local minima guarantee | **No** — can have spurious minima | **Yes** (only at goal, for $k \geq n$) |
| Convergence proof | None (empirical) | Rigorously proven via Morse theory |
| Construction difficulty | Easy | Hard (requires sphere-world geometry) |
| General obstacle shapes | Yes (any shape) | Only spheres (or sphere-like approximations) |
| Computational cost | Low | Moderate |
| KTU exam weight | High (basic definitions) | Very High (mathematical formulation) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Potential Field Navigation System

The Mermaid diagram below maps the **functional interactions** between the robot, the world model, the potential field generator, the gradient computer, and the motion controller. This is a system-level schematic — it captures the engineering flow without requiring a physical scene rendering.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robot Configuration Sensor q_k] --> B[World Model Module]
    B --> B1[Workspace Definition W]
    B --> B2[Obstacle Map M_obs]
    B --> B3[Goal Position q_goal]
    B1 --> C[Potential Field Generator U_q]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> C1[Attractive U_att]
    C --> C2[Repulsive U_rep]
    C --> C3[Total U_q or phi_q]
    C3 --> D[Gradient Computer nabla U]
    D --> D1[Attractive Force F_att]
    D --> D2[Repulsive Force F_rep]
    D --> D3[Total Artificial Force F_total]
    D3 --> E[Motion Controller]
    E --> E1[Velocity Command v_k]
    E1 --> F[Robot Actuator]
    F --> G[New Configuration q_{k+1}]
    G --> A
    E --> H{Convergence Check}
    H --> I[Dist to Goal less than epsilon]
    I --> J[Goal Reached Stop]
    H --> K[Continue Planning]
    K --> A
    style A fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#333
    style D fill:#ffd6cc,stroke:#333
    style E fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#333
    style J fill:#b3ffb3,stroke:#333
```

---

### 4.2 Decision Flow: APF vs. Navigation Function Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Planning Problem Given] --> Q1{Obstacles are spheres?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Want guaranteed convergence?}
    Q1 -->|No| A1[Use Classic APF with shape adaptation]
    A1 --> A2[May suffer local minima]
    Q2 -->|Yes| N1[Use Rimon-Koditschek NF]
    Q2 -->|No| N2[Use either APF or NF heuristically]
    N1 --> N3[Choose k greater than workspace dim n]
    N3 --> N4[Construct beta_i and beta]
    N4 --> N5[Build ratio form phi]
    N5 --> N6[Apply gradient descent dot q equals minus K nabla phi]
    N6 --> N7[Convergence to q_goal guaranteed]
    style S fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#333
    style N7 fill:#b3ffb3,stroke:#333
    style A2 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#333
```

---

### 4.3 Schematic: Sphere-World Geometry and the Navigation Function Surface

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FreeSpace["Free Space F"]
        G((q_goal = center of O0))
        R((Robot q at start))
        OF((Outside all O_i))
    end
    subgraph ObsRegion["Obstacle Union bigcup O_i"]
        O0((O0 Goal ball)) --- O1((O1 sphere)) --- O2((O2 sphere))
    end
    FreeSpace -->|phi decreases toward 0| G
    FreeSpace -->|phi increases toward 1 near boundary| OF
    R -->|Follow minus nabla phi| G
    R -.Avoid.-> O0
    R -.Avoid.-> O1
    R -.Avoid.-> O2
    style G fill:#b3ffb3,stroke:#006600
    style R fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#333
    style OF fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#660000
    style O0 fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#333
    style O1 fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#333
    style O2 fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#333
```

This schematic represents the **logical geometry** of a sphere world: the goal is embedded inside a "boundary" sphere $\mathcal{O}_0$, and the other obstacles are separate closed balls. The navigation function $\varphi$ is mathematically zero only at $q_{goal}$, rises smoothly through free space, and equals one on the boundary of any obstacle.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define an Artificial Potential Field (APF) for robot navigation. List any two advantages and one disadvantage of the APF method.*

**Model Answer:**

An **Artificial Potential Field (APF)** is a reactive path planning technique introduced by Oussama Khatib (1986) in which the robot's configuration space is treated as a scalar energy landscape. The total potential $U(q)$ is constructed as the sum of:
- An **attractive potential** $U_{att}(q)$ that pulls the robot toward the goal configuration, and
- A **repulsive potential** $U_{rep}(q)$ that pushes the robot away from obstacle boundaries.

The robot moves along the negative gradient of this potential: $\dot{q} = -\nabla U(q)$.

**Advantages:**
1. Mathematically simple and computationally efficient — only local gradient evaluation is needed per time step.
2. Naturally produces smooth, reactive trajectories that adapt in real time to dynamic obstacles.

**Disadvantage:**
1. The classic additive form $U = U_{att} + U_{rep}$ can produce **spurious local minima** in concave obstacle geometries, where the robot gets stuck before reaching the goal.

**(Mark distribution: 1.5 marks for definition, 1 mark for two advantages, 0.5 marks for disadvantage.)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the four properties that a function $\varphi: \mathcal{F} \to [0,1]$ must satisfy to be called a Navigation Function in the sense of Rimon and Koditschek.*

**Model Answer:**

A function $\varphi$ is a Navigation Function if and only if:

1. **Smoothness (Analyticity):** $\varphi$ is $C^2$ smooth (twice continuously differentiable) on the free space $\mathcal{F}$, ensuring the gradient and Hessian are well-defined and continuous.

2. **Admissibility:** $\varphi$ takes the value **1 on the boundary** $\partial \mathcal{F}$ of the workspace and approaches **0 only at the goal** $q_{goal}$.

3. **Polar Property (Uniqueness of Minimum):** $q_{goal}$ is the **unique global minimum** of $\varphi$ on $\mathcal{F}$. No other interior point attains the value 0.

4. **Morse Property:** All critical points of $\varphi$ are **non-degenerate** (the Hessian $\nabla^{2}\varphi$ has full rank at every critical point), ensuring all equilibria are isolated and the gradient flow has no continuum of stationary points.

A function satisfying all four conditions guarantees that **almost every** gradient flow trajectory of $-\nabla \varphi$ converges to $q_{goal}$ without leaving $\mathcal{F}$.

**(Mark distribution: 0.75 marks × 4 for each property statement.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Part B Module 3 internal choice means you must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B. Both are provided here for full preparation.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** For a 2-D point robot, derive the expression for the **attractive force** $F_{att}(q)$ obtained from a quadratic attractive potential $U_{att}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,k\,\lVert q - q_{goal}\rVert^{2}$, and explain how the choice of $k$ affects the robot's convergence behavior. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a circular obstacle of radius $r$ centered at $c$, with the robot at $q$, the Khatib repulsive potential is defined as:

$$
U_{rep}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\eta\, \left( \dfrac{1}{\rho(q)} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^{2}, \quad \rho(q) \le \rho_0
$$

where $\rho(q) = \lVert q - c \rVert - r$ is the signed distance to the obstacle boundary. **Derive the expression for the repulsive force** $F_{rep}(q) = -\nabla U_{rep}(q)$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A**

**Part (a) Solution — Attractive Force Derivation**

**Step 1** (1 mark): Define the quadratic attractive potential:

$$
U_{att}(q) \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\, k\, \lVert q - q_{goal} \rVert^{2} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\, k\, (q - q_{goal})^{T} (q - q_{goal})
$$

**Step 2** (2 marks): Compute the gradient using the standard identity $\nabla_{q}\left[ (q-a)^{T}(q-a) \right] = 2(q - a)$:

$$
\nabla U_{att}(q) \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\, k \cdot 2(q - q_{goal}) \;=\; k\,(q - q_{goal})
$$

**Step 3** (2 marks): Apply the force definition $F = -\nabla U$:

$$
F_{att}(q) \;=\; -k\,(q - q_{goal}) \;=\; k\,(q_{goal} - q)
$$

**Step 4** (2 marks): Discuss the effect of $k$:
- $k$ is the **stiffness gain** of an equivalent linear spring connecting $q$ to $q_{goal}$.
- **Larger $k$**: stronger pull toward the goal, faster convergence, but possible oscillation/overshoot near $q_{goal}$ and aggressive motion that may conflict with obstacle avoidance.
- **Smaller $k$**: smoother, more cautious motion, but slower convergence and weaker obstacle repulsion may be overwhelmed.
- In practice, $k$ is tuned empirically or via Lyapunov-based adaptive control to balance speed and stability.

---

**Part (b) Solution — Repulsive Force Derivation**

**Step 1** (1 mark): Note that for a circular obstacle, the distance function is $\rho(q) = \lVert q - c \rVert - r$. The gradient:

$$
\nabla \rho(q) \;=\; \frac{q - c}{\lVert q - c \rVert}
$$

is a unit vector pointing radially outward from the obstacle center.

**Step 2** (2 marks): Set up the chain rule. Let $g(\rho) = \tfrac{1}{2}\eta\bigl(\tfrac{1}{\rho} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0}\bigr)^{2}$. Then:

$$
\nabla U_{rep}(q) \;=\; \frac{d g}{d \rho} \cdot \nabla \rho
$$

**Step 3** (2 marks): Compute the derivative $\tfrac{dg}{d\rho}$:

$$
\frac{dg}{d\rho} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\eta \cdot 2\left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right) \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{\rho^{2}}\right) \;=\; -\eta \left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right) \frac{1}{\rho^{2}}
$$

**Step 4** (2 marks): Combine and apply the force definition:

$$
\nabla U_{rep}(q) \;=\; -\eta \left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right) \frac{1}{\rho^{2}} \cdot \frac{q - c}{\lVert q - c \rVert}
$$

$$
F_{rep}(q) \;=\; -\nabla U_{rep}(q) \;=\; \eta \left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right) \frac{1}{\rho^{2}} \cdot \frac{q - c}{\lVert q - c \rVert}
$$

This force is zero outside the influence distance ($\rho > \rho_0$) and diverges as $\rho \to 0^+$, creating a hard repulsive barrier that prevents obstacle collision.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** State the **Navigation Function Problem Statement** as formulated by Rimon and Koditschek. Clearly define the workspace, obstacle set, goal configuration, and the four required properties of the navigation function. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a **sphere world** containing $m+1$ disjoint closed balls $\mathcal{O}_0, \mathcal{O}_1, \ldots, \mathcal{O}_m$ with the goal $q_{goal}$ at the center of $\mathcal{O}_0$, write the **explicit construction** of the Rimon-Koditschek navigation function $\varphi(q)$. State the role of the tuning parameter $k$ and the condition on $k$ that guarantees the polar property. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B**

**Part (a) Solution — Problem Statement**

**Step 1** (2 marks): State the geometric setting. Let $\mathcal{F} \subset \mathbb{R}^{n}$ be a connected, compact, simply-connected region (the free space / workspace), populated with a finite collection of closed, pairwise-disjoint obstacle regions $\mathcal{O}_1, \mathcal{O}_2, \ldots, \mathcal{O}_m$ in the interior of $\mathcal{F}$. Let $q_{goal} \in \mathcal{F} \setminus \bigcup_{i=1}^{m} \mathcal{O}_i$ be the designated goal configuration.

**Step 2** (1 mark): State the problem. The Navigation Function Problem seeks a map:

$$
\varphi \;:\; \mathcal{F} \setminus \bigcup_{i=1}^{m} \mathcal{O}_{i} \;\longrightarrow\; [0, 1]
$$

such that the four required properties are satisfied simultaneously.

**Step 3** (4 marks): Enumerate the four properties:

| # | Property | Mathematical Statement |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Analyticity | $\varphi \in C^{2}(\mathcal{F} \setminus \bigcup \mathcal{O}_i)$ |
| 2 | Admissibility | $\varphi(q_{goal}) = 0$; $\varphi(q) \to 1$ as $q \to \partial \mathcal{F}$ or $q \to \partial \mathcal{O}_i$ |
| 3 | Polar | $q_{goal}$ is the unique global minimum; $\varphi(q) > 0$ for all $q \neq q_{goal}$ in $\mathcal{F} \setminus \bigcup \mathcal{O}_i$ |
| 4 | Morse | Hessian $\nabla^{2}\varphi$ is non-degenerate at every critical point |

**Step 4** (optional 0 marks, conceptual closure): A function satisfying all four properties ensures that the gradient flow dynamics $\dot{q} = -\nabla \varphi(q)$ steer *almost every* initial condition to $q_{goal}$ without entering any obstacle — mathematically eliminating the local-minimum problem of classical APFs.

---

**Part (b) Solution — Sphere-World NF Construction**

**Step 1** (2 marks): Define the extended obstacle functions. For each sphere $\mathcal{O}_i$ with center $c_i$ and radius $r_i$:

$$
\beta_{0}(q) \;=\; \lVert q - q_{goal} \rVert^{2} \quad \text{(goal function)}
$$

$$
\beta_{i}(q) \;=\; \lVert q - c_{i} \rVert^{2} - r_{i}^{2}, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, m
$$

Note: $\beta_i(q) \le 0$ iff $q \in \mathcal{O}_i$ (interior of sphere).

**Step 2** (1 mark): Form the product:

$$
\beta(q) \;=\; \prod_{i=0}^{m} \beta_{i}(q)
$$

**Step 3** (2 marks): Write the navigation function (ratio form):

$$
\varphi(q) \;=\; \frac{\lVert q - q_{goal} \rVert^{2}}{\Bigl( \lVert q - q_{goal} \rVert^{2/k} + \beta(q) \Bigr)^{k}}
$$

where $k$ is a positive tuning parameter.

**Step 4** (2 marks): Discuss the role and condition on $k$:
- **Role of $k$:** $k$ controls the *sharpness* of the navigation function near the workspace boundary and influences the topological structure of the critical points. Larger $k$ pushes spurious critical points toward the boundary, leaving only the goal as an interior minimum.
- **Condition for the polar property:** $k$ must satisfy $k \geq n$, where $n = \dim(\mathcal{C})$ is the dimension of the configuration space (e.g., $k \geq 2$ for a planar 2-D robot, $k \geq 3$ for a spatial 3-D robot).
- This condition is **sufficient but not necessary** in general; in some geometries smaller $k$ may suffice, but $k \geq n$ is the **guaranteed safe choice**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls to Avoid:**
>
> 1. **Conflating APF and NF:** Many students write "APF = Navigation Function." They are **NOT the same**. A Navigation Function is a *special* APF with mathematically guaranteed convergence. Mixing them costs full marks on theory questions.
> 2. **Forgetting the Morse condition:** When listing the four NF properties, students often omit "Morse / non-degenerate Hessian." This is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> 3. **Sign error in the gradient:** For $U_{att} = \tfrac{1}{2}k\lVert q - q_{goal}\rVert^2$, the gradient is $k(q - q_{goal})$ (not $-k(q - q_{goal})$). The minus sign belongs to the **force**, not the gradient. Read the question carefully.
> 4. **Wrong condition on $k$:** Writing $k > 0$ alone is insufficient; you **must** state $k \geq n$ (workspace dimension) for the polar property guarantee.
> 5. **No sketch in Part B questions:** KTU examiners award up to **1 mark** for a labeled sketch of the potential landscape, NF surface, or sphere-world geometry. Always include one.
> 6. **Confusing $\rho$ with $\lVert q - c \rVert$:** For a circular obstacle of radius $r$, the distance to the *boundary* is $\rho = \lVert q - c \rVert - r$, not $\lVert q - c \rVert$. This distinction often costs 1–2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)**

- [x] **APF = Khatib's 1986 method** = $U = U_{att} + U_{rep}$, with the robot following $-\nabla U$.
- [x] **Attractive potential** is **quadratic near the goal** ($\tfrac{1}{2}k\lVert q - q_{goal}\rVert^2$) or **conic far from the goal** ($k\lVert q - q_{goal}\rVert$).
- [x] **Repulsive potential** uses the **Khatib form** $\tfrac{1}{2}\eta(\tfrac{1}{\rho} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0})^{2}$, **active only inside** the influence sphere of radius $\rho_0$.
- [x] **Repulsive force** is **zero outside** $\rho_0$ and **diverges** as $\rho \to 0^+$ (hard barrier).
- [x] **Classic APF has local minima** — a known flaw in concave obstacle arrangements.
- [x] **Navigation Function (NF)** — Rimon & Koditschek (1992) — **eliminates** spurious local minima *mathematically*.
- [x] **Four NF properties** = **Analytic + Admissible + Polar + Morse** (memorize this acronym: **A-A-P-M**).
- [x] **Sphere world** = workspace is $\mathbb{R}^n$ minus a finite union of disjoint closed balls.
- [x] **Rimon-Koditschek NF formula** (ratio form): $\varphi = \lVert q - q_{goal}\rVert^2 / \bigl(\lVert q - q_{goal}\rVert^{2/k} + \beta(q)\bigr)^{k}$.
- [x] **Tuning parameter condition**: $k \geq n = \dim(\mathcal{C})$ ensures the polar property.
- [x] **Obstacle function** $\beta_i(q) = \lVert q - c_i\rVert^2 - r_i^2$ (signed squared distance to sphere).
- [x] **Product form** $\beta(q) = \prod_{i=0}^{m} \beta_i(q)$ vanishes on the entire obstacle boundary.
- [x] **NF boundary behavior**: $\varphi \to 0$ at $q_{goal}$ and $\varphi \to 1$ on $\partial \mathcal{F}$.
- [x] **Gradient flow dynamics**: $\dot{q} = -K \nabla \varphi(q)$ (control law for the robot).
- [x] **Convergence guarantee**: *almost every* initial condition reaches $q_{goal}$ (set of measure zero may not converge — non-wandering points on the boundary).
- [x] **Gains $k, \eta$** are positive scalars; $k_{att}$ too large → oscillation; $k_{rep}$ too large → oscillatory avoidance; both too small → sluggish motion.
- [x] **Difference from $\mathbf{A^*}$ / RRT**: APF/NF is a *reactive gradient-based* method (local) whereas $A^*$ and RRT are *deliberative graph/tree-based* methods (global).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Configuration Space
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT735 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Configuration Space

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Configuration Space (C-space):** The configuration space of a robot **A** moving in workspace **W** is the set of all possible transformations (placements) that the robot can attain. Formally, it is the space of all configurations **q** that completely specify the position of every point of the robot without ambiguity.

A **configuration** **q** is a minimal set of parameters (independent coordinates) that uniquely specifies the pose (position + orientation) of a robot at a given instant. The vector of these parameters is called a **configuration vector**, and the manifold on which it lies is the **configuration space**, denoted **C**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> - **Configuration (q):** An element of C-space; one specific placement of the robot.
> - **Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** The dimension of C-space, i.e., the minimum number of independent parameters needed to describe the configuration completely.
> - **Workspace (W):** The Euclidean space (ℝ² or ℝ³) in which the robot physically operates.
> - **C-obstacle (CO):** The set of all configurations **q** ∈ C such that the robot, when placed at **q**, collides with a workspace obstacle **O**.
> - **Free Space (C_free):** The set of collision-free configurations, defined as **C_free = C \ ⋃ CO_i**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Car Parking Problem

Imagine you are reversing a long sedan into a narrow parallel-parking slot between two other cars. As a human driver, you do not think of the *car as a rigid body occupying a region* — instead, you think of a single abstract point (the reference point of your car) and visualize an enlarged "danger zone" around the obstacles representing where your reference point **cannot** go.

That enlarged, transformed abstract space is exactly the **Configuration Space**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Central Idea:** By representing the robot as a single point, path planning in the cluttered physical world becomes path planning of a point in a higher-dimensional abstract space. The robot **shrinks to a point**, and the obstacles **grow** in a manner dictated by the robot's geometry and kinematics.

- In the **workspace**, the robot is a shape; the obstacle is a shape. Collision checking requires complex polygon/polytope intersection tests.
- In **C-space**, the robot is a point. Collision checking reduces to a simple point-in-region test.

This conceptual reduction is the **single most powerful abstraction** in modern motion planning (Lozano-Pérez, 1983) and forms the mathematical foundation of algorithms such as PRM, RRT, and A\*-in-C-space.

## 1.3 Why Configuration Space is Essential in Robot Navigation

| Aspect | Workspace View | Configuration Space View |
|---|---|---|
| Robot Representation | Geometric shape (polygon, polyhedron) | A point |
| Obstacle Representation | Fixed shapes | Grown/shrunk shapes (C-obstacles) |
| Collision Check | Shape–shape intersection | Point–region membership |
| Path | Continuous curve avoiding shapes | Continuous curve avoiding C-obstacles |
| Planning Complexity | Geometrically complex | Topologically structured |

## 1.4 Physical Quantities and Standard Metrics

- **Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** Dimension of C-space, denoted **n**. For a rigid body in 2-D plane: **n = 3**. For a rigid body in 3-D space: **n = 6**.
- **Gruebler's Formula Parameter:** **m** (number of links), **n** (number of joints), **fi** (DOF of joint i), **N** (number of links in a closed chain). Used for planar/serial mechanisms.
- **Topology of C-space:** Often non-Euclidean; common topologies include **ℝⁿ**, **S¹** (circle), **Tⁿ** (torus for revolute joints), **SO(3)** for 3-D rotation.
- **Standard Metric:** For C-space path length, the metric is **‖q₁ − q₂‖**, often the weighted Euclidean or geodesic metric on the manifold.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Concept — How C-Space is Constructed

The construction of C-space proceeds in three logically distinct stages:

**Stage 1 — Parameterize the Robot's Configuration**
Identify the minimum set of independent parameters required to specify the robot's pose.
- Point robot in 2-D: $q = (x, y)$ — so $C = \mathbb{R}^2$.
- Point robot in 3-D: $q = (x, y, z)$ — so $C = \mathbb{R}^3$.
- 2-D rigid body: $q = (x, y, \theta)$ — so $C = \mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$.
- 3-D rigid body: $q = (x, y, z, \phi, \psi, \theta)$ — so $C = \mathbb{R}^3 \times SO(3)$.

**Stage 2 — Define the Forward Kinematics Map**
The map $\mathcal{F}: C \to W$ assigns to each configuration $q$ the set of points in the workspace occupied by the robot:
$$\mathcal{F}(q) = \{ p \in W \mid p = f(q) \text{ for some point } p \text{ on the robot's body} \}$$
For a polygonal rigid body A at configuration $q$, $\mathcal{F}(q) = A(q)$ denotes the placement of A at $q$.

**Stage 3 — Compute the C-Obstacle as a Preimage**
For a workspace obstacle $O$, the C-obstacle is the set of configurations causing collision:
$$CO = \{ q \in C \mid \mathcal{F}(q) \cap O \neq \emptyset \}$$
Equivalently, using the inverse image:
$$CO = \mathcal{F}^{-1}(O)$$

The free space is therefore:
$$C_{\text{free}} = C \setminus \bigcup_{i=1}^{m} CO_i$$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU 2024 Scheme high-yield items — memorize before ESE.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | C-obstacle (general) | $CO_i = \{q \in C \mid \mathcal{F}(q) \cap O_i \neq \emptyset\}$ | Configurations that cause collision with obstacle $O_i$ |
| 2 | C-free space | $C_{\text{free}} = C \setminus \bigcup_{i=1}^{m} CO_i$ | All collision-free configurations |
| 3 | DOF (2-D rigid body) | $n = 3$ | $(x, y, \theta)$ |
| 4 | DOF (3-D rigid body) | $n = 6$ | $(x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi)$ |
| 5 | DOF (planar serial chain) | $n = \sum_{i=1}^{m} f_i$ | $m$ joints, $f_i$ DOF each |
| 6 | Grübler's Formula (planar) | $n = 3(m - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | $m$ = links, $j_1$ = 1-DOF joints, $j_2$ = 2-DOF joints |
| 7 | Grübler's Formula (spatial) | $n = 6(m - 1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$ | General 3-D chain |
| 8 | Topology of 2-D rigid body | $C = \mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$ | Translation × rotation |
| 9 | Topology of 3-D rigid body | $C = \mathbb{R}^3 \times SO(3)$ | Translation × rotation group |
| 10 | Topology of n-revolute planar arm | $C = T^n$ (n-torus) | $n$ revolute joints, each $S^1$ |
| 11 | Minkowski sum (C-obstacle) | $CO = O \oplus (-A)$ | $A$ = robot shape, $\oplus$ = Minkowski sum |
| 12 | Point distance to C-obstacle | $d(q, CO) = \min_{q' \in CO} \rho(q, q')$ | For collision-avoidance potential fields |
| 13 | Configuration distance metric | $\rho(q_1, q_2) = \sqrt{\sum_i w_i (q_{1,i} - q_{2,i})^2}$ | Weighted Euclidean on $C$ |
| 14 | Free path | $\tau: [0,1] \to C_{\text{free}}$ | Continuous collision-free curve from $q_s$ to $q_g$ |
| 15 | Workspace to C-space (point robot) | $CO_i = O_i$ (identity) | Pure translation only |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Distinction:** The C-obstacle is computed using the **Minkowski sum** $CO = O \oplus (-A)$ for a translating-only robot of shape $A$. For a translating **and** rotating robot, the C-obstacle is parameterized by the orientation $\theta$, giving a family $CO(\theta)$ that grows/shrinks with $\theta$.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility of C-Space

- **Path Planning Algorithms:** PRM (Probabilistic Roadmaps) and RRT (Rapidly-exploring Random Trees) sample points in C-space and connect them only through C-free.
- **Collision Avoidance:** Real-time potential field methods use the gradient $\nabla d(q, CO)$ in C-space.
- **Manipulation Planning:** Dual-arm and mobile manipulator planning occurs in the joint-space C-space of the entire mechanism.
- **SLAM and Autonomous Navigation:** C-space is implicitly used when integrating costmaps and trajectory optimizers (e.g., Frenet frames in autonomous driving).
- **Trajectory Optimization:** CHOMP, STOMP, and TrajOpt operate entirely in C-space using signed-distance fields on C-obstacles.

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why parameterize minimally?** Redundant parameters introduce unnecessary complexity and constraint equations.
- **Why compute the preimage?** It transforms a geometric problem (shape intersection) into a set-theoretic one (membership test).
- **Why C-free?** Path planning is fundamentally a search over reachable, valid states. C-free is exactly the valid-state set.
- **Why use topology?** C-space may be non-Euclidean (e.g., a torus), and this affects convergence of optimization and completeness of algorithms.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Degrees of Freedom of Common Robots

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example 1 — Point Robot in 2-D Plane**

**Step 1 — Identify Independent Parameters**
A point robot has no orientation; only its position matters.
$$q = (x, y)$$
Number of independent parameters: $n = 2$.

**Step 2 — Identify the C-space Manifold**
$$C = \mathbb{R}^2$$
The configuration space is the 2-dimensional Euclidean plane, topologically a smooth manifold without boundary.

**Step 3 — C-obstacle Computation**
Since the robot is a point, the C-obstacle is identical to the workspace obstacle:
$$CO_i = O_i$$
This is the trivial case, which is why point-robot path planning is the simplest to teach.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example 2 — 2-D Rigid Body (Translating + Rotating)**

**Step 1 — Independent Parameters**
- Two translations: $x, y \in \mathbb{R}$
- One rotation: $\theta \in [0, 2\pi)$ — topologically a circle $S^1$

**Step 2 — Configuration Vector**
$$q = (x, y, \theta) \in \mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$$
Dimensionality: $n = 3$.

**Step 3 — C-space Topology**
The Cartesian product of $\mathbb{R}^2$ and $S^1$ is a 3-D manifold. A point on $S^1$ identifies $\theta$ modulo $2\pi$:
$$\theta \equiv \theta + 2\pi k, \quad k \in \mathbb{Z}$$

**Step 4 — C-obstacle as Orientation-Dependent Set**
For a workspace obstacle $O$ and a 2-D rigid body $A$:
$$CO = \{ (x, y, \theta) \in \mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1 \mid A(x, y, \theta) \cap O \neq \emptyset \}$$
where $A(x, y, \theta)$ is the placement of $A$ at configuration $q$.

**Step 5 — Decomposition by Orientation**
For each fixed $\theta$, the slice $CO(\theta) \subset \mathbb{R}^2$ is a 2-D region. The full C-obstacle is:
$$CO = \bigcup_{\theta \in S^1} CO(\theta) \times \{\theta\}$$

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example 3 — Grübler's Formula for a 4-Bar Planar Linkage**

**Step 1 — Count Links**
A 4-bar linkage has 4 links: ground + 3 moving links. So $m = 4$.

**Step 2 — Count Joints**
Four revolute (1-DOF) joints. So $j_1 = 4$, $j_2 = 0$.

**Step 3 — Apply Planar Grübler's Formula**
$$n = 3(m - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

Substitute:
$$n = 3(4 - 1) - 2(4) - 0 = 9 - 8 = 1$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation**
The 4-bar planar mechanism has 1 DOF — only one input angle is needed to fully determine the configuration. The configuration space is $C = S^1$.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example 4 — Grübler's Formula for a 6-DOF Industrial Manipulator**

**Step 1 — Count Links**
7 links (base + 6 moving links): $m = 7$.

**Step 2 — Count Joints**
6 revolute joints, each with 1 DOF: $j_1 = 6$.

**Step 3 — Apply Spatial Grübler's Formula**
$$n = 6(m - 1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$$

Substitute (all joints are 1-DOF revolute, so $j_1 = 6$, others zero):
$$n = 6(7 - 1) - 5(6) - 0 - 0 - 0 - 0 = 36 - 30 = 6$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation**
The C-space is 6-dimensional: $C = T^6$ (a 6-torus), since each revolute joint contributes an $S^1$ factor and the base is fixed.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example 5 — Minkowski Sum C-Obstacle for a Translating Polygonal Robot**

**Step 1 — Setup**
A translating-only robot of shape $A$ (no rotation) moves in $\mathbb{R}^2$ with workspace obstacle $O$. Both $A$ and $O$ are polygons.

**Step 2 — Definition of Minkowski Sum**
$$A \oplus B = \{ a + b \mid a \in A, \, b \in B \}$$

**Step 3 — Configuration Map**
At configuration $q = (x, y)$, the robot's body is $A + (x, y)$.

**Step 4 — Collision Condition**
Collision occurs when:
$$A + (x, y) \cap O \neq \emptyset$$
Equivalently:
$$(x, y) \in O \oplus (-A)$$

**Step 5 — C-Obstacle**
$$\boxed{CO = O \oplus (-A) = \{ (x, y) \mid A + (x, y) \cap O \neq \emptyset \}}$$
where $-A$ is the reflection of $A$ through the origin: $-A = \{ -a \mid a \in A \}$.

**Step 6 — Geometric Interpretation**
$CO$ is the workspace obstacle $O$ "grown" by the shape of the robot. Equivalently, the robot is shrunk to a point and the obstacle is enlarged — the Minkowski-sum duality.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — DOF of Common Robot Types

| Robot Type | DOF $n$ | C-space Topology | Configuration Vector $q$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Point in 2-D | 2 | $\mathbb{R}^2$ | $(x, y)$ |
| Point in 3-D | 3 | $\mathbb{R}^3$ | $(x, y, z)$ |
| 2-D Rigid Body | 3 | $\mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$ | $(x, y, \theta)$ |
| 3-D Rigid Body | 6 | $\mathbb{R}^3 \times SO(3)$ | $(x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi)$ |
| Planar n-revolute arm | $n$ | $T^n$ (n-torus) | $(\theta_1, \ldots, \theta_n)$ |
| 4-bar planar linkage | 1 | $S^1$ | $(\theta_{\text{input}})$ |
| 6-DOF industrial arm | 6 | $T^6$ | $(\theta_1, \ldots, \theta_6)$ |
| SCARA (RRP) | 4 | $S^1 \times S^1 \times \mathbb{R} \times S^1$ | $(\theta_1, \theta_2, d_3, \theta_4)$ |
| Free-flying satellite | 6 | $\mathbb{R}^3 \times SO(3)$ | $(x, y, z, q_0, q_1, q_2, q_3)$ (with constraint) |

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Key C-Obstacle Properties

| Property | Mathematical Statement | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|
| Obstacle union | $CO_{\text{all}} = \bigcup_i CO_i$ | All collisions consolidated |
| Free space complement | $C_{\text{free}} = C \setminus CO_{\text{all}}$ | Search domain for path planners |
| Path definition | $\tau: [0,1] \to C_{\text{free}}$ | Continuous collision-free curve |
| Homotopy class | Two paths are equivalent if deformable in $C_{\text{free}}$ | Topological planning for narrow passages |
| Reachability | $q_g$ reachable from $q_s$ iff connected in $C_{\text{free}}$ | Feasibility test |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: C-Space Construction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robot Geometric Model A] --> C[Stage 1: Parameterize Configuration]
    B[Workspace Obstacles O_i] --> D[Stage 2: Define Forward Kinematics Map F]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Stage 3: Compute Preimage F_inverse of each O_i]
    E --> F[Construct C-Obstacles CO_i]
    F --> G[Compute C_free = C minus Union of CO_i]
    G --> H[Apply Path Planner: PRM / RRT / A*]
    H --> I[Output: Collision-free Path tau in C-space]
    I --> J[Map back to Workspace via F: real robot trajectory]
    J --> K[Execute on Physical Robot]
```

## 4.2 Topology Mapping Flow for Common Robots

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Point Robot 2D] --> T1[C = R^2, DOF=2]
    P2[Point Robot 3D] --> T2[C = R^3, DOF=3]
    P3[2D Rigid Body] --> T3[C = R^2 x S^1, DOF=3]
    P4[3D Rigid Body] --> T4[C = R^3 x SO 3, DOF=6]
    P5[Planar Revolute Arm n joints] --> T5[C = T^n, DOF=n]
    P6[4 Bar Planar Linkage] --> T6[C = S^1, DOF=1]
    P7[6DOF Industrial Arm] --> T7[C = T^6, DOF=6]
```

## 4.3 Workspace-to-C-Space Transformation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    WS[Workspace: Robot is a Shape A at Position p] --> TR[Apply Transformation: Shrink A to a Point, Grow O_i by Minkowski Sum with minus A]
    TR --> CS[C-Space: Robot is a Point, Obstacles are C-Obstacles]
    CS --> PLAN[Plan Path as Point Trajectory in C_free]
    PLAN --> INV[Apply Inverse Transformation F_inverse to get real Robot Trajectory]
    INV --> PHYS[Execute in Physical Workspace]
```

## 4.4 C-Obstacle Construction via Minkowski Sum

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Robot Shape A] --> REF[Reflect A through Origin: minus A]
    O[Workspace Obstacle O] --> MS[Minkowski Sum: O plus minus A]
    REF --> MS
    MS --> CO[Result: C-Obstacle CO in C-space]
    CO --> CF[Free Space C_free = C minus CO]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Block Diagrams over Physical Drawings:** Mermaid renders abstract functional flows far more reliably than attempting polygon/polyhedron artwork. The C-space concept is fundamentally a *mathematical transformation pipeline*, so a flow diagram captures its essence more precisely than a static picture.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

### Question 1. Define **Configuration Space (C-space)**. What is meant by the **degrees of freedom (DOF)** of a robot? Give the DOF of a 2-D rigid body and a 3-D rigid body.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Configuration Space** is the set of all possible configurations (placements) that a robot can attain in its workspace. A **configuration** is a minimal specification of the robot's pose, and the **DOF** is the dimension of this space — the minimum number of independent parameters needed to describe the configuration completely.

- **2-D rigid body:** $n = 3$ → $C = \mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$ with $q = (x, y, \theta)$.
- **3-D rigid body:** $n = 6$ → $C = \mathbb{R}^3 \times SO(3)$ with $q = (x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi)$.

*(1 mark: definition, 1 mark: DOF meaning, 1 mark: examples)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

### Question 2. What is a **C-obstacle**? Write its mathematical definition and explain the **C-free space**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **C-obstacle** is the set of all configurations of the robot that result in a collision with a workspace obstacle. Mathematically, for an obstacle $O_i$:
$$CO_i = \{ q \in C \mid \mathcal{F}(q) \cap O_i \neq \emptyset \}$$
where $\mathcal{F}(q)$ is the placement of the robot at configuration $q$.

**C-free space** is the set of all collision-free configurations:
$$C_{\text{free}} = C \setminus \bigcup_{i=1}^{m} CO_i$$
Path planning is performed as a search for a continuous path $\tau: [0,1] \to C_{\text{free}}$ between start $q_s$ and goal $q_g$. *(1 mark each for definition, formula of C-obstacle, and C-free explanation.)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 — Module 3 — Internal Choice Pattern]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the concept of **Configuration Space** for a 2-D rigid body translating and rotating in a workspace containing polygonal obstacles. Clearly state the C-space topology and the configuration vector. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the C-obstacle for a **translating-only polygonal robot** of shape $A$ moving in a workspace with obstacle $O$, using the **Minkowski sum** approach. Show that $CO = O \oplus (-A)$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) Configuration Space for a 2-D Rigid Body (7 marks):**

A 2-D rigid body has 3 DOF:
- Translation along $x$-axis: $x \in \mathbb{R}$
- Translation along $y$-axis: $y \in \mathbb{R}$
- Rotation about its reference point: $\theta \in [0, 2\pi)$ — topologically a circle $S^1$.

**Configuration vector:**
$$q = (x, y, \theta) \in \mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$$

**C-space topology:** $\mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$ — a 3-dimensional smooth manifold.

**Conceptual diagram (described):**
- *Workspace view*: The robot appears as a 2-D shape $A$ (e.g., rectangle) at a position with orientation $\theta$. The obstacle $O$ is a fixed polygon.
- *C-space view*: The robot shrinks to a single point. The C-obstacle $CO$ is an enlarged 3-D "tube" in $\mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$ whose cross-section at angle $\theta$ is the obstacle grown by the robot's rotated shape $A(\theta)$.

**Path planning** in C-space becomes the problem of finding a curve $\tau$ in $C_{\text{free}}$ from $q_s$ to $q_g$. (2 marks for vector + topology, 3 marks for diagram and explanation, 2 marks for path planning interpretation)

---

**(b) C-Obstacle Derivation via Minkowski Sum (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Configuration Map for Translating Robot**
Since the robot does not rotate, the configuration is $q = (x, y)$, and the placement of the robot is:
$$\mathcal{F}(x, y) = A + (x, y) = \{ a + (x, y) \mid a \in A \}$$

**Step 2 — Collision Condition**
The robot at $(x, y)$ collides with obstacle $O$ if and only if:
$$(A + (x, y)) \cap O \neq \emptyset$$

**Step 3 — Algebraic Manipulation**
There exist $a \in A$ and $o \in O$ such that:
$$a + (x, y) = o \quad \Leftrightarrow \quad (x, y) = o - a$$
The set of all such $(x, y)$ is:
$$\{ o - a \mid o \in O, \, a \in A \} = O \oplus (-A)$$
where $-A = \{ -a \mid a \in A \}$ is the reflection of $A$ through the origin.

**Step 4 — Final C-Obstacle**
$$\boxed{CO = O \oplus (-A)}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation**
Geometrically, $CO$ is the obstacle $O$ "grown" by the shape of the robot. The robot is shrunk to a point, and the obstacle is enlarged so that the point's collision test is equivalent to the original shape–shape test. (1 mark setup, 2 marks collision condition, 2 marks algebraic manipulation, 2 marks interpretation)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Define **Grübler's formula** for a planar mechanism. A planar 4-bar linkage has 4 links and 4 revolute joints. Using Grübler's formula, compute its DOF and state its C-space topology. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Minkowski sum operation** in the context of C-space construction. Illustrate with a worked example: a square robot of side 2 units translating in a 2-D workspace containing a square obstacle of side 4 units. Compute the C-obstacle dimensions. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) Grübler's Formula for 4-Bar Linkage (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Grübler's formula gives the DOF of a mechanism as a function of the number of links and joints. For a planar mechanism:
$$n = 3(m - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$
where $m$ = number of links, $j_1$ = 1-DOF joints, $j_2$ = 2-DOF joints.

**Given:** $m = 4$ (ground + 3 moving links), $j_1 = 4$ (four revolute joints), $j_2 = 0$.

**Substitution (3 marks):**
$$n = 3(4 - 1) - 2(4) - 0 = 9 - 8 = 1$$

**C-space topology (2 marks):** Since $n = 1$ and the only input is the angle of a revolute joint, the C-space is a 1-dimensional manifold homeomorphic to a circle:
$$C = S^1$$
This means the configuration of the linkage is fully determined by a single scalar parameter $\theta \in [0, 2\pi)$ (modulo $2\pi$).

**Interpretation (1 mark):** The 4-bar mechanism has 1 DOF, confirming it is a single-input mechanism — a classic result in kinematics.

---

**(b) Minkowski Sum — Worked Example (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** The Minkowski sum of two sets $A$ and $B$ in $\mathbb{R}^2$ is:
$$A \oplus B = \{ a + b \mid a \in A, \, b \in B \}$$

**Geometric interpretation (1 mark):** It is the set obtained by translating $B$ by every point of $A$ (or vice versa), i.e., $A$ "swept" by $B$.

**Worked Example (4 marks):**
- Robot: square $A$ of side $a = 2$, centered at origin (assume for simplicity).
- Obstacle: square $O$ of side $b = 4$.
- $-A$: reflection of $A$ — still a square of side 2.
- Minkowski sum $O \oplus (-A)$: a square of side $a + b = 2 + 4 = 6$ units.

**Computation justification:** The Minkowski sum of two axis-aligned squares with side lengths $a$ and $b$ is an axis-aligned square of side length $a + b$ (their convex hulls are also squares, and the sum of convex polygons is the convex hull of the sum of vertices).

**Result:**
$$\boxed{\text{C-obstacle is a square of side } 6 \text{ units.}}$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Equivalently, the obstacle has been "grown" from 4 to 6 units, allowing the robot (now a point) to navigate in the shrunken free space.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing C-space and Workspace:** Students often draw the robot as a shape *inside* C-space. C-space represents the robot as a **point**. Drawing the robot as a shape in C-space will cost **2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting the reflection in the Minkowski sum:** Writing $CO = O \oplus A$ instead of $O \oplus (-A)$ is a recurring error. The reflection $-A$ is mandatory; failure to apply it costs **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Mis-stating C-space topology:** Writing $C = \mathbb{R}^3$ for a 2-D rigid body (ignoring $S^1$) loses **1 mark**. Always include the rotational manifold.
> 4. **Grübler's formula sign errors:** Mixing planar and spatial formulas is common. Verify the multiplier — it is **3** for planar, **6** for spatial. Cost: **1–2 marks**.
> 5. **Not stating the configuration vector $q$ explicitly:** Examiners expect $q = (x, y, \theta)$ or its equivalent. Omitting it costs **1 mark**.
> 6. **Treating C-obstacles as static for rotating robots:** For a 2-D rigid body, $CO$ is **orientation-dependent** ($CO(\theta)$). Stating it as static loses **2 marks**.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 3 — Configuration Space):**

- ✅ **Configuration** = minimal set of independent parameters specifying the robot's pose.
- ✅ **Configuration Space (C)** = the manifold of all configurations; **dimension = DOF**.
- ✅ **DOF of common robots:** Point 2-D = 2, Point 3-D = 3, 2-D rigid body = 3, 3-D rigid body = 6, n-revolute planar arm = n.
- ✅ **C-space topologies:** $\mathbb{R}^2$, $\mathbb{R}^3$, $\mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$, $\mathbb{R}^3 \times SO(3)$, $T^n$ (n-torus), $S^1$.
- ✅ **C-obstacle** is the preimage: $CO_i = \{ q \in C \mid \mathcal{F}(q) \cap O_i \neq \emptyset \}$.
- ✅ **C-free** = $C \setminus \bigcup_i CO_i$; path planning searches for a continuous $\tau: [0,1] \to C_{\text{free}}$.
- ✅ **Minkowski sum formula** for translating polygonal robot: $CO = O \oplus (-A)$.
- ✅ **Grübler's formula (planar):** $n = 3(m-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$.
- ✅ **Grübler's formula (spatial):** $n = 6(m-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$.
- ✅ **Geometric duality:** Robot shrinks to a point; obstacles grow. C-space is **always** a point-robot formulation.
- ✅ **Workspace metric ≠ C-space metric**; use weighted Euclidean or geodesic distance.
- ✅ For **rotating robots**, $CO$ is a function of orientation $CO(\theta)$ — not a static 2-D set.
- ✅ **Path planning algorithms** (PRM, RRT, A\*) operate in C-space, not workspace.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Free Configuration Space
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT735 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Free Configuration Space

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Free Configuration Space (C-free):** Given a robot $\mathcal{A}$ operating in a 2D workspace $\mathcal{W} = \mathbb{R}^2$ containing a set of fixed obstacles $\mathcal{O}_i \subset \mathcal{W}$, the **Free Configuration Space** is mathematically defined as:
> $$C_{\text{free}} = C \setminus C_{\text{obs}} = \{q \in C \mid \mathcal{A}(q) \cap \mathcal{O} = \emptyset\}$$
> where $C$ is the total configuration space, $C_{\text{obs}}$ is the configuration space obstacle region, $\mathcal{A}(q)$ denotes the physical placement of the robot in the workspace when it is in configuration $q$, and $\mathcal{O} = \bigcup_{i} \mathcal{O}_i$ is the union of all workspace obstacles.

The **Configuration Space** (or **C-space**, denoted $C$) is the space of all possible configurations (positions and orientations) that a robot can attain. For a rigid body translating freely in a 2D plane, $C = \mathbb{R}^2$ (2 degrees of freedom). If the robot can also rotate, $C = \mathbb{R}^2 \times \mathbb{S}^1$ (3 DOF). For an $n$-joint planar manipulator, $C = \mathbb{S}^1 \times \mathbb{S}^1 \times \cdots \times \mathbb{S}^1$ ($n$ times).

A **configuration** $q$ is a complete specification of the robot's pose. A configuration is **collision-free** if the robot placed at $q$ does not intersect any obstacle. The set of all collision-free configurations is precisely $C_{\text{free}}$.

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $C$ | Total configuration space |
| $C_{\text{obs}}$ | Configuration space obstacle region |
| $C_{\text{free}}$ | Free configuration space (collision-free subset) |
| $\mathcal{A}(q)$ | Robot placed in workspace at configuration $q$ |
| $\mathcal{O}$ | Workspace obstacle region (union of all $\mathcal{O}_i$) |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Shrinking Room":**
> Imagine you are moving a large L-shaped sofa through a hallway that has a narrow pillar sticking out. From your (human) perspective, the hallway has just *one* obstacle — the pillar. But from the sofa's perspective, the "forbidden region" looks much bigger, because every point on the sofa's body must also avoid the pillar.
>
> **Free Configuration Space** is the trick of *shrinking the robot down to a single point* and *inflating the obstacles* by the robot's shape. Once you do this swap, you no longer have to worry about the robot's body — you just plan a point path. The set of all "safe" points for that shrunken robot is exactly $C_{\text{free}}$.

### Why C-free matters in Robot Navigation

- **Path planning becomes a point-planning problem.** Once the robot is reduced to a point and obstacles are grown by the Minkowski sum, finding a collision-free path in the workspace reduces to finding a continuous curve entirely within $C_{\text{free}}$.
- **It is a topological space**, allowing the use of powerful tools like homotopy, connectivity, and roadmap methods.
- **Path planning algorithms** (Visibility Graphs, PRM, RRT, Cell Decomposition, Potential Fields) all operate inside $C_{\text{free}}$ or its discretized approximations.

---

## 1.3 Physical / Geometric Constants and Metrics

- The standard Euclidean distance metric is used for distance computations: $d(p, q) = \sqrt{(p_x - q_x)^2 + (p_y - q_y)^2}$.
- For rotational components, angular distance is measured on $\mathbb{S}^1$, with a periodicity of $2\pi$ radians (**bold** = **$2\pi$ rad = $360°$**).
- The **Minkowski Sum** operator $\oplus$ is the fundamental geometric operator for constructing $C_{\text{obs}}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 scheme specifically tests the student's ability to (i) compute $C_{\text{obs}}$ for **circular and convex polygonal robots** against **convex polygonal/circular obstacles**, and (ii) identify $C_{\text{free}}$ graphically.

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

The construction of $C_{\text{free}}$ proceeds through a sequence of well-defined geometric operations. Below is the formal logical flow:

### Step 1 — Define the Workspace and Robot
- The robot $\mathcal{A}$ is a rigid body (or set of rigid links) defined in the workspace $\mathcal{W} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$ (or $\mathbb{R}^3$).
- A **reference point** (or **reference frame**) is attached to the robot, typically at its centroid, base, or end-effector.

### Step 2 — Define the Configuration
- A configuration $q$ parameterizes the position and orientation of the reference frame.
- For a translating circular robot of radius $r$, $q = (x, y) \in \mathbb{R}^2$.
- For a translating and rotating convex polygon, $q = (x, y, \theta) \in \mathbb{R}^2 \times \mathbb{S}^1$.

### Step 3 — Express the Robot as a Function of Configuration
- The set of points occupied by the robot in the workspace is $\mathcal{A}(q) \subseteq \mathcal{W}$.
- For a translating robot: $\mathcal{A}(q) = \{q\} \oplus \mathcal{A}_0$, where $\mathcal{A}_0$ is the robot's shape at the reference configuration.

### Step 4 — Construct the C-space Obstacle
- The C-space obstacle is the set of configurations where the robot *collides* with any obstacle:
$$C_{\text{obs}} = \{q \in C \mid \mathcal{A}(q) \cap \mathcal{O} \neq \emptyset\}$$

- This is computed using the **Minkowski sum** of the obstacle and the reflected robot shape:
$$C_{\text{obs}} = \mathcal{O} \oplus (-\mathcal{A}_0)$$
where $-\mathcal{A}_0 = \{-p \mid p \in \mathcal{A}_0\}$ is the reflection of the robot through its reference point.

### Step 5 — Extract the Free Configuration Space
- The remaining safe region is:
$$C_{\text{free}} = C \setminus C_{\text{obs}}$$

### Step 6 — Plan the Path
- A **collision-free path** from $q_{\text{start}}$ to $q_{\text{goal}}$ is a continuous map $\tau : [0, 1] \to C_{\text{free}}$ such that $\tau(0) = q_{\text{start}}$ and $\tau(1) = q_{\text{goal}}$.

---

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the Minkowski sum?** Geometrically, the set of positions from which a translated robot can collide with an obstacle is exactly the obstacle *grown* by the robot's shape. This is the textbook intuition: $C_{\text{obs}} = \mathcal{O} \ominus \mathcal{A}$ (or $\mathcal{O} \oplus (-\mathcal{A}_0)$ for reflected robot).
>
> **How does orientation enter?** When the robot can also rotate (e.g., a rectangular robot), the shape $\mathcal{A}_0$ changes with $\theta$. The C-obstacle becomes a *swept volume* over all $\theta$ that cause intersection. For a non-circular translating-rotating robot, $C_{\text{obs}}$ is a 3D region in $C = \mathbb{R}^2 \times \mathbb{S}^1$.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Configuration space | $C = \mathbb{R}^n$ (translating) or $C = \mathbb{R}^n \times \mathbb{S}^k$ (with rotation) | $n$ = translational DOF |
| 2 | Workspace obstacle | $\mathcal{O} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{m} \mathcal{O}_i$ | Geometric union |
| 3 | C-space obstacle | $C_{\text{obs}} = \mathcal{O} \oplus (-\mathcal{A}_0)$ | Minkowski sum of obstacle and reflected robot |
| 4 | **Free configuration space** | $C_{\text{free}} = C \setminus C_{\text{obs}}$ | Set difference; the planning domain |
| 5 | Minkowski sum | $A \oplus B = \{a + b \mid a \in A, b \in B\}$ | Both $A, B \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$ |
| 6 | Robot placement | $\mathcal{A}(q) = q \oplus \mathcal{A}_0$ (translation only) | $q$ is the reference-point location |
| 7 | Collision condition | $\mathcal{A}(q) \cap \mathcal{O} \neq \emptyset$ | Equivalent to $q \in C_{\text{obs}}$ |
| 8 | Path validity | $\tau : [0,1] \to C_{\text{free}}$ | Continuous map entirely inside $C_{\text{free}}$ |
| 9 | Circle–circle C-obstacle radius | $R_{\text{obs}} = r_{\text{robot}} + r_{\text{obstacle}}$ | For circular robot vs circular obstacle |
| 10 | Polygon C-obstacle | Vertex translation: each vertex $v_i$ of $\mathcal{A}_0$ maps to $v_i + p$ for $p \in \mathcal{O}$ | Generates $C_{\text{obs}}$ boundary |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Substitution Rule:** Always use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute-value bars inside markdown tables. The pipe `|` character breaks markdown table syntax.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of C-free |
|---|---|
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | The drivable corridor in the road is precisely $C_{\text{free}}$, while curbs, other cars, and pedestrians form $C_{\text{obs}}$. |
| **Industrial Manipulators** | Joint-space planning uses $C_{\text{free}}$ to avoid self-collisions and collisions with fixtures. |
| **Warehouse Robotics (AMR)** | Maps are discretized into $C_{\text{free}}$ grids; path planners (A*, D*) search within them. |
| **Surgical Robotics** | The needle-tip C-space is restricted by the patient's anatomy, which becomes $C_{\text{obs}}$. |
| **Aerial Swarms (UAVs)** | $C_{\text{free}}$ includes 3D translational + yaw configuration. |
