topics.title: Gradient Descent without and with Constraints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT735 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Gradient Descent without and with Constraints

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gradient Descent** is a first-order iterative optimization algorithm used to find the **local minimum** of a differentiable scalar-valued cost function $J(\mathbf{x}): \mathbb{R}^n \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$. The algorithm updates the parameter vector $\mathbf{x}$ in the direction of the **negative gradient** $-\nabla J(\mathbf{x})$, which corresponds to the direction of steepest *descent* on the cost surface.

In the context of **Robot Navigation (PERUT735)**, gradient descent is a foundational computational tool used to minimize cost functions representing:
- **Path length** between a start and goal configuration.
- **Energy expenditure** during trajectory execution.
- **Obstacle potential fields** in potential field methods.
- **Tracking error** between actual and reference trajectories.

The mathematical statement of the unconstrained problem is:

$$\min_{\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n} J(\mathbf{x})$$

where the *unconstrained* notation signifies that the design variable $\mathbf{x}$ is free to take any value in $\mathbb{R}^n$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Module 3 objective explicitly demands understanding the role of gradient methods in generating collision-free and energy-optimal trajectories. Gradient descent is the *numerical engine* that powers this generation.

### 1.2 Constrained Gradient Descent — The Generalization

When physical, geometric, or dynamic restrictions exist, the problem becomes:

$$\min_{\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n} J(\mathbf{x}) \quad \text{subject to} \quad g_i(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \; h_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$$

For robot navigation, these constraints typically model:
- **Joint limits** of manipulators ($h_j(\mathbf{q}) \leq 0$).
- **Obstacle avoidance** regions ($h_j(\mathbf{q}) \leq 0$ defines a forbidden half-space).
- **Non-holonomic motion constraints** for wheeled robots ($g_i(\mathbf{x}, \dot{\mathbf{x}}) = 0$).
- **Initial and final pose** boundary conditions ($g_i(\mathbf{x}) = 0$).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Blind Mountain Hiker"

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine you are a hiker stranded on a foggy mountain at sunset, and you must reach the valley floor (the minimum). You cannot see the terrain, but you can feel the slope of the ground beneath your feet. The **gradient** $\nabla J$ is the slope — it tells you which direction is *uphill*. Since you want to go *down*, you step in the **opposite** direction, $-\nabla J$. The **step size** (learning rate $\alpha$) is how large a stride you take. Too small a step, and you take forever. Too large, and you may overshoot the valley and end up on another peak.

**Mapping the analogy to robot navigation:**

| Mountain Hiking Concept | Robot Navigation Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Mountain surface $J(\mathbf{x})$ | Cost map of the workspace |
| Hiker’s position $\mathbf{x}$ | Robot configuration $\mathbf{q}$ |
| Slope $\nabla J$ | Gradient of obstacle + goal potential |
| Path constraint (e.g., no cliffs) | Joint limits / obstacle constraints |
| Step size $\alpha$ | Learning rate / gain $K$ |

### 1.4 Why Gradient Descent is Pivotal in Robot Navigation

- **Computational efficiency** — only first-order information is required, ideal for real-time control loops operating at 100 Hz or higher.
- **Local optimality** — guaranteed to descend, suitable when the cost surface is well-behaved (e.g., convex artificial potentials).
- **Compatibility with constraints** — extends naturally to **Lagrangian** and **KKT** frameworks for constrained problems.
- **Foundation for advanced methods** — Newton’s method, SQP, and modern trajectory optimization all generalize gradient descent.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Unconstrained Gradient Descent Algorithm — Step-by-Step Logic

The algorithmic procedure to minimize $J(\mathbf{x})$ unconstrained is logically decomposed as follows:

1. **Initialization:** Choose an initial guess $\mathbf{x}^{(0)} \in \mathbb{R}^n$, a step-size (learning rate) $\alpha > 0$, and a tolerance $\epsilon > 0$.
2. **Gradient Evaluation:** Compute the gradient $\nabla J(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})$ at the current iterate.
3. **Direction Selection:** The descent direction is $\mathbf{d}^{(k)} = -\nabla J(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})$. This is mathematically proven to be the direction of *steepest descent* because it points along the negative of the gradient — the direction of maximum rate of decrease of $J$.
4. **Update Step:** Move along the descent direction:
   $$\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)} = \mathbf{x}^{(k)} + \alpha^{(k)} \mathbf{d}^{(k)} = \mathbf{x}^{(k)} - \alpha^{(k)} \nabla J(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})$$
5. **Convergence Test:** Terminate when $\Vert \nabla J(\mathbf{x}^{(k)}) \Vert \leq \epsilon$ (first-order necessary condition) or when the relative change in cost is below a threshold.
6. **Iteration:** Otherwise set $k \leftarrow k+1$ and return to Step 2.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Why behind each step:**
> - Step 3 ensures **monotonic cost reduction**: $J(\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)}) \leq J(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})$ for sufficiently small $\alpha$.
> - The learning rate $\alpha^{(k)}$ may be held **fixed** or determined by **line search** (Armijo, Goldstein, or Wolfe conditions).
> - Convergence to a *local* minimum is guaranteed under Lipschitz continuity of $\nabla J$.

### 2.2 Steepest Descent Direction — Mathematical Justification

Among *all* unit vectors $\mathbf{u}$ (where $\Vert \mathbf{u} \Vert = 1$), the direction that minimizes the directional derivative $D_{\mathbf{u}}J = \nabla J^{\top} \mathbf{u}$ is found by Cauchy-Schwarz inequality:

$$\nabla J^{\top} \mathbf{u} \geq -\Vert \nabla J \Vert \cdot \Vert \mathbf{u} \Vert = -\Vert \nabla J \Vert$$

Equality is achieved when $\mathbf{u} = -\nabla J / \Vert \nabla J \Vert$. This proves the gradient direction is the *steepest descent* direction.

### 2.3 Constrained Optimization — Lagrange Multiplier Method

For a problem with **equality constraints**:

$$\min J(\mathbf{x}) \quad \text{subject to} \quad g(\mathbf{x}) = 0$$

Construct the **Lagrangian** function:

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}) = J(\mathbf{x}) + \boldsymbol{\lambda}^{\top} g(\mathbf{x})$$

The first-order **necessary conditions** for a stationary point $(\mathbf{x}^*, \boldsymbol{\lambda}^*)$ are:

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} \mathcal{L} = \nabla J(\mathbf{x}^*) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} \lambda_i^* \nabla g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$$

$$g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0, \quad \forall i = 1, \ldots, m$$

### 2.4 Inequality Constraints — The KKT Conditions

For problems with **mixed constraints**:

$$\min J(\mathbf{x}) \quad \text{s.t.} \quad g_i(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \; h_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$$

The **Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) conditions** state that at a local optimum $\mathbf{x}^*$, there exist multipliers $(\boldsymbol{\lambda}^*, \boldsymbol{\mu}^*)$ such that:

$$\nabla J(\mathbf{x}^*) + \sum_{i} \lambda_i^* \nabla g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) + \sum_{j} \mu_j^* \nabla h_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$$

$$g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0, \quad \forall i$$

$$h_j(\mathbf{x}^*) \leq 0, \quad \forall j$$

$$\mu_j^* \geq 0, \quad \forall j$$

$$\mu_j^* h_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0, \quad \forall j \quad \text{(complementary slackness)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why KKT matters in Robot Navigation:** When a robot's configuration lies in free space, $\mu_j = 0$ (inactive constraint) and the robot follows pure gradient descent of the goal potential. When the robot approaches an obstacle boundary, the corresponding $\mu_j > 0$ becomes *active*, repelling the robot tangentially — this is the mathematical essence of **potential field path planning**.

### 2.5 Projected Gradient Descent (A Computationally Tractable Method)

When the feasible set $\mathcal{C}$ is convex, the **projected gradient** method provides a simple way to enforce constraints:

$$\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)} = \Pi_{\mathcal{C}}\left(\mathbf{x}^{(k)} - \alpha^{(k)} \nabla J(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})\right)$$

where $\Pi_{\mathcal{C}}(\cdot)$ denotes the **Euclidean projection** onto the feasible set $\mathcal{C}$:

$$\Pi_{\mathcal{C}}(\mathbf{y}) = \arg\min_{\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{C}} \tfrac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{y} \Vert^2$$

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Condition | Expression | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Unconstrained Update Rule | $\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)} = \mathbf{x}^{(k)} - \alpha \nabla J(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})$ | Generic path cost minimization |
| 2 | Gradient Direction | $\mathbf{d}^{(k)} = -\nabla J(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})$ | Steepest descent direction |
| 3 | First-Order Optimality | $\nabla J(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$ | Termination criterion |
| 4 | Lagrangian (Equality) | $\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}) = J + \boldsymbol{\lambda}^{\top} g$ | Constraint incorporation |
| 5 | KKT Stationarity | $\nabla J + \sum \lambda_i \nabla g_i + \sum \mu_j \nabla h_j = 0$ | Mixed constraint optimum |
| 6 | Complementary Slackness | $\mu_j \cdot h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ | Active vs. inactive status |
| 7 | Projected Gradient | $\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)} = \Pi_{\mathcal{C}}(\mathbf{x}^{(k)} - \alpha \nabla J)$ | Simple box / sphere constraints |
| 8 | Backtracking Line Search | $\alpha^{(k)} = \tau \alpha^{(k-1)}$ until Armijo holds | Adaptive step size |
| 9 | Convergence Rate (Convex) | $\Vert \mathbf{x}^{(k)} - \mathbf{x}^* \Vert^2 \leq \mathcal{O}(1/k)$ | Theoretical guarantee |
| 10 | Conjugate Gradient Direction | $\mathbf{d}^{(k)} = -\nabla J + \beta_k \mathbf{d}^{(k-1)}$ | Faster convergence |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Real-time lane-keeping and obstacle-avoidance use projected gradient on a constrained cost surface.
- **Industrial Manipulators:** Joint-limit constraints $q_{\min} \leq q \leq q_{\max}$ are box constraints; projected gradient enforces them at every step.
- **Drone Swarms:** Distributed gradient descent under collision-avoidance constraints scales to fleets of 100+ UAVs.
- **Humanoid Robots:** Zero-Moment-Point (ZMP) stability is enforced as an inequality constraint during online gait optimization.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Why the Negative Gradient is the Descent Direction

**Problem Setup:** For a scalar function $J(\mathbf{x})$ differentiable at $\mathbf{x}^{(k)}$, consider a small perturbation $\delta \mathbf{x}$ from $\mathbf{x}^{(k)}$. The first-order Taylor expansion of $J$ is:

$$J(\mathbf{x}^{(k)} + \delta \mathbf{x}) \approx J(\mathbf{x}^{(k)}) + \nabla J(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})^{\top} \delta \mathbf{x}$$

We wish to find $\delta \mathbf{x}$ of fixed length $\Vert \delta \mathbf{x} \Vert = \alpha$ that **minimizes** the change in $J$, i.e., minimizes $\nabla J^{\top} \delta \mathbf{x}$.

**Step 1 — Normalize the search direction.** Let $\mathbf{d} = \delta \mathbf{x} / \Vert \delta \mathbf{x} \Vert$ be a unit vector, so $\delta \mathbf{x} = \alpha \mathbf{d}$ with $\Vert \mathbf{d} \Vert = 1$. The change in cost is:

$$\Delta J \approx \nabla J^{\top} (\alpha \mathbf{d}) = \alpha \, \nabla J^{\top} \mathbf{d}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality.** For any unit vector $\mathbf{d}$:

$$\nabla J^{\top} \mathbf{d} \geq -\Vert \nabla J \Vert \cdot \Vert \mathbf{d} \Vert = -\Vert \nabla J \Vert$$

**Step 3 — Identify the minimizing direction.** Equality in Cauchy-Schwarz holds if and only if $\mathbf{d}$ is anti-parallel to $\nabla J$, i.e.:

$$\mathbf{d}^* = -\frac{\nabla J}{\Vert \nabla J \Vert}$$

**Step 4 — Write the update rule.** Substituting $\delta \mathbf{x} = \alpha \mathbf{d}^* = -\alpha \nabla J / \Vert \nabla J \Vert$ into $\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)} = \mathbf{x}^{(k)} + \delta \mathbf{x}$:

$$\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)} = \mathbf{x}^{(k)} - \alpha \frac{\nabla J(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})}{\Vert \nabla J(\mathbf{x}^{(k)}) \Vert}$$

If we absorb the normalization into the scalar step size and define $\eta = \alpha / \Vert \nabla J \Vert$, we recover the canonical form:

$$\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)} = \mathbf{x}^{(k)} - \eta \, \nabla J(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})$$

This is the **steepest descent update rule**, and it is the unique direction that produces the largest *local* cost reduction per unit step length.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Lagrangian Stationarity from Constrained First-Order Optimality

**Problem:** Minimize $J(\mathbf{x})$ subject to $g(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ (a single equality for clarity; the proof extends to multiple constraints by linearity).

**Step 1 — Taylor expansion of the cost along feasible perturbations.** A perturbation $\delta \mathbf{x}$ is *feasible* if it satisfies the linearized constraint $\nabla g(\mathbf{x}^*)^{\top} \delta \mathbf{x} = 0$ (this is the first-order necessary condition for remaining on the constraint surface). The change in cost is:

$$\Delta J = \nabla J(\mathbf{x}^*)^{\top} \delta \mathbf{x} \quad \text{to first order}$$

**Step 2 — Stationarity requirement.** If $\mathbf{x}^*$ is a constrained local minimum, then for *every* feasible $\delta \mathbf{x}$:

$$\Delta J \geq 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \nabla J(\mathbf{x}^*)^{\top} \delta \mathbf{x} \geq 0$$

**Step 3 — Use the Lagrange multiplier ansatz.** Introduce $\lambda$ and form the augmented change:

$$\Delta J + \lambda \cdot \Delta g = (\nabla J + \lambda \nabla g)^{\top} \delta \mathbf{x} \quad \text{with} \quad \Delta g = \nabla g^{\top} \delta \mathbf{x} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Demand the inner product vanish for all $\delta \mathbf{x}$.** The only way $(\nabla J + \lambda \nabla g)^{\top} \delta \mathbf{x} = 0$ for arbitrary $\delta \mathbf{x}$ is:

$$\nabla J(\mathbf{x}^*) + \lambda \nabla g(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$$

**Step 5 — Conclude.** Combined with the constraint $g(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$, the **Lagrangian stationarity conditions** are:

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} \mathcal{L} = 0, \quad g(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$$

where $\mathcal{L} = J + \lambda g$. This justifies the KKT stationarity equation and reveals the multiplier $\lambda$ as the *sensitivity* of the optimal cost to constraint relaxation.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — KKT Conditions from the Lagrangian of a Robot Path-Planning Problem

**Setup.** Consider a planar point-mass robot at position $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2)$ navigating toward a goal $\mathbf{x}_g$ while avoiding a circular obstacle of radius $r$ centered at $\mathbf{x}_o$. Define:

$$J(\mathbf{x}) = \tfrac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_g \Vert^2 \quad \text{(goal-attraction cost)}$$

$$h(\mathbf{x}) = r^2 - \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_o \Vert^2 \leq 0 \quad \text{(obstacle: inside circle is forbidden)}$$

**Step 1 — Form the Lagrangian for the inequality-constrained problem:**

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}, \mu) = \tfrac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_g \Vert^2 + \mu \left[ r^2 - \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_o \Vert^2 \right]$$

**Step 2 — Compute the gradients:**

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} \mathcal{L} = (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_g) - 2 \mu (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_o) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the optimal $\mathbf{x}^*$:**

$$\mathbf{x}^* - \mathbf{x}_g - 2 \mu (\mathbf{x}^* - \mathbf{x}_o) = 0$$

$$\mathbf{x}^* (1 - 2\mu) = \mathbf{x}_g - 2\mu \mathbf{x}_o$$

$$\mathbf{x}^* = \frac{\mathbf{x}_g - 2\mu \mathbf{x}_o}{1 - 2\mu}$$

**Step 4 — Apply complementary slackness:** Either the obstacle is *inactive* ($\mu = 0$) yielding $\mathbf{x}^* = \mathbf{x}_g$ (go straight to the goal), or it is *active* ($\mu > 0$ and $h(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$) yielding the boundary-tangent equilibrium.

**Step 5 — The active case:** Set $h(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$:

$$\Vert \mathbf{x}^* - \mathbf{x}_o \Vert^2 = r^2$$

Substituting $\mathbf{x}^* = \mathbf{x}_o + (\mathbf{x}_g - \mathbf{x}_o)/(1 - 2\mu) \cdot \text{projection}$ and solving gives the constrained equilibrium $\mathbf{x}^*$ lying on the obstacle boundary. This $\mathbf{x}^*$ is the tangent-touch point — exactly the geometry used in **Artificial Potential Field** path planning for collision-free navigation.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm Selection for Robot Navigation

| Algorithm | Constraint Type | Convergence | Computational Cost | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Steepest Descent** | Unconstrained | Linear | Low (1st order) | Smooth, unconstrained cost |
| **Newton's Method** | Unconstrained | Quadratic (locally) | High (2nd order) | High-precision planning, small $n$ |
| **Projected Gradient** | Convex set | Linear | Low | Box / sphere joint limits |
| **Lagrangian (Equality)** | $g(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ | Superlinear | Moderate | Hard kinematic constraints |
| **KKT / Penalty Method** | $h(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$ | Linear–superlinear | Moderate | Obstacle avoidance |
| **Sequential Quadratic Prog.** (SQP) | General nonlinear | Superlinear | High | Real-time MPC of robots |
| **Augmented Lagrangian** | Mixed | Robust | Moderate | Ill-conditioned constraints |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Flow — Unconstrained Gradient Descent Algorithm

The diagram below maps the iterative control flow of the canonical gradient descent routine as applied inside a robot's trajectory optimization loop.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize x0, alpha, epsilon] --> B[Compute cost J at xk]
    B --> C[Compute gradient nabla J at xk]
    C --> D{Gradient norm less than epsilon}
    D -- Yes --> E[Return x star as solution]
    D -- No --> F[Compute descent direction dk = minus nabla J]
    F --> G[Select step size alpha k via line search]
    G --> H[Update xk plus 1 = xk plus alpha k dk]
    H --> I[Increment iteration counter k]
    I --> B
```

### 4.2 Block Architecture — Constrained Optimization via Lagrangian / KKT Pipeline

This schematic illustrates the architectural flow of a constrained gradient solver as used in a robot navigation stack: the unconstrained descent direction is modified by the multiplier feedback from each active constraint.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: xk, lambda, mu] --> B[Compute J at xk]
    B --> C[Compute nabla J at xk]
    C --> D[Compute nabla g and nabla h at xk]
    D --> E[Form KKT stationarity residual]
    E --> F{Residual norm less than tol}
    F -- Yes --> G[Converged: Return optimal x star]
    F -- No --> H[Compute descent direction d from Lagrangian gradient]
    H --> I[Project or correct d onto feasible set]
    I --> J[Update xk plus 1 using projected step]
    J --> K[Update multipliers lambda plus and mu plus]
    K --> L[Increment k]
    L --> A
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Map — Gradient Descent Variants vs. Constraint Type

```mermaid
graph LR
    Root[Optimization Problem] --> A1[Unconstrained]
    Root --> A2[Constrained]

    A1 --> B1[Steepest Descent]
    A1 --> B2[Newton Method]
    A1 --> B3[Conjugate Gradient]

    A2 --> C1[Equality g of x equals 0]
    A2 --> C2[Inequality h of x less or equal 0]
    A2 --> C3[Set Membership x in C]

    C1 --> D1[Lagrange Multipliers]
    C2 --> D2[KKT Conditions]
    C2 --> D3[Penalty and Barrier Methods]
    C3 --> D4[Projected Gradient Descent]

    B1 --> E1[Use case: Goal only]
    D2 --> E2[Use case: Obstacle avoidance]
    D4 --> E3[Use case: Joint limit box]
```

### 4.4 Robot Navigation Application Block Diagram

The functional role of gradient descent in a complete robot navigation pipeline is shown below.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sensor Inputs: Lidar, Cameras, IMU] --> B[World Model: Cost Map J of x]
    B --> C[Gradient Solver: Compute nabla J]
    C --> D{Constraint Module}
    D -- Joint Limits --> D1[Project onto Box Feasible Set]
    D -- Obstacles --> D2[Active Set KKT Adjustment]
    D -- Dynamics --> D3[Lagrangian Multiplier Correction]
    D1 --> E[Final Velocity Command v star]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Low Level Motor Controller]
    F --> G[Robot Actuator Output]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading Tip for Examiners:** When drawing schematic diagrams in your answer script, label the *flow direction* and the *mathematical operator* at each block. This earns the design-clarity marks that KTU examiners specifically award.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**
*State the steepest descent update rule and explain why the negative gradient direction yields the maximum local decrease in the cost function.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The steepest descent update rule is:

$$\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)} = \mathbf{x}^{(k)} - \alpha^{(k)} \nabla J(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})$$

To see why $-\nabla J$ is the optimal direction, consider any unit vector $\mathbf{d}$. By the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality:

$$\nabla J^{\top} \mathbf{d} \geq -\Vert \nabla J \Vert$$

with equality when $\mathbf{d} = -\nabla J / \Vert \nabla J \Vert$. Since the change in cost is $\Delta J \approx \alpha \nabla J^{\top} \mathbf{d}$, the most negative $\Delta J$ is achieved when $\mathbf{d}$ is anti-parallel to $\nabla J$. Hence the negative gradient is the **direction of steepest descent**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**
*Write the KKT stationarity, primal feasibility, dual feasibility, and complementary slackness conditions for the problem $\min J(\mathbf{x})$ subject to $g(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ and $h(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Stationarity:** $\nabla J(\mathbf{x}^*) + \sum_i \lambda_i^* \nabla g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) + \sum_j \mu_j^* \nabla h_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$
2. **Primal Feasibility:** $g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0, \; h_j(\mathbf{x}^*) \leq 0$
3. **Dual Feasibility:** $\mu_j^* \geq 0$
4. **Complementary Slackness:** $\mu_j^* h_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$

These four sets together form the KKT necessary conditions for a local optimum under constraint qualifications (e.g., LICQ).

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module 3, Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU ESE Part B Module-Internal Choice: Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B, each carrying 14 marks split as 7 + 7.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper Pattern]**

(a) **[7 Marks]** *Derive the steepest descent update rule from the first-order Taylor expansion of a cost function $J(\mathbf{x})$ and the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality. Clearly state the role of the step size $\alpha$.*

(b) **[7 Marks]** *A planar robot must reach the goal $\mathbf{x}_g = (5, 5)$ from start $\mathbf{x}_0 = (0, 0)$ while avoiding a circular obstacle centered at $\mathbf{x}_o = (2, 2)$ with radius $r = 1$. Using the cost $J(\mathbf{x}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_g \Vert^2$ and the inequality constraint $h(\mathbf{x}) = r^2 - \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_o \Vert^2 \leq 0$, formulate the Lagrangian, derive the KKT conditions, and find the constrained equilibrium point $\mathbf{x}^*$.*

**Model Solution for (a):**

Begin with the first-order Taylor expansion about $\mathbf{x}^{(k)}$:

$$J(\mathbf{x}^{(k)} + \delta \mathbf{x}) \approx J(\mathbf{x}^{(k)}) + \nabla J^{\top} \delta \mathbf{x}$$

For a unit step ($\Vert \delta \mathbf{x} \Vert = \alpha$), write $\delta \mathbf{x} = \alpha \mathbf{d}$ with $\Vert \mathbf{d} \Vert = 1$. Then:

$$\Delta J \approx \alpha \nabla J^{\top} \mathbf{d}$$

The Cauchy–Schwarz inequality states $\vert \nabla J^{\top} \mathbf{d} \vert \leq \Vert \nabla J \Vert$, so the most negative $\Delta J$ corresponds to $\mathbf{d}^* = -\nabla J / \Vert \nabla J \Vert$. Substituting:

$$\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)} = \mathbf{x}^{(k)} - \alpha \frac{\nabla J(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})}{\Vert \nabla J(\mathbf{x}^{(k)}) \Vert}$$

The step size $\alpha$ controls how far we move per iteration. If $\alpha$ is too large, we may overshoot the minimum and diverge. If too small, convergence is slow. **Adaptive line search** (e.g., Armijo rule) ensures sufficient decrease:

$$J(\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)}) \leq J(\mathbf{x}^{(k)}) - c \alpha \Vert \nabla J \Vert^2, \quad 0 < c < 1$$

**(Full 7 marks awarded for: clear Taylor setup + application of C-S + identification of optimal d + correct update + discussion of α.)**

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Lagrangian formulation:**

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}, \mu) = \tfrac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_g \Vert^2 + \mu \left[ r^2 - \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_o \Vert^2 \right]$$

**Step 2 — KKT stationarity:**

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} \mathcal{L} = (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_g) - 2\mu (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_o) = 0$$

Solving for $\mathbf{x}$:

$$\mathbf{x}(1 - 2\mu) = \mathbf{x}_g - 2\mu \mathbf{x}_o$$

$$\mathbf{x} = \frac{\mathbf{x}_g - 2\mu \mathbf{x}_o}{1 - 2\mu}$$

**Step 3 — Complementary slackness analysis:**

**Case 1: $\mu = 0$ (obstacle inactive).** Then $\mathbf{x}^* = \mathbf{x}_g = (5, 5)$. The straight-line path does not cross the obstacle (the line from $(0,0)$ to $(5,5)$ passes through $(2,2)$ — at the *center* of the obstacle), so this case is **infeasible** for the constraint $h(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$.

**Case 2: $\mu > 0$ (obstacle active).** The constraint is tight: $\Vert \mathbf{x}^* - \mathbf{x}_o \Vert^2 = r^2 = 1$.

The line from $(0,0)$ to $(5,5)$ has parametric form $\mathbf{x}(t) = (t, t)$, $t \in [0,5]$. Its closest point to $\mathbf{x}_o = (2,2)$ is at $t = 2$, giving distance $0$. The trajectory is **infeasible** unless redirected.

**Step 4 — Geometric resolution.** The constrained equilibrium lies on the obstacle boundary along the direction of the gradient of $J$ from the goal. The optimal tangent point $\mathbf{x}^*$ satisfies:

$$\mathbf{x}^* = \mathbf{x}_o + r \cdot \hat{\mathbf{n}}$$

where $\hat{\mathbf{n}} = (\mathbf{x}_g - \mathbf{x}_o) / \Vert \mathbf{x}_g - \mathbf{x}_o \Vert = (1/\sqrt{2}, 1/\sqrt{2})$.

Hence:

$$\mathbf{x}^* = (2, 2) + 1 \cdot (1/\sqrt{2}, 1/\sqrt{2}) = (2 + 0.707, \; 2 + 0.707) \approx (2.707, \; 2.707)$$

**Step 5 — Multiplier value.** From stationarity, $\mu = \tfrac{1}{2}(1 - 1) = $ undefined directly; the projection method is more practical. Using the projection $\mu = 1$ gives the equilibrium on the circle boundary in the direction of the goal.

**Conclusion:** $\mathbf{x}^* \approx (2.707, 2.707)$ with $\mu^* = 1$, which the robot reaches tangentially to the obstacle and then continues toward $(5, 5)$. **(7 marks for: Lagrangian + KKT + Case analysis + Geometric resolution + Final answer.)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper Pattern]**

(a) **[7 Marks]** *Explain the projected gradient descent method. Derive the projection operator for a box constraint $a_i \leq x_i \leq b_i$ and demonstrate one iteration on the problem $J(\mathbf{x}) = \tfrac{1}{2}(x_1^2 + 4x_2^2)$ starting from $\mathbf{x}_0 = (3, 3)$ with box $-2 \leq x_i \leq 2$ and step size $\alpha = 0.1$.*

(b) **[7 Marks]** *Discuss the convergence properties of unconstrained gradient descent. Distinguish between linear, sublinear, and quadratic convergence rates. State the conditions under which each is achieved, and identify which one applies to a strictly convex, Lipschitz-gradient cost function.*

**Model Solution for (a):**

The projected gradient method is:

$$\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)} = \Pi_{\mathcal{C}}\left(\mathbf{x}^{(k)} - \alpha \nabla J(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})\right)$$

**Box Constraint Projection Derivation.** For $\mathcal{C} = \{\mathbf{x} : a_i \leq x_i \leq b_i\}$, the projection is componentwise:

$$\Pi_{\mathcal{C}}(\mathbf{y})_i = \min(b_i, \max(a_i, y_i))$$

**Apply to the given problem.** Compute the gradient:

$$\nabla J(\mathbf{x}) = (x_1, 4 x_2)$$

At $\mathbf{x}_0 = (3, 3)$: $\nabla J(\mathbf{x}_0) = (3, 12)$.

**Unconstrained step:**

$$\tilde{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{x}_0 - \alpha \nabla J = (3, 3) - 0.1(3, 12) = (2.7, 1.8)$$

**Project onto the box $[-2, 2]^2$:**

$$\mathbf{x}_1 = (2, 1.8)$$

(since $2.7 > 2$ clamps to $2$, and $1.8 \in [-2, 2]$ stays as is). After one iteration, the algorithm moves from $(3, 3)$ to $(2, 1.8)$ — within the feasible set.

**(7 marks for: method statement + projection derivation + gradient computation + step + projection.)**

**Model Solution for (b):**

Convergence of unconstrained gradient descent is characterized by the *rate* at which the error $e_k = \Vert \mathbf{x}^{(k)} - \mathbf{x}^* \Vert$ shrinks:

1. **Linear (Geometric) Convergence:** $e_{k+1} \leq \rho e_k$ for some $0 < \rho < 1$. Achieved when $J$ is **strongly convex** with Lipschitz gradient; the contraction factor depends on the condition number $\kappa = L/\mu$ of the Hessian.

2. **Sublinear Convergence:** $e_{k+1} \leq e_k / k$ (or $\sqrt{e_k}$). Achieved for *convex but not strongly convex* cost functions, or when using a diminishing step size $\alpha_k \to 0$.

3. **Quadratic Convergence:** $e_{k+1} \leq C e_k^2$. Achieved by **Newton's method** when $\mathbf{x}^{(k)}$ is sufficiently close to $\mathbf{x}^*$ and the Hessian is Lipschitz continuous. Requires second-order information.

**Application to strictly convex, Lipschitz-gradient $J$:** This combination gives *strong convexity* with parameter $\mu > 0$ and gradient Lipschitz constant $L < \infty$. The error bound is:

$$J(\mathbf{x}^{(k)}) - J(\mathbf{x}^*) \leq \frac{L \Vert \mathbf{x}^{(0)} - \mathbf{x}^* \Vert^2}{2} \left( 1 - \frac{\mu}{L} \right)^{2k}$$

This is **linear convergence** with rate $\rho = (1 - \mu/L) = (1 - 1/\kappa)$. Hence, for a strictly convex Lipschitz-gradient cost, **gradient descent converges linearly**.

**(7 marks for: defining three rates + stating conditions + applying to the given case + mathematical bound.)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks in This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting complementary slackness** — KKT has *four* sets of conditions; omitting any one costs a mark. Always write all four explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing stationarity with optimality** — KKT is a *necessary* condition at an interior/regular point, not sufficient. State this nuance explicitly to earn full credit.
> 3. **Sign errors in Lagrangian** — The constraint $h_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$ enters the Lagrangian with a **positive** sign; for $h_j(\mathbf{x}) \geq 0$ the sign flips. Mixing this up invalidates all subsequent work.
> 4. **Skipping the step-size discussion** — Examiners specifically look for the role of $\alpha$ (fixed vs. adaptive, Armijo line search). Vague or absent discussion loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Missing geometric interpretation** — In robot navigation problems, students must *draw or describe* the geometry (goal point, obstacle, tangent point). Pure algebra without geometric insight is penalized.
> 6. **No projection in box-constrained problems** — Even a small numerical iteration requires explicit projection. Skipping $\Pi_{\mathcal{C}}$ is a common oversight.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Unconstrained gradient descent update** is $\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)} = \mathbf{x}^{(k)} - \alpha \nabla J(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})$; the descent direction is mathematically *the* steepest local direction.
- **Lagrangian** for equality constraints is $\mathcal{L} = J + \boldsymbol{\lambda}^{\top} g$; stationarity gives $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} \mathcal{L} = 0$.
- **KKT conditions** are: (i) stationarity, (ii) primal feasibility, (iii) dual feasibility ($\mu \geq 0$), (iv) complementary slackness ($\mu h = 0$).
- **Projected gradient** is the simplest constrained variant for convex feasible sets: $\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)} = \Pi_{\mathcal{C}}(\mathbf{x}^{(k)} - \alpha \nabla J)$.
- **Strong convexity + Lipschitz gradient** $\Rightarrow$ **linear convergence** with rate $(1 - \mu/L)$; standard gradient descent on such problems is *robust* but slow if $\kappa = L/\mu$ is large.
- **Robot navigation uses**: goal-attraction potential, obstacle-repulsion potential, KKT multipliers for active constraints, and projected gradient for joint limits.
- **Algorithm selection rule of thumb**: use *steepest descent* for unconstrained; *projected gradient* for box constraints; *Lagrangian/KKT* for nonlinear equality and active inequality; *Newton* for small-scale, high-precision.
- **Convergence termination** typically uses $\Vert \nabla J \Vert \leq \epsilon$ or $\Vert \mathbf{x}^{(k+1)} - \mathbf{x}^{(k)} \Vert \leq \epsilon$.
- **Real-time feasibility** of gradient descent in robot control loops hinges on cheap gradient evaluations; potential fields give closed-form $\nabla J$, making 100 Hz updates tractable.
- **Exam mantra**: Always state the *update rule*, the *constraint form*, the *KKT conditions*, and the *geometric interpretation* — these four pillars fetch full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Navigation Function - in Static Deterministic Environment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT735 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Navigation Function - in Static Deterministic Environment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)

A **Navigation Function (NF)** is a real-valued, analytic (or $C^2$ smooth) map $\phi : \mathcal{F} \to [0, 1]$ defined on the free configuration space $\mathcal{F} \subset \mathcal{C}$ of a robot, satisfying the following four axioms originally proposed by **Koditschek and Rimon (1992)**:

1. **Boundary Condition:** $\phi(q) \to 1$ as $q$ approaches the boundary $\partial \mathcal{F}$ (i.e., the robot approaches an obstacle surface).
2. **Goal Condition:** $\phi(q)$ attains a unique global minimum of value $0$ exclusively at the goal configuration $q_{goal} \in \mathcal{F}_{int}$.
3. **Admissibility / Polar Condition:** $\phi(q)$ has no local minima in the interior of $\mathcal{F}$ other than the goal.
4. **Morse Condition:** All critical points of $\phi$ in $\mathcal{F}$ are non-degenerate (the Hessian has full rank).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Static Deterministic Environment** in KTU 2024 syllabus refers to a workspace where **all obstacle geometries, positions, and the goal location are perfectly known a priori** and **do not change with time**. There is **no stochastic noise** in the robot's perception or actuation. The robot is treated as a point in the configuration space after appropriate mapping of obstacles.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Hill and Valley Terrain":**
> Imagine you are blindfolded and placed on a vast terrain. Your **goal** is the deepest valley (the global minimum). The **obstacles** are unscalable vertical cliffs (boundary of $\mathcal{F}$). A **navigation function** is a specially sculpted "elevation map" handed to you. When you simply always walk downhill (gradient descent), you will be guaranteed to reach the goal — and crucially, **there are no other valleys or potholes (local minima) to trap you on the way**. The terrain is engineered so that the cliffs (obstacles) are the only "high walls" preventing your descent, and the deepest point is exactly where you want to go.

This analogy captures the central engineering virtue of a Navigation Function: **provable, complete, collision-free path planning reduced to local gradient computation**.

### 1.3 Key Constants and Standard Metrics

- The **tuning parameter** $\kappa \in \mathbb{Z}^+$ is the single most important design hyperparameter, with the constraint $\kappa \geq \dim(\mathcal{F})$ for existence guarantees in a sphere world.
- The function range is **standardized to $[0, 1]$**, where $0$ denotes the goal and $1$ denotes the obstacle boundary.
- Configuration space **dimension** $n = \dim(\mathcal{F})$ dictates the minimum number of spherical obstacles permissible in a sphere world setup, which is $n + 1$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Decomposed Operational Logic

The construction of a Navigation Function proceeds through a structured pipeline of logical steps:

- **Step 1 — Configuration Space Modelling:** The workspace $\mathcal{W} \subset \mathbb{R}^m$ is mapped to configuration space $\mathcal{C}$. Obstacles $\mathcal{O}_i \subset \mathcal{W}$ are inflated by the robot radius $r$ to form C-space obstacles $\mathcal{CB}_i$. The free space is $\mathcal{F} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \bigcup_i \mathcal{CB}_i$.

- **Step 2 — Goal Distance Function:** Define the smooth distance-to-goal squared function:
  $$ \sigma_d(q) = \Vert q - q_{goal} \Vert^2 $$
  This term is a convex bowl attracting the robot to $q_{goal}$.

- **Step 3 — Obstacle Function Synthesis:** For each C-space obstacle $\mathcal{CB}_i$, construct a smooth signed-distance-like function $\beta_i(q)$ that vanishes on $\partial \mathcal{CB}_i$ and is positive inside the obstacle. The combined obstacle function is their product:
  $$ \beta(q) = \prod_{i=1}^{M} \beta_i(q) $$
  This product vanishes on **any** obstacle boundary, ensuring the NF approaches $1$ there.

- **Step 4 — Rimon-Koditschek Combination (NF Core):** Combine $\sigma_d$ and $\beta$ through a parameterized sum-of-powers structure to eliminate spurious local minima:
  $$ \phi_K(q) = \frac{\sigma_d(q)}{\left( \sigma_d^{\kappa}(q) + \beta(q) \right)^{1/\kappa}} $$
  where $\kappa \geq n$ is the tuning parameter.

- **Step 5 — Normalization to $[0, 1]$:** The result is rescaled (often by setting $\phi_{final} = 1 - \phi_K / \phi_K^{max}$ or similar affine remapping) so that $\phi(q_{goal}) = 0$ and $\phi(q) \to 1$ at the boundary.

- **Step 6 — Gradient Descent Execution:** At runtime, the robot computes $-\nabla \phi_K(q)$ and moves along this vector field. The properties of $\phi_K$ guarantee that the resulting flow is globally convergent to $q_{goal}$ without collisions.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a product $\beta(q) = \prod \beta_i$?** A product creates a "barrier" at the boundary of **any** obstacle, ensuring that the boundary condition is satisfied for the entire environment at once. A sum, in contrast, would allow the robot to graze one obstacle while being far from others.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the exponent $\kappa$?** The exponentiation mathematically deforms the potential landscape to "saddle out" any incipient local minima. As $\kappa$ grows large, the NF behaves like the minimum of its two component functions, which is provably free of spurious minima under the star-world assumption.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Boundary Condition | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\mathcal{F} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \bigcup_i \mathcal{CB}_i$ | Free configuration space | $q \in \mathcal{F}_{int}$ for valid configurations | Dimensionless (topological) |
| $\sigma_d(q) = \Vert q - q_{goal} \Vert^2$ | Squared distance to goal | $\sigma_d \to 0$ at $q_{goal}$ | $\text{m}^2$ (in $\mathbb{R}^n$ workspace) |
| $\beta_i(q)$ | $i$-th obstacle signed function | $\beta_i = 0$ on $\partial \mathcal{CB}_i$, $\beta_i > 0$ inside | $\text{m}^{2k}$ (depends on $k$) |
| $\beta(q) = \prod_{i=1}^{M} \beta_i(q)$ | Combined obstacle barrier | $\beta = 0$ on $\partial \mathcal{F}$ | $\text{m}^{2Mk}$ |
| $\phi_K(q) = \sigma_d \, \left( \sigma_d^{\kappa} + \beta \right)^{-1/\kappa}$ | Rimon-Koditschek NF | $\phi_K \in [0,1]$, $\phi_K(q_{goal}) = 0$ | Dimensionless |
| $\kappa \geq n$ | Tuning exponent (sphere world) | Larger $\kappa \Rightarrow$ smoother flow | Dimensionless integer |
| $-\nabla \phi_K(q)$ | Robot control velocity vector | Tangent to collision-free flow | $\text{m/s}$ |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Navigation functions are foundational in **warehouse robotics** (Amazon Kiva systems), **surgical robotics** (where the static anatomical map is pre-operative), **autonomous vacuum cleaners** (known room geometry), and **planetary exploration rovers** operating with pre-uploaded terrain maps. They guarantee **provable safety** — a regulatory prerequisite in medical and aerospace applications where certification demands formal completeness proofs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Rimon-Koditschek Navigation Function

We will derive the navigation function systematically, starting from the simplest formulation and progressively addressing each failure mode.

#### **Stage 1 — Naive Goal Attractor (No Obstacles)**

Define the simplest goal-attracting potential:
$$ \phi_1(q) = \Vert q - q_{goal} \Vert^2 $$

**Logic:** This is a convex paraboloid bowl. The gradient is:
$$ \nabla \phi_1(q) = 2(q - q_{goal}) $$

**Failure Mode:** $-\nabla \phi_1$ drives the robot straight to $q_{goal}$, but offers **zero obstacle avoidance**. The robot will collide.

#### **Stage 2 — Add an Obstacle Barrier**

Introduce a single obstacle with a smooth barrier function $\beta_1(q) > 0$ inside the obstacle, vanishing on the boundary. A natural choice for a spherical C-obstacle of radius $\rho$ centered at $c$ is:
$$ \beta_1(q) = \Vert q - c \Vert^2 - \rho^2 $$

The combined potential is then:
$$ \phi_2(q) = \phi_1(q) + \beta_1(q) $$

**Logic:** The gradient descent now repels the robot from inside the obstacle. **However**, the robot may settle at a point $q^*$ where the attractive and repulsive forces balance:
$$ \nabla \phi_1(q^*) = -\nabla \beta_1(q^*) $$

This is a **spurious local minimum** not at the goal. The naive sum creates new traps.

#### **Stage 3 — Product Form for Multi-Obstacle Coverage**

Replace the sum with the product to handle multiple obstacles simultaneously:
$$ \beta(q) = \prod_{i=1}^{M} \beta_i(q) $$

**Logic:** Whenever the robot approaches the boundary of **any** obstacle, at least one $\beta_i \to 0$, making the entire product vanish. This satisfies the boundary condition globally.

#### **Stage 4 — The Rimon-Koditschek Deformation**

To eliminate spurious minima introduced by the product, deform the simple sum into a parameterized family:
$$ \phi_3(q) = \frac{\sigma_d(q)}{\left( \sigma_d^{\kappa}(q) + \beta(q) \right)^{1/\kappa}} $$

**Derivation of the gradient (key step for control law):**

Let $D(q) = \sigma_d^{\kappa}(q) + \beta(q)$, so $\phi_3 = \sigma_d \cdot D^{-1/\kappa}$.

Compute $\nabla \phi_3$ using the product rule:
$$ \nabla \phi_3 = D^{-1/\kappa} \, \nabla \sigma_d + \sigma_d \cdot \left( -\frac{1}{\kappa} D^{-1/\kappa - 1} \right) \nabla D $$

Since $\nabla D = \kappa \sigma_d^{\kappa - 1} \nabla \sigma_d + \nabla \beta$:
$$ \nabla \phi_3 = D^{-1/\kappa} \nabla \sigma_d - \sigma_d \cdot D^{-1/\kappa - 1} \left( \sigma_d^{\kappa - 1} \nabla \sigma_d + \frac{1}{\kappa} \nabla \beta \right) $$

Collecting terms with $\nabla \sigma_d$:
$$ \nabla \phi_3 = D^{-1/\kappa} \nabla \sigma_d \left[ 1 - \sigma_d^{\kappa} D^{-1} \right] - \frac{\sigma_d}{\kappa} D^{-1/\kappa - 1} \nabla \beta $$

Notice that $\sigma_d^{\kappa} D^{-1} = \sigma_d^{\kappa} / (\sigma_d^{\kappa} + \beta)$. Substituting:
$$ \nabla \phi_3 = D^{-1/\kappa - 1} \left[ \beta \cdot \nabla \sigma_d - \frac{\sigma_d}{\kappa} \nabla \beta \right] $$

This compact form makes it explicit that the gradient is a balance between attraction ($\nabla \sigma_d$ scaled by $\beta$) and repulsion ($\nabla \beta$ scaled by $\sigma_d$), and the prefactor $D^{-1/\kappa - 1}$ is always strictly positive in $\mathcal{F}_{int}$, so **the sign of $-\nabla \phi_3$ is well-defined everywhere in the interior**.

#### **Stage 5 — Choice of $\kappa$ and Existence Proof (Sphere World)**

For a sphere world with $n+1$ spherical obstacles in $\mathbb{R}^n$, Koditschek and Rimon proved that:
$$ \phi_K \text{ is a navigation function} \iff \kappa \geq n $$

The proof proceeds by showing that the critical points of $\phi_K$ correspond to a topological Morse function on the sphere world, and that all non-goal critical points have index (number of negative Hessian eigenvalues) exactly $n - 1$, making them **saddle points** — gradient descent slides past them.

#### **Stage 6 — Normalization to $[0, 1]$**

Define the rescaled function:
$$ \phi_{final}(q) = \frac{\phi_K(q)}{\phi_K^{max}} $$

where $\phi_K^{max}$ is the supremum of $\phi_K$ over $\mathcal{F}$. After this affine map, we have:
$$ \phi_{final}(q_{goal}) = 0, \quad \phi_{final}(q) \in [0, 1], \quad \phi_{final}(q) \to 1 \text{ as } q \to \partial\mathcal{F} $$

This completes the construction.

### 3.2 Summary of the Analytical Pipeline

| Stage | Construct | Purpose | Failure Mode Addressed |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\phi_1 = \sigma_d$ | Pure goal attraction | No obstacle avoidance |
| 2 | $\phi_2 = \sigma_d + \beta_1$ | Single obstacle avoidance | Spurious local minima |
| 3 | $\beta = \prod_i \beta_i$ | Multi-obstacle boundary | Multi-obstacle coverage |
| 4 | $\phi_K = \sigma_d(\sigma_d^{\kappa} + \beta)^{-1/\kappa}$ | Spurious-minima elimination | Saddle point guarantee |
| 5 | $\kappa \geq n$ | Mathematical existence | Completeness proof |
| 6 | $\phi_{final} = \phi_K / \phi_K^{max}$ | Standardized range | Output normalization |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Navigation Function Construction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[STATIC WORKSPACE W] --> B[CONFIGURATION SPACE C]
    B --> C[INFLATE OBSTACLES BY ROBOT RADIUS]
    C --> D[FREE SPACE F EQUALS C MINUS UNION OF C-BS]
    D --> E[GOAL DISTANCE FUNCTION SIGMA D]
    D --> F[OBSTACLE FUNCTIONS BETA I]
    F --> G[COMBINED BARRIER BETA EQUALS PRODUCT OF BETA I]
    E --> H[RIMON KODITSCHEK NF PHI K]
    G --> H
    H --> I[NORMALIZE TO RANGE 0 TO 1]
    I --> J[GRADIENT FIELD NABLA PHI K]
    J --> K[ROBOT CONTROL VELOCITY V EQUALS MINUS NABLA PHI K]
    K --> L[COLLISION FREE TRAJECTORY TO GOAL]
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style L fill:#c8e6c9
    style H fill:#fff9c4
```

### 4.2 Gradient Descent Execution Loop on the NF

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[START AT INITIAL CONFIG Q0] --> Q[READ CURRENT CONFIG Q]
    Q --> R[COMPUTE SIGMA D AT Q]
    Q --> B[COMPUTE BETA AT Q]
    R --> PHI[COMPUTE NF PHI K AT Q]
    B --> PHI
    PHI --> GRAD[COMPUTE GRADIENT NABLA PHI K AT Q]
    GRAD --> V[CONTROL V EQUALS MINUS NABLA PHI K]
    V --> STEP[UPDATE Q EQUALS Q PLUS DELTA TIMES V]
    STEP --> CK{IS Q NEAR Q GOAL}
    CK -->|NO| Q
    CK -->|YES| DONE[GOAL REACHED]
    style S fill:#e1f5ff
    style DONE fill:#c8e6c9
    style PHI fill:#fff9c4
```

### 4.3 Topological Structure of the Navigation Function Landscape

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[OBSTACLE BOUNDARY PHI EQUALS 1] -->|REJECTED| B[BOUNDARY OF FREE SPACE]
    C[SADDLE POINTS PHI BETWEEN 0 AND 1] -->|AVOIDED| D[GRADIENT DESCENT PASSES BY]
    E[GLOBAL MINIMUM AT Q GOAL PHI EQUALS 0] -->|ATTRACTED| F[UNIQUE GOAL CONFIG]
    B --> G[FREE SPACE F]
    D --> G
    F --> G
    style E fill:#c8e6c9
    style A fill:#ffcdd2
    style C fill:#fff9c4
    style G fill:#e1f5ff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the four axioms that a real-valued function $\phi : \mathcal{F} \to [0,1]$ must satisfy to be called a Navigation Function. Mention the role of the tuning parameter $\kappa$.**

**Model Answer:**

A function $\phi$ is a Navigation Function if it satisfies the **Koditschek–Rimon axioms**:

1. **Boundary Condition:** $\phi(q) \to 1$ as $q$ approaches the obstacle boundary $\partial \mathcal{F}$.
2. **Goal Condition:** $\phi(q_{goal}) = 0$ is the unique global minimum.
3. **Admissibility (Polar Condition):** $\phi$ has no local minima in $\mathcal{F}_{int}$ other than $q_{goal}$.
4. **Morse Condition:** All critical points of $\phi$ are non-degenerate.

**Role of $\kappa$:** The tuning exponent $\kappa \geq n$ (configuration space dimension) mathematically eliminates spurious local minima by deforming the potential landscape, converting any incipient local minima into saddle points. Without sufficient $\kappa$, the gradient field can have trap configurations. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between a Static Deterministic Environment and a Dynamic Stochastic Environment in the context of Navigation Function design.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Static Deterministic | Dynamic Stochastic |
|---|---|---|
| Obstacle knowledge | Fully known a priori | Partially observed / unknown |
| Time evolution | Time-invariant | Time-varying |
| Sensor noise | None | Probabilistic |
| NF applicability | Provably convergent gradient flow | NF may fail; requires reactive or probabilistic methods |
| Example | Pre-operative surgical map | Crowded pedestrian walkway |

In a **static deterministic environment**, the navigation function can be computed offline once and the gradient field is a complete solution. In contrast, **dynamic stochastic** settings require replanning and cannot rely on a single global gradient field. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the gradient of the Rimon-Koditschek navigation function:
$$ \phi_K(q) = \frac{\sigma_d(q)}{\left( \sigma_d^{\kappa}(q) + \beta(q) \right)^{1/\kappa}} $$
and explain why the gradient is well-defined and non-zero in the interior of the free space $\mathcal{F}_{int}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a 2-DOF point robot in a workspace containing two circular obstacles of radii $r_1 = 0.5\,\text{m}$ and $r_2 = 0.5\,\text{m}$ centered at $c_1 = (2, 2)$ and $c_2 = (5, 2)$ respectively, with goal at $q_{goal} = (8, 2)$ and starting configuration $q_0 = (0, 2)$, write the explicit expressions for $\sigma_d(q)$, $\beta_1(q)$, $\beta_2(q)$, and $\beta(q)$. State the minimum value of $\kappa$ required and justify. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Let $D(q) = \sigma_d^{\kappa}(q) + \beta(q)$, so $\phi_K = \sigma_d \cdot D^{-1/\kappa}$.

Using the product rule:
$$ \nabla \phi_K = D^{-1/\kappa} \nabla \sigma_d - \frac{\sigma_d}{\kappa} D^{-1/\kappa - 1} \nabla D $$

Substituting $\nabla D = \kappa \sigma_d^{\kappa-1} \nabla \sigma_d + \nabla \beta$:
$$ \nabla \phi_K = D^{-1/\kappa - 1} \left[ \beta \cdot \nabla \sigma_d - \frac{\sigma_d}{\kappa} \nabla \beta \right] $$

**Well-definedness:** In $\mathcal{F}_{int}$, both $\sigma_d > 0$ and $\beta > 0$, hence $D > 0$. The prefactor $D^{-1/\kappa - 1}$ is therefore strictly positive and finite, and the bracket term is bounded. The gradient vanishes **only** when $\beta \nabla \sigma_d = (\sigma_d / \kappa) \nabla \beta$, which by the Morse property occurs only at the goal or at saddles — never at a spurious local minimum. Thus the negative gradient provides a valid descent direction everywhere in $\mathcal{F}_{int}$.

**(b)** With $q = (x, y)$:

**Goal distance squared:**
$$ \sigma_d(q) = (x - 8)^2 + (y - 2)^2 $$

**Individual obstacle functions (treating the point robot with inflated obstacles of radius 0.5 m):**
$$ \beta_1(q) = (x - 2)^2 + (y - 2)^2 - (0.5)^2 = (x-2)^2 + (y-2)^2 - 0.25 $$
$$ \beta_2(q) = (x - 5)^2 + (y - 2)^2 - 0.25 $$

**Combined barrier:**
$$ \beta(q) = \beta_1(q) \cdot \beta_2(q) $$

**Minimum $\kappa$:** Since the configuration space is 2-dimensional ($n = 2$), the Koditschek–Rimon theorem requires $\kappa \geq n = 2$. In practice, choosing $\kappa = 2$ or $\kappa = 3$ is standard for 2-D point robots. The integer $\kappa$ ensures that the function is a Morse function with all non-goal critical points being saddles.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of a **sphere world**. Why must the workspace contain exactly $n+1$ spherical obstacles for the existence of a navigation function in $\mathbb{R}^n$? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mobile robot navigates a 3-D configuration space (3-DOF) with three spherical obstacles. Explain the construction of the navigation function step-by-step and determine the critical value of $\kappa$ at which the function becomes a valid NF. Discuss what happens if $\kappa$ is chosen below this critical value. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** A **sphere world** is the simplest analytically tractable workspace in which the existence of a navigation function can be formally proved. It consists of:

- A bounded workspace $W \subset \mathbb{R}^n$,
- A target point $q_{goal} \in W$,
- A collection of $n+1$ disjoint open balls $\mathcal{B}_1, \mathcal{B}_2, \ldots, \mathcal{B}_{n+1}$ inside $W$.

**Why exactly $n+1$ obstacles?** The topological reason is rooted in Morse theory on manifolds with boundary. In $\mathbb{R}^n$, a non-degenerate critical point of index $k$ has a stable manifold of dimension $k$. The boundary $\partial \mathcal{F}$ of the free space is topologically an $n$-sphere (after one-point compactification), and the goal is an index-$0$ critical point. For the function to be admissible (no spurious minima), the Euler characteristic of the manifold must match the alternating sum of critical points. With exactly $n+1$ obstacles, the topology admits precisely the critical point structure required: one minimum (at goal) and $n$ saddles of index 1, which are the unavoidable topological obstructions. Fewer obstacles and the topology would force additional minima; more obstacles and the index counts would not match.

**(b)** Construction for 3-D space with 3 spherical obstacles:

**Step 1:** Define $\sigma_d(q) = \Vert q - q_{goal} \Vert^2$.

**Step 2:** Define each $\beta_i(q) = \Vert q - c_i \Vert^2 - \rho_i^2$.

**Step 3:** Form the product $\beta(q) = \beta_1(q) \beta_2(q) \beta_3(q)$.

**Step 4:** Construct the NF:
$$ \phi_K(q) = \frac{\sigma_d(q)}{\left( \sigma_d^{\kappa}(q) + \beta(q) \right)^{1/\kappa}} $$

**Critical $\kappa$:** For $n = 3$ configuration space, the Koditschek–Rimon existence theorem requires:
$$ \kappa_{crit} = n = 3 $$

**Behaviour below $\kappa_{crit}$:** If $\kappa < 3$, the function $\phi_K$ may still be smooth and bounded in $[0,1]$, but it is **not guaranteed** to be a navigation function. Specifically:

- New local minima may appear near the obstacles,
- The Morse property may fail (degenerate critical points),
- Gradient descent from certain initial configurations may converge to spurious equilibria,
- The completeness guarantee is lost.

Empirically, lower $\kappa$ makes the flow more "curved" near obstacles but increases the chance of trapping. The standard engineering practice is to use $\kappa$ slightly above the critical value (e.g., $\kappa = 4$ or $5$ for 3-D spaces) to provide a safety margin.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Examiner's Eye):**
> 1. **Forgetting the Morse condition:** Many students list only 3 axioms and forget the **Morse (non-degeneracy) condition**. This costs 1 full mark on definition questions.
> 2. **Confusing the NF formula:** The Rimon-Koditschek NF is $\phi_K = \sigma_d (\sigma_d^{\kappa} + \beta)^{-1/\kappa}$, **not** $\phi_K = \sigma_d / (\sigma_d + \beta)$. Writing the simple quotient loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Incorrect $\kappa$ criterion:** The constraint is $\kappa \geq n$ (configuration space dimension), not $\kappa \geq 2$ universally. Students often write a generic statement and lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Sign error in gradient derivation:** In $\nabla \phi_K = D^{-1/\kappa - 1} [\beta \nabla \sigma_d - (\sigma_d/\kappa) \nabla \beta]$, the minus sign is critical. Sign errors in the bracket cost 1–2 marks depending on context.
> 5. **Saying "local minima" instead of "saddles":** When discussing $\kappa < \kappa_{crit}$, students often say "local minima appear" but should say "the Morse property fails and degenerate critical points form." This is a key KTU distinction.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Navigation Function** = analytic map $\phi : \mathcal{F} \to [0,1]$ with **4 axioms** (boundary, goal, admissibility, Morse).
- **Static Deterministic Environment** = obstacles are known a priori and time-invariant.
- **Rimon-Koditschek formula:**
  $$ \phi_K(q) = \frac{\sigma_d(q)}{\left( \sigma_d^{\kappa}(q) + \beta(q) \right)^{1/\kappa}} $$
- **Tuning parameter:** $\kappa \geq n$ (configuration space dimension) is **mandatory** for existence in a sphere world.
- **Sphere world:** contains exactly $n+1$ spherical obstacles in $\mathbb{R}^n$.
- **Obstacle barrier:** $\beta(q) = \prod_i \beta_i(q)$ — product form ensures boundary condition holds for the entire environment.
- **Gradient field:** $-\nabla \phi_K$ defines the control law; the robot simply moves downhill.
- **Critical points:** only the goal is a minimum; all other critical points are **saddles** (Morse function property).
- **Path planning = gradient descent:** globally convergent, complete, and provably collision-free in static deterministic environments.
- **Limitation:** NFs require precise global knowledge of the environment — they are **not applicable** to dynamic or unknown environments without re-planning.
- **Practical extensions:** star-shaped worlds, multiple goals, and extended sphere-world theorems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Navigation Function and Potential Fields in Dynamic Environment - Estimation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT735 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Navigation Function and Potential Fields in Dynamic Environment - Estimation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Navigation Function (NF) — Formal Definition

A **Navigation Function** is a real-valued, sufficiently smooth (at least $C^2$ continuous) map constructed on the robot's free configuration space $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ such that:

$$f : \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} \subset \mathbb{R}^n \rightarrow [0, 1]$$

with the following defining properties (Koditschek & Rimon, 1990 framework, rigorously adapted in KTU 2024 syllabus):

1. **Polar Structure:** $f$ has a unique minimum at the goal configuration $q_d$, where $f(q_d) = 0$.
2. **Admissibility:** $f$ is admissible (bounded) — meaning $f \leq 1$ everywhere on $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ and reaches the boundary value of $1$ on the obstacle boundary $\partial \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$.
3. **Morse Property:** $f$ is a **Morse function** — its critical points (excluding the goal) are non-degenerate and isolated, ensuring no flat plateaus trap the robot.
4. **Tangent (Saddle) Criticality:** All critical points other than the goal are **saddles**, not local maxima or minima. This guarantees that almost every gradient-descent trajectory converges to $q_d$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A *true* Navigation Function must have **exactly one minimum** (the goal). All other stationary points must be saddles. Functions that are merely attractive to the goal but may have spurious local minima are called **Artificial Potential Fields (APF)** — they are weaker than NFs.

## 1.2 Potential Fields in Dynamic Environments — Definition

A **Dynamic Potential Field** extends the classical static potential field by adding a **time-dependent repulsive component** for moving obstacles. The total field is:

$$U_{\text{total}}(q, t) = U_{\text{att}}(q) + U_{\text{rep}}(q, t) + U_{\text{dyn}}(q, \dot{q}_{\text{obs}}, t)$$

where $t$ denotes the time variable, $\dot{q}_{\text{obs}}$ is the velocity of dynamic obstacles, and $U_{\text{dyn}}$ incorporates **velocity-obstacle (VO)**, **time-to-collision (TTC)**, and **relative motion** considerations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** In a **static** potential field, the repulsion depends only on the robot's current position. In a **dynamic** potential field, the repulsion is **a function of time** because obstacles move, meaning "safe" and "unsafe" regions shift in $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$.

## 1.3 Estimation — Definition in the Context of NF/Potential Fields

**Estimation** refers to the process of inferring hidden/uncertain state variables — such as the **robot's pose** $q(t)$, **velocity** $\dot{q}(t)$, and **dynamic obstacle states** $x_{\text{obs}}(t)$ — from noisy sensor measurements $z(t)$ using recursive filters like the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)**, **Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF)**, or **Particle Filter (PF)**.

The general Bayesian estimation form is:

$$\hat{x}_{t \vert t} = \arg\max_{x_t} \, p(x_t \mid z_{1:t})$$

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

### Real-World Analogy 1: "The Glowing Ball in a Hilly Terrain"
Imagine a robot is a **marble** rolling on a frictionless, rubber-sheet-like terrain. The **goal** is the lowest valley (potential = 0). **Obstacles** are **upward bumps** in the terrain (potential = 1). The marble naturally rolls downhill following the **negative gradient** $-\nabla U$ to reach the goal without bumping into any hill. For **dynamic obstacles**, the hills themselves **move and grow** over time — a hill representing an approaching car grows taller as it gets closer!

### Real-World Analogy 2: "GPS in a Fog"
**Estimation** in a dynamic NF context is like a **driver in dense fog** using a noisy GPS. The GPS gives scattered readings (measurements), and the driver combines these with a mental motion model (kinematics) to estimate the *true* position. In a robot, **Kalman filtering** is exactly this mathematical fusion of motion models and noisy sensor data.

### Real-World Analogy 3: "The Magnetic Field of a Moving Train"
A **dynamic potential field** is like a magnetic field around a moving train. The field is repulsive (pushes you away), but its shape and strength change as the train moves — closer = stronger repulsion, moving = direction of repulsion shifts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric for NF Smoothness:** $C^2$ continuity (twice continuously differentiable). This is the **Koditschek-Rimon standard** mandated for KTU 2024 advanced questions.

---

## 1.5 Standard Boundary Conditions

| Condition | Mathematical Form | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Goal attainment | $f(q_d) = 0$ | Minimum of NF at goal |
| Obstacle boundary | $f(\partial \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}) = 1$ | Maximum on obstacle surfaces |
| Free space admissibility | $0 < f(q) < 1$ | Robot is safe and progressing |
| Smoothness | $\nabla f, \nabla^2 f$ continuous | Stable gradient-following |
| Off-goal critical points | $\det(\nabla^2 f) < 0$ | All saddles (unstable equilibria) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Foundation of Navigation Functions

### 2.1.1 The Koditschek-Rimon Sphere-World Construction

For a robot in $\mathbb{R}^n$ with $M$ disjoint spherical obstacles $O_i$ (centered at $c_i$ with radius $\rho_i$) and goal $q_d$, the classical NF takes the form:

$$f(q) = \frac{\Vert q - q_d \Vert^2}{\left( \Vert q - q_d \Vert^{2k} + \beta(q) \right)^{1/k}}$$

where the **obstacle function** $\beta(q)$ encodes all obstacle geometry:

$$\beta(q) = \prod_{i=1}^{M} \beta_i(q), \quad \beta_i(q) = \Vert q - c_i \Vert^2 - \rho_i^2$$

The parameter $k > 0$ is a **tuning gain** that controls the smoothness and saddle-point structure. A typical choice is $k \geq 1$.

### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Logic of NF Construction

1. **Step 1 — Identify Free Space:** Compute the free configuration space $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \bigcup_i O_i$, where $O_i$ is the $i$-th obstacle region in configuration space.
2. **Step 2 — Define Distance-to-Goal:** Use a smooth, positive-definite function (e.g., squared Euclidean norm) for the attractive component $\Vert q - q_d \Vert^2$.
3. **Step 3 — Define Distance-to-Obstacle Function:** Construct $\beta_i(q)$ for each obstacle. It must be zero on the obstacle surface and positive in free space.
4. **Step 4 — Construct the Combined Obstacle Function:** Multiply all $\beta_i(q)$ to ensure $\beta \to 0$ as the robot approaches any obstacle.
5. **Step 5 — Apply the NF Normalization Formula:** Combine the attractive and repulsive components to keep $f \in [0, 1]$.
6. **Step 6 — Verify Morse Property:** Check that all critical points are non-degenerate (Hessian is invertible).
7. **Step 7 — Verify Saddle Property:** For all critical points other than $q_d$, the Hessian must have at least one negative and one positive eigenvalue.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Works:** The denominator $(...) ^{1/k}$ grows much faster than the numerator as the robot approaches an obstacle (since $\beta \to 0$), forcing $f \to 1$ — the maximum value. This creates a "repulsive cliff" precisely on the obstacle surface, ensuring collision avoidance.

## 2.2 Artificial Potential Field (APF) — Classical Khatib Formulation

The classical APF is **not** a true NF (it can have spurious local minima) but is widely used:

### Attractive Potential:
$$U_{\text{att}}(q) = \frac{1}{2} \, k_a \Vert q - q_d \Vert^2$$

Resulting attractive force:
$$F_{\text{att}}(q) = -\nabla U_{\text{att}}(q) = -k_a (q - q_d)$$

### Repulsive Potential (Khatib):
$$U_{\text{rep}_i}(q) = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{2} k_r \left( \dfrac{1}{d_i(q)} - \dfrac{1}{Q^*} \right)^2 & \text{if } d_i(q) \leq Q^* \\ 0 & \text{if } d_i(q) > Q^* \end{cases}$$

where:
- $d_i(q) = \Vert q - q_{\text{obs}_i} \Vert - \rho_i$ is the **signed distance** to obstacle $i$
- $Q^*$ is the **threshold distance of influence**
- $k_r$ is the repulsive gain

The repulsive force:
$$F_{\text{rep}_i}(q) = \begin{cases} k_r \left( \dfrac{1}{d_i(q)} - \dfrac{1}{Q^*} \right) \dfrac{1}{d_i^2(q)} \, \nabla d_i(q) & \text{if } d_i \leq Q^* \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

## 2.3 Dynamic Potential Fields — Extensions for Moving Obstacles

### 2.3.1 Time-Varying Repulsive Potential (Ge & Cui, 2002)

For a moving obstacle with position $q_{\text{obs}}(t)$ and velocity $v_{\text{obs}}(t)$:

$$U_{\text{dyn}}(q, t) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{2} k_r \left( \dfrac{1}{d(q,t)} - \dfrac{1}{Q^*(t)} \right)^2 \cdot \eta(t) & \text{if } d \leq Q^*(t) \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

where:
- $d(q, t) = \Vert q - q_{\text{obs}}(t) \Vert - \rho$ is the instantaneous distance
- $Q^*(t) = Q^* \cdot e^{-\lambda \cdot \text{TTC}(t)}$ shrinks as time-to-collision decreases
- $\text{TTC}(t) = \dfrac{d(q, t)}{v_{\text{rel}}(q, t)}$ is **time-to-collision**
- $\eta(t) = 1 + \alpha \cdot \Vert v_{\text{obs}}(t) \Vert$ weights by obstacle speed
- $\alpha, \lambda$ are positive tuning constants

### 2.3.2 Velocity Obstacle (VO) Concept

The **velocity obstacle** $VO_{A \mid B}$ for robot $A$ relative to obstacle $B$ is the set of relative velocities that would cause a collision:

$$VO_{A \mid B} = \left\{ v \mid \exists \, t > 0 : q_A + v \cdot t \in B(q_B + v_B t) \right\}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** VO methods are the basis of **autonomous driving stacks** (e.g., NVIDIA's PilotNet, Mobileye) and **drone swarm coordination**.

## 2.4 Estimation Filters for Dynamic Navigation

### 2.4.1 Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)

**Prediction Step:**
$$\hat{x}_{t \mid t-1} = f(\hat{x}_{t-1 \mid t-1}, u_{t-1})$$

$$P_{t \mid t-1} = F_{t-1} \, P_{t-1 \mid t-1} \, F_{t-1}^T + Q_{t-1}$$

where $F_{t-1} = \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x} \bigg\vert_{\hat{x}_{t-1 \mid t-1}}$ is the **Jacobian** of the motion model.

**Update Step:**
$$K_t = P_{t \mid t-1} \, H_t^T \left( H_t \, P_{t \mid t-1} \, H_t^T + R_t \right)^{-1}$$

$$\hat{x}_{t \mid t} = \hat{x}_{t \mid t-1} + K_t \left( z_t - h(\hat{x}_{t \mid t-1}) \right)$$

$$P_{t \mid t} = (I - K_t H_t) P_{t \mid t-1}$$

where $H_t = \dfrac{\partial h}{\partial x} \bigg\vert_{\hat{x}_{t \mid t-1}}$ is the **measurement Jacobian**, $K_t$ is the **Kalman gain**, and $Q_t, R_t$ are the process and measurement noise covariances.

### 2.4.2 Robot State Model (Differential Drive Example)

State vector: $x = [x_r, y_r, \theta_r, \dot{x}_r, \dot{y}_r]^T$

Motion model:
$$x_{r, t+1} = \begin{bmatrix} x_{r,t} + \dot{x}_{r,t} \Delta t \cos\theta_t \\ y_{r,t} + \dot{x}_{r,t} \Delta t \sin\theta_t \\ \theta_{r,t} + \dfrac{\dot{x}_{r,t} \Delta t \tan\phi}{L} \\ \dot{x}_{r,t} \\ \dot{y}_{r,t} \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why estimation matters for NF/PF:** Dynamic obstacles have *uncertain* positions due to sensor noise. The robot's NF must use the **estimated obstacle state** $\hat{x}_{\text{obs}}$, not the raw measurement, to ensure collision-free motion.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Units | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| $f(q)$ | Navigation Function | dimensionless | $f \in [0, 1]$ |
| $U_{\text{att}}$ | Attractive Potential | Joule (J) | $U_{\text{att}}(q_d) = 0$ |
| $U_{\text{rep}}$ | Repulsive Potential | Joule (J) | $U_{\text{rep}} \to \infty$ on boundary |
| $U_{\text{dyn}}$ | Dynamic Repulsive Potential | Joule (J) | Time-dependent |
| $k_a$ | Attractive gain | N/m | $k_a \in [0.5, 5.0]$ |
| $k_r$ | Repulsive gain | N | $k_r \in [10, 1000]$ |
| $Q^*$ | Influence distance | m | $Q^* = 1$ to $5$ m |
| $\rho_i$ | Obstacle radius | m | Varies |
| $\beta_i(q)$ | Obstacle function | dimensionless | $\beta_i = \Vert q-c_i\Vert^2 - \rho_i^2$ |
| $Q_t$ | Process noise covariance | state units$^2$ | $Q_t = \text{diag}(\sigma_x^2, \sigma_y^2, \sigma_\theta^2)$ |
| $R_t$ | Measurement noise covariance | sensor units$^2$ | $R_t = \text{diag}(\sigma_{\text{range}}^2, \sigma_{\text{bearing}}^2)$ |
| $K_t$ | Kalman gain | dimensionless | Computed at each step |
| $F_t$ | Process Jacobian | dimensionless | $F_t = \partial f / \partial x$ |
| $H_t$ | Measurement Jacobian | dimensionless | $H_t = \partial h / \partial x$ |
| $P_t$ | State covariance | state units$^2$ | $P_0 = I \cdot 10^{-2}$ |
| $\text{TTC}$ | Time-to-Collision | s | $\text{TTC} > 0$ |
| $\alpha, \lambda$ | Dynamic PF tuning | dimensionless | $\alpha, \lambda > 0$ |
| $k$ | NF tuning exponent | dimensionless | $k \geq 1$ |
| $\gamma$ | NF scaling parameter | dimensionless | $\gamma > 0$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The NF/PF framework is foundational to **autonomous mobile robots (AMRs) in warehouses** (Amazon Kiva), **robotic surgery** (da Vinci), and **Mars rover path planning** (NASA's AutoNav).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Gradient of the Koditschek-Rimon NF

**Given:** $f(q) = \dfrac{\Vert q - q_d \Vert^2}{\left( \Vert q - q_d \Vert^{2k} + \beta(q) \right)^{1/k}}$

**Let** $N = \Vert q - q_d \Vert^2$ and $D = (N^k + \beta)^{1/k}$. So $f = N/D$.

**We compute** $\nabla f$ using the quotient rule:

$$\nabla f = \frac{D \cdot \nabla N - N \cdot \nabla D}{D^2}$$

**Step 1:** Compute $\nabla N$. Let $r = q - q_d$, so $N = r^T r$.

$$\nabla N = 2(q - q_d) = 2r$$

**Step 2:** Compute $\nabla D$. We have $D^k = N^k + \beta$, so:

$$\nabla(D^k) = k \cdot D^{k-1} \cdot \nabla D$$

By chain rule on the right side:

$$\nabla(D^k) = \nabla(N^k) + \nabla \beta = k \cdot N^{k-1} \cdot \nabla N + \nabla \beta$$

Equating:

$$k \cdot D^{k-1} \cdot \nabla D = k \cdot N^{k-1} \cdot (2r) + \nabla \beta$$

Solving for $\nabla D$:

$$\nabla D = \frac{N^{k-1} \cdot 2r + \frac{1}{k} \nabla \beta}{D^{k-1}}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the quotient rule. Since $D > 0$ in $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$:

$$\nabla f = \frac{D \cdot 2r - N \cdot \left( \frac{N^{k-1} \cdot 2r + \frac{1}{k}\nabla \beta}{D^{k-1}} \right)}{D^2}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $D^{k-1}$:

$$\nabla f = \frac{2r \cdot D^k - N \cdot N^{k-1} \cdot 2r - \frac{N}{k} \nabla \beta}{D^{k+1}}$$

Substitute $D^k = N^k + \beta$:

$$\nabla f = \frac{2r (N^k + \beta) - 2r \cdot N^k - \frac{N}{k} \nabla \beta}{D^{k+1}} = \frac{2r \beta - \frac{N}{k} \nabla \beta}{D^{k+1}}$$

**Final Compact Form:**

$$\boxed{\nabla f = \frac{2(q - q_d) \, \beta(q) - \frac{1}{k} \Vert q - q_d \Vert^2 \nabla \beta(q)}{(N^k + \beta)^{(k+1)/k}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The first term $2(q - q_d)\beta$ represents the *attractive gradient scaled by $\beta$* (vanishes on obstacle surface). The second term $-\frac{N}{k}\nabla\beta$ represents the *repulsive gradient scaled by distance-to-goal*. They are in **opposition** near obstacles — a hallmark of NF design.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Khatib Repulsive Force

**Given:** $U_{\text{rep}_i}(q) = \frac{1}{2} k_r \left( \dfrac{1}{d_i} - \dfrac{1}{Q^*} \right)^2$

Let $g = \dfrac{1}{d_i} - \dfrac{1}{Q^*}$, so $U_{\text{rep}} = \frac{1}{2} k_r g^2$.

The force is $F_{\text{rep}_i} = -\nabla U_{\text{rep}_i}$:

$$\nabla U_{\text{rep}_i} = k_r \, g \cdot \nabla g$$

Compute $\nabla g$:

$$\nabla g = \nabla \left( \frac{1}{d_i} \right) - 0 = -\frac{1}{d_i^2} \nabla d_i$$

The gradient of the signed distance $d_i = \Vert q - q_{\text{obs}_i} \Vert - \rho_i$ is:

$$\nabla d_i = \frac{q - q_{\text{obs}_i}}{\Vert q - q_{\text{obs}_i} \Vert}$$

Substituting:

$$\nabla U_{\text{rep}_i} = k_r \left( \frac{1}{d_i} - \frac{1}{Q^*} \right) \cdot \left( -\frac{1}{d_i^2} \right) \cdot \frac{q - q_{\text{obs}_i}}{\Vert q - q_{\text{obs}_i} \Vert}$$

**Final Form of Repulsive Force:**

$$\boxed{F_{\text{rep}_i}(q) = k_r \left( \frac{1}{d_i} - \frac{1}{Q^*} \right) \frac{1}{d_i^2} \cdot \frac{q - q_{\text{obs}_i}}{\Vert q - q_{\text{obs}_i} \Vert} \quad \text{for } d_i \leq Q^*}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Direction Analysis:** The force vector $\dfrac{q - q_{\text{obs}_i}}{\Vert q - q_{\text{obs}_i} \Vert}$ points **away from the obstacle**, which is physically correct for repulsion.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: EKF Update for Obstacle State Estimation

**Goal:** Estimate moving obstacle state $x_{\text{obs}} = [x_o, y_o, \dot{x}_o, \dot{y}_o]^T$ from range-bearing sensor measurements $z = [r, \phi]^T$.

### Motion Model (Constant Velocity):
$$x_{\text{obs}, t} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & \Delta t & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & \Delta t \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} x_{\text{obs}, t-1} + w_t, \quad w_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t)$$

### Measurement Model:
$$r_t = \sqrt{(x_o - x_r)^2 + (y_o - y_r)^2} + v_{r,t}$$
$$\phi_t = \text{atan2}(y_o - y_r, \; x_o - x_r) - \theta_r + v_{\phi,t}$$

where $v_{r,t}, v_{\phi,t}$ are zero-mean Gaussian noises with $R_t = \text{diag}(\sigma_r^2, \sigma_\phi^2)$.

### Measurement Jacobian $H_t$:
$$H_t = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial r}{\partial x_o} & \dfrac{\partial r}{\partial y_o} & \dfrac{\partial r}{\partial \dot{x}_o} & \dfrac{\partial r}{\partial \dot{y}_o} \\ \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial x_o} & \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial y_o} & \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial \dot{x}_o} & \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial \dot{y}_o} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 1:** Compute partial derivatives for $r$. Let $\Delta_x = x_o - x_r$, $\Delta_y = y_o - y_r$, and $d = \sqrt{\Delta_x^2 + \Delta_y^2}$.

$$\frac{\partial r}{\partial x_o} = \frac{\Delta_x}{d}, \quad \frac{\partial r}{\partial y_o} = \frac{\Delta_y}{d}, \quad \frac{\partial r}{\partial \dot{x}_o} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial r}{\partial \dot{y}_o} = 0$$

**Step 2:** Compute partial derivatives for $\phi = \text{atan2}(\Delta_y, \Delta_x)$. Using standard atan2 derivatives:

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x_o} = -\frac{\Delta_y}{d^2}, \quad \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y_o} = \frac{\Delta_x}{d^2}, \quad \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial \dot{x}_o} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial \dot{y}_o} = 0$$

**Final $H_t$ Matrix:**

$$H_t = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\Delta_x}{d} & \dfrac{\Delta_y}{d} & 0 & 0 \\ -\dfrac{\Delta_y}{d^2} & \dfrac{\Delta_x}{d^2} & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3:** Apply Kalman update. Innovation:
$$\nu_t = z_t - h(\hat{x}_{\text{obs}, t \mid t-1})$$

Innovation covariance:
$$S_t = H_t P_{t \mid t-1} H_t^T + R_t$$

Kalman gain:
$$K_t = P_{t \mid t-1} H_t^T S_t^{-1}$$

State update:
$$\hat{x}_{\text{obs}, t \mid t} = \hat{x}_{\text{obs}, t \mid t-1} + K_t \nu_t$$

Covariance update:
$$P_{t \mid t} = (I - K_t H_t) P_{t \mid t-1}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convergence Property:** Under observability conditions (sensor moves or obstacle is non-degenerate), $P_{t \mid t} \to P_\infty$ where $P_\infty$ solves the **Discrete Algebraic Riccati Equation (DARE)**.

## 3.4 Numerical Example: Static NF on a 2D Workspace

**Setup:**
- Goal: $q_d = (5, 5)$
- Obstacle 1: center $c_1 = (2, 3)$, radius $\rho_1 = 0.8$
- Obstacle 2: center $c_2 = (4, 2)$, radius $\rho_2 = 0.6$
- Tuning: $k = 2$

**Step 1:** Compute $\beta_1(q) = (q_x - 2)^2 + (q_y - 3)^2 - 0.64$
**Step 2:** Compute $\beta_2(q) = (q_x - 4)^2 + (q_y - 2)^2 - 0.36$
**Step 3:** $\beta(q) = \beta_1(q) \cdot \beta_2(q)$
**Step 4:** $f(q) = \dfrac{\Vert q - q_d \Vert^2}{\left( \Vert q - q_d \Vert^{4} + \beta(q) \right)^{1/2}}$

**Verification at $q_d = (5, 5)$:** $N = 0$, $\beta(q_d) > 0$, so $f(q_d) = 0$. ✓
**Verification at obstacle surface:** $\beta_i = 0$ implies $\beta = 0$, so denominator $\to 0$ and $f \to 1$. ✓

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Methods

| Method | Optimality | Local Minima Free | Dynamic Obstacle | Computational Cost | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Classical APF (Khatib) | Suboptimal | ❌ No | ❌ | $\mathcal{O}(M)$ | Simple indoor nav |
| Koditschek-Rimon NF | Provably convergent | ✅ Yes | ❌ | $\mathcal{O}(M)$ | Industrial arms |
| Dynamic APF (Ge-Cui) | Suboptimal | ❌ | ✅ Yes | $\mathcal{O}(M)$ | Crowded warehouses |
| Velocity Obstacle (VO) | Local optimal | ❌ | ✅ Yes | $\mathcal{O}(M^2)$ | Autonomous cars |
| RRT* with NF | Asymptotically optimal | ✅ Yes | ⚠ Partial | $\mathcal{O}(M \log M)$ | Outdoor UAVs |
| EKF + NF | Filtered | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ | Mobile robots |
| Particle Filter + NF | Multi-modal | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | $\mathcal{O}(N_{\text{p}} n^2)$ | Multi-hypothesis |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Always mention the **saddle-point property** when discussing NFs. Many students lose marks by calling APFs "navigation functions" — they are NOT because APFs lack the Morse/saddle property.
