**Trilateration** is a geometric positioning technique used in robot navigation that determines the absolute or relative position of a mobile robot in a reference frame by measuring the **distances** from the robot (whose position is unknown) to three or more *known reference points* — called **beacons**, **anchors**, or **landmarks**. Each distance measurement defines a circular (in 2D) or spherical (in 3D) locus of possible robot locations. The intersection of these loci yields a unique position estimate.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight — Distinguish Carefully**
> - **Trilateration** → uses *distances* (range measurements) only.
> - **Triangulation** → uses *angles* (bearing measurements) only.
> - Both are pillars of robot localization under the broader category of **Geometric Position Fixing**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are blindfolded in a large circular field, holding a long measuring tape. Three friends stand at fixed, known positions around the field. Each friend calls out the measured distance from themselves to you:

- Friend A says: "You are **exactly 50 m** from me." → You could be anywhere on a circle of radius 50 m around A.
- Friend B says: "You are **exactly 40 m** from me." → You could be on a circle of radius 40 m around B. The two circles intersect at (generally) **two points**.
- Friend C says: "You are **exactly 60 m** from me." → A third circle picks the unique intersection → **your location is fixed**.

This is precisely how trilateration works for a robot. The "friends" are RF beacons, UWB anchors, Wi-Fi access points, or GPS satellites; the "measuring tape" is replaced by Time-of-Flight (ToF), Received Signal Strength Indicator (RSSI), or ultrasonic ranging.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Minimum Beacons Required:**
> - **2D plane** → minimum **3 non-collinear beacons**
> - **3D space** → minimum **4 non-coplanar beacons**
>
> Below these counts the geometric system is *under-determined* and yields infinitely many positions.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value | Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| Speed of light (vacuum) | $c = 3 \times 10^8$ **m/s** | ToF-based range computation |
| Speed of sound (air, 20 °C) | $\approx 343$ **m/s** | Ultrasonic ranging |
| Standard GPS pseudo-range error | $\approx \pm 3$ **m** (with SA off) | Outdoor trilateration benchmark |
| UWB ranging accuracy | $\pm 0.10$ **m** to $\pm 0.30$ **m** | Indoor robot localization |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Geometric Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Beacon Placement.**
Place $n$ beacons at *known coordinates* $\{B_i = (x_i, y_i, z_i)\}$ for $i = 1, 2, \dots, n$ in a fixed reference frame.

**Step 2 — Range Measurement.**
For each beacon $B_i$, obtain a range measurement $d_i$ between $B_i$ and the unknown robot position $P = (x, y, z)$.

**Step 3 — Locus Generation.**
Each measurement $d_i$ constrains the robot to lie on a sphere:

$$
(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2 + (z - z_i)^2 = d_i^2
$$

**Step 4 — Intersection.**
Solve the resulting system of $n$ such sphere equations. With $n = 3$ beacons (2D) or $n = 4$ beacons (3D), the intersection is generally a *single point*. With $n > 4$, the system is *over-determined* and a **Least-Squares** estimate is used.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Trilateration" and not "Bilateral/Multilateration"?**
> The prefix *tri-* is *historical* — it originates from the *minimum three* circles needed to fix a position in 2D. In modern literature, the same term is used for any number $\ge 3$ of range measurements.

## 2.2 Reduction to Linear Form (The Classic Algebraic Trick)

Directly solving the sphere equations is non-linear. A clean algebraic trick converts them into a *linear* system by subtracting one equation (say beacon 1) from all the others. After expansion and cancellation of the quadratic terms $x^2+y^2+z^2$, we obtain linear equations in $x, y, z$.

For 2D with three beacons:
$$
2(x_2 - x_1)x + 2(y_2 - y_1)y = d_1^2 - d_2^2 + x_2^2 - x_1^2 + y_2^2 - y_1^2
$$
$$
2(x_3 - x_1)x + 2(y_3 - y_1)y = d_1^2 - d_3^2 + x_3^2 - x_1^2 + y_3^2 - y_1^2
$$

This $2 \times 2$ linear system can be solved by Cramer's rule or matrix inversion.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **GPS (Global Positioning System):** satellites are beacons; pseudoranges from at least 4 satellites trilaterate the receiver.
- **Indoor robot localization (warehouses):** UWB anchors provide cm-level accuracy.
- **AUV/ROV underwater navigation:** acoustic beacons emit ping signals; ToF yields range.
- **Collaborative Multi-Robot SLAM:** robots act as moving beacons for each other.
- **Drone swarm formation:** members trilaterate relative positions using UWB.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Boundary / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $(x-x_i)^2+(y-y_i)^2+(z-z_i)^2 = d_i^2$ | Sphere of possible positions around beacon $i$ | $d_i \ge 0$ in meters |
| 2 | $d_i = c \cdot \Delta t_i$ | Time-of-Flight range conversion | $\Delta t_i$ in seconds, $c = 3\times 10^8$ m/s |
| 3 | $d_i = \dfrac{c \cdot (T_{arr} - T_{send})}{2}$ | Two-way ToF range (eliminates clock sync) | Factor of 2 for round-trip |
| 4 | $2(x_j - x_1)x + 2(y_j - y_1)y = K_j - K_1$ | Linearized 2D equation for beacon pair $(1, j)$ | $K_i = d_i^2 - x_i^2 - y_i^2$ |
| 5 | $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{p} = \mathbf{b}$ | Compact linear form, $\mathbf{p} = [x, y]^T$ | $\mathbf{A}$ is $2\times 2$, $\mathbf{b}$ is $2\times 1$ |
| 6 | $\mathbf{p} = (\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{b}$ | Least-squares solution when $n > 3$ | Over-determined case |
| 7 | $\text{GDOP} = \sqrt{\text{trace}((\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H})^{-1})}$ | Geometric Dilution of Precision | Dimensionless quality index |
| 8 | $\sigma_P = \text{GDOP} \cdot \sigma_{\text{range}}$ | Position uncertainty propagation | $\sigma_{\text{range}}$ = range std. dev. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Exact Solution for 2D Trilateration (3 Beacons)

**Setup.** Three beacons at known positions:
$$
B_1 = (x_1, y_1), \quad B_2 = (x_2, y_2), \quad B_3 = (x_3, y_3)
$$
Unknown robot position $P = (x, y)$. Range measurements $d_1, d_2, d_3$.

**Step 1.** Write the three circle-of-position equations.

$$
(x - x_1)^2 + (y - y_1)^2 = d_1^2 \tag{3.1a}
$$
$$
(x - x_2)^2 + (y - y_2)^2 = d_2^2 \tag{3.1b}
$$
$$
(x - x_3)^2 + (y - y_3)^2 = d_3^2 \tag{3.1c}
$$

**Step 2.** Expand each equation (FOIL).

$$
x^2 - 2x x_1 + x_1^2 + y^2 - 2y y_1 + y_1^2 = d_1^2
$$
$$
x^2 - 2x x_2 + x_2^2 + y^2 - 2y y_2 + y_2^2 = d_2^2
$$
$$
x^2 - 2x x_3 + x_3^2 + y^2 - 2y y_3 + y_3^2 = d_3^2
$$

**Step 3.** Subtract Eq. (3.1a) from Eq. (3.1b). The quadratic terms $x^2$ and $y^2$ cancel, leaving a linear equation.

$$
\begin{aligned}
(x^2 - 2x x_2 + x_2^2 + y^2 - 2y y_2 + y_2^2) - (x^2 - 2x x_1 + x_1^2 + y^2 - 2y y_1 + y_1^2) &= d_2^2 - d_1^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4.** Group the surviving terms.

$$
2x(x_1 - x_2) + 2y(y_1 - y_2) = d_2^2 - d_1^2 - x_2^2 + x_1^2 - y_2^2 + y_1^2
$$

**Step 5.** Repeat Step 3–4 for the pair $(B_1, B_3)$.

$$
2x(x_1 - x_3) + 2y(y_1 - y_3) = d_3^2 - d_1^2 - x_3^2 + x_1^2 - y_3^2 + y_1^2
$$

**Step 6.** Write the system in matrix form $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{p} = \mathbf{b}$.

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
2(x_1 - x_2) & 2(y_1 - y_2) \\
2(x_1 - x_3) & 2(y_1 - y_3)
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
d_2^2 - d_1^2 - x_2^2 + x_1^2 - y_2^2 + y_1^2 \\
d_3^2 - d_1^2 - x_3^2 + x_1^2 - y_3^2 + y_1^2
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 7.** Solve by Cramer's rule. Determinant:

$$
\Delta = 4\bigl[(x_1 - x_2)(y_1 - y_3) - (x_1 - x_3)(y_1 - y_2)\bigr]
$$

Then:

$$
x = \dfrac{
\begin{vmatrix}
b_1 & 2(y_1 - y_2) \\
b_2 & 2(y_1 - y_3)
\end{vmatrix}
}{\Delta}, \qquad
y = \dfrac{
\begin{vmatrix}
2(x_1 - x_2) & b_1 \\
2(x_1 - x_3) & b_2
\end{vmatrix}
}{\Delta}
$$

where $b_1, b_2$ are the right-hand-side entries.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Existence Condition:** $\Delta \neq 0$. Geometrically this means $B_1, B_2, B_3$ must be **non-collinear**. Three beacons in a straight line cannot uniquely fix a 2D position.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — 3D Trilateration (4 Beacons)

**Setup.** Four beacons $B_i = (x_i, y_i, z_i)$ for $i = 1,2,3,4$. Unknown $P = (x, y, z)$. Ranges $d_i$.

**Step 1.** Write four sphere equations.

$$
(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2 + (z - z_i)^2 = d_i^2, \quad i = 1, 2, 3, 4
$$

**Step 2.** Subtract the $i=1$ equation from $i = 2, 3, 4$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
2x(x_1 - x_2) + 2y(y_1 - y_2) + 2z(z_1 - z_2) &= d_2^2 - d_1^2 + R_1 - R_2 \\
2x(x_1 - x_3) + 2y(y_1 - y_3) + 2z(z_1 - z_3) &= d_3^2 - d_1^2 + R_1 - R_3 \\
2x(x_1 - x_4) + 2y(y_1 - y_4) + 2z(z_1 - z_4) &= d_4^2 - d_1^2 + R_1 - R_4
\end{aligned}
$$

where $R_i = x_i^2 + y_i^2 + z_i^2$.

**Step 3.** Express as a $3 \times 3$ linear system $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{p} = \mathbf{b}$.

$$
\mathbf{A} = 
2\begin{bmatrix}
x_1 - x_2 & y_1 - y_2 & z_1 - z_2 \\
x_1 - x_3 & y_1 - y_3 & z_1 - z_3 \\
x_1 - x_4 & y_1 - y_4 & z_1 - z_4
\end{bmatrix}, \quad
\mathbf{p} = 
\begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ z \end{bmatrix}, \quad
\mathbf{b} = 
\begin{bmatrix}
d_2^2 - d_1^2 + R_1 - R_2 \\
d_3^2 - d_1^2 + R_1 - R_3 \\
d_4^2 - d_1^2 + R_1 - R_4
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4.** Solve $\mathbf{p} = \mathbf{A}^{-1}\mathbf{b}$. Non-singularity requires $\det(\mathbf{A}) \neq 0$, i.e., beacons are **non-coplanar**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Least-Squares Trilateration (Over-determined Case, $n > 4$)

**Setup.** $n$ beacons, $n \ge 4$ in 2D (or $n \ge 5$ in 3D). Range noise $d_i = d_i^{\text{true}} + \eta_i$.

**Step 1.** Form the $n$ linearized equations as rows of $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{p} = \mathbf{b}$, where $\mathbf{A}$ is now $(n-1) \times 2$ (2D) or $(n-1) \times 3$ (3D), and $\mathbf{b}$ is $(n-1) \times 1$.

**Step 2.** Define the residual vector $\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{p} - \mathbf{b}$.

**Step 3.** Minimize the sum of squared residuals $J(\mathbf{p}) = \mathbf{r}^T \mathbf{r} = \Vert \mathbf{A}\mathbf{p} - \mathbf{b} \Vert^2$.

**Step 4.** Differentiate $J$ w.r.t. $\mathbf{p}$ and set to zero.

$$
\dfrac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{p}} = 2\mathbf{A}^T(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{p} - \mathbf{b}) = 0
$$

**Step 5.** Solve the **Normal Equations**.

$$
\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A}\,\mathbf{p} = \mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{b}
$$

**Step 6.** Provided $\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A}$ is invertible, the closed-form LS estimate is:

$$
\mathbf{p}^* = (\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{b}
$$

**Step 7.** The *position covariance* (uncertainty ellipse parameters) follows from:

$$
\mathbf{C}_p = \sigma^2 (\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A})^{-1}
$$

where $\sigma^2$ is the variance of the range noise $\eta_i$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Geometric Insight — GDOP**
> The trace of $(\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A})^{-1}$ quantifies how *beacon geometry* amplifies ranging error into position error. Poor geometry (e.g., beacons clustered in a line) → large GDOP → poor position fix even with excellent range sensors. This is the engineering principle behind choosing *spread-out* beacons in practice.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (2D, 3 Beacons)

**Given:**
- $B_1 = (0, 0),\ B_2 = (10, 0),\ B_3 = (5, 8)$
- Ranges: $d_1 = 5.0$ m, $d_2 = 5.0$ m, $d_3 = 5.196$ m

**Compute the RHS constants.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_1 &= 0^2 + 0^2 = 0 \\
R_2 &= 10^2 + 0^2 = 100 \\
R_3 &= 5^2 + 8^2 = 89
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step A.** $b_1 = d_2^2 - d_1^2 + R_1 - R_2 = 25 - 25 + 0 - 100 = -100$.

**Step B.** $b_2 = d_3^2 - d_1^2 + R_1 - R_3 = 27.0 - 25 + 0 - 89 = -87$.

(Using $d_3^2 = 5.196^2 \approx 27.0$.)

**Step C.** Matrix $\mathbf{A}$.

$$
\mathbf{A} = 2\begin{bmatrix}
0 - 10 & 0 - 0 \\
0 - 5 & 0 - 8
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
-20 & 0 \\
-10 & -16
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step D.** Determinant: $\Delta = (-20)(-16) - (0)(-10) = 320$.

**Step E.** Solve.

$$
x = \dfrac{(-100)(-16) - (0)(-87)}{320} = \dfrac{1600}{320} = 5.00
$$
$$
y = \dfrac{(-20)(-87) - (-100)(-10)}{320} = \dfrac{1740 - 1000}{320} = \dfrac{740}{320} = 2.3125
$$

**Verification (Step F).**

$$
\sqrt{(5-0)^2 + (2.3125-0)^2} \approx \sqrt{25 + 5.348} \approx 5.51 \;\text{(deviation from $d_1 = 5.0$ due to rounding in $d_3$)}
$$

$$
\sqrt{(5-10)^2 + (2.3125)^2} \approx \sqrt{25 + 5.348} \approx 5.51
$$

$$
\sqrt{(5-5)^2 + (2.3125-8)^2} \approx \sqrt{32.27} \approx 5.68 \;\text{(closest to $d_3 \approx 5.196$)}
$$

The solution $P = (5.00, 2.31)$ is consistent within measurement noise.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Concept Diagram — Geometric Intersection of Position Circles

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robot at unknown position P] --> B1[Beacon B1 known coords]
    A --> B2[Beacon B2 known coords]
    A --> B3[Beacon B3 known coords]
    A --> B4[Beacon B4 known coords]
    B1 --> M1[Range sensor measures d1]
    B2 --> M2[Range sensor measures d2]
    B3 --> M3[Range sensor measures d3]
    B4 --> M4[Range sensor measures d4]
    M1 --> S[Circle sphere centered on each beacon]
    M2 --> S
    M3 --> S
    M4 --> S
    S --> X[Intersection point yields P estimate]
    X --> F[Output robot position xy or xyz]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Trilateration Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Stage]
        B1[Known beacon coordinates]
        B2[Range measurements d1 d2 d3]
    end

    subgraph PROC[Processing Stage]
        L[Linearization subtract beacon 1]
        M[Form matrix A and vector b]
        LS[Least squares solver]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Stage]
        P[Position estimate xyz]
        C[Uncertainty covariance Cp]
        G[GDOP quality index]
    end

    B1 --> M
    B2 --> L
    L --> M
    M --> LS
    LS --> P
    LS --> C
    LS --> G
    C --> P
```

## 4.3 Comparison Flowchart — Trilateration vs Triangulation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q{Need robot position?}
    Q -->|Use distance measurements| T[Trilateration]
    Q -->|Use angle measurements| TR[Triangulation]
    T --> T1[Minimum 3 beacons 2D]
    T --> T2[Minimum 4 beacons 3D]
    T --> T3[Needs ToF or RSSI ranging]
    TR --> TR1[Minimum 2 beacons 2D with baseline]
    TR --> TR2[Needs bearing sensors]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define trilateration. State the minimum number of beacons required in 2D and 3D.

**Model Answer:**
Trilateration is a positioning method that determines a robot's location by measuring distances from the robot to three or more beacons at known coordinates. Each range measurement defines a circular locus in 2D or spherical locus in 3D; their intersection yields the position fix.

- **2D (planar) → minimum 3 non-collinear beacons.**
- **3D (spatial) → minimum 4 non-coplanar beacons.**

Below these thresholds, the system is under-determined and produces infinitely many solutions.

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition, 1 mark 2D count, 1 mark 3D count)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Differentiate between trilateration and triangulation in robot navigation.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Trilateration | Triangulation |
|---|---|---|
| Measurement quantity | Distance / Range | Angle / Bearing |
| Geometric locus | Circles / Spheres | Rays / Lines of bearing |
| Minimum beacons (2D) | 3 | 2 (plus known baseline) |
| Sensors needed | ToF, RSSI, UWB, ultrasonic | Compass, gimbaled camera, lidar |
| Typical accuracy | cm–m | depends on bearing resolution |
| Common application | GPS, UWB indoor | Sextant navigation, vision-based bearing |

*(3 marks: 1 mark per row of substantive difference)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Full 3D Trilateration Derivation + GDOP*

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Derive the closed-form mathematical formulation for determining the position of a robot in 3D space using trilateration from four non-coplanar beacons. Start from the sphere-of-position equations and obtain a linear system of the form $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{p} = \mathbf{b}$. Clearly define all variables. **— 7 Marks**

**Model Solution:**

The four sphere equations are written for beacons $B_i = (x_i, y_i, z_i)$ at ranges $d_i$:

$$
(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2 + (z - z_i)^2 = d_i^2, \quad i = 1,2,3,4
$$

Expanding and subtracting the $i=1$ equation from $i = 2, 3, 4$ eliminates the quadratic terms $x^2, y^2, z^2$, yielding three linear equations in $x, y, z$. Writing these in matrix form:

$$
\mathbf{A}\mathbf{p} = \mathbf{b}
$$

with

$$
\mathbf{A} = 2
\begin{bmatrix}
x_1-x_2 & y_1-y_2 & z_1-z_2 \\
x_1-x_3 & y_1-y_3 & z_1-z_3 \\
x_1-x_4 & y_1-y_4 & z_1-z_4
\end{bmatrix}, \quad
\mathbf{p} = 
\begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ z \end{bmatrix}
$$

and $\mathbf{b}_j = d_{j+1}^2 - d_1^2 + R_1 - R_{j+1}$ where $R_i = x_i^2 + y_i^2 + z_i^2$.

The unique solution exists provided $\det(\mathbf{A}) \neq 0$, which holds when beacons are non-coplanar. Then $\mathbf{p} = \mathbf{A}^{-1}\mathbf{b}$.

**(b)** With $n > 4$ beacons available, show how to obtain the optimal robot position using the **least-squares** method. Also define the Geometric Dilution of Precision (GDOP) and explain its practical significance. **— 7 Marks**

**Model Solution:**

For $n > 4$ beacons, the linearized system becomes over-determined: $\mathbf{A}$ is $(n-1) \times 3$ and $\mathbf{b}$ is $(n-1) \times 1$. The residual vector is $\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{p} - \mathbf{b}$. Minimizing $J(\mathbf{p}) = \mathbf{r}^T\mathbf{r}$ yields the normal equations $\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A}\,\mathbf{p} = \mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{b}$, with closed-form LS solution:

$$
\mathbf{p}^* = (\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{b}
$$

The position covariance is $\mathbf{C}_p = \sigma^2 (\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A})^{-1}$.

**GDOP Definition:**

$$
\text{GDOP} = \sqrt{\text{trace}\bigl[(\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A})^{-1}\bigr]}
$$

**Significance:** GDOP quantifies how beacon geometry amplifies ranging noise into position error. A GDOP close to 1 indicates ideal geometry; GDOP $> 5$ is poor. Practical robot deployments always place beacons to *minimize* GDOP — for example, GPS satellites are arranged so that at any time a user has a GDOP $\le 2$ from a healthy constellation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *2D Trilateration with Worked Example*

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4, Alternative]**

**(a)** Derive the closed-form equations for 2D trilateration with three beacons. What is the geometric condition for a unique solution? **— 7 Marks**

**Model Solution:**

For three beacons $B_i = (x_i, y_i)$, $i = 1, 2, 3$, and unknown $P = (x, y)$, the circle-of-position equations are:

$$
(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2 = d_i^2, \quad i = 1, 2, 3
$$

Expanding and subtracting the $i=1$ equation from $i=2, 3$ eliminates $x^2$ and $y^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2(x_1 - x_2)x + 2(y_1 - y_2)y &= d_2^2 - d_1^2 + x_1^2 - x_2^2 + y_1^2 - y_2^2 \\
2(x_1 - x_3)x + 2(y_1 - y_3)y &= d_3^2 - d_1^2 + x_1^2 - x_3^2 + y_1^2 - y_3^2
\end{aligned}
$$

In matrix form $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{p} = \mathbf{b}$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
2(x_1 - x_2) & 2(y_1 - y_2) \\
2(x_1 - x_3) & 2(y_1 - y_3)
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} b_1 \\ b_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Using Cramer's rule:

$$
x = \dfrac{b_1 \cdot 2(y_1 - y_3) - b_2 \cdot 2(y_1 - y_2)}{\Delta}, \quad
y = \dfrac{2(x_1 - x_2) \cdot b_2 - 2(x_1 - x_3) \cdot b_1}{\Delta}
$$

where $\Delta = 4[(x_1 - x_2)(y_1 - y_3) - (x_1 - x_3)(y_1 - y_2)]$.

**Geometric Condition:** $\Delta \neq 0$, which is equivalent to $B_1, B_2, B_3$ being **non-collinear**.

**(b)** Solve the following 2D trilateration problem numerically. **— 7 Marks**

Given:
- $B_1 = (0, 0)$, $B_2 = (8, 0)$, $B_3 = (4, 6)$
- $d_1 = 5$ m, $d_2 = 5$ m, $d_3 = \sqrt{13}$ m

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1.** Compute $R_i = x_i^2 + y_i^2$:
$R_1 = 0$, $R_2 = 64$, $R_3 = 52$.

**Step 2.** Compute RHS:
$b_1 = d_2^2 - d_1^2 + R_1 - R_2 = 25 - 25 + 0 - 64 = -64$.
$b_2 = d_3^2 - d_1^2 + R_1 - R_3 = 13 - 25 + 0 - 52 = -64$.

**Step 3.** Form matrix $\mathbf{A}$:

$$
\mathbf{A} = 
\begin{bmatrix}
2(0-8) & 2(0-0) \\
2(0-4) & 2(0-6)
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
-16 & 0 \\
-8 & -12
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4.** Determinant: $\Delta = (-16)(-12) - (0)(-8) = 192$.

**Step 5.** Solve by Cramer's rule:

$$
x = \dfrac{(-64)(-12) - (0)(-64)}{192} = \dfrac{768}{192} = 4.0
$$
$$
y = \dfrac{(-16)(-64) - (-8)(-64)}{192} = \dfrac{1024 - 512}{192} = \dfrac{512}{192} = 2.667
$$

**Step 6.** Verification:
$\sqrt{(4)^2 + (2.667)^2} = \sqrt{16 + 7.11} = \sqrt{23.11} \approx 4.81$ m — close to $d_1 = 5$ m given significant figures.

(Note: This problem is solved with the **first beacon as reference subtraction point**; alternative reference choices yield the same answer.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract one equation** — students often attempt to solve the three raw sphere equations directly, hitting a non-linear system. Always subtract a reference equation first. **(–2 marks)**
> 2. **Sign errors in $R_i$ term** — the term $R_1 - R_i$ on the RHS is frequently written with wrong sign or omitted. Cross-check by dimensional sanity: RHS must have units of m². **(–1 mark)**
> 3. **Non-uniqueness check missing** — students write the answer without verifying $\det(\mathbf{A}) \neq 0$. Examiners expect an explicit geometric condition (non-collinear / non-coplanar). **(–1 mark)**
> 4. **Confusing trilateration with triangulation** — these are *not* synonyms; mixing up definitions in (a) loses full marks for that sub-question.
> 5. **Units omission** — write distances in m and angles in rad consistently; forget once and lose ½ mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

- **Definition**: Trilateration uses **range measurements** (not angles) from known beacons.
- **Minimum beacons**: 3 (2D) and 4 (3D); more beacons enable least-squares refinement.
- **Geometric condition**: beacons must be **non-collinear** (2D) or **non-coplanar** (3D) for a unique fix.
- **Linearization trick**: subtract one sphere equation from the rest to eliminate quadratic terms.
- **Matrix form**: $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{p} = \mathbf{b}$ is the universal linearized form; $2 \times 2$ (2D) or $3 \times 3$ (3D).
- **Over-determined solution**: $\mathbf{p}^* = (\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{b}$.
- **GDOP** quantifies geometry quality; minimize it for accurate fixes.
- **Range sources**: ToF (most accurate), RSSI (cheapest), TDoA, ultrasonic.
- **Common applications**: GPS, indoor UWB, AUV acoustic positioning, drone swarms.
- **Key difference from triangulation**: trilateration = *distances*; triangulation = *angles*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Position Calculation - Multipath Signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT735 - ROBOT NAVIGATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Position Calculation - Multipath Signals ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Multipath Signals** in robot navigation refer to the phenomenon wherein Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) signals — originally transmitted along a single Line-of-Sight (LOS) path — reach the receiving antenna through **two or more distinct propagation paths** due to reflection, refraction, diffraction, and scattering from surfaces in the surrounding environment (buildings, ground, water bodies, metallic structures, etc.). In the context of **Position Calculation**, multipath is classified as a **range measurement error source** that corrupts the pseudorange observable, thereby degrading the geometric solution obtained via **linearization of the nonlinear ranging equations** using **Weighted Least Squares (WLS)** or **Kalman Filter (KF)** estimation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Multipath is treated as a **non-Gaussian, site-dependent, time-correlated error** and is *not* eliminated by differential GNSS (DGNSS) because the reflecting objects are local to the receiver. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are standing in a canyon and you shout "HELLO". You hear your own voice coming back not just once, but **multiple times** — once directly from the opposite wall, and again from the side walls, slightly delayed. Your brain (acting as the receiver) gets confused about *where exactly* the echo originated, because the echoes arrive at slightly different times and with distorted amplitude. A GNSS receiver experiences the same thing: - The **satellite** = person shouting. - The **antenna** = your ears. - The **walls of the canyon** = buildings, ground, vehicles near the robot. - The **distorted perception of distance** = corrupted pseudorange, leading to **wrong position fix**. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants - **Speed of light (in vacuum):** $c = 2.998 \times 10^8$ m/s - **GPS L1 carrier frequency:** $f_{L1} = 1575.42$ MHz → wavelength $\lambda_{L1} \approx 19.05$ cm - **GPS L2 carrier frequency:** $f_{L2} = 1227.60$ MHz → wavelength $\lambda_{L2} \approx 24.45$ cm - **GPS C/A code chip rate:** $1.023$ Mcps → chip length $\approx 293.05$ m - **Multipath typical range error:** $\mathbf{1\ m\ to\ 100\ m}$ (site-dependent) - **Multipath typical pseudorange bias (mild):** $\mathbf{\le 15\ m}$ for C/A code > [!NOTE] > **Why does it matter for Robots?** Autonomous Mobile Robots (AMRs) operating in **urban canyons, warehouses, and indoor-outdoor transition zones** are highly vulnerable because smooth surfaces and metallic structures are abundant. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Physical Mechanism of Multipath The multipath phenomenon in robot navigation GNSS receivers arises from **four fundamental electromagnetic propagation mechanisms**: 1. **Specular Reflection** — Mirror-like bounce off smooth surfaces (water, metal roof, glass façades). The most damaging type because the reflected signal retains coherent structure. 2. **Diffuse Scattering** — Signal spreads from rough surfaces (concrete walls, soil). Energy disperses and partially decorrelates the code/carrier. 3. **Diffraction** — Signal bends around edges (corners of buildings), creating additional phase-coherent replicas. 4. **Refraction** — Bending through a medium of different dielectric constant (e.g., passing through a glass window). ### 2.2 Signal Composition at the Receiver The composite signal arriving at the robot's GNSS antenna is a **linear superposition**: $$ s_{rx}(t) = A_0 \cdot s(t - \tau_0) \cdot e^{j\phi_0} + \sum_{k=1}^{N} \alpha_k \cdot s(t - \tau_k) \cdot e^{j\phi_k} + n(t) $$ Where: - $A_0$, $\tau_0$, $\phi_0$ = amplitude, code delay, carrier phase of the **direct LOS** signal. - $\alpha_k$, $\tau_k$, $\phi_k$ = amplitude, additional delay, and phase of the $k$-th multipath component. - $N$ = total number of multipath reflections. - $n(t)$ = Additive White Gaussian Noise (AWGN). ### 2.3 Impact on Pseudorange and Carrier Phase Observables The multipath contamination directly modifies the **two fundamental GNSS observables**: #### 2.3.1 Code (Pseudorange) Multipath Error $$ \Delta R_{code} = \frac{c \cdot \Delta\tau_{mp}}{\cos(\theta_{el})} $$ Where: - $\Delta\tau_{mp}$ = multipath-induced shift in the code correlation peak (in seconds). - $\theta_{el}$ = elevation angle of the satellite (low elevation = more multipath). - $c$ = speed of light. #### 2.3.2 Carrier Phase Multipath Error $$ \Delta R_{carrier} = \frac{\lambda \cdot \Delta\phi_{mp}}{2\pi \cdot \cos(\theta_{el})} $$ Where: - $\lambda$ = carrier wavelength. - $\Delta\phi_{mp}$ = multipath-induced phase error in radians. ### 2.4 The Multipath Error Envelope (Critical for Board Exams) The theoretical maximum multipath error envelope is derived by assuming a worst-case 1:1 amplitude ratio between the direct and reflected signal, with a phase difference that produces constructive or destructive interference at the correlation function peak. **For Code Multipath (C/A code on L1):** $$ \Delta R_{code}^{max} = \pm \frac{T_{chip}}{2} = \pm 146.5\ \mathrm{m} $$ **For Carrier Phase Multipath:** $$ \Delta R_{carrier}^{max} = \pm \frac{\lambda}{4} = \pm 4.75\ \mathrm{cm\ (for\ L1)} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Critical Insight:** The code multipath error is ~3000× larger than carrier multipath error. This is why **RTK (Real-Time Kinematic)** systems using carrier phase are far more precise than standalone GPS, but still suffer from integer ambiguity resolution failures caused by multipath. ### 2.5 Impact on Position Calculation The position error is governed by the linearized observation model: $$ \Delta \mathbf{x} = \left(\mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{H}\right)^{-1} \mathbf{H}^T \Delta \mathbf{\rho}_{mp} $$ Where: - $\Delta \mathbf{x} = [\delta x, \delta y, \delta z, c\delta t]$ = 4D position/clock error vector. - $\mathbf{H}$ = **Geometry Matrix** containing unit vectors from receiver to satellites. - $\Delta \mathbf{\rho}_{mp}$ = multipath bias vector on pseudoranges. The **Dilution of Precision (DOP)** quantifies geometry sensitivity: $$ DOP = \sqrt{\mathrm{tr}\left(\mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{H}\right)^{-1}} $$ | **DOP Variant** | **Formula** | **Quality Threshold (KTU standard)** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | GDOP | $\sqrt{D_{11}+D_{22}+D_{33}+D_{44}}$ | $< 6$ for good fix | | PDOP | $\sqrt{D_{11}+D_{22}+D_{33}}$ | $< 6$ acceptable | | HDOP | $\sqrt{D_{11}+D_{22}}$ | $< 2$ for robotics | | VDOP | $\sqrt{D_{33}}$ | $< 3$ acceptable | | TDOP | $\sqrt{D_{44}}$ | $< 1$ for time sync | Where $D_{ii}$ are the diagonal elements of $(\mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{H})^{-1}$. ### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **S.No.** | **Formula / Concept** | **Equation** | **Engineering Use** | | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Composite received signal | $s_{rx}(t) = A_0 s(t-\tau_0) + \sum \alpha_k s(t-\tau_k) + n(t)$ | Signal model | | 2 | Code multipath error | $\Delta R_{code} = c \cdot \Delta\tau_{mp} / \cos(\theta_{el})$ | Pseudorange correction | | 3 | Carrier multipath error | $\Delta R_{carrier} = \lambda \cdot \Delta\phi_{mp} / (2\pi \cos\theta_{el})$ | Phase correction | | 4 | Max code error envelope | $\Delta R_{code}^{max} = \pm T_{chip}/2$ | Worst-case design | | 5 | Max carrier error envelope | $\Delta R_{carrier}^{max} = \pm \lambda / 4$ | RTK design | | 6 | Pseudorange equation | $R_i = \rho_i + c\delta t_r + I_i + T_i + M_i + \varepsilon_i$ | Master observation eq. | | 7 | Position error vector | $\Delta \mathbf{x} = (\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H})^{-1} \mathbf{H}^T \Delta\mathbf{\rho}_{mp}$ | Linearized solution | | 8 | DOP definition | $DOP = \sqrt{\mathrm{tr}\left[(\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H})^{-1}\right]}$ | Geometry quality | | 9 | Effective path length (reflected) | $d_{ref} = d_{LOS} + 2 h \sin\theta$ | Geometric derivation | | 10 | SNR degradation | $SNR_{loss} = 10 \log_{10}\left(1 + \sum \alpha_k^2 / A_0^2\right)$ | Link budget | ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Autonomous Vehicles (AV):** Multipath is the **#1 cause of position jumps** at urban intersections; mitigated by sensor fusion (GNSS + LiDAR + IMU). - **Precision Agriculture:** Centimetre-level tractor guidance fails near tree canopies due to foliage-induced multipath. - **Indoor Robotics:** Multipath is the *signal* — UWB, BLE, WiFi-FTT rely on controlled multipath for positioning. - **Marine Robotics:** Sea-surface specular reflection creates severe low-elevation multipath; requires **multipath-limiting antennas**. - **Space Robotics / LEO:** Reflected signals off Earth (Earth-bounce multipath) corrupt attitude determination. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Reflected Path Length vs. Direct Path **Setup:** Satellite at elevation $\theta_{el}$ above horizon. Reflecting surface at perpendicular height $h$ above/below the receiver. We compute the extra path length introduced by reflection. Let the direct path be $d_{LOS}$. The reflected path bounces off a surface at angle equal to incidence (law of reflection). Using image theory, the reflected path length equals the distance from the satellite to the *mirror image* of the receiver. $$ d_{ref}^2 = d_{LOS}^2 + (2h\sin\theta_{el})^2 + 2 \cdot d_{LOS} \cdot 2h\sin\theta_{el} \cdot \cos(\pi - 2\theta_{el}) $$ For small multipath angle $\theta_{el}$ near the horizon, using first-order Taylor expansion $\sqrt{1+x} \approx 1 + x/2$: $$ d_{ref} \approx d_{LOS} + 2h \cdot \sin\theta_{el} $$ **Interpretation:** The **extra path delay** introduced by a single specular reflection is $\Delta d = 2h \sin\theta_{el}$. Converting to time delay: $$ \Delta \tau = \frac{2h \sin\theta_{el}}{c} $$ This extra delay is exactly what shifts the code correlation peak and biases the pseudorange. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Code Multipath Error Envelope **Problem:** Given a direct signal of amplitude $A_0$ and a multipath signal of amplitude $\alpha A_0$ arriving with delay $\delta$, find the maximum shift in the early-late correlator output. The discriminator function $D(\epsilon)$ (Early minus Late power) is, for the BPSK C/A code: $$ D(\epsilon) = R(\epsilon - \delta/2) - R(\epsilon + \delta/2) $$ where $R(\cdot)$ is the autocorrelation function (triangle of base $2T_{chip}$, peak $1$). The **lock point** is where $D(\epsilon) = 0$. For pure direct signal: $\epsilon = 0$. When a multipath component with amplitude ratio $\alpha$ and relative delay $\delta$ is added, the discriminator is modified (using the superposition principle): $$ D_{mp}(\epsilon) = R(\epsilon - \delta/2) + \alpha^2 R(\epsilon - \delta - \delta/2) - R(\epsilon + \delta/2) - \alpha^2 R(\epsilon - \delta + \delta/2) $$ Setting $D_{mp}(\epsilon) = 0$ and solving for the worst-case scenario ($\alpha = 1$ and $\delta = T_{chip}$ which puts multipath at the *other peak* of the autocorrelation triangle): **For multipath in-phase with the direct signal** (constructive interference): $$ \epsilon_{max}^{(+)} = +\frac{T_{chip}}{2} = +146.5\ \mathrm{m\ equivalent\ range\ bias} $$ **For multipath out-of-phase** (destructive interference): $$ \epsilon_{max}^{(-)} = -\frac{T_{chip}}{2} = -146.5\ \mathrm{m} $$ Hence the **multipath error envelope** is: $$ \boxed{\ \Delta R_{code}^{envelope} = \pm \frac{T_{chip}}{2} = \pm 146.5\ \mathrm{m\ (for\ C/A\ code\ on\ L1)}\ } $$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Maximum Carrier Phase Multipath Error **Setup:** Composite signal = direct + 1 multipath of equal amplitude ($\alpha = 1$). $$ s(t) = A_0 e^{j\phi_0} + A_0 e^{j(\phi_0 + \Delta\phi_{mp})} $$ The receiver phase-lock loop (PLL) tracks the **phase of the composite vector**, not the direct signal alone. The composite phase is: $$ \phi_{composite} = \phi_0 + \arctan\left(\frac{\sin\Delta\phi_{mp}}{1 + \cos\Delta\phi_{mp}}\right) $$ Using the half-angle identity $\arctan\left(\frac{\sin x}{1+\cos x}\right) = \tan^{-1}(\tan(x/2)) = x/2$: $$ \phi_{composite} = \phi_0 + \frac{\Delta\phi_{mp}}{2} $$ The **maximum phase error** occurs when $\Delta\phi_{mp} = \pi$ (destructive interference), giving: $$ \Delta\phi_{err}^{max} = \frac{\pi}{2}\ \mathrm{radians} $$ Converting to range error using $\Delta R = \lambda \cdot \Delta\phi / (2\pi)$: $$ \Delta R_{carrier}^{max} = \frac{\lambda}{4} = \frac{0.1905}{4} \approx 4.76\ \mathrm{cm\ (L1)} $$ --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Multipath vs. Observable vs. Mitigation | **Observable** | **Error Magnitude** | **Time Correlation** | **Mitigation Technique** | **Residual Error** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | C/A Code Pseudorange | $\pm 146.5$ m | $10^{-1}$ to $10^2$ s | Narrow Correlator (chip spacing $< 0.1$ chip) | $\pm 5$ m | | P(Y) Code Pseudorange | $\pm 14.65$ m | $10^{-1}$ to $10^2$ s | NL correlator / MEDLL | $\pm 1$ m | | L1 Carrier Phase | $\pm 4.75$ cm | $10^1$ to $10^3$ s | Phase-smoothed pseudorange | $\pm 1$ cm | | L2 Carrier Phase | $\pm 6.11$ cm | $10^1$ to $10^3$ s | Triple-frequency ionospheric-free | $\pm 2$ cm | | Doppler | $\pm 0.05$ m/s | $10^0$ s | Carrier-aided code loop | $\pm 0.005$ m/s | ### 3.5 Work-Through Numerical Example > [!NOTE] > **Worked Example (Past KTU Numerical Style)** **Problem:** A robot is at the base of a glass building. A GPS satellite is at elevation $30°$ and azimuth $180°$. A signal reflects off a glass panel of effective height $h = 20$ m. Compute: > (a) The extra path length introduced by the reflection. > (b) The corresponding range bias on the C/A code pseudorange. > (c) Whether this satellite should be included in the position solution (apply elevation mask criterion). **Solution:** **(a)** Extra path length: $$ \Delta d = 2h \sin\theta_{el} = 2 \times 20 \times \sin(30°) = 40 \times 0.5 = 20\ \mathrm{m} $$ **(b)** Range bias (assuming direct path is unblocked, but reflection adds in-phase): The reflected path is 20 m longer. The discriminator will bias the pseudorange by 20 m in the worst case (constructive interference). Compared to the 146.5 m envelope, this is a 13.6% bias. **(c)** Elevation mask: Standard KTU robotics practice uses $\theta_{mask} = 10°$ to $15°$. With $\theta_{el} = 30°$, the satellite is geometrically acceptable, **but** the multipath is still significant. A 3D-MAP (3D mapping) or consistency check is recommended. **Final Pseudorange with Multipath:** $$ R_{i,measured} = \rho_i + c\delta t_r + I_i + T_i + \underbrace{M_i}_{=20\ \mathrm{m}} + \varepsilon_i $$ In the position solution, the linearized residual after WLS would be: $$ \hat{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{x}_0 + \left(\mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{H}\right)^{-1} \mathbf{H}^T \Delta\mathbf{\rho}_{mp} $$ The multipath bias propagates into the position domain scaled by DOP. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Multipath Propagation Block Diagram (Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD SatA[GNSS Satellite
Transmits L1 L2 Signal] EnvA[Direct LOS Path
No Reflection] EnvB[Reflected Path 1
Building Facade] EnvC[Reflected Path 2
Ground Surface] EnvD[Diffracted Path
Building Edge] CompA[Signal Superposition
at Antenna Front-End] AmpA[Low Noise Amplifier
LNA] FiltA[RF Bandpass Filter
BPF] DownA[Down-Converter
Mixer LO] ADC[Analog to Digital
Converter] AcqA[Acquisition Block
Code Phase Doppler Search] TrkA[Tracking Loops
PLL DLL FLL] DiscA[Discriminator Output
Code Carrier Phase] ErrA[Multipath Error
Bias Generation] WLS[Weighted Least Squares
Position Solver] KF[Kalman Filter
State Estimation] OutA[Robot Position Fix
x y z clock] SatA --> EnvA SatA --> EnvB SatA --> EnvC SatA --> EnvD EnvA --> CompA EnvB --> CompA EnvC --> CompA EnvD --> CompA CompA --> AmpA AmpA --> FiltA FiltA --> DownA DownA --> ADC ADC --> AcqA AcqA --> TrkA TrkA --> DiscA DiscA --> ErrA ErrA --> WLS WLS --> KF KF --> OutA ``` ### 4.2 Multipath Error Envelope Visualization (Block-Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Code Multipath
Envelope PMax = 146.5 m] --> B{Path Difference d} B -->|Short d less than Tchip| C[Severe Distortion
Tracking Loop Stress] B -->|Long d greater than 2 Tchip| D[Negligible Effect
Multipath Uncorrelated] C --> E[Position Error
Propagated via DOP] D --> F[Position Solution
Within Tolerance] G[Carrier Multipath
Envelope PMax = 4.75 cm] --> H{Phase Offset} H -->|In Phase| I[Maximum +ve Bias] H -->|Out of Phase| J[Maximum -ve Bias] I --> K[Integer Ambiguity
Resolution Failure] J --> K ``` ### 4.3 Position Calculation Pipeline (With Multipath Injection Point) ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Satellite 1 Pseudorange R1 with M1] --> Stack P2[Satellite 2 Pseudorange R2 with M2] --> Stack P3[Satellite 3 Pseudorange R3 with M3] --> Stack P4[Satellite 4 Pseudorange R4 with M4] --> Stack P5[Satellite N Pseudorange RN with MN] --> Stack Stack[Ranging Vector R of size Nx1] Stack --> Lin[Linearization at Initial Guess x0] Lin --> Geom[Geometry Matrix H Unit Vectors] Geom --> Norm[Normal Equation HtH dx = Ht dR] Norm --> Inv[Inverse of HtH] Inv --> Sol[Position Update dx] Sol --> Converge{Convergence Check} Converge -->|Not Converged| Lin Converge -->|Converged| Out[Final Robot Position] ``` ### 4.4 Antenna-Based vs. Receiver-Based Mitigation Strategy Map ```mermaid flowchart TD Root[Multipath Mitigation
in Robot Navigation] Root --> Ant[Antenna Level] Root --> Rec[Receiver Level] Root --> Post[Post-Processing Level] Ant --> Ant1[Choke Ring Antenna] Ant --> Ant2[Pinwheel Antenna] Ant --> Ant3[Ground Plane Design] Ant --> Ant4[RHCP Polarization
Rejection] Rec --> Rec1[Narrow Correlator
Spacing less than 0.1 chip] Rec --> Rec2[Multipath Estimating
Delay Lock Loop MEDLL] Rec --> Rec3[Vision Correlator] Rec --> Rec4[Strobe Correlator] Post --> Post1[Carrier Smoothing
Hatch Filter] Post --> Post2[Multipath Template
Matching] Post --> Post3[SIDEBAND SNR
Weighting in WLS] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023 Model]** Define multipath in GNSS-based robot navigation. State two antenna-level mitigation techniques. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Multipath is the phenomenon where GNSS signals reach the robot's receiving antenna via multiple paths due to reflection, refraction, or diffraction from nearby surfaces, in addition to the direct line-of-sight path. This corrupts pseudorange and carrier phase measurements, leading to position errors. (2 marks) Two antenna-level mitigation techniques: - (i) Use of a **choke ring antenna** with multiple concentric rings to reject signals arriving from below the horizon. - (ii) Use of a **Right-Hand Circularly Polarized (RHCP) antenna** which suppresses Left-Hand Circularly Polarized (LHCP) reflected signals from specular surfaces. (1 mark) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model]** Why is the code multipath error envelope larger than the carrier phase multipath error envelope? Provide numerical values for C/A code on L1. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The error envelope scales with the *resolution unit* of the observable. The C/A code has a chip length of approximately 293.05 m, and the maximum bias equals half a chip; the carrier wavelength is 19.05 cm, and the maximum bias equals a quarter wavelength. (2 marks) - Code multipath envelope: $\pm T_{chip}/2 = \pm 146.5$ m. - Carrier phase multipath envelope: $\pm \lambda/4 = \pm 4.75$ cm. (1 mark) --- ### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model 2024, Module 4]* **(a)** Derive the maximum code multipath error envelope for a C/A code receiver when a single specular reflection of equal amplitude ($\alpha = 1$) is superposed on the direct signal. Show the discriminator shift in the DLL. (7 Marks) **(b)** A robot operates in a steel-structured warehouse. Five GPS satellites are visible at elevations $15°, 35°, 55°, 75°$ and $90°$ with measured pseudorange multipath biases (in meters) of $25, 12, 6, 2, 0$ respectively. Compute the approximate position error magnitude if the geometry matrix yields $DOP = 2.3$. (7 Marks) --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Solution Outline (7 marks):** - State the discriminator function $D(\epsilon) = R(\epsilon - \delta/2) - R(\epsilon + \delta/2)$ for early-late correlator (1 mark). - Introduce multipath: $D_{mp}(\epsilon) = R(\epsilon - \delta/2) + \alpha^2 R(\epsilon - \delta - \delta/2) - R(\epsilon + \delta/2) - \alpha^2 R(\epsilon - \delta + \delta/2)$ (1 mark). - Set $\alpha = 1$, $\delta = T_{chip}$ (worst case, multipath at adjacent autocorrelation peak) (1 mark). - Solve $D_{mp}(\epsilon) = 0$ analytically using the triangular autocorrelation shape (2 marks). - Conclude with the result: $\Delta R_{code}^{max} = \pm T_{chip}/2 = \pm 146.5$ m (2 marks). **(b) Solution:** The position error due to pseudorange multipath is: $$ \Delta \mathbf{x} = DOP \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} M_i^2} $$ RMS multipath bias: $$ M_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{25^2 + 12^2 + 6^2 + 2^2 + 0^2}{5}} = \sqrt{\frac{625 + 144 + 36 + 4 + 0}{5}} $$ $$ M_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{809}{5}} = \sqrt{161.8} \approx 12.72\ \mathrm{m} $$ Position error: $$ \Delta \mathbf{x} \approx DOP \times M_{rms} = 2.3 \times 12.72 \approx 29.3\ \mathrm{m} $$ **Conclusion (1 mark):** The position error of ~29 m is unacceptable for a warehouse robot; mitigation via multipath-limiting antenna and elevation mask (rejecting the 15° satellite, reducing M_rms to ~8 m → error ~18 m) is recommended. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Choice Alternative* **(a)** Explain the four physical mechanisms that cause multipath. Compare code and carrier phase multipath errors with mathematical derivation of their respective maximum envelopes. (7 Marks) **(b)** Design a multipath mitigation strategy for an autonomous car navigating an urban canyon. Justify the choice of antenna, correlator type, and post-processing filter. (7 Marks) --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Solution Outline (7 marks):** - Define the four mechanisms: **Specular Reflection, Diffuse Scattering, Diffraction, Refraction** (2 marks). - Code multipath derivation: $\Delta R_{code}^{max} = \pm T_{chip}/2 = \pm 146.5$ m (2 marks). - Carrier phase derivation: $\Delta R_{carrier}^{max} = \pm \lambda/4 = \pm 4.75$ cm (2 marks). - Comparison table concluding carrier is ~3000× more robust to multipath (1 mark). **(b) Solution Outline (7 marks):** - **Antenna Choice:** Choke ring or pinwheel antenna with sharp roll-off below 5° elevation. **Justification:** Rejects ground-bounce multipath that is the dominant error source in urban canyons. (2 marks) - **Correlator Choice:** Narrow correlator with chip spacing of 0.05 chips (or MEDLL). **Justification:** Narrow spacing reduces the code multipath envelope to ±7 m or less. (2 marks) - **Post-Processing:** Hatch filter for carrier smoothing of pseudorange + Multi-Epoch Weighted Least Squares with SNR-based weighting. **Justification:** Smooths high-frequency multipath, SNR weighting downweights noisy/low-elevation satellites. (2 marks) - **Sensor Fusion:** GNSS + IMU + LiDAR tightly-coupled Kalman Filter to bound GNSS outages caused by severe multipath. (1 mark) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:** > 1. **Confusing "Multipath Error Envelope" with "Typical Multipath Error"** — The envelope is the *worst-case* bound (±146.5 m for C/A), not the *typical* value (5–15 m). Examiners deduct 1 mark if these are interchanged. > 2. **Forgetting to apply $\cos(\theta_{el})$ scaling** in slant-range multipath calculations; the elevation correction must be included for low-elevation satellites. > 3. **Conflating multipath with atmospheric errors** (ionospheric/tropospheric) — multipath is **local and non-differential-correctable**, while atmospheric errors are *regional and DGNSS-correctable*. > 4. **Missing the polarisation argument** — Multipath from specular reflectors flips polarization from RHCP to LHCP; ignoring this in antenna design loses 2 marks in design questions. > 5. **Forgetting Hatch Filter limits** — Carrier smoothing smooths multipath, but if the smoothing time constant exceeds the multipath correlation time, residual errors reappear. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Position Calculation under Multipath** - **Definition:** Multipath = multiple signal paths from satellite to robot antenna via reflection/scatter/diffraction. - **Max Code Multipath Envelope:** $\pm T_{chip}/2 = \pm 146.5$ m (C/A, L1). - **Max Carrier Multipath Envelope:** $\pm \lambda/4 = \pm 4.75$ cm (L1) and $\pm 6.11$ cm (L2). - **Pseudorange with multipath:** $R_i = \rho_i + c\delta t_r + I_i + T_i + M_i + \varepsilon_i$. - **Position error formula:** $\Delta \mathbf{x} = (\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H})^{-1} \mathbf{H}^T \Delta\mathbf{\rho}_{mp}$; DOP scales the impact. - **Extra reflected path length:** $\Delta d = 2h \sin\theta_{el}$ (image theory). - **Multipath is NOT corrected by DGNSS** (site-dependent, short spatial correlation $< 100$ m). - **Polarization flip:** Direct = RHCP; Specular reflection = LHCP; antenna discrimination suppresses LHCP. - **Best mitigation stack:** Choke ring antenna + Narrow Correlator + Hatch filter + Multi-sensor KF fusion. - **Elevation mask rule of thumb:** Reject satellites below $10°$–$15°$ elevation to reduce low-elevation multipath. - **Receiver Autonomous Integrity Monitoring (RAIM):** Uses redundancy to detect and exclude multipath-corrupted satellites. - **RTK caveat:** Carrier multipath, though small, causes *integer ambiguity resolution failures* — major RTK issue. - **For robotics projects:** Always complement GNSS with IMU, wheel odometry, LiDAR SLAM to overcome GNSS multipath outages. - **Emerging trend:** Dual-polarization antennas and 3D city models (3D-MAP) for **predictive multipath mitigation** in V2X and AMR applications. - **Key terms to memorize:** Pseudorange, Carrier Phase, DLL, PLL, Narrow Correlator, MEDLL, Hatch Filter, DOP, GDOP, Choke Ring, RHCP, LHCP, RAIM.

topics.title: GNSS Accuracy Analysis
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT735 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** GNSS Accuracy Analysis

# 🛰️ GNSS Accuracy Analysis — Foundational Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **GNSS Accuracy Analysis** is the systematic quantification of the degree of conformance between an estimated position/velocity/time (PVT) solution derived from satellite ranging signals and the **true** geometric location of the receiver. It encompasses the **propagation, modeling, and statistical combination** of all pseudorange and carrier-phase error sources, weighted through the **satellite-receiver geometry** expressed via **Dilution of Precision (DOP)** matrices.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this topic sits at the intersection of:
- **Statistical Estimation Theory** (Weighted Least Squares)
- **Linear Algebra** (Jacobians, covariance propagation)
- **Atmospheric Physics** (Ionosphere/Troposphere)
- **Robot Localization Stack** (EKF measurement update step)

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Blindfolded Archer"

Imagine a **blindfolded archer** trying to hit a target. The archer's *skill* represents the receiver's intrinsic measurement quality (UERE — User Equivalent Range Error), while the **position of the targets (satellites) in the sky** represents the *geometry*. If the satellites are clustered in one corner of the sky (poor geometry → high DOP), even a skilled archer will scatter arrows in a stretched ellipse. If satellites are evenly spread (good geometry → low DOP), arrows cluster tightly around the bullseye.

> **Mnemonic for Robot Navigation:**
> $$\boxed{\text{Accuracy} \;\neq\; \text{Precision} \;\neq\; \text{Integrity}}$$
> - **Accuracy** = *How close to true position?* (Bias-dominated)
> - **Precision** = *How repeatable are my fixes?* (Noise-dominated)
> - **Integrity** = *Am I confident this fix is safe?* (Worst-case bound, probability of misleading info)

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of GNSS Position Quality

| Pillar | Metric (Standard) | Robot Navigation Use-Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Accuracy** | 2DRMS, CEP, R95 | Lane-level path tracking for outdoor AGVs |
| **Precision** | Standard deviation σ | Repeatable docking at charging station |
| **Availability** | % time fix available | Forest/urban canyon field robotics |
| **Integrity** | Protection Level (PL), HAL/VAL | Safety-critical autonomous vehicles |
| **Continuity** | Probability of uninterrupted service | UAV survey missions |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Positioning Service (SPS)** delivers **≤ 13 m (95%)** horizontal accuracy for civilian GPS — but this is a *system specification*. Real-world robot applications require a much deeper error-decomposition analysis.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The GNSS Error Budget — Anatomy of a Pseudorange Measurement

A raw pseudorange observation $\rho_i$ to satellite $i$ is modeled as:

$$\rho_i = R_i + c \cdot (dt_u - dt^s) + I_i + T_i + \varepsilon_i$$

Where every term on the right-hand side is a **contributor to total ranging error**. The accuracy of the position fix is only as good as our ability to model/cancel these terms.

### 2.1.1 Hierarchical Error Decomposition

The **User Equivalent Range Error (UERE)** is the *root-sum-square* of all independent error sources:

$$\boxed{\sigma_{UERE}^2 \;=\; \sigma_{UIRE}^2 + \sigma_{UTRE}^2 + \sigma_{EE}^2 + \sigma_{CE}^2 + \sigma_{ME}^2 + \sigma_{RN}^2}$$

| Error Source | Symbol | Magnitude (1σ, m) | Origin | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **User Ionospheric Range Error** | $\sigma_{UIRE}$ | **1.5 – 7.0** | Charged particles delay L-band signal | Klobuchar model, dual-frequency, SBAS |
| **User Tropospheric Range Error** | $\sigma_{UTRE}$ | **0.5 – 2.0** | Neutral atmosphere (dry + wet) | Saastamoinen/Moore models, tropo corrections |
| **Ephemeris Error** | $\sigma_{EE}$ | **0.5 – 1.0** | Imperfect satellite orbit prediction | Broadcast vs. precise ephemeris |
| **Satellite Clock Error** | $\sigma_{CE}$ | **0.5 – 1.5** | Oscillator drift on satellite | Broadcast clock corrections, precise clocks |
| **Multipath Error** | $\sigma_{ME}$ | **0.1 – 3.0** | Signal reflections from surfaces | Choke-ring antennas, receiver tracking, carrier smoothing |
| **Receiver Noise** | $\sigma_{RN}$ | **0.1 – 1.0** | Thermal noise, code resolution | Better RF front-end, longer integration |

## 2.2 Dilution of Precision (DOP) — The Geometric Amplifier

DOP quantifies how satellite geometry *amplifies* UERE into position error. It arises from the **measurement geometry matrix** $\mathbf{G}$ used in the linearized WLS solution.

### 2.2.1 Construction of the Geometry Matrix

For $n$ satellites, the linearized observation equation yields:

$$\mathbf{G} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} -\hat{\mathbf{e}}_1^T & 1 \\ -\hat{\mathbf{e}}_2^T & 1 \\ \vdots & \vdots \\ -\hat{\mathbf{e}}_n^T & 1 \end{bmatrix}_{n \times 4}$$

where $\hat{\mathbf{e}}_i = (\cos el_i \cos az_i, \cos el_i \sin az_i, \sin el_i)^T$ is the unit vector from receiver to satellite $i$.

The position-time covariance matrix is:

$$\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{PVT} \;=\; (\mathbf{G}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{G})^{-1}$$

where $\mathbf{W} = \text{diag}(1/\sigma_{\rho_i}^2)$ is the measurement weighting matrix.

### 2.2.2 The DOP Family Tree

$$\boxed{\begin{aligned}
GDOP^2 &= PDOP^2 + TDOP^2 \\
PDOP^2 &= HDOP^2 + VDOP^2 \\
HDOP^2 &= EDOP^2 + NDOP^2
\end{aligned}}$$

### 2.2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| Quantity | Formula | Practical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Position Error Std-Dev** | $\sigma_{3D} = PDOP \cdot \sigma_{UERE}$ | 3D scatter radius |
| **Horizontal Error** | $\sigma_{H} = HDOP \cdot \sigma_{UERE}$ | Plan view scatter |
| **Vertical Error** | $\sigma_{V} = VDOP \cdot \sigma_{UERE}$ | Height scatter (typically 1.5× horizontal) |
| **Time Error** | $\sigma_{T} = TDOP \cdot \sigma_{UERE}/c$ | Clock bias scatter |
| **2DRMS (Horizontal)** | $2 \cdot \sqrt{\sigma_E^2 + \sigma_N^2}$ | 95–98% confidence radius |
| **CEP (50%)** | $\approx 1.1774 \cdot \sigma_{H}$ | Half the time inside this circle |
| **R95 (95%)** | $\approx 2.0807 \cdot \sigma_{H}$ | 95% confidence radius |
| **Total RMS Range Error** | $\sigma_{UERE}$ | Input from error budget |
| **Protection Level (V)** | $PL_V = K_V \cdot \sigma_V$ | Integrity bound (Stanford-ESA) |
| **Protection Level (H)** | $PL_H = K_H \cdot \sigma_{2D}$ | Integrity bound |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Aid — DOP Threshold Convention:**
> - $DOP < 2$ → **Excellent** (open sky)
> - $2 \le DOP < 5$ → **Good** (normal conditions)
> - $5 \le DOP < 10$ → **Moderate** (use cautiously)
> - $DOP \ge 10$ → **Poor**, discard fix

## 2.3 Why This Matters for Robot Navigation

A mobile robot relying on GNSS for outdoor localization must:
1. **Pre-screen satellite visibility** to keep PDOP below a mission threshold.
2. **Use a measurement noise covariance** $\mathbf{R}_k = \text{diag}(\sigma_{UERE,i}^2)$ inside its **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** — otherwise the filter trusts bad measurements as much as good ones.
3. **Account for non-Gaussian tails** (multipath causes heavy-tailed errors) — Gaussian-only KF can be optimistic.
4. **Trigger integrity monitoring** (RAIM) to reject faulty satellites before they corrupt the pose estimate feeding the motion planner.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📐 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the DOP Expression from WLS

### 3.1.1 Linearized Pseudorange Observation Model

Starting from the true pseudorange equation $P_i = \sqrt{(x-x_i)^2+(y-y_i)^2+(z-z_i)^2} + c\cdot b$, where $b = dt_u - dt^s$, we apply a **first-order Taylor expansion** about an a-priori state $(\hat{x}_0, \hat{y}_0, \hat{z}_0, \hat{b}_0)$:

$$\Delta P_i \;=\; -\hat{\mathbf{e}}_i^T \begin{bmatrix} \Delta x \\ \Delta y \\ \Delta z \end{bmatrix} + c \cdot \Delta b + \nu_i$$

Stacking all $n$ satellites into vector form:

$$\Delta \mathbf{P} \;=\; \mathbf{G} \cdot \Delta \mathbf{x} + \boldsymbol{\nu}, \qquad \boldsymbol{\nu} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{R})$$

### 3.1.2 Weighted Least Squares (WLS) Solution

Minimize the weighted squared residual $J(\Delta \mathbf{x}) = \Delta \mathbf{P}^T \mathbf{R}^{-1} \Delta \mathbf{P}$ subject to the linear model. Taking the gradient and setting to zero:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \Delta \mathbf{x}} = 2 \mathbf{G}^T \mathbf{R}^{-1}(\Delta \mathbf{P} - \mathbf{G}\Delta\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{0}$$

Solving:

$$\boxed{\Delta\hat{\mathbf{x}} \;=\; (\mathbf{G}^T \mathbf{R}^{-1} \mathbf{G})^{-1} \mathbf{G}^T \mathbf{R}^{-1} \Delta \mathbf{P}}$$

### 3.1.3 Covariance Propagation → DOP

Since $\Delta \mathbf{P} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{R})$, propagate the covariance:

$$\begin{aligned}
\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\hat{\mathbf{x}}} &= (\mathbf{G}^T \mathbf{R}^{-1} \mathbf{G})^{-1} \mathbf{G}^T \mathbf{R}^{-1} \cdot \mathbf{R} \cdot \mathbf{R}^{-1} \mathbf{G} (\mathbf{G}^T \mathbf{R}^{-1} \mathbf{G})^{-1} \\
&= (\mathbf{G}^T \mathbf{R}^{-1} \mathbf{G})^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

Assuming **uniform** measurement variance $\sigma_{UERE}^2$, then $\mathbf{R} = \sigma_{UERE}^2 \mathbf{I}$ and:

$$\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\hat{\mathbf{x}}} \;=\; \sigma_{UERE}^2 (\mathbf{G}^T \mathbf{G})^{-1} \;\equiv\; \sigma_{UERE}^2 \mathbf{H}$$

Defining the DOP matrix $\mathbf{H} = (\mathbf{G}^T \mathbf{G})^{-1}$:

$$\mathbf{H} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_x^2 & \sigma_{xy} & \sigma_{xz} & \sigma_{xb} \\ \sigma_{yx} & \sigma_y^2 & \sigma_{yz} & \sigma_{yb} \\ \sigma_{zx} & \sigma_{zy} & \sigma_z^2 & \sigma_{zb} \\ \sigma_{bx} & \sigma_{by} & \sigma_{bz} & \sigma_b^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

| DOP Type | Definition | Physical Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **GDOP** | $\sqrt{\text{tr}(\mathbf{H})} = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2+\sigma_y^2+\sigma_z^2+\sigma_b^2}$ | Total 3D + time |
| **PDOP** | $\sqrt{\sigma_x^2+\sigma_y^2+\sigma_z^2}$ | 3D position only |
| **TDOP** | $\sigma_b \cdot c$ | Clock error in meters |
| **HDOP** | $\sqrt{\sigma_E^2+\sigma_N^2}$ | East-North plane |
| **VDOP** | $\sigma_U$ | Up component |

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Accuracy Estimation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU-style):** A robot's GNSS receiver tracks **5 satellites** with uniform UERE $\sigma_{UERE} = 2.5$ m. The local DOP values reported by the receiver are: HDOP = 1.4, VDOP = 2.2, TDOP = 1.1. Compute the (a) horizontal 2DRMS accuracy, (b) vertical 95% accuracy, (c) total GDOP-based 3D RMS error, and (d) interpret the result for autonomous navigation.

### 3.2.1 (a) Horizontal 2DRMS Accuracy

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_H &= HDOP \cdot \sigma_{UERE} = 1.4 \times 2.5 = 3.5 \text{ m} \\
2DRMS &= 2 \times \sigma_H = 2 \times 3.5 = 7.0 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** 95% of horizontal fixes lie within a **7.0 m radius** circle.

### 3.2.2 (b) Vertical 95% Accuracy

For a 1D Gaussian error, 95% corresponds to $1.96\sigma$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_V &= VDOP \cdot \sigma_{UERE} = 2.2 \times 2.5 = 5.5 \text{ m} \\
\text{Accuracy}_{V,95\%} &= 1.96 \times \sigma_V = 1.96 \times 5.5 = 10.78 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.2.3 (c) Total GDOP-Based 3D RMS

First compute PDOP from HDOP and VDOP:

$$\begin{aligned}
PDOP &= \sqrt{HDOP^2 + VDOP^2} = \sqrt{1.4^2 + 2.2^2} = \sqrt{1.96 + 4.84} = \sqrt{6.80} = 2.608 \\
GDOP &= \sqrt{PDOP^2 + TDOP^2} = \sqrt{6.80 + 1.21} = \sqrt{8.01} = 2.830 \\
\sigma_{3D, RMS} &= GDOP \cdot \sigma_{UERE} = 2.830 \times 2.5 = 7.075 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.2.4 (d) Engineering Interpretation

| Parameter | Value | Navigation Implication |
|---|---|---|
| Horizontal 2DRMS | 7.0 m | ❌ Insufficient for lane-keeping (< 0.5 m required) |
| Vertical 95% | 10.78 m | ❌ Poor for terrain-relative altitude |
| 3D RMS | 7.075 m | ⚠️ Acceptable only for waypoint navigation (> 5 m) |
| GDOP | 2.83 | ✅ Geometry is good; error dominated by UERE |

> **Conclusion:** To improve accuracy, **reduce UERE** (use differential GNSS, dual-frequency, choke-ring antenna) rather than seeking more satellites, since GDOP is already excellent.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — DOP vs. UERE Trade-off

This matrix helps a roboticist decide the *minimum hardware/effort investment* to reach a target accuracy:

| Target Horizontal Accuracy (2DRMS) | Required $\sigma_H$ | If $HDOP=1.5$ → $\sigma_{UERE}$ needed | Practical Solution |
|---|---|---|---|
| **0.5 m** (lane-keep) | 0.25 m | 0.167 m | **RTK GNSS** (carrier-phase) |
| **1.0 m** (precision ag) | 0.50 m | 0.333 m | **SBAS + dual-freq** |
| **2.5 m** (mapping) | 1.25 m | 0.833 m | **DGNSS** or **PPP** |
| **5.0 m** (waypoint) | 2.50 m | 1.667 m | **Single-freq w/ correction** |
| **10 m** (geotag) | 5.00 m | 3.333 m | **Raw SPS** (GPS L1 only) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End GNSS Accuracy Analysis Pipeline

The figure below traces the complete information flow from raw satellite signal to a final integrity-checked position fix usable by the robot's motion planner.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Satellite RF Signal L1/L2/L5] --> B[Antenna and Front-End]
    B --> C[Code and Carrier Tracking Loops]
    C --> D[Pseudorange and Carrier Phase Extraction]
    D --> E[Error Source Decomposition]
    E --> E1[Ionospheric Delay]
    E --> E2[Tropospheric Delay]
    E --> E3[Ephemeris Error]
    E --> E4[Clock Bias]
    E --> E5[Multipath]
    E --> E6[Receiver Noise]
    E1 --> F[UERE Budget via RSS]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    E5 --> F
    E6 --> F
    F --> G[Measurement Covariance Matrix R]
    G --> H[Geometry Matrix G from Satellite Positions]
    H --> I[Weighted Least Squares Solver]
    G --> I
    I --> J[Position Time Covariance Sigma PVT]
    J --> K[DOP Computation]
    J --> L[Accuracy Metrics 2DRMS, R95]
    I --> M[RAIM Integrity Check]
    M --> N{Protection Level OK?}
    N -->|Yes| O[Final PVT Fix to Robot EKF]
    N -->|No| P[Reject Fix or Use Backup Sensor]
```

## 4.2 DOP Hierarchy — Conceptual Decomposition

```mermaid
graph TD
    root[GDOP] --> pos[PDOP]
    root --> t[TDOP]
    pos --> h[HDOP]
    pos --> v[VDOP]
    h --> e[EDOP East]
    h --> n[NDOP North]
    style root fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#333
    style pos fill:#A8E6CF,stroke:#333
    style h fill:#FFD3B6,stroke:#333
    style v fill:#DCD6F7,stroke:#333
    style t fill:#FAD2D2,stroke:#333
```

## 4.3 Error Propagation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Satellite Clock] -->|CE| E((RSS Sum))
    B[Ephemeris] -->|EE| E
    C[Ionosphere] -->|UIRE| E
    D[Troposphere] -->|UTRE| E
    E --> sigma[Sigma UERE]
    F[Antenna and Site] -->|ME| E
    G[Receiver Noise] -->|RN| E
    sigma --> H[Position Covariance Sigma]
    H --> I[DOP Amplification]
    I --> J[Final Accuracy]
```

## 4.4 Architecture: GNSS Module Inside Robot Navigation Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Per[Perception Layer]
        L[LiDAR]
        C[Camera]
        I[IMU]
        W[Wheel Odometry]
        G[GNSS Receiver]
    end
    subgraph Fus[Sensor Fusion EKF]
        K[Prediction Step]
        L1[Measurement Update]
        P[Pose Estimate]
    end
    subgraph Plan[Planning Layer]
        PL[Global Planner]
        RL[Local Planner]
        CT[Controller]
    end
    G -->|Position plus Covariance| L1
    I --> K
    W --> K
    L --> L1
    C --> L1
    L1 --> P
    K --> P
    P --> PL
    PL --> RL
    RL --> CT
    G -. Integrity RAIM Flag .-> CT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Reading:** The GNSS receiver does *not* directly command the robot — its covariance $\Sigma_{PVT}$ enters the EKF as the measurement noise matrix $\mathbf{R}_k$. If integrity is violated, the RAIM flag prevents the fix from entering the planner (broken red line), forcing reliance on LiDAR/IMU/odometry.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define Dilution of Precision (DOP). How does it affect GNSS position accuracy?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

DOP is a dimensionless geometric factor that quantifies how the **spatial configuration of visible satellites** amplifies ranging errors into position errors (1 mark). It is derived from the inverse of the geometry matrix $(\mathbf{G}^T \mathbf{G})^{-1}$, where $\mathbf{G}$ contains unit vectors to each satellite plus a column of ones for clock bias estimation (1 mark).

Position accuracy is given by $\sigma_{pos} = DOP \times \sigma_{UERE}$. A **high DOP (poor geometry, e.g., clustered satellites)** inflates the position error, while a **low DOP (well-spread satellites)** yields accurate fixes even with the same UERE. Hence DOP is a *geometric amplifier* of ranging errors (1 mark).

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**List the major sources of error in GNSS pseudorange measurements. Which is the largest contributor for single-frequency users?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The major pseudorange error sources are: **(i) Ionospheric delay (UIRE)** — 1.5–7 m; **(ii) Tropospheric delay (UTRE)** — 0.5–2 m; **(iii) Ephemeris error (EE)** — 0.5–1 m; **(iv) Satellite clock error (CE)** — 0.5–1.5 m; **(v) Multipath (ME)** — 0.1–3 m; **(vi) Receiver noise (RN)** — 0.1–1 m (2 marks).

For **single-frequency civilian users**, the **ionospheric delay** is the dominant contributor, since it is not eliminated by the receiver without a second frequency or external correction model (1 mark).

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU Examiner's Note:]** Per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, Part B carries an **Internal Choice**. You must attempt **ONE** of the two questions below.

---

### ✏️ Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **complete GNSS error budget** for single-frequency pseudorange-based positioning. Derive the expression for **User Equivalent Range Error (UERE)** in terms of the individual error variances. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A survey-grade robot uses a GNSS receiver with reported **UERE = 1.8 m (1σ)**. The current satellite geometry yields **HDOP = 1.2, VDOP = 2.0, PDOP = 2.33**. Compute the **(i) 2DRMS horizontal accuracy, (ii) R95 vertical accuracy, (iii) total 3D position RMS**. Comment on the suitability of this fix for **lane-keeping in an autonomous vehicle (requires < 0.5 m lateral error)**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a):

**Block diagram** of the GNSS error budget:

```
   ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │           SATELLITE SEGMENT ERRORS             │
   │  Clock Error (σ_CE)   Ephemeris Error (σ_EE)   │
   └─────────────┬──────────────────┬───────────────┘
                 │                  │
                 ▼                  ▼
   ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │        PROPAGATION CHANNEL ERRORS              │
   │  Ionospheric Delay (σ_UIRE)  Tropospheric (σ_UTRE)│
   │            Multipath (σ_ME)                   │
   └─────────────┬──────────────────┬───────────────┘
                 │                  │
                 ▼                  ▼
   ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │          RECEIVER SEGMENT ERRORS               │
   │        Receiver Noise (σ_RN)                   │
   └────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                 │
                 ▼
        ┌────────────────┐
        │  UERE (σ_UERE) │  ← RSS sum of all components
        └────────────────┘
```

**Derivation:**

Each pseudorange measurement is the sum of true range plus several error terms. Assuming the errors are **independent, zero-mean, and Gaussian**:

$$\rho_i = R_i + \delta_{CE,i} + \delta_{EE,i} + \delta_{UIRE,i} + \delta_{UTRE,i} + \delta_{ME,i} + \delta_{RN,i}$$

For independent errors, the **variance of the sum equals the sum of variances**:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}(\rho_i) &= \text{Var}(\delta_{CE}) + \text{Var}(\delta_{EE}) + \text{Var}(\delta_{UIRE}) \\
&\quad + \text{Var}(\delta_{UTRE}) + \text{Var}(\delta_{ME}) + \text{Var}(\delta_{RN})
\end{aligned}$$

By definition, this is the **square of UERE**:

$$\boxed{\sigma_{UERE}^2 = \sigma_{CE}^2 + \sigma_{EE}^2 + \sigma_{UIRE}^2 + \sigma_{UTRE}^2 + \sigma_{ME}^2 + \sigma_{RN}^2}$$

(2 marks for derivation, 2 marks for assumptions/justification, 3 marks for the labeled diagram and explanation of each term.)

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b):

**Given:** $\sigma_{UERE} = 1.8$ m, $HDOP = 1.2$, $VDOP = 2.0$, $PDOP = 2.33$.

**(i) 2DRMS Horizontal Accuracy:**

$$\sigma_H = HDOP \times \sigma_{UERE} = 1.2 \times 1.8 = 2.16 \text{ m}$$
$$\boxed{2DRMS = 2 \times \sigma_H = 2 \times 2.16 = 4.32 \text{ m}}$$

**(ii) R95 Vertical Accuracy:**

The 95th percentile of a 1D Gaussian distribution is at $1.96\sigma$:

$$\sigma_V = VDOP \times \sigma_{UERE} = 2.0 \times 1.8 = 3.6 \text{ m}$$
$$\boxed{R95_V = 1.96 \times \sigma_V = 1.96 \times 3.6 = 7.06 \text{ m}}$$

**(iii) Total 3D Position RMS:**

$$\boxed{\sigma_{3D} = PDOP \times \sigma_{UERE} = 2.33 \times 1.8 = 4.194 \text{ m}}$$

**Suitability for Lane-Keeping:**

| Requirement | Computed | Pass/Fail |
|---|---|---|
| Lane-keeping lateral error < 0.5 m | 2DRMS = 4.32 m | ❌ **FAILS** by ~8.6× |

**Recommendation:** The current fix is **not suitable** for autonomous lane-keeping. The geometry is excellent (HDOP < 2), so the error is dominated by UERE. To achieve < 0.5 m accuracy, the robot must use **(a) RTK GNSS** with base-station corrections ($\sigma_{UERE} \to 0.02$ m), **(b) tightly-coupled INS/GNSS fusion**, or **(c) LiDAR-based localization** as the primary sensor with GNSS only for global initialization.

(Marks: 2 for each sub-calculation, 1 for the comparison table/recommendation.)

---

### ✏️ Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **Weighted Least Squares (WLS)** formulation for GNSS positioning. Show how the position covariance matrix is derived and relate it to **Dilution of Precision (DOP)**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **DOP hierarchical relationship** $GDOP^2 = HDOP^2 + VDOP^2 + TDOP^2$. A robot is operating in a location where only **4 satellites** are visible at elevations $(30°, 45°, 60°, 75°)$ and azimuths $(0°, 90°, 180°, 270°)$. Given $\sigma_{UERE} = 3$ m, compute the **expected horizontal accuracy in 2DRMS**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a):

The WLS estimator solves the over-determined system $\mathbf{G}\Delta\mathbf{x} = \Delta\mathbf{P}$ by minimizing the **weighted squared error**:

$$J(\Delta\mathbf{x}) = (\Delta\mathbf{P} - \mathbf{G}\Delta\mathbf{x})^T \mathbf{W} (\Delta\mathbf{P} - \mathbf{G}\Delta\mathbf{x})$$

where $\mathbf{W} = \mathbf{R}^{-1}$ is the inverse of the measurement covariance matrix.

Setting $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \Delta\mathbf{x}} = 0$:

$$\Delta\hat{\mathbf{x}} = (\mathbf{G}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{G})^{-1} \mathbf{G}^T \mathbf{W} \Delta\mathbf{P}$$

The covariance of the estimate, assuming measurements are unbiased:

$$\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\hat{\mathbf{x}}} = (\mathbf{G}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{G})^{-1}$$

For uniform measurement noise, $\mathbf{W} = (1/\sigma_{UERE}^2) \mathbf{I}$, giving:

$$\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\hat{\mathbf{x}}} = \sigma_{UERE}^2 (\mathbf{G}^T \mathbf{G})^{-1} \equiv \sigma_{UERE}^2 \mathbf{H}$$

The diagonal of $\mathbf{H} = (\mathbf{G}^T \mathbf{G})^{-1}$ yields:

- $H_{11}+H_{22}+H_{33}+H_{44} = GDOP^2$ (with $H_{44}$ scaled by $c^2$)
- $H_{11}+H_{22}+H_{33} = PDOP^2$
- $H_{44} = TDOP^2$
- $H_{11}+H_{22} = HDOP^2$
- $H_{33} = VDOP^2$

(7 marks: 2 for WLS formulation, 2 for covariance derivation, 2 for DOP definition, 1 for summary.)

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b):

**Hierarchical DOP Relation:**

The geometry matrix $\mathbf{G}$ has its first three columns as unit-vector components (X, Y, Z) and the fourth column as ones. The covariance matrix $\mathbf{H} = (\mathbf{G}^T\mathbf{G})^{-1}$ is therefore a $4\times 4$ symmetric matrix whose **diagonal sum** equals the trace:

$$GDOP^2 = \text{tr}(\mathbf{H}) = H_{11}+H_{22}+H_{33}+H_{44}$$

Decomposing by physical meaning of each diagonal element (X,Y,Z,clock):

$$GDOP^2 = \underbrace{(H_{11}+H_{22})}_{HDOP^2} + \underbrace{H_{33}}_{VDOP^2} + \underbrace{H_{44}}_{TDOP^2}$$

$$\boxed{GDOP^2 = HDOP^2 + VDOP^2 + TDOP^2}$$

**Numerical Computation:**

Construct unit vectors $\hat{\mathbf{e}}_i = (\cos el_i \cos az_i, \cos el_i \sin az_i, \sin el_i)^T$:

| Sat | el (°) | az (°) | $\cos el \cos az$ | $\cos el \sin az$ | $\sin el$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 30 | 0 | $0.866 \times 1 = 0.866$ | $0.866 \times 0 = 0$ | $0.500$ |
| 2 | 45 | 90 | $0.707 \times 0 = 0$ | $0.707 \times 1 = 0.707$ | $0.707$ |
| 3 | 60 | 180 | $0.500 \times (-1) = -0.500$ | $0.500 \times 0 = 0$ | $0.866$ |
| 4 | 75 | 270 | $0.259 \times 0 = 0$ | $0.259 \times (-1) = -0.259$ | $0.966$ |

Geometry matrix (4×4, last column = 1 for clock):

$$\mathbf{G} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.866 & 0 & 0.500 & 1 \\ 0 & -0.707 & 0.707 & 1 \\ 0.500 & 0 & 0.866 & 1 \\ 0 & 0.259 & 0.966 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

(Using $-x,y,z$ column convention; signs in the table above indicate direction.)

Compute $\mathbf{H} = (\mathbf{G}^T\mathbf{G})^{-1}$. By symmetry and orthogonality of the four cardinal azimuth directions, the off-diagonal cross-terms between E and N vanish:

$$HDOP = \sqrt{H_{11} + H_{22}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\cos^2 el_1} + \frac{1}{\cos^2 el_2 \cdot 2} + \frac{1}{\cos^2 el_3} + \frac{1}{\cos^2 el_4 \cdot 2}}$$

Using $\sec el_i$ values: $\sec 30° = 1.155$, $\sec 45° = 1.414$, $\sec 60° = 2.000$, $\sec 75° = 3.864$:

$$HDOP \approx \sqrt{1.155^2 + \frac{1.414^2}{2} + 2.000^2 + \frac{3.864^2}{2}} \approx \sqrt{1.334 + 1.000 + 4.000 + 7.465} = \sqrt{13.80} \approx 3.715$$

$$\sigma_H = HDOP \times \sigma_{UERE} = 3.715 \times 3 = 11.145 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{2DRMS = 2 \times \sigma_H = 2 \times 11.145 = 22.29 \text{ m}}$$

(7 marks: 3 for hierarchical proof, 2 for $\mathbf{G}$ construction, 2 for numerical answer.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing accuracy vs precision** — Accuracy is bias-dominated (true vs estimated), Precision is noise-dominated (repeatability). Mixing these costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in 2DRMS vs 1σ — students often state 2DRMS = $\sigma_H$ instead of $2\sigma_H$. This is the **#1 error** in KTU answer scripts.
> 3. **Using $1\sigma$ instead of $1.96\sigma$** for 95% vertical accuracy — 95% confidence is *not* 2σ in 1D; it is exactly $1.96\sigma$.
> 4. **Adding DOPs instead of squaring and summing** — e.g., writing $GDOP = HDOP + VDOP + TDOP$ (wrong). Correct: $GDOP = \sqrt{HDOP^2 + VDOP^2 + TDOP^2}$.
> 5. **Neglecting to label units** — $\sigma_{UERE}$ is in meters; clock TDOP-related terms must be converted from seconds to meters via multiplication by $c$.
> 6. **Ignoring dependence on elevation angle** — Multipath and tropospheric errors scale with $\csc(el)$; full-marks answers weight $\mathbf{R}$ by elevation, not assume uniform $\sigma_{UERE}$.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- ⭐ **GNSS Accuracy** is decomposed into **UERE** (ranging quality) and **DOP** (geometric amplification): $\sigma = DOP \cdot \sigma_{UERE}$.
- ⭐ **UERE** is the **RSS of 6 independent error components**: ionospheric, tropospheric, ephemeris, clock, multipath, receiver noise.
- ⭐ **DOP family**: $GDOP^2 = PDOP^2 + TDOP^2 = HDOP^2 + VDOP^2 + TDOP^2$.
- ⭐ **WLS covariance**: $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\hat{\mathbf{x}}} = (\mathbf{G}^T\mathbf{W}\mathbf{G})^{-1}$ with $\mathbf{G}$ containing unit-satellite vectors and a column of ones.
- ⭐ **Accuracy metrics**: $2DRMS \approx 2\sigma_H$ (95–98%), $CEP \approx 1.18\sigma_H$ (50%), $R95 \approx 2.08\sigma_H$ (95%).
- ⭐ **Vertical error is ~1.5× to 2× horizontal** because satellites are always above the receiver (poor vertical geometry).
- ⭐ **Multipath is non-Gaussian** — KF-based GNSS integration can underestimate tail risk; use **RAIM** for safety-critical robots.
- ⭐ **DOP threshold**: $DOP < 2$ excellent, $2$–$5$ good, $5$–$10$ moderate, $>10$ reject fix.
- ⭐ **Single-frequency ionospheric error is largest**; mitigated by **Klobuchar model**, **SBAS**, or **dual-frequency receivers**.
- ⭐ **RTK/PPP** reduce $\sigma_{UERE}$ from meters to centimeters — essential for **lane-keeping and precision-ag robotics**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dilution of Precision
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT735 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Dilution of Precision

# Dilution of Precision (DOP) — KTU 2024 Premium Study Notes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition**
> *Dilution of Precision (DOP)* is a dimensionless scalar quantity that expresses the geometric strength of a satellite (or beacon) configuration and quantifies how measurement errors from individual ranging sources are **amplified** into the final position/velocity/time estimate of a receiver. Mathematically, it is the ratio of the positioning accuracy achieved from a given geometry to the ranging accuracy of the individual measurements.

In KTU Robot Navigation terminology, DOP is a *first-order propagation metric* used in the **GNSS/INS integrated localization stack** to evaluate the trustworthiness of a position fix before it is fed into the robot's state estimator (EKF / Particle Filter).

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Triangulation Telescope"

Imagine you are a spy in a dark room trying to locate a hidden radio transmitter using only directional antennas. If the three antennas you use are **all clustered on one side of the room**, the triangles they form are long, thin, and ambiguous — a small error in angle measurement translates to a *huge* positional error. But if the three antennas are **spread evenly around the room (120° apart)**, the triangles are equilateral and well-conditioned — small angle errors translate to small position errors.

> DOP is exactly this *geometric condition number*. **Low DOP = good geometry = precise fix. High DOP = poor geometry = unreliable fix.**

### Standard DOP Nomenclature (KTU Mandatory)

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus lists **five canonical DOP variants**, each isolating a different sub-dimension of the 4-state navigation solution $(x, y, z, t)$:

| Acronym | Full Form | What it Describes | KTU Use-Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **GDOP** | Geometric DOP | Combined 3D position + time | Overall system quality |
