$$E = k_0 N \left[ A + (1 - T + C)\frac{A^3}{6} + (5 - 58T + T^2 + 72C - 58e'^2)\frac{A^5}{120} \right] + 500000$$
$$N_{\text{utm}} = k_0 \left\{ M + N\tan\phi \left[ \frac{A^2}{2} + (5 - T + 9C + 4C^2)\frac{A^4}{24} + \cdots \right] \right\}$$

**Why UTM in Robot Navigation:**
- **Metric** coordinates (meters, not degrees) → simple distance & heading.
- **Cartesian** (flat) → linear path planning, SLAM, occupancy grids.
- **Low distortion** in a 6° zone → matches real-world robot scale.
- **Standardized** worldwide → enables multi-robot, multi-vehicle coordination.

**Common Exam Pitfalls:**
- Forgetting to convert $\phi, \lambda$ to **radians** before applying trig.
- Confusing $e^2$ (first eccentricity) with $e'^2$ (second eccentricity).
- Off-by-one errors in zone number.
- Forgetting the false easting offset (+500,000 m).
- Applying UTM across zone boundaries (causes jumps and discontinuities).
- Treating Earth as a sphere instead of an **ellipsoid** in high-precision work.

**When NOT to use UTM:**
- Intercontinental navigation (use lat/lon or ECEF).
- Polar regions (above 84° N or 80° S) — use UPS (Universal Polar Stereographic).
- When sub-millimeter accuracy is required (use local grid + geoid model).

**Memory Trick — "PEST-NA" for UTM Conversion:**
1. **P**ick the zone (use $\lambda$).
2. **E**valuate central meridian.
3. **S**et up auxiliary terms $T, C, A, N, M$.
4. **T**runcate series at $A^5$ (or $A^7$ for higher precision).
5. **N**orthing formula (don't forget $k_0$ and FN for S. hemisphere).
6. **A**dd false easting (+500,000 m).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Local Cartesian Coordinates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT735 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Local Cartesian Coordinates

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of mobile robotics, a **Local Cartesian Coordinate Frame** is a two-dimensional (2D) or three-dimensional (3D) orthogonal, right-handed reference frame rigidly attached to the robot's body (or a local reference point such as the robot's center of mass, wheel-base midpoint, or sensor mounting position). All measurements of nearby objects, obstacles, landmarks, and goal points are expressed as signed displacements $\left(x_l, y_l\right)$ or $\left(x_l, y_l, z_l\right)$ along mutually perpendicular axes originating at this local origin.

Unlike a **Global (World) Frame** which is fixed to the environment, a local frame moves, rotates, and translates with the robot through its operational space. The complete pose of a point $P$ in the local frame is fully described by its position vector and, where orientation matters, a rotation matrix $R_l^w$ that maps local-frame vectors to the global frame.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — PERUT735 Module 4**
> Local Cartesian coordinates form the mathematical backbone for **sensor data interpretation**, **relative localization**, **obstacle avoidance**, and **local path planning**. Every range-bearing measurement from a LiDAR, sonar, or camera is initially acquired in this local frame before being projected to the global map.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing in the middle of a dark room holding a torch. The torch is fixed to your hand, so wherever you turn, the beam rotates with you. The point where the torch is held is the **origin** of your local frame. The forward direction of the beam is the local **$+X$ axis**, your right side is the local **$+Y$ axis**, and straight up is the local **$+Z$ axis**.

If someone asks "Where is the chair?", you do not answer using the building's main entrance as the reference point. Instead, you instinctively say, "The chair is **2 meters ahead and 1 meter to my right**." That is precisely a local Cartesian description — a description relative to *you*, not the world.

In robotics:
- The robot is the observer.
- The local frame is "robot-centric."
- The world frame is "room-centric."

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Takeaway:** A local Cartesian frame is the robot's personal ruler-and-compass. It always points forward (along the robot's heading), it always sits at the robot's geometric center, and it rotates as the robot rotates. The price for this convenience is that every measurement must later be transformed into the global frame for cross-robot communication or global map building.

### 1.3 Standard Metric Conventions in KTU Board Examinations

- **Forward axis:** $X_l$ — aligned with the robot's instantaneous heading direction.
- **Lateral axis:** $Y_l$ — pointing to the robot's left (in a right-handed frame) or right (in some automotive conventions).
- **Vertical axis:** $Z_l$ — pointing upward.
- **Standard Units:** **meters (m)** for distance, **radians (rad)** or **degrees (°)** for angles.
- **Coordinate Sign Convention:** **Right-Hand Rule** is the universally accepted convention in KTU robotics papers.

### 1.4 Distinction Between Local and Global Frames

| Feature | Global Frame $(X_w, Y_w, Z_w)$ | Local Frame $(X_l, Y_l, Z_l)$ |
|---|---|---|
| Origin Location | Fixed at a known environment landmark | Attached to the robot body |
| Orientation | Aligned with a known datum (e.g., North) | Aligned with robot's heading |
| Motion | Stationary (non-inertial for our 2D plane) | Translates & rotates with the robot |
| Use Case | Global path planning, map merging | Sensor interpretation, local avoidance |
| Time-Variant? | **No** (assumed constant) | **Yes** (changes at every time step $t$) |

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Construction of a Local Cartesian Frame

A standard 2D local Cartesian frame is constructed using the following logical sequence:

1. **Choose the Local Origin $\left(O_l\right)$:** Select a geometrically meaningful point on the robot — typically the **midpoint of the wheel axle** (differential drive robots) or the **center of mass**. The choice should minimize the kinematic complexity of subsequent equations.

2. **Align the $X_l$ Axis:** Point $X_l$ along the robot's instantaneous forward direction of motion. This is determined by the robot's heading angle $\theta$ measured counter-clockwise from the global $X_w$ axis.

3. **Align the $Y_l$ Axis:** Using the right-hand rule, $Y_l$ points to the **left** of the robot (or right, depending on convention — KTU follows the **ISO 8855** convention where $Y_l$ is to the left).

4. **Maintain Orthogonality:** Ensure $X_l \perp Y_l$ at all times. The local frame is rigidly attached, so the angle between $X_l$ and $Y_l$ is always **$\frac{\pi}{2}$ radians (90°)**.

5. **Express All Local Vectors:** Any point $P$ in the vicinity is represented as $\mathbf{p}_l = \left[x_l, y_l, z_l\right]^T$ where each component is the signed orthogonal projection of $\overrightarrow{O_l P}$ onto the corresponding axis.

### 2.2 The 2D Transformation Equations (Core KTU Formula)

When a robot at global pose $\left(x_r, y_r, \theta\right)$ observes a point $P$ with global coordinates $\left(x_p^w, y_p^w\right)$, the local coordinates of that point are computed by:

**Step 1 — Translate to robot origin:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_p^{\text{translated}} &= x_p^w - x_r \\
y_p^{\text{translated}} &= y_p^w - y_r
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Rotate into the local frame (counter-rotate by $\theta$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_l &= x_p^{\text{translated}} \cos\theta + y_p^{\text{translated}} \sin\theta \\
y_l &= -x_p^{\text{translated}} \sin\theta + y_p^{\text{translated}} \cos\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

This pair of equations is the **most-tested formula** in KTU board examinations for Module 4 of Robot Navigation. Mastery of the sign conventions is non-negotiable.

### 2.3 The Homogeneous Transformation Matrix (Compact Form)

The translation and rotation can be encapsulated into a single $3 \times 3$ homogeneous matrix for 2D, or a $4 \times 4$ matrix for 3D:

$$
T_l^w = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & x_r \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & y_r \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

This matrix transforms any point expressed in the local frame $\mathbf{p}_l = \left[x_l, y_l, 1\right]^T$ to its global-frame representation:

$$
\mathbf{p}_w = T_l^w \cdot \mathbf{p}_l
$$

To go from global to local, the inverse transformation is used (which is the **transpose of the rotation block** combined with the negated translation):

$$
T_w^l = \left(T_l^w\right)^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta & -x_r\cos\theta - y_r\sin\theta \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta & x_r\sin\theta - y_r\cos\theta \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Property:** For pure rotation matrices, $R^{-1} = R^T$. This is one of the most exploited algebraic shortcuts in KTU derivations. Always remember it — board examiners specifically test whether students know when to apply $R^T$ instead of computing the matrix inverse from scratch.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Units / Range | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2D Global → Local Position | $x_l = (x_p^w - x_r)\cos\theta + (y_p^w - y_r)\sin\theta$ | meters (m) | Sensor reading interpretation |
| 2 | 2D Global → Local Position | $y_l = -(x_p^w - x_r)\sin\theta + (y_p^w - y_r)\cos\theta$ | meters (m) | Sensor reading interpretation |
| 3 | 2D Local → Global Position | $x_p^w = x_r + x_l\cos\theta - y_l\sin\theta$ | meters (m) | Mapping, plotting |
| 4 | 2D Local → Global Position | $y_p^w = y_r + x_l\sin\theta + y_l\cos\theta$ | meters (m) | Mapping, plotting |
| 5 | Homogeneous Transform (2D) | $T_l^w \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 3}$ | dimensionless | Composite transformations |
| 6 | Inverse Transform Property | $T_w^l = \left(T_l^w\right)^{-1}$ | dimensionless | Global-to-local conversion |
| 7 | Rotation Inversion Property | $R^{-1}(\theta) = R^T(\theta) = R(-\theta)$ | dimensionless | Angular arithmetic |
| 8 | Range-Bearing to Cartesian | $x_l = r\cos\phi, \quad y_l = r\sin\phi$ | meters, radians | LiDAR / Sonar data |
| 9 | Cartesian to Range-Bearing | $r = \sqrt{x_l^2 + y_l^2}, \quad \phi = \text{atan2}(y_l, x_l)$ | meters, radians | Inverse sensor model |
| 10 | Differential Local Velocity | $\dot{x}_l = v - \omega \cdot y_l$ | m/s | Local frame dynamics |
| 11 | Differential Local Velocity | $\dot{y}_l = \omega \cdot x_l$ | m/s | Local frame dynamics |
| 12 | Body-to-World Velocity | $\dot{x}_w = v\cos\theta, \quad \dot{y}_w = v\sin\theta$ | m/s | Forward kinematics |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute-value and conditional bars in the above table have been replaced with parentheses to preserve Markdown table integrity, in compliance with KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 formatting protocols.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Local Cartesian coordinates are indispensable in:
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Lane detection, obstacle ranging, and adaptive cruise control all operate in a vehicle-centric frame updated at 50–100 Hz.
- **Indoor Service Robots:** Vacuum robots build local occupancy grids centered on themselves before stitching them into a global map.
- **Aerial Swarms (UAVs):** Drones localize neighbors in a moving local frame to maintain flocking formations.
- **Surgical Robotics:** Tool-tip positions are tracked in the patient's local anatomy frame, decoupled from operating-room vibrations.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Global-to-Local Coordinate Transformation

We will derive the global-to-local transformation from first principles, with no algebraic step skipped.

**Setting:** A differential-drive robot is located in the global frame at position $\left(x_r, y_r\right)$ and is oriented at heading angle $\theta$ measured counter-clockwise from the global $X_w$ axis. An external landmark $P$ is observed at global coordinates $\left(x_p^w, y_p^w\right)$. We wish to find the local coordinates $\left(x_l, y_l\right)$ of $P$ as seen by the robot.

**Step 1 — Express the global position of $P$ relative to the global origin $O_w$:**

$$
\mathbf{p}_w = \begin{bmatrix} x_p^w \\ y_p^w \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Express the robot's position relative to the global origin:**

$$
\mathbf{r}_w = \begin{bmatrix} x_r \\ y_r \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the relative position vector from the robot to $P$ in global coordinates (by vector subtraction):**

$$
\mathbf{p}_{\text{rel}}^w = \mathbf{p}_w - \mathbf{r}_w = \begin{bmatrix} x_p^w - x_r \\ y_p^w - y_r \end{bmatrix}
$$

This step "moves the origin" of our coordinate system from the global origin to the robot's location. Geometrically, we are translating the entire system so the robot sits at the new origin. However, the axes are still aligned with the global frame — we have only translated, not rotated.

**Step 4 — Apply rotation correction.** The local frame is rotated by angle $\theta$ relative to the global frame. To express the relative vector in local coordinates, we must rotate it by $-\theta$ (clockwise by $\theta$) to undo the robot's rotation.

The 2D rotation matrix for angle $\alpha$ is:

$$
R(\alpha) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\alpha & -\sin\alpha \\ \sin\alpha & \cos\alpha \end{bmatrix}
$$

We apply $R(-\theta)$ to $\mathbf{p}_{\text{rel}}^w$:

$$
\mathbf{p}_l = R(-\theta) \cdot \mathbf{p}_{\text{rel}}^w
$$

**Step 5 — Expand using the trigonometric form of $R(-\theta)$:**

$$
R(-\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that $R(-\theta)$ is obtained from $R(\theta)$ by negating $\theta$, which simultaneously negates both $\sin$ terms. This is a direct consequence of the odd-function property of sine: $\sin(-\theta) = -\sin\theta$.

**Step 6 — Perform the matrix-vector multiplication explicitly:**

$$
\mathbf{p}_l = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_p^w - x_r \\ y_p^w - y_r \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute the top row (local $x$-coordinate):**

$$
x_l = \cos\theta \cdot (x_p^w - x_r) + \sin\theta \cdot (y_p^w - y_r)
$$

**Step 8 — Compute the bottom row (local $y$-coordinate):**

$$
y_l = -\sin\theta \cdot (x_p^w - x_r) + \cos\theta \cdot (y_p^w - y_r)
$$

**Step 9 — Final form (KTU board-ready):**

$$
\boxed{\begin{aligned} x_l &= (x_p^w - x_r)\cos\theta + (y_p^w - y_r)\sin\theta \\ y_l &= -(x_p^w - x_r)\sin\theta + (y_p^w - y_r)\cos\theta \end{aligned}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign-Convention Trap:** The plus sign in $x_l$ and the minus sign in $y_l$ are the most common sources of mark deduction in KTU examinations. Memorize the rule: "Subtract position first (translation), then rotate by $-\theta$ (counter-rotation)." If you remember the rotation direction, the signs follow automatically.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Range-Bearing to Local Cartesian

Most 2D sensors (LiDAR, sonar, IR rangefinder) natively report measurements as a tuple $\left(r, \phi\right)$ where $r$ is the range (radial distance) and $\phi$ is the bearing (angle measured from the sensor's $X_l$ axis). We need to convert this into Cartesian form.

**Given:** A sensor measurement $\left(r, \phi\right)$ in the local frame.

**Step 1 — Set up the polar-to-Cartesian conversion:**

By definition of polar coordinates, the Cartesian coordinates of a point at range $r$ and bearing $\phi$ are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_l &= r \cos\phi \\
y_l &= r \sin\phi
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Verify with a sanity check.** When $\phi = 0$ (object directly ahead of the robot), we obtain $x_l = r$ and $y_l = 0$, which is correct. When $\phi = \frac{\pi}{2}$ (object directly to the left), we obtain $x_l = 0$ and $y_l = r$, which is also correct for an ISO-convention left-positive $Y_l$ axis.

**Step 3 — Inverse transformation.** Given $\left(x_l, y_l\right)$, the range and bearing are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
r &= \sqrt{x_l^2 + y_l^2} \\
\phi &= \text{atan2}(y_l, x_l)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why atan2 and not atan?** The standard `atan` function has a range of $\left(-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$ and cannot distinguish between quadrants. The `atan2(y, x)` function has a full range of $\left(-\pi, \pi\right]$ and correctly identifies the bearing in all four quadrants. KTU board solutions explicitly require `atan2` — using `atan` in your derivation will cost you marks.

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Inverse Homogeneous Transformation

We derive the matrix $T_w^l = \left(T_l^w\right)^{-1}$ block-by-block.

**Given:** 
$$
T_l^w = \begin{bmatrix} R & \mathbf{t} \\ \mathbf{0}^T & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$
where $R = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$ and $\mathbf{t} = \begin{bmatrix} x_r \\ y_r \end{bmatrix}$.

**Step 1 — Use the partitioned matrix inverse formula:**

$$
\left(T_l^w\right)^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} R^{-1} & -R^{-1}\mathbf{t} \\ \mathbf{0}^T & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Recognize that $R$ is orthogonal, so $R^{-1} = R^T$:**

$$
R^{-1} = R^T = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the translation block $-R^{-1}\mathbf{t}$:**

$$
-R^{-1}\mathbf{t} = -\begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_r \\ y_r \end{bmatrix} = -\begin{bmatrix} x_r\cos\theta + y_r\sin\theta \\ -x_r\sin\theta + y_r\cos\theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify with the negative sign distributed:**

$$
-R^{-1}\mathbf{t} = \begin{bmatrix} -x_r\cos\theta - y_r\sin\theta \\ x_r\sin\theta - y_r\cos\theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Assemble the full inverse matrix:**

$$
\boxed{T_w^l = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta & -x_r\cos\theta - y_r\sin\theta \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta & x_r\sin\theta - y_r\cos\theta \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-Verification with the Derivation in Section 3.1:** Substitute $\mathbf{p}_w = \begin{bmatrix} x_p^w \\ y_p^w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$ into $T_w^l \cdot \mathbf{p}_w$. The top row reduces to $(x_p^w - x_r)\cos\theta + (y_p^w - y_r)\sin\theta$, which matches $x_l$ exactly. This confirms algebraic consistency between the matrix and the explicit formula.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Worked Numerical Example

**Problem Statement:** A robot is at global position $\left(2, 3\right)$ m with heading $\theta = 30° = \frac{\pi}{6}$ rad. It detects a landmark at global position $\left(5, 7\right)$ m. Find the local Cartesian coordinates of the landmark as seen by the robot.

**Solution — Step 1: Compute the relative position (translation):**

$$
x_p^w - x_r = 5 - 2 = 3 \text{ m}
$$

$$
y_p^w - y_r = 7 - 3 = 4 \text{ m}
$$

**Solution — Step 2: Evaluate the trigonometric functions:**

$$
\cos 30° = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.866
$$

$$
\sin 30° = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5
$$

**Solution — Step 3: Apply the global-to-local formulas:**

$$
x_l = (3)(0.866) + (4)(0.5) = 2.598 + 2.0 = 4.598 \text{ m}
$$

$$
y_l = -(3)(0.5) + (4)(0.866) = -1.5 + 3.464 = 1.964 \text{ m}
$$

**Solution — Step 4: Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{\left(x_l, y_l\right) \approx (4.60, \ 1.96) \text{ m}}
$$

**Interpretation:** The landmark is approximately **4.60 m ahead** of the robot and **1.96 m to its left**. This is consistent with the global geometry: the landmark lies to the northeast of the robot, and the robot is facing northeast (30° from east), so the landmark should appear mostly forward and slightly to the left — which matches our result.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Local Cartesian Coordinate Processing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sensor Data Acquisition] --> B{Sensor Type}
    B -->|Range-Bearing| C[Polar Tuple r phi]
    B -->|Image Pixels| D[Pixel Coordinates u v]
    B -->|Encoder Ticks| E[Odometry deltas ds dtheta]
    C --> F[Polar to Cartesian Conversion]
    D --> G[Camera Calibration Undistortion]
    E --> H[Dead Reckoning Integration]
    F --> I[Local Frame Coordinates x_l y_l]
    G --> I
    H --> J[Robot Pose Update xr yr theta]
    J --> I
    I --> K{Application Layer}
    K --> L[Obstacle Avoidance Vector]
    K --> M[Local Occupancy Grid Map]
    K --> N[Relative Localization Output]
    L --> O[Motor Command Layer]
    M --> P[Map Stitching to Global Frame]
    N --> P
    P --> Q[Global World Map]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** This flowchart traces the lifecycle of a local Cartesian coordinate from raw sensor acquisition to its eventual use in downstream tasks. The pivotal block is the central node $I$ (Local Frame Coordinates), which serves as the universal interchange format. The transformation from polar/pixel/encoder inputs into this format is the core competency expected of KTU students in Module 4.

### 4.2 Geometric Schematic: World Frame vs Local Frame Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph WorldFrame[Global World Frame]
        Ow[Origin O_w] -->|X_w axis| EastDir[East]
        Ow -->|Y_w axis| NorthDir[North]
    end
    subgraph LocalFrame[Robot Local Frame]
        Or[Robot Origin O_l] -->|X_l axis| ForwardDir[Robot Forward]
        Or -->|Y_l axis| LeftDir[Robot Left]
    end
    WorldFrame -->|Translation vector| LocalFrame
    WorldFrame -->|Heading angle theta| LocalFrame
    LandmarkP[Landmark P] -->|Project to local axes| LocalFrame
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Schematic Insight:** The two key geometric relationships to internalize are (1) the **translation vector** $\left(x_r, y_r\right)$ connecting the origins $O_w$ and $O_l$, and (2) the **heading angle** $\theta$ that quantifies the rotational offset between the world and local axes. A single pose $\left(x_r, y_r, \theta\right)$ fully defines this relationship in 2D.

### 4.3 Transformation Pipeline Dataflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    InputP[Point in Global Frame x_p^w, y_p^w] --> Step1[Step 1: Subtract Robot Position]
    Step1 --> Step2[Step 2: Apply Rotation by -theta]
    Step2 --> OutputP[Point in Local Frame x_l, y_l]
    OutputP --> Decision{Is Point Within Sensor Range?}
    Decision -->|Yes| Valid[Valid Local Measurement]
    Decision -->|No| Reject[Discard as Out of Range]
    Valid --> Storage[Store in Local Buffer]
    Storage --> Plot[Plot on Local Occupancy Grid]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** The decision block represents a critical filter in any real robotic system. Only measurements that pass the range check are admitted into the local coordinate buffer, preventing spurious readings from corrupting downstream maps and planners.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q1.** Define a **local Cartesian coordinate frame** as used in robot navigation. How does it differ from a global frame in terms of origin location, orientation, and time variance?

**Model Answer:**
A local Cartesian coordinate frame is an orthogonal, right-handed reference frame whose origin is rigidly attached to a point on the robot (typically the wheel-base midpoint or center of mass) and whose $X_l$ axis is aligned with the robot's instantaneous heading. It differs from a global frame in three respects: **(1) Origin Location** — the local origin moves with the robot, whereas the global origin is fixed at a known environmental landmark; **(2) Orientation** — the local axes rotate with the robot's heading $\theta$, while the global axes remain aligned with a fixed datum; **(3) Time Variance** — the local frame's pose $\left(x_r, y_r, \theta\right)$ changes at every time step $t$, whereas the global frame is time-invariant. Local frames are essential for sensor data interpretation and local obstacle avoidance, while global frames support path planning and map merging.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** What is the significance of the right-hand rule convention in defining a local Cartesian frame for a mobile robot? What happens if the sign convention for the $Y_l$ axis is reversed?

**Model Answer:**
The right-hand rule enforces a **consistent, unambiguous mapping** between rotational angles and their corresponding algebraic signs. With the $X_l$ axis pointing forward and the $Z_l$ axis pointing up, the right-hand rule dictates that $Y_l$ must point to the **left** of the robot. This convention guarantees that counter-clockwise rotations (viewed from above) correspond to **positive** angles in all trigonometric identities. If the $Y_l$ axis is reversed, the coordinate system becomes **left-handed**, which would invert the sign of all angular quantities, flip the cross-product orientation, and produce mirror-image transformations. This leads to physically incorrect results such as obstacles appearing on the wrong side of the robot, severely compromising navigation safety. KTU strictly follows the right-hand convention as per ISO 8855.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 — Module 4 Internal Choice]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** A differential-drive mobile robot is currently located at global coordinates $\left(4, 5\right)$ m and is moving with a heading angle of $\theta = 60°$ measured counter-clockwise from the global $X$ axis. The robot's onboard LiDAR detects an obstacle at global coordinates $\left(9, 11\right)$ m. Compute the **local Cartesian coordinates** of the obstacle as observed by the robot. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **2D homogeneous transformation matrix** $T_l^w$ that maps a point from the local frame to the global frame. Show all intermediate steps, including the construction of the rotation block and the translation block. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Identify the given data:**
- Robot global position: $x_r = 4$ m, $y_r = 5$ m
- Obstacle global position: $x_p^w = 9$ m, $y_p^w = 11$ m
- Heading: $\theta = 60°$

**Step 2 — Compute the relative position (translation component):**

$$
x_p^w - x_r = 9 - 4 = 5 \text{ m}
$$

$$
y_p^w - y_r = 11 - 5 = 6 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the trigonometric values for $\theta = 60°$:**

$$
\cos 60° = 0.5
$$

$$
\sin 60° = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.866
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the global-to-local transformation formulas:**

$$
x_l = (x_p^w - x_r)\cos\theta + (y_p^w - y_r)\sin\theta
$$

$$
x_l = (5)(0.5) + (6)(0.866) = 2.5 + 5.196 = 7.696 \text{ m}
$$

$$
y_l = -(x_p^w - x_r)\sin\theta + (y_p^w - y_r)\cos\theta
$$

$$
y_l = -(5)(0.866) + (6)(0.5) = -4.330 + 3.0 = -1.330 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{\left(x_l, y_l\right) \approx (7.70, \ -1.33) \text{ m}}
$$

**Interpretation:** The obstacle is approximately **7.70 m ahead** of the robot and **1.33 m to its right** (negative $y_l$ in the ISO left-positive convention indicates the right side).

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Step 1 — State the form of $T_l^w$:**

The general form of a 2D homogeneous transformation matrix is:

$$
T_l^w = \begin{bmatrix} R(\theta) & \mathbf{t} \\ \mathbf{0}^T & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $R(\theta)$ is the $2 \times 2$ rotation matrix and $\mathbf{t}$ is the $2 \times 1$ translation vector.

**Step 2 — Construct the rotation block $R(\theta)$:**

The 2D rotation matrix is constructed by projecting the local frame's unit vectors onto the global frame's axes. Since the local $X_l$ axis makes angle $\theta$ with the global $X_w$ axis:

$$
R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Construct the translation block $\mathbf{t}$:**

The translation block holds the position of the local origin in global coordinates:

$$
\mathbf{t} = \begin{bmatrix} x_r \\ y_r \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Assemble the full $3 \times 3$ matrix:**

$$
T_l^w = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & x_r \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & y_r \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify the transformation by applying it to a sample point.**

Consider a point $P$ in the local frame at $\mathbf{p}_l = \left[x_l, y_l, 1\right]^T$. Multiplying:

$$
\mathbf{p}_w = T_l^w \cdot \mathbf{p}_l = \begin{bmatrix} x_r + x_l\cos\theta - y_l\sin\theta \\ y_r + x_l\sin\theta + y_l\cos\theta \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

The first two components match the local-to-global formulas from Section 2.4, confirming the matrix is correct.

**Step 6 — Statement of physical meaning:**

$T_l^w$ is the **pose** of the local frame as observed from the world. Its rotation block encodes the orientation $\theta$, and its translation block encodes the position $\left(x_r, y_r\right)$. Any point in the local frame is mapped to the global frame by a single matrix multiplication.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** A robot's LiDAR returns a polar measurement $\left(r = 8 \text{ m}, \phi = 45°\right)$ for an object in its local frame. Convert this measurement to local Cartesian coordinates and verify the result geometrically. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A second robot is located at global position $\left(10, 6\right)$ m with heading $\theta = 120°$. Its LiDAR detects the same obstacle at polar coordinates $\left(r = 5 \text{ m}, \phi = -30°\right)$ in its own local frame. Determine the **global Cartesian coordinates** of the obstacle using the homogeneous transformation approach. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Apply the polar-to-Cartesian conversion:**

$$
x_l = r\cos\phi = 8 \cos 45° = 8 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} = 4\sqrt{2} \approx 5.657 \text{ m}
$$

$$
y_l = r\sin\phi = 8 \sin 45° = 8 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} = 4\sqrt{2} \approx 5.657 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{\left(x_l, y_l\right) \approx (5.66, \ 5.66) \text{ m}}
$$

**Step 3 — Geometric verification.** A range of 8 m at a bearing of exactly 45° must place the object equidistant along the $X_l$ and $Y_l$ axes (because $\cos 45° = \sin 45°$). The Cartesian distance from the origin must equal $r$:

$$
\sqrt{(5.657)^2 + (5.657)^2} = \sqrt{32 + 32} = \sqrt{64} = 8 \text{ m} \quad \checkmark
$$

The verification confirms the conversion is algebraically correct.

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Convert the polar measurement to local Cartesian coordinates:**

$$
x_l = r\cos\phi = 5\cos(-30°) = 5 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 4.330 \text{ m}
$$

$$
y_l = r\sin\phi = 5\sin(-30°) = 5 \cdot (-0.5) = -2.5 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Construct the homogeneous transformation matrix $T_l^w$ for the second robot:**

Given $x_r = 10$, $y_r = 6$, $\theta = 120°$:

$$
\cos 120° = -0.5, \quad \sin 120° = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.866
$$

$$
T_l^w = \begin{bmatrix} -0.5 & -0.866 & 10 \\ 0.866 & -0.5 & 6 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert the local point to homogeneous form:**

$$
\mathbf{p}_l = \begin{bmatrix} 4.330 \\ -2.5 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the transformation $\mathbf{p}_w = T_l^w \cdot \mathbf{p}_l$:**

$$
x_p^w = (-0.5)(4.330) + (-0.866)(-2.5) + (10)(1)
$$

$$
x_p^w = -2.165 + 2.165 + 10 = 10.0 \text{ m}
$$

$$
y_p^w = (0.866)(4.330) + (-0.5)(-2.5) + (6)(1)
$$

$$
y_p^w = 3.750 + 1.250 + 6 = 11.0 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{\mathbf{p}_w = \left(10.0, \ 11.0\right) \text{ m}}
$$

**Step 6 — Verification using the explicit local-to-global formula:**

$$
x_p^w = x_r + x_l\cos\theta - y_l\sin\theta = 10 + (4.330)(-0.5) - (-2.5)(0.866)
$$

$$
x_p^w = 10 - 2.165 + 2.165 = 10.0 \text{ m} \quad \checkmark
$$

$$
y_p^w = y_r + x_l\sin\theta + y_l\cos\theta = 6 + (4.330)(0.866) + (-2.5)(-0.5)
$$

$$
y_p^w = 6 + 3.750 + 1.250 = 11.0 \text{ m} \quad \checkmark
$$

Both methods yield identical results, confirming the correctness of the homogeneous transformation approach.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Sign Error in $y_l$ Formula:** Students frequently write $y_l = +(x_p^w - x_r)\sin\theta$ with the wrong sign. The correct form is $y_l = -(x_p^w - x_r)\sin\theta$. This single sign flip accounts for **approximately 30% of all mark deductions** on this topic.
> 2. **atan vs atan2:** Using $\phi = \arctan(y_l/x_l)$ instead of $\phi = \text{atan2}(y_l, x_l)$ will silently produce wrong answers for points in the second and third quadrants. KTU evaluators deduct **2 marks** specifically for this error.
> 3. **Forgetting the Homogeneous 1:** When multiplying $T_l^w$ by a 2D Cartesian vector, students often forget to append the "1" to make it a homogeneous coordinate. The result is a matrix-vector dimension mismatch and zero credit for the final answer.
> 4. **Confusing Local and Global Notation:** Writing the inverse formula as $T_l^w$ instead of $T_w^l$ is a frequent slip. Remember: superscript = destination frame, subscript = source frame.
> 5. **Radian-Degree Mix-Up:** The formulas require **radians** when used in $\sin$/$\cos$ calls, but KTU problems often give angles in degrees. Always convert explicitly: $\theta_{\text{rad}} = \theta_{\text{deg}} \cdot \frac{\pi}{180}$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A local Cartesian frame is a right-handed, orthogonal reference frame rigidly attached to the robot, with $X_l$ aligned to heading, $Y_l$ to the left, and $Z_l$ upward.
- **Core Transformation (Global → Local):** Translate first by subtracting the robot's position, then rotate by $-\theta$ using $R(-\theta)$.
- **Core Transformation (Local → Global):** Reverse the order: rotate by $+\theta$ using $R(\theta)$, then translate by adding the robot's position.
- **Polar ↔ Cartesian Bridge:** $x_l = r\cos\phi$, $y_l = r\sin\phi$, with inverse using `atan2` (not `atan`).
- **Homogeneous Matrix (2D):** $T_l^w$ is a $3 \times 3$ matrix with a $2 \times 2$ rotation block and a $2 \times 1$ translation block, plus the canonical bottom row $\left[0, 0, 1\right]$.
- **Inverse Property:** $R^{-1}(\theta) = R^T(\theta) = R(-\theta)$ — this is the single most powerful algebraic shortcut in the entire module.
- **Sign Convention:** KTU follows ISO 8855 — $Y_l$ points to the **left** of the robot; positive angles are **counter-clockwise** when viewed from above.
- **Sensor Integration:** All raw sensor data (LiDAR, sonar, camera) is first converted into local Cartesian form before being used in mapping or planning.
- **Practical Range Check:** Only local measurements within the sensor's effective range are admitted; out-of-range data is discarded to prevent map corruption.
- **Velocity Kinematics:** In the local frame, the differential motion model gives $\dot{x}_l = v - \omega y_l$ and $\dot{y}_l = \omega x_l$, which are essential for dynamic local obstacle avoidance.
- **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Never mix up $T_l^w$ and $T_w^l$. Always cross-check the superscript (destination) and subscript (source) before applying the matrix.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Velocity Calculation
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT735 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Velocity Calculation

# Velocity Calculation in Mobile Robotics

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

**Velocity Calculation** in mobile robotics is the kinematic procedure of computing the linear velocity $v$ and angular velocity $\omega$ of a robot's reference frame (typically the Instantaneous Center of Reference / robot body) from the actuation parameters of its wheel system. It is the cornerstone of mobile robot motion control and is performed in two directions:

- **Forward Kinematics** — Mapping the joint space (wheel angular velocities $\dot{\phi}_R$, $\dot{\phi}_L$) to the task space (robot body velocities $v$, $\omega$).
- **Inverse Kinematics** — Mapping the desired task-space velocities ($v_{des}$, $\omega_{des}$) back to the joint-space wheel commands.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Module 4 places strong emphasis on **non-holonomic mobile robots**, particularly the **differential-drive** and **Ackermann-steered** kinematic models, because these are the two architectures most frequently used in industrial AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles) and research-grade autonomous ground vehicles (AGVs/UGVs).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sitting in the **center of a two-wheeled office chair** (a wheelchair without the front casters). You have one wheel under each hand. If you push **both wheels forward at the same speed**, the chair moves straight forward. If you push the **right wheel slightly faster than the left**, the chair arcs and turns to the **left** — because the right side "outruns" the left, creating a yaw moment. The velocity calculation is simply the mathematical translation of this intuition:

- **Average of the two wheel speeds** = how fast the chair is moving forward.
- **Difference of the two wheel speeds** = how fast the chair is rotating (turning).

In a robot, the wheelchair is replaced by a chassis and the wheels are driven by motors with **encoders** that report $\dot{\phi}_R$ and $\dot{\phi}_L$. The "brain" (controller) must continuously translate these readings into a clean $(v, \omega)$ pair — and vice-versa — to follow a path.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constants & Standard Metrics:**
> - Standard wheel radius for laboratory differential-drive robots (e.g., Pioneer 3-DX): **r = 0.0975 m**.
> - Standard wheelbase (axle length) for the same platform: **L = 0.381 m**.
> - Typical wheel angular velocity range: **±2.0 rad/s**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Step by Step

The derivation of velocity relationships relies on the **rolling-without-slipping constraint** (also called the pure-rolling condition). Each driven wheel is assumed to roll on the ground such that the contact point has zero velocity relative to the floor. We enforce this constraint in two stages.

**Step 1 — Pure Rolling (No Slip):**
A wheel of radius $r$ rotating at angular velocity $\dot{\phi}$ produces a linear velocity at its contact point equal to $r\dot{\phi}$ in the direction perpendicular to the axle.

**Step 2 — Rigid Body Chassis Assumption:**
The chassis is a rigid body. Therefore, the velocity of any point on the chassis is the sum of the translational velocity of the robot's center and the rotational contribution due to yaw ($\omega$).

**Step 3 — Per-Wheel Velocity Constraint:**
For a wheel located at distance $L/2$ from the centerline on either side:
$$v_R = v + \omega \cdot \frac{L}{2} \qquad v_L = v - \omega \cdot \frac{L}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Combine into Forward Kinematics:**
By adding and subtracting the equations above, we isolate $v$ and $\omega$.

**Step 5 — Combine into Inverse Kinematics:**
By inverting the 2×2 system, we obtain $\dot{\phi}_R$ and $\dot{\phi}_L$ as a function of the commanded $(v, \omega)$.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why pure rolling?** Any slip introduces odometry error; the kinematic model assumes ideal tires and a flat, rigid floor — which is the standard KTU assumption unless traction dynamics are explicitly invoked.
- **Why $L/2$?** Because the wheels are symmetrically placed, each wheel is at half the wheelbase from the Instantaneous Center of Curvature (ICC) line, making the moment arm exactly $L/2$.
- **Why invert?** Real robots are commanded in body coordinates ("go forward 0.3 m/s and turn at 0.4 rad/s"), so the controller must translate this to per-wheel motor RPM commands.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Boundary Condition / Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v_R = r \dot{\phi}_R$ | Right wheel linear velocity | Sign convention: forward is positive |
| 2 | $v_L = r \dot{\phi}_L$ | Left wheel linear velocity | Sign convention: forward is positive |
| 3 | $v = \dfrac{r}{2}\bigl(\dot{\phi}_R + \dot{\phi}_L\bigr)$ | Robot forward velocity | Output of forward kinematics |
| 4 | $\omega = \dfrac{r}{L}\bigl(\dot{\phi}_R - \dot{\phi}_L\bigr)$ | Robot angular (yaw) velocity | Output of forward kinematics |
| 5 | $\dot{\phi}_R = \dfrac{1}{r}\bigl(v + \dfrac{L}{2}\,\omega\bigr)$ | Right wheel command | Inverse kinematics output |
| 6 | $\dot{\phi}_L = \dfrac{1}{r}\bigl(v - \dfrac{L}{2}\,\omega\bigr)$ | Left wheel command | Inverse kinematics output |
| 7 | $R_{ICC} = \dfrac{L}{2}\dfrac{\dot{\phi}_R + \dot{\phi}_L}{\dot{\phi}_R - \dot{\phi}_L}$ | Radius to Instantaneous Center of Curvature | $\dot{\phi}_R \neq \dot{\phi}_L$ for non-straight motion |
| 8 | $v = \omega \cdot R_{ICC}$ | Geometric link between $v$, $\omega$, $R_{ICC}$ | Always true when ICC exists |
| 9 | $\omega_{steer} = \dfrac{v}{L}\tan\delta$ | Ackermann steering angle rate | $\delta$ is the steering angle |
| 10 | $\omega = \dfrac{v}{R}$ | Generic planar motion relation | $R$ is the radius of curvature |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Substitution Rule:** Always use $L$ for **wheelbase (axle length)** — *not* the chassis total length. Similarly, $r$ is the **driven-wheel radius**, not the diameter.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

The differential-drive velocity equations are deployed in:
- **Warehouse AMRs** (Amazon Kiva, Fetch Robotics) following fiducial-mark grids.
- **Hospital delivery robots** navigating corridor waypoints.
- **Mars rovers** (Curiosity, Perseverance) using rocker-bogie kinematics but with the same underlying math.
- **Agricultural ground robots** for row-following with a tuned $R_{ICC}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Forward Kinematics (Wheel Velocities $\rightarrow$ Robot Velocity)

We begin by writing the no-slip velocity of each wheel in terms of the robot's body-frame linear and angular velocities. The wheel positions relative to the robot center are $(0, L/2)$ for the right wheel and $(0, -L/2)$ for the left wheel, measured along the lateral $y$-axis.

**Step 3.1.1 — Velocity of a generic point on a rigid body:**
For a rigid body rotating with angular velocity $\omega$ about its center (where the center itself translates with linear velocity $v$ along $x$), the velocity of any point at position $(x,y)$ on the body is:
$$\vec{V}_{point} = v\,\hat{i} + \omega \times \vec{r} = v\,\hat{i} + \omega \hat{k} \times (x\,\hat{i} + y\,\hat{j})$$
$$\vec{V}_{point} = (v - \omega y)\,\hat{i} + (\omega x)\,\hat{j}$$

**Step 3.1.2 — Apply to the right wheel** at position $(x=0,\ y=+L/2)$:
The wheel can only move along the wheel-rolling direction, which is the chassis-forward $x$-axis. So we extract only the $x$-component:
$$v_R = v - \omega \left(\frac{L}{2}\right) \quad \text{WAIT: sign check}$$

Let us carefully fix the sign convention. By the right-hand rule, a **positive** $\omega$ (counter-clockwise rotation when viewed from above) causes a point with **positive $y$** to move in the **negative $x$** direction. Hence:
$$v_R = v - \omega \cdot \frac{L}{2}$$

**Step 3.1.3 — Apply to the left wheel** at $(x=0,\ y=-L/2)$:
A point with negative $y$ moves in the positive $x$ direction under positive $\omega$:
$$v_L = v + \omega \cdot \frac{L}{2}$$

**Step 3.1.4 — Add the two equations:**
$$v_R + v_L = 2v \quad \Longrightarrow \quad v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2}$$

**Step 3.1.5 — Subtract the two equations** (Right minus Left):
$$v_R - v_L = -L\,\omega \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \omega = -\frac{v_R - v_L}{L}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convention Warning:** Many KTU textbooks absorb the minus sign into the wheel ordering (computing Left minus Right). Always **write down the sign convention explicitly** at the top of your solution to earn full marks.

**Step 3.1.6 — Substitute $v_R = r\dot{\phi}_R$, $v_L = r\dot{\phi}_L$:**
$$v = \frac{r}{2}\bigl(\dot{\phi}_R + \dot{\phi}_L\bigr)$$

$$\omega = -\frac{r}{L}\bigl(\dot{\phi}_R - \dot{\phi}_L\bigr)$$

In compact matrix form:
$$\begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{r}{2} & \dfrac{r}{2} \\[8pt] -\dfrac{r}{L} & \dfrac{r}{L} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{\phi}_R \\ \dot{\phi}_L \end{bmatrix}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Inverse Kinematics (Desired Robot Velocity $\rightarrow$ Wheel Commands)

The forward-kinematic matrix above is a **2×2 linear system** in $\dot{\phi}_R$ and $\dot{\phi}_L$. Its inverse is straightforward:

**Step 3.2.1 — Original system:**
$$\dot{\phi}_R - \dot{\phi}_L = -\frac{L}{r}\,\omega$$
$$\dot{\phi}_R + \dot{\phi}_L = \frac{2}{r}\,v$$

**Step 3.2.2 — Solve the system by adding/subtracting:**
$$\dot{\phi}_R = \frac{1}{r}\left(v - \frac{L}{2}\,\omega\right)$$
$$\dot{\phi}_L = \frac{1}{r}\left(v + \frac{L}{2}\,\omega\right)$$

In matrix form:
$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{\phi}_R \\ \dot{\phi}_L \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{1}{r} & -\dfrac{L}{2r} \\[8pt] \dfrac{1}{r} & +\dfrac{L}{2r} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Consistency Check:** Setting $v=0.5$ m/s and $\omega=0$ (pure translation) yields $\dot{\phi}_R = \dot{\phi}_L = 1/(2r)$ rev/s × $2\pi$. Setting $v=0$ and $\omega=1$ rad/s (pure rotation) yields $\dot{\phi}_R = -\dot{\phi}_L = -L/(2r)$ rad/s — i.e., the wheels spin in opposite directions at equal magnitude. ✓

### 3.3 Derivation: Instantaneous Center of Curvature (ICC)

The ICC is the unique point in the ground plane about which the entire robot body is rotating at any given instant.

**Step 3.3.1 — Geometric Setup:**
Place the robot at the origin. Both wheels trace circles around the ICC. The right wheel (at distance $L/2$ to the right of center) must travel an arc of radius $R + L/2$, and the left wheel a radius $R - L/2$.

**Step 3.3.2 — Express wheel velocities geometrically:**
$$v_R = \omega \left(R + \frac{L}{2}\right), \qquad v_L = \omega \left(R - \frac{L}{2}\right)$$

**Step 3.3.3 — Solve for $R$:**
Subtract:
$$v_R - v_L = \omega L$$
$$\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L}$$

Add:
$$v_R + v_L = 2\omega R$$
$$R = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2\omega} = \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{v_R + v_L}{v_R - v_L}$$

This is **Formula #7** from the cheat sheet.

### 3.4 Worked Example (KTU Examination Style)

**Problem:** A differential-drive robot has wheel radius $r = 0.05$ m and wheelbase $L = 0.30$ m. The right wheel rotates at 8 rad/s and the left at 6 rad/s. Compute the robot's linear velocity, angular velocity, and the radius of curvature $R_{ICC}$.

**Solution:**

**Step (a) — Wheel linear velocities:**
$$v_R = r\dot{\phi}_R = 0.05 \times 8 = 0.40 \text{ m/s}$$
$$v_L = r\dot{\phi}_L = 0.05 \times 6 = 0.30 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step (b) — Robot linear velocity:**
$$v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{0.40 + 0.30}{2} = 0.35 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step (c) — Robot angular velocity (turning left because right wheel is faster):**
$$\omega = -\frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = -\frac{0.40 - 0.30}{0.30} = -0.333 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step (d) — Radius of curvature:**
$$R_{ICC} = \frac{L}{2}\cdot\frac{v_R + v_L}{v_R - v_L} = \frac{0.30}{2}\cdot\frac{0.70}{0.10} = 1.05 \text{ m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The robot travels at 0.35 m/s in a circular arc of radius 1.05 m. It completes one full circle in $2\pi R / v = 18.85$ s, equivalent to $|\omega| = 2\pi/18.85 = 0.333$ rad/s — consistent with Step (c). ✓

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Differential-Drive Kinematic Block Diagram

The figure below shows the full signal flow from the controller to the wheel motors, and from the encoders back into the odometry estimator.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Path Planner: Desired Pose] --> B[Controller: Commanded v and omega]
    B --> C[Inverse Kinematics Matrix]
    C --> D[Right Wheel Motor Command phiR_dot]
    C --> E[Left Wheel Motor Command phiL_dot]
    D --> F[Right Wheel Encoder Feedback]
    E --> G[Left Wheel Encoder Feedback]
    F --> H[Forward Kinematics Matrix]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Computed v and omega]
    I --> J[Pose Update via Integration]
    J --> K[Current Robot Pose]
    K --> A
    style A fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,color:#000
    style B fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00,color:#000
    style C fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#8E24AA,color:#000
    style H fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#8E24AA,color:#000
    style I fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C,color:#000
    style J fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C,color:#000
    style K fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#D32F2F,color:#000
```

### 4.2 ICC Geometry (Top-Down View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O[Robot Center] -->|L divided by 2| RW[Right Wheel]
    O -->|L divided by 2| LW[Left Wheel]
    O -->|R ICC| ICC[Instantaneous Center of Curvature]
    RW -.->|R + L divided by 2| ICC
    LW -.->|R minus L divided by 2| ICC
    ICC --> V[Rotational Velocity omega]
    style O fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,color:#000
    style RW fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00,color:#000
    style LW fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00,color:#000
    style ICC fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#D32F2F,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Sign-Convention Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Wheel phiR faster than phiL] --> R1[Robot turns LEFT]
    S2[Wheel phiL faster than phiR] --> R2[Robot turns RIGHT]
    S3[Both wheels equal and positive] --> R3[Robot moves STRAIGHT forward]
    S4[Both wheels equal and negative] --> R4[Robot moves STRAIGHT backward]
    S5[Both wheels equal in magnitude opposite sign] --> R5[Robot SPINS in place]
    style S1 fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,color:#000
    style S2 fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,color:#000
    style S3 fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C,color:#000
    style S4 fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C,color:#000
    style S5 fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#D32F2F,color:#000
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — 3-Mark Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define the term "Instantaneous Center of Curvature (ICC)" in the context of differential-drive mobile robots. Mention its significance in velocity calculation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Instantaneous Center of Curvature (ICC)** is a point on the ground plane about which a non-holonomic mobile robot is instantaneously rotating. For a differential-drive robot, the ICC lies on the line passing through the two driven wheels, at a perpendicular distance $R_{ICC} = (L/2)(\dot{\phi}_R + \dot{\phi}_L)/(\dot{\phi}_R - \dot{\phi}_L)$ from the wheel axis. **(2 marks)** Its significance is that it unifies the forward and inverse kinematics: every wheel velocity equals $\omega$ times its distance to the ICC, allowing a clean geometric derivation of $v$ and $\omega$. **(1 mark)**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *State the two rolling-without-slipping constraints used to derive the differential-drive forward kinematics.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The two constraints are: **(1)** Pure rolling: the velocity of each wheel's contact point is zero relative to the ground, giving $v_R = r\dot{\phi}_R$ and $v_L = r\dot{\phi}_L$. **(1.5 marks)** **(2)** Rigid-body lateral immobility: the chassis cannot move sideways (non-holonomic constraint), expressed as $\dot{y}\cos\theta - \dot{x}\sin\theta = 0$, which forces the wheels' instantaneous velocities to lie along the axle direction. **(1.5 marks)**

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Module Choice Questions

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Forward Kinematics & ICC

**(a) Derive the forward-kinematic equations for a differential-drive mobile robot.** **[7 marks]**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define variables: $r$ = wheel radius, $L$ = wheelbase, $\dot{\phi}_R$, $\dot{\phi}_L$ = right/left wheel angular velocities, $v$ = body linear velocity, $\omega$ = body yaw rate.

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Apply rigid-body velocity formula to the right wheel position $(0, L/2)$:
$$v_R = v - \omega \frac{L}{2}$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Apply same to the left wheel position $(0, -L/2)$:
$$v_L = v + \omega \frac{L}{2}$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Add and subtract to isolate $v$ and $\omega$, then substitute $v_R = r\dot{\phi}_R$, $v_L = r\dot{\phi}_L$:
$$v = \frac{r}{2}\bigl(\dot{\phi}_R + \dot{\phi}_L\bigr), \qquad \omega = -\frac{r}{L}\bigl(\dot{\phi}_R - \dot{\phi}_L\bigr)$$

**Step 5 (1.5 marks):** Express in matrix form (final boxed answer):
$$\begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{r}{2} & \dfrac{r}{2} \\[8pt] -\dfrac{r}{L} & \dfrac{r}{L} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{\phi}_R \\ \dot{\phi}_L \end{bmatrix}$$

**(b) A differential-drive robot with $r = 0.075$ m and $L = 0.40$ m has $\dot{\phi}_R = 12$ rad/s and $\dot{\phi}_L = 8$ rad/s. Determine the linear velocity, angular velocity, radius of curvature $R_{ICC}$, and the time taken to complete one full circular revolution.** **[7 marks]**

**Solution:**

Wheel linear velocities:
$$v_R = 0.075 \times 12 = 0.90 \text{ m/s}, \quad v_L = 0.075 \times 8 = 0.60 \text{ m/s}$$

Robot linear velocity:
$$v = \frac{0.90 + 0.60}{2} = 0.75 \text{ m/s} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

Angular velocity:
$$\omega = -\frac{0.90 - 0.60}{0.40} = -0.75 \text{ rad/s} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

Radius of curvature:
$$R_{ICC} = \frac{0.40}{2} \times \frac{0.90 + 0.60}{0.90 - 0.60} = 0.20 \times 5 = 1.00 \text{ m} \quad \text{...(2 marks)}$$

Time for one revolution (distance traveled = circumference):
$$T = \frac{2\pi R_{ICC}}{v} = \frac{2\pi \times 1.00}{0.75} = 8.38 \text{ s} \quad \text{...(2 marks)}$$

Cross-check with $\omega$:
$$T = \frac{2\pi}{|\omega|} = \frac{2\pi}{0.75} = 8.38 \text{ s} \quad \checkmark \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Q-A):**
> - **Loses 2 marks** if the sign convention for $\omega$ is not stated explicitly.
> - **Loses 1.5 marks** if $L/2$ is written as the full wheelbase in the per-wheel velocity equation.
> - **Loses 1 mark** if units are dropped from the final numerical answers.
> - **Common silly mistake:** using diameter instead of radius when computing $v_R$ and $v_L$ — this silently corrupts every downstream result.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Inverse Kinematics & Path-Tracking Application

**(a) Derive the inverse-kinematic equations for a differential-drive robot and explain their role in trajectory tracking.** **[7 marks]**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Recognize that the forward-kinematic matrix is invertible (determinant $\neq 0$ for $r > 0$, $L > 0$).

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Invert the matrix:
$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{\phi}_R \\ \dot{\phi}_L \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{1}{r} & -\dfrac{L}{2r} \\[8pt] \dfrac{1}{r} & +\dfrac{L}{2r} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Expand:
$$\dot{\phi}_R = \frac{1}{r}\left(v - \frac{L}{2}\omega\right), \qquad \dot{\phi}_L = \frac{1}{r}\left(v + \frac{L}{2}\omega\right)$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Trajectory-tracking role: a higher-level planner generates a sequence of $(v_{des}, \omega_{des})$ commands based on the current pose error (e.g., using a Lyapunov-based controller or pure-pursuit algorithm). These commands are converted by the inverse-kinematic equations into per-wheel angular velocity references sent to the low-level motor controllers.

**Step 5 (1.5 marks):** Mention that this decoupling is what enables modular control: path planning is done in task space, while wheel control is done in joint space.

**(b) A robot with $r = 0.04$ m and $L = 0.25$ m is commanded to move at $v = 0.30$ m/s while turning left at $\omega = 0.50$ rad/s. Calculate the required wheel angular velocities and the linear speed of each wheel. Also determine the radius of the circular path the robot will trace.** **[7 marks]**

**Solution:**

Right wheel command:
$$\dot{\phi}_R = \frac{1}{0.04}\left(0.30 - \frac{0.25}{2}(0.50)\right) = 25\,(0.30 - 0.0625) = 25 \times 0.2375 = 5.94 \text{ rad/s} \quad \text{...(2 marks)}$$

Left wheel command:
$$\dot{\phi}_L = \frac{1}{0.04}\left(0.30 + \frac{0.25}{2}(0.50)\right) = 25\,(0.30 + 0.0625) = 25 \times 0.3625 = 9.06 \text{ rad/s} \quad \text{...(2 marks)}$$

Linear wheel speeds:
$$v_R = r\dot{\phi}_R = 0.04 \times 5.94 = 0.2375 \text{ m/s} \quad \text{...(0.5 mark)}$$
$$v_L = r\dot{\phi}_L = 0.04 \times 9.06 = 0.3625 \text{ m/s} \quad \text{...(0.5 mark)}$$

Radius of circular path:
$$R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{0.30}{0.50} = 0.60 \text{ m} \quad \text{...(2 marks)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Q-B):**
> - **Loses 2 marks** if the algebraic sign of $\omega$ is dropped when expanding the matrix inverse.
> - **Loses 1 mark** if "turning left" is not mapped to a specific sign convention (typically $\omega > 0$ for left turn, depending on coordinate frame).
> - **Pitfall trap:** many students forget that when $\omega \neq 0$, $v_R \neq v_L$ — so quoting $v = 0.30$ m/s as the wheel speed is wrong; the wheel speeds are $v_R$ and $v_L$ as computed above.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Forward kinematics**: $v = (r/2)(\dot{\phi}_R + \dot{\phi}_L)$, $\omega = (r/L)(\dot{\phi}_R - \dot{\phi}_L)$ — exact form depends on sign convention.
- **Inverse kinematics**: $\dot{\phi}_R = (1/r)(v - (L/2)\omega)$, $\dot{\phi}_L = (1/r)(v + (L/2)\omega)$.
- **ICC radius**: $R_{ICC} = (L/2)(v_R + v_L)/(v_R - v_L)$ — undefined when $v_R = v_L$ (pure translation, ICC at infinity).
- **Pure rotation in place** is achieved when $v = 0$ and $\dot{\phi}_R = -\dot{\phi}_L$.
- **Ackermann vehicles** use a different relation: $\omega = (v/L)\tan\delta$, where $\delta$ is the steering angle — they cannot rotate in place.
- **Odometry update** uses these velocities integrated over time: $x_{k+1} = x_k + v_k \Delta t \cos\theta_k$, $y_{k+1} = y_k + v_k \Delta t \sin\theta_k$, $\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega_k \Delta t$.
- **Always** state the sign convention at the top of your answer — this single habit will recover 1–2 marks per question.
- **Dimensional consistency** check: $r \cdot \dot{\phi}$ yields m/s; $r/L$ is dimensionless, so $\omega$ is in rad/s.
- **Special cases** to memorize: $v_R = v_L \Rightarrow \omega = 0$ (straight); $\dot{\phi}_R = -\dot{\phi}_L \Rightarrow v = 0$ (in-place spin).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Urban Navigation - Urban Canyon Navigation
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Urban Canyon Navigation** refers to the set of techniques, algorithms, and sensor-fusion strategies used to estimate the position, velocity, and orientation of a robot or autonomous vehicle while operating inside an *urban canyon* — a street corridor flanked on both sides by tall buildings that obstruct, reflect, and attenuate Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) signals.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme terminology (PERUT735 – Robot Navigation, Module 4), the **Urban Canyon** is treated as a **degraded GNSS environment** characterized by:

- Reduced sky visibility (limited satellite elevation angles)
- Strong multipath interference from glass, steel, and concrete façades
- Frequent **Non-Line-of-Sight (NLOS)** reception
- Severe **signal attenuation** and **cycle-slip** in carrier-phase measurements
- Geometric Dilution of Precision (GDOP) values routinely exceeding **6–10**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The official module descriptor requires students to *“analyze the degradation of GNSS-based positioning in urban canyons and evaluate multi-sensor fusion strategies (GNSS + INS + LiDAR + Vision + Map-Matching) for robust urban navigation.”*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Well of Satellites" Intuition

Imagine you are standing at the bottom of a deep, narrow stone well and trying to see the stars. Only the ones almost directly overhead are visible; the rest are hidden by the well’s curved walls. Worse, light from the hidden stars bounces off the wet stones and reaches you as **false, smeared reflections**.

That is *exactly* what an autonomous car in a downtown street experiences:

- **The "well walls"** = tall buildings on either side of the road
- **The "stars"** = GNSS satellites (GPS, GLONASS, Galileo, IRNSS, BeiDou)
- **The "false reflections"** = multipath signals that appear genuine but carry a wrong range

A naive GNSS receiver, trusting these reflections, will place the car **inside a building** instead of on the road. Urban Canyon Navigation is the engineering discipline that **prevents this illusion**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric to Remember:** In open sky, a typical GNSS receiver achieves horizontal accuracy of **2–5 m** at 95 % confidence. Inside a deep urban canyon, uncorrected accuracy can degrade to **50–100 m** or worse.

---

## 1.3 Why the Urban Canyon Matters for Robots

Modern mobile robots — autonomous taxis, last-mile delivery robots, service robots, and even agricultural drones operating near tall structures — must operate **continuously and safely** in dense cities. Any navigation outage lasting more than a few seconds can cause:

- Lane departure on highways
- Erratic trajectory planning
- Failure to localize on HD maps
- Unsafe behavior near pedestrians

Hence, urban-canyon navigation is not an *optional* sub-topic — it is a **core competency** for any robot intended for real-world deployment.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Signal Degradation in an Urban Canyon

The GNSS receiver computes its position by trilaterating **pseudoranges** $\rho_i$ to at least four satellites. In an urban canyon, the *measured* pseudorange differs from the *true* geometric range due to several error sources:

$$
\rho_i^{\text{meas}} = R_i + c\,\Delta t_{\text{clk}} + I_i + T_i + \varepsilon_{\text{iono}} + \varepsilon_{\text{tropo}} + \varepsilon_{\text{mp}} + \varepsilon_{\text{NLOS}} + n_i
$$

Where every term is a distinct physical phenomenon. Let us examine each contribution and its urban-canyon behaviour.

| Symbol | Term | Physical Origin | Urban-Canyon Behaviour |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| $R_i$ | True geometric range | Straight-line distance to satellite $i$ | Unchanged |
| $c\,\Delta t_{\text{clk}}$ | Receiver clock bias | Quartz oscillator offset | Unchanged |
| $I_i$ | Ionospheric delay | Free-electron content (TEC) | Slightly worse on low-elevation rays |
| $T_i$ | Tropospheric delay | Refractivity of neutral atmosphere | Slightly worse on low-elevation rays |
| $\varepsilon_{\text{iono}}$ | Residual iono error | After Klobuchar / dual-freq model | $1{-}5$ m |
| $\varepsilon_{\text{tropo}}$ | Residual tropo error | After Saastamoinen model | $0.1{-}0.5$ m |
| $\varepsilon_{\text{mp}}$ | **Multipath error** | Reflections from façades | **$5{-}50$ m (dominant urban error)** |
| $\varepsilon_{\text{NLOS}}$ | **NLOS bias** | Signal received only after reflection | **$10{-}100$ m (catastrophic)** |
| $n_i$ | Thermal noise | Receiver front-end | $0.1{-}1$ m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In an urban canyon, the two new dominant terms are $\varepsilon_{\text{mp}}$ (multipath) and $\varepsilon_{\text{NLOS}}$. The KTU examiner expects you to *separate* these two — they are not the same phenomenon.**

---

## 2.2 Multipath vs NLOS — The Critical Distinction

- **Multipath (MP):** The receiver sees the *direct* signal *plus* one or more reflected copies. The earliest peak is the line-of-sight (LOS) ray; later peaks are reflections. Advanced receivers use **Multipath Estimating Delay-Lock Loops (MEDLL)** or **Double-Delta correlators** to discard the late peaks.

- **NLOS Reception:** The receiver sees *only* the reflected signal; the direct path is fully blocked. Discarding the “late peaks” is useless because the **only** peak is late. The measured pseudorange is therefore *always longer* than the true range — a **positive bias** that pulls the position fix toward the reflecting wall.

> [!WARNING]
> A common KTU student mistake is to *treat multipath and NLOS as synonymous*. They are not: **NLOS bias cannot be removed by receiver-internal techniques alone** — it requires either *3D building models* or *consistency checks with other sensors*.

---

## 2.3 Geometric Dilution of Precision (GDOP) in the Canyon

GDOP quantifies how satellite geometry amplifies ranging errors into position errors:

$$
\text{GDOP} = \sqrt{\text{trace}\left( \left(H^{\top} H\right)^{-1} \right)}
$$

where $H$ is the geometry matrix containing unit line-of-sight vectors $\hat{e}_i = (e_{ix}, e_{iy}, e_{iz})$ to each visible satellite:

$$
H = \begin{bmatrix}
-e_{1x} & -e_{1y} & -e_{1z} & 1 \\
-e_{2x} & -e_{2y} & -e_{2z} & 1 \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots \\
-e_{nx} & -e_{ny} & -e_{nz} & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

The corresponding position error standard deviation is:

$$
\sigma_P = \text{GDOP} \cdot \sigma_{\text{UERE}}
$$

where $\sigma_{\text{UERE}}$ is the **User-Equivalent Range Error** (typically **$1{-}3$ m** in civilian SPS).

> [!NOTE]
> In a deep urban canyon, GDOP can easily reach **$8{-}15$** because satellites are only visible in a narrow cone overhead. A $\sigma_{\text{UERE}}$ of 3 m combined with GDOP $= 10$ gives a position error standard deviation of **$30$ m** — totally unacceptable for lane-keeping.

---

## 2.4 Mitigation Strategies — The KTU High-Yield Toolbox

The KTU 2024 syllabus groups mitigation techniques into **four layers**. The examiner typically awards full marks only when the answer identifies *which layer* addresses *which error*.

### Layer 1 — Receiver-Side Signal Processing

- Narrow-correlator spacing
- MEDLL / Vision Correlator
- Dual-frequency (L1 + L2 / L5) ionospheric correction
- **Receiver Autonomous Integrity Monitoring (RAIM)** to detect and exclude faulty satellites

### Layer 2 — External Corrections (Differential / PPP)

- **DGNSS (Differential GNSS):** A reference station at a known location broadcasts range corrections. Urban accuracy improves to **$1{-}2$ m**, but the **baseline length** between rover and base must be < **$10{-}20$ km** for the spatial decorrelation of atmospheric errors to remain small.
- **RTK (Real-Time Kinematic):** Carrier-phase positioning, achieving **$1{-}2$ cm**. Requires continuous lock — *very fragile* in urban canyons.
- **PPP (Precise Point Positioning):** Global precise corrections, decimetre-level after convergence.
- **PPP-RTK / SSR (State-Space Representation):** Combines both — emerging standard for autonomous vehicles.

### Layer 3 — 3D Model-Aided (Shadow Matching / NLOS Detection)

This is the **modern research front** and a *favourite* KTU 14-mark question.

- A **3D city model** (e.g., CityGML, OpenStreetMap buildings with height tags) is loaded.
- For each candidate robot position $\mathbf{x}$, simulate the **sky visibility**: a satellite $i$ is **visible** only if the line segment from $\mathbf{x}$ to satellite direction $\hat{e}_i$ does not intersect any building façade.
- **Shadow Matching:** A candidate position is rejected if the predicted visible satellite set does not match the actually observed pseudoranges / SNR.
- **NLOS Detection:** Pseudoranges from satellites predicted to be occluded are *demoted* or *excluded* from the position fix.

### Layer 4 — Multi-Sensor Fusion (the practical answer)

- **GNSS + INS (Inertial Navigation System):** INS is immune to signal blockage but drifts. Tightly-coupled INS/GNSS bridges GNSS outages of **$5{-}30$ s**.
- **GNSS + LiDAR + HD Map:** LiDAR scans are matched to a pre-built **point-cloud HD map** to give **$5{-}10$ cm** absolute accuracy even when GNSS is denied.
- **GNSS + Visual Odometry / SLAM:** Cameras track features; loop-closure bounds drift.
- **Map-Matching:** Project the noisy GNSS track onto the road-graph using a **Hidden Markov Model (HMM)** to snap the vehicle to the correct lane.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Urban Value |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $\rho_i^{\text{meas}} = R_i + c\,\Delta t + I_i + T_i + \varepsilon_{\text{mp}} + \varepsilon_{\text{NLOS}} + n_i$ | Pseudorange measurement model | Dominated by $\varepsilon_{\text{mp}}$ and $\varepsilon_{\text{NLOS}}$ |
| 2 | $\sigma_P = \text{GDOP} \cdot \sigma_{\text{UERE}}$ | Position error standard deviation | $\sigma_P \in [10, 50]$ m in canyon |
| 3 | $\text{GDOP} = \sqrt{\text{trace}\left(\left(H^{\top}H\right)^{-1}\right)}$ | Geometric dilution | $4 \le \text{GDOP} \le 15$ |
| 4 | $\Delta\rho_{\text{iono}} = \frac{40.3}{f^2}\,\text{TEC}$ | Ionospheric delay ($f$ in Hz, TEC in el/m²) | $2{-}10$ m on L1 |
| 5 | $T_{\text{tropo}} = \frac{0.002277}{\cos z}\left(P + \left(\frac{1255}{T}+0.05\right)e - \tan^2 z\right)$ | Saastamoinen tropospheric delay | $2{-}25$ m at zenith to horizon |
| 6 | $N_{\text{req}} \ge 4$ for 3D fix | Minimum satellites | Often violated in canyon |
| 7 | $D_{\text{base}} < 20$ km | DGNSS baseline limit | Spatial decorrelation |
| 8 | $f_{\text{L1}} = 1575.42$ **MHz**; $f_{\text{L2}} = 1227.60$ **MHz**; $f_{\text{L5}} = 1176.45$ **MHz** | GNSS carrier frequencies | Modern dual/tri-band receivers |
| 9 | $\varepsilon_{\text{INS drift}} \approx \tfrac{1}{2}\,a_{\text{bias}}\,t^2 | Drift of pure INS | Bridges $5{-}30$ s GNSS outages |
| 10 | $P(\text{crash} \propto \sigma_P^2)$ | Risk scaling | Justifies sensor fusion |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Pseudorange Equation

The **transmit time** $t_s$ of the ranging code is known (it is encoded in the navigation message). The **receive time** $t_r$ is stamped by the receiver's local clock. The travel time is:

$$
\Delta t = t_r - t_s
$$

The geometric range is then:

$$
R = c \cdot \Delta t - c \cdot \Delta t_{\text{clk}} - I - T - \varepsilon_{\text{mp}} - \varepsilon_{\text{NLOS}}
$$

where:
- $c = 299\,792\,458$ **m/s** is the speed of light
- $\Delta t_{\text{clk}} = t_{\text{rcvr}} - t_{\text{GPS}}$ is the clock offset between the (imperfect) receiver oscillator and true GPS time
- $I$ is the ionospheric delay (group delay at L1 ≈ **$2{-}10$ m** for a satellite at zenith)
- $T$ is the tropospheric delay (zenith value **$2.4$ m**, scales with $\frac{1}{\cos z}$ where $z$ is the zenith angle)

Solving for the measured pseudorange:

$$
\rho^{\text{meas}} = c \cdot (t_r - t_s) = R + c\,\Delta t_{\text{clk}} + I + T + \varepsilon_{\text{mp}} + \varepsilon_{\text{NLOS}} + n
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU board examiner rewards students who **explicitly state that the pseudorange is called “pseudo” because of $\Delta t_{\text{clk}}$**, which is the *fourth unknown* that forces the requirement of *four* simultaneous satellite equations.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Position-Fix Linear System

Let the *true* user position be $\mathbf{x}_u = (x_u, y_u, z_u)^{\top}$ and the clock bias be $b = c\,\Delta t_{\text{clk}}$. Let satellite $i$ be at known position $\mathbf{x}_s^{(i)} = (x_s^{(i)}, y_s^{(i)}, z_s^{(i)})^{\top}$ with measured pseudorange $\rho_i$.

The true geometric range is:

$$
R_i = \Vert \mathbf{x}_u - \mathbf{x}_s^{(i)} \Vert = \sqrt{(x_u - x_s^{(i)})^2 + (y_u - y_s^{(i)})^2 + (z_u - z_s^{(i)})^2}
$$

Linearising about an *initial guess* $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_0 = (\hat{x}_0, \hat{y}_0, \hat{z}_0)^{\top}$ and $\hat{b}_0$:

$$
R_i \approx \hat{R}_i + \frac{\partial R_i}{\partial x}\Delta x + \frac{\partial R_i}{\partial y}\Delta y + \frac{\partial R_i}{\partial z}\Delta z
$$

The partial derivatives are the components of the **unit line-of-sight vector**:

$$
\frac{\partial R_i}{\partial x} = \frac{\hat{x}_0 - x_s^{(i)}}{\hat{R}_i} = -e_{ix}
$$

Substituting into the pseudorange equation:

$$
\rho_i = \hat{R}_i + \hat{b} - e_{ix}\Delta x - e_{iy}\Delta y - e_{iz}\Delta z + \varepsilon_{\text{mp}} + \varepsilon_{\text{NLOS}}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\Delta\rho_i = \rho_i - \hat{R}_i - \hat{b} = -e_{ix}\Delta x - e_{iy}\Delta y - e_{iz}\Delta z + \varepsilon_{\text{mp}} + \varepsilon_{\text{NLOS}}
$$

Stacking for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$ satellites:

$$
\Delta\boldsymbol{\rho} = H \Delta\mathbf{x} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}
$$

with $\Delta\mathbf{x} = (\Delta x, \Delta y, \Delta z, \Delta b)^{\top}$. The least-squares solution is:

$$
\Delta\hat{\mathbf{x}} = \left(H^{\top}H\right)^{-1}H^{\top}\Delta\boldsymbol{\rho}
$$

The covariance of the position estimate is:

$$
\text{Cov}(\Delta\hat{\mathbf{x}}) = \sigma_{\text{UERE}}^{2}\left(H^{\top}H\right)^{-1}
$$

The position-error variance along the $x$-axis, for instance, is:

$$
\sigma_x^{2} = \sigma_{\text{UERE}}^{2}\left[\left(H^{\top}H\right)^{-1}\right]_{11}
$$

Summing the variances of the three position components plus the clock variance yields the well-known GDOP identity:

$$
\text{GDOP}^{2} = \sigma_x^{2} + \sigma_y^{2} + \sigma_z^{2} + \sigma_b^{2} \big/ c^{2} = \text{trace}\left(\left(H^{\top}H\right)^{-1}\right)
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A vehicle in an urban canyon tracks **5 GNSS satellites**. Their unit line-of-sight vectors and measured pseudoranges are tabulated below. The UERE is **3 m**. Compute the GDOP and the $1\sigma$ horizontal position error.

| Satellite $i$ | $e_{ix}$ | $e_{iy}$ | $e_{iz}$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | -0.42 | -0.55 |  0.72 |
| 2 | -0.30 | -0.45 |  0.84 |
| 3 | -0.50 | -0.20 |  0.84 |
| 4 | -0.10 | -0.60 |  0.79 |
| 5 | -0.35 | -0.10 |  0.93 |

**Step 1 — Form the matrix $H$ (5 × 4):**

$$
H = \begin{bmatrix}
 0.42 &  0.55 & -0.72 & 1 \\
 0.30 &  0.45 & -0.84 & 1 \\
 0.50 &  0.20 & -0.84 & 1 \\
 0.10 &  0.60 & -0.79 & 1 \\
 0.35 &  0.10 & -0.93 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $G = H^{\top}H$:**

$$
G = H^{\top}H = \begin{bmatrix}
 0.6174 & 0.4220 & -1.8720 & 1.6700 \\
 0.4220 & 0.8350 & -1.7160 & 1.9000 \\
-1.8720 & -1.7160 &  5.7070 & -4.1100 \\
 1.6700 & 1.9000 & -4.1100 & 5.0000
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $G^{-1}$ (numeric values, in m² per (m/s)² of clock):**

$$
G^{-1} \approx \begin{bmatrix}
 7.6 & -0.4 &  1.2 & -2.1 \\
-0.4 &  6.2 &  0.8 & -1.5 \\
 1.2 &  0.8 &  3.4 & -0.6 \\
-2.1 & -1.5 & -0.6 &  1.7
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute GDOP:**

$$
\text{GDOP} = \sqrt{7.6 + 6.2 + 3.4 + 1.7} = \sqrt{18.9} \approx 4.35
$$

**Step 5 — Compute horizontal position error standard deviation** (sum of variances of $x$ and $y$):

$$
\sigma_H = \sigma_{\text{UERE}} \cdot \sqrt{G^{-1}_{11} + G^{-1}_{22}} = 3 \cdot \sqrt{7.6 + 6.2} = 3 \cdot \sqrt{13.8} \approx 3 \cdot 3.715 \approx 11.1 \text{ m}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A GDOP of 4.35 and a horizontal $1\sigma$ error of **$11.1$ m** is *typical* for a moderately open intersection. In a deeper canyon, GDOP can exceed 10 and horizontal $1\sigma$ reaches **$30{-}50$ m**, demanding sensor fusion.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sensor Comparison for Urban Canyons

| Sensor | Strength in Urban Canyon | Weakness in Urban Canyon | Update Rate | Typical Range / Drift |
|:---|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| GNSS (L1) | Wide availability, $5{-}10$ Hz | Multipath, NLOS, GDOP | $1{-}10$ Hz | $5{-}50$ m abs. error |
| GNSS (L1/L5 dual-freq) | Better iono correction | Still vulnerable to NLOS | $1{-}10$ Hz | $3{-}30$ m abs. error |
| RTK-GNSS | Centimetre-level in open sky | Cycle slips on every turn | $10{-}20$ Hz | $0.02{-}0.05$ m when locked |
| INS (MEMS) | High rate, no external dep. | Drift $\sim a_{\text{bias}}\,t^{2}$ | $100{-}1000$ Hz | $50{-}200$ m after 1 min |
| LiDAR (3-D) | $5{-}10$ cm rel. accuracy | Degrades in fog / heavy rain | $10{-}20$ Hz | $0.05$ m rel. |
| Camera (Mono/Stereo) | Rich semantics | Fails in low light / glare | $30{-}60$ Hz | $0.5{-}5$ % of distance |
| Wheel Odometry | Cheap, drift-bounded on flat | Fails on ice, slopes | $100$ Hz | $0.1{-}1$ % of distance |
| Radar | Penetrates fog/rain | Sparse, low resolution | $20$ Hz | $0.3{-}1$ m rel. |
| UWB (anchor-based) | Centimetre-level indoors | Needs infrastructure | $10{-}100$ Hz | $0.1$ m rel. to anchors |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** No single sensor solves the urban-canyon problem. Modern autonomous stacks use a **loosely- or tightly-coupled EKF (Extended Kalman Filter)** that fuses **at least three** of the above. The KTU 2024 examiner rewards answers that *justify* the choice of sensor set using a concrete error budget.

---

## 3.5 Derivation of INS-GNSS Fusion Drift Bound

A pure inertial solution drifts quadratically with time:

$$
\varepsilon_{\text{position}}(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,a_{\text{bias}}\,t^{2} + \tfrac{1}{6}\,\dot{a}_{\text{bias}}\,t^{3} + \varepsilon_{\text{velocity}}\,t
$$

where:
- $a_{\text{bias}}$ = accelerometer bias ($\sim 0.1{-}1$ **mg** for tactical grade)
- $\dot{a}_{\text{bias}}$ = bias instability / scale-factor drift
- $\varepsilon_{\text{velocity}}$ = initial velocity error

A GNSS update every $\Delta t$ seconds **resets** this drift. The residual error after one update cycle is:

$$
\varepsilon_{\text{INS}}(\Delta t) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,a_{\text{bias}}\,\Delta t^{2}
$$

For a tactical-grade IMU with $a_{\text{bias}} = 0.5$ mg $= 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 9.81 = 4.9 \times 10^{-3}$ m/s² and $\Delta t = 5$ s:

$$
\varepsilon_{\text{INS}}(5) = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 4.9 \times 10^{-3} \times 25 \approx 0.06 \text{ m} = 6 \text{ cm}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This explains the rule of thumb — *“tactical-grade INS bridges a $5{-}10$ s GNSS outage with sub-decimetre drift.”* It is precisely why autonomous vehicles in urban canyons run INS at **$200$ Hz** and accept even a few seconds of GNSS loss.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of an Urban-Canyon Navigation Stack

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional flow** of a modern urban-canyon navigation system. It shows how raw sensor streams are conditioned, NLOS-flagged, fused, and finally delivered to the planning stack.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[GNSS Receiver L1 L2 L5]:::src --> B1[Signal Conditioning & MEDLL Correlator]
    A2[MEMS IMU 200Hz]:::src --> B2[INS Mechanization]
    A3[LiDAR 3D 20Hz]:::src --> B3[Point Cloud Pre-Processing]
    A4[Camera 30Hz]:::src --> B4[Visual Odometry Frontend]
    A5[Wheel Odometry]:::src --> B5[Velocity Update]

    B1 --> C1[GNSS Position Engine Pseudorange Solver]
    C1 --> D1[NLOS Detection via 3D City Model]
    D1 --> E1[Sky Visibility Classifier LOS Borderline NLOS]

    E1 --> F1[Extended Kalman Filter Tightly Coupled]
    B2 --> F1
    B3 --> F1
    B4 --> F1
    B5 --> F1

    F1 --> G1[State Vector x y z vx vy vz roll pitch yaw bclk IMUbias]
    G1 --> H1[Pose Output at 100Hz]
    H1 --> I1[HD Map Matcher HMM]
    I1 --> J1[Final Vehicle Pose on Lane Graph]
    J1 --> K1[Motion Planner Trajectory Generator]

    classDef src fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

---

## 4.2 Geometric Schematic of the Urban Canyon Visibility Problem

The following diagram illustrates the **shadow-matching** concept — a satellite is either *visible* (LOS), *partially blocked* (borderline), or *fully occluded* (NLOS) depending on the candidate robot position.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Sky[Sky Hemisphere]
        SAT1[SAT 1 Elev 70 deg LOS]
        SAT2[SAT 2 Elev 30 deg Borderline]
        SAT3[SAT 3 Elev 10 deg NLOS]
    end

    subgraph Canyon[Urban Canyon]
        BLDG1[Building Left Height 60m]
        BLDG2[Building Right Height 80m]
        ROBOT[Robot Candidate Position x y]
    end

    SAT1 -- Direct Ray --> ROBOT
    SAT2 -- Skims Roof --> ROBOT
    SAT3 -- Reflected Ray from BLDG2 --> ROBOT
    BLDG1 -. Blocks .-> SAT3
    BLDG2 -. Reflects .-> SAT3
```

---

## 4.3 Pseudorange Error-Source Decomposition (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TRUETRUE[True Geometric Range R]:::true --> ADD
    CLOCK[Receiver Clock Bias c dt]:::err --> ADD[Measured Pseudorange rho]
    IONO[Ionospheric Delay I]:::err --> ADD
    TROPO[Tropospheric Delay T]:::err --> ADD
    MP[Multipath Error eps_mp]:::err --> ADD
    NLOS[NLOS Bias eps_NLOS]:::err --> ADD
    NOISE[Receiver Noise n]:::err --> ADD

    ADD --> SOLVER[Least-Squares Position Solver]
    SOLVER --> GDOP[GDOP Amplification]
```
