```mermaid
    GDOP --> OUT[Reported Position with sigma_P]

    classDef true fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef err fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The green block is the *desired* signal; the red blocks are *error contributors*. In a deep urban canyon, the two red blocks that grow the most are **MP** and **NLOS**. Mitigation strategies discussed in Section 2 are aimed precisely at shrinking these two contributors.

---

## 4.4 Tightly-Coupled INS/GNSS Fusion Loop (Functional View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IMU[IMU Measurements acc gyro] --> MECH[INS Mechanization Strapdown]
    MECH --> PRED[State Prediction x_k|k-1 P_k|k-1]
    GNSS[GNSS Pseudoranges plus NLOS Flags] --> KF_MEAS[Measurement Model H k]
    PRED --> KF[Extended Kalman Filter Update]
    KF_MEAS --> KF
    KF --> POST[Posteriori State x_k|k P_k|k]
    POST --> OUT[Pose Velocity Clock Bias]
    OUT --> FB[Feedback to INS Error States]
    FB --> MECH
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **feedback loop** is the difference between a *loosely-coupled* and a *tightly-coupled* architecture. In a tightly-coupled filter, the IMU error states (biases, scale factors) are *estimated* and *fed back* to the INS every epoch, keeping the INS solution accurate even between GNSS updates.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Define the term *Urban Canyon* in the context of robot navigation. Why does it pose a challenge to GNSS-based positioning?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An *urban canyon* is a street corridor flanked by tall buildings whose façades obstruct and reflect GNSS signals. It degrades GNSS positioning by **(i)** reducing the visible satellite set (poor GDOP), **(ii)** introducing multipath reflections from glass and concrete, and **(iii)** causing NLOS reception where only reflected signals are received. The combined effect is a horizontal position error that can grow from **$2{-}5$ m** in open sky to **$50{-}100$ m** in deep canyons.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for multipath/NLOS, 1 mark for quantitative degradation.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Distinguish between *multipath error* and *NLOS bias* in GNSS measurements.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Multipath error:** The direct LOS signal is present, *plus* one or more delayed reflections. The error oscillates (can be positive or negative) and can be partially suppressed by narrow-correlator or MEDLL receivers.
- **NLOS bias:** The direct LOS signal is **completely blocked**; only a reflected ray is received. The error is **always positive** (range too long) and **cannot** be removed by receiver-internal techniques — it requires a 3-D building model or external sensor consistency check.

*(Award 1.5 marks each for the correct distinction.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Students commonly *omit the numerical example* when explaining shadow matching — *always include at least one equation or numerical value* to score the “analysis” marks.
> - Students frequently confuse **GDOP** with **UERE** — *GDOP is a pure geometric multiplier, UERE is the ranging error*. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> - For 14-mark questions, you must provide **both theory and a diagram or numerical illustration**. A purely descriptive answer caps at **9–10 marks**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the linearised GNSS position-fix equation starting from the pseudorange model. Show that the position covariance is $\sigma_{\text{UERE}}^{2}\,(H^{\top}H)^{-1}$ and define GDOP in terms of the trace of this covariance.

**(b) [7 marks]** A vehicle in an urban canyon observes five satellites. The geometry matrix $H$ yields $\text{trace}\bigl((H^{\top}H)^{-1}\bigr) = 18.9$. The UERE is $3$ m. Compute **(i)** the GDOP, **(ii)** the $1\sigma$ position error, and **(iii)** the $95\%$ horizontal error assuming two-dimensional geometry and $\sigma_x = \sigma_y$.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation [7 marks]**
- **Step 1 (1 mark):** Write the pseudorange model with clock bias, ionospheric, tropospheric, multipath, NLOS, and noise terms.
- **Step 2 (2 marks):** Linearise $\sqrt{(x_u - x_s^{(i)})^2 + (y_u - y_s^{(i)})^2 + (z_u - z_s^{(i)})^2}$ about an initial guess, showing that the partial derivatives yield the unit line-of-sight components $(-e_{ix}, -e_{iy}, -e_{iz})$.
- **Step 3 (2 marks):** Form the matrix equation $\Delta\boldsymbol{\rho} = H\Delta\mathbf{x} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$ with the augmented state including clock bias.
- **Step 4 (1 mark):** Apply least squares to obtain $\Delta\hat{\mathbf{x}} = (H^{\top}H)^{-1}H^{\top}\Delta\boldsymbol{\rho}$ and the covariance $\text{Cov}(\Delta\hat{\mathbf{x}}) = \sigma_{\text{UERE}}^{2}\,(H^{\top}H)^{-1}$.
- **Step 5 (1 mark):** Define GDOP $= \sqrt{\text{trace}\bigl((H^{\top}H)^{-1}\bigr)}$.

**(b) Numerical [7 marks]**
- **(i) GDOP (2 marks):** $\text{GDOP} = \sqrt{18.9} = 4.35$
- **(ii) $1\sigma$ 3-D position error (2 marks):** $\sigma_P = \text{GDOP} \cdot \sigma_{\text{UERE}} = 4.35 \times 3 = 13.05$ m
- **(iii) $95\%$ horizontal error (3 marks):** With $\sigma_x = \sigma_y$ and using the two-dimensional 95% radius of $\approx 2.45\,\sigma$ (Rayleigh distribution, $2D$ Gaussian):

$$
\sigma_H = \sigma_{\text{UERE}}\sqrt{G^{-1}_{11} + G^{-1}_{22}} = 3 \cdot \sqrt{13.8} \approx 11.1 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\text{Error}_{95\%} = 2.45 \cdot \sigma_H \approx 2.45 \times 11.1 \approx 27.2 \text{ m}
$$

This 27 m uncertainty explains why **lane-keep assist cannot rely on GNSS alone** in a canyon.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the *shadow-matching* technique for urban-canyon positioning. State clearly **(i)** what information is required, **(ii)** the algorithm's main steps, and **(iii)** its limitations.

**(b) [7 marks]** With the aid of a block diagram, describe a *tightly-coupled INS/GNSS/LiDAR* fusion architecture for an autonomous car in a deep urban canyon. Justify the choice of fusion level and state how the system handles a complete $10$ s GNSS outage.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Shadow Matching [7 marks]**
- **(i) Required information (2 marks):** A **3-D building model** (e.g., CityGML or OSM-with-height), the receiver's *approximate* position (from a coarse GNSS fix or dead reckoning), and the list of *measured* satellite pseudoranges / SNR values.
- **(ii) Algorithm steps (3 marks):**
  1. Generate a grid of candidate positions around the approximate fix.
  2. For each candidate and each satellite, ray-trace from the candidate to the satellite and test for intersection with building façades (use simple line-vs-AABB test).
  3. Classify each satellite as *visible* / *occluded*.
  4. Compute a likelihood score: candidates whose predicted visibility set matches the *measured* SNR ranking receive higher scores.
  5. Take the weighted centroid (or the maximum-likelihood candidate) as the new position estimate.
- **(iii) Limitations (2 marks):** Requires an up-to-date 3-D model (out-of-date buildings cause errors); computationally heavy for grid sizes > $10 \times 10$ m; degrades near open intersections where many candidates have identical visibility sets.

**(b) Tightly-coupled fusion [7 marks]**
- **Block diagram (3 marks):** Show INS mechanization feeding an EKF predictor, GNSS pseudoranges (raw, before position fix) feeding the EKF update, LiDAR scan-matching providing a third measurement, and the posterior feeding back to the INS error states. *(Refer to Section 4.4 Mermaid diagram.)*
- **Justification of tight coupling (2 marks):** Tight coupling is essential because in a deep canyon fewer than four satellites are visible, so a *loosely-coupled* filter (which needs a full position fix) would fail. A tightly-coupled filter uses **raw pseudoranges**, so even **two or three** satellites plus INS can produce an update.
- **Handling a 10 s outage (2 marks):** During the outage, the EKF **propagates** using INS only. The accumulated position error is:

$$
\varepsilon \approx \tfrac{1}{2}\,a_{\text{bias}}\,\Delta t^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 4.9 \times 10^{-3} \times 100 = 0.245 \text{ m}
$$

  which is well within lane-keeping tolerance. When GNSS returns, the filter **re-acquires lock** and the INS errors are immediately bounded by the GNSS update. The LiDAR-to-map matching runs continuously and provides an independent absolute reference that prevents the INS drift from accumulating unobserved.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Urban Canyon Navigation**

- **Urban Canyon:** Street corridor with tall buildings that block and reflect GNSS signals.
- **Two dominant urban errors:** **Multipath** (oscillates, partial LOS) and **NLOS bias** (always positive, no LOS).
- **Pseudorange equation:** $\rho = R + c\,\Delta t + I + T + \varepsilon_{\text{mp}} + \varepsilon_{\text{NLOS}} + n$. *Always* write this in full for KTU derivations.
- **Position-error formula:** $\sigma_P = \text{GDOP} \cdot \sigma_{\text{UERE}}$ — GDOP is a *pure geometry multiplier*.
- **GNSS carrier frequencies to memorise:** L1 $= 1575.42$ **MHz**, L2 $= 1227.60$ **MHz**, L5 $= 1176.45$ **MHz**.
- **DGNSS baseline limit:** $< 20$ km for sub-metre accuracy.
- **RTK accuracy:** $1{-}2$ cm in open sky, but **fails** in deep urban canyons due to cycle slips.
- **Shadow matching:** Requires a **3-D city model**; rejects candidates whose predicted sky visibility does not match observed SNR.
- **Tightly-coupled vs loosely-coupled:** Tightly-coupled uses *raw pseudoranges* (works with $< 4$ satellites); loosely-coupled needs a full position fix (fails with $< 4$ satellites).
- **INS drift law:** $\varepsilon \approx \tfrac{1}{2}\,a_{\text{bias}}\,t^{2}$ — bridges $5{-}30$ s GNSS outages.
- **Sensor stack for autonomous cars in canyons:** **GNSS + INS + LiDAR + HD-Map + Vision** (at minimum).
- **Why fusion is non-negotiable:** No single sensor achieves $< 1$ m accuracy in a deep canyon — the **safety argument** rests on multi-sensor redundancy.
- **Map-matching HMM:** Snaps noisy GNSS fixes to the lane-graph using a Hidden Markov Model — a *cheap* and *high-impact* software mitigation.
- **Future trend (write once in 14-mark answers):** **PPP-RTK** + **5G NR positioning** + **3-D model-aided GNSS** is the emerging urban-canyon stack.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Map Matching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT735 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Map Matching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Map Matching (MM)** is the computational process of aligning a sequence of observed, noisy, and discretely-sampled positioning measurements (typically obtained from Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSS), Inertial Measurement Units (IMU), LiDAR, or odometry sensors) with the corresponding digital road network or trajectory segments represented in an Electronic Map (E-Map) database, in order to infer the most probable true position of the vehicle/robot on the map.

In the context of **Robot Navigation (PERUT735)**, map matching is considered a critical **perception-to-planning bridge module**. It resolves the spatial discrepancy between the raw continuous trajectory (in the sensor's local coordinate frame) and the discrete topological road network (in the map's coordinate frame). The output is a *snapped* trajectory where every GPS fix is projected onto the most plausible road link.

### Key Terminology (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **GNSS Fix** | A single point coordinate (latitude, longitude) reported by a satellite receiver. |
| **Road Link (Edge)** | A vector segment between two nodes in a road network graph. |
| **Candidate Segment** | A road link considered as a possible match for a given GPS sample. |
| **Snapped Position** | The corrected (projected) point on a road segment after map matching. |
| **E-Map (ADAS Map)** | An Electronic Map database with topological and geometric road data. |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine you are blindfolded and walking through a city with a paper map.** Every few seconds, a friend taps your shoulder and tells you your *approximate* GPS coordinate — but the coordinate is **noisy** (off by 20–50 meters) and lands *inside* buildings, on parks, or in rivers. You cannot walk through buildings. So, intuitively, you *snap* your reported position to the *nearest reachable road*. **That snapping logic is map matching.**

**Intuitive Real-World Example (Self-Driving Cars):**
- A Waymo robotaxi receives GPS fixes every **100 ms** at an accuracy of **±3 to ±5 m** in urban canyons.
- Due to multipath reflection off skyscrapers, a fix might appear **inside a building**.
- The map matching algorithm looks at the road graph (nodes + edges) and decides: *"The robot is actually 92% likely on Elm Street, not Maple Avenue."*
- It outputs the corrected position: the perpendicular projection of the GPS point onto Elm Street's centerline.

This correction is **essential** because downstream modules (lane-keeping, traffic rule compliance, path planning) operate on the **map frame**, not the raw sensor frame.

---

## 1.3 Why Map Matching is Non-Trivial

Three challenges make map matching a hard problem:

1. **Sensor Noise:** GNSS accuracy varies from **±1 m** (RTK-GPS) to **±15 m** (standard GPS) depending on environment.
2. **Map Density:** Urban downtowns have road density of **>200 km/km²**, where parallel streets are only **10–20 m** apart.
3. **Temporal Ambiguity:** A single isolated GPS point cannot be matched uniquely — it requires a **sequence** of points (temporal context).

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Pure geometric proximity is insufficient. The algorithm must consider **historical trajectory continuity** and **road connectivity** to resolve ambiguity.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Map Matching Algorithms

Map matching algorithms are formally classified into four families, ordered by increasing sophistication:

### 2.1.1 Geometric / Topological Methods

These are the simplest and fastest. They rely purely on the geometric relationship between the GPS point and nearby road segments.

**Sub-types:**

**(a) Point-to-Point Matching**
- Snaps each GPS fix to the **nearest node** (intersection) in the road network.
- **Distance formula:** Euclidean distance between point P and node Nᵢ.
- **Limitation:** Ignores road geometry between intersections; produces jagged trajectories on curves.

**(b) Point-to-Curve Matching**
- Projects each GPS fix onto the **nearest point on a road segment** (curve).
- The projection point $P'$ is found by minimizing perpendicular distance to the segment's line equation.
- **Limitation:** Susceptible to wrong-segment errors on parallel roads.

**(c) Curve-to-Curve Matching**
- Compares the **shape (curvature signature)** of the GPS trajectory fragment to the shape of candidate road segments.
- Uses **Fréchet distance** as the similarity metric.
- **Advantage:** Robust to parallel road confusion.

### 2.1.2 Topological Methods

- Uses the **connectivity graph** of the road network.
- A path is considered valid only if the candidate segments form a **connected sequence** (no teleportation between disconnected roads).
- Often implemented via **Dijkstra's shortest path** on the road graph using heading difference as cost.

### 2.1.3 Probabilistic / Statistical Methods

- Models each GPS measurement with a **probability distribution** (typically a 2D Gaussian centered at the reported fix with covariance $\Sigma$).
- The probability of the true position $x$ on segment $s$ given observation $z$ is:

$$P(x \mid z) = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{\vert \Sigma \vert}} \exp\left( -\frac{1}{2}(z - x)^T \Sigma^{-1} (z - x) \right)$$

- The matched segment is the one maximizing the **likelihood** integrated along the segment.

### 2.1.4 Advanced Methods (Kalman Filter, HMM, Particle Filter)

- Treat map matching as a **state estimation problem**.
- The hidden state is the true road position; the observation is the noisy GPS fix.
- **Hidden Markov Model (HMM)** is the gold standard — it integrates emission probabilities (GPS-to-road distance) and transition probabilities (connectivity + heading).

## 2.2 The Hidden Markov Model (HMM) Framework

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The HMM-based map matching algorithm (Newson & Krumm, 2009) is the de-facto industry standard** and is the most likely topic for a 14-mark KTU question.

### Formal HMM Specification

- **States (hidden):** Candidate road segments $r_i$ from a set $R$ within a search radius of each GPS fix.
- **Observations:** GPS fixes $z_t = (lat_t, lon_t)$ at time $t = 1, 2, \ldots, T$.
- **Emission Probability $P(z_t \mid r_i)$:** Gaussian likelihood of observing $z_t$ given the true position is on segment $r_i$.

$$P(z_t \mid r_i) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi} \sigma} \exp\left( -\frac{d(z_t, r_i)^2}{2\sigma^2} \right)$$

where $d(z_t, r_i)$ is the **perpendicular distance** from GPS fix to segment $r_i$, and $\sigma$ is the GPS noise standard deviation (typically **10–50 m**).

- **Transition Probability $P(r_j \mid r_i)$:** Probability of transitioning from segment $r_i$ at time $t-1$ to segment $r_j$ at time $t$.

$$P(r_j \mid r_i) = \frac{1}{\beta} \exp\left( -\frac{\Delta_{ij}}{\beta} \right)$$

where:
  - $\Delta_{ij}$ is the **great-circle distance gap** between the projections of consecutive GPS fixes on segments $r_i$ and $r_j$, minus the **great-circle distance between the GPS fixes themselves**.
  - $\beta$ is a scaling parameter (typically set via empirical tuning, e.g., **β = 10–50 m**).

- **Decoding:** The Viterbi algorithm finds the most-likely sequence of states:

$$\hat{r}_{1:T} = \arg\max_{r_{1:T}} \prod_{t=1}^{T} P(z_t \mid r_t) \cdot P(r_t \mid r_{t-1})$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $d_{perp}(P, AB) = \frac{\vert (B-A) \times (A-P) \vert}{\vert B-A \vert}$ | Perpendicular distance from point P to segment AB | Meters (m) |
| 2 | $d_{g}(P_1, P_2) = 2R \arcsin\left(\sqrt{\sin^2(\tfrac{\Delta\phi}{2}) + \cos\phi_1 \cos\phi_2 \sin^2(\tfrac{\Delta\lambda}{2})}\right)$ | Haversine great-circle distance | R = 6,371,000 m (Earth radius) |
| 3 | $P(z \mid r) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\sigma} \exp\left(-\frac{d^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ | Emission probability (Gaussian) | $\sigma$ in meters |
| 4 | $P(r_j \mid r_i) = \frac{1}{\beta}\exp\left(-\frac{\Delta_{ij}}{\beta}\right)$ | Transition probability (exponential) | $\Delta_{ij} \geq 0$ in meters |
| 5 | $\hat{r}_{1:T} = \arg\max \prod_t P(z_t \vert r_t) P(r_t \vert r_{t-1})$ | Viterbi-decoded matched sequence | Dimensionless probability |
| 6 | $\text{FH}(A, B) = \inf_{\alpha, \beta} \max_{t \in [0,1]} d(A(\alpha(t)), B(\beta(t)))$ | Fréchet distance (curve similarity) | Meters |
| 7 | $H = \arctan 2(\Delta E, \Delta N)$ | Heading (azimuth) from consecutive GPS fixes | Radians or degrees |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always specify units in the final answer. The single most common mark-loss reason in numerical map-matching problems is missing units.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | Lane-level localization, intersection behavior prediction. |
| **Fleet Management** | Real-time vehicle tracking on logistics dashboards. |
| **ADAS** | Curve speed warning, road curvature estimation ahead. |
| **V2X Communication** | Providing map-relative coordinates for cooperative perception. |
| **Urban Mobility Research** | Origin-Destination matrix estimation from probe vehicles. |
| **Robotics (last-mile delivery)** | Correcting odometry drift in sidewalk robots. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Perpendicular Distance from a Point to a Line Segment

> **Problem Setup:** Given a road segment with endpoints $A = (x_1, y_1)$ and $B = (x_2, y_2)$, and a GPS fix $P = (x_0, y_0)$, find the **shortest Euclidean distance** from $P$ to the segment $AB$.

**Step 1 — Parametric Equation of the Line:**
Any point on the line passing through $A$ and $B$ can be written as:

$$L(\lambda) = A + \lambda(B - A), \quad \lambda \in \mathbb{R}$$

**Step 2 — Foot of Perpendicular:**
The foot of the perpendicular from $P$ onto the *infinite line* occurs at the value of $\lambda$ that minimizes the squared distance $\vert L(\lambda) - P \vert^2$. Taking the derivative and setting it to zero:

$$\frac{d}{d\lambda}\vert L(\lambda) - P \vert^2 = 0$$

Expanding:

$$\frac{d}{d\lambda}\left( (A - P) + \lambda(B - A) \right) \cdot \left( (A - P) + \lambda(B - A) \right) = 0$$

This gives:

$$2(B - A) \cdot \left( (A - P) + \lambda(B - A) \right) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\lambda$:**

$$\lambda = \frac{(P - A) \cdot (B - A)}{\vert B - A \vert^2}$$

**Step 4 — Clamp $\lambda$ to [0, 1]:**
If $\lambda < 0$, closest point is $A$. If $\lambda > 1$, closest point is $B$. Otherwise, the closest point is $L(\lambda^*)$.

**Step 5 — Compute Perpendicular Distance:**

If $0 \leq \lambda \leq 1$:

$$d_{perp} = \frac{\vert (B - A) \times (P - A) \vert}{\vert B - A \vert}$$

In 2D, the cross product magnitude reduces to:

$$d_{perp} = \frac{\vert (x_2 - x_1)(y_1 - y_0) - (x_1 - x_0)(y_2 - y_1) \vert}{\sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}}$$

**Step 6 — Sanity Check:**
- If $P$ lies exactly on the line, numerator = 0 ⇒ $d_{perp} = 0$ ✓
- Distance scales linearly with lateral offset from the line ✓
- Units are preserved as meters if coordinates are in a local ENU frame ✓

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Emission Probability

> **Given:** GPS noise standard deviation $\sigma = 20$ m. A GPS fix is reported at perpendicular distance $d = 8$ m from candidate road segment $r$.

**Step 1 — Substitute into emission formula:**

$$P(z \mid r) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi} \cdot 20} \exp\left( -\frac{8^2}{2 \cdot 20^2} \right)$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the prefactor:**

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi} \cdot 20} = \frac{1}{2.5066 \cdot 20} = 0.01994$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the exponent:**

$$-\frac{64}{800} = -0.08$$

**Step 4 — Compute the exponential:**

$$\exp(-0.08) = 0.9231$$

**Step 5 — Multiply:**

$$P(z \mid r) = 0.01994 \times 0.9231 = 0.01841 \; \text{(m}^{-1}\text{)}$$

**Interpretation:** This is the likelihood density of the observation given the true position is on segment $r$. Higher $P$ ⇒ stronger match.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Transition Probability

> **Given:** Two consecutive candidate segments $r_i$ and $r_j$. The great-circle distance between the two GPS fixes is **120 m**. The summed projection distance along $r_i$ then $r_j$ is **135 m**. Take $\beta = 25$ m.

**Step 1 — Compute the distance gap:**

$$\Delta_{ij} = \vert 135 - 120 \vert = 15 \text{ m}$$

This gap represents the "extra distance" the robot would have to travel if it teleported between segments — a measure of physical plausibility.

**Step 2 — Apply transition formula:**

$$P(r_j \mid r_i) = \frac{1}{25} \exp\left( -\frac{15}{25} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate:**

$$P(r_j \mid r_i) = 0.04 \times \exp(-0.6) = 0.04 \times 0.5488 = 0.02195 \; \text{(m}^{-1}\text{)}$$

**Interpretation:** A small gap (well-connected segments) yields a higher transition probability.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Algorithm | Complexity | Memory | Real-Time? | Parallel-Road Robustness | Heading Sensitivity |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Point-to-Point | $O(N)$ | Low | Yes | Poor | None |
| Point-to-Curve | $O(N \log N)$ with R-tree | Low | Yes | Poor | None |
| Curve-to-Curve (Fréchet) | $O(NM \log(NM))$ | Medium | No | Good | Low |
| Topological (Dijkstra) | $O(E \log V)$ | Medium | Yes | Moderate | High |
| HMM (Viterbi) | $O(T \cdot \vert R \vert^2)$ | High | Yes (with pruning) | Excellent | Implicit |
| Kalman Filter | $O(T \cdot n^2)$, $n$=state dim | Low | Yes | Moderate | Implicit |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Map Matching Pipeline

The following Mermaid block illustrates the **end-to-end data flow** of a typical real-time map matching system as deployed in an autonomous vehicle or fleet tracker.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[GNSS Receiver] --> B[Raw GPS Fix Sequence]
    C[IMU Odometry] --> D[Dead Reckoning Drift Estimate]
    E[LiDAR / Camera] --> F[Lane Marking Detection]
    B --> G[Preprocessing Filter]
    D --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Kalman Filtered Position]
    H --> I[Candidate Segment Search R-tree]
    J[E-Map Database] --> I
    I --> K[HMM Emission Probabilities]
    H --> L[Heading Extraction]
    L --> M[HMM Transition Probabilities]
    K --> N[Viterbi Decoder]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Matched Trajectory on Map]
    O --> P[Path Planning Module]
    O --> Q[Vehicle Dynamics Controller]
    O --> R[Fleet Dashboard Telemetry]
```

### Block-Level Explanation

| Node | Function |
|---|---|
| **A, C, E** | Heterogeneous sensor inputs (satellite, inertial, visual). |
| **G** | Sensor fusion preprocessing — outlier rejection, Kalman smoothing. |
| **J** | E-Map database (OpenStreetMap, HERE, RoadDNA). |
| **I** | Search for road segments within a radius (typically 50–100 m) of the GPS fix using a spatial index (R-tree / KD-tree). |
| **K** | Compute Gaussian likelihood for each candidate. |
| **L, M** | Heading difference used to penalize unlikely segment-to-segment transitions. |
| **N** | Viterbi algorithm finds the globally optimal state sequence. |
| **O** | Output: snapped polyline, ready for downstream consumption. |

## 4.2 HMM Trellis Diagram (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1["t=1"] --> S1A["Segment A"]
    T1 --> S1B["Segment B"]
    T1 --> S1C["Segment C"]
    T2["t=2"] --> S2X["Segment X"]
    T2 --> S2Y["Segment Y"]
    T2 --> S2Z["Segment Z"]
    T3["t=3"] --> S3P["Segment P"]
    T3 --> S3Q["Segment Q"]
    S1A --> S2X
    S1A --> S2Y
    S1B --> S2X
    S1B --> S2Y
    S1B --> S2Z
    S1C --> S2Z
    S2X --> S3P
    S2X --> S3Q
    S2Y --> S3P
    S2Z --> S3Q
```

In the trellis above, **rows represent time steps** $t = 1, 2, 3$ and **columns represent candidate road segments** active at each step. The Viterbi algorithm traverses this trellis in $O(T \cdot \vert R \vert^2)$ time, retaining only the highest-scoring path to each node.

## 4.3 Geometric Intuition: Perpendicular Projection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["GPS Fix P (x0, y0)"] -.->|"d_perp"| M["Projection P' on segment AB"]
    A["Endpoint A (x1, y1)"] --> B["Endpoint B (x2, y2)"]
    M --- AB["Segment AB"]
```

The perpendicular foot $P'$ is the optimal snap point when the algorithm uses a **point-to-curve** geometric criterion. The distance $d_{perp}$ feeds directly into the Gaussian emission probability in the HMM.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper Style]**

### Q1. Define Map Matching. List any two challenges encountered in the map matching process. *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Map Matching** is the process of aligning a sequence of noisy GPS observations with the digital road network to determine the most probable road segment on which the vehicle/robot is traveling.

**Two key challenges:**
1. **GPS noise and multipath errors**, especially in urban canyons where signals reflect off buildings, causing position errors of **10–50 m**.
2. **High road density** in urban areas — parallel roads separated by only 10–20 m can cause frequent mis-matches when relying on pure geometric proximity.

*(Any two valid challenges accepted: missing data, sparse sampling, tunnels, GPS outages, map inaccuracy.)*

---

### Q2. What is the difference between Point-to-Curve and Curve-to-Curve map matching? *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Point-to-Curve | Curve-to-Curve |
|---|---|---|
| **Input** | A single GPS fix + a road segment. | A sequence (curve) of GPS fixes + a candidate road curve. |
| **Metric** | Perpendicular distance. | Fréchet distance (shape similarity). |
| **Robustness to noise** | Moderate. | High — uses temporal shape context. |
| **Computational cost** | Low. | High. |
| **Use case** | Real-time, high-frequency matching. | Offline, post-processing of trajectories. |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Pattern (KTU ESE 2024 Scheme):** Answer **ONE** of the following.

---

### QUESTION A *(14 marks)*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Explain the geometric approach to map matching. Discuss point-to-point, point-to-curve, and curve-to-curve matching with their merits and demerits. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the Hidden Markov Model (HMM) based map matching algorithm in detail, including the formulation of emission and transition probabilities. *(7 marks)*

#### MODEL SOLUTION

**(a) Geometric Approach to Map Matching:**

The geometric approach uses **only spatial coordinates** of GPS fixes and road segments, ignoring temporal continuity.

**Point-to-Point Matching:**
- Each GPS fix $P_i$ is snapped to the **nearest node** (intersection) in the road graph.
- **Mathematical formulation:** Find node $N^*$ minimizing $d(P_i, N_k)$ for all nodes $N_k$.
- **Merit:** Computationally trivial, $O(N)$ per fix.
- **Demerit:** Ignores road geometry; on long straight roads, the matched trajectory "jumps" between intersections.

**Point-to-Curve Matching:**
- Each GPS fix is projected onto the **nearest point on a road segment** (the perpendicular foot).
- **Mathematical formulation:**

$$P' = A + \lambda^* (B - A), \quad \lambda^* = \text{clamp}\left( \frac{(P - A) \cdot (B - A)}{\vert B-A \vert^2}, 0, 1 \right)$$

- The matched distance is $d_{perp}$.
- **Merit:** Produces smooth trajectories on curves.
- **Demerit:** Cannot distinguish between parallel roads if both are within the GPS error circle.

**Curve-to-Curve Matching:**
- Compares the **shape** of a polyline of consecutive GPS fixes to the shape of candidate road segments.
- Uses the **Fréchet distance** as a similarity metric — informally, "the leash distance" between two curves walked simultaneously by a person and a dog.
- **Merit:** Highly robust against parallel road confusion.
- **Demerit:** Computationally expensive, $O(NM)$; not suitable for real-time applications.

**(b) HMM-Based Map Matching:**

The HMM formalization treats the **true road position** as a hidden Markov chain and the **GPS observations** as emissions.

**State Space:** Candidate road segments $R = \{r_1, r_2, \ldots, r_K\}$ within a search radius (e.g., 100 m) of each GPS fix.

**Observation:** GPS fix $z_t = (\phi_t, \lambda_t)$ at time $t$.

**Emission Probability** — likelihood of observing $z_t$ if the true position is on segment $r_i$:

$$P(z_t \mid r_i) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi} \sigma} \exp\left( -\frac{d(z_t, r_i)^2}{2\sigma^2} \right)$$

where $d(z_t, r_i)$ is the perpendicular distance from the fix to the segment, and $\sigma$ is the GPS noise standard deviation.

**Transition Probability** — likelihood of moving from segment $r_i$ at time $t-1$ to segment $r_j$ at time $t$:

$$P(r_j \mid r_i) = \frac{1}{\beta} \exp\left( -\frac{\vert \text{path length}(r_i, r_j) - \text{gps distance}(z_{t-1}, z_t) \vert}{\beta} \right)$$

The numerator measures the "route continuity gap" — if there is a large gap, transition is penalized. The parameter $\beta$ is a smoothing constant.

**Decoding via Viterbi Algorithm:**
The Viterbi recursion finds the maximum-likelihood state sequence:

$$V_t(r_j) = P(z_t \mid r_j) \cdot \max_{r_i} \left[ V_{t-1}(r_i) \cdot P(r_j \mid r_i) \right]$$

The final decoded path is recovered by backtracking the argmax pointers.

**Practical Tuning:** $\sigma$ is typically 10–50 m based on receiver quality. $\beta$ is empirically tuned (commonly 20–50 m) using ground-truthed trajectories.

---

### QUESTION B *(14 marks)*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the perpendicular distance computation from a GPS point to a road segment. Derive the formula for the same. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare and contrast geometric, topological, and probabilistic map matching algorithms. Discuss their suitability for real-time autonomous vehicle applications. *(7 marks)*

#### MODEL SOLUTION

**(a) Perpendicular Distance Computation:**

*(Derivation as given in Section 3.1 — full step-by-step expansion required.)*

The foot of the perpendicular from GPS point $P = (x_0, y_0)$ to segment $AB$ is found by solving:

$$\lambda^* = \frac{(x_0 - x_1)(x_2 - x_1) + (y_0 - y_1)(y_2 - y_1)}{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}$$

Clamping logic:
- If $\lambda^* < 0$: closest point is $A$.
- If $\lambda^* > 1$: closest point is $B$.
- Else: closest point is on the segment, at $P' = A + \lambda^*(B-A)$.

Final perpendicular distance formula:

$$d_{perp} = \frac{\vert (x_2 - x_1)(y_1 - y_0) - (x_1 - x_0)(y_2 - y_1) \vert}{\sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}}$$

*(A diagram with labeled coordinates must be drawn — see Section 4.3 for the schematic.)*

**(b) Comparison of Map Matching Algorithm Families:**

| Feature | Geometric | Topological | Probabilistic (HMM) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Information Used** | Position only. | Position + connectivity. | Position + connectivity + uncertainty. |
| **Noise Handling** | Poor. | Moderate. | Excellent. |
| **Parallel Road Issue** | Fails often. | Sometimes fails. | Resolved via Viterbi. |
| **Computational Cost** | Lowest. | Medium. | High. |
| **Real-Time Suitability** | Yes (embedded). | Yes. | Yes (with Viterbi pruning). |
| **Output Confidence** | None. | None. | Posterior probabilities. |

**Suitability for Autonomous Vehicles:**

For a Level-4/5 autonomous vehicle operating in dense urban environments, the **HMM-based approach is the de-facto choice** because:
1. It produces a **confidence-weighted** output, essential for safety validation.
2. It explicitly models **GPS uncertainty** (σ), which is highly variable in urban canyons.
3. The Viterbi algorithm runs in **real time** with candidate pruning (radius ~50 m, top-K candidates per fix).
4. It integrates **heading information** implicitly via the transition probability, which captures vehicle dynamics.

Geometric and topological methods serve as **fast pre-filters** in a two-stage pipeline — geometric for initial candidate selection, HMM for final disambiguation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the clamp operation** in perpendicular distance derivation → loses 1–2 marks. Always check if the projection lies **on the segment** (0 ≤ λ ≤ 1).
> 2. **Confusing emission and transition probabilities** in HMM. Emission = "how likely is this GPS fix on this segment?" Transition = "how likely is moving from segment A to segment B?"
> 3. **Not specifying units** in numerical answers. GPS noise σ is in meters; probability is dimensionless. The prefactor 1/(√2π σ) has units of m⁻¹.
> 4. **Skipping the log-transform** in Viterbi. Working in log-probability space prevents numerical underflow. KTU may deduct marks for not mentioning this.
> 5. **Ignoring the great-circle correction.** Earth is curved; using planar Euclidean distance at large scales introduces error. Use Haversine for inter-fix distances.
> 6. **Failing to mention heading/connectivity as a tie-breaker.** Pure distance-based matching is naive; KTU expects awareness of the multi-criteria nature.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Map Matching**

- **Definition:** Map matching = aligning noisy GPS sequence to digital road network. **Output:** snapped trajectory on map frame.
- **Four algorithm families:** Geometric, Topological, Probabilistic, Advanced (Kalman/HMM/Particle).
- **Three geometric sub-types:** Point-to-Point (node snap), Point-to-Curve (perpendicular projection), Curve-to-Curve (Fréchet shape match).
- **Perpendicular distance formula** is the workhorse — derive from vector projection with clamp [0,1] on the parameter $\lambda$.
- **HMM is the industry standard.** Know emission probability (Gaussian on perpendicular distance) and transition probability (exponential on route gap).
- **Viterbi algorithm** is the decoder — runs in $O(T \cdot K^2)$ where $K$ is the number of candidate segments per fix.
- **Real-world parameters:** GPS noise σ ≈ 10–50 m; transition scaling β ≈ 20–50 m; search radius ≈ 50–100 m.
- **Key engineering challenges:** urban canyon multipath, parallel road confusion, GPS outages in tunnels, map versioning mismatches.
- **Real-time considerations:** candidate pruning (R-tree spatial index), log-probability computation, sliding window of 5–20 fixes.
- **Applications:** Autonomous driving, ADAS, fleet tracking, V2X, urban mobility analytics, last-mile delivery robotics.
- **Earth geometry:** Use **Haversine** formula for inter-fix distances; Euclidean is acceptable only for small-scale local frames (ENU).
- **Failure modes to remember:** Cold-start ambiguity, off-road GPS fixes (e.g., parking lots), sparse sampling at high speed.
- **Famous reference algorithms:** Newson-Krumm HMM (2009), Velodyne LiDAR-augmented MM, OSRM matchers, GraphHopper.

> **End of Module 4 — Map Matching Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dead Reckoning - Inertial Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT735 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Dead Reckoning - Inertial Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Dead Reckoning?

**Dead Reckoning (DR)** is a classical navigation technique in which the current position of a mobile robot is estimated by **incrementally updating** a previously known position using measured (or estimated) velocity, heading, and elapsed time. The word "dead" historically derives from "deduced" — hence, **deduced reckoning**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PERUT735 – Module 4):**
> Dead reckoning is the process of estimating a robot's current pose $(x, y, \theta)$ relative to a known starting pose by integrating the robot's motion (velocity and angular rate) over time, using proprioceptive sensors such as wheel encoders and inertial measurement units (IMUs).

**Formal Pose Update Rule:**

$$x_{k+1} = x_k + v_k \cos(\theta_k)\,\Delta t$$

$$y_{k+1} = y_k + v_k \sin(\theta_k)\,\Delta t$$

$$\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega_k\,\Delta t$$

where $v_k$ is the linear velocity, $\omega_k$ is the angular velocity, and $\Delta t$ is the sampling interval.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Dead reckoning is **open-loop**: errors are **never corrected** unless fused with absolute sensors (GPS, LiDAR, cameras). Drift grows **unbounded** with time.

---

### 1.2 What are Inertial Sensors?

**Inertial sensors** are proprioceptive (internal) sensors that measure a vehicle's motion based on **Newtonian inertial principles** — without requiring any external reference (no landmarks, no satellites, no beacons).

The two fundamental inertial sensor types are:

| Sensor Type | Measures | Output Signal |
|---|---|---|
| **Accelerometer** | Linear acceleration (specific force) | $a \, [m/s^2]$ |
| **Gyroscope** | Angular velocity (rate of rotation) | $\omega \, [rad/s]$ |

> [!TIP]
> A combination of 3-axis accelerometers + 3-axis gyroscopes (± optional magnetometer) forms an **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)**, the workhorse of robot dead reckoning.

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "Walking Blindfolded"

Imagine you are **blindfolded** in an unfamiliar room, but you have a known starting position (the doorway). You are given:

- A **pedometer** that counts your steps (analogous to a wheel encoder / accelerometer integration).
- A **compass** that gives your facing direction (analogous to a gyroscope / magnetometer).

If you walk 10 steps North, then 5 steps East, you can **deduce** your new position on a mental map. This is dead reckoning!

**The catch:** if your step length estimate is off by even 5%, after 100 steps your position error is enormous. **Errors accumulate** — this is the central weakness of inertial dead reckoning.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Metrics to Remember:**
> - Standard gravity: $g = 9.80665 \, m/s^2$
> - Inertial sensors typically sample at **50 Hz – 1 kHz**
> - MEMS-grade IMUs drift at $\sim 1°$–$10°$ per minute
> - Tactical-grade IMUs drift at $\sim 0.01°$ per hour

---

### 1.4 Why Dead Reckoning + Inertial Sensors?

In mobile robotics, dead reckoning using inertial sensors is **indispensable** because:

1. **Self-contained** — no external infrastructure required (unlike GPS, beacons).
2. **High bandwidth** — kHz sampling for fast maneuvers.
3. **Short-term accuracy** — excellent over short horizons (sub-second).
4. **All-weather** — works through dust, smoke, darkness.
5. **Complementary to absolute sensors** — fused with GPS/LiDAR in EKF/particle filters.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Dead Reckoning Pipeline

The dead reckoning process follows a strict **measurement → integration → pose update** flow:

**Step 1 — Acquire inertial measurements**
- Accelerometer triad: $\mathbf{a} = [a_x, a_y, a_z]^T$
- Gyroscope triad: $\boldsymbol{\omega} = [\omega_x, \omega_y, \omega_z]^T$

**Step 2 — Remove gravity bias from accelerometer**
- The accelerometer cannot distinguish between gravity and true acceleration.
- In a static case, the accelerometer reads $1g$ upward.
- True acceleration: $\mathbf{a}_{\text{true}} = \mathbf{a}_{\text{measured}} - \mathbf{R}^T \mathbf{g}$
- where $\mathbf{R}$ is the rotation matrix from body to world frame.

**Step 3 — Integrate angular velocity to get orientation**
- Orientation is updated via rotation matrix: $\dot{\mathbf{R}} = \mathbf{R}\,\boldsymbol{\omega}^\wedge$
- Or using Euler/quaternion integration.

**Step 4 — Integrate linear acceleration to get velocity**
- $\mathbf{v}(t) = \mathbf{v}(0) + \int_0^t \mathbf{a}(\tau)\,d\tau$

**Step 5 — Integrate velocity to get position**
- $\mathbf{p}(t) = \mathbf{p}(0) + \int_0^t \mathbf{v}(\tau)\,d\tau$

**Step 6 — Combine into 2D pose** $(x, y, \theta)$ for planar robots.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" of Two Integrations:** Position is the **second integral** of acceleration. Any small **bias error** in the accelerometer is integrated twice, so bias grows quadratically with time. This is the **fundamental cause of drift**.

---

### 2.2 Inertial Sensor Operating Principles

**Accelerometer (MEMS proof-mass model):**
A small proof mass suspended by springs displaces under acceleration. The displacement is measured capacitively.

$$F = ma = kx \quad \Rightarrow \quad a = \frac{k}{m}\,x$$

**Gyroscope (MEMS Coriolis effect model):**
A vibrating mass experiences a Coriolis force when the structure rotates, producing a perpendicular displacement proportional to angular rate.

$$F_{\text{Coriolis}} = 2m\,\boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{v}$$

where $m$ is the mass, $\boldsymbol{\omega}$ is angular velocity, and $\mathbf{v}$ is the velocity of the vibrating element.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize this table** — these formulas appear in nearly every KTU exam question on this topic.

| # | Equation | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v(t) = v_0 + \int_0^t a(\tau)\,d\tau$ | Velocity from acceleration | $m/s$ |
| 2 | $x(t) = x_0 + \int_0^t v(\tau)\,d\tau$ | Position from velocity | $m$ |
| 3 | $\theta(t) = \theta_0 + \int_0^t \omega(\tau)\,d\tau$ | Heading from angular rate | $rad$ |
| 4 | $x_{k+1} = x_k + v_k \cos\theta_k \Delta t$ | 2D position update (X) | $m$ |
| 5 | $y_{k+1} = y_k + v_k \sin\theta_k \Delta t$ | 2D position update (Y) | $m$ |
| 6 | $\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega_k \Delta t$ | Heading update | $rad$ |
| 7 | $\mathbf{a}_{\text{true}} = \mathbf{a}_{\text{meas}} - \mathbf{R}^T \mathbf{g}$ | Gravity compensation | $m/s^2$ |
| 8 | $\dot{\mathbf{R}} = \mathbf{R}\,[\boldsymbol{\omega}]_\times$ | Rotation matrix kinematics | $rad/s$ |
| 9 | $\sigma_p \propto \sigma_a\, t^2$ | Position error from accel bias | $m$ |
| 10 | $\sigma_\theta \propto \sigma_\omega\, t$ | Heading error from gyro bias | $rad$ |
| 11 | $F_{\text{Coriolis}} = 2m(\boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{v})$ | Gyroscope physics | $N$ |
| 12 | $\omega_{\text{true}} = \omega_{\text{meas}} - b_\omega - n_\omega$ | Gyro bias model | $rad/s$ |
| 13 | $a_{\text{true}} = a_{\text{meas}} - b_a - n_a$ | Accel bias model | $m/s^2$ |

> **Notation note:** $b$ denotes **bias** (slowly varying offset), $n$ denotes **noise** (white Gaussian).

---

### 2.4 Sources of Error in Inertial Dead Reckoning

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks **"Why does dead reckoning drift?"** — know these six error sources cold.

1. **Bias** — constant offset in sensor output (e.g., $b_a = 0.02 \, m/s^2$).
2. **Scale-factor error** — sensor gain is not exactly 1.
3. **Misalignment** — sensor axes not perfectly orthogonal.
4. **White noise** — high-frequency stochastic error.
5. **Bias instability / random walk** — bias slowly drifts.
6. **Integration drift** — quadratic accumulation in position, linear in heading.

**Engineering consequence:** A MEMS IMU accumulates $\sim 50\,m$ position error after just **1 minute** of dead reckoning without aiding.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

| Application | Use of Inertial DR |
|---|---|
| **Autonomous Cars** | High-rate pose updates between GPS fixes |
| **Drones / UAVs** | Stabilization, dead reckoning during GPS outage |
| **AGVs in Warehouses** | Floor-level navigation when markers are occluded |
| **Mars Rovers (NASA)** | Visual-inertial odometry on planetary surfaces |
| **Humanoid Robots** | Balance control via IMU feedback |
| **Aircraft (INS)** | Navigation during radio blackout or over oceans |
| **Submarines** | Subsea navigation without GPS reception |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — 2D Dead Reckoning Update Equations

**Starting premise:** A differential-drive robot moves with linear velocity $v$ and angular velocity $\omega$ in the global frame. Its pose is $\mathbf{p} = (x, y, \theta)^T$.

**Step 1 — Express velocity vector in world frame.**

The robot's body-frame velocity $(v, 0, 0)$ is rotated into the world frame via the rotation matrix $\mathbf{R}_z(\theta)$:

$$
\mathbf{R}_z(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Therefore, the world-frame velocity components are:

$$
\dot{x} = v\cos\theta
$$

$$
\dot{y} = v\sin\theta
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Euler forward integration with sampling interval $\Delta t$.**

$$
x_{k+1} = x_k + v_k\cos\theta_k \cdot \Delta t
$$

$$
y_{k+1} = y_k + v_k\sin\theta_k \cdot \Delta t
$$

**Step 3 — Update heading using direct integration of angular velocity.**

$$
\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega_k \cdot \Delta t
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This assumes **constant $v$ and $\omega$** over $\Delta t$. For higher accuracy, use **Runge-Kutta 4th order** or **trapezoidal integration**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Position from Double Integration of Acceleration

**Given:** Accelerometer measures $a(t)$ in body frame. Robot starts at rest at origin $(x_0 = 0, v_0 = 0)$ with $\theta_0 = 0$.

**Step 1 — Velocity by first integration (fundamental theorem of calculus):**

$$
v(t) = v_0 + \int_0^t a(\tau)\,d\tau = 0 + \int_0^t a(\tau)\,d\tau
$$

$$
\therefore v(t) = \int_0^t a(\tau)\,d\tau
$$

**Step 2 — Position by second integration:**

$$
x(t) = x_0 + \int_0^t v(\tau)\,d\tau = 0 + \int_0^t \left[\int_0^\tau a(s)\,ds\right]d\tau
$$

Using Fubini's theorem to reverse the order of integration:

$$
x(t) = \int_0^t (t-\tau)\,a(\tau)\,d\tau
$$

**Step 3 — Discrete-time approximation (trapezoidal rule):**

$$
x_{k+1} = x_k + v_k\,\Delta t + \tfrac{1}{2}\,a_k\,(\Delta t)^2
$$

where $v_k = v_{k-1} + a_k\,\Delta t$.

**Step 4 — Demonstrate drift from bias.** Suppose the accelerometer has constant bias $b_a$. The measured acceleration is $\tilde{a} = a + b_a$. Then:

$$
\tilde{x}(t) = \int_0^t (t-\tau)\,b_a\,d\tau = \tfrac{1}{2}\,b_a\,t^2
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Position error grows as $t^2$** due to bias. This is why inertial navigation **without aiding** is useless for long durations — at $t = 60\,s$ with $b_a = 0.01\,m/s^2$, the error is **$18\,m$**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Heading from Gyroscope Integration

**Given:** Gyroscope measures angular velocity $\omega(t)$ in body z-axis.

**Step 1 — Direct integration:**

$$
\theta(t) = \theta_0 + \int_0^t \omega(\tau)\,d\tau
$$

**Step 2 — Discrete form:**

$$
\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega_k\,\Delta t
$$

**Step 3 — Bias-induced heading error:**

$$
\tilde{\theta}(t) = \theta_0 + \int_0^t (\omega(\tau) + b_\omega)\,d\tau = \theta(t) + b_\omega\,t
$$

**Step 4 — Convert heading error to lateral position error.** A heading error of $\delta\theta$ over distance $d$ produces lateral drift:

$$
\delta x_{\text{lateral}} = d \cdot \sin(\delta\theta) \approx d\cdot\delta\theta
$$

For a robot moving at $v = 1\,m/s$ with $b_\omega = 1°/hr = 4.85 \times 10^{-6}\,rad/s$, after 1 hour:

$$
\delta x_{\text{lateral}} \approx 3600 \cdot 4.85\times 10^{-6} \approx 0.017\,m
$$

(Low-cost MEMS gyros drift much faster: $\sim 1°/s \Rightarrow$ $\sim 3600\,m$ error per hour.)

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Discrete Dead Reckoning Algorithm

```
INITIALIZE:  x ← x0, y ← y0, θ ← θ0, t ← 0
LOOP every Δt:
    1. READ IMU → (a_x, a_y, a_z), (ω_x, ω_y, ω_z)
    2. PROJECT to ground plane:
         v = a_x (longitudinal)
         ω = ω_z (yaw rate)
    3. UPDATE pose:
         x ← x + v·cos(θ)·Δt
         y ← y + v·sin(θ)·Δt
         θ ← θ + ω·Δt
    4. STORE (x, y, θ)
END LOOP
```

---

### 3.5 Derivation 4 — 3D Orientation Update via Rotation Matrix

For 3D motion, the orientation is propagated via:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{R}} = \mathbf{R}\,[\boldsymbol{\omega}]_\times
$$

where $[\boldsymbol{\omega}]_\times$ is the **skew-symmetric matrix** of angular velocity:

$$
[\boldsymbol{\omega}]_\times = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -\omega_z & \omega_y \\ \omega_z & 0 & -\omega_x \\ -\omega_y & \omega_x & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Discrete integration:

$$
\mathbf{R}_{k+1} = \mathbf{R}_k \exp\!\left([\boldsymbol{\omega}_k]_\times \Delta t\right)
$$

For small $\Delta t$, $\exp([\boldsymbol{\omega}]_\times \Delta t) \approx \mathbf{I} + [\boldsymbol{\omega}]_\times \Delta t$, yielding:

$$
\mathbf{R}_{k+1} \approx \mathbf{R}_k \left(\mathbf{I} + [\boldsymbol{\omega}_k]_\times \Delta t\right)
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Quaternions** are preferred over rotation matrices in practice (no gimbal lock, more efficient). The quaternion update is:
> $$\mathbf{q}_{k+1} = \mathbf{q}_k + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\mathbf{q}_k \otimes (0, \boldsymbol{\omega}_k)\,\Delta t$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 IMU Internal Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["3 Axis MEMS Accelerometer"] --> B["Signal Conditioning and ADC"]
    A1["3 Axis MEMS Gyroscope"] --> B
    A2["Optional 3 Axis Magnetometer"] --> B
    B --> C["Sensor Fusion Engine"]
    C --> D["Bias and Scale Calibration"]
    D --> E["Coordinate Frame Transformation"]
    E --> F["Output Linear Acceleration a x a y a z"]
    E --> G["Output Angular Velocity omega x omega y omega z"]
    E --> H["Output Orientation Roll Pitch Yaw"]
    F --> I["Robot Pose Estimator Dead Reckoning"]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J["Updated Pose x y theta"]
```

---

### 4.2 Dead Reckoning Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0["Initial Pose x0 y0 theta0"] --> S1["Sample IMU at Time t"]
    S1 --> S2["Read Accelerometer a t"]
    S1 --> S3["Read Gyroscope omega t"]
    S3 --> S4["Integrate omega to get theta t"]
    S2 --> S5["Subtract Gravity g from a t"]
    S5 --> S6["Integrate a net to get velocity v t"]
    S6 --> S7["Integrate v t to get position x t y t"]
    S7 --> S8["Combine x y theta into Pose"]
    S4 --> S8
    S8 --> P1["Updated Pose x t y t theta t"]
    P1 --> S1
```

---

### 4.3 Sensor Error Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    E["Inertial Sensor Error Sources"] --> E1["Bias Constant Offset"]
    E --> E2["Scale Factor Error Gain Mismatch"]
    E --> E3["Misalignment Axis Non Orthogonality"]
    E --> E4["White Noise High Frequency"]
    E --> E5["Bias Instability Random Walk"]
    E --> E6["Temperature Drift"]
    E1 --> R["Cumulative Pose Drift Over Time"]
    E2 --> R
    E3 --> R
    E4 --> R
    E5 --> R
    E6 --> R
```

---

### 4.4 Comparison — Encoder-Based vs Inertial Dead Reckoning

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ENC["Encoder Based Dead Reckoning"]
        E1A["Wheel Encoders"] --> E1B["Compute v from wheel rpm"]
        E1B --> E1C["Heading from gyro or steering angle"]
        E1C --> E1D["Pose Update"]
    end
    subgraph INERT["Inertial Based Dead Reckoning"]
        I1A["Accelerometer"] --> I1B["Double Integrate to Position"]
        I1C["Gyroscope"] --> I1D["Integrate to Orientation"]
        I1B --> I1E["Pose Update"]
        I1D --> I1E
    end
    ENC --> COMP["Pros Low Cost Reliable Short Term"]
    ENC --> CONS["Cons Wheel Slippage Errors"]
    INERT --> COMP2["Pros No Slippage High Bandwidth"]
    INERT --> CONS2["Cons Bias Drift Quadratic Error Growth"]
```

---
