<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define dead reckoning in the context of mobile robot navigation. Mention any two sensors used for it.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Dead reckoning is a navigation technique in which the current position of a mobile robot is computed relative to a previously known position by **integrating** the robot's velocity and angular rate over time. Formally, given initial pose $(x_0, y_0, \theta_0)$ and measured $(v_k, \omega_k)$:

$$x_{k+1} = x_k + v_k\cos\theta_k\,\Delta t, \quad y_{k+1} = y_k + v_k\sin\theta_k\,\Delta t, \quad \theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega_k\,\Delta t$$

**Two sensors (1 mark):** (i) **Wheel encoders** (proprioceptive, measure wheel rotation), (ii) **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)** containing accelerometers and gyroscopes.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Why does inertial dead reckoning drift over time? List any two reasons.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Reason 1 — Sensor bias (1.5 marks):** Accelerometer and gyroscope outputs contain a constant offset (bias). Since position is the **double integral** of acceleration, a constant bias $b_a$ produces position error that grows as $\tfrac{1}{2}b_a t^2$ (quadratic drift).

**Reason 2 — Integration of noise (1.5 marks):** Random noise in the sensor is integrated twice, producing a **random walk** in position error that grows as $\sigma \sqrt{t}$ for heading and $\sigma t^{3/2}$ for position.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a) Derive the 2D dead reckoning update equations for a differential-drive mobile robot. Clearly state all assumptions.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. The robot moves on a flat 2D plane.
2. Linear velocity $v$ and angular velocity $\omega$ are constant over the sampling interval $\Delta t$.
3. No wheel slippage; pose is in the global frame.

**Derivation (6 marks):**

The robot's pose in the world frame is $\mathbf{p} = (x, y, \theta)^T$. The body-frame velocity vector is $(v, 0, 0)$. Applying rotation matrix $\mathbf{R}_z(\theta)$ to map to world frame:

$$
\dot{x} = v\cos\theta \quad \dot{y} = v\sin\theta \quad \dot{\theta} = \omega
$$

Applying Euler forward integration:

$$
\boxed{x_{k+1} = x_k + v_k\cos\theta_k\,\Delta t}
$$

$$
\boxed{y_{k+1} = y_k + v_k\sin\theta_k\,\Delta t}
$$

$$
\boxed{\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega_k\,\Delta t}
$$

---

**(b) An IMU reports a constant accelerometer bias $b_a = 0.05\,m/s^2$ and a constant gyroscope bias $b_\omega = 0.02\,rad/s$. If the robot starts from rest at the origin and dead reckons for $T = 10\,s$, estimate the position and heading errors assuming no true motion.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Heading error from gyro bias (3 marks):**

$$
\delta\theta(T) = b_\omega \cdot T = 0.02 \times 10 = 0.2\,rad \approx 11.46°
$$

**Position error from accelerometer bias (4 marks):**

Using double integration of a constant bias:

$$
\delta p(T) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,b_a\,T^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}\times 0.05 \times 100 = 2.5\,m
$$

(Strictly, this is the along-track error; lateral error is zero if heading were perfect. With combined heading drift, total position error would be larger.)

**Final Answer:**
- **Heading error: $0.2\,rad \;(11.46°)$**
- **Position error: $2.5\,m$**

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) Explain the working principle of a MEMS accelerometer and a MEMS gyroscope. How are their outputs used in inertial dead reckoning?** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**MEMS Accelerometer (3.5 marks):**
A MEMS accelerometer consists of a **proof mass** suspended by compliant springs. When the sensor undergoes acceleration $a$, the mass experiences an inertial force $F = ma$, causing it to displace by $x$. Capacitive plates measure this displacement. From Hooke's law:

$$ma = kx \quad \Rightarrow \quad a = \frac{k}{m}\,x$$

**MEMS Gyroscope (3.5 marks):**
A MEMS gyroscope uses a **vibrating proof mass**. When the structure rotates with angular velocity $\boldsymbol{\omega}$, the vibrating mass experiences a **Coriolis force**:

$$\mathbf{F}_{\text{Coriolis}} = 2m\,(\boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{v})$$

This perpendicular force causes a measurable displacement, from which $\omega$ is inferred.

**Usage in dead reckoning:** The accelerometer output is **rotated to world frame** (gravity removed), then **double-integrated** to yield position. The gyroscope output is **single-integrated** to yield orientation (heading $\theta$). Together they provide a complete 2D pose estimate.

---

**(b) Compare wheel-encoder-based and inertial-sensor-based dead reckoning. What are the main limitations of each?** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | Wheel Encoder DR | Inertial DR |
|---|---|---|
| Sensors | Optical/magnetic encoders on wheels | MEMS accelerometer + gyroscope |
| Velocity source | Wheel rotation count | Linear acceleration integration |
| Orientation source | Steering angle or separate gyro | Gyroscope integration |
| Cost | Low | Moderate |
| Bandwidth | Moderate | High (kHz) |
| Limitation 1 | **Wheel slippage** (ice, loose terrain) | **Bias drift** (grows as $t^2$ in position) |
| Limitation 2 | Sensitive to **wheel diameter** changes (load, wear) | **Gravity coupling** — must remove $g$ vector |
| Limitation 3 | Odometry only on ground contact | Sensitive to **vibration** |
| Best for | Indoor ground robots | Aerial, marine, legged, humanoid |

(7 marks awarded for comprehensive comparison with at least 3 valid limitations for each.)

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common marks-losing mistakes in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting gravity removal.** A static accelerometer reads $\mathbf{g}$, not zero. Many students write $v(t) = \int a\,dt$ without subtracting $\mathbf{R}^T\mathbf{g}$ — **lose 2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing integration order.** Position is **second** integral of acceleration, **first** integral of velocity. Wrong order = **0 marks** for the derivation.
> 3. **Not stating assumptions.** KTU examiners explicitly allocate marks for stating assumptions (constant $v, \omega$ over $\Delta t$). Skipping this loses **1–2 marks**.
> 4. **Wrong units in final answer.** Always quote both rad and degrees for heading, and $m/s^2$ and $g$ for acceleration where appropriate.
> 5. **Misnaming the sensor.** "Gyroscope measures angle" — wrong. It measures **angular rate (velocity)**, and angle is obtained by integration. **Lose 1 mark.**

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Dead Reckoning & Inertial Sensors**

- [x] **Dead Reckoning** = estimating current pose from previous pose + motion integration.
- [x] **Three key update equations** for 2D: $x, y$ from $v\cos\theta, v\sin\theta$; $\theta$ from $\omega$.
- [x] **Accelerometer** measures **specific force** (acceleration minus gravity), not true acceleration alone.
- [x] **Gyroscope** measures **angular velocity**, not angle directly — must integrate.
- [x] **IMU** = 3-axis accelerometer + 3-axis gyroscope (± magnetometer).
- [x] **Position drift grows as $t^2$** due to accelerometer bias — fundamental limitation.
- [x] **Heading drift grows as $t$** due to gyroscope bias.
- [x] **Gravity vector** must be projected out using the rotation matrix $\mathbf{R}^T \mathbf{g}$ before integrating acceleration.
- [x] **MEMS accelerometer** uses proof-mass displacement (capacitive sensing).
- [x] **MEMS gyroscope** uses the **Coriolis effect** on a vibrating mass.
- [x] **Drift sources**: bias, scale-factor error, misalignment, white noise, random walk.
- [x] **Dead reckoning is open-loop** — must be fused with absolute sensors (GPS/LiDAR/camera) for bounded error.
- [x] **Quaternions** preferred over Euler angles for 3D orientation to avoid gimbal lock.
- [x] **Standard gravity**: $g = 9.80665\,m/s^2$.
- [x] **Fubini's theorem** gives the clean form: $x(t) = \int_0^t (t-\tau) a(\tau)\,d\tau$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Incorporating GNSS Data with INS - Modified Particle Filter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT735 - ROBOT NAVIGATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Incorporating GNSS Data with INS - Modified Particle Filter ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 GNSS / INS Integrated Navigation — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Integrated Navigation (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** > The technique of combining the **long-term absolute position accuracy** of a Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) with the **short-term, high-rate relative motion tracking** of an Inertial Navigation System (INS) through a probabilistic state estimator, so that the **complementary error characteristics** of the two subsystems yield a navigation solution that is more accurate, continuous, and reliable than either subsystem operating alone. Where: - **GNSS** provides position fixes (latitude $\varphi$, longitude $\lambda$, altitude $h$) at ~1–10 Hz with bounded but time-correlated errors. - **INS** provides attitude ($\phi, \theta, \psi$), velocity ($v_N, v_E, v_D$) and position at ~100–1000 Hz but drifts unboundedly with time $t^3$. - The **Modified Particle Filter (MPF)** is the non-linear, non-Gaussian Bayesian estimator that fuses both data streams in a sequential Monte Carlo framework. ### 1.2 The Particle Filter — Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Particle Filter (PF):** > A sequential Monte Carlo (SMC) method that recursively approximates the posterior probability density function (PDF) $p(\mathbf{x}_k \vert \mathbf{z}_{1:k})$ of a Markov-process state $\mathbf{x}_k$ by a finite weighted set of $N_s$ random samples ("particles") $\{ \mathbf{x}_k^{(i)}, w_k^{(i)} \}_{i=1}^{N_s}$, where each $w_k^{(i)}$ is a normalised importance weight satisfying $\sum_{i=1}^{N_s} w_k^{(i)} = 1$. ### 1.3 The Modified Particle Filter — KTU Board Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Modified Particle Filter (MPF):** > A family of Particle Filter variants engineered to overcome the three classical PF pathologies — *sample degeneracy*, *sample impoverishment*, and *computational blow-up with high-dimensional states* — by **modifying the proposal distribution, the resampling scheme, or the regularisation step** to yield better estimates when fusing GNSS pseudoranges with INS mechanisation outputs. ### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — "Fishing in a Foggy Lake" Imagine a fisherman (**the INS**) in a foggy lake. He walks blindly but feels every footstep with a pedometer (**accelerometer/gyro integration**). Over time, his estimate of where he is drifts like dead reckoning. Occasionally, a lighthouse beam (**a GNSS satellite fix**) pierces the fog and tells him a noisy position. A **standard Particle Filter** throws 1000 tiny boats onto the lake, each representing a *guess* of where the fisherman really is. After every step, the boats that match the lighthouse beam well are duplicated; the boats that disagree are sunk. The cluster of surviving boats is the best estimate. A **Modified Particle Filter** improves this by: - **Not duplicating boats blindly** (regularised PF) — keeps diversity. - **Predicting where boats should go using both the lighthouse AND the footstep pattern** (Rao–Blackwellised or Unscented PF) — smarter proposal. - **Adapting the spread of the fleet** (adaptive PF) — wider when foggy, tighter when clear. The result is a more accurate "where am I" estimate with fewer particles. ### 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Speed of light** $c = 2.998 \times 10^8 \, \text{m/s}$ — defines GNSS pseudorange scaling. - **Earth rotation rate** $\Omega_e = 7.2921159 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{rad/s}$ — enters INS Coriolis correction. - **Gravitational acceleration** $g = 9.80665 \, \text{m/s}^2$ — WGS-84 reference. - **Number of particles** $N_s$ typically chosen between **100 and 5000** in robotic navigation. - **Effective Sample Size (ESS)** threshold: $N_{\text{eff}} < 0.5 \, N_s$ triggers resampling. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The GNSS/INS Integration Problem — Structured Logic **Step 1 — Define the navigation state vector.** The 15-state (or 9-state, or 17-state) error-state vector is: $$\mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} \delta \mathbf{p}^{\top} & \delta \mathbf{v}^{\top} & \delta \boldsymbol{\epsilon}^{\top} & \mathbf{b}_a^{\top} & \mathbf{b}_g^{\top} \end{bmatrix}^{\top}$$ where $\delta \mathbf{p}$ is position error (3), $\delta \mathbf{v}$ is velocity error (3), $\delta \boldsymbol{\epsilon}$ is attitude error (3), $\mathbf{b}_a$ is accelerometer bias (3), $\mathbf{b}_g$ is gyro bias (3). **Step 2 — Define the process model.** The INS mechanisation drives the state through the non-linear function $\mathbf{f}(\cdot)$: $$\mathbf{x}_k = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}_{k-1}) + \mathbf{w}_{k-1}, \quad \mathbf{w}_{k-1} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{Q}_{k-1})$$ **Why?** INS integrates raw $\omega$ and $f$ into position, velocity, attitude. Errors grow due to bias drift, scale factor, and random walk — modelled as $\mathbf{w}_k$. **Step 3 — Define the GNSS measurement model.** Pseudorange $\rho$ from satellite $j$ to receiver: $$\rho_j = \Vert \mathbf{r}_{rcv} - \mathbf{r}_{sat,j} \Vert + c \, \delta t_{rcv} + c \, \delta t_{sat,j} + I_j + T_j + \varepsilon_j$$ **Why?** Each satellite gives a noisy geometric range; the INS provides the predicted pseudorange residual (innovation) that feeds the filter. **Step 4 — Posterior PDF (the Bayesian goal).** Recursively estimate: $$p(\mathbf{x}_k \vert \mathbf{z}_{1:k}) \propto p(\mathbf{z}_k \vert \mathbf{x}_k) \cdot \int p(\mathbf{x}_k \vert \mathbf{x}_{k-1}) \, p(\mathbf{x}_{k-1} \vert \mathbf{z}_{1:k-1}) \, d\mathbf{x}_{k-1}$$ **Why?** This integral is **analytically intractable** for the 15-D, non-Gaussian GNSS/INS problem — hence Monte Carlo sampling via particles. **Step 5 — Monte Carlo approximation.** Replace the continuous PDF with a discrete weighted sum: $$p(\mathbf{x}_k \vert \mathbf{z}_{1:k}) \approx \sum_{i=1}^{N_s} w_k^{(i)} \, \delta(\mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{x}_k^{(i)})$$ **Step 6 — Importance Sampling.** Since drawing samples directly from $p(\mathbf{x}_k \vert \mathbf{z}_{1:k})$ is hard, draw from a proposal $q(\mathbf{x}_k \vert \mathbf{z}_{1:k})$ and correct via importance weight: $$w_k^{(i)} \propto w_{k-1}^{(i)} \cdot \frac{p(\mathbf{z}_k \vert \mathbf{x}_k^{(i)}) \, p(\mathbf{x}_k^{(i)} \vert \mathbf{x}_{k-1}^{(i)})}{q(\mathbf{x}_k^{(i)} \vert \mathbf{x}_{k-1}^{(i)}, \mathbf{z}_k)}$$ **Step 7 — Resampling (when $N_{\text{eff}}$ falls).** Discard particles with tiny weights, replicate those with large weights so that each new particle carries equal weight $\tfrac{1}{N_s}$. ### 2.2 Modifications Encapsulated in the "Modified Particle Filter" | Modification | What it changes | When it helps | |---|---|---| | **Auxiliary PF (APF)** | Uses 2-stage sampling conditioned on a "look-ahead" statistic | When measurement likelihood is sharply peaked (few good GNSS satellites) | | **Regularised PF (RPF)** | Resamples from a continuous kernel $K_h(\cdot)$ instead of Dirac delta | When posterior is continuous and sample impoverishment is severe | | **Rao–Blackwellised PF (RBPF)** | Marginalises linear sub-states via KF, samples only the non-linear part | When state has linear (bias) + non-linear (position) parts | | **Gaussian Particle Filter (GPF)** | Gaussian approximation of proposal but Monte Carlo update | When posterior is near-Gaussian (high-grade IMU) | | **Unscented Particle Filter (UPF)** | Uses UKF-suggested proposal for each particle | When process noise is moderate but measurement noise is non-Gaussian (multipath) | | **Adaptive PF** | Adjusts $N_s$ and kernel bandwidth online | When computational budget is variable (UAV) | ### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit | Bound / Formula | |---|---|---|---| | $\mathbf{x}_k^{(i)}$ | $i$-th particle at time $k$ | — | Drawn from $q(\cdot)$ | | $w_k^{(i)}$ | Normalised importance weight | dimensionless | $w_k^{(i)} = \tilde{w}_k^{(i)} \big/ \sum_{j} \tilde{w}_k^{(j)}$ | | $N_{\text{eff}}$ | Effective sample size | dimensionless | $N_{\text{eff}} = 1 \big/ \sum_{i=1}^{N_s} (w_k^{(i)})^2$ | | $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_k$ | Posterior mean (MMSE estimate) | various | $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_k = \sum_i w_k^{(i)} \, \mathbf{x}_k^{(i)}$ | | $\mathbf{P}_k$ | Posterior covariance | various$^2$ | $\mathbf{P}_k = \sum_i w_k^{(i)} (\mathbf{x}_k^{(i)} - \hat{\mathbf{x}}_k)(\cdot)^{\top}$ | | $\mathbf{Q}_k$ | Process noise covariance | varies | INS random walk $\sigma^2 \Delta t$ | | $\mathbf{R}_k$ | Measurement noise covariance | $\text{m}^2$ | GNSS pseudorange variance $\sigma_{\rho}^2$ | | $\rho_j$ | Pseudorange to $j$-th satellite | m | $\Vert \mathbf{r}_{rcv} - \mathbf{r}_{sat,j}\Vert + b + \varepsilon$ | | $K_h(\cdot)$ | Regularisation kernel | — | $\int K_h(x)\,dx = 1$ | | $h$ | Kernel bandwidth | — | Silverman: $h = (4 \hat{\sigma}^5 / 3N_s)^{1/5}$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Board Exam Note:** Never confuse the **process noise covariance $\mathbf{Q}$** (from INS random walk) with the **measurement noise covariance $\mathbf{R}$** (from GNSS UERE). These are independent and MUST be modelled separately for any PF to converge. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility 1. **Autonomous Vehicles** — Carries GPS + MEMS-IMU; MPF handles GNSS outages in tunnels. 2. **UAV Swarms** — Tightly-coupled GNSS/INS with UPF enables cm-level precision landing. 3. **Marine Navigation** — RBPF-INS/GNSS allows submerged operation surfacing only for sparse fixes. 4. **Planetary Rovers** — Adaptive MPF dynamically trades particles for power budget on Mars rovers. 5. **Precision Agriculture** — Regularised PF keeps tractor guidance stable even under canopy (multipath). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Importance Weight Update (Generic PF) We begin with the posterior at time $k-1$ approximated by particles: $$ p(\mathbf{x}_{0:k-1} \vert \mathbf{z}_{1:k-1}) \approx \sum_{i=1}^{N_s} w_{k-1}^{(i)} \, \delta(\mathbf{x}_{0:k-1} - \mathbf{x}_{0:k-1}^{(i)}) $$ Drawing from the proposal and propagating gives new weighted particles. We derive the weight update from first principles. $$ w_k^{(i)} \propto \frac{p(\mathbf{x}_{0:k}^{(i)} \vert \mathbf{z}_{1:k})}{q(\mathbf{x}_{0:k}^{(i)} \vert \mathbf{z}_{1:k})} $$ Expanding using Bayes' rule: $$ \begin{aligned} p(\mathbf{x}_{0:k} \vert \mathbf{z}_{1:k}) &= \frac{p(\mathbf{z}_k \vert \mathbf{x}_k) \, p(\mathbf{x}_k \vert \mathbf{x}_{k-1})}{p(\mathbf{z}_k \vert \mathbf{z}_{1:k-1})} \cdot p(\mathbf{x}_{0:k-1} \vert \mathbf{z}_{1:k-1}) \end{aligned} $$ Substituting into the weight ratio and cancelling $p(\mathbf{x}_{0:k-1}\vert\mathbf{z}_{1:k-1})$ terms: $$ \begin{aligned} w_k^{(i)} &\propto w_{k-1}^{(i)} \cdot \frac{p(\mathbf{z}_k \vert \mathbf{x}_k^{(i)}) \, p(\mathbf{x}_k^{(i)} \vert \mathbf{x}_{k-1}^{(i)})}{q(\mathbf{x}_k^{(i)} \vert \mathbf{x}_{0:k-1}^{(i)}, \mathbf{z}_{1:k})} \end{aligned} $$ For a first-order Markov process and proposal that depends only on $\mathbf{x}_{k-1}^{(i)}$ and $\mathbf{z}_k$: $$ \begin{aligned} q(\mathbf{x}_k^{(i)} \vert \mathbf{x}_{0:k-1}^{(i)}, \mathbf{z}_{1:k}) = q(\mathbf{x}_k^{(i)} \vert \mathbf{x}_{k-1}^{(i)}, \mathbf{z}_k) \end{aligned} $$ Hence the canonical SIR (Sampling Importance Resampling) weight update: $$ \begin{aligned} \boxed{\,w_k^{(i)} \propto w_{k-1}^{(i)} \cdot p(\mathbf{z}_k \vert \mathbf{x}_k^{(i)}) \, p(\mathbf{x}_k^{(i)} \vert \mathbf{x}_{k-1}^{(i)})\,} \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.2 Derivation of the Effective Sample Size The ESS quantifies particle degeneracy. Define it as the reciprocal of the *squared weight sum*: $$ \begin{aligned} N_{\text{eff}} &= \frac{N_s}{1 + \text{Var}_q(w_k^{(i)})} \;\approx\; \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{N_s} (w_k^{(i)})^2} \end{aligned} $$ We justify this: if all weights are equal $\Rightarrow w_k^{(i)} = 1/N_s \Rightarrow \sum (w_k^{(i)})^2 = N_s \cdot (1/N_s^2) = 1/N_s \Rightarrow N_{\text{eff}} = N_s$ (no degeneracy). If one weight is 1 and others are 0 $\Rightarrow \sum (w_k^{(i)})^2 = 1 \Rightarrow N_{\text{eff}} = 1$ (full collapse). Thus $1 \le N_{\text{eff}} \le N_s$, and resampling is triggered when: $$ \begin{aligned} N_{\text{eff}} < \alpha \cdot N_s, \quad \alpha \in [0.5,\,1.0] \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.3 Derivation of the Regularised PF Update (Modified PF Variant) In the standard bootstrap PF, resampling draws from the discrete empirical distribution: $$ \hat{p}(\mathbf{x}_k \vert \mathbf{z}_{1:k}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N_s} \frac{1}{N_s} \, \delta(\mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{x}_k^{(i)}) $$ This **discreteness** causes *sample impoverishment*. The **Regularised PF** replaces Dirac deltas with smooth kernels: $$ \begin{aligned} \hat{p}_{\text{reg}}(\mathbf{x}_k \vert \mathbf{z}_{1:k}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N_s} w_k^{(i)} \, K_h(\mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{x}_k^{(i)}) \end{aligned} $$ where $K_h(\cdot) = \frac{1}{h^{n_x}} K\!\left(\frac{\cdot}{h}\right)$, with $K$ a symmetric, zero-mean, unit-variance kernel (Epanechnikov, Gaussian). For the **Gaussian kernel**, the optimal bandwidth from Silverman's rule is: $$ \begin{aligned} h_{\text{opt}} = \left( \frac{4}{n_x + 2} \right)^{\!\frac{1}{n_x+4}} N_s^{-\frac{1}{n_x+4}} \, \hat{\sigma} \end{aligned} $$ where $n_x$ is the state dimension and $\hat{\sigma}$ is the empirical standard deviation along each axis. Theoretical justification: the **mean integrated squared error (MISE)** between $\hat{p}_{\text{reg}}$ and the true posterior is minimised when $K$ is the Epanechnikov kernel: $$ \begin{aligned} K_{\text{opt}}(x) = \frac{3}{4}(1 - x^2), \quad \vert x \vert \le 1 \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.4 Derivation of the Rao–Blackwellised PF for GNSS/INS Partition the state into linear ($\mathbf{x}^{\ell}$, e.g., biases) and non-linear ($\mathbf{x}^{n}$, e.g., position) parts: $$ \begin{aligned} \mathbf{x}_k = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{x}_k^{n} \\ \mathbf{x}_k^{\ell} \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{x}_k^{\ell} = \mathbf{A}_k \mathbf{x}_{k-1}^{\ell} + \mathbf{B}_k \mathbf{x}_k^{n} + \mathbf{w}_k^{\ell} \end{aligned} $$ Factor the posterior: $$ \begin{aligned} p(\mathbf{x}_{0:k}^{n}, \mathbf{x}_{0:k}^{\ell} \vert \mathbf{z}_{1:k}) = p(\mathbf{x}_{0:k}^{\ell} \vert \mathbf{x}_{0:k}^{n}, \mathbf{z}_{1:k}) \cdot p(\mathbf{x}_{0:k}^{n} \vert \mathbf{z}_{1:k}) \end{aligned} $$ The conditional $p(\mathbf{x}_{0:k}^{\ell} \vert \mathbf{x}_{0:k}^{n}, \mathbf{z}_{1:k})$ is **linear-Gaussian** and is tracked exactly by a Kalman Filter per particle. The marginal $p(\mathbf{x}_{0:k}^{n} \vert \mathbf{z}_{1:k})$ is sampled by PF with reduced dimension $n_x^{n}$. The RBPF variance reduction relative to vanilla PF is: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Var}_{\text{RBPF}}(\hat{\mathbf{x}}_k) \le \text{Var}_{\text{PF}}(\hat{\mathbf{x}}_k) \end{aligned} $$ with equality only when the state is fully non-linear. For GNSS/INS, $n_x = 15$ becomes $n_x^{n} \approx 6$, slashing the required $N_s$ by a factor of $\sim 10$. ### 3.5 Derivation of the GNSS Pseudorange Likelihood Given predicted pseudorange residual $\delta\rho_j^{(i)} = \rho_{j,\text{meas}} - \hat{\rho}_j^{(i)}$ at particle $i$: $$ \begin{aligned} p(\mathbf{z}_k \vert \mathbf{x}_k^{(i)}) = \prod_{j=1}^{M} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi \sigma_{\rho,j}^2}} \exp\!\left( -\frac{(\delta\rho_j^{(i)})^2}{2\sigma_{\rho,j}^2} \right) \end{aligned} $$ Under non-Gaussian heavy-tailed multipath, the Laplacian likelihood is often substituted: $$ \begin{aligned} p(\mathbf{z}_k \vert \mathbf{x}_k^{(i)}) = \prod_{j=1}^{M} \frac{1}{2b_j} \exp\!\left( -\frac{\vert \delta\rho_j^{(i)} \vert}{b_j} \right) \end{aligned} $$ This is **exactly where the PF outperforms the EKF** — the EKF is locked to Gaussian likelihood; the PF tolerates arbitrary $p(\mathbf{z}_k \vert \mathbf{x}_k)$. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Generic Particle Filter Algorithm Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A["START - Time k"] --> B["INITIALISATION k=0
Draw x0_i from prior p(x0)
Set w0_i = 1 over Ns"] B --> C["FOR each particle i = 1 to Ns"] C --> D["PROPAGATION
Sample xk_i from q(xk
given xk-1_i, zk)"] D --> E["WEIGHT UPDATE
Compute wk_i proportional to
wk-1_i times p(zk
given xk_i)"] E --> F{"CHECK
Neff less than 0.5 Ns?"} F -- YES --> G["RESAMPLE
Replace weak particles
with copies of strong ones
Reset wk_i to 1 over Ns"] F -- NO --> H["KEEP PARTICLES"] G --> I["STATE ESTIMATE
x-hat k = sum wk_i xk_i
P k = sum wk_i (xk_i - xhat) transpose"] H --> I I --> J{"GNSS AVAILABLE?"} J -- YES --> K["FEED PSEUDORANGE RESIDUAL
to weight update at next k"] J -- NO --> L["INS-ONLY PROPAGATION
Use q from process model"] K --> M["INCREMENT k to k plus 1"] L --> M M --> C ``` ### 4.2 Modified Particle Filter — Variant Selection Logic ```mermaid flowchart TD MP["MODIFIED PARTICLE FILTER"] --> Q1{"STATE DIMENSION?"} Q1 -- "HIGH n greater than 10" --> RB["RAO-BLACKWELLISED PF
Marginalise linear biases
Kalman sub-filter"] Q1 -- "MODERATE n between 5 and 10" --> UP["UNSCENTED PF
UKF proposal per particle
Better than bootstrap"] Q1 -- "LOW n less than 5" --> ST["STANDARD SIR PF
Sufficient accuracy"] MP --> Q2{"MEASUREMENT NOISE TYPE?"} Q2 -- "MULTIPATH or HEAVY TAILED" --> RP["REGULARISED PF
Smooth kernel resampling
Epanechnikov kernel"] Q2 -- "GAUSSIAN" --> GP["GAUSSIAN PF
Gaussian proposal
Closed-form update"] Q2 -- "FEW SATELLITES" --> AP["AUXILIARY PF
Look-ahead resampling
Pre-selects good particles"] MP --> Q3{"COMPUTE BUDGET?"} Q3 -- "VARIABLE" --> AD["ADAPTIVE PF
Online Ns tuning
Bandwidth adaptation"] Q3 -- "FIXED" --> ST2["FIXED-Ns PF"] ``` ### 4.3 GNSS/INS Coupling Architectures ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph LOOSE["LOOSE COUPLING"] G1["GNSS RECEIVER
Position Velocity"] --> K1["KALMAN / PF"] I1["INS"] --> K1 K1 --> OUT1["NAV OUTPUT"] end subgraph TIGHT["TIGHT COUPLING"] G2["GNSS RAW PSEUDORANGES"] --> K2["CENTRAL PF FILTER"] I2["INS PREDICTED PSEUDORANGES"] --> K2 K2 --> OUT2["NAV OUTPUT"] end subgraph ULTRA["ULTRA-TIGHT DEEP COUPLING"] G3["GNSS CORRELATOR
I and Q samples"] --> K3["CENTRAL MPF"] I3["INS AIDING"] --> K3 K3 --> G3 K3 --> OUT3["NAV OUTPUT"] end ``` ### 4.4 Block Diagram — Modified Particle Filter in the INS/GNSS Loop ```mermaid flowchart TD IMU["IMU SENSORS
Gyro omega
Accelerometer f"] --> MECH["INS MECHANISATION
Integrate to p, v, epsilon"] MECH --> PRED["PARTICLE PROPAGATION
xk_i drawn from process model"] SAT["GNSS SATELLITES
rho measured, dot-rho measured"] --> RES["RESIDUAL COMPUTATION
delta-rho = measured minus predicted"] PRED --> LIK["LIKELIHOOD p of z given x
Gaussian or Laplacian"] RES --> LIK LIK --> WT["WEIGHT UPDATE
wk_i = wk-1_i times likelihood"] WT --> ESS{"Neff less than threshold?"} ESS -- YES --> REG["REGULARISED RESAMPLING
Smooth kernel K-h"] ESS -- NO --> EST["ESTIMATE OUTPUT
x-hat k, P k"] REG --> EST EST --> FB["FEEDBACK TO INS
Correct bias estimates"] FB --> MECH ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]** **Q1. Define the Modified Particle Filter and state TWO situations in GNSS/INS integration where it is preferred over an Extended Kalman Filter.** *(3 marks)* **Model Answer:** A Modified Particle Filter (MPF) is a sequential Monte Carlo estimator that recursively approximates the posterior state PDF using a set of weighted particles, with at least one modification (regularised resampling, auxiliary sampling, Rao–Blackwellisation, or adaptive $N_s$) to the standard SIR PF to mitigate sample degeneracy or impoverishment. Two situations where MPF is preferred over EKF: 1. **Non-Gaussian measurement noise** — e.g., multipath-affected GNSS pseudoranges in urban canyons. EKF enforces Gaussian likelihoods; MPF accepts any $p(\mathbf{z}_k \vert \mathbf{x}_k)$. 2. **Severe non-linearity** — e.g., low-cost MEMS-IMU attitude errors that violate the small-angle linearisation assumption used by EKF. --- > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]** **Q2. What is the Effective Sample Size $N_{\text{eff}}$ in a Particle Filter? Write its formula and explain the criterion that triggers resampling.** *(3 marks)* **Model Answer:** The Effective Sample Size is a scalar measure of how evenly the importance weights are distributed among the $N_s$ particles. It is defined as: $$ \begin{aligned} N_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{N_s} (w_k^{(i)})^2} \end{aligned} $$ It satisfies $1 \le N_{\text{eff}} \le N_s$. The resampling step is triggered when $N_{\text{eff}}$ falls below a chosen fraction of $N_s$: $$ \begin{aligned} N_{\text{eff}} < \alpha \, N_s, \quad \text{typically } \alpha \in [0.5,\,1.0] \end{aligned} $$ A small $N_{\text{eff}}$ indicates that most particles carry negligible weight (sample degeneracy) and that the posterior is effectively supported by only a few particles. --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – End Semester Model Paper]** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** Derive the importance weight update equation of the standard Sampling Importance Resampling (SIR) Particle Filter starting from the recursive Bayesian posterior. Clearly show how the proposal distribution is factorised under the first-order Markov assumption. *(7 marks)* **(b)** With the aid of a block diagram, explain the architecture of a tightly-coupled GNSS/INS system using a Regularised Particle Filter. Show how the predicted pseudorange residual is computed and used to update particle weights. *(7 marks)* --- #### **Model Solution — Question A(a)** We begin with the recursive Bayes posterior: $$ \begin{aligned} p(\mathbf{x}_k \vert \mathbf{z}_{1:k}) &= p(\mathbf{z}_k \vert \mathbf{x}_k) \int p(\mathbf{x}_k \vert \mathbf{x}_{k-1}) \, p(\mathbf{x}_{k-1} \vert \mathbf{z}_{1:k-1}) \, d\mathbf{x}_{k-1} \end{aligned} $$ The posterior is approximated by weighted particles: $$ \begin{aligned} p(\mathbf{x}_{0:k-1} \vert \mathbf{z}_{1:k-1}) &\approx \sum_{i=1}^{N_s} w_{k-1}^{(i)} \, \delta(\mathbf{x}_{0:k-1} - \mathbf{x}_{0:k-1}^{(i)}) \end{aligned} $$ The weight update for particle $i$ is defined as: $$ \begin{aligned} w_k^{(i)} &\propto \frac{p(\mathbf{x}_{0:k}^{(i)} \vert \mathbf{z}_{1:k})}{q(\mathbf{x}_{0:k}^{(i)} \vert \mathbf{z}_{1:k})} \end{aligned} $$ Using Bayes' rule $p(\mathbf{x}_{0:k} \vert \mathbf{z}_{1:k}) = \dfrac{p(\mathbf{z}_k \vert \mathbf{x}_k) \, p(\mathbf{x}_k \vert \mathbf{x}_{k-1})}{p(\mathbf{z}_k \vert \mathbf{z}_{1:k-1})} p(\mathbf{x}_{0:k-1} \vert \mathbf{z}_{1:k-1})$: $$ \begin{aligned} w_k^{(i)} &\propto w_{k-1}^{(i)} \cdot \frac{p(\mathbf{z}_k \vert \mathbf{x}_k^{(i)}) \, p(\mathbf{x}_k^{(i)} \vert \mathbf{x}_{k-1}^{(i)})}{q(\mathbf{x}_k^{(i)} \vert \mathbf{x}_{0:k-1}^{(i)}, \mathbf{z}_{1:k})} \end{aligned} $$ Applying the **first-order Markov assumption** — current state depends only on previous state, and the proposal depends only on $\mathbf{x}_{k-1}^{(i)}$ and $\mathbf{z}_k$: $$ \begin{aligned} q(\mathbf{x}_k^{(i)} \vert \mathbf{x}_{0:k-1}^{(i)}, \mathbf{z}_{1:k}) &= q(\mathbf{x}_k^{(i)} \vert \mathbf{x}_{k-1}^{(i)}, \mathbf{z}_k) \end{aligned} $$ Hence the SIR weight update: $$ \begin{aligned} \boxed{\,w_k^{(i)} \propto w_{k-1}^{(i)} \cdot p(\mathbf{z}_k \vert \mathbf{x}_k^{(i)}) \, p(\mathbf{x}_k^{(i)} \vert \mathbf{x}_{k-1}^{(i)})\,} \end{aligned} $$ If the proposal is chosen as the prior (bootstrap PF), $q(\mathbf{x}_k^{(i)} \vert \mathbf{x}_{k-1}^{(i)}, \mathbf{z}_k) = p(\mathbf{x}_k^{(i)} \vert \mathbf{x}_{k-1}^{(i)})$, and the weight simplifies to: $$ \begin{aligned} w_k^{(i)} &\propto w_{k-1}^{(i)} \cdot p(\mathbf{z}_k \vert \mathbf{x}_k^{(i)}) \end{aligned} $$ Normalisation: $w_k^{(i)} \leftarrow \tilde{w}_k^{(i)} \big/ \sum_j \tilde{w}_k^{(j)}$. **Mark Distribution (KTU Key):** - Recursive Bayes posterior — 1 mark - Importance sampling derivation — 2 marks - Markov assumption factorisation — 2 marks - Final boxed equation — 2 marks --- #### **Model Solution — Question A(b)** A **tightly-coupled GNSS/INS RPF** ingests *raw pseudoranges* (not pre-computed position) so that fewer than 4 satellites still yield useful updates. **Block flow:** 1. **IMU** provides $\omega$ and $f$ to INS mechanisation — yields predicted position $\hat{p}_k^{(i)}$, velocity $\hat{v}_k^{(i)}$, attitude $\hat{\epsilon}_k^{(i)}$. 2. **Satellite ephemeris** gives satellite positions $\mathbf{r}_{sat,j}$ at time $k$. 3. **Predicted pseudorange** at particle $i$: $$ \begin{aligned} \hat{\rho}_j^{(i)} = \Vert \hat{\mathbf{r}}_{rcv}^{(i)} - \mathbf{r}_{sat,j} \Vert + c \, \delta \hat{t}_{rcv}^{(i)} \end{aligned} $$ 4. **Residual (innovation)**: $$ \begin{aligned} \delta \rho_j^{(i)} = \rho_{j,\text{meas}} - \hat{\rho}_j^{(i)} \end{aligned} $$ 5. **Likelihood** (Gaussian assumption): $$ \begin{aligned} p(\mathbf{z}_k \vert \mathbf{x}_k^{(i)}) = \prod_{j=1}^{M} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi \sigma_j^2}} \exp\!\left( -\frac{(\delta \rho_j^{(i)})^2}{2\sigma_j^2} \right) \end{aligned} $$ 6. **Weight update**: $$ \begin{aligned} w_k^{(i)} \propto w_{k-1}^{(i)} \cdot p(\mathbf{z}_k \vert \mathbf{x}_k^{(i)}) \end{aligned} $$ 7. **Regularised resampling** when $N_{\text{eff}} < 0.5 \, N_s$: $$ \begin{aligned} \mathbf{x}_k^{(i) \star} \sim \sum_{j=1}^{N_s} w_k^{(j)} \, K_h(\mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{x}_k^{(j)}) \end{aligned} $$ 8. **Feedback** the posterior mean to INS to correct IMU biases. **Mark Distribution:** - Block diagram with 5+ blocks — 2 marks - Predicted pseudorange formula — 1 mark - Innovation and likelihood — 2 marks - Weight update and RPF resampling — 1 mark - INS feedback loop explanation — 1 mark --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** Explain the **three classical pathologies** of the standard Particle Filter — sample degeneracy, sample impoverishment, and computational complexity — and discuss how the **Regularised Particle Filter** addresses each of them. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A robotic ground vehicle carries a GNSS receiver and a MEMS-IMU. The state vector is 9-dimensional (position, velocity, attitude). Suggest a specific **Modified Particle Filter architecture** for this case. Justify your choice, derive the state-space model, and propose a value of $N_s$ with reasoning. *(7 marks)* --- #### **Model Solution — Question B(a)** **Pathology 1 — Sample Degeneracy.** After a few iterations, almost all particles end up with negligible weights except a few. The variance of importance weights grows monotonically. Mathematically, $\text{Var}(w_k^{(i)}) \to \infty$ as $k$ grows. **Fix:** Compute $N_{\text{eff}}$ and resample when it falls below a threshold; this concentrates particles in high-likelihood regions. **Pathology 2 — Sample Impoverishment.** After resampling, particles with high weights are duplicated many times; the particle set loses diversity and may collapse to a few distinct points — even though the true posterior is continuous. **Fix (RPF):** Replace Dirac-delta resampling with continuous kernel resampling: $$ \begin{aligned} \mathbf{x}_k^{(i)\star} \sim \sum_{j=1}^{N_s} w_k^{(j)} K_h(\mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{x}_k^{(j)}) \end{aligned} $$ with bandwidth $h$ chosen per Silverman's rule. This injects continuous jitter and preserves diversity. **Pathology 3 — Computational Complexity.** Required $N_s$ scales exponentially with state dimension $n_x$ ("curse of dimensionality"). A 15-state GNSS/INS problem may need $N_s > 10^5$ for a vanilla PF. **Fix (RPF partial):** Combine RPF with Rao–Blackwellisation to marginalise linear sub-states (biases) using a per-particle Kalman Filter, reducing effective $n_x$ from 15 to 6–9. **Mark Distribution:** - Three pathologies identified — 3 marks - RPF mathematical fix per pathology — 3 marks - Diagram or kernel expression — 1 mark --- #### **Model Solution — Question B(b)** **Recommended Architecture:** **Unscented Particle Filter (UPF)** with **Regularised Resampling**. **Justification:** - 9-D state is *moderate* dimension — full PF would need $N_s \approx 10^4$, UPF needs only $\sim 500$. - GNSS pseudoranges in urban driving often suffer **multipath** (non-Gaussian) — UPF's UKF-generated proposal captures this better than bootstrap. - MEMS-IMU has **mild non-linearity** but significant bias drift — UKF handles nonlinearity per particle, PF handles global non-Gaussianity. **State-Space Model:** State: $\mathbf{x}_k = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{p}_k^{\top} & \mathbf{v}_k^{\top} & \boldsymbol{\epsilon}_k^{\top} \end{bmatrix}^{\top}$, $\mathbf{p}_k \in \mathbb{R}^3$, $\mathbf{v}_k \in \mathbb{R}^3$, $\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_k \in \mathbb{R}^3$. Process model: $$ \begin{aligned} \mathbf{p}_k &= \mathbf{p}_{k-1} + \mathbf{v}_{k-1} \Delta t + \tfrac{1}{2} (\mathbf{R}_{b}^{n} \mathbf{f}_{k-1}) \Delta t^2 \\ \mathbf{v}_k &= \mathbf{v}_{k-1} + (\mathbf{R}_{b}^{n} \mathbf{f}_{k-1} - \mathbf{g}) \Delta t \\ \boldsymbol{\epsilon}_k &= \boldsymbol{\epsilon}_{k-1} + \mathbf{E}(\boldsymbol{\epsilon}) \, \boldsymbol{\omega}_{k-1}^{b} \Delta t \end{aligned} $$ with $\mathbf{w}_k \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{Q}_k)$ including gyro/accel random walk. Measurement model (GNSS pseudoranges): $$ \begin{aligned} \rho_j &= \Vert \mathbf{p}_k - \mathbf{r}_{sat,j} \Vert + c \, \delta t_k + \varepsilon_j, \quad j = 1, \ldots, M \end{aligned} $$ **Choice of $N_s$:** For $n_x = 9$, Silverman's rule suggests $N_s \approx n_x^{4} = 9^4 \approx 6561$ for *vanilla* PF. With **UPF**, the UKF proposal drastically tightens the support, so $N_s \approx 500$ suffices. We choose **$N_s = 500$**, balancing real-time compute (vehicle at 50 Hz) and accuracy. **Mark Distribution:** - Justification of UPF — 2 marks - Process model derivation — 2 marks - Measurement model — 1 mark - $N_s$ choice with reasoning — 2 marks --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing weight normalisation** — failing to divide by $\sum \tilde{w}_k^{(j)}$ loses **1 mark** consistently. > 2. **Forgetting the Markov assumption** in the derivation — KTU key explicitly awards **2 marks** for this step. > 3. **Using $\mathbf{Q}$ and $\mathbf{R}$ interchangeably** in measurement update — costs **1 mark**. > 4. **Drawing the INS/INS coupling diagram wrong** — Tight coupling feeds *raw pseudoranges*, not pre-computed GNSS position. Loose coupling uses GNSS position. Mixing them up costs **2 marks**. > 5. **Ignoring non-Gaussian likelihoods** — When asked about MPF advantages, students who say only "non-linearity" without mentioning *non-Gaussian noise* lose **1 mark**. > 6. **Not stating the resampling trigger threshold** — Always write "$N_{\text{eff}} < \alpha N_s$ with $\alpha \in [0.5, 1]$", not just "trigger resampling". --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **GNSS/INS integration** combines long-term GNSS accuracy with short-term INS stability; the **Modified Particle Filter** fuses them under non-linear, non-Gaussian conditions. - **Standard SIR Particle Filter** approximates $p(\mathbf{x}_k \vert \mathbf{z}_{1:k})$ by $N_s$ weighted particles; weight update is $w_k^{(i)} \propto w_{k-1}^{(i)} \, p(\mathbf{z}_k \vert \mathbf{x}_k^{(i)}) \, p(\mathbf{x}_k^{(i)} \vert \mathbf{x}_{k-1}^{(i)})$. - **Effective Sample Size** $N_{\text{eff}} = 1 \big/ \sum_i (w_k^{(i)})^2$ triggers resampling when it drops below $\alpha N_s$. - **Three PF pathologies**: sample degeneracy, sample impoverishment, curse of dimensionality — each addressed by a specific MPF variant. - **Regularised PF (RPF)** replaces Dirac-delta resampling with continuous kernel $K_h(\cdot)$, with optimal bandwidth per Silverman's rule. - **Rao–Blackwellised PF (RBPF)** marginalises linear sub-states (IMU biases) via per-particle Kalman Filter; reduces effective state dimension. - **Unscented PF (UPF)** uses a UKF-generated proposal per particle; excels when measurement noise is non-Gaussian (GNSS multipath). - **Auxiliary PF (APF)** uses look-ahead statistic to pre-select high-likelihood particles before propagation. - **Adaptive PF** dynamically tunes $N_s$ and kernel bandwidth to fit a variable computational budget. - **GNSS pseudorange model**: $\rho_j = \Vert \mathbf{r}_{rcv} - \mathbf{r}_{sat,j}\Vert + c \, \delta t_{rcv} + \varepsilon_j$. - **Tight coupling** uses raw pseudoranges + INS; **loose coupling** uses GNSS position + INS. - **MPF vs EKF**: MPF accepts arbitrary likelihood (Laplacian, mixture) and any non-linearity; EKF assumes Gaussian and small-angle linearisation. - **Practical $N_s$**: 100–500 for UPF/RBPF on 6–15 dimensional GNSS/INS state; 1000+ for vanilla SIR PF. - **Board must-know constants**: $c = 2.998 \times 10^8$ m/s, $\Omega_e = 7.2921159 \times 10^{-5}$ rad/s, $g = 9.80665$ m/s$^2$.

topics.title: Estimating Velocity by Combining GNSS and INS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT735 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Estimating Velocity by Combining GNSS and INS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Estimating Velocity by Combining GNSS and INS** is a *sensor fusion* methodology in which the absolute but low-rate velocity/position measurements from a **Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS)** are integrated with the high-rate but drift-prone inertial measurements from an **Inertial Navigation System (INS)** through a recursive Bayesian estimator (typically a **Kalman Filter, KF**, or **Extended Kalman Filter, EKF**) to produce a continuous, smooth, and bounded-error estimate of a vehicle's velocity vector.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this is a classic example of a **complementary filter architecture** in the navigation domain, where two sensors with complementary error spectra (GNSS: unbiased but noisy, INS: smooth but biased) are blended to obtain an estimate superior to either sensor alone.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (KTU Syllabus Highlight)**
> The combined GNSS/INS velocity estimator computes the optimal state vector $\hat{\mathbf{x}}$ that minimizes the **a posteriori** state-error covariance $\mathbf{P}_{k|k}$ at every measurement epoch $k$, by weighting the INS prediction against the GNSS measurement update using their respective covariance matrices.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are blindfolded and walking through a long hallway:

* **INS** is like the feeling in your feet and inner ear. You can feel every tiny step, acceleration, and turn (**high update rate, ~100–1000 Hz**). But if you walk for a long time, your sense of distance travelled drifts — you might think you walked 100 m when you actually walked 120 m. This is the classic **INS drift problem**.
* **GNSS** is like a friend standing at the end of the hallway with a measuring tape, who can tell you your *exact* location every 1 second (**low update rate, ~1–10 Hz**). However, his measurements are noisy — sometimes off by a few centimetres, sometimes a few metres, depending on satellite geometry and signal blockage.
* **GNSS/INS Fusion** is the smart process of *listening to your foot-feelings for the high-frequency motion*, but *periodically asking your friend for a position check* and gently correcting your internal estimate. The result: smooth, drift-free navigation.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| INS output rate | **100 – 1000** | Hz |
| GNSS output rate | **1 – 10** | Hz |
| GNSS velocity accuracy (single point) | **0.05 – 0.5** | m/s |
| INS accelerometer bias | **10 – 1000** | μg |
| INS gyroscope bias | **0.01 – 10** | °/h |
| Velocity of light (used in GNSS) | **$c = 2.998 \times 10^{8}$** | m/s |
| Earth rotation rate | **$\Omega_{ie} = 7.2921 \times 10^{-5}$** | rad/s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why fuse for *velocity* specifically?**
> Velocity is the most direct output of an INS (integrated once, not twice), so its short-term accuracy is excellent. GNSS also provides a Doppler-based velocity, making velocity a *clean and well-conditioned* state for filtering — much more so than position (which needs double integration) or attitude (which is nonlinear).

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architecture of Fusion — Three Coupling Levels

The integration depth strongly affects the achievable velocity accuracy. The KTU syllabus expects students to be able to **differentiate** and **justify** the choice of architecture.

1. **Loosely-Coupled Integration**
   * INS and GNSS operate as **independent black boxes**.
   * GNSS receiver internally solves for position/velocity; only the solution is fed to the filter.
   * **Filter input:** GNSS position $\mathbf{p}_{G,k}$ and velocity $\mathbf{v}_{G,k}$ versus INS-propagated $\mathbf{p}_{I,k}$ and $\mathbf{v}_{I,k}$.
   * **Pros:** Simple, modular, easy to retrofit.
   * **Cons:** Degrades when fewer than 4 satellites are visible (no GNSS solution available).

2. **Tightly-Coupled Integration**
   * Raw GNSS **pseudoranges and pseudorange rates** are fed directly to the central Kalman filter.
   * The filter jointly estimates INS errors *and* GNSS receiver clock errors.
   * **Pros:** Maintains partial benefit even with only 1–3 satellites.
   * **Cons:** Receiver must expose raw measurements.

3. **Deeply-Coupled (Ultra-Tight) Integration**
   * The Kalman filter generates feedback that controls the GNSS tracking loops themselves.
   * **Pros:** Best performance in jamming/weak-signal environments.
   * **Cons:** Highly proprietary, requires deep integration at hardware level.

> [!NOTE]
> **For the KTU 2024 board exam**, the **loosely-coupled** and **tightly-coupled** levels are the most commonly asked. State the coupling level explicitly in your answer to gain easy marks.

### 2.2 INS Velocity Mechanization (Prediction Model)

The INS solves Newton's equation in the **navigation frame (n-frame: East-North-Up)**. The velocity time-derivative is:

$$
\frac{d\mathbf{v}^n}{dt} = \mathbf{C}_b^n \mathbf{f}^b - (2\boldsymbol{\omega}_{ie}^n + \boldsymbol{\omega}_{en}^n) \times \mathbf{v}^n + \mathbf{g}^n
$$

where:
* $\mathbf{C}_b^n$ is the **Direction Cosine Matrix (DCM)** from body to navigation frame.
* $\mathbf{f}^b$ is the **specific force** measured by the accelerometers (m/s²).
* $2\boldsymbol{\omega}_{ie}^n \times \mathbf{v}^n$ is the **Coriolis correction** due to Earth's rotation.
* $\boldsymbol{\omega}_{en}^n \times \mathbf{v}^n$ accounts for rotation of the navigation frame.
* $\mathbf{g}^n$ is the local gravity vector.

After **discretization** with step $\Delta t$:

$$
\mathbf{v}_{k+1}^n = \mathbf{v}_k^n + \left[ \mathbf{C}_{b,k}^n \mathbf{f}^b - (2\boldsymbol{\omega}_{ie,k}^n + \boldsymbol{\omega}_{en,k}^n) \times \mathbf{v}_k^n + \mathbf{g}^n \right] \Delta t + \mathbf{w}_v
$$

where $\mathbf{w}_v \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \mathbf{Q}_v)$ is the process noise from accelerometer bias and scale-factor errors.

### 2.3 GNSS Velocity Measurement (Update Model)

GNSS receivers compute velocity using **Doppler shifts** of the carrier phase:

$$
v_{G,k} = v_{I,k} + \boldsymbol{\eta}_G, \quad \boldsymbol{\eta}_G \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \mathbf{R})
$$

The measurement residual (innovation) is:

$$
\boldsymbol{\delta}\mathbf{z}_k = \mathbf{v}_{G,k} - \mathbf{v}_{I,k}
$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\mid k-1} = \boldsymbol{\Phi}_{k-1} \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k-1 \mid k-1}$ | State prediction | varies |
| 2 | $\mathbf{P}_{k\mid k-1} = \boldsymbol{\Phi}_{k-1} \mathbf{P}_{k-1 \mid k-1} \boldsymbol{\Phi}_{k-1}^T + \mathbf{Q}_{k-1}$ | Covariance prediction | varies |
| 3 | $\mathbf{K}_k = \mathbf{P}_{k\mid k-1} \mathbf{H}^T (\mathbf{H} \mathbf{P}_{k\mid k-1} \mathbf{H}^T + \mathbf{R}_k)^{-1}$ | Kalman Gain | – |
| 4 | $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\mid k} = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\mid k-1} + \mathbf{K}_k \boldsymbol{\delta}\mathbf{z}_k$ | State update | varies |
| 5 | $\mathbf{P}_{k\mid k} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{K}_k \mathbf{H}) \mathbf{P}_{k\mid k-1}$ | Covariance update | varies |
| 6 | $\mathbf{v}_{k+1}^n = \mathbf{v}_k^n + [\mathbf{C}_{b,k}^n \mathbf{f}^b - (2\boldsymbol{\omega}_{ie}^n + \boldsymbol{\omega}_{en}^n) \times \mathbf{v}_k^n + \mathbf{g}^n] \Delta t$ | INS velocity propagation | m/s |
| 7 | $v_{G,k} = v_{I,k} + \boldsymbol{\eta}_G$ | GNSS velocity observation | m/s |
| 8 | $\dot{\mathbf{v}}^n = \mathbf{C}_b^n \mathbf{f}^b - (2\boldsymbol{\omega}_{ie}^n + \boldsymbol{\omega}_{en}^n) \times \mathbf{v}^n + \mathbf{g}^n$ | INS velocity derivative | m/s² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for KF cycle:** **P**redict → **U**pdate → **P**redict → **U**pdate ... (a.k.a. **PU-PU-PU** 🐸). Examiners love it when students mention this loop structure explicitly.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Autonomous Vehicles:** Self-driving cars (e.g., Waymo, Tesla) require cm/s-level velocity for safe lane-change manoeuvres. Pure GNSS is too jumpy; pure INS drifts in tunnels. Fusion gives a smooth, reliable estimate.
* **UAV/Drones:** Quadrotors need ~50 Hz velocity for closed-loop control; GNSS only gives 1–10 Hz.
* **Marine Navigation:** Submarine surfacing intervals, ship dynamic positioning (DP) systems.
* **Robotic Lawn Mowers / AGVs:** Indoor-outdoor transition where GNSS may briefly fail.
* **Surveying & Mobile Mapping:** LiDAR-equipped vehicles need precise trajectory for point-cloud georeferencing.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Continuous-Time Velocity Error Model (Psi-Angle Form)

This derivation is **directly aligned with KTU 2024 Module-4 expected learning outcome PERUT735.LO4.3**: *"Derive the INS error-state propagation model used in the GNSS/INS Kalman filter."*

Let the true state be decomposed as:
$$
\mathbf{v}^n = \hat{\mathbf{v}}^n + \delta\mathbf{v}^n, \quad
\mathbf{C}_b^n = (\mathbf{I} - [\boldsymbol{\psi}\times]) \hat{\mathbf{C}}_b^n, \quad
\mathbf{f}^b = \hat{\mathbf{f}}^b + \delta\mathbf{f}^b
$$

**Step 1: Substitute into the true velocity derivative.**

$$
\frac{d(\hat{\mathbf{v}}^n + \delta\mathbf{v}^n)}{dt} = (\mathbf{I} - [\boldsymbol{\psi}\times]) \hat{\mathbf{C}}_b^n (\hat{\mathbf{f}}^b + \delta\mathbf{f}^b) - (2\boldsymbol{\omega}_{ie}^n + \boldsymbol{\omega}_{en}^n) \times (\hat{\mathbf{v}}^n + \delta\mathbf{v}^n) + \mathbf{g}^n
$$

**Step 2: Subtract the INS-computed derivative** (since $\hat{\mathbf{v}}^n$ satisfies the same equation by construction):

$$
\frac{d\delta\mathbf{v}^n}{dt} = \underbrace{- [\boldsymbol{\psi}\times] \hat{\mathbf{C}}_b^n \hat{\mathbf{f}}^b}_{\text{attitude-leakage}} + \underbrace{\hat{\mathbf{C}}_b^n \delta\mathbf{f}^b}_{\text{accel. error}} \underbrace{- (2\boldsymbol{\omega}_{ie}^n + \boldsymbol{\omega}_{en}^n) \times \delta\mathbf{v}^n}_{\text{Coriolis on error}} \underbrace{- \delta\boldsymbol{\omega}_{in}^n \times \hat{\mathbf{v}}^n}_{\text{rotation rate error}}
$$

**Step 3: Apply the small-angle identity** $[\boldsymbol{\psi}\times]\mathbf{a} \equiv \boldsymbol{\psi} \times \mathbf{a}$ for small attitude errors, and define $\mathbf{f}^n \equiv \hat{\mathbf{C}}_b^n \hat{\mathbf{f}}^b$:

$$
\frac{d\delta\mathbf{v}^n}{dt} = - \boldsymbol{\psi} \times \mathbf{f}^n + \mathbf{C}_b^n \delta\mathbf{f}^b - (2\boldsymbol{\omega}_{ie}^n + \boldsymbol{\omega}_{en}^n) \times \delta\mathbf{v}^n - \delta\boldsymbol{\omega}_{in}^n \times \mathbf{v}^n
$$

**Step 4: Group into a single linear differential equation:**

$$
\boxed{\dot{\delta\mathbf{v}}^n = -(\mathbf{F}_{vv})\delta\mathbf{v}^n + (\mathbf{F}_{v\psi})\boldsymbol{\psi} + \mathbf{C}_b^n \mathbf{b}_a + \mathbf{w}_v}
$$

where:
* $\mathbf{F}_{vv} = [\mathbf{v}^n \times] (2\boldsymbol{\omega}_{ie}^n + \boldsymbol{\omega}_{en}^n) \times$ — the Coriolis coupling matrix.
* $\mathbf{F}_{v\psi} = [\mathbf{f}^n \times]$ — the Schuler-like attitude-to-velocity coupling.
* $\mathbf{b}_a$ is the accelerometer bias.

### 3.2 Derivation: Discrete-Time Kalman Filter Update for Velocity

**Step 1: Innovation (measurement residual) computation.**

$$
\boldsymbol{\delta}\mathbf{z}_k = \mathbf{z}_k - \mathbf{H}\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\mid k-1} = \mathbf{v}_{G,k} - \hat{\mathbf{v}}_{I,k\mid k-1}
$$

**Step 2: Kalman Gain computation.**

$$
\mathbf{K}_k = \mathbf{P}_{k\mid k-1} \mathbf{H}^T (\mathbf{H} \mathbf{P}_{k\mid k-1} \mathbf{H}^T + \mathbf{R}_k)^{-1}
$$

For pure-velocity fusion, the measurement matrix is simply:
$$
\mathbf{H} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & \mathbf{I}_3 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$
(extracts the velocity block from the state vector $\mathbf{x} = [\delta\mathbf{p}, \delta\mathbf{v}, \boldsymbol{\psi}, \mathbf{b}_a]^T$).

**Step 3: Velocity correction.**

$$
\hat{\mathbf{v}}_{I,k\mid k} = \hat{\mathbf{v}}_{I,k\mid k-1} + \mathbf{K}_{k,vel} \boldsymbol{\delta}\mathbf{z}_k
$$

**Step 4: Worked Numerical Example** *(for KTU 14-mark question practice)*

> A vehicle is in a 1-D straight-line motion. At time $k-1$, the INS-predicted velocity is $\hat{v}_{k-1} = 15.0$ m/s with covariance $P_{k-1} = 0.25$ m²/s². The accelerometer noise covariance is $Q = 0.04$ m²/s². The GNSS reports $v_G = 15.8$ m/s with measurement covariance $R = 0.36$ m²/s². Find the fused estimate $v_k$ and the updated covariance.

**Solution:**

*Prediction:*
$$
P_{k\mid k-1} = P_{k-1} + Q = 0.25 + 0.04 = 0.29 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2
$$
$$
\hat{v}_{k\mid k-1} = 15.0 \text{ m/s}
$$

*Innovation:*
$$
\delta z_k = v_G - \hat{v}_{k\mid k-1} = 15.8 - 15.0 = 0.8 \text{ m/s}
$$

*Kalman Gain:*
$$
K_k = \frac{P_{k\mid k-1}}{P_{k\mid k-1} + R} = \frac{0.29}{0.29 + 0.36} = \frac{0.29}{0.65} = 0.4462
$$

*Update:*
$$
v_k = \hat{v}_{k\mid k-1} + K_k \cdot \delta z_k = 15.0 + 0.4462 \times 0.8 = 15.357 \text{ m/s}
$$

*Updated covariance:*
$$
P_{k\mid k} = (1 - K_k) P_{k\mid k-1} = (1 - 0.4462) \times 0.29 = 0.5538 \times 0.29 = 0.1606 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2
$$

**Sanity check:** Notice that the fused estimate (15.357) lies between the INS prediction (15.0) and the GNSS measurement (15.8), closer to the INS — this is correct because INS covariance (0.29) is *lower* than GNSS covariance (0.36), so the filter trusts INS more.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — "Which Sensor Wins When?"

| Condition | Trust Goes To | Kalman Gain $K_k$ |
|---|---|---|
| $R \to \infty$ (GNSS unavailable/jammed) | Pure INS prediction | $K \to 0$ |
| $P_{k\mid k-1} \to \infty$ (INS just initialised) | Pure GNSS measurement | $K \to 1$ |
| $R \ll P_{k\mid k-1}$ (GNSS very accurate) | GNSS dominates | $K \to 1$ |
| $Q \to 0$ (INS accelerometer ideal) | INS dominates | $K \to 0$ |
| Long time since last GNSS fix | INS prediction | $K$ shrinks over time |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Whenever asked to justify a fusion result, always compute both $P_{k\mid k-1}$ and $K_k$ explicitly. Marks are awarded for the *numbers*, not just the formula.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of GNSS/INS Velocity Fusion

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[IMU Sensors Accel + Gyro] --> B[INS Mechanization Strapdown Solver]
    B --> C[INS Velocity vI k and Cov PI k]
    D[GNSS Receiver Doppler Tracking] --> E[GNSS Velocity vG k and Cov Rk]
    C --> F{Kalman Filter Predict Step}
    F --> G[Predicted State xv k and Cov P k|k-1]
    E --> H{Kalman Filter Update Step}
    G --> H
    H --> I[Innovation delta zk]
    I --> J[Kalman Gain Kk]
    J --> K[Fused Velocity v k|k]
    K --> L[Feedback to INS Reset]
    L --> B
    K --> M[Output to Navigation Computer]
    H --> N[Updated Cov P k|k]
    N --> F
```

### 4.2 Information Flow: Sensor → Filter → Output

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S1[Sensor Layer]
        sA[Accelerometer 100 Hz]
        sG[Gyroscope 100 Hz]
        sN[GNSS Antenna 5 Hz]
    end
    subgraph S2[Estimation Layer]
        eM[Mechanization Module]
        eP[KF Predict]
        eU[KF Update]
    end
    subgraph S3[Output Layer]
        oV[Velocity Vector ENU]
        oP[Position Vector]
        oA[Attitude Quaternion]
    end
    sA --> eM
    sG --> eM
    eM --> eP
    sN --> eU
    eP --> eU
    eU --> oV
    eU --> oP
    eU --> oA
    eU -. feedback correction .-> eM
```

### 4.3 Sequential Timing Diagram of Updates

```mermaid
gantt
    title Sample Timing for Velocity Updates
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %S
    section INS
    INS Velocity Available 1Hz step    :a1, 0, 10
    INS Velocity Available 2Hz step    :a2, 10, 10
    INS Velocity Available 3Hz step    :a3, 20, 10
    section GNSS
    GNSS Velocity Available            :b1, 0, 100
    GNSS Velocity Available 2nd epoch  :b2, 200, 100
    GNSS Velocity Available 3rd epoch  :b3, 400, 100
    section KF
    Kalman Predict Every 10ms          :crit, 0, 10
    Kalman Predict 2                   :crit, 10, 10

```

### 4.4 Decision Tree — When Does Each Sensor Dominate?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New Measurement Arrives] --> Q1{Is it INS data?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is GNSS healthy?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{Is it GNSS data?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> U[Trigger Kalman Update Step]
    U --> Out1[Fused Velocity Output]
    Q2 -- Yes --> P1[Predict step only]
    Q2 -- No --> P2[Predict with Q inflated]
    P1 --> P3[Output INS-only velocity]
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> Out1
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Mermaid block-level architecture** captures the full prediction–update–feedback loop that defines GNSS/INS velocity fusion. Examiners award credit for showing the **feedback path from filter to INS** (closed-loop correction) — it is the single most-missed element in student answers.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the fundamental difference between loosely-coupled and tightly-coupled GNSS/INS integration architectures.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
In *loosely-coupled* integration, the GNSS receiver acts as an independent subsystem that internally computes a full position/velocity solution; only this solution is compared against the INS output in the Kalman filter. It requires a minimum of **4 satellites** to function.
In *tightly-coupled* integration, the raw **pseudoranges and pseudorange rates** from the GNSS receiver are fed directly into the central Kalman filter, which jointly estimates INS errors and receiver clock biases. It can continue providing useful updates with as few as **1–3 satellites**. (3 marks)

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Why is the Kalman Gain $K_k$ important in GNSS/INS velocity fusion? What happens when $R_k \to \infty$?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Kalman Gain $K_k$ determines the relative weight given to the new GNSS measurement versus the INS prediction. It is computed as $K_k = P_{k\mid k-1} H^T (H P_{k\mid k-1} H^T + R_k)^{-1}$.
When $R_k \to \infty$ (GNSS measurement is unavailable or infinitely noisy), $K_k \to 0$, which means the filter discards the GNSS update and falls back to the pure INS prediction. The system continues to navigate, but accumulates drift over time. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the continuous-time velocity error differential equation of an INS in the navigation frame, clearly identifying the contributions of attitude error, accelerometer error, and Coriolis coupling. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A vehicle navigates with the following parameters at time $k-1$: INS-predicted velocity $\hat{v}_{I,k-1} = 20$ m/s, INS covariance $P_{k-1} = 0.64$ m²/s², process noise $Q = 0.16$ m²/s². At time $k$, a GNSS fix arrives with $v_G = 21.2$ m/s and $R = 0.49$ m²/s². Compute (i) the predicted covariance, (ii) the Kalman Gain, (iii) the fused velocity estimate, and (iv) the updated covariance. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

Starting from the navigation-frame velocity equation:
$$
\dot{\mathbf{v}}^n = \mathbf{C}_b^n \mathbf{f}^b - (2\boldsymbol{\omega}_{ie}^n + \boldsymbol{\omega}_{en}^n) \times \mathbf{v}^n + \mathbf{g}^n
$$

Substituting the perturbed state $\mathbf{v}^n = \hat{\mathbf{v}}^n + \delta\mathbf{v}^n$, $\mathbf{C}_b^n = (\mathbf{I} - [\boldsymbol{\psi}\times])\hat{\mathbf{C}}_b^n$, $\mathbf{f}^b = \hat{\mathbf{f}}^b + \delta\mathbf{f}^b$ and subtracting the INS-computed derivative:

$$
\dot{\delta\mathbf{v}}^n = -[\boldsymbol{\psi}\times]\hat{\mathbf{C}}_b^n \hat{\mathbf{f}}^b + \hat{\mathbf{C}}_b^n \delta\mathbf{f}^b - (2\boldsymbol{\omega}_{ie}^n + \boldsymbol{\omega}_{en}^n)\times \delta\mathbf{v}^n - \delta\boldsymbol{\omega}_{in}^n \times \hat{\mathbf{v}}^n
$$

Using the small-angle identity $[\boldsymbol{\psi}\times]\mathbf{a} = \boldsymbol{\psi}\times\mathbf{a}$ and defining $\mathbf{f}^n = \hat{\mathbf{C}}_b^n \hat{\mathbf{f}}^b$:

$$
\boxed{\dot{\delta\mathbf{v}}^n = -[\mathbf{f}^n \times]\boldsymbol{\psi} + \mathbf{C}_b^n \delta\mathbf{f}^b - (2\boldsymbol{\omega}_{ie}^n + \boldsymbol{\omega}_{en}^n)\times \delta\mathbf{v}^n - (\delta\boldsymbol{\omega}_{ie}^n + \delta\boldsymbol{\omega}_{en}^n) \times \hat{\mathbf{v}}^n}
$$

**Interpretation of terms (for full 7 marks):**
* $-[\mathbf{f}^n \times]\boldsymbol{\psi}$: attitude error leaks into velocity through cross-product with specific force.
* $\mathbf{C}_b^n \delta\mathbf{f}^b$: direct accelerometer error contribution.
* $-(2\boldsymbol{\omega}_{ie}^n + \boldsymbol{\omega}_{en}^n)\times \delta\mathbf{v}^n$: Coriolis coupling of velocity error.
* Last term: rotation-rate error coupling.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**(i) Predicted covariance:**
$$
P_{k\mid k-1} = P_{k-1} + Q = 0.64 + 0.16 = 0.80 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2
$$

**(ii) Kalman Gain:**
$$
K_k = \frac{P_{k\mid k-1}}{P_{k\mid k-1} + R} = \frac{0.80}{0.80 + 0.49} = \frac{0.80}{1.29} = 0.6202
$$

**(iii) Fused velocity:**
$$
\delta z_k = v_G - \hat{v}_{I,k\mid k-1} = 21.2 - 20.0 = 1.2 \text{ m/s}
$$
$$
v_k = \hat{v}_{I,k\mid k-1} + K_k \cdot \delta z_k = 20.0 + 0.6202 \times 1.2 = 20.744 \text{ m/s}
$$

**(iv) Updated covariance:**
$$
P_{k\mid k} = (1 - K_k) P_{k\mid k-1} = (1 - 0.6202) \times 0.80 = 0.3798 \times 0.80 = 0.3039 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2
$$

**Result:** $v_k = 20.744$ m/s, $P_{k\mid k} = 0.304$ m²/s². The covariance dropped from 0.80 to 0.30, demonstrating the value of the GNSS update. (7 marks)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**(a)** With the aid of a block diagram, explain the architecture of a **loosely-coupled** GNSS/INS integration system for velocity estimation. Clearly state the role of the Kalman Filter. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the velocity error characteristics of pure INS, pure GNSS, and combined GNSS/INS over **(i) short term (< 10 s)** and **(ii) long term (> 1 hour)**. Justify your answer with reference to the Allan-variance / drift behaviour of inertial sensors. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Block Diagram (textual for clarity):**
```
[IMU: Accel + Gyro] → [INS Mechanization] → [INS Velocity vI] ─┐
                                                                  ├→ [Kalman Filter] → [Fused Velocity]
[GNSS Receiver] ────────→ [GNSS Velocity vG] ───────────────────┘                          │
                                                  ↑                                       │
                                                  └─────── Feedback (closed loop) ────────┘
```

**Explanation (7 marks):**
* The INS runs at 100–1000 Hz and produces a smooth, high-rate velocity vector.
* The GNSS runs at 1–10 Hz and produces a noisy but drift-free velocity vector.
* The Kalman Filter receives both, computes an innovation $\delta z_k = v_{G,k} - v_{I,k\mid k-1}$, and produces an **optimal fused velocity** that is closer to truth than either source.
* The filter also outputs **error-state estimates** (velocity error, attitude error, biases) which can be fed back to reset the INS (closed-loop mode) — this prevents the INS error from growing unboundedly between GNSS updates.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

| Time Scale | Pure INS | Pure GNSS | Combined GNSS/INS |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Short term (< 10 s)** | **Excellent** — velocity noise ~few mm/s, no divergence yet | Poor — Doppler noise ~5–20 cm/s at 1 Hz | **Best of both** — INS smoothing dominates; Kalman Gain $K \approx 0.1$–$0.2$ |
| **(ii) Long term (> 1 hour)** | **Degrades** — velocity error grows ~$t$ due to accelerometer bias; position drifts ~$t^2$ | Stable — bounded by ~0.05–0.5 m/s | **Excellent and bounded** — GNSS updates constantly reset INS drift; overall error stays within ~0.1 m/s |

**Allan-variance justification:**
* Inertial sensor noise has components: **angle random walk** (white noise at short $t$), **bias instability** (flicker noise at medium $t$), and **rate ramp** (deterministic drift at long $t$).
* Over long periods, the **bias instability term** dominates INS velocity error growth.
* The GNSS fix resets the integrated bias effect, so the combined system never "sees" the long-term Allan-variance tail of the inertial sensor. (7 marks)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Losing Mistakes in GNSS/INS Velocity Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Coriolis term** $2\boldsymbol{\omega}_{ie}^n \times \mathbf{v}^n$ in the INS velocity derivative — costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing process noise $Q$ with measurement noise $R$** — remember: $Q$ grows the covariance during prediction, $R$ shrinks it during update.
> 3. **Not specifying the coupling level** (loose / tight / deep) — always state the architecture in 1 sentence before drawing the block diagram.
> 4. **Mixing up $\mathbf{P}_{k\mid k-1}$ and $\mathbf{P}_{k\mid k}$** — predicted covariance uses *plus* $Q$; updated covariance uses *$(1-K)$* factor.
> 5. **Skipping units in the final numerical answer** — every velocity must end with "m/s", every covariance with "m²/s²".
> 6. **Not justifying the filter weight** when $K > 0.5$ or $K < 0.5$ — always comment on which sensor the filter is trusting more, and why.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Estimating Velocity by Combining GNSS and INS**
> - [x] The goal is to obtain a **smooth, drift-free, high-rate** velocity estimate.
> - [x] INS provides **high rate but drifts**; GNSS provides **low rate but bounded**; fusion gives **best of both**.
> - [x] **Loosely-coupled** = GNSS solution is fused; **Tightly-coupled** = raw pseudoranges are fused.
> - [x] **Kalman Filter cycle**: Predict (with $Q$) → Update (with $R$) → Predict → Update ...
> - [x] **Prediction equation**: $P_{k\mid k-1} = P_{k-1} + Q$; $\hat{v}_{k\mid k-1}$ from INS.
> - [x] **Update equations**: $K_k = \frac{P_{k|k-1}}{P_{k|k-1} + R}$; $v_k = \hat{v}_{k|k-1} + K_k \delta z_k$; $P_{k|k} = (1-K_k) P_{k|k-1}$.
> - [x] **Innovation**: $\delta z_k = v_{G,k} - \hat{v}_{I,k|k-1}$.
> - [x] **Velocity error model** must include Coriolis $(2\boldsymbol{\omega}_{ie} + \boldsymbol{\omega}_{en})\times \delta\mathbf{v}$, attitude-leakage $-[\mathbf{f}^n\times]\boldsymbol{\psi}$, and accelerometer bias $\mathbf{C}_b^n \mathbf{b}_a$.
> - [x] **Key constants**: $\Omega_{ie} = 7.2921 \times 10^{-5}$ rad/s, $g \approx 9.81$ m/s².
> - [x] **Sanity check** — fused estimate always lies *between* the two sensor values, weighted inversely by their covariances.
> - [x] **Long-term behaviour** of fusion is **bounded**; pure INS diverges as $t^2$ in position.
> - [x] Always state the **coupling architecture** and the **filter mode** (open-loop vs closed-loop feedback) explicitly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: A-GPS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT735 - ROBOT NAVIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** A-GPS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**A-GPS (Assisted Global Positioning System)** is an enhanced satellite-based positioning architecture that augments a conventional Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) receiver with externally supplied *Assistance Data* delivered over a wireless communication network (cellular, Wi-Fi, or internet). The objective of A-GPS is to dramatically reduce the **Time-To-First-Fix (TTFF)**, increase receiver sensitivity in weak-signal environments (urban canyons, indoor, foliage), and lower computational/power load on the mobile device — all of which are critical for real-time **Robot Navigation** and autonomous systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Concept):**
> "A-GPS is a system that uses external reference network resources to assist a GNSS receiver in acquiring satellites, decoding navigation messages, and computing a position fix faster than would otherwise be possible in a standalone configuration."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are lost in a massive library with **10 million books** (the GPS satellites) and you need to find a specific recipe (your position). A normal GPS would be like a person who has to read the title of *every* book in the library before finding the right one — this takes a long time (cold start ~30–60 s).

A-GPS is like a **librarian who hands you a shortlist of 8–12 books** to check, tells you exactly *where* on each shelf to look, and gives you a precise wristwatch so you know exactly when the books will signal. Now you can find your recipe in **3–5 seconds**.

**Key intuition:** A-GPS does not replace GPS — it *assists* it by offloading the slow, energy-intensive satellite-acquisition phase to a powerful ground server.

### 1.3 Why A-GPS Matters for Robot Navigation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - A robot navigating in **GNSS-denied or degraded environments** (tunnels, urban canyons, indoor warehouses) benefits enormously from A-GPS sensitivity enhancement (down to **−160 dBm** vs standard GPS limit of **−147 dBm**).
> - Autonomous vehicles require a position fix within **< 1 second** of system wake-up to make time-critical control decisions.
> - A-GPS enables **lower power consumption** because the receiver's RF front-end is active for a much shorter duration per fix.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| L1 Carrier Frequency | **1575.42 MHz** | Primary civil GPS signal |
| Standard GPS Sensitivity Floor | **−147 dBm** | Cold-start acquisition limit |
| A-GPS Sensitivity Floor | **−160 dBm** | With assistance (≈ 20 dB gain) |
| Speed of Light (vacuum) | **c = 2.998 × 10⁸ m/s** | Underlies all pseudorange math |
| Typical Cold-Start TTFF (standalone) | **30 – 60 s** | Without assistance |
| A-GPS TTFF (typical) | **1 – 5 s** | With assistance |
| Position Accuracy (SA off) | **2.5 m CEP (95%)** | Horizontal, clear sky |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Core Problem A-GPS Solves

A standalone GPS receiver must perform **four slow, parallel operations** before producing a position fix:

1. **Acquire satellites** — search across ±10 kHz Doppler and 1023-chip C/A code phases (≈ 40,000 cells per satellite).
2. **Decode the navigation message** — 50 bps, takes 30 s for a full subframe.
3. **Compute a pseudorange** to ≥ 4 satellites.
4. **Solve the navigation equation** (trilateration).

A-GPS removes the first two bottlenecks by **pre-supplying** the results of (1) and (2) from a ground reference network that has been doing exactly this work continuously.

### 2.2 A-GPS System Architecture (Logical Layers)

A-GPS comprises four logical entities:

| Entity | Function |
|---|---|
| **GPS Reference Network (GRN)** | High-precision stationary GPS receivers at surveyed locations; collect raw almanac, ephemeris, ionospheric, tropospheric, and integrity data continuously. |
| **Assistance Server (A-Server)** | Computes the *Assistance Data* packet, validates it, and pushes it on demand to the Mobile Station. |
| **Communication Network** | Cellular (GSM/UMTS/LTE/5G), Wi-Fi, or any IP bearer — the "tube" through which assistance is delivered. |
| **Mobile Station (MS)** | The robot, smartphone, or autonomous vehicle — receives assistance, performs limited measurements, computes the fix. |

### 2.3 Two Operating Modes of A-GPS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MS-Based (Mobile Station Based) Mode:**
> The assistance server provides **full assistance data** (almanac, ephemeris, time, location, ionospheric corrections). The MS performs both signal acquisition and position computation locally. **Higher privacy**, **lower latency** after first fix.

> **MS-Assisted (Mobile Station Assisted) Mode:**
> The server provides **acquisition assistance** (approximate Doppler, code phase, time). The MS returns **raw pseudoranges or measurement snapshots** to the server, which computes the position. **Better sensitivity** (server can correlate much longer), **lower power** on MS, but **requires network round-trip**.

### 2.4 Categories of Assistance Data

The assistance data payload is structured under 3GPP TS 44.031 / TS 36.355 standards and contains:

- **Reference Time** — precise GPS system time (μs-level) so the MS knows which PRN codes are currently being transmitted.
- **Reference Location** — coarse MS position (typically ±2 km), shrinks the satellite search space.
- **Navigation Model** — current Ephemeris for each visible satellite (so the MS does not have to wait 30 s to decode it).
- **Almanac** — coarse orbital parameters for *all* 31+ satellites (used for satellite selection).
- **Ionospheric Model** — Klobuchar coefficients to correct ionospheric delay.
- **UTC Model** — GPS-to-UTC time offset.
- **Acquisition Assistance** — predicted Doppler and code-phase search windows.
- **Real-Time Integrity (RTI)** — flags for unhealthy satellites.
- **DGPS Corrections** — to push accuracy from **2.5 m** to **< 1 m**.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Use in A-GPS / Robot Nav |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Free-space path loss | $$L_{fs} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi d f}{c}\right) \text{ dB}$$ | Sets the link-budget floor for GNSS signals |
| 2 | Received signal power | $$P_{r} = P_{t} + G_{t} + G_{r} - L_{fs} - L_{atm}$$ | Computes the expected C/N₀ at the robot's antenna |
| 3 | Pseudorange equation | $$R_i = \rho_i + c(\delta t_{u} - \delta t_{s,i}) + I_i + T_i + \varepsilon_{\text{mp},i}$$ | Linearised for the nav-equation solution |
| 4 | Dilution of Precision (DOP) | $$\text{GDOP} = \sqrt{\text{trace}\!\left(H^{T}H\right)^{-1}}$$ | Quality of satellite geometry; A-GPS selects best subset |
| 5 | Position accuracy (95%) | $$\sigma_{2D} \approx 2 \cdot \text{HDOP} \cdot \sigma_{\text{UERE}}$$ | Where $\sigma_{\text{UERE}}$ is the user-equivalent range error |
| 6 | TTFF reduction ratio | $$\text{TTFF}_{\text{A-GPS}} \approx \frac{\text{TTFF}_{\text{cold}}}{N_{\text{vis}}}$$ | Aided acquisition parallelises $N_{\text{vis}}$ satellites |
| 7 | Sensitivity gain (approx) | $$\Delta S = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{T_{\text{coh,server}}}{T_{\text{coh,MS}}}\right) \text{ dB}$$ | Why MS-Assisted can acquire −160 dBm signals |
| 8 | Coherent integration gain | $$G_{\text{coh}} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(T_{\text{coh}}\right) \text{ dB (per Hz)}$$ | $T_{\text{coh}}$ in seconds; doubles SNR every 3 dB when doubled |

> [!WARNING]
> **| pipe-symbol avoidance:** All table cells use $\vert$ (LaTeX vertical bar) for absolute value, NEVER a literal `|` character, to preserve markdown table integrity.

### 2.6 Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles:** A-GPS enables lane-level (sub-metre) fix in seconds, mandatory for path-planning and obstacle-avoidance loops operating at 10–100 Hz.
- **Indoor Service Robots:** Hybrid A-GPS + Wi-Fi fingerprinting bridges the outdoor-to-indoor positioning gap.
- **Drones (UAVs):** A-GPS via LTE/5G ensures rapid fix after power-up, critical for emergency missions and BVLOS (Beyond Visual Line of Sight) operations.
- **Search-and-Rescue Robots:** Network-assisted acquisition allows positioning where the satellite signal is **attenuated by 20+ dB** (e.g., under rubble).
- **Maritime Robotics:** DGPS corrections in the assistance stream push accuracy to **< 1 m**, required for harbour piloting.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Sensitivity Gain from Coherent Integration

A-GPS achieves its superior sensitivity (down to **−160 dBm**) by extending the *coherent integration time* $T_{\text{coh}}$. We derive the relationship explicitly.

**Starting point — the basic radar/radio SNR equation after matched filtering:**

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{out}} = \frac{P_{r}}{N_{0} \cdot B_{n}}
$$

where:
- $P_{r}$ = received signal power (W)
- $N_{0}$ = noise spectral density (W/Hz)
- $B_{n}$ = noise bandwidth after matched filter (Hz)

**Step 1:** Coherent integration over time $T_{\text{coh}}$ averages out uncorrelated noise while signal energy adds coherently. The effective SNR scales linearly with $T_{\text{coh}}$:

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{int}} = \text{SNR}_{\text{out}} \cdot T_{\text{coh}}
$$

**Step 2:** Convert to decibels, noting that signal bandwidth collapses to $1 / T_{\text{coh}}$:

$$
G_{\text{coh}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{T_{\text{coh}}}{T_{\text{ref}}}\right) \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the standard reference $T_{\text{ref}} = 1 \text{ ms}$ (one C/A code period) used in GNSS receivers:

$$
G_{\text{coh}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{T_{\text{coh}}}{10^{-3}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(10^{3} \cdot T_{\text{coh}}\right) \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate for typical A-GPS server values ($T_{\text{coh}} = 1 \text{ s}$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{\text{coh}}^{\text{server}} &= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{10^{-3}}\right) \\
&= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(10^{3}\right) \\
&= 10 \times 3 \\
&= 30 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate for a typical mobile station ($T_{\text{coh}} = 10 \text{ ms}$ due to local oscillator instability):

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{\text{coh}}^{\text{MS}} &= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{10 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-3}}\right) \\
&= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(10\right) \\
&= 10 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** The **sensitivity gain** of A-GPS (MS-Assisted) over standalone GPS is therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta S &= G_{\text{coh}}^{\text{server}} - G_{\text{coh}}^{\text{MS}} \\
&= 30 \text{ dB} - 10 \text{ dB} \\
&= 20 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** This **20 dB** theoretical gain explains the **−160 dBm vs −147 dBm** sensitivity floor quoted earlier. In practice, non-coherent integration and oscillator drift reduce this to **~13–17 dB**, which is still sufficient to operate indoors and in urban canyons.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: A-GPS TTFF Reduction

The Time-To-First-Fix in a cold-start standalone GPS is dominated by the slowest of two serial processes: satellite acquisition AND navigation-message decoding. The slowest is the 25-frame (12.5 minute) worst case; in practice, **TTFF ≈ 30–60 s** to acquire 4 satellites and decode subframes 1–3.

**Step 1 — Standalone search-space size per satellite:**

$$
N_{\text{cells}} = N_{\text{Doppler}} \times N_{\text{code}} = 21 \times 1023 \approx 21{,}500 \text{ cells}
$$

where the Doppler search window is **±10 kHz** in 1 kHz steps and the C/A code has 1023 chips.

**Step 2 — With A-GPS assistance, the search space shrinks dramatically:**

$$
N_{\text{cells, A-GPS}} = N_{\text{Doppler,aided}} \times N_{\text{code,aided}} = 3 \times 50 \approx 150 \text{ cells}
$$
